confirmed.title: getBaseChance keys tier-scaled logic off the raw settType sentinel ('random'/'custom') instead of the resolved tier
confirmed.category: wrong simulation output / inter-step data contract violation
confirmed.evidence: resolveConfig resolves the tier and writes BOTH the raw sentinel (effectiveConfig.settType stays 'random'/'custom' via `...config`) AND the resolved tier (effectiveConfig.tier) — see src/generators/steps/resolveConfig.js:144-160. But getBaseChance reads `config.settType || config.tier` (settType wins, and it is always set): line 80 `tierMagicPenalty = {thorp:0.15,...}[config.settType || config.tier || 'town'] ?? 0.75` — for settType 'random' or 'custom' the lookup misses and falls back to 0.75 (town-level) regardless of the actual tier. Empirically verified with a node probe: getBaseChance(0.2,'Magic','Hedge wizard', {settType:'thorp',...}) = 0.03 vs {settType:'random', tier:'thorp',...} = 0.15 — a 5x over-spawn of arcane institutions in random-rolled thorps (and under-suppression at city/metropolis: 0.75 vs 1.0). Same pattern at line 134 (`isHighMagicIsolated` checks ['town','city','metropolis'].includes(settType||tier) — never true for settType 'random', so the 4x teleportation/planar boost for isolated high-magic town+ never fires in random mode), lines 211 and 248 (`ownTier = config.settType === 'custom' ? 'village' : (config.settType || 'village')` — 'random' gives TIER_ORDER.indexOf(-1), so tierDiff < -1 for ANY neighbour >= village and the x0.4 government suppression fires regardless of real tier; probe: metropolis + village neighbour, explicit settType gives 0.5, random settType gives 0.2; 'custom' hardcodes 'village' even though popToTier resolved the real tier), and line 260 (getPriorityModifiers keyed GOODS_MODIFIERS_BY_TIER['random'] = {} — goods-toggle penalties inert in random mode). DEFAULT_CONFIG.settType is 'random' (src/store/configSlice.js:10), so this hits the app's default generation path on every run. assembleInstitutions (steps/assembleInstitutions.js:288-290) passes effectiveConfig, which carries the correct resolved `tier` — it is simply consulted second.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In institutionProbability.js, resolve the tier as `config.tier || (TIER_ORDER.includes(config.settType) ? config.settType : 'town')` (resolved tier first); apply the same fix at lines 80, 134, 211, 248, and 260. Add a regression test with settType:'random' + tier:'thorp'/'metropolis' configs.

verdicts.reasoning: Empirically verified, not refutable. resolveConfig.js builds effectiveConfig as {...config, tier, ...} (lines 144-160): raw settType ('random'/'custom') survives the spread and the resolved tier is added alongside it. assembleInstitutions.js:288-289 and factionCorrelationPass.js:49 pass that effectiveConfig into getBaseChance, where every tier lookup reads `config.settType || config.tier` — settType wins and is always set, so the sentinel string is used as the tier key. Node probes against the actual module reproduce all claimed numbers: (a) line 80 magic tier penalty — explicit thorp gives 0.03 vs settType:'random'+tier:'thorp' gives 0.15 (5x over-spawn of arcane institutions in random-rolled thorps; cities get 0.75 instead of 1.0); (b) line 134 — the documented 4x isolated-high-magic teleportation/planar boost (which the nearby comment says exists so the violation check can downgrade critical to warning) never fires in random mode (0.756 vs 0.189 for an isolated magic-70 town); (c) lines 211/248 — ownTier='random' yields TIER_ORDER.indexOf=-1, so the x0.4 government suppression fires for any neighbour >= village regardless of real tier (metropolis+village neighbour: 0.5 explicit vs 0.2 random). DEFAULT_CONFIG.settType is 'random' (configSlice.js:10) and the store invokes generateSettlementPipeline (settlementSlice.js:558/866), so this hits the app's default generation path. It is not intentional: economicGenerator.js:118/477/1175 use the tier-first ordering with an explicit comment "settType may be 'random' in random mode, so check config.tier too" — the codebase already fixed this exact bug class elsewhere. No tests cover getBaseChance or lock in the sentinel behavior. Wrong simulation output on the main path = high.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct but should use tier-first ordering to match the established pattern in economicGenerator.js: `config.tier || (TIER_ORDER.includes(config.settType) ? config.settType : fallback)`. Two refinements: (1) the line-260 sub-claim is overstated — getPriorityModifiers is inert in ALL modes, not just random, because no entry in GOODS_MODIFIERS_BY_TIER defines `institutionKeywords` (the data uses `institutions` arrays); fixing the key there is harmless but changes no behavior today. (2) Same-bug-class siblings exist outside the cited file and are worth a follow-up sweep: servicesGenerator.js lines 206, 347, 2016, 2046 use settType-first ordering (`s.settType || s.tier`), and economicGenerator.js:386 reads `config?.settType` alone for its town+ check — these would silently miss in random/custom mode the same way.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the code and by a read-only node probe against the real module. resolveConfig (src/generators/steps/resolveConfig.js:144-160) spreads `...config` into effectiveConfig, preserving the raw sentinel settType ('random'/'custom') alongside the resolved `tier`; getBaseChance (src/generators/institutionProbability.js) consults `config.settType || config.tier` so the sentinel always wins. Probe results: tierMagicPenalty (line 80) — explicit thorp 0.03 vs random+tier:thorp 0.15 (5x), explicit metropolis 0.2 vs random 0.15; isHighMagicIsolated (line 134) — explicit city teleportation 1.0 vs random+tier:city 0.216 (4x boost never fires, breaking the documented isolation-self-resolution mechanic); neighbour gov suppression (lines 211-217) — explicit metropolis + village neighbour 0.5 vs random 0.2 (TIER_ORDER.indexOf('random') = -1 makes tierDiff < -1 for any neighbour >= village); goods-toggle penalties (lines 259-262) — GOODS_MODIFIERS_BY_TIER['random'] is undefined so penalties are inert, while the UI (TradeDynamicsPanel.jsx:121-124 via resolveDisplayTier returning 'all', selectors.js:36) is reachable in random mode and writes real-tier-prefixed toggle keys the engine then ignores. Both pipeline call sites pass the affected effectiveConfig: assembleInstitutions.js:288-290 and factionCorrelationPass.js:48-51 -> factionCorrelation.js:196-198. DEFAULT_CONFIG.settType is 'random' (src/store/configSlice.js:10), so this hits the default generation path on every run. Consequence is deterministic-but-wrong simulation output on the main path: arcane over-spawn in small random settlements, wrongful government suppression next to any village+ neighbour, dead isolation escape hatch, and silently inert goods exclusions. Not a crash, money, or security issue, and determinism is preserved, so per the rubric this is high (wrong simulation output / broken feature on the main path), not critical.
verdicts.fix_note: The implied fix (key off resolved tier) is right but needs four corrections. (1) Prefer `config.tier` first at ALL five sites: `config.tier || config.settType || fallback` — effectiveConfig.tier is always set by resolveConfig, and explicit-mode legacy callers where only settType is a real tier name still degrade gracefully. Only two production callers exist (assembleInstitutions, factionCorrelation), both passing effectiveConfig, so the change is safe. (2) Lines 211/248/260 must also drop the 'custom' hardcodes ('village'/'town'): popToTier already resolved the real tier into effectiveConfig.tier, so a custom-population metropolis currently behaves as a village in neighbour-tier math and as a town in goods lookups. (3) Fixing the tier does NOT fully restore goods penalties (line 260): in random mode the UI's 'all' view writes the toggle key under the FIRST tier in TIER_ORDER containing the good (TradeDynamicsPanel.jsx getKey, lines 119-124), which may differ from the settlement's resolved tier for goods defined at multiple tiers — getPriorityModifiers should check the toggle key across tiers (or the key scheme should be normalized), otherwise random-mode exclusions still miss whenever first-occurrence tier != resolved tier. (4) Expect snapshot-test churn: any seed generated in random/custom mode will produce a different institution roster after the fix; that is the intended correction, but snapshots will need regeneration and the change should be flagged as output-affecting.

confirmed.title: Pipeline-rail step summaries read context keys the pipeline never writes; resolveStress summary always asserts 'No active stressors'
confirmed.category: inter-step data contract violation / broken feature
confirmed.evidence: STEP_METADATA summaries are called with the live pipeline ctx (store/settlementSlice.js:568-574 pushes summary(ctx) into pipelineHistory for the PipelineReveal/PipelineRail 'How this was simulated' UI). Several summaries reference keys that never exist on ctx: line 41 resolveStress reads `ctx.stressors?.active?.length` but the step provides `stress`/`stressTypes` (ctx.stressors only exists on the final settlement object) — so the falsy branch returns the string 'No active stressors' even when stressors ARE active; line 27 resolveConfig reads `ctx.config?.tier` (raw config has settType, not tier — resolved tier lives at ctx.tier); line 33 resolveResources reads `ctx.resources?.local` (step provides nearbyResources); line 88 generateEconomy reads `ctx.economy?.prosperityBand` (step provides economicState, and that object has prosperityLevel — grep shows no generator writes prosperityBand); line 96 generatePower reads `ctx.power?.factions` (step provides powerStructure; ctx.factions is minted later by generatePopulation); line 109 factionCorrelationPass reads `ctx.tensions` / `ctx.history?.currentTensions` (history is generated later by generateNarratives). Empirically verified by running the pipeline with forced famine+plague_onset and evaluating each summary per step: resolveStress printed "No active stressors" while stressConfirmPass printed "2 stressors confirmed"; resolveConfig/resolveResources/generateEconomy/generatePower/factionCorrelationPass all returned null (their facts never display). The resolveStress case is a factually false receipt shown to the user on every stressed generation.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Rewrite the summaries against the real ctx contract: resolveStress -> ctx.stressTypes.length (or Array-normalize ctx.stress), resolveConfig -> ctx.tier, resolveResources -> ctx.nearbyResources?.length, generateEconomy -> ctx.economicState?.prosperityLevel, generatePower -> ctx.powerStructure?.factions?.length, factionCorrelationPass -> ctx._rosterChangedAfterEconomy or trace counts. Add a test that runs the pipeline and asserts each STEP_METADATA summary returns non-null for a representative config.

verdicts.reasoning: Fully confirmed by source reading and an empirical pipeline run. The pipeline runner (pipeline.js:108-112) merges step patches into ctx and calls onStep with the post-step ctx; settlementSlice.js:567-573 stores summary(ctx) and PipelineRail.jsx:98-100 renders it to the user. resolveStress provides {stress, stressTypes} (resolveStress.js:16,153); ctx.stressors never exists at top level (the only 'stressors:' write is inside the settlement object at assembleSettlement.js:88, returned as {settlement} at line 178, and it is the raw stress array with no .active field), so stepMetadata.js:41 always evaluates to 0 and returns the literal string 'No active stressors'. Running the pipeline with forced famine+plague_onset reproduced exactly the claimed output: resolveStress -> "No active stressors" immediately followed by stressConfirmPass -> "2 stressors confirmed", with both stressors present on the final settlement — a factually false, self-contradicting receipt on the 'How this was simulated' trust surface for every stressed generation. The other five summaries (resolveConfig ctx.config?.tier — raw config has settType; resolveResources ctx.resources?.local — step provides nearbyResources; generateEconomy ctx.economy?.prosperityBand — step provides economicState and no generator writes prosperityBand; generatePower ctx.power?.factions / ctx.factions — powerStructure is the real key and factions is minted 5 steps later by generatePopulation; factionCorrelationPass ctx.tensions / ctx.history — no step provides tensions and history comes from the later generateNarratives) all returned null empirically, so those facts silently never display. No guard, no dead path, no contradicting test (the DEV test only checks metadata/registration name sync). Medium severity is right: user-visible wrong output on a secondary explanatory UI surface plus five dead summaries, but no data loss, determinism break, or security impact.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is directionally correct with two corrections: (1) generateEconomy's economicState field is `prosperity` (economicGenerator.js:2530), not `prosperityLevel` — `prosperityLevel` belongs to the neighbour-economy shape (neighbourGenerator.js:136); use ctx.economicState?.prosperity. (2) For resolveStress, counting ctx.stressTypes misses custom authored stressors ('dragon_tax' style), which resolveStress.js:148 deliberately filters out of stressTypes (catalog-only); Array-normalizing ctx.stress gives the true count. Also note factionCorrelationPass returning _rosterChangedAfterEconomy (factionCorrelationPass.js:141) means that key IS readable post-step, but economyReconcilePass's summary already reports that exact fact (stepMetadata.js:116) — pick a distinct fact (e.g. conflict/tension counts off ctx.powerStructure) to avoid duplicate receipts. The proposed test (assert each STEP_METADATA summary is exercised non-null for a representative config) is the right prevention.

verdicts.reasoning: Reproduced end-to-end against the real modules. The flow: store/settlementSlice.js:558-574 passes onStep into generateSettlementPipeline; pipeline.js:108-112 calls onStep(name, ctx) with the accumulated ctx after each step; the slice pushes metaForStep(name).summary(ctx) into pipelineHistory; PipelineRail.jsx:98-100 renders entry.summary when truthy. Running the actual pipeline (seed 'verify-claim-1', config forcing famine+plague_onset) produced: resolveStress summary = "No active stressors" while stressConfirmPass in the SAME run printed "2 stressors confirmed" and the final settlement.stressors contained both — a factually false receipt rendered to the user on every stressed generation. Root cause confirmed by grep: no pipeline step ever writes ctx.stressors / ctx.resources / ctx.economy / ctx.power / ctx.tensions (the only references in src/generators + src/store are stepMetadata.js itself; the aiLayer.js hits are a different local ctx). resolveStress provides stress/stressTypes (resolveStress.js:16). The five other summaries (resolveConfig at stepMetadata.js:27 reading ctx.config.tier — raw config has settType, resolved tier is ctx.tier; resolveResources:33 reading ctx.resources.local vs the real ctx.nearbyResources; generateEconomy:88 reading ctx.economy.prosperityBand vs ctx.economicState which carries `prosperity` not prosperityBand/prosperityLevel; generatePower:96 reading ctx.power.factions vs ctx.powerStructure.factions, with the ctx.factions fallback minted later by generatePopulation; factionCorrelationPass:109 reading ctx.tensions/ctx.history.currentTensions where history is only provided by the later generateNarratives step) all empirically returned null on a live run — those rail facts can never display. Severity: the settlement data itself is correct and deterministic; this is a broken seam between step `provides` keys and the metadata summaries, producing one actively false user-visible statement (resolveStress) and five dead summaries in the 'How this was simulated' rail — medium (real broken seam + user-visible false receipt, but no data/money/security impact and no wrong simulation output). Confidence 0.97.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested key corrections are right except one: generateEconomy's economicState has NO `prosperityLevel` key either — empirically the key is `prosperity` (e.g. 'Prosperous'); the summary should read ctx.economicState?.prosperity. Correct mappings verified against live ctx at each step's onStep point: resolveConfig → ctx.tier (or ctx.effectiveConfig?.tier); resolveResources → ctx.nearbyResources?.length; resolveStress → ctx.stressTypes?.length (note this is the pre-confirmation count, which is the honest fact for that step — stressConfirmPass re-provides stress/stressTypes later); generatePower → ctx.powerStructure?.factions?.length (6 in test run; the ctx.factions fallback can never fire there since generatePopulation runs later); factionCorrelationPass → no tension list exists on ctx at that point — the ctx.history?.currentTensions fallback can never fire (generateNarratives runs after); either derive a count from ctx.powerStructure?.factionRelationships or deliberately return null. Also note the final settlement's `stressors` is a plain array (dual-write of `stress`), so `stressors.active` matches no shape anywhere in the codebase. The file header claims metadata/step drift is 'loud in DEV through the test below' — whatever test exists only catches name-level drift, not summary-key drift; a worthwhile addition is a test that runs the pipeline once and asserts each non-trivial summary returns a non-null string at its own step (and that resolveStress's summary disagrees with reality on a stressed config).

confirmed.title: applySubsistenceMode's famine flag (_isolationFoodStress) is written onto the institutions array and never read anywhere — dead seam from the monolith migration
confirmed.evidence: In applySubsistenceMode (called from steps/isolationPass.js:63 for isolated thorps/hamlets), line 123-125: `if (chance(0.35) && !effectiveConfig.stressTypes?.includes('famine')) { institutions._isolationFoodStress = true; }`. The flag is set as an expando property on the institutions ARRAY (not on effectiveConfig or ctx), and a repo-wide grep (src/ + tests/) finds exactly one occurrence — the write. No consumer exists, so the clearly intended behavior (an isolated subsistence settlement picking up food/famine pressure ~35% of the time) silently does nothing; the seeded roll is consumed for no effect. An array expando also wouldn't survive JSON serialization even if a reader existed. This looks like the consumer was lost when the legacy monolithic generateSettlement was decomposed into pipeline steps (the legacy file itself has since been deleted).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Either wire the seam: set the flag on effectiveConfig (e.g. effectiveConfig._isolationFoodStress = true) and have stressConfirmPass (or resolveStress's overlay) inject/keep a famine-class stressor when present — or delete the roll entirely so it stops consuming an rng draw and implying behavior that doesn't exist.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed on every substantive point. The write at src/generators/isolationGenerator.js:124 sets `institutions._isolationFoodStress = true` as an expando on the institutions array, gated by a seeded chance(0.35) roll (rng.chance via the wrapper at steps/isolationPass.js:35, called at line 63) for isolated thorps/hamlets without an existing famine stressor. Repo-wide grep (src/, tests/, supabase/) finds exactly one code occurrence — the write; no reader exists. The path is live (applySubsistenceMode's only guard is tier in [thorp,hamlet] && tradeRoute==='isolated'), so the clearly intended behavior — isolated subsistence settlements picking up famine pressure ~35% of the time — silently does nothing, and the seeded draw is consumed for no effect (no determinism break, just a dead roll). Decisively, the project's OWN audit (docs/GENERATION_COHERENCE_AUDIT.md:165 lists _isolationFoodStress under 'ORPHAN (producer, no consumer)', and lines 333-334 describe this exact defect verbatim with the same fix proposal), yet COHESION_REMEDIATION_PLAN.md never scoped it and it remains unfixed at HEAD after all 8 remediation waves. One provenance correction: git archaeology (dd1bca2 minified bundle has exactly one occurrence — the write `_isolationFoodStress=!0`; the legacy generateSettlement.js at e608542 has no reader) shows the consumer never existed even in the original monolith, so this is a day-one unfinished seam rather than a consumer lost during the strangler-fig decomposition. That nuance doesn't change validity. Severity: medium fits the rubric's 'broken seam' category — intended simulation behavior never lands on a real (if narrow) config slice — though the project's own audit graded the bundled item [low], so it sits at the low/medium boundary.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested wiring point is ordering-correct: stressConfirmPass declares deps ['resolveStress', 'isolationPass'] (steps/stressConfirmPass.js:60-61) and runs before generateEconomy reads config.stressTypes, so a flag set on effectiveConfig in isolationPass (matching the existing _magicTradeOnly/_isolationInfraType pattern at isolationGenerator.js:90-91) would be visible there. However, stressConfirmPass's documented contract explicitly says it 'only confirms or drops, it NEVER rolls new stressors' — injecting a famine stressor there requires a deliberate carve-out of that contract (or doing the injection in isolationPass itself / teaching resolveStress about isolation+subsistence). Also note: either fix (wiring or deleting the roll) changes the seeded draw sequence for isolated thorp/hamlet configs, so snapshot/property tests covering those seeds will need regeneration. Finally, the project's own audit bundles this with a sibling defect in the same function (subsistence mode stripping the smith/alehouse, isolationGenerator.js:102-116) — worth fixing together since both were flagged in docs/GENERATION_COHERENCE_AUDIT.md:333-334.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed end-to-end with a read-only run against the real pipeline. (1) The write exists and fires on the main isolated-thorp/hamlet path: generateSettlementPipeline({settType:'hamlet', tradeRouteAccess:'isolated'}) over 60 seeds set institutions._isolationFoodStress on 25/60 final settlements (consistent with chance(0.35)), via steps/isolationPass.js:63 -> isolationGenerator.js:123-125. (2) No consumer exists: repo-wide grep finds exactly one src occurrence (the write at src/generators/isolationGenerator.js:124); the only other mention is docs/GENERATION_COHERENCE_AUDIT.md:165,333-334, which already documents it as a producer-no-consumer orphan — and famine appeared in 0/60 of those runs, proving the flag has zero downstream effect. (3) The expando-on-array detail is real: JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(institutions))._isolationFoodStress === undefined, so the flag also cannot cross any serialization boundary (persist/export/edge functions). The intended behavior — isolated subsistence settlements picking up food pressure ~35% of the time — silently never lands, and a seeded roll is consumed for nothing (deterministic, so no determinism break, but removal would shift the downstream RNG stream). One historical correction: git history (git log -S across all commits) shows a consumer NEVER existed — even the original minified bundle (dd1bca2 index.html) and the legacy full build (e608542) contain only the single write — so this is a born-dead seam, not one lost in the monolith-to-pipeline migration. Severity: real broken seam, but narrow — affects only isolated thorp/hamlet configs, the lost effect is an additive probabilistic pressure, food deficits for isolated settlements are still modeled independently (economicGenerator/foodGenerator), and the repo's own audit rates it [low]. Low severity, high confidence (~0.95) it is a real defect.
verdicts.fix_note: The audit's suggested fix ('wire _isolationFoodStress into resolveStress or delete it') targets the wrong step: resolveStress is step 3 and runs BEFORE isolationPass (step 8), so it cannot read the flag. The correct consumer is stressConfirmPass (src/generators/steps/stressConfirmPass.js:60-63), which deps on ['resolveStress','isolationPass'] and re-provides stress/stressTypes after institutions exist — have applySubsistenceMode write the flag onto effectiveConfig (or return it) instead of the institutions array (array expandos die on JSON serialization and are an anti-pattern here), then have stressConfirmPass translate it into a famine/food pressure entry. If deleting instead, note that removing the chance(0.35) draw shifts the downstream seeded RNG stream, so determinism/snapshot tests for isolated thorp/hamlet seeds will need regeneration; wiring a consumer (keeping the existing draw) avoids that churn. Also fold in the adjacent documented issue (same function, audit line 333): subsistence stripping deletes the hamlet's own smith/alehouse, which the audit flags as backwards — worth fixing in the same pass.

confirmed.title: corruptionPass is registered in the pipeline but missing from STEP_METADATA; the 'loud in DEV' sync test claimed in the file header does not exist
confirmed.evidence: steps/index.js:24 imports corruptionPass.js (registered step, runs every generation), but STEP_METADATA (lines 23-144) has no corruptionPass entry — metaForStep falls back to `{ label: name, ... }`, so the user-facing pipeline rail/reveal shows the raw machine name 'corruptionPass' with an empty description. The file's own header (lines 8-11) says 'Keep entries here in sync with the registrations in steps/index.js … Both are loud in DEV through the test below' — but no test imports STEP_METADATA/stepMetadata (repo grep: only src/components/PipelineRail.jsx and src/store/settlementSlice.js import it; tests/domain/trace.coverage.test.js:167-178 merely allowlists step names for traces, and its VALID_STEPS set also omits corruptionPass — it passes only because corruptionPass emits no traces).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a STEP_METADATA entry for corruptionPass (label e.g. 'Seed corruption', summary counting npcs with npc.corrupt === true), and add the promised sync test: import getStepMeta() after importing steps/index.js and assert every registered step name has a STEP_METADATA entry (and vice versa).

verdicts.reasoning: Verified all three claims. (1) corruptionPass is registered (steps/index.js:24, corruptionPass.js:35 registerStep('corruptionPass',...)) and pipeline.js:112 fires onStep unconditionally for every executed step, so settlementSlice.js:572 pushes a pipelineHistory entry with id 'corruptionPass' on every generation — even when the step's body no-ops. (2) STEP_METADATA (stepMetadata.js:23-144) has 18 entries vs 19 registered steps; corruptionPass is the missing one. metaForStep (line 152) falls back to { label: name, description: '' }, and PipelineRail.jsx:94 renders meta.label directly, so the user-facing 'How this was simulated' rail shows the raw machine name 'corruptionPass' with no description. (3) The header (stepMetadata.js:8-11) promises 'Both are loud in DEV through the test below', but no such test exists: repo grep shows only PipelineRail.jsx and settlementSlice.js import metaForStep; trace.coverage.test.js:166-178 never imports STEP_METADATA, its hardcoded VALID_STEPS allowlist also omits corruptionPass (it includes the non-step string 'stepMetadata'), and it passes only because corruptionPass.js emits no traces (verified — no trace calls). Attempted refutations all failed: no rail-side filter of unknown steps, no DEV console guard, no registerStep opt-out flag (options are deps/provides/phase only), no test under another import path. Real defect: cosmetic raw-identifier leak in a polished user-facing surface plus a documented-but-absent sync guard, exactly the unfinished-seam class. Severity low is honest — no wrong simulation output, no determinism/data/billing impact.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound. Two refinements: (a) the summary should account for the step's no-op path — corruptionPass only sets npc.corrupt when a criminal institution exists, so use something like counting ctx.npcs with corrupt === true and returning 'No corruption climate' (or null) otherwise; (b) the sync test needs a way to enumerate registered step names — check whether pipeline.js exports the registry (e.g. a getRegisteredSteps/listSteps helper); if not, one must be added or the test can import steps/index.js and read the registry via an existing export. Also worth fixing in the same pass: tests/domain/trace.coverage.test.js:167-174 — its VALID_STEPS allowlist omits 'corruptionPass' (a latent misleading failure if the step ever emits traces) and contains the bogus entry 'stepMetadata', which is not a registered step; deriving that set from the registry instead of hardcoding would close both.

verdicts.reasoning: Reproduced end-to-end against the real modules (node one-liner importing steps/index.js + pipeline.js + stepMetadata.js): getStepOrder() returns 19 steps including corruptionPass; STEP_METADATA has 18 entries; the only registered-but-missing step is corruptionPass; metaForStep('corruptionPass') returns the fallback { label: 'corruptionPass', description: '' }. The concrete failure path: corruptionPass runs on every generation (pipeline.js:69 orders all registered steps; pipeline.js:112 fires onStep), settlementSlice.js:567-573 pushes { id: 'corruptionPass', summary: null } into pipelineHistory, and PipelineRail.jsx:289/:50/:94 renders meta.label — so the user-facing "How this was simulated" rail shows the raw camelCase machine name 'corruptionPass' with no summary and a dead expand chevron (description '' fails the gate at PipelineRail.jsx:103). The second claim is also verified: the file header (stepMetadata.js:8-11) promises a DEV-loud sync test ("the test below") that does not exist — no test in the file, and repo grep shows zero test imports STEP_METADATA; trace.coverage.test.js:167-174 is a trace allowlist (which itself omits corruptionPass and passes only because corruptionPass emits no traces). git history confirms the drift: stepMetadata.js last touched in Wave 4b, corruptionPass registered later. Severity is medium, not higher: simulation output and determinism are unaffected, nothing crashes (metaForStep is deliberately crash-proof), but it is a real user-visible defect on the main generation path plus a broken seam — the documented sync guard is fictional, leaving the bug class unguarded.
verdicts.fix_note: The fix should have three parts, not one. (1) Add a corruptionPass entry to STEP_METADATA between generatePopulation and generateNarratives (matching execution order), e.g. label 'Seed corruption', description about generation-time corruption onset in criminal-institution settlements, and a summary like (ctx) => { const n = (ctx.npcs || []).filter(x => x.corrupt === true).length; return n ? `${n} NPC${n === 1 ? '' : 's'} corrupted` : null; } — note npc.corrupt is only set when a criminal institution exists (corruptionPass.js:46), so null/silent is the right no-climate fallback. (2) Make the header's promise true: add the missing sync test asserting set-equality between getStepOrder() (after importing steps/index.js) and Object.keys(STEP_METADATA) — without it the same drift recurs on the next new step; alternatively delete the false "loud in DEV through the test below" comment, but the test is the better fix since metaForStep's crash-proof fallback makes this failure silent by design. (3) Add 'corruptionPass' to VALID_STEPS in tests/domain/trace.coverage.test.js:167-174, ideally by deriving that set from getStepOrder() instead of a hand-copied list (it has the same drift problem, and currently passes only by accident); while there, audit the stray 'stepMetadata' string in that set — it is not a registered step name.

confirmed.title: monster_pressure gets walls/military suppression applied twice in buildStressContext, skewing stressConfirmPass keep-probability
confirmed.evidence: buildStressContext has two blocks that both match monster_pressure: lines 95-102 (`if (stressType === 'under_siege' || stressType === 'monster_pressure')`) apply hasWalls x0.6 and hasMilitary x0.7, then lines 165-170 — a monster_pressure-only block whose comment scopes it to 'timber boosts — forests hide monsters' — re-apply the identical hasWalls x0.6 and hasMilitary x0.7 on top of the timber x1.3. Net suppression is x0.36 (walls) / x0.49 (military) instead of the single x0.6/x0.7 every other stress type gets. At initial roll time (resolveStress) institutions=[] so it's inert, but stressConfirmPass (steps/stressConfirmPass.js:94-99) computes keepProbability = clamp(modified/unmodified, 0.4, 1) with the real roster: a walls-only town's monster_pressure keep-probability becomes the 0.4 clamp floor instead of 0.6 (60% drop chance vs the intended 40%). The duplicated lines read as a copy-paste remnant since the first block already explicitly covers monster_pressure.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Remove the hasWalls/hasMilitary lines from the second monster_pressure block (keep only the hasTimber x1.3), or remove monster_pressure from the first shared block — pick one location for the fortification suppression.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified by direct reading. src/generators/stressGenerator.js:95-102 applies hasWalls ×0.6 / hasMilitary ×0.7 for under_siege AND monster_pressure; lines 165-170 re-apply the identical multipliers for monster_pressure only, under a comment scoped to 'timber boosts'. Net ×0.36/×0.49 suppression instead of the single ×0.6/×0.7 every other fortified-suppressed type gets. At initial roll (steps/resolveStress.js:22) institutions=[] so both blocks are inert, but steps/stressConfirmPass.js:94-99 re-evaluates with the real roster: a walls-only town's monster_pressure keepProbability becomes clamp(0.36, 0.4, 1)=0.4 instead of the intended 0.6; military-only becomes 0.49 instead of 0.7. Intent evidence is unambiguous: (a) the second block's own comment scopes it to timber, contradicting the walls/military lines; (b) tests/joins/ordering.test.js:206 documents the intended siege-family keep p as a single clamp(0.6×0.7)=0.42 and groups monster_pressure into the same SIEGEY family; (c) stressConfirmPass.js SUPPRESSOR_KEYWORDS (lines 41-44) gives under_siege/monster_pressure/occupied identical suppressor sets; (d) the confirm pass docstring frames the 0.4 clamp as damping ('institutions reduce odds, they don't grant immunity'), which squaring defeats; (e) git log -L shows the block untouched since the initial commit — a copy-paste remnant, never deliberately tuned. No test pins the squared behavior (the existing test uses walls+garrison where the clamp floor masks 0.40 vs 0.42). The 0.35 cap at line 172 never distorts the ratio for monster_pressure (base prob 0.03, max ~0.11). Only two callers of buildStressContext exist; both are accounted for. Effect is narrow (emergent monster_pressure on walled/military settlements, ~0.2 keep-probability skew), so low severity is honest.
verdicts.fix_note: Prefer the first option: delete the hasWalls/hasMilitary lines from the second block (lines 168-169), keeping hasTimber ×1.3. The shared block's grouping with under_siege matches the documented model (ordering.test.js:206 comment, SUPPRESSOR_KEYWORDS parity in stressConfirmPass.js). The fix changes no rng draw counts (buildStressContext consumes no rng; keepProbability stays <1 for affected rosters so the confirm-pass draw still occurs), and the existing ordering.test.js thresholds tolerate the 0.40→0.60/0.42 shift, but any settlement snapshot tests covering walled towns with emergent monster_pressure may need regeneration since drop outcomes can flip on some seeds.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed end-to-end by reading both files and running the real modules. (1) src/generators/stressGenerator.js:95-102 applies hasWalls x0.6 and hasMilitary x0.7 to BOTH under_siege and monster_pressure; lines 165-170 then re-apply the identical x0.6/x0.7 to monster_pressure only, inside a block whose comment scopes it to 'timber boosts — forests hide monsters'. Clear copy-paste remnant: the walls/military suppression is already explicitly handled in the first block. (2) Inert at initial roll: steps/resolveStress.js:22 calls generateStress with institutions: [], so hasWalls/hasMilitary are false there. (3) Live at confirm: steps/stressConfirmPass.js:94-99 is the only call site with a real roster; keepProbability = clamp(modified/unmodified, 0.4, 1). Node one-liner against the actual modules (frontier threat, town tier): under_siege walls-only ratio=0.600 keepP=0.600, walls+military keepP=0.420; monster_pressure walls-only ratio=0.360 (=0.6^2) keepP clamped to 0.400 instead of 0.600, walls+military ratio=0.176 keepP=0.400 instead of 0.420; military-only would be 0.49 vs intended 0.70. Concrete consequence: any settlement whose institution roster includes a wall/citadel/palisade (or garrison/militia/watch) and whose emergent stress roll fired monster_pressure has a 60% drop chance in stressConfirmPass instead of the designed 40% (walls-only case) — the stressor and everything downstream of it (economy, power, NPCs, narratives, defense all consume the confirmed stress set per the step's own header) is systematically under-represented in exactly the settlements the timber boost was meant to keep monster-threatened. The timber x1.3 itself cancels in the modified/unmodified ratio (it is config-derived, present in both), so only the duplicated institution factors distort it. Not a determinism break (same seed -> same result; draw count is unchanged since ratio<1 in both buggy and fixed forms whenever walls/military exist) and bounded by the 0.4 clamp floor, so the worst distortion is the walls-only 0.6->0.4 and military-only 0.7->0.49 shifts: a real, demonstrated, but probabilistically bounded skew in one stress type's survival weighting. Severity medium.
verdicts.fix_note: The implied fix is correct: delete only the two duplicated lines (stressGenerator.js:168-169, `if (hasWalls) prob *= 0.6;` and `if (hasMilitary) prob *= 0.7;`) from the monster_pressure-only block, keeping the hasTimber x1.3 at line 167 — the first block (lines 95-102) already applies the intended single walls/military suppression to monster_pressure. Two caveats: (a) do NOT instead remove monster_pressure from the first block's condition, since that block also carries the threat-level and low-military multipliers monster_pressure needs at initial roll time; (b) the fix changes the keep threshold (0.4 -> 0.6 walls-only) without changing rng draw counts, so seeded snapshot/golden tests that include a walled settlement with an emergent monster_pressure stressor can flip keep/drop outcomes where the draw lands between the old and new thresholds — expect possible snapshot regeneration, not a determinism regression.

confirmed.title: resolveConfig's noMagic treats an absent priorityMagic as 0 while the effective magic priority defaults to 50 — contradictory magic semantics for sparse configs
confirmed.evidence: Line 67: `priorityMagicEffective = config.magicExists === false ? 0 : (basePriorityMagic ?? 50)` — undefined defaults to 50 (magicLevel 'medium'). Line 86: `noMagic = config.magicExists === false || (basePriorityMagic || 0) === 0` — undefined collapses to 0, so noMagic = TRUE. A town+ config that simply omits priorityMagic (headless callers, older saved _config blobs) is simultaneously treated as a medium-magic world (magicLevel, all downstream magic gates) AND a no-magic world for trade-route resolution: 'isolated' is filtered out of random route pools (line 105/109) and an explicit 'isolated' choice is force-rewritten to 'road' (line 119) with a trace claiming 'no-magic forces road access' — even though isolationPass would happily seat a teleportation circle in this medium-magic world. servicesGenerator.js:1964 uses the opposite (correct) convention: `(d.priorityMagic || 50) === 0`. The app's DEFAULT_CONFIG always sets priorityMagic:50, so the main wizard path is unaffected — this bites headless/legacy-config generation only.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change line 86 to `(basePriorityMagic ?? 50) === 0` so an unspecified magic priority matches the effective default, mirroring servicesGenerator's convention.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified by direct code reading and an empirical headless pipeline run. resolveConfig.js:67 defaults an absent priorityMagic to 50 (priorityMagicEffective → magicLevel 'medium', effectiveConfig.priorityMagic=50), while line 86 collapses the same absent value to 0, setting noMagic=true. Reproduced: generateSettlementPipeline({settType:'town', tradeRouteAccess:'isolated'}) returns magicLevel 'medium' / priorityMagic 50 but rewrites the explicit 'isolated' route to 'road' and emits a trace claiming 'no-magic forces road access' — internally contradictory output and a false causal receipt, in a product whose core promise is internal coherence. The path is reachable: generateSettlementPipeline accepts sparse configs by design (config = {}, documented headless support), and applyChange (settlementSlice.js:847) replays raw _config blobs without layering DEFAULT_CONFIG, with comments explicitly supporting legacy pre-_config saves. No test or comment defends the line-86 convention; the rest of the codebase (magicFilter.js:43, priorityHelpers.js:100, servicesGenerator.js:1964, and line 67 itself) uses ?? 50 for the no-magic decision. Severity low is honest: the main wizard path always supplies priorityMagic:50 (configSlice.js:19), and the blast radius is limited to trade-route resolution plus one false trace for headless/legacy configs.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix `(basePriorityMagic ?? 50) === 0` is correct and consistent with the codebase convention. A slightly tighter equivalent: `const noMagic = priorityMagicEffective === 0;` — line 67 already encodes both conditions (magicExists===false → 0, else basePriorityMagic ?? 50), so deriving noMagic from it makes the two values impossible to drift apart again. Either form is behavior-identical for all explicit-config cases and passes the existing resolveConfigReceipts tests, which only use explicit magicExists/priorityMagic values.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED by code reading and a live reproduction against the real pipeline. The contradiction is exactly as claimed: src/generators/steps/resolveConfig.js:66-67 resolves an absent priorityMagic to 50 (`basePriorityMagic ?? 50` -> priorityMagicEffective=50 -> magicLevel 'medium' via getMagicLevel, src/data/constants.js:34-38), while line 86 collapses the same absent value to 0 (`(basePriorityMagic || 0) === 0` -> noMagic=true). noMagic then drives trade-route resolution only (lines 105/109/119): 'isolated' is filtered from random pools for town+ tiers and an EXPLICIT tradeRouteAccess:'isolated' is force-rewritten to 'road'.

Reproduction (node, read-only, seed 12345): `generateSettlementPipeline({ settType:'town', tradeRouteAccess:'isolated', culture:'germanic', monsterThreat:'frontier', magicExists:true })` yields config.tradeRouteAccess='road', config.magicLevel='medium', config.priorityMagic=50, no teleport infrastructure, and a recorded simulationTrace entry: targetId 'tradeRoute.road', result 'overridden', reason "Explicit 'isolated' chosen, but town-plus + no-magic forces road access." — a provably false receipt in a settlement whose own resolved config says magicLevel 'medium'. The identical config plus explicit priorityMagic:50, same seed, honors 'isolated' and seats a teleportation circle (isolationGenerator.js:17ff gates on `effectiveConfig.priorityMagic || 50`, which is 50 here, so the rewrite's premise is contradicted by the engine's own isolation machinery). Note the trap is even wider than claimed: an explicit `magicExists: true` does NOT protect you — only an explicit priorityMagic number does.

Every other magic-absence gate in the codebase uses the opposite (absent->50, magic ON) convention: servicesGenerator.js:1964 `(d.priorityMagic || 50) === 0`, domain/magicFilter.js:43 `config.priorityMagic ?? 50`, isolationGenerator.js `effectiveConfig.priorityMagic || 50`. resolveConfig.js:86 is the lone outlier.

Exposure assessment (why medium, not high): generateSettlementPipeline.js:41 takes `config = {}` raw with no DEFAULT_CONFIG merge, so any sparse caller hits this. However, DEFAULT_CONFIG (src/store/configSlice.js:19) has included priorityMagic:50 since the repo's initial commit (e608542), so the wizard path, persisted configs, and settlement._config blobs produced by this app all carry priorityMagic — the applyChange/_config regeneration path (settlementSlice.js:847) is safe for app-generated saves. The bite surface is headless/programmatic callers, hand-written or externally imported config blobs, and sparse-config tests, where it silently produces a self-contradictory settlement (medium-magic world with no-magic trade semantics), drops an explicitly requested isolated route plus its teleportation-circle story, and records a false causal trace consumed by PipelineRail and AI-grounding. Real wrong-simulation-output bug on a real but secondary input path: medium.
verdicts.fix_note: The cleanest fix is not to patch line 86's expression in place but to derive noMagic from the already-resolved value: `const noMagic = priorityMagicEffective === 0;` (line 86, moved after line 67). priorityMagicEffective already folds in both the magicExists===false short-circuit and the `?? 50` absent-default, so this single expression aligns resolveConfig with the convention used by servicesGenerator.js:1964, magicFilter.js:43, and isolationGenerator.js, and removes the duplicated magicExists check. Equivalent alternative: `(basePriorityMagic ?? 50) === 0`. Two caveats: (1) behavior changes ONLY for configs with absent/null priorityMagic — explicit 0 still yields noMagic=true, explicit numbers are unchanged, and the _randomizePriorities path rolls 5-95 so can never produce 0 — but any snapshot/property tests that use sparse configs may shift trade routes under the same seed; that shift is the point of the fix, not a regression. (2) Consider also defending the seam itself: generateSettlementPipeline's `config = {}` signature invites sparse configs, so either documenting that resolveConfig owns all defaulting (as this fix completes) or asserting/normalizing in the entry point would prevent the next single-field divergence of this kind.

confirmed.title: Section regen (regenNPCsPipeline / regenHistoryPipeline) runs generators with no seeded PRNG — silent Math.random fallback breaks seed-replayability of the stored settlement
confirmed.evidence: regenNPCsPipeline (lines 78-102) and regenHistoryPipeline (lines 107-112) call generateNPCs/generateRelationships/generateFactions/generateConflicts/generateHistory directly, without createPRNG, without setActiveRng, and without minting/recording any seed. Those generators draw randomness through rngContext helpers, which silently fall back to Math.random() when no rng is active (src/generators/rngContext.js:35-37). They are invoked from the store's regenSection action (src/store/settlementSlice.js:690-693) and the results are Object.assign'd onto the live settlement, which still carries its original _seed. prng.js:76-79 documents generateSeed() as 'the ONE place non-determinism enters' — this path is a second, undocumented one. Consequence: after a section regen, the persisted settlement's _seed no longer reproduces its own NPCs/history (snapshot/property tests and the 'same seed => same settlement' contract no longer hold for that object), and there is no recorded seed that does. Even if 'reroll = intentionally different' is the product intent, the roll should be seeded and recorded so the result is replayable.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Mint a seed (or derive one, e.g. `${settlement._seed}::regen-npcs::${counter}`), create a PRNG from it, wrap the generator calls in setActiveRng/clearActiveRng (try/finally), and record the regen seed on the settlement so the section regen is reproducible.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified every link in the claimed chain by reading the code. (1) regenNPCsPipeline (generateSettlementPipeline.js:78-102) and regenHistoryPipeline (107-112) call the NPC/power/history generators directly with no createPRNG, no setActiveRng, no seed minted or recorded. (2) Those generators draw randomness via rngContext helpers (npcGenerator.js:8, powerGenerator.js:6, historyGenerator.js:14), and rngContext.js:35-37 falls back to Math.random() when no rng is active. (3) setActiveRng/clearActiveRng are used ONLY inside runPipeline's per-step loop with clearActiveRng in a finally (pipeline.js:106-115), so the regen path is guaranteed to hit the Math.random fallback — not an edge case. (4) The caller regenSection (settlementSlice.js:689-695) Object.assigns the parts onto the live settlement, which retains its original _seed; nothing records the regen randomness, and no test covers these functions. (5) The path is reachable from the main dossier UI (OutputContainer.jsx:143). (6) The violated invariant is the codebase's own: prng.js:76-77 documents generateSeed() as "the ONE place non-determinism enters," and regenerationMode.js:34-35 treats _seed as a hard anchor that survives even a Reforge — the system assumes _seed stays meaningful for the object carrying it. Refutation attempts failed: there is no comment designating regen as intentionally unseeded; no leftover pipeline rng can be active; and a real downstream consumer exists — applyChange (settlementSlice.js:819-891) re-runs the whole pipeline from lastSeed with the documented intent that "only the genuinely impacted parts move," so after a section regen, any applied what-if change silently reverts the rerolled NPCs/history to the original seed's output. The finding is real; "low" severity is honest (regen output is meant to differ, no data loss — the harm is the broken seed-replayability contract, an undocumented second non-determinism entry point, and a contribution to the silent regen-revert seam).
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is directionally right but needs three adjustments. (1) Prefer minting via generateSeed() and recording it (e.g. settlement._regenSeeds = [...(settlement._regenSeeds||[]), { section, seed }]) over deriving `${_seed}::regen-npcs::${counter}` — a derived counter must itself be persisted on the settlement or reloaded/saved settlements will reuse counter values and produce identical "rerolls". (2) The setActiveRng/clearActiveRng try/finally must wrap the ENTIRE body of both regen functions (generateRelationships, generateFactions, generateConflicts, and generateHistory all draw randomness, not just generateNPCs). (3) Recording the regen seed alone does not restore the "same _seed reproduces this object" contract — true replay would require re-applying recorded regen seeds on top of the base pipeline; and it does not fix the related seam that applyChange (settlementSlice.js:864-891) re-runs the full pipeline from lastSeed and silently discards any prior section regen regardless. That interaction deserves its own decision (either replay recorded regens after applyChange, or surface that applying a change discards section rerolls).

verdicts.reasoning: Reproduced end-to-end against the real modules. (1) Reachable: dossier header "Reroll NPCs"/"Reroll History" buttons (OutputContainer.jsx:110,603-606; NPCsTab.jsx:68) call store.regenSection (settlementSlice.js:665), which calls regenNPCsPipeline/regenHistoryPipeline (generateSettlementPipeline.js:78,107). (2) Unseeded: those functions call generateNPCs/generateRelationships/generateFactions/generateConflicts/generateHistory directly with no createPRNG/setActiveRng/seed; the generators draw all randomness via rngContext (npcGenerator.js:8, powerGenerator.js:6, historyGenerator.js:14) whose helpers fall back to Math.random() when no rng is active (rngContext.js:35-37); only pipeline.js:106 ever sets an active rng and clears it in finally. (3) Demonstrated with a node one-liner: getActiveRng() is null during regen; two consecutive regen calls on the same settlement yield different NPC rosters and histories; replaying the settlement's _seed reproduces the ORIGINAL NPCs, not the regenerated ones; no seed reproducing the regenerated content exists. (4) Consequences: regenSection Object.assigns the unseeded result onto the live settlement which keeps its original _seed (settlementSlice.js:691,694); seed is persisted next to the data in saves (lib/saves.js:62,131) and surfaced in ProvenanceBlock.jsx:8,37 explicitly "for replay / sharing" — now false. Sharpest user-visible symptom: applyChange (settlementSlice.js:864-873) re-runs the WHOLE pipeline under lastSeed for any applied what-if change, so the next applied change silently reverts rerolled NPCs/history to the original-seed versions (reconcileSettlementChange preserves conditions, not NPCs). Violates the documented contract (prng.js:76-79: generateSeed is "the ONE place non-determinism enters") and the project's explicit rule that unseeded global randomness inside engine code is a bug. Severity high per the rubric: a determinism break plus wrong/silently-reverted simulation output on a main interaction path; not critical since saved data itself stores full JSON and no money/security/crash is involved.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested direction (mint + record a seed for each regen) is right, with corrections: (a) Passing an rng argument to the generators is NOT sufficient — generateNPCs/generateHistory etc. draw via the global rngContext, so the fix must wrap the generator calls in setActiveRng(createPRNG(regenSeed)) ... finally clearActiveRng(), mirroring pipeline.js:106-114. (b) Prefer deriving the regen seed deterministically rather than via generateSeed(): e.g. createPRNG(`${settlement._seed}::regen:npcs:${n}`) where n is a stored per-section regen counter — then the entire settlement (base + rerolls) is replayable from _seed plus small recorded counters, and rerolling still produces new content each click. (c) Record the regen seed/counter on the settlement (and thus in saves) — recording alone documents provenance but does not restore replayability unless replay logic re-applies section regens; the counter-derivation approach makes that cheap. (d) The fix is incomplete without addressing the adjacent seam it exposes: applyChange (settlementSlice.js:864) re-runs the full pipeline under lastSeed and will silently discard regenerated sections regardless of seeding; with recorded regen seeds, applyChange can re-apply section regens after the pipeline rerun (or at minimum the revert should be surfaced to the user). (e) Apply the same treatment to regenHistoryPipeline, and note regenNPCsPipeline also overwrites settlement.factions/conflicts via Object.assign — those must be covered by the same seeded scope.

confirmed.title: Case-broken processor check makes import-dependency warnings/hooks unreachable (and terrain mustImport is never threaded into config)
confirmed.evidence: generateStabilityScore lowercases the institution name (`const n = (i.name || '').toLowerCase()`, line 309) then tests `n.includes('Mill')`, `n.includes('Sawmill')`, `n.includes('Smith')`, `n.includes('Smelter')` (lines 311-313) — capitalized substrings can never appear in a lowercased string, so `hasProcessor` is always false and the 'Imports <resource>' dependency warning plus the timber/metal plot hooks (lines 316-337) never fire. Additionally, the whole block is gated on `config?.mustImport` (line 306), and grep shows `mustImport` is only ever defined on TERRAIN_DATA entries in src/data/geographyData.js (lines 109/190/284/388/494/588/685); no materialized code copies terrain.mustImport into config (resolveConfig.js builds effectiveConfig without it), so the terrain must-import data never reaches the viability layer at all.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Use lowercase patterns ('mill', 'sawmill', 'smith', 'smelter') against the lowercased name, and thread the resolved terrain's mustImport list into effectiveConfig (e.g. in resolveConfig or generateEconomy) so the branch is reachable.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified both claims by direct reading. (1) src/generators/economicGenerator.js:308-314 lowercases the institution name then tests capitalized substrings ('Mill', 'Sawmill', 'Smith', 'Smelter') — mathematically always false, so hasProcessor is always false and lines 316-337 (the 'Imports <resource>' dependency warning plus timber/metal plot hooks) are unreachable. (2) Grep over all of src confirms mustImport exists only on TERRAIN_DATA entries (src/data/geographyData.js:109/190/284/388/494/588/685) and is consumed only as terrain.mustImport in src/generators/resourceGenerator.js:56; the cfg passed to generateStabilityScore (economicGenerator.js:2589-2591, 2615) derives from settlement.config = {...effectiveConfig} (steps/assembleSettlement.js:95), and steps/resolveConfig.js builds effectiveConfig solely from user-config fields with no mustImport. So the guard at line 306 is never truthy through any real path — the block is doubly dead. No test covers this branch (no hits for mustImport/Resource Import/hasProcessor in tests), and the comment at line 305 indicates it is intended to fire, so this is not intentional dead code. Impact: the viability report silently omits a class of import-dependency warnings and two plot-hook categories — missing simulation output, no crash/security/money impact, so medium is correct.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct but two additions matter: (1) both defects must be fixed together — fixing only the lowercase patterns still leaves the block unreachable because config.mustImport is never set, and vice versa. (2) Rather than re-deriving terrain.mustImport in resolveConfig, consider reusing resourceGenerator.js buildViabilityReport (line 49), which already computes the must-import list as report.critical (terrain.mustImport plus institution/resource mismatches); threading that existing result into generateEconomicViability avoids two divergent definitions of "must import". Also preserve the semantics: the warning is meant to fire when the settlement HAS the processing institution (mill/sawmill/smith) for a resource it must import — the lowercase-pattern fix keeps that intent.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed both legs by reading the code and reproducing against the real module. (1) Case-broken check: src/generators/economicGenerator.js:309 lowercases the institution name, then lines 311-313 test n.includes('Mill'), n.includes('Sawmill'), n.includes('Smith'), n.includes('Smelter') with capitalized literals — hasProcessor is provably always false. Live repro: calling generateEconomicViability with a forced mustImport=['grain','timber','metals'] and institutions 'Grain Mill'/'Sawmill'/'Blacksmith'/'Smelter' produced 0 'Imports X' warnings and 0 timber/metal plot hooks. (2) Dead gate: the block is guarded by config?.mustImport (line 306); the only production caller is steps/generateNarratives.js:38 which passes {...effectiveConfig}, and resolveConfig.js:144-160 never threads terrain.mustImport into effectiveConfig; repo-wide grep confirms no code or test ever sets mustImport on a config — only TERRAIN_DATA entries in data/geographyData.js carry it. So lines 306-339 (Imports-resource dependency warnings + 'Resource Conflict' and 'Strategic Resource' plot hooks) are unreachable for every possible input: a designed slice of the viability simulation never fires in any dossier or PDF. One correction to the claim's framing: terrain.mustImport is NOT fully orphaned product-wide — resourceGenerator.js:56 (buildViabilityReport, consumed at line 223 by generateResourceAnalysis) reads terrain.mustImport directly and surfaces critical imports via resourceAnalysis.imports. So the import facts still reach the dossier through a sibling channel; what is dead is specifically the economic-viability warning/plot-hook layer. Severity: medium — real broken seam / never-firing designed feature (missing simulation content, two dormant plot-hook categories), but no incorrect output, no crash, no determinism/security/billing impact, and partially mitigated by the parallel resourceAnalysis path.
verdicts.fix_note: Three corrections/refinements to the implied fix. (a) Cheapest correct fix for the gate: generateStabilityScore already receives the terrain object as a parameter (line 264), so use the terrain data directly — e.g. const mustImport = config?.mustImport || terrain?.mustImport || [] — rather than threading mustImport through resolveConfig.js's effectiveConfig; effectiveConfig is snapshotted into the assembled settlement (steps/assembleSettlement.js), so widening it changes persisted shape unnecessarily. (b) When lowercasing the literals, beware that n.includes('mill') also matches 'sawmill', so a settlement with only a Sawmill would falsely register a grain processor and emit a spurious 'Imports grain' warning; disambiguate (e.g. match 'mill' but exclude 'sawmill', or word-boundary match). (c) Note that TERRAIN_DATA mustImport entries are descriptive strings ('timber (for large vessels)', 'Bulk grain and flour', 'metals') — the existing resource.toLowerCase().includes('grain'|'timber'|'metal') matching handles these, but entries like 'stone', 'salt', 'luxury goods', textiles will (by design) never match any processor branch. Finally, enabling this block adds new deterministic warnings/hooks to generated settlements, so snapshot tests over viability output will need regeneration.

confirmed.title: External-mill export lockout and banalité note applied to EVERY chain, not just the grain chain
confirmed.evidence: Inside the per-chain loop (`need.chains.forEach(chain => ...)`, line 160), `externalMillOnly` (line 236) is a settlement-level condition (has 'Access to external mill', no local mill). `const effectiveExportable = externalMillOnly ? false : chain.exportable;` (line 237) and `externalMillNote` (lines 238-242) are then stamped onto EVERY active chain (lines 258, 261), not just the grain/milling chain the comment describes ('the grain chain functions ... but surplus cannot be exported'). Consequence: a road-connected hamlet/village with 'Access to external mill' (a common low-tier catalog institution — it appears in economicGenerator's required-eco list, line 2510) has ALL processed chain outputs suppressed in deriveExportsFromChains (filter `ch.exportable`, line 585) — leather, fishing, hunting, pottery chains all become non-exportable — and every one of those chains carries the grain-mill feudal-monopoly note ('Grain is processed at the lord's mill...') in the dossier.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Scope the override to grain/milling chains only, e.g. `const isGrainChain = chain.id === 'grain' || /grain|flour|mill/i.test(chain.label); const effectiveExportable = (externalMillOnly && isGrainChain) ? false : chain.exportable;` and attach externalMillNote only to that chain.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified by reading the code and by executing the actual module. In src/generators/computeActiveChains.js, the externalMillOnly check (lines 232-236) is computed from settlement-level instNames inside the per-chain loop (line 160), so effectiveExportable=false (line 237) and the grain-specific banalité note (lines 238-242) are stamped onto every active chain (lines 258, 261), not just the grain chain the comment at lines 226-231 describes. Reachability is broad: 'Access to external mill' is required with baseChance 1 at thorp (institutionalCatalog.js:104) and hamlet (line 403); the thorp catalog has no local-mill institution at all and the hamlet's only one (Maltster) has baseChance 0.15, so externalMillOnly holds for essentially all thorps and ~85% of hamlets. A node run against the real module confirms: a road-connected hamlet with hunting/fishing institutions yields fishing, grain, and river_fishing chains all with exportable=false and all carrying the 'Grain is processed at the lord's mill' note. Downstream, deriveExportsFromChains (line 585) filters on ch.exportable, suppressing ALL processed chain outputs from exports for connected low-tier settlements; the PDF (src/pdf/sections/Services.jsx:223) prints the grain-mill feudal-monopoly note under unrelated chains, and the export badge disappears in src/components/new/SupplyChainsPanel.jsx:95 and src/pdf/sections/SupplyChainFlow.jsx:70. No test pins this broad behavior (no test references externalMillNote), and the in-code comment proves the intent was grain-only. This is wrong simulation output on a common configuration path: high severity is appropriate.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix direction is right but the scoping predicate can be made more robust than a label regex: scope by whether the chain itself is mill-processed, e.g. const isMillChain = chain.processingInstitutions.some(p => /\bmill/i.test(p)) — which captures the grain chain (supplyChainData.js:24 lists 'Maltster', 'Mills (2-5)', 'Mill', 'Access to external mill') without risking false label matches. If the food_processing chain (downstream of grain per CHAIN_DEPS) should also be covered, include it explicitly by chainId rather than regex. Note also that the chain in question has chainId 'grain' under needKey 'food_security' (full id 'food_security.grain'), and that CHAIN_OUTPUT_INST_GATES already independently gates 'Milled flour' on a true local mill via _hasInstForGate, so the grain chain's flour export stays correctly suppressed either way — the fix mainly restores exportability and removes the bogus note on non-grain chains.

verdicts.reasoning: Reproduced end-to-end with a node one-liner against the real modules. In src/generators/computeActiveChains.js, the settlement-level externalMillOnly check (lines 232-236) sits inside the per-chain loop (need.chains.forEach, line 160), and effectiveExportable (line 237) plus externalMillNote (lines 238-242) are stamped onto EVERY active chain (lines 258, 261) with no chain-identity guard. 'Access to external mill' is required:true/baseChance:1 at thorp (institutionalCatalog.js:104) and hamlet (line 403), so every generated thorp/hamlet hits this path via economicGenerator.js:2260. Demo: a road-connected hamlet with hunting/fishing/charcoal institutions produced 5 active chains (fishing, grain, river_fishing, hunting, furs_north) ALL with exportable=false and ALL carrying the grain banalité note; swapping in a local 'Mill' restored exportable=true everywhere and recovered the lost exports ('Salted fish', 'Smoked seafood', 'Baked goods', 'Milled flour'). Consequences: deriveExportsFromChains (filter ch.exportable, line 585) suppresses all processed chain outputs (raw resource tradeGoods survive, so exports shrink rather than vanish); the PDF dossier (src/pdf/sections/Services.jsx:219, 223) loses the 'EXPORT: yes' badge on every chain and prints the feudal grain-mill paragraph on hunting/fishing cards where it is nonsense (on-screen EconomicsTab renders only entrepotNote, so the bogus note is PDF-only — incidental parity drift); worldPulse institutionLifecycle.js:167 inherits the same wrong flags. Two evidence corrections that do not change the verdict: the institution's guaranteed presence comes from the institutional catalog required list, not 'economicGenerator line 2510' (that string does not appear there), and villages get a real Mill so 'village' is overstated — the affected tiers are thorp and hamlet (plus legacy/DM-edited settlements lacking a local mill). Severity high: wrong simulation output (exports) and wrong dossier prose on the default path for the two lowest tiers, but no crash, data loss, money, or security impact.
verdicts.fix_note: Scope both effectiveExportable and externalMillNote to mill-processed grain chains instead of all chains. Cleanest guard: const isMillChain = chain.processingInstitutions.includes('Access to external mill') (true only for food_security.grain, supplyChainData.js:24), then effectiveExportable = (externalMillOnly && isMillChain) ? false : chain.exportable, and the note likewise. Two cautions: (1) raw_extraction.river_milling (supplyChainData.js:390) also fuzzy-matches 'Access to external mill' via its 'Mill' pattern and can activate solely through it — decide whether banalité should export-lock it too (defensible either way; if yes, gate on the chain's only matched processor being the external mill rather than on the literal pattern list). (2) Do NOT touch the chain-activation match itself: tests/joins/institutionIdentity.test.js:284 and tests/generators/economicViabilitySuggestions.test.js:83 deliberately pin 'Access to external mill' as a live grain processor; only the export-lockout/note spread is the bug. No existing test pins the buggy all-chains spread, so the fix will not fight the suite. Residual design tension (out of scope but worth noting): grain_fields raw tradeGoods 'Grain surplus'/'Bulk food exports' still export under banalité via the step-1 raw-resource path even though the note says surplus cannot leave.

confirmed.title: computeFactionPowers resource↔chain matching cannot bridge snake_case resource keys to space-separated chain labels — chain suggestions and supply checks mostly dead
confirmed.evidence: nearbyResources are RESOURCE_DATA snake_case keys ('grain_fields', 'iron_deposits' — confirmed by resolveResources.js RARE_RESOURCES, DEPLETED_IMPORT_MAP at economicGenerator.js:2299-2309, and EventComposer.jsx's 'stores the underscore key (the config format)'). chain.resource is a Title-Case space label ('Grain fields' — confirmed by RESOURCE_LOCAL_PRODUCERS keys at lines 175-209 being looked up directly by chain.resource, and by resourceLabelToKey existing in computeActiveChains.js precisely to map labels→keys). The matcher at lines 132-133 does raw 8-char prefix `includes`: 'grain fields'.includes('grain_fi') and 'grain_fields'.includes('grain fi') are both false — the underscore/space mismatch falls inside the prefix for almost every resource (grain_fields, iron_deposits, stone_quarry, grazing_land, salt_flats, hunting_grounds, fishing_grounds all fail; only resources whose first word is ≥8 chars, e.g. 'foraging_areas'/'mountain_timber', match). The same defect repeats with slice(0,6) at lines 228-229 for hasTerrainResource. Net effect: 'Opportunity: process X' / 'Incomplete chain' suggestions and the terrain-resource leg of the no-supply implausibility check silently never fire for most resources.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Normalize both sides before comparing (replace [_\s]+ with a single separator), or reuse computeActiveChains' resourceLabelToKey to map chain.resource to a RESOURCE_DATA key and compare keys exactly.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified by executing the exact matcher against the real inputs. The only live caller (src/generators/steps/generateNarratives.js:38-41) passes TERRAIN_DATA[terrain].allowedResources.slice(0,7) into generateEconomicViability -> computeFactionPowers. For mountains and desert terrains those arrays are snake_case keys (iron_deposits, stone_quarry, salt_flats, oasis_water, camel_herds...) while chain.resource values in src/data/supplyChainData.js are Title-Case space labels (Iron ore deposits, Stone quarry, Salt flats...). The 8-char-prefix bidirectional includes at economicGenerator.js:132-133 fails exactly as claimed: mountains match 2/7 resources (only precious_metals and mountain_timber, whose first 8 chars contain no underscore), desert 1/7. The reviewer's premise about the input source is slightly off (it is TERRAIN_DATA.allowedResources, not config.nearbyResources/RESOURCE_DATA keys), and the other five terrains use plain commodity words (wheat, livestock, game, wool, furs) that miss for a different reason — net only ~15 of 49 terrain resource entries ever match any chain, so the 'Opportunity: process X'/'Incomplete chain' suggestions are mostly dead, plus a false-positive: mountain_timber matches 'Mountain Pass'. The repeated slice(0,6) defect at lines 228-229 was also verified (stone_quarry, iron_deposits, salt_flats, coal_deposits, camel_herds, oasis_water, glass_sand, date_palms all miss), making hasTerrainResource false when the resource is genuinely nearby; since hasInfrastructure = hasTerrainResource || hasProducingInstitution || hasTradeAccess, this yields false 'no viable resource supply' IMPLAUSIBLE issues only for isolated settlements. Refutation attempts failed: no upstream normalization, no tests cover the affected strings (grep hits only the source file), and the defensive null-resource comment at lines 121-125 proves the matcher is intended to fire — the dead behavior is unintentional. Severity medium is right: dead advisory suggestions plus edge-case (isolated-only) wrong implausibility flags, not a main-path crash or determinism break.
verdicts.fix_note: Separator normalization ([_\s]+ -> one separator) fixes only the mountains/desert snake_case leg. The live input is TERRAIN_DATA[terrain].allowedResources (generateNarratives.js:33), which for the other five terrains contains plain commodity words ('wheat', 'livestock', 'game', 'wool', 'furs', 'freshwater fish') that no separator normalization will bridge to labels like 'Grain fields'/'Grazing land'/'Hunting grounds' — those need an explicit commodity->chain-resource map (or unifying TERRAIN_DATA vocab on RESOURCE_DATA keys plus reusing computeActiveChains.js resourceLabelToKey at line 69). Also fix the false-positive the prefix matcher creates: 'mountain_timber' currently matches the unrelated 'Mountain Pass' chain; exact key comparison after mapping eliminates it.

verdicts.reasoning: The matcher defect is real and demonstrated by executing the exact match logic against the real data modules, but the claim mis-traced the input source, which narrows the blast radius considerably. The only call path is steps/generateNarratives.js:33,38-41 -> generateEconomicViability -> computeFactionPowers (economicGenerator.js:2601-2602), and the nearbyResources argument is TERRAIN_DATA[terrain].allowedResources.slice(0,7) from geographyData.js — NOT config.nearbyResources snake_case keys. The claim's evidence chain (resolveResources.js RARE_RESOURCES, DEPLETED_IMPORT_MAP, EventComposer) all describes config.nearbyResources, which never reaches this function; 'grain_fields', 'fishing_grounds', 'grazing_land', 'hunting_grounds' are never inputs here. However, TERRAIN_DATA.allowedResources is itself snake_case for mountain and desert terrains, so the underscore-inside-prefix failure is fully real there: executed against the real modules, mountain is 5/7 dead (iron_deposits, stone_quarry, coal_deposits, alpine_pasture, gemstone_deposits never match; only precious_metals and mountain_timber do) and desert is 6/7 dead (oasis_water, date_palms, glass_sand, desert_salt, camel_herds, salt_flats all dead). The other five terrains use natural words ('grain', 'fish', 'clay', 'iron ore', 'stone', 'salt') for which the prefix matcher works on the major chains, so 'mostly dead for almost every resource' is overstated globally. Concrete consequences, both verified: (1) every mountain/desert generation silently loses its resource-chain suggestions for the terrain's defining resources (no 'Opportunity: process iron_deposits' ever) — though note the suggestions array is weakly surfaced (ViabilityTab.jsx:44-54 explicitly excludes the 'Resource Chain' category and EconomicsTab renders resourceAnalysis.exploitation instead; suggestions appear mainly in ChroniclePanel's JSON dump); (2) the slice(0,6) hasTerrainResource leg at :226-230 reads Iron ore deposits/Stone quarry/Coal as ABSENT from mountain terrain, so an isolated mountain/desert settlement with a processing institution whose name lacks a RESOURCE_LOCAL_PRODUCERS keyword gets a spurious IMPLAUSIBLE 'no viable resource supply' issue, which renders in ViabilityTab otherIssues, counts toward the >3-implausible verdict flip at :106, and feeds historyGenerator.js:666-674 event generation. Incidentally, 'mountain_timber' also false-positively matches the unrelated 'Mountain Pass' chain via the shared 8-char prefix. Severity is medium rather than high: it is a real, always-executed matching defect producing wrong simulation diagnostics for 2 of 7 terrains, but the dead output is advisory-layer content that is only weakly user-visible, and the spurious-issue path requires isolated trade config plus a producer-keyword miss.
verdicts.fix_note: Two corrections to the suggested fix. (1) The input is TERRAIN_DATA.allowedResources words ('fish', 'iron ore', 'livestock', 'iron_deposits'), not config.nearbyResources keys — any fix must be tested against that vocabulary, and ideally geographyData.js's mountain/desert allowedResources should be normalized to one vocabulary too, since they are the only snake_case lists. (2) Plain underscore-to-space normalization before the prefix compare is NOT sufficient: verified by execution that 'iron_deposits' vs 'Iron ore deposits', 'coal_deposits' vs 'Coal or peat deposits', 'oasis_water' vs 'Oasis and Water Rights', 'desert_salt', and 'camel_herds' still fail the 8-char prefix match after normalization (word-order/extra-word mismatches). The robust fix is a word-overlap join like the existing resourceLabelToKey (computeActiveChains.js:69-79): map chain.resource into RESOURCE_DATA key space and the terrain resource the same way, then compare keys — or extract one shared canonical matcher for both this site and the slice(0,6) duplicate at economicGenerator.js:228-229 (note the two prefix lengths currently produce different failure sets, e.g. alpine_pasture passes slice(0,6) but fails slice(0,8)). Preserve the existing null-resource guard (the comment at :121-125 documents the prior empty-prefix bug). Also note the matcher has a second, non-underscore coverage gap the fix could address or at least not regress: 'livestock', 'game', 'wool', 'furs', 'hardwood' match no chain under any prefix scheme ('Grazing land'/'Hunting grounds' share no word stems), and when a snake key does match today the user-facing suggestion title interpolates raw snake_case ('Opportunity: process mountain_timber') — the resource should be label-ized for display.

confirmed.title: getUpgradeChain compares the trade route against tier names — fromCityOrMetropolis/fromMetropolis/fromHigher import branches unreachable
confirmed.evidence: `route` is the trade-route key ('isolated'/'road'/'river'/'port'/'crossroads' — see resolveConfig.js route pools, lines 20-27/109-119), but getUpgradeChain tests `route === 'city' || route === 'metropolis'` (line 1488) and `route === 'metropolis'` (line 1491) to select the fromCityOrMetropolis/fromMetropolis buckets — conditions that can never be true. `isFromHigher` is always passed false by the only live caller (line 1221; line 973 is in the dead `_computeIncomeStreams`), so village `fromHigher` is unreachable too. Result: town falls through to fromHinterland, city falls through to fromHinterland, and the luxury/banking/legal import tables in UPGRADE_GOODS_BY_TIER (lines 1339-1434) are dead data. Final primaryImports are chain-derived (line 2327-2328), so the live blast radius is `v.imports` → the `transit` list (line 1239, returned as `transit: I` at line 2536): entrepôt towns/cities show hinterland goods (Food surplus, Raw wool) as transit goods instead of the intended higher-tier through-trade.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Select the source bucket from settlement tier and route semantics (e.g. towns/cities on any non-isolated route import fromCityOrMetropolis/fromMetropolis), not by comparing the route string to tier names; wire isFromHigher from a real signal or remove it.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified every load-bearing claim.
