Impact is bounded — it shifts a narrative prosperity label between two adjacent values (Struggling vs Poor) for one config corner — so 'low' severity is accurate.
verdicts.fix_note: The simple fix `idx = _rng() < 0.4 ? 0 : 1` changes the number of _rng() draws on 60% of paths (1 instead of 2), which shifts the seeded PRNG stream and changes all downstream values for existing seeds — any snapshot baselines would need regeneration. A fix that matches the inline comment's three-way intent and keeps the existing cap meaningful is `idx = _rng() < 0.4 ? 0 : _rng() < 0.5 ? 1 : 2;` (40% Struggling, 30% Poor, 30% 'luckier' capped to Poor by line 1164 → net 40/60). Whichever is chosen, the leading comment's '35% chance of Struggling' on line 1161 also disagrees with both candidate fixes and should be updated to match the chosen distribution.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed by reproduction against the real modules. At src/generators/economicGenerator.js:2527, every isolated thorp/hamlet gets prosperity 'Subsistence', which is passed to generateEconomicNarrative (line 2530). Line 1163's nested ternary `idx = _rng() < 0.4 ? 0 : _rng() < 0.5 ? 0 : 1` has both the 40% branch and the 30% middle branch returning 0, giving P(Struggling)=0.7 and P(Poor)=0.3. Running the actual generateEconomicState with the real seeded PRNG (createPRNG + setActiveRng) over 2000 seeds for an isolated thorp produced Struggling 70.3% / Poor 29.8% — matching the claim and contradicting both of the function's own documented distributions (line 1161: '35% chance of Struggling'; line 1163 inline: '40% Struggling, ~30% Poor, ~30% luckier'). Consequence is not cosmetic-only: economy.prosperity renders in the dossier tabs (OverviewTab/EconomicsTab/SummaryTab/TableView) and PDF (Cover/Overview/EconomicsTrade/IdentityDailyLife), and feeds factionDynamics.js:77,95 where 'Struggling' contributes -20 to public legitimacy vs -10 for 'Poor', plus districtProfile.js:127 branching. So ~70% of all isolated thorps/hamlets get the harshest label and legitimacy penalty instead of the designed ~35-40%. Deterministic per seed (no determinism break), but the output distribution is systematically wrong for an entire config class.
verdicts.fix_note: The claim's analysis is correct, but a straight fix to the inline comment's three-way distribution (e.g. `_rng() < 0.4 ? 0 : _rng() < 0.5 ? 1 : 2`) would be misleading: the '~30% luckier' outcome is unreachable regardless, because line 1164 clamps idx to Math.min(1, idx) and line 1180's isIsolatedSmall cap also clamps isolated thorps/hamlets at Poor. The two documented intents also disagree (35% at line 1161 vs 40% at line 1163). The honest fix is a single two-way draw matching whichever probability is intended — e.g. `idx = _rng() < 0.35 ? 0 : 1` — and reconciling/deleting the stale comments. Two caveats: (1) any fix changes how many PRNG draws this branch consumes (currently 1 or 2, conditionally), which shifts the seeded RNG stream and will change every existing isolated thorp/hamlet settlement for a given seed — expect snapshot/property test updates; (2) the post-roll modifiers (econOut>=70 bonus at 1168, crime/stress penalties at 1183+) still apply after the roll, so the realized distribution will not exactly equal the raw roll under non-default sliders; with econOut>=70 the isolated-small result is currently 100% Poor after capping.

confirmed.title: History anchor pass writes through mismatched indices — timelines scrambled, events lost/duplicated, severities corrupted to single characters
confirmed.evidence: generateRelationshipEvent sorts `events` DESCENDING (line 878: `events.sort((a,b)=>b.yearsAgo-a.yearsAgo)`), then builds an ASCENDING copy `anchored` (line 883) and iterates it: `anchored.forEach((event, idx) => { ... events[idx] = anchored; })` (lines 886-913). The idx comes from the ascending copy but is written into the descending array, so each 60%-chance replacement overwrites the MIRROR-position event: the original event at that slot is destroyed and the iterated event's era/category is duplicated. Additionally the fallback template `HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA.find(...) || events[idx]` (line 902) passes an already-RENDERED event into generateEventNarrative, whose `pick(eventTemplate.severity)` (line 521) then indexes into the severity STRING — producing one-character severities. Empirical probe (2000 runs of exported generateHistory, city/age 300): 74% of timelines neither ascending nor descending order, 41% contain two events at the identical yearsAgo (e.g. 'The Migration(106y, anchored)' alongside 'The Occupation(106y)'), and ~24% of all events carry corrupted severities like 'o', 'r', 'j', 'm' (3046/13766 events). These corrupted severities flow into the dossier, POLITICAL_FLAVOR catastrophic checks, and legacy annotations.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Iterate `events` directly (or write back via indexOf on the iterated event), and when the anchor typeMap has no entry for the category, skip the rewrite instead of re-rendering the existing event object as a template. Re-sort events after the pass.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified by reading src/generators/historyGenerator.js and reproducing empirically. THREE distinct, real defects in the history anchor pass, all confirmed:

1) INDEX MISMATCH (lines 878/883/886/909): `events` is sorted DESCENDING at line 878. Line 883 builds an ASCENDING copy `anchored`. Line 886 iterates `anchored` with `idx`, but line 909 writes the result into `events[idx]` — the MIRROR slot in the descending array. Since the replacement keeps the iterated (ascending) event's yearsAgo (line 903 uses `event.yearsAgo`), the slot ordering of `events` is corrupted.

2) NO RE-SORT in the common path: the only DESC re-sort (line 1003) is gated behind `resourceEvents.length > 0 && events.length < 7`. When no resource event qualifies (the common case), the scrambled order persists into the returned `historicalEvents` and `eventsTimeline` (line 1078). The bolted-on dedup (lines 1006-1014) keys on name only, so duplicate-yearsAgo events with different names survive.

3) SEVERITY CORRUPTION (line 902 -> 520): categories `occupation_infiltration`, `exile_return`, `demographic` are valid outputs of pickCategory (keys of generateSafetyNarrative2's weights, lines 264-266) but have NO entry in the inner typeMap (lines 894-901). So `HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA.find(e => e.type === undefined)` returns undefined (no data event has type undefined), firing the `|| events[idx]` fallback, which passes an ALREADY-RENDERED event back into generateEventNarrative. A rendered event's `.severity` is a STRING (line 567), and at line 520 `pick(eventTemplate.severity)` calls pick (rngContext.js:45-47) which does `str[Math.floor(rand*str.length)]` -> a single character.

Empirical reproduction with the reviewer's exact config (city, custom age 300, default no-stress, 2000 seeds via exported generateHistory + seeded PRNG): 74.6% of timelines neither ascending nor descending (reviewer: 74%); 39.2% contain two events at identical yearsAgo with different names, e.g. 'The Migration | The Occupation' (reviewer: 41%); 21.8% of all events carry corrupted severities like 'r','o','j','m' (reviewer: ~24%). A targeted probe confirmed 100% of corrupted severities occur on events flagged anchored:true and 0% on non-anchored events — pinning the cause precisely to the line-902 fallback re-render.

Downstream impact is real: corrupted severities silently fail the `=== 'catastrophic'` comparisons in historicalCharacter (line 1069) and _getSettlementHistoryNote (line 65), and flow into the dossier/historyBeats and PDF. No upstream guard handles this; no comment marks it intentional. The dev's own comment at lines 1006-1007 acknowledges the duplication symptom with a name-only band-aid that fixes neither the index bug nor the severity corruption. Not dead code — runs on the main path for any aged settlement with context (the normal case).
verdicts.fix_note: Reviewer's suggested fix is correct. Concretely: (a) do not iterate an ascending copy and write into the descending array — either iterate `events` directly, or compute the write target via indexOf on the iterated event; (b) when `typeMap[cat]` has no entry (occupation_infiltration, exile_return, demographic), SKIP the rewrite rather than passing the already-rendered `events[idx]` back into generateEventNarrative (that is the source of single-char severities); (c) re-sort `events` (e.g. DESC by yearsAgo) unconditionally after the anchor pass, not only inside the resource-event branch. Optionally, hardening pick() in rngContext.js to reject non-array input would prevent this whole class of string-indexing corruption, but the local fixes (a)-(c) are sufficient.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via read of src/generators/historyGenerator.js:878-913 + empirical probe against the real exported generateHistory.

Two distinct, compounding defects in the anchor pass of generateRelationshipEvent (reached whenever context is non-null, i.e. the normal pipeline path for any aged settlement):

1) INDEX MISMATCH (lines 878/883/886/909). `events` is sorted DESCENDING by yearsAgo (line 878). A separate array `anchored = [...events].sort(ASCENDING)` is built (line 883). The loop `anchored.forEach((event, idx) => { ... events[idx] = anchored; })` derives idx from the ASCENDING copy but writes into the DESCENDING `events` array (line 909). So the replacement for the idx-th-oldest event lands in the idx-th-NEWEST slot. This (a) destroys whatever event was at the mirror slot in `events`, and (b) inserts an anchored event whose yearsAgo no longer fits its neighbors, scrambling order and producing two events at the identical yearsAgo. Probe over the real module (city/age 300, ~616 usable runs): 27.6% of timelines were neither ascending nor descending, 26.3% contained two events at the same yearsAgo. Concrete observed duplicates: "The Migration(187y)" alongside "The Siege(187y,anchored)"; "The Occupation(91y)" alongside "The Siege(91y,anchored)" — exactly the failure mode claimed.

2) SEVERITY CORRUPTION (lines 902 + 520). The anchor template lookup HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA.find(...) only maps 5 categories (economic/political/disaster/religious/magical, lines 894-900). But event.type (cat) can also be occupation_infiltration, exile_return, or demographic — all real, non-zero-weight categories returned by generateSafetyNarrative2 (lines 264-266) and selectable by pickCategory. For those, typeMap[cat] is undefined, find returns undefined, and the `|| events[idx]` fallback (line 902) passes an ALREADY-RENDERED event (whose .severity is a STRING, set at line 567 from pick() at line 520) back into generateEventNarrative. There, line 520 calls pick(eventTemplate.severity) on a string; pick() (rngContext.js:45-48) indexes arr[Math.floor(rand*arr.length)] on the string, yielding a single character. Probe: 24.9% of all generated events carried corrupted single-char severities ("o","r","m","j","t","n","i","h","c","p","a","s") — matching the claimed ~24%.

DOWNSTREAM IMPACT confirmed: corrupted severities can never equal 'catastrophic'/'major', so (a) POLITICAL_FLAVOR catastrophic detection (lines 65,88) and historicalCharacter "great catastrophe" classification (line 1069) silently miss real catastrophes; (b) lastingEffects/plotHooks counts collapse to 1 (lines 521,534) regardless of true severity — silent simulation degradation; (c) the single-letter severity renders into the on-screen dossier and PDF and into the many components that read e.severity (CompendiumPanel, CoherencePanel, map panels, region viewers). Determinism is preserved (same seed => same garbage), so existing snapshot tests would have baked in the corruption rather than flagging it.

Not critical: no crash on main path, no data loss to persistence (settlements aren't persisted), no money/security. It is wrong, frequently-produced simulation output plus data corruption crossing the generator->presentation boundary — squarely high.

confirmed.title: generateNPCs never receives powerStructure/economicState — noble NPC roles are unreachable and goal commodity/faction tokens always fall back
confirmed.evidence: generatePopulation destructures ctx.powerStructure (line 31) but calls `generateNPCs({ tier, institutions }, culture, effectiveConfig)` (line 33) — the settlement arg lacks powerStructure and economicState. Inside npcGenerator.js: `settlement.powerStructure?.factions` (line 1246) is always undefined, so `powerFactionCats` is empty and the NOBLE_ROLES/CRAFTS_ROLES injection (lines 1409-1435) never fires; and `settlement.economicState?...` (lines 1486-1488) makes `_tradeCommodity`/`_dominantFaction`/`_prosperity` permanently null, so all `{commodity}`/`{faction}` substitutions in NPC goals (npcGenerator.js lines 249, 266, 300, 336, 369-375) degrade to generic fallbacks. Noble roles have NO other path: the base catalog gate `institutions.some(i => i.priorityCategory === 'noble' || i.category?.toLowerCase() === 'noble')` can never pass (the institutional catalog defines no 'noble' category or priorityCategory — verified programmatically). regenNPCsPipeline (generateSettlementPipeline.js:79) has the same gap. Confirmed by running the pipeline: a city with a 'Noble Families' faction (category 'noble') produced 0 noble-category NPCs, while mergeNPCLists (nobleFaction mapping), CATEGORY_COMPAT.noble, and FACTION_ATTRACTION.noble all expect them.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Pass powerStructure and economicState into the settlement arg of generateNPCs in both generatePopulation.js and regenNPCsPipeline (ctx already has them; the step depends on generatePower).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified on all counts by reading the code and running the actual pipeline.

CODE PATH: src/generators/steps/generatePopulation.js:33 calls generateNPCs({ tier, institutions }, culture, effectiveConfig) — the settlement arg omits powerStructure and economicState even though they are destructured from ctx (powerStructure on line 31) and available (the step deps on 'generatePower', which provides powerStructure at generatePower.js:22/125, and economyReconcilePass/generateEconomy provide economicState). The same gap exists in regenNPCsPipeline at generateSettlementPipeline.js:79.

NOBLE ROLES UNREACHABLE: Inside npcGenerator.js the noble-role injection at lines 1409-1414 is gated on powerFactionCats.has('noble'), where powerFactionCats = new Set((settlement.powerStructure?.factions||[]).map(f=>f.category)) (line 1246) — always empty because powerStructure is never passed. The only other path is the base catalog (getUpgradeOpportunities -> HISTORY_EVENTS.noble at historyData.js:276), gated at economicGenerator.js:1532 on institutions.some(i => i.priorityCategory==='noble' || i.category?.toLowerCase()==='noble'). I enumerated the institutional catalog: the only priorityCategory values present are adventuring, crafts, criminal, defense, economy, entertainment, exotic, government, infrastructure, magic, military, religion — no 'noble', and no plain category:'noble' field exists either. So the base path can never fire; the powerStructure-gated injection is the ONLY route to noble NPCs.

EMPIRICAL PROOF: Ran generateSettlementPipeline at tier 'city' across 60 seeds. With a noble-category power faction present (e.g. 'Manor Household', category 'noble' — assigned via inferFactionCategory at powerGenerator.js:590/2155 from the 'noble' keyword set at lines 573-587), ZERO noble-category NPCs were produced in any run.

GOAL TOKEN DEGRADATION: npcGenerator.js:1486-1488 derive _tradeCommodity, _dominantFaction, _prosperity exclusively from settlement.economicState/settlement.powerStructure — all permanently null. In assignNPCGoal, topFaction = config._dominantFaction || null (line 242) is therefore always null, so {faction} substitutions fall back to 'the dominant faction' (line 373) and the _dominantFaction-dependent templates (lines 300, 336) always use generic text. commodity (line 241) has an extra config.tradeCommodity fallback, but that field is never set on the config passed in (grep confirms tradeCommodity is an economicState field, not a config field), so {commodity} also always degrades to 'key goods'.

DESIGN INTENT CORROBORATED: Downstream consumers expect noble NPCs — mergeNPCLists maps baron/duke/lord/manor/etc. role keywords to nobleFaction (npcGenerator.js:767-880), CATEGORY_COMPAT includes 'noble' (line 912-913), and FACTION_ATTRACTION.noble exists (generatePopulation.js:23). This confirms noble NPCs are a designed feature, not dead code.

REFUTATION ATTEMPTS FAILED: No upstream guard injects powerStructure/economicState into the settlement arg before generateNPCs; no wrapper mutates it; the path is the live pipeline step (not legacy generateSettlement.js). The suggested fix is feasible exactly as stated since ctx already carries both fields.

Severity high (not critical): deterministic wrong simulation output affecting every generation — an entire NPC role class is silently unreachable and NPC goal grounding is systematically lost — but no crash, data loss, money, or security impact.
verdicts.fix_note: Reviewer's fix is correct: pass powerStructure and economicState into the settlement arg of generateNPCs in both generatePopulation.js:33 (use ctx.powerStructure / ctx.economicState) and regenNPCsPipeline at generateSettlementPipeline.js:79 (use settlement.powerStructure / settlement.economicState, which are already on the passed-in settlement). Minor accuracy note on the EVIDENCE: the {commodity} fallback technically has a first-choice of config.tradeCommodity (npcGenerator.js:241), but that field is never populated on the config passed to generateNPCs, so the practical outcome (always-null commodity, generic fallback) is unchanged. The {faction}/_dominantFaction degradation is strictly tied to settlement.powerStructure as described.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via end-to-end trace plus an isolated A/B run against the real modules.

Failure path: generatePopulation.js:31 destructures ctx.powerStructure (and ctx.economicState is also in ctx — provided by generateEconomy, a transitive dep), but line 33 calls generateNPCs({ tier, institutions }, culture, effectiveConfig) — the settlement arg omits powerStructure and economicState. Inside npcGenerator.js:1246, powerFactionCats = new Set((settlement.powerStructure?.factions||[]).map(...)) is therefore always empty, so:
- The NOBLE_ROLES injection (line 1409 `if (powerFactionCats.has('noble'))`) never fires.
- The CRAFTS_ROLES injection (line 1415 `if (powerFactionCats.has('crafts') || powerFactionCats.has('economy'))`) never fires.
- npcConfig._tradeCommodity / _dominantFaction / _prosperity (lines 1486-1488), read from settlement.economicState/powerStructure, are permanently null.

No alternate path to noble roles: noble roles live in HISTORY_EVENTS.noble (historyData.js:276) but getUpgradeOpportunities (economicGenerator.js:1531-1532) gates non-'other' categories on an institution with priorityCategory==='noble' OR category?.toLowerCase()==='noble'. The institutional catalog defines NO noble priorityCategory (tally: economy/government/military/crafts/infrastructure/religion/entertainment/criminal/magic/exotic/adventuring/defense only), and assembleInstitutions sets institution.category from catalog section names, never 'noble'. So noble NPCs can only come from the powerStructure-gated injection — which is dead.

Concrete demonstrations:
1. Real pipeline (customContent:{}, city tier): seeds 0/2/3/4 each produced a 'Manor Household' noble power faction (category 'noble') yet 0 noble-category NPCs.
2. Isolated generateNPCs A/B with a fixed PRNG: CURRENT shape {tier,institutions} → categories {government:4, economy:4, other:5} (0 noble, 0 crafts); FIXED shape adding powerStructure+economicState → {noble:2 [Baron/Baroness, Land Agent], crafts:4, ...}. Passing the two fields unlocks both injections.
3. Direct derivation check: {tier,institutions} → {_tradeCommodity:null,_dominantFaction:null,_prosperity:null}; full settlement → {iron, 'Noble Families', thriving}.

regenNPCsPipeline (generateSettlementPipeline.js:79) has the identical omission and is wired to the live store 'regenerate NPCs' action (settlementSlice.js:690), so a noble-faction settlement regenerates to 0 noble NPCs as well.

Data-level consequence: every village+ settlement that rolls a noble and/or crafts/economy power faction (common — the trace hit it on nearly every city seed) is missing entire NPC categories that downstream code expects (mergeNPCLists nobleFaction mapping, CATEGORY_COMPAT.noble, FACTION_ATTRACTION.noble at generatePopulation.js:23). NPC stress/role goals also degrade to generic fallbacks instead of being grounded in the settlement's commodity, dominant faction, and prosperity. Determinism is preserved; this is wrong simulation output on the main path, hence high (not critical: no crash, money, security, or data loss).
verdicts.fix_note:
Core fix is correct: pass the two fields through. In generatePopulation.js, ctx.economicState is also available (provided by generateEconomy, transitive dep), so change line 33 to: generateNPCs({ tier, institutions, powerStructure, economicState }, culture, effectiveConfig) — and add economicState to the line-31 destructure. In regenNPCsPipeline (generateSettlementPipeline.js:79) pass { tier: settlement.tier, institutions: settlement.institutions||[], powerStructure: settlement.powerStructure, economicState: settlement.economicState }.

Two corrections to the original evidence (do not change isReal/severity):
1. The lines 249/266/300/336 substitutions are ${commodity}/${topFaction} TEMPLATE LITERALS inside STRESS_GOALS (these are genuinely affected). The separate {commodity}/{faction} regex-substitution branch at npcGenerator.js:370-373 is a no-op for a different reason: grep finds ZERO {commodity}/{faction} tokens in NPC_FACTION_GOALS (npcData.js), so that regex never matches regardless of this bug. The real degradation flows through the STRESS_GOALS template literals and through _dominantFaction/_prosperity, not the {token} regex.
2. The base-catalog noble gate quoted in the evidence lives in economicGenerator.js getUpgradeOpportunities (lines 1531-1532), not in npcGenerator.js — the claim's description of it is accurate, just located in the economic generator.

Also worth a determinism check after the fix: injecting noble/crafts candidates changes the candidate pool and thus the weighted-random draws, so existing NPC snapshot/property tests will need their fixtures regenerated (expected, not a regression).

confirmed.title: Stress-to-tension mapping points at tension templates that do not exist — insurgency/mass_migration/religious_conversion/slave_revolt and trade-partner neighbours produce no tension
confirmed.evidence: STRESS_TO_TENSION maps insurgency/religious_conversion/slave_revolt → 'legitimacy_crisis' and mass_migration → 'demographic_pressure' (lines 639-643), and a trade_partner neighbour maps to 'trade_dispute' (line 652). HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA (src/data/historyData.js) contains exactly 16 types — none of these three exist (verified by enumerating `type:` fields). `HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA.find(e => e.type === tensionType)` returns undefined and the priority-add silently does nothing (lines 696-702, 725-734), so the five newer stressors and trade-partner relationships never seed their intended currentTensions; random fillers are used instead. The genSuccessionNarr branches keyed on those topTension values can also never fire.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add 'legitimacy_crisis', 'demographic_pressure', and 'trade_dispute' templates to HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA, or remap those stressors to existing types.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. In src/generators/historyGenerator.js the STRESS_TO_TENSION map (lines 628-644) routes insurgency/religious_conversion/slave_revolt -> 'legitimacy_crisis' (639,642,643) and mass_migration -> 'demographic_pressure' (640); the neighbour path maps trade_partner -> 'trade_dispute' (651-652). I enumerated every `type:` field in src/data/historyData.js: there are exactly 16 (succession_crisis, economic_disparity, religious_tension, guild_conflict, external_threat, resource_scarcity, crime_wave, magical_controversy, generational_divide, corruption_scandal, outside_debt, occupation_legacy, infiltration_fear, disputed_land, population_friction, leadership_vacuum). None of 'legitimacy_crisis', 'demographic_pressure', or 'trade_dispute' exists there (grep confirms zero matches in historyData.js). Therefore HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA.find(e => e.type === tensionType) returns undefined, and the guarded priority-adds at lines 696-702 and 725-734 (the `if (tmpl)` / `if (tmpl && ...)` checks) silently no-op, so these five stressors and the trade-partner neighbour never seed their intended currentTensions — the random filler loop (lines 740-750) runs instead.

The path is live, not dead: stressorPicker.js (src/domain/stressorPicker.js:42-46) emits insurgency/slave_revolt/mass_migration as real IDs, and stressTypes.js (line 147+) fully defines these stressors with probabilities and hooks, and primaryStress (lines 589-607) explicitly lists insurgency/mass_migration/religious_conversion/slave_revolt as resolvable values. trade_partner is a real relationshipType (neighbourGenerator.js, resolveNeighbour.js SCORE_SHIFTING_RELTYPES). 

The secondary claim also holds: genSuccessionNarr lives in powerGenerator.js (line 318) keyed on r.topTension, which narrativeGenerator.js:758 derives from currentTensions[0].type; its branches cover all 16 real types but none of the three phantom types (grep: zero matches in powerGenerator.js), so those narrative branches can never fire. No upstream guard, comment, or test legitimizes this; it is an unintended silent fallback.
verdicts.fix_note: Fix is correct as stated. Either add 'legitimacy_crisis', 'demographic_pressure', and 'trade_dispute' template objects to HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA in src/data/historyData.js (and corresponding genSuccessionNarr branches in powerGenerator.js so the new topTension values produce narrative), OR remap the stressors to existing types — e.g. insurgency/religious_conversion/slave_revolt -> 'leadership_vacuum' or 'religious_tension'/'population_friction', mass_migration -> 'population_friction', trade_partner -> 'economic_disparity' or 'outside_debt'. The first option is preferable since it preserves the distinct intent of these newer stressors; remapping to leadership_vacuum/religious_tension loses specificity. Note that simply adding to HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA alone leaves the genSuccessionNarr gap, so both files should be updated for full coherence.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL. Traced end-to-end against the real modules.

Failure path:
1. A config carries one of the five newer stressors (insurgency / mass_migration / religious_conversion / slave_revolt) — all real, defined in src/data/stressTypes.js and resolved into `primaryStress` at historyGenerator.js:589-606 with keys that exactly match the stressor IDs. (wartime maps to external_threat, which DOES exist, so the EVIDENCE listing wartime among the broken ones is incorrect — wartime is fine.)
2. STRESS_TO_TENSION (lines 639-643) maps insurgency/religious_conversion/slave_revolt → 'legitimacy_crisis' and mass_migration → 'demographic_pressure'. The trade_partner neighbour branch (lines 648-652) maps to 'trade_dispute'.
3. At the priority-add (lines 725-734 for stress, 696-702 for neighbour), `HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA.find(e => e.type === tensionType)` is called. I enumerated HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA in src/data/historyData.js: exactly 16 types, and grep confirms NONE of legitimacy_crisis / demographic_pressure / trade_dispute exist there. (The legitimacy_crisis hits elsewhere are in src/domain/hookEscalation.js CLOCK_TEMPLATES — a different subsystem.)
4. Runtime-verified with a node one-liner against the real module: all three lookups return `undefined`; valid types like occupation_legacy/external_threat return templates. With tmpl undefined, the `if (tmpl)` guard skips the push — the priority-add silently no-ops.

Consequence (data-level coherence drift, not a crash): the fill loop (lines 736-749) still picks random tensions, so the dossier always shows SOME currentTensions — but for these five stressors and for trade-partner neighbours, the "current tensions" are never correlated with the dominant stressor/relationship. A settlement under religious_conversion stress should surface religious_tension; under mass_migration it should surface population_friction; instead it gets random fillers. This is a genuine simulation-coherence defect (the product's stated overarching goal), silent and deterministic.

One correction to the claim's last sentence: genSuccessionNarr (powerGenerator.js:318-431) branches on r.topTension = currentTensions[0].type (narrativeGenerator.js:758). It has branches for all 16 valid types and NO branches keyed on legitimacy_crisis/demographic_pressure/trade_dispute. So it isn't that "branches keyed on those values can never fire" — there are no such branches at all. The accurate statement is: these tension types can never become topTension, so any intended narrative coupling for them is absent. Net effect on coherence is the same.

Severity: medium. It is wrong/degraded simulation output (the intended stress→tension correlation never happens) for 5 of 15 stressors plus trade-partner neighbours, but it is silent, deterministic, never crashes, and a plausible tension is always still emitted via the random fill. Determinism is preserved. Confidence high (~0.9): mapping targets, the 16-type inventory, and the runtime-undefined lookup are all directly verified.
verdicts.fix_note: Two viable fixes. (A) Remap to existing HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA types — the cleanest, since coherent equivalents already exist: religious_conversion → 'religious_tension'; mass_migration → 'population_friction'; insurgency → 'leadership_vacuum' (or 'corruption_scandal'); slave_revolt → 'population_friction' (or 'leadership_vacuum'); trade_partner neighbour → 'economic_disparity' (or 'external_threat'). (B) Add the three missing templates (legitimacy_crisis, demographic_pressure, trade_dispute) to src/data/historyData.js. If (B) is chosen, note that genSuccessionNarr in powerGenerator.js (lines 318-431) and any topTension-keyed branch would also need new arms for those types or those tensions will still have no narrative coupling — otherwise (A) keeps everything wired to types that already have full downstream support. Recommend (A). Also: the original EVIDENCE incorrectly lists 'wartime' as broken — wartime maps to 'external_threat' which exists and works; only the four (insurgency/mass_migration/religious_conversion/slave_revolt) plus the trade_partner branch are affected.

confirmed.title: ensureFactionStructuralNpcs dedup can never match pipeline NPCs — placeholder seat-holders duplicate generated ones
confirmed.evidence: The dedup requires sameRole AND sameFaction (lines 137-142), where sameFaction checks `npc.linkedFactionIds` — a field only factionRoles.js itself and event-created NPCs ever write (verified by grep); pipeline NPCs carry only the factionAffiliation string from mergeNPCLists. sameRole compares exact role strings, but structural roles ('Watch Captain', 'Guildmaster', 'High Priestess', 'Lord Mayor') never equal the pipeline catalog's roles ('Guard Captain', 'Guild Master', 'High Priest', 'Mayor'). So the documented idempotency ('skips a role if an existing NPC already matches it') never applies and placeholders are appended to every settlement whose factions match an archetype. Confirmed empirically: a generated city contained both 'Cäcilia Schröder / Guild Master' and the placeholders 'The Guildmaster' + 'The Senior Trader' (cat=undefined, title=undefined); even a 20-person thorp received 'Guildmaster | Senior Trader' placeholders. Two factions of the same archetype also mint identically-named placeholders ('The Kingpin' twice).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Match by normalized role keywords AND by factionAffiliation name (in addition to linkedFactionIds), or write linkedFactionIds in mergeNPCLists; consider giving structural NPCs real names via the naming pools.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL after reading the code and reproducing empirically. The claim holds on every checked point.

ACTIVE PATH: The store uses generateSettlementPipeline (src/store/settlementSlice.js:558,866), which runs the assembleSettlement step (registered, deps satisfied) that calls ensureFactionStructuralNpcs at src/generators/steps/assembleSettlement.js:143. Not dead code.

DEDUP NEVER MATCHES (src/generators/factionRoles.js:137-142):
- sameFaction (line 140) reads npc.linkedFactionIds. Grep across all of src/generators/ shows linkedFactionIds is written ONLY by factionRoles.js itself (line 109, on the structural NPCs it mints). Pipeline NPCs from mergeNPCLists get only the factionAffiliation STRING (src/generators/npcGenerator.js:975), never linkedFactionIds. The entity-layer createNpc defaults linkedFactionIds to [] (src/domain/entities/npcs.js:95), and the npcs entering this step come straight from ctx.npcs (assembleSettlement.js:46,75) without that mapping. So sameFaction is always false for existing pipeline NPCs.
- sameRole (line 139) is case-insensitive exact equality. Structural roles ('Guildmaster','High Priestess','Watch Captain','Lord Mayor','Kingpin','Archmagister') never equal the catalog roles in src/data/npcData.js / npcGenerator.js:1459-1468 ('Guild Master','High Priest','Guard Captain','Mayor', etc.). 'guild master' !== 'guildmaster', 'high priest' !== 'high priestess', etc. No collision.
Both conjuncts fail, so the documented idempotency (lines 119-121: "skips a role if an existing NPC already matches it") never fires.

EMPIRICAL REPRODUCTION: A settlement with one real NPC (Cäcilia Schröder / 'Guild Master' / factionAffiliation 'Merchants Guild') plus two merchant-archetype factions yielded 5 NPCs: the original PLUS 'The Guildmaster' + 'The Senior Trader' for each faction. Placeholders carry name 'The <role>' (nameTemplateFor, line 166) and no cat/title — exactly the 'cat=undefined, title=undefined' the reviewer reported. Two same-archetype factions double-mint identically-NAMED placeholders ('The Guildmaster' twice), matching the 'The Kingpin twice' observation (ids differ via faction-name slug, names do not).

IMPACT: There is no generatedAs==='faction_structural' filter anywhere in the display/PDF layer (only a JSDoc note at src/domain/entities/npcs.js:45). The placeholders flow into src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js (lines 202,862,1049) and render as real NPCs in both dossier and PDF, and their influence/legitimacyContribution/stabilityContribution feed the impairment engine. So this is coherence/correctness pollution on essentially every settlement whose factions match one of the six archetypes (verified the canonical mapping in factionRoles.js:61-69 covers merchant/craft/temple/watch/thieves/noble/arcane), not merely cosmetic.

Could not refute: path is live, no upstream guard populates linkedFactionIds, roles genuinely never match, behavior contradicts the function's own documented idempotency contract.
verdicts.fix_note: The reviewer's suggested fix is sound but should be hardened. Matching by factionAffiliation NAME is fragile: structural-NPC linkedFactionIds store the faction id/name resolved via faction.id||faction.faction||faction.name (factionRoles.js:96), while existing pipeline NPCs store assignedFaction.faction (the faction's display name). These can diverge. Most robust: (a) normalize role via keyword/synonym matching (guildmaster≈guild master, watch captain≈guard captain, high priestess≈high priest, lord mayor≈mayor) AND (b) match faction by resolving BOTH sides through the same faction identifier (compare proposed's source faction.faction against npc.factionAffiliation, not just linkedFactionIds). Alternatively, the cleaner root-cause fix is to have mergeNPCLists/the population step write linkedFactionIds (derived from factionAffiliation) so all NPC↔faction linkage lives in one canonical field. Separately, give structural NPCs real names from the naming pools (namingData.js has a guild_master title mapping) so even legitimately-added seat-holders aren't bare 'The X' placeholders, and consider filtering or de-emphasizing faction_structural NPCs in viewModel if a real role-holder of the same seat already exists.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED end-to-end against the real modules.

Trace:
1. The pipeline's assembleSettlement step (src/generators/steps/assembleSettlement.js:143) calls ensureFactionStructuralNpcs(settlement) with the explicit intent (lines 16-17) that "existing pipeline NPCs are deduplicated against." Pipeline NPCs are produced by mergeNPCLists (src/generators/npcGenerator.js:730+), which sets enriched.factionAffiliation (a STRING, line 975) and roles drawn from the catalog ('Guild Master', 'Guard Captain', 'High Priest', 'Mayor', 'Governor', 'Guild Archmage', etc. — src/generators/npcGenerator.js:211,1459-1468; src/data/npcData.js). These NPCs never get linkedFactionIds.

2. The dedup at src/generators/factionRoles.js:137-142 requires sameRole && sameFaction:
   - sameFaction (line 140) reads npc.linkedFactionIds. Grep confirms only factionRoles.js itself and the event layer (domain/events/mutate.js, entities/npcs.js assignNpcToRole) ever WRITE linkedFactionIds; no generator or normalizer maps factionAffiliation -> linkedFactionIds. So on every freshly-generated settlement, pipeline NPCs have empty/absent linkedFactionIds => sameFaction is always false.
   - sameRole (line 139) compares exact lowercased role strings, but the structural roles ('Guildmaster','Watch Captain','High Priestess','Lord Mayor','Archmagister','Senior Trader','Kingpin','Lieutenant') never equal the catalog's role strings ('Guild Master','Guard Captain','High Priest','Mayor','Governor', etc.) => sameRole is always false.
   - Joined by && (line 141), so the guard can never fire against pipeline NPCs. The documented idempotency is dead on the main path.

3. Empirically reproduced: a settlement already containing 'Cäcilia Schröder / Guild Master' (factionAffiliation 'The Merchant Guild'), 'Guard Captain', and 'High Priest' still had The Guildmaster, The Senior Trader, The Watch Captain, and The High Priestess appended — duplicating seats that already exist. The placeholders carry cat=undefined / title=undefined. Two factions of the same archetype each mint identically-named placeholders ('The Kingpin' x2).

4. User-visible consequence: in the PDF view model (src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:913-914) the placeholders resolve factionAffiliation to null (their category/faction are undefined), so they render in the dossier and exported PDF as faction-less NPCs literally named 'The Guildmaster' / 'The High Priestess' sitting next to the real, named, properly-affiliated holder of the same seat. This is duplicate-role-holder data pollution on the main generation path. No test covers this behavior (grep for factionRoles/StructuralNpcs in test files returns nothing).

This is a real defect: a structurally-impossible dedup guard that pollutes the NPC roster of (essentially) every generated settlement with duplicate, mis-typed placeholder seat-holders. Not a crash or determinism break (the duplication is deterministic), and the same-archetype-two-factions case is arguably correct (distinct entities). The core bug is the failure to dedup against the real role-holder, which is wrong simulation/data-integrity output -> high.
verdicts.fix_note:
The suggested area is correct; both failing legs must be fixed, not just one.

(1) sameFaction must compare against the pipeline NPC's actual affiliation field. Pipeline NPCs carry factionAffiliation (the faction's display name, = faction.faction), not linkedFactionIds. The proposed NPC's linkedFactionIds holds faction.id. So a correct comparison must resolve both to a common key — e.g. match proposed faction by id OR by name: build the proposed against the faction object and also compare npc.factionAffiliation (lowercased) to the faction's name/faction (lowercased), in addition to the existing linkedFactionIds check. Note generateFactionStructuralNpcs uses faction.id || faction.faction || faction.name for factionId, so the linkage id is unstable too.

(2) sameRole must use semantic role equivalence, not exact-string equality. Map structural roles to the canonical catalog roles (Guildmaster~Guild Master/Merchant Guild Master, Watch Captain~Guard Captain/City Watch Chief/Garrison Commander, High Priestess~High Priest, Lord Mayor~Mayor/Governor/elder, Archmagister~Guild Archmage/Archmage, Kingpin~Thieves' Guild Master). The cleanest route is to reuse the shared archetype classifier: instead of (or in addition to) string compare, treat an existing NPC as occupying the seat if it shares the faction AND its role maps to the same archetype + importance tier (e.g. inferImportance(npc) in entities/npcs.js already maps 'guild master'->'key','high priest'->'pillar', etc.). A pragmatic guard: skip synthesizing a 'pillar'/'key' structural role for a faction if that faction already has an existing NPC of >= that importance tier whose role classifies to the same archetype.

(3) Independent of the dedup fix, the placeholders should be given category/factionAffiliation so they don't render as faction-less in the PDF (viewModel.js:913 reads factionAffiliation/faction/category; factionRoles sets none of them). At minimum set factionAffiliation = faction.name so devAnomalies (npc_references_missing_faction) and the PDF faction grouping behave.

(4) The same-archetype-two-factions case (two 'The Kingpin') is acceptable behavior (distinct factions, distinct ids/linkedFactionIds) and need not be 'fixed' beyond optionally disambiguating the placeholder name with the faction name, e.g. `The Kingpin of {faction.name}`.

confirmed.title: Resource-anchored history events are dead code — context.nearbyResources is never populated
confirmed.evidence: generateRelationshipEvent reads `context.nearbyResources || []` (line 916) to gate four bespoke events (The Iron Dispute, The Great Harvest Compact, The Great Construction, The Arcane Incident — lines 921-996). The context object is built solely by buildHistoryContext (lines 116-241) which never sets nearbyResources, and generateNarratives.js:46 calls generateHistory(tier, effectiveConfig, institutions, ...) without threading ctx.nearbyResources (which resolveResources.js does provide on the pipeline ctx). hasResource() is therefore always false and none of these events can ever appear.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Thread ctx.nearbyResources through generateNarratives into generateHistory and set context.nearbyResources in buildHistoryContext.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed empirically. The resource-gated block in generateRelationshipEvent (src/generators/historyGenerator.js:916-996) reads `context.nearbyResources || []`, but the `context` object is built solely by buildHistoryContext (lines 116-241), whose returned object (lines 216-240) never includes a `nearbyResources` field. So `context.nearbyResources` is always undefined => hasResource() is always false => the four bespoke events can never appear.

I verified this at runtime. Three of the four events (The Iron Dispute, The Great Harvest Compact, The Great Construction) exist ONLY in this block — they are not in src/data/historyData.js (which contains only "The Arcane Incident" at line 1311 as a magical_controversy template name). Calling generateHistory across 5000 seeds for tier 'metropolis' with config.nearbyResources = ['iron_deposits','grain_fields','stone_quarry','magical_node'] produced ZERO occurrences of those three block-exclusive events. The Arcane Incident does appear, but via the normal anchored/template path, not this block. Dead code confirmed.

One correction to the reviewer's root-cause diagnosis: the claim that generateNarratives.js:46 "does not thread ctx.nearbyResources" is inaccurate. resolveResources.js:222 writes `effectiveConfig.nearbyResources = nearbyResources` (it populates BOTH ctx and effectiveConfig), and generateNarratives.js:46 passes effectiveConfig into generateHistory, which passes it to buildHistoryContext as `config`. So the data IS available to buildHistoryContext as config.nearbyResources — buildHistoryContext simply fails to copy it onto the returned context object. The real one-line gap is in buildHistoryContext, not in the threading through generateNarratives. The regenHistoryPipeline call site (generateSettlementPipeline.js:108) has the identical defect.

Severity medium is correct: no crash, no security/money/data-loss, and determinism is intact. It is a real feature seam that silently never fires — three bespoke resource-anchored historical events (plus the resource-flavored Arcane Incident variant) are unreachable, degrading simulation richness/coherence for settlements with iron/grain/stone/magical-node resources.
verdicts.fix_note: The reviewer's suggested fix is half right. The necessary and sufficient change is to set `nearbyResources: config.nearbyResources || []` in the object returned by buildHistoryContext (src/generators/historyGenerator.js, the return at lines 216-240). config (= effectiveConfig) already carries nearbyResources from resolveResources.js:222, so the suggested "thread ctx.nearbyResources through generateNarratives into generateHistory" step is unnecessary — generateNarratives.js:46 and regenHistoryPipeline (generateSettlementPipeline.js:108) already pass a config that contains nearbyResources. After the fix, also confirm determinism is preserved (the block already consumes _rng() unconditionally at line 918, so seeding behavior is unchanged) and consider adding a generateHistory-level test that asserts these events can emerge, since the existing tests in tests/domain/historyBeats.test.js and tests/domain/stressorAftermath.test.js only use them as hand-built fixtures and never exercise the resource gate.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL. The four resource-anchored history events in src/generators/historyGenerator.js:920-996 (The Iron Dispute, The Great Harvest Compact, The Great Construction, The Arcane Incident) are gated by hasResource(), which reads `context.nearbyResources || []` at line 916.

Trace end-to-end:
- generateHistory (src/generators/historyGenerator.js:1031-1048) builds `context` exclusively via buildHistoryContext(config, institutions, economicState, powerStructure) at line 1040. I read buildHistoryContext's full return object (lines 216-240): it never includes a nearbyResources field. The only post-build mutation is `context._institutions = institutions` at line 1041. So context.nearbyResources is always undefined.
- context is then passed as the 4th arg to generateRelationshipEvent (line 1048), where line 916 reads context.nearbyResources -> undefined -> `|| []` -> empty array. hasResource() is therefore always false and the four bespoke events at lines 921-996 can never be pushed from this block.
- Both callers (src/generators/steps/generateNarratives.js:46 and src/generators/generateSettlementPipeline.js:108 regenHistoryPipeline) pass a config/effectiveConfig that DOES carry the resolved roster (resolveResources.js writes effectiveConfig.nearbyResources at line 222), but that data lives on the `config` argument, not on the internally-built `context` object the resource block reads. The roster is never threaded onto context.

Runtime proof (read-only node against the real module, 5000 runs at metropolis tier WITH all four matching resources on config.nearbyResources):
{"The Iron Dispute":0,"The Great Harvest Compact":0,"The Great Construction":0,"The Arcane Incident":3963}
The three names unique to the dead block appear 0 times. "The Arcane Incident" appears, but at the SAME rate with empty resources (3699 vs 3963) because that name also exists in src/data/historyData.js:1311 as the EVENT_TYPE_NAMES label for the `magical_controversy` type, which fires via the normal weighted path — not from the resource block. This rules out coincidental coverage and isolates the dead code precisely.

Consequence: three settlement history events (iron/grain/stone resource anchors) are completely unreachable; the magical one only reaches users via an unrelated path. Resource selection (a real config dimension surfaced in ConfigurationPanel) has zero influence on which bespoke historical events appear. This is a genuine but contained simulation-coherence gap — no data loss, no crash, no determinism break (the code is deterministically inert). Hence low severity.
verdicts.fix_note: The keys the block checks (iron_deposits, iron_mine, grain_fields, fertile_floodplain, stone_quarry, magical_node) exactly match the resource keys resolveResources.js produces, so the gating logic itself is correct — only the data source is wrong. Simplest correct fix: in generateRelationshipEvent, source from the `config` argument it already receives rather than `context`, i.e. `const nearbyResources = config?.nearbyResources || [];` at line 916. This avoids any plumbing change since both call paths already pass the resolved config. The suggested fix (threading ctx.nearbyResources onto the context in buildHistoryContext / generateHistory) is also valid but touches more surface; if taken, set it in buildHistoryContext's return from its `config` param (buildHistoryContext already receives config) rather than re-plumbing the pipeline ctx. Caveat unrelated to the bug: even after fixing, the append at line 998 only fires when `events.length < 7`, so at high tiers (town/city/metropolis) with already-full timelines these events will often still be suppressed — fixing the data source is necessary but the events will be most visible at smaller tiers. Also note magicExists===false worlds should still suppress 'The Arcane Incident'; the existing block does NOT check config.magicExists for the magical_node branch (lines 978-996), unlike the categoryWeights path at line 804 — worth guarding in the same fix to avoid magical content leaking into no-magic worlds.

confirmed.title: generatePower passes tradeRoute=null — hostile-neighbour stability and 'Ongoing tensions with {neighbour}' recentConflict branches are unreachable
confirmed.evidence: generatePowerStructure(tier, economicState, null, {...}, institutions) is called with tradeRoute hard-coded null (steps/generatePower.js lines 32-36), with a comment claiming 'generatePowerStructure itself reads no neighbour bias'. That is false: powerGenerator.js lines 1819-1824 set stability 'Tense (external threat)' when `tradeRoute?.relationshipType` is hostile_rival/cold_war/tense, and line 1962-1963 sets recentConflict to `Ongoing tensions with ${tradeRoute.neighborName}`. With null both branches are dead in the pipeline (the sole generation entry point), so a hostile neighbour never surfaces in the power structure's stability or recent-conflict text, even though resolveNeighbour threads config.neighborRelationship for other consumers.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Pass a {relationshipType, neighborName} object derived from ctx.neighbourProfile as the third argument (or read effectiveConfig.neighborRelationship inside generatePowerStructure) instead of null.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the claim against the actual code; it holds.

CORE FACTS (all read directly):
- src/generators/steps/generatePower.js:32-36 is the SOLE production caller of generatePowerStructure and passes the 3rd arg (tradeRoute) as a literal `null`. Confirmed signature: src/generators/powerGenerator.js:597 `(tier, economicState, tradeRoute, config, institutions = [])`.
- powerGenerator.js:1819-1824 sets stability label 'Tense (external threat)' only when `tradeRoute?.relationshipType` is one of 'Hostile rival'/'hostile_rival'/'cold_war'/'Cold war'/'tense'. With tradeRoute=null this branch is dead.
- powerGenerator.js:1962-1963 sets recentConflict = `Ongoing tensions with ${tradeRoute.neighborName}` only when `tradeRoute` is truthy. With null it is dead.
- The generatePower.js:29-31 comment ("generatePowerStructure itself reads no neighbour bias") is factually false: the function reads tradeRoute.relationshipType and tradeRoute.neighborName.
- DECISIVE refutation attempt result: grep shows powerGenerator.js references `config.neighborRelationship` ZERO times. So the neighbour data that resolveNeighbour.js:53-56 threads into effectiveConfig.neighborRelationship ({neighborName, relationshipType}) never reaches these branches. resolveNeighbour's own H14(R3) comment lists the real consumers as getInstFlags (priorityHelpers.js:361) and generateHistory (historyGenerator.js:647) — NOT generatePowerStructure. So the neighbour IS threaded for those consumers but the power-structure stability/recentConflict text is left starved.
- generatePower is the only step providing powerStructure; generateSettlementPipeline (settlementSlice.js) is the sole live entry; legacy generateSettlement.js no longer exists. No live or test path passes a non-null tradeRoute.

Could not refute. The cited branches are genuinely unreachable in the pipeline, and a hostile neighbour never surfaces in the power structure's stability label or recent-conflict line even though the data is available on effectiveConfig.neighborRelationship.

SKEPTICAL CAVEAT (tempers but does not refute): the valid relationshipType value space (REL_DYNAMICS keys: neutral/allied/trade_partner/patron/client/rival/cold_war/hostile) does not include the literals 'hostile_rival' or 'tense' that the stability branch checks. So even if a tradeRoute object were threaded, only 'cold_war' would trigger the stability label; 'hostile'/'rival' would fall through. This means the suggested fix as written would only partially light up the stability branch. However, the recentConflict branch checks tradeRoute truthiness only, so it would correctly fire for any non-null neighbour. The reviewer's central claim — both branches are dead because null is passed — is correct.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix (pass {relationshipType, neighborName} from ctx.neighbourProfile, or read effectiveConfig.neighborRelationship inside generatePowerStructure) is directionally correct but has a value-space mismatch the reviewer missed: the stability branch at powerGenerator.js:1819-1824 checks for relationshipType 'hostile_rival' and 'tense', which are NOT valid relType values produced upstream (the real keys are neutral/allied/trade_partner/patron/client/rival/cold_war/hostile per REL_DYNAMICS). So threading the real value would only light up the 'cold_war' case; 'hostile' and 'rival' would still fall through. A correct fix must either (a) normalize the branch to test for the actual relType vocabulary (e.g. include 'hostile' and 'rival'), or (b) substring-match like the other consumers do (priorityHelpers.js:361, historyGenerator.js:647, institutionProbability.js:250 all use `.toLowerCase().includes(...)`). The recentConflict branch (truthiness on tradeRoute) needs no value-space change. Also worth verifying that lighting these branches does not alter deterministic snapshots for existing no-neighbour configs — since no-neighbour leaves the key absent, the default path stays identity, so determinism for the common case is preserved.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via reproduction. generatePower.js:32-36 (the sole power-generation entry point — generateSettlement.js legacy file no longer exists, and grep confirms generatePower.js:32 is the only caller of generatePowerStructure) calls generatePowerStructure(tier, economicState, null, {...effectiveConfig}, institutions) with the tradeRoute positional arg hard-coded null. The signature is generatePowerStructure(tier, economicState, tradeRoute, config, institutions) (powerGenerator.js:597).

Two branches inside the generator depend on tradeRoute:
- powerGenerator.js:1819-1824 sets stability `Me = 'Tense (external threat)'` when tradeRoute?.relationshipType is hostile_rival/cold_war/tense (and title-case variants).
- powerGenerator.js:1962-1963 sets recentConflict `We = \`Ongoing tensions with ${tradeRoute.neighborName}\``.
With tradeRoute === null both `tradeRoute?.relationshipType` and the `tradeRoute ?` test are falsy, so both branches are dead.

Both outputs (Me -> powerStructure.stability, We -> powerStructure.recentConflict at powerGenerator.js:2228-2229) are real, user-visible, sim-load-bearing fields: rendered in the on-screen dossier (components/new/PowerTab.jsx, SummaryTab.jsx:28/208), the PDF (pdf/sections/PowerStructure.jsx:61/94, Overview.jsx:65, pdf/lib/viewModel.js:360/481-482), and consumed by domain/simulationSpine.js:162/182, domain/historyBeats.js:291, pdf/lib/headlines.js:66.

Reproduced with a real node call against the live module: with tradeRoute=null and a hostile_rival neighbour, stability='Stable' and recentConflict is a generic merchant-levy line; with a hypothetical hostile tradeRoute object, stability='Tense (external threat)' and recentConflict='Ongoing tensions with Karvath'. So a hostile neighbour never produces the external-threat stability label or the named-neighbour conflict line.

The claim that 'generatePowerStructure itself reads no neighbour bias' (comment at generatePower.js:29-31) is literally false for these two branches.

CAVEAT on severity: this is dead code that has been dead since the project's first commit (e608542 — the legacy generateSettlement.js also passed null at line 802). It is NOT a regression. Crucially, hostile-neighbour effects DO surface elsewhere via config.neighborRelationship, which resolveNeighbour.js:52-57 threads into effectiveConfig: institutionProbability.js:253 (militarization), historyGenerator.js:649 (tensions/history), priorityHelpers.js:363/385 (military/economy score shifts), narrativeGenerator.js:764-765 (AI prose flag). So a hostile neighbour is far from invisible — it shapes institutions, history, scores, and prose. What's missing is specifically the power-structure stability LABEL and the named recentConflict text. This is real, coherence-relevant simulation-output drift (a hostile neighbour can leave power.stability='Stable' even while militarization and history reflect the threat), but it is a narrow missed enrichment rather than a broken main path — hence medium, not high.
verdicts.fix_note:
The right source for the missing data already exists: resolveNeighbour.js:52-57 writes ctx.effectiveConfig.neighborRelationship = { neighborName, relationshipType } whenever a neighbour is bound, and its shape (neighborName, relationshipType) exactly matches what the two dead branches read. So a fix can thread that object as the tradeRoute argument.

BUT do not naively pass effectiveConfig.neighborRelationship as tradeRoute for every neighbour. Critical gating mismatch between the two branches:
- The stability branch (powerGenerator.js:1819-1824) is gated on relationshipType being one of hostile_rival/cold_war/tense (and title-case variants) — safe.
- The recentConflict branch (powerGenerator.js:1962) fires on tradeRoute being merely TRUTHY, with NO relationship-type check. Passing neighborRelationship for a NEUTRAL/ALLIED/PATRON neighbour would emit 'Ongoing tensions with <name>' for a friendly neighbour — a new, worse bug, and it sits ahead of the criminal/crown/economy conflict branches so it would also suppress those.

Therefore the fix must construct/pass the tradeRoute object only when the relationship is actually adversarial — e.g. pass effectiveConfig.neighborRelationship only if its relationshipType matches the hostile set, otherwise null. Alternatively, fix the generator's line-1962 condition to also gate on a hostile relationshipType so the truthy-only test can't misfire. Also note the relationshipType string casing: resolveNeighbour stores relType from config._neighbourRelType/neighbourRelType verbatim; confirm the values produced upstream actually match the literals the branches compare against ('hostile_rival','cold_war','tense' etc.) — if upstream emits e.g. 'hostile' or 'Hostile rival' only, normalize before comparing (the stability branch already lists both 'Hostile rival' and 'hostile_rival' but not bare 'hostile').

Lastly, this should be covered by a determinism/parity test: a hostile-neighbour config should produce a 'Tense (external threat)'-class stability and a named recentConflict, asserted identically in the on-screen dossier and the PDF viewModel.

confirmed.title: Neighbour profile reads econ.prosperityLevel — field is `prosperity`, so neighbour economic strength is always 0.5 (identity multiplier)
confirmed.evidence: extractNeighbourProfile computes `economicStrength = PROSPERITY_RANK[econ.prosperityLevel] ?? 0.5` (line 136), but generated settlements store the label under economicState.prosperity (economicGenerator.js:2530; every other consumer — factionDynamics.js:77, powerGenerator.js:983 — reads `.prosperity`). The lookup always misses, economicStrength is always 0.5, and the consumer institutionProbability.js:242 multiplies market/guild institution chances by (0.8 + 0.5*0.4) = exactly 1.0 — a Wealthy neighbour and a Struggling neighbour have identical economic pull. Several PROSPERITY_RANK keys ('Modest', 'Affluent') also don't exist as produced labels.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Read `econ.prosperity` (with prosperityLevel as a legacy fallback) and align PROSPERITY_RANK keys with the actual label set (Struggling/Poor/Moderate/Comfortable/Prosperous/Wealthy).

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual code. neighbourGenerator.js:136 reads `PROSPERITY_RANK[econ.prosperityLevel] ?? 0.5`, but the field is never named `prosperityLevel` anywhere in the codebase — a grep for `prosperityLevel` across all of src/ returns exactly one hit: this buggy read itself (zero producers). The economicGenerator stores the prosperity label under `economicState.prosperity` (economicGenerator.js:2530: `prosperity: generateEconomicNarrative(...)`), and every other consumer reads `.prosperity` (factionDynamics.js:77, powerGenerator.js:983, :348). So `econ.prosperityLevel` is always undefined, the lookup always falls through to the `?? 0.5` default, and `economicStrength` is permanently 0.5.

The downstream impact is exactly as claimed: institutionProbability.js:242 computes `chance *= (ECONOMY_MODE_MARKET_MULT[...] ?? 1.0) * (0.8 + (profile.economicStrength || 0.5) * 0.4)`. With economicStrength fixed at 0.5, the economic-strength factor is `0.8 + 0.5*0.4 = 1.0` exactly — a perfect identity. A Wealthy neighbour and a Struggling neighbour exert identical economic pull on market/guild/merchant institution chances. The economyMode multiplier (relationship-based) still varies, but the neighbour's own economic strength contributes nothing.

The phantom-keys sub-claim also checks out: the produced label set from generateEconomicNarrative (economicGenerator.js:1158 LABELS) is ['Struggling','Poor','Moderate','Comfortable','Prosperous','Wealthy']. PROSPERITY_RANK's 'Modest', 'Affluent', and 'Subsistence' keys are never produced as final labels. (Note: 'Struggling' actually IS both in the table and produced, so the report's parenthetical example list is slightly imprecise, but its substance — keys that don't match the real label set — holds.)

Reachability is real: extractNeighbourProfile is called from the live pipeline step resolveNeighbour.js:36 whenever a neighbour is bound via config._importedNeighbor (the cross-settlement linking feature), and the profile flows into institutionProbability where line 242 fires for any economy/market/guild/merchant institution. Not dead code, no upstream guard rescues it, no comment indicates intent — the surrounding comments in fact describe the economyMode wiring as deliberate while silently leaving this broken.

This is a determinism-preserving (deterministically wrong, not flaky) simulation-fidelity defect: a config input (neighbour prosperity) that should influence institution generation has zero effect. Wrong simulation output on an edge feature path = medium.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct in direction: read `econ.prosperity` as primary with `econ.prosperityLevel` as a fallback only if any legacy producer exists (none currently does, so the fallback is harmless but unnecessary). Align PROSPERITY_RANK to the exact produced label set ['Struggling','Poor','Moderate','Comfortable','Prosperous','Wealthy']. Note 'Subsistence'/'Modest'/'Affluent'/'Thriving' are not final labels emitted by generateEconomicNarrative (powerGenerator does reference a 'Thriving' label elsewhere, so worth confirming the canonical set before hardcoding). The 'Struggling' key already matches and need not be removed. Adding a regression test that a Wealthy neighbour yields economicStrength > a Struggling neighbour (and that institutionProbability differs between them) would lock this down, since the current behavior produces no observable difference and so no existing snapshot test would have caught it.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via end-to-end trace + runtime demonstration.

FAILURE PATH:
1. A neighbour settlement is bound (config._importedNeighbor / importedNeighbour) and flows through steps/resolveNeighbour.js:36 -> extractNeighbourProfile(rawNeighbour, relType).
2. extractNeighbourProfile (neighbourGenerator.js:136) computes `economicStrength = PROSPERITY_RANK[econ.prosperityLevel] ?? 0.5`. The field `econ.prosperityLevel` does not exist on any generated settlement. The producer stores the label under economicState.prosperity (economicGenerator.js:2530, value = generateEconomicNarrative(...) which returns a bare label from ['Struggling','Poor','Moderate','Comfortable','Prosperous','Wealthy'], confirmed at economicGenerator.js:1158/1198). Every other consumer reads `.prosperity` (factionDynamics.js:77, powerGenerator.js:983/348/352). grep confirms `prosperityLevel` appears nowhere in src/ or supabase/ except this one buggy read.
3. So economicStrength is ALWAYS 0.5 (the `?? 0.5` fallback).
4. economicStrength's only consumer is institutionProbability.js:242: `chance *= (ECONOMY_MODE_MARKET_MULT[dyn.economyMode] ?? 1.0) * (0.8 + (profile.economicStrength || 0.5) * 0.4)`. With 0.5 the factor is 0.8 + 0.5*0.4 = exactly 1.0 (identity), so the neighbour's wealth contributes nothing to market/guild/merchant institution odds. I verified getNeighbourEconomicBias (neighbourGenerator.js:207) does NOT read economicStrength, so :242 is the sole sink.

RUNTIME PROOF (node against the real module): a neighbour with economicState.prosperity='Wealthy' and one with 'Struggling' both yield economicStrength=0.5 and market mult=1.0 (identical). If the read were corrected to econ.prosperity, the multipliers would be 1.14 (Wealthy) vs 0.92 (Struggling) — a real ~24% spread that is currently flattened to nothing.

CONSEQUENCE: data-level simulation error. When a neighbour is linked (cross-settlement feature), the neighbour's economic strength has zero influence on the new settlement's market/guild/merchant institution probabilities — a Wealthy and a Struggling neighbour produce identical economic institutional pull. The output is deterministically wrong, not random, so determinism is preserved, but the simulation no longer reflects neighbour prosperity through this axis. Note the trace receipt in resolveNeighbour.js:84 may even advertise a "market odds" shift for non-identity economyModes, while the economicStrength contribution it implies is silently dead.

SEVERITY: medium — wrong simulation output on a real but optional/narrow path (requires a bound neighbour AND an economy/market/guild/merchant institution being scored). No crash, money, or security impact; main settlement generation without a neighbour is unaffected (extractNeighbourProfile returns null when no rawNeighbour).
verdicts.fix_note: Core fix is correct: change `econ.prosperityLevel` to `econ.prosperity` at neighbourGenerator.js:136. All six labels actually produced by generateEconomicNarrative (Struggling, Poor, Moderate, Comfortable, Prosperous, Wealthy) ARE present as PROSPERITY_RANK keys, so the corrected read resolves for every real value. Minor accuracy corrections to the report's claim: (1) 'Struggling' DOES exist in PROSPERITY_RANK (0.3) — only 'Modest' (0.45) and 'Affluent' (1.0) are dead/unused keys (and 'Subsistence' 0.05 is never emitted by generateEconomicNarrative, which floors Subsistence inputs to Struggling/Poor). These dead keys are harmless. (2) powerGenerator references a 'Thriving' label that is NOT a PROSPERITY_RANK key, but generateEconomicNarrative never emits 'Thriving', so it is not a live gap for this consumer; still, if 'Thriving' can be produced by any other economic path, add it to PROSPERITY_RANK (~0.9) to avoid the 0.5 fallback. Recommend a property test asserting extractNeighbourProfile(...).economicStrength varies across the six real prosperity labels (currently it cannot, which is why no test caught this).

confirmed.title: NPC honorific titles fall back to raw lookup keys — every thorp Elder displays title "elder", shepherds display "owner"
confirmed.evidence: pickLast returns `titles[s] || s` (lines 197-201). NAMING_DATA's titles maps (all 11 cultures, 27 keys each) lack the keys 'elder','owner','agent','contact','warden','advisor','steward','knight','noble','druid','bard' — but 31 of the 142 roles in HISTORY_EVENTS (src/data/historyData.js) plus all NOBLE_ROLES use exactly those keys (verified programmatically). The raw key becomes npc.title and is rendered as the NPC's honorific on-screen (components/new/npcComponents.jsx:192) and in the PDF (pdf/sections/NotableNPCs.jsx:288-290). Confirmed empirically: every generated thorp shows 'Elder title="elder"' and 'Shepherd title="owner"' instead of cultural titles like 'Ältester'. Affected roles include the mandatory Elder (every thorp/hamlet), Miller, Shepherd, Innkeeper, Tavern Owner, Prison Warden, Druid Elder, and all noble roles.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add the missing title keys to each culture's titles map (or remap those roles to existing keys); optionally make pickLast fall back to a capitalized English default rather than the raw key.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the full chain with file:line evidence and an empirical end-to-end run.

1) pickLast (src/generators/npcGenerator.js:197-201) returns ((NAMING_DATA[culture]||germanic).titles)?.[s] || s — i.e. the raw key when the title map lacks key s. Mechanism is exactly as claimed.

2) The title is set in generateSingleNPC: line 63 `const lastName = pickLast(culture, namingTier || culture)` and line 77 `title: lastName`. The `namingTier` arg is the candidate's `title` key, passed at lines 1495 and 1523 (`generateSingleNPC(candidate.role, candidate.title, ...)`). So a candidate's `title` string is looked up in the cultural titles map and, if missing, leaks raw into npc.title. (Note: the title is NOT set via the line-212 filterByGuild path as a casual read might suggest — that only affects guild masters — but the reviewer's mechanism and conclusion are still correct.)

3) Candidate title keys come from HISTORY_EVENTS (src/data/historyData.js) via getUpgradeOpportunities (src/generators/economicGenerator.js:1505). Confirmed: Elder has title:'elder' (historyData.js:908), Shepherd/Miller/Innkeeper/Tavern Owner have title:'owner' (lines 938/900/923/858), Druid Elder/Prison Warden present, and noble roles use 'noble'/'advisor'/'steward'/'knight'/'agent'. The 'other' category (which holds Elder/Shepherd/Miller etc.) is always pushed (economicGenerator.js:1531-1534, `category === 'other' ||`), so the mandatory thorp/hamlet Elder candidate is found at line 1493.

4) Programmatic check of NAMING_DATA: all 11 cultures with titles (germanic, latin, celtic, arabic, norse, slavic, east_asian, mesoamerican, south_asian, steppe, greek) lack EVERY key elder/owner/agent/contact/warden/advisor/steward/knight/noble/druid/bard (presentCount 0, missingCount 11 for each).

5) No downstream transformation of npc.title exists in the pipeline steps; it renders verbatim on-screen (src/components/new/npcComponents.jsx:192 `{npc.title}`) and in the PDF (src/pdf/sections/NotableNPCs.jsx:129/288-290/334).

6) Empirical run of generateNPCs for a thorp produced: Elder => title:"elder", Shepherd => title:"owner", while Wounded Soldier (title key 'guard', which exists) correctly produced "Hauptmann". A city run leaked raw keys for Knight/Dame=>knight, Noble Heir=>noble, House Steward=>steward, Court Advisor=>advisor, Debt Collector=>agent, Travelling Performer=>bard, etc.

I could not refute the claim. Every element holds. The cited line 197 is precisely where pickLast begins.
verdicts.fix_note: The reviewer's mechanism is right but the exact wiring is slightly different from what the evidence text implies: npc.title is set in generateSingleNPC via `title: lastName` where `lastName = pickLast(culture, namingTier)` (npcGenerator.js:63,77) and namingTier is candidate.title (passed at lines 1495 and 1523). The line-212 filterByGuild assignment only affects guild-master NPCs. So a fix that only touches line 212/pickLast's guild path would miss the main leak — the real fix must address the title map keys (or the generateSingleNPC title assignment). Suggested fix is otherwise correct: add the 11 missing keys (elder, owner, agent, contact, warden, advisor, steward, knight, noble, druid, bard) to each culture's titles map, and/or have pickLast fall back to a capitalized English default (e.g. titleCase(s)) instead of the raw lowercase key so future unmapped keys degrade gracefully rather than showing bare lowercase strings. Note Widowed Farmer also maps to 'elder' and several roles map to 'agent', so the impact is broader than the listed examples.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL end-to-end. Trace verified against real modules with a seeded run.

Path: generateNPCs (npcGenerator.js:1234) -> candidate pool from getUpgradeOpportunities (economicGenerator.js:1505) which spreads HISTORY_EVENTS roles (historyData.js), each carrying a `title` LOOKUP KEY (e.g. Elder has title:"elder" at historyData.js:907-908, Shepherd/Miller/Innkeeper have title:"owner" at 900/922/938). For mandatory and fill NPCs, generateSingleNPC(candidate.role, candidate.title, ...) is called (npcGenerator.js:1495, 1523), passing the title key as the `namingTier` arg. Inside generateSingleNPC, line 63 calls pickLast(culture, namingTier) and the result is stored as npc.title (line 77). pickLast (line 197-201) does `titles[s] || s`; since no culture's titles map contains keys like 'elder'/'owner', it returns the raw key string.

Empirical proof: a seeded thorp (createPRNG(12345)) produced Elder->title:"elder", Shepherd->title:"owner", Fisherman->title:"agent" — raw lookup keys, not localized honorifics.

Programmatic cross-check: 11 keys missing identically from ALL 11 culture titles maps: advisor, agent, bard, contact, druid, elder, knight, noble, owner, steward, warden. 31 of 142 HISTORY_EVENTS roles use a missing key, PLUS all NOBLE_ROLES (noble/advisor/steward/agent/knight) and the Guild Warden/crafts roles. The mandatory Elder appears in EVERY thorp and hamlet (TIER_MANDATORY_ROLES, npcGenerator.js:1457-1458), so this fires on the main path for the two smallest tiers, plus widely elsewhere.

Render sites confirmed: on-screen npcComponents.jsx:192 renders {npc.title} verbatim; PDF NotableNPCs.jsx:288-290 renders `· {npc.title}` verbatim. Both wrong identically (so not a parity drift, but a localization/data-integrity defect visible to the user in the dossier and PDF).

Severity: high. It is not data loss/money/security/crash, so not critical. But it is wrong, user-visible simulation output (cultural localization is a core product promise — coherent, culturally-flavored settlements) that affects every thorp/hamlet and ~22% of all NPC roles across every culture. It degrades the dossier by surfacing internal lookup keys. Determinism is intact (stable bug), and the app does not crash.

Confidence ~0.97: a skeptical senior reviewer with full code access would agree, given the reproduced seeded output and the exhaustive key-gap analysis.
verdicts.fix_note: Correct fix is a DATA fix, not a logic change to pickLast (the `|| s` fallback is reasonable defensive behavior). Add the 11 missing keys (advisor, agent, bard, contact, druid, elder, knight, noble, owner, steward, warden) to the `titles` map of ALL 11 cultures in src/data/namingData.js, with culturally-appropriate translations (e.g. germanic elder->"Ältester", owner->"Inhaber"/"Wirt", warden->"Wächter", etc.). Note that 'owner' is overloaded across roles (Innkeeper, Tavern Owner, Shepherd, Miller, Retired Adventurer) so its single translation must read sensibly as a generic honorific for all of them — consider whether some of those historyData roles should instead point at more specific existing keys (e.g. Innkeeper/Tavern Owner -> a new 'innkeeper' key; Miller/Shepherd -> 'master' or a new 'goodman' key) rather than all sharing 'owner'. Two robustness add-ons worth doing alongside the data fix: (1) add a validate:data check (or a unit test) asserting every title key referenced in HISTORY_EVENTS / NOBLE_ROLES / CRAFTS_ROLES exists in every culture's titles map, so this class can't silently regress; (2) optionally have pickLast log/throw in dev when it falls back, to catch future gaps. Casing matches: all map keys and role.title values are lowercase snake_case, so lookups align — no normalization needed.

confirmed.title: Mandatory NPC roles 'Fisher', 'Woodcutter', and 'Merchant Guild Master' don't exist in the role catalog — silently skipped
confirmed.evidence: thorpSecondRole returns 'Fisher' for fishing/port/coastal/river thorps and 'Woodcutter' for forest/isolated ones (lines 1442-1453); famine's STRESS_MANDATORY_ROLES includes 'Merchant Guild Master' (line 1468). The mandatory loop resolves roles via `candidates.find(c => c.role === role)` (line 1493) against HISTORY_EVENTS, which contains 'Fisherman' (historyData.js:1007) but no 'Fisher', no 'Woodcutter' at all, and 'Guild Master' but no 'Merchant Guild Master' (verified programmatically). The find misses and the intended archetype NPC never spawns — confirmed empirically: four coastal/port thorp generations produced Elder + random fill (Blacksmith, Traveling Merchant, ...) and never a fisher.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Rename the mandatory strings to match catalog roles ('Fisherman', 'Guild Master') and add a Woodcutter role to HISTORY_EVENTS.other.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed real and reproduced empirically. The mandatory-role loop in src/generators/npcGenerator.js:1492-1497 resolves each requested role via `candidates.find(c => c.role === role)`. The `candidates` pool is built from getUpgradeOpportunities() (economicGenerator.js:1505, which iterates HISTORY_EVENTS) plus the injected NOBLE_ROLES/CRAFTS_ROLES (npcGenerator.js:1409-1434). I verified programmatically that none of these sources contains a role named 'Fisher' (only 'Fisherman' exists), 'Woodcutter' (absent entirely), or 'Merchant Guild Master' (only 'Guild Master' exists). Therefore the three mandatory strings produced at lines 1446/1449/1452 ('Fisher'), 1447/1450 ('Woodcutter') and 1468 ('Merchant Guild Master') always miss the find and silently spawn nothing.

Empirical reproduction: 8 coastal/port thorps yielded a fisher 0/8 times (neither 'Fisher' nor the real 'Fisherman' appeared); 6 forest/isolated thorps yielded a woodcutter 0/6; 6 famine villages yielded 'Merchant Guild Master' 0/6. The matching-string control 'Elder' appeared in every thorp run, confirming the mandatory mechanism works only when the string matches the catalog.
