Notably 'Fisherman' is an 'other'-category role with minTier 'thorp' and no requiresPort gate, so it IS a valid candidate that *could* be guaranteed — the rename would actually fix coastal coherence, not just rename a no-op.

The EVIDENCE's data-source description is slightly loose (candidates come through getUpgradeOpportunities, plus the noble/crafts injections, not a bare HISTORY_EVENTS lookup), but the conclusion is exactly correct and the suggested fix direction is sound.

Severity is low, not higher: this degrades simulation coherence (a coastal thorp may lack a fisher) but is not a crash, data loss, billing/security issue, or determinism break — generation stays deterministic, just with a wrong/missing archetype, and the slot is back-filled by random candidates. The famine 'Merchant Guild Master' miss is additionally masked because a guild-master NPC is added separately via filterByGuild (lines 1499-1502), so that case is largely cosmetic.
verdicts.fix_note: Rename mandatory strings to match the catalog: 'Fisher' -> 'Fisherman' (lines 1446, 1449, 1452) and 'Merchant Guild Master' -> 'Guild Master' (line 1468). For 'Woodcutter' there is no equivalent catalog role, so it must be ADDED to HISTORY_EVENTS.other in src/data/historyData.js with minTier:'thorp' (mirroring the existing Miller/Shepherd/Fisherman 'other' entries) before lines 1447/1450 will resolve. Note 'Guild Master' requiresGuild:true and minTier:'village', so it will only resolve for famine settlements that are village+ with a guild institution — acceptable since famine NPC sets target larger tiers, but worth confirming.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED — reproduced end-to-end against the real modules.

Failure path: generateNPCs (npcGenerator.js:1234) builds `candidates` from getUpgradeOpportunities (economicGenerator.js:1505), which only emits role objects whose `.role` string comes verbatim from HISTORY_EVENTS (plus the inlined NOBLE_ROLES/CRAFTS_ROLES). thorpSecondRole (lines 1442-1454) and STRESS_MANDATORY_ROLES (lines 1466-1477) feed `mandatoryRoles`, which the loop at line 1492 resolves via `candidates.find(c => c.role === role)`. The guard at line 1494 (`if (candidate && npcs.length < targetCount)`) silently no-ops when find returns undefined.

Three emitted role strings have no matching catalog entry (verified programmatically):
- 'Fisher' (lines 1446/1449/1452) — catalog has 'Fisherman' (historyData.js:1007), not 'Fisher'.
- 'Woodcutter' (lines 1447/1450) — no woodcutter/lumberjack/forester role exists anywhere in HISTORY_EVENTS (only forest-adjacent role is 'Scout/Ranger').
- 'Merchant Guild Master' (line 1468, famine) — catalog has 'Guild Master' (historyData.js:526), not 'Merchant Guild Master'.

Empirical reproduction (real generateNPCs):
- PLAINS thorp -> 'Shepherd' (name matches catalog): present 20/20. This control proves the mandatory mechanism works correctly when the name matches.
- FOREST thorp -> 'Woodcutter': 0/20.
- COAST/port thorp -> role=='Fisher': 0/20 (a 'Fisherman' occasionally appears, but only via the weighted-random fill at lines 1509-1526, not the mandatory loop — absent in most generations).
- FAMINE village -> 'Healer' (valid mandatory name): 20/20; 'Merchant Guild Master': 0/20. Again the control (Healer) proves the famine path is live and the failure is isolated to the bad name.

Data-level consequence: an intended coherence guarantee is silently void. Coastal/port/river thorps are not guaranteed a fisher archetype; forest/isolated thorps never get a woodcutter; famine settlements never get the guaranteed merchant-guild-master NPC. No crash, no error — the slot is dropped and may be backfilled by a generic random NPC. This is wrong simulation output (a deliberate tier/stress invariant fails to hold), which fits the product's 'coherent simulation / data integrity' concern. Severity medium: it degrades NPC coherence on a real, common path (port/coastal/forest thorps and famine settlements) but causes no data loss, crash, money, or security impact, and a plausible substitute NPC still fills the slot.

The EVIDENCE's only imprecision: the find runs against the `candidates` pool, not HISTORY_EVENTS directly — but that pool is derived from HISTORY_EVENTS, so the conclusion stands.
verdicts.fix_note:
The suggested rename is correct but incomplete — there are three distinct sub-cases:

1. 'Fisher' -> 'Fisherman' (lines 1446, 1449, 1452): clean fix. 'Fisherman' is category 'other' (always passes the category gate at economicGenerator.js:1531), minTier 'thorp', no port/guild gate, so it will reliably resolve for any coastal/port/river thorp once renamed.

2. 'Merchant Guild Master' -> 'Guild Master' (line 1468): correct target name, BUT 'Guild Master' has minTier:'village' and requiresGuild:true. So even after the rename, a famine settlement below village tier, or without a guild institution, will still silently skip it (the candidate won't be in the pool). The rename fixes the name mismatch; if the design intent is a guaranteed guild-master in every famine settlement regardless of guild presence/tier, that gating also needs addressing (or accept the gate as intended). At minimum the rename is required.

3. 'Woodcutter' (lines 1447, 1450): there is NO equivalent role anywhere in HISTORY_EVENTS — a rename has no valid target. Options: (a) add a 'Woodcutter' role to HISTORY_EVENTS (category 'other', minTier 'thorp', mirroring Fisherman/Shepherd/Miller), or (b) remap the branch to an existing role (e.g. 'Scout/Ranger', though that is a different archetype). Without one of these, the forest/isolated-thorp second role stays broken.

Defensive hardening worth considering: the silent miss at line 1494 hides this class of bug. A dev-mode assertion/warn when a mandatory role fails to resolve (candidate === undefined) would surface future name drift between the mandatory lists and the catalog. A data-validation test asserting every string in TIER_MANDATORY_ROLES/STRESS_MANDATORY_ROLES exists as a role somewhere in the candidate catalog would catch this in the `npm run check` gate.

confirmed.title: assembleSettlement legitimacy patch re-labels publicLegitimacy and multipliers without re-applying them to faction powers or relationships
confirmed.evidence: Faction powers are multiplied by the PROVISIONAL legitimacy multipliers during generatePower (powerGenerator.js:2184-2187 → factionDynamics.applyLegitimacyMultipliers), using a defense label guessed from institution presence. assembleSettlement then patches publicLegitimacy with the real defenseProfile readiness (lines 110-133): it rewrites score, label, govMultiplier/crimMultiplier and the is* flags — the provisional vs real defense contribution can differ by up to ~17 points (provisional 'Well-Defended' +7 vs real 'Undefended' -10), enough to cross bands (e.g. Tolerated→Contested). But faction powers keep the provisional multipliers, factionRelationships keep narratives/directions derived from the provisional legitimacy flags, and the governing faction's legitimacyCrisis tag is never added/removed. The dossier can therefore show 'Legitimacy Crisis, gov ×0.60' beside faction powers computed at ×1.0.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Either compute defenseProfile before generatePower (it only needs institutions/config/economicState), or re-run applyLegitimacyMultipliers + computeFactionRelationships against the patched legitimacy when the band changes.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed the defect by reading the full evidence chain.

PROVISIONAL PASS (powerGenerator.js:2173-2214): a defense label is guessed from institution name presence (_provDefLabel, lines 2173-2182), fed to computePublicLegitimacy, then applyLegitimacyMultipliers(p, publicLegitimacy, tier) MUTATES faction.power in place using publicLeg.govMultiplier/crimMultiplier (factionDynamics.js:482-500), tags the governing faction with f.legitimacyCrisis + f.crisisNote when publicLeg.governanceFractured (lines 485-488), and sets f.rawPower/f.powerLabel. computeFactionRelationships(p, ...) then derives relationship type/direction/narrative from the multiplier-adjusted .power ratios (e.g. mil.power/gov.power at lines 260,288,311) and from the provisional publicLeg flags.

PATCH (assembleSettlement.js:110-133): after generateDefenseProfile, it rewrites ONLY provLeg.score, label, color, govMultiplier, crimMultiplier and the is*/governanceFractured flags from the REAL defenseProfile.readiness.label. It never re-runs applyLegitimacyMultipliers or computeFactionRelationships. The only callers of those two functions anywhere in src/ are in powerGenerator.js (verified via grep). No post-patch step (generateCoherence, ensureFactionStructuralNpcs, normalizeSettlement) re-derives powers — generateCoherence actually READS the stale crimeFaction.power (narrativeGenerator.js:794,851).

MAGNITUDE: DEFENSE_CONTRIB (factionDynamics.js:46-54) Well-Defended=+7, Undefended=-10. Tier-scaled by defScale (city 1.0, town 0.85). Provisional Well-Defended vs real Undefended = 17 pts (city) / 15 pts (town) — enough to cross legitimacy bands at the 30/45 boundaries (e.g. Contested↔Legitimacy Crisis). The defense profile genuinely differs because the provisional label is a name-based heuristic while the real one comes from computeDefenseReadiness scores (defenseGenerator.js:512-517).

RENDERED DIVERGENCE: PowerTab.jsx (182-224) and PDF PowerStructure.jsx (279-286) render f.power, f.powerLabel, f.legitimacyCrisis ⚠, and f.crisisNote (all provisional) directly beside the patched legitimacy badge (govMultiplier). So a dossier can show 'Legitimacy Crisis, gov ×0.60' next to powers computed at ×1.0 — exactly the described symptom. Additionally, because the patch updates the BADGE's governanceFractured/isLegitimacyCrisis but the faction-level f.legitimacyCrisis tag was set during the provisional pass, the two can diverge in BOTH directions (badge crisis with no tagged faction, or stale-on faction tag after the badge leaves crisis).

No tests assert this behavior (grep found none), and the nearby comments (powerGenerator.js:2160-2162) explicitly acknowledge defenseProfile is added later and the legitimacy uses a provisional label — but the comment only anticipates the SCORE being added/patched, not that multipliers/relationships/crisis tags would go stale. The suggested fix (compute defenseProfile before generatePower, or re-run applyLegitimacyMultipliers + computeFactionRelationships on band change) is sound. Re-running needs care: applyLegitimacyMultipliers mutates f.power from f.power (not f.rawPower), so a naive re-run would double-apply — it should reset to rawPower first or be made idempotent.

Severity medium is honest: incoherent simulation output (violates the product's internal-coherence goal) and parity-adjacent, but edge-case (only when provisional vs real defense crosses a band) and not data loss/security/crash.
verdicts.fix_note: The "re-run applyLegitimacyMultipliers" option needs an idempotency guard: applyLegitimacyMultipliers computes f.power = round(f.power * mult) from the CURRENT f.power, not f.rawPower (factionDynamics.js:499-500), so a second invocation would multiply the already-multiplied value. Before re-running, restore each faction's f.power = f.rawPower (and clear stale f.legitimacyCrisis/f.crisisNote), or refactor the function to base the multiply on rawPower. Also note the divergence is bidirectional: the patch updates only the badge's governanceFractured/isLegitimacyCrisis flags, so the faction-level f.legitimacyCrisis tag (set in the provisional pass) can be both falsely-present (band left crisis after patch) and falsely-absent (band entered crisis after patch). The cleanest fix is the orchestration option: compute defenseProfile before generatePower so the real readiness label feeds computePublicLegitimacy the first time — defenseProfile only needs institutions/config/economicState, all available pre-power.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via end-to-end trace and a real-module reproduction.

Failure path:
1. generatePower step (src/generators/powerGenerator.js:2173-2214) computes legitimacy from a PROVISIONAL defense label derived purely from institution NAMES (_hasWalls && _hasGarrison => 'Well-Defended', etc.). It then bakes that provisional legitimacy into the faction array and relationships:
   - applyLegitimacyMultipliers (factionDynamics.js:469-508) mutates each faction's power/rawPower/powerLabel by govMultiplier/crimMultiplier AND stamps f.legitimacyCrisis + f.crisisNote on the governing faction (lines 482-488) when publicLeg.governanceFractured.
   - computeFactionRelationships (factionDynamics.js:222) sets relationship `direction` directly from publicLeg flags at lines 358-359 (isLegitimacyCrisis/isEndorsed) and 406 (isContested/isLegitimacyCrisis), and computes ratios from the already-multiplied powers.
2. Later, assembleSettlement.js:107 computes the REAL defenseProfile via generateDefenseProfile, whose readiness label (defenseGenerator.js:483-518) is an average of 5 score dimensions minus a threat penalty — completely independent of the institution-name heuristic, and can diverge by a full band or more.
3. The patch at assembleSettlement.js:110-133 rewrites ONLY settlement.powerStructure.publicLegitimacy (score, label, color, govMultiplier, crimMultiplier, all is* flags, governanceFractured). It does NOT re-run applyLegitimacyMultipliers (faction powers/labels stay at provisional multipliers), does NOT add/remove the governing faction's legitimacyCrisis tag + crisisNote, and does NOT recompute factionRelationships (directions stay derived from the stale flags).

Reproduction (real modules, /tmp/repro_leg3.mjs): tier=city, prosperity Thriving, safety Unsafe, food Famine, provisional 'Well-Defended' (+7) vs real 'Undefended' (-10), delta -17:
- Provisional band = Contested (score 39): gov power baked at gov×0.80 = 24; crim at crim×1.15; governing.legitimacyCrisis=false, crisisNote ABSENT; gov<->crim direction='stable'.
- Patched publicLegitimacy = 'Legitimacy Crisis', score 22, gov×0.60, governanceFractured=true.
Result: the dossier (src/components/new/tabs/PowerTab.jsx) renders leg.label='Legitimacy Crisis' (line 82) beside a governing faction power computed at gov×0.80 (line 130/200), with NO ⚠ crisis flag and NO crisisNote (lines 189, 223-224) because f.legitimacyCrisis is stale-false, and the gov<->crim relationship shows direction 'stable' instead of the 'escalating' the Crisis band implies. The same divergent fields flow into the PDF via src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js (legitimacy line 483, governanceFractured 485, faction power/powerLabel/crisisNote 435/437/442), so on-screen and PDF are wrong identically — an internal-coherence defect, not mere parity drift.

Reachability: a city with a wall + garrison institution (=> provisional 'Well-Defended') but low monster/internal/economic/magical defense scores and a plagued/frontier threat penalty legitimately computes a low-band readiness (Undefended/Vulnerable). This is exactly the scenario the in-file comment at assembleSettlement.js:31-32 acknowledges. The band-crossing also flips relationship directions and the criminal multiplier even when it does not reach the Crisis tag.

Why high (not critical): no data loss, money, security, or crash — it is wrong/incoherent SIMULATION OUTPUT visible to the user (the product's core 'internally-coherent settlement' promise). It violates coherence and is also a soft determinism/consistency concern, but the settlement still generates.
verdicts.fix_note:
The suggested fix direction is correct but must be more than patching publicLegitimacy. The clean fix is to defer the entire legitimacy/dynamics computation until the real defense label is known, OR re-run it in assembleSettlement after generateDefenseProfile: recompute publicLegitimacy = computePublicLegitimacy(economicState, realDefLabel, tier), then re-run applyLegitimacyMultipliers(factions, publicLegitimacy, tier) (note it reads f.rawPower-free — it sets rawPower=f.power, so re-running on already-multiplied powers would double-apply; it must reset f.power from f.rawPower first, or be made idempotent), re-add/remove f.legitimacyCrisis+crisisNote on the governing faction, and recompute factionRelationships with the new publicLegitimacy. Cleanest is to thread realDefLabel back so powerGenerator's dynamics run once with the correct label, eliminating the provisional path entirely.

Also note a SECONDARY bug in the current patch itself (confirmed in /tmp/repro_tier.mjs): line 113 uses the RAW DEFENSE_CONTRIB[realDefLabel], but provLeg.breakdown.defense was stored tier-SCALED (factionDynamics.js:97: Math.round(DEFENSE_CONTRIB[label]*defScale)). So at sub-city tiers the patched score is over-corrected — e.g. village Defensible->Vulnerable applies delta -5 instead of the correct tier-scaled -3 (2-point error; larger for bigger bands). Any real fix must apply tier scaling (defScale) to the real defense contribution, ideally by calling computePublicLegitimacy with the real label rather than hand-rolling the delta.

confirmed.title: Faction rank-comparison narratives are baked before legitimacy multipliers re-scale and re-sort powers — descriptions can contradict final standings
confirmed.evidence: The IIFE at lines 1561-1802 appends positional prose to faction descs based on the normalized pre-legitimacy ordering ('The gap between them and the governing council is narrow', 'The craft guilds currently outweigh them in raw political numbers', etc., keyed on Ct===1/2 positions and power deltas). applyLegitimacyMultipliers is applied AFTERWARD (line 2187) and multiplies governing power by 0.60-1.30 and criminal by 0.75-1.30, then re-sorts the array by effective power (factionDynamics.js:511). Whenever legitimacy is outside the Tolerated band (score <45 or >=60 — common), the baked comparisons and rank phrasing can invert relative to the powers and ordering the dossier actually displays.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Move the rank-narrative pass after applyLegitimacyMultipliers (it only reads the factions array), or compute it from rawPower consistently and label it as such.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the full chain; the claim holds at every link.

1) Narrative baked pre-legitimacy: The IIFE at powerGenerator.js:1561-1802 runs immediately after the normalization+pre-legitimacy sort (line 1556-1560). That sort pins isGoverning first, then orders by power: `p.sort((N, ye) => (N.isGoverning ? -1 : ye.isGoverning ? 1 : ye.power - N.power))`. The IIFE reads p[0]/p[1] (he/De/Mi/cr at 1570-1574), positional rank (Ct===1 at 1594, Ct===2 at 1613), and pairwise pre-legitimacy `.power` comparisons (e.g. craft-vs-merchant `Se.power > at` 1639, military-vs-thieves `Zt.power > at+10` 1669, criminal-vs-merchant `Rt.power > at` 1698). It appends that prose to `qt.desc` at line 1800.

2) Re-scale + re-sort happen AFTER: applyLegitimacyMultipliers(p, publicLegitimacy, tier) runs at line 2187. In factionDynamics.js:469-511 it multiplies governing power by govMultiplier (0.60-1.30, line 111-116) and criminal by crimMultiplier (0.75-1.30, line 119-124), preserves rawPower, recomputes powerLabel from the NEW power (503-507), then re-sorts by pure effective power `factions.sort((a,b)=>(b.power||0)-(a.power||0))` (511) — with NO isGoverning pin, so the governing entry is no longer guaranteed at position 0. No narrative regeneration or governing-first re-pin occurs after this (confirmed lines 2187-2234).

3) The no-op band is narrow/uncommon: only the Tolerated band (score 45-59, factionDynamics.js:106/136) yields govMultiplier=1.00 AND crimMultiplier=1.00. Endorsed (>=75), Approved (60-74), Contested (30-44), and Crisis (<30) all rescale, and these are common given score = 50 + prosperity/safety/defense/food contributions (line 100).

4) The contradiction reaches the displayed dossier and PDF: pdf/lib/viewModel.js:440 sets `description: f?.description || f?.desc || null` — pulling the mutated desc prose into the rendered description — and line 448 re-sorts the rendered list by post-legitimacy `b.power - a.power`, while line 452 builds the power distribution from post-legitimacy f.power. PowerStructure.jsx renders f.power (273/277), f.powerLabel (279), and the description blurb (299) in that post-legitimacy order (134). So the displayed numbers, labels, and ordering are post-legitimacy while the prose ("the gap between them and the governing council is narrow", "the craft guilds currently outweigh them", "the criminal network has more operational reach right now") is pre-legitimacy and can flatly invert.

The most acute case is the governing vs criminal pairing: govMultiplier can be 0.60 while crimMultiplier is 1.30, and the post-multiplier sort drops the governing-first pin, so the very "the governing council is at the top / narrow gap behind them" framing the prose is keyed on (he = p[0], lines 1597-1612) can be wrong against the displayed standings.

This is a prose/coherence defect only — no determinism break (still seed-deterministic), no data loss, no security/billing impact, no crash. Low severity is honest.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound and low-risk. Moving the entire IIFE (powerGenerator.js:1561-1802) to run AFTER applyLegitimacyMultipliers (line 2187) is the cleanest option: the IIFE only reads the `p` array's `.faction`, `.power`, `.isGoverning` fields and mutates `.desc`, so it is order-independent except that it must observe the final powers/ordering. Caveat to flag for the implementer: applyLegitimacyMultipliers's pure-power sort (factionDynamics.js:511) does NOT keep the governing faction at index 0, whereas the pre-legitimacy sort (line 1560) did. The IIFE's `Ct===1`/`Ct===2` positional prose and `he = p[0]` assume position 0 is meaningful (and the prose explicitly special-cases "the governing council" as p[0]). After moving, p[0] is whoever has the highest effective power, which may not be the governing faction — that is actually the *correct* behavior for the prose to describe, but the implementer should confirm the "governing council" phrasing still reads coherently when the governing faction is not on top (it likely should, since that scenario is exactly a legitimacy crisis the prose can speak to). Alternatively, compute the comparisons from `rawPower` consistently and explicitly label them as pre-performance/structural standing — but mixing rawPower prose with effective-power display is more confusing than just regenerating against final powers.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED with two working reproductions against the real factionDynamics.js module.

Ordering of operations (verified by reading powerGenerator.js): line 1556-1560 normalizes powers to sum 100 and sorts with isGoverning pinned to index 0; the IIFE at lines 1561-1802 bakes positional/comparative prose onto each faction's `desc` from THAT pre-multiplier ordering and the power deltas it computes (cr = p[0].power - p[1].power at line 1574; pairwise comparisons like Se.power > at, He.power > at+8, Zt.power > at+10, etc.). Then at line 2187 applyLegitimacyMultipliers(p, publicLegitimacy, tier) runs, multiplying the governing faction by govMultiplier (0.60-1.30) and the criminal faction by crimMultiplier (0.75-1.30) per factionDynamics.js:111-124, then RE-SORTS purely by power at factionDynamics.js:511 — and notably does NOT pin isGoverning first the way line 1560 did. I confirmed there is no desc regeneration after line 1800: every .desc mutation in powerGenerator.js is at or before the IIFE.

The narrow Tolerated band (score 45-59) where all multipliers are exactly 1.0 is the only band immune. Scores routinely fall outside it: contributions are prosperity ±20, safety ±20, defense ±10, food ±10 on a base of 50 (factionDynamics.js:76-100), so e.g. Comfortable prosperity alone -> 58, Safe -> 65 (Approved, gov ×1.15), Poor+Unsafe -> 32 (Contested, gov ×0.80). So the out-of-band condition is common, not a rare edge case.

Reproduction A (Contested, score 38, gov ×0.80) against the real module: pre-mult order Merchant Council(38,gov) > Craft Guilds(33); the IIFE bakes onto Craft Guilds 'a single shift could swap their positions' and 'running close behind the merchant guilds'. After applyLegitimacyMultipliers the displayed order is Craft Guilds(33) > Merchant Council(30,gov) — Craft Guilds are now rendered AT THE TOP, above the governing council, while their prose still says they trail it. The displayed power numbers and ordering directly contradict the displayed prose.

Reproduction B (Legitimacy Crisis, score 24, gov ×0.60) against the real module: governing City Council 35→21 sorts to LAST, displayed below both Town Guard(33) and Thieves' Guild(26) in the power-bar list/stacked distribution, while its narrative was baked as the dominant #1.

Both render paths consume the post-multiplier numbers with the pre-multiplier prose: on-screen PowerTab.jsx renders pf in array order (lines 156/171 pf.map), showing f.power (line 200) and the expandable f.desc (lines 220-222); the PDF viewModel.js:448 independently re-sorts factions by power descending and carries f.desc as description. The 'governing' callout (PowerTab.jsx:32) keys off isGoverning so the WHO-governs label stays correct, but the list ordering, power bars, stacked distribution, and per-faction prose drift apart.

Severity medium: this is a real internal-coherence/parity defect that produces self-contradicting dossier text on a common configuration band, undermining the product's 'internally-coherent settlement' goal. It is not data loss, money, security, or a crash, and it does not break determinism (same seed still yields the same contradictory output deterministically), so it is below high.
verdicts.fix_note:
The evidence's diagnosis is correct. Sanity-checks on the suggested fix direction:

1. Simplest robust fix: move the narrative IIFE (lines 1561-1802) to run AFTER applyLegitimacyMultipliers (line 2187), so all comparative/positional prose is computed from final effective powers and the final ordering. Re-derive he/De/Mi/cr and all pairwise deltas from the post-multiplier `p`. Verify nothing the IIFE writes (desc strings) is read by code between 1802 and 2187 — from my read it is not, but confirm before moving.

2. Note a SECOND, separable defect the trace surfaced: applyLegitimacyMultipliers' sort (factionDynamics.js:511) does NOT pin isGoverning first, unlike the sort at powerGenerator.js:1560. This is why the governing faction can be displayed mid/bottom of the list (Reproduction B). Whether that is intended (a delegitimized government SHOULD visually drop in raw power) or a bug is a product call — but if the intent is 'governing faction always renders first regardless of effective power,' the fix is to make line 511 use the same isGoverning-pinned comparator as line 1560. Decide this explicitly; the narrative-relocation fix alone will make the prose self-consistent but the governing faction will still render by power rank.

3. Do NOT 'fix' this by recomputing prose against rawPower — the dossier displays the multiplied `power` (PowerTab.jsx:200 shows f.power with an optional rawPower→ prefix), so prose must be keyed to the same `power` values the reader sees, i.e. post-multiplier.

confirmed.title: Neighbour-injected factions bypass normalization, labels, and the relationship matrix
confirmed.evidence: neighbourFactions runs after generatePower and pushes mirror/opposition factions with raw power randInt(10,30)/randInt(8,26) (lines 33-41, 66-74) into an array whose other members were normalized to sum 100 and then legitimacy-multiplied. The injected entries get no rawPower/powerLabel (applyLegitimacyMultipliers already ran — PowerTab.jsx:192 renders no badge, while the PDF defaults to 'Influence', pdf/sections/PowerStructure.jsx:279 — a small dossier/PDF divergence), are appended unsorted (array elsewhere sorted by power), and are excluded from powerStructure.factionRelationships. genCoherence's 'holds X% of power' notes (narrativeGenerator.js:799, 858) also become inaccurate once total power exceeds 100.
confirmed.suggested_fix: After injection, stamp powerLabel/rawPower, re-sort, and either renormalize or scale injected powers to the post-normalization scale; optionally extend computeFactionRelationships to cover neighbour factions.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified every cited mechanism against the actual code.

CONFIRMED — neighbourFactions.js (registered pipeline step, deps ['generatePower','resolveNeighbour']) pushes mirror factions with power=rng.randInt(10,30) (line 36) and opposition factions with power=rng.randInt(8,26) (line 69), each with faction/category/power/desc/source/neighbourName/isGoverning but NO rawPower and NO powerLabel.

CONFIRMED — generatePower (step 10) calls generatePowerStructure (powerGenerator.js), which (a) normalizes faction powers to sum ~100 at lines 1556-1558 (N.power = round(N.power/dt*100)), (b) calls applyLegitimacyMultipliers (factionDynamics.js:469) which is the ONLY place rawPower (line 499) and powerLabel (lines 503-507) are stamped and which sorts the array (line 511), and (c) computes factionRelationships (line 2204) over the pre-injection array. neighbourFactions runs AFTER all of this and no later step (factionCorrelationPass, subsumptionPass, cascadePass, corruptionPass, assemble*, normalizeSettlement) re-stamps powerLabel/rawPower, re-sorts, re-normalizes, or recomputes factionRelationships — grep confirmed.

CONFIRMED — dossier/PDF divergence: PowerTab.jsx:192 renders the badge only when f.powerLabel is truthy (so injected factions show none), while PDF PowerStructure.jsx:279 uses `f.powerLabel || 'Influence'`, defaulting to the 'Influence' label for the same faction.

CONFIRMED — narrativeGenerator.js:799 (and 858) emits 'holds {f.power}% of power' notes; once injected raw powers (10-30, 8-26) are added the total exceeds 100, so the implied percentages no longer sum to 100 (the literal value for the cited crime/gov faction is unchanged, but the aggregate-percent integrity is broken — exactly as the reviewer framed it).

CONFIRMED — path is live: resolveNeighbour provides neighbourFacBias via getNeighbourFactionBias (neighbourGenerator.js:263), which populates mirrorFactions/opposeFactions with non-trivial weights (allied mirror 0.20, hostile oppose 0.40). Injection uses the seeded rng (rng.chance/rng.randInt), so determinism is NOT broken.

All mechanical claims hold. This is a genuine but minor defect: missing power label/raw-power on injected factions, a real on-screen-vs-PDF label divergence, unsorted appended entries, exclusion from the relationship matrix, and a mixed power scale that makes '% of power' wording imprecise. None of it crashes, loses data, breaks billing/auth, or breaks determinism, so 'low' severity is correct.
verdicts.fix_note: Fix is sound. After injection, stamp powerLabel/rawPower on each new faction (reuse the same threshold logic in applyLegitimacyMultipliers — ideally factor that label computation into a shared helper so PowerTab/PDF stay consistent), then re-sort powerStructure.factions by effective power. For scale coherence, either renormalize the whole array back to sum-100 after injection or scale the injected raw powers into the post-normalization band before pushing (a fixed randInt(10,30) is roughly a third of a 100-point pie for a single neighbour faction, which over-weights them and is what makes the '% of power' notes drift). Extending computeFactionRelationships to cover neighbour factions is genuinely optional and higher-cost; the label/sort/scale fixes are the load-bearing part. Note one nuance for the fix author: the existing faction's own displayed '% of power' value is literally unchanged — the inaccuracy is purely that the set no longer sums to 100, so renormalization (not just relabeling) is what actually restores that note's correctness.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED and reproduced end-to-end against the real modules.

Pipeline ordering (verified): generatePower (Step 10, phase 'power') runs powerGenerator.generatePowerStructure, which fully finalizes the faction array BEFORE neighbourFactions touches it:
- powerGenerator.js:1556-1558 normalizes every faction power so the set sums to ~100 (Math.round(power/total*100)).
- powerGenerator.js:2187 calls applyLegitimacyMultipliers (factionDynamics.js:469-512), which stamps f.rawPower (line 499) and f.powerLabel (lines 503-507) on every faction and re-sorts descending (line 511).
- powerGenerator.js:2204 computes factionRelationships from the same pre-injection array `p` and returns it on the powerStructure (line 2231).

neighbourFactions.js (Step 11, deps:['generatePower']) then mutates the SAME powerStructure.factions array, pushing entries with raw power: rng.randInt(10,30) (line 36) / rng.randInt(8,26) (line 69) and NO rawPower, NO powerLabel, isGoverning:false, appended at the end. Nothing downstream re-runs applyLegitimacyMultipliers, re-sorts, re-normalizes, or recomputes factionRelationships (grep across steps/ + domain/ confirms none).

Reproduction (node, real modules):
- Base 'city' power structure: 6 factions, total power 93, every faction has powerLabel + rawPower, factionRelationships length 6.
- After emulating one mirror injection at power=30: 7 factions, total 123, injected faction has powerLabel=undefined and rawPower=undefined, factionRelationships still length 6 (injected absent), power array becomes [30,21,14,13,8,7,30] — trailing 30 breaks the descending sort the rest of the array maintains.

User-visible / data consequences (all verified at the cited lines):
1. Missing label badge — PowerTab.jsx:192 gates the badge on `f.powerLabel &&`, so injected factions show NO power-tier badge on screen; pdf/sections/PowerStructure.jsx:279 uses `label={f.powerLabel || 'Influence'}` (fed by viewModel.js:437 `powerLabel || null`), so the PDF labels the SAME faction 'Influence'. Genuine dossier/PDF parity divergence.
2. Missing rawPower — PowerTab.jsx:175/199 only shows the raw→final delta when rawPower exists; harmless but inconsistent.
3. Unsorted append — array invariant (sorted by power desc, governing first) is violated; injected faction lands below weaker base factions in the row list.
4. Excluded from factionRelationships — the injected faction participates in no relationship-matrix edge despite being a real power-bearing faction; oppose factions explicitly carry a 'tension source' downstream trace (line 87) yet generate no actual relationship edge — a broken seam between the trace claim and the data.
5. Narrative %% drift — genCoherence (narrativeGenerator.js:799, 858, called from assembleSettlement.js:135, Step 15, post-injection) renders `${crimeFaction.power}% of power` treating per-faction power as a literal percentage. Valid only when the set sums to 100; after injection the total reached 123 in repro, so a power-30 faction actually holds ~24% but the note says 30%. Weakest sub-claim: the on-screen bar (PowerTab.jsx:174) recomputes pct over the live `total` so bars stay self-consistent, and base total is already 93 not exactly 100, so the prose was already loosely framed.

Determinism is NOT broken — injection uses the seeded rng (rng.chance/rng.randInt), so same seed => same output.

Severity medium: real simulation-coherence drift + a real dossier/PDF parity divergence, but it only triggers when neighbourFacBias exists AND rng.chance rolls true (not the main path), causes no crash/data-loss/money/security/determinism issue, and the bar percentages on screen remain internally consistent.
verdicts.fix_note:
The cleanest fix is ordering, not patching at the injection site: move neighbour-faction injection to BEFORE powerGenerator finalizes the array (i.e., inject candidate factions into `p` before powerGenerator.js:1556 normalize / 2187 applyLegitimacyMultipliers / 2204 computeFactionRelationships). That single change makes injected factions participate in normalization (total back to ~100, so the narrative %% notes stay accurate), in legitimacy labeling (gets rawPower + powerLabel for both PowerTab and PDF), in the descending sort, and in the relationship matrix — fixing all five sub-issues at once. The current architecture splits this across two pipeline steps (generatePower vs neighbourFactions), so the comment at generatePower.js:29-31 ('generatePowerStructure reads no neighbour bias') would need revisiting; the neighbour bias would have to be threaded into the power generator (or the normalize/label/relationship tail factored into a helper that neighbourFactions re-invokes).

If keeping the two-step structure, the minimum viable fix at neighbourFactions.js is to, after all pushes: (a) re-run normalize-to-100 + applyLegitimacyMultipliers over powerStructure.factions (so injected entries get rawPower/powerLabel and the set re-sums), (b) re-sort the array, and (c) recompute (or incrementally extend) powerStructure.factionRelationships to include the new factions. Just stamping powerLabel/rawPower on the injected entries (the narrow fix) would close the PowerTab/PDF label divergence but would NOT fix the sort, the relationship-matrix exclusion, or the narrative %% drift, so it is insufficient.

Also note an independent latent issue surfaced while tracing: neighbourFactions.js:37 and :70 read neighbourProfile.name / neighbourProfile.relationshipType directly (unguarded), whereas the surrounding code uses optional chaining (neighbourProfile?.name) and `relType` defaults relationshipType to 'neutral'. If neighbourFacBias is truthy but neighbourProfile is null, line 37's `neighbourProfile.relationshipType.replace(...)` throws — worth confirming resolveNeighbour always sets both together, but out of scope for this finding.

confirmed.title: ROLE_FACTION_MAP duplicate keywords shadow the noble/crafts mappings — manor lords and knights always join the governing/military faction
confirmed.evidence: mergeNPCLists' ROLE_FACTION_MAP is first-match-wins (lines 929-934). 'lord' appears at line 808 mapped to govFaction and again at line 873 mapped to nobleFaction; 'knight' at 822 (milFaction) and 877 (nobleFaction); 'overseer' at 853 (econFaction) and 891 ('journeyman overseer' → craftsFaction). The later noble/crafts entries are unreachable, so a 'Lord/Lady of the Manor' or 'Knight/Dame' NPC would be affiliated with the Town Council or Military/Guard even when a Noble Families faction exists. (Currently masked for nobles by the generateNPCs powerStructure gap, but it bites event-created and structural noble NPCs and will bite the moment that gap is fixed.)
confirmed.suggested_fix: Order more-specific keywords first ('lord of the manor', 'noble', 'journeyman overseer') or check noble keywords before the generic government block.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED as a real defect. mergeNPCLists' ROLE_FACTION_MAP is first-match-wins (npcGenerator.js:929-934: ordered iteration, roleLower.includes(keyword), break on first hit). Confirmed duplicate keywords where the earlier entry shadows the later: ['lord', govFaction] at line 808 vs ['lord', nobleFaction] at 873; ['knight', milFaction] at 822 vs ['knight', nobleFaction] at 877; ['overseer', econFaction] at 853 vs ['journeyman overseer', craftsFaction] at 891 (plain substring "overseer" matches before the more specific entry). The later noble/crafts entries for these keywords are dead code. The .filter(([,f]) => f!=null) at line 892 does not reorder, so first-match order holds. factionAffiliation (set at line 975) is a load-bearing field consumed by the on-screen dossier (npcComponents.jsx:195, NPCsTab.jsx:31 grouping), the PDF view model (viewModel.js:249), the AI grounding bundle sent to the LLM, dev anomaly detection, and plot hooks — so a misassignment is observable in product output and is a dossier/PDF concern. The reviewer's "masking" parenthetical is also accurate and verified: generateNPCs is called without powerStructure in BOTH the main pipeline (steps/generatePopulation.js:33) and the regen helper (generateSettlementPipeline.js:79), so the NOBLE_ROLES/CRAFTS_ROLES injection at npcGenerator.js:1409/1415 never fires; thus 'Lord/Lady of the Manor' and 'Knight/Dame' are not produced via the population path today, making the noble half latent until that gap is fixed.

Two honesty caveats that justify keeping this at low (the reviewer was upfront about the dormancy): (1) The structural-NPC path the reviewer cites does NOT actually trigger this bug — factionRoles.js's noble archetype produces "Lord Mayor", and ensureFactionStructuralNpcs (assembleSettlement.js:143) runs AFTER generateCoherence/mergeNPCLists (line 135), so those structural NPCs never receive a ROLE_FACTION_MAP affiliation at all. So that specific sub-claim is inaccurate. (2) The 'overseer'->crafts case is the most live today (Journeyman/Market Overseer roles are generatable and pass through mergeNPCLists) but only diverges when both economy and crafts factions co-exist. I also found the bug class is broader than scoped: 'Crime Lord' (data) contains "lord" and would hit ['lord', govFaction] before ['crime lord', crimeFaction] at line 863, mapping a crime boss to the governing faction — though that role is event-spawned, whose re-entry through mergeNPCLists I could not fully confirm. Net: a real dead-mapping defect, correctly low severity given it is mostly dormant, with a correct suggested fix.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix (order specific keywords before generic blocks / check noble keywords before the government block) is correct. While fixing, also move ['crime lord', crimeFaction] above ['lord', govFaction], since the same first-match-wins shadowing currently routes a 'Crime Lord' NPC to the governing faction instead of the criminal faction — that instance is more impactful than the noble/crafts ones the report scopes. A more robust fix is to sort ROLE_FACTION_MAP by descending keyword length before matching, so longer/more-specific phrases ('journeyman overseer', 'crime lord', 'lord of the manor') always win over shorter substrings.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL, with a correction to the claim's masking caveat.

The mechanism is exactly as described. In src/generators/npcGenerator.js, mergeNPCLists builds ROLE_FACTION_MAP and resolves Pass-1 affiliation first-match-wins (loop at lines 929-934: `for (const [keyword, faction] of ROLE_FACTION_MAP) { if (faction && roleLower.includes(keyword)) { assignedFaction = faction; break; } }`). The duplicate substring keywords appear as claimed:
- 'lord' → govFaction (line 808) shadows 'lord' → nobleFaction (line 873)
- 'knight' → milFaction (line 822) shadows 'knight' → nobleFaction (line 877)
- 'overseer' → econFaction (line 853, substring) shadows 'journeyman overseer' → craftsFaction (line 891)
The later noble/crafts entries are unreachable dead code.

Reproduced directly against the real module. mergeNPCLists with factions [Town Council(gov,governing), Noble Families(noble), City Watch(mil), Crafters Guild(crafts), Merchant League(econ)] returned:
- 'Lord/Lady of the Manor' => Town Council (should be Noble Families)
- 'Knight/Dame' => City Watch (should be Noble Families)
- 'Journeyman Overseer' => Merchant League (should be Crafters Guild)
- 'Baron/Baroness' => Noble Families (correct — no shadow)

CORRECTION to the claim's caveat: the claim says this is "currently masked for nobles by the generateNPCs powerStructure gap" and only bites event/structural NPCs. That is wrong. generateNPCs is indeed called WITHOUT powerStructure (generatePopulation.js:33 passes only {tier, institutions}), so the powerStructure-gated NOBLE_ROLES/CRAFTS_ROLES injection (npcGenerator.js:1409/1415, gated on powerFactionCats which is empty) never fires. BUT there is a SECOND, ungated path: getUpgradeOpportunities (economicGenerator.js:1505) pulls noble roles from HISTORY_EVENTS/historyData.js (noble category, lines 276-325: 'Lord/Lady of the Manor', 'Knight/Dame', etc., which have NO requiresInstKeyword). Those roles are admitted whenever a noble-category institution exists (gate at economicGenerator.js:1530-1534), independent of powerStructure. I reproduced the full pipeline-equivalent chain: generateNPCs({tier:'city', institutions:[Manor House(noble), ...]}, ...) (powerStructure absent) produced a 'Knight/Dame' NPC, and mergeNPCLists with a real Noble Houses faction assigned it to 'Garrison Watch' (the military faction) instead. So the bug bites the NORMAL generation path today, not only event-created NPCs.

User-visible/data impact: factionAffiliation is rendered on-screen (components/new/npcComponents.jsx:195 shows '{role} · {faction}'; NPCsTab.jsx:22-31 GROUPS NPCs by factionAffiliation) and in the PDF (pdf/lib/viewModel.js:249, 913-914). A manor lord or knight is therefore displayed and grouped under the Town Council / Guard faction rather than the Noble Houses faction, and a journeyman overseer under the merchant/economy faction — wrong simulation output and a coherence break, surfaced in both dossier and PDF. It is deterministic-correctly wrong (no determinism break).

Severity medium: wrong-but-coherent-looking faction attribution for a subset of NPCs (noble/knight/overseer roles), only when both the relevant role and a distinct noble/crafts faction co-exist; no crash, no data loss, no money/security. It degrades the simulation's internal coherence (a core product goal) but does not break the main path.
verdicts.fix_note:
Do NOT blindly dedup. The map is first-match-wins, so the safe fix depends on desired classification:

1. 'knight': simplest correct fix is to make the noble intent win when a noble faction exists. Either remove the military 'knight' entry (line 822) so 'Knight/Dame' routes to nobleFaction, OR — better — keep both but reorder so the noble block precedes the military block for the knight token, OR gate by NPC category (the NPCs carry category:'noble'; Pass 1 ignores category). Note a town-guard 'knight' is plausibly military, so category-aware resolution (prefer the faction matching npc.category when multiple keyword entries match) is the most robust fix and also fixes lord/overseer.

2. 'lord': do not just delete line 808. Other 'lord' roles exist: 'Lord Mayor' (factionRoles.js:50, civic) and bare 'Lord' (historyData.js:15). 'Lord Mayor' is safe because 'mayor' (line 803) precedes 'lord' and both point to govFaction. But removing line 808 would send generic 'Lord' to nobleFaction. Prefer a more specific noble keyword such as 'of the manor' / 'manor' (line 875 'manor' is already noble) and let 'manor' carry 'Lord/Lady of the Manor', while keeping a govFaction mapping for generic civic lords. Verify 'Crime Lord' still hits crimeFaction (it does — line 863 precedes line 808).

3. 'overseer': the econ entry at line 853 is a broad substring that swallows 'Journeyman Overseer'. Narrow line 853 to a more specific economy role keyword (e.g. 'trade overseer' or just rely on 'factor'/'broker'), or move the crafts 'journeyman overseer' entry before the econ 'overseer' entry.

The cleanest single fix that resolves all three: make Pass 1 category-aware — when multiple ROLE_FACTION_MAP keywords match an NPC's role, prefer the entry whose faction.category matches the NPC's own category (npc.category) before falling back to first-match order. That respects npc.category:'noble'/'crafts' and eliminates the shadowing without per-keyword reordering. Add a regression test asserting a noble-category 'Knight/Dame' and 'Lord/Lady of the Manor' bind to the noble faction when one exists, and a crafts 'Journeyman Overseer' binds to crafts over economy.

confirmed.title: politically_fractured intends two rival Council Members but Set-dedup collapses them to one
confirmed.evidence: STRESS_MANDATORY_ROLES.politically_fractured is ['Council Member', 'Council Member'] (line 1470) — clearly intending two opposed council members for a fractured settlement — but mandatoryRoles is built as `[...new Set([...tierRoles, ...stressRoles])]` (line 1481), which dedupes the pair to a single entry. The mandatory loop also looks up one shared candidate per role name, so even without the Set only one would spawn.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Allow duplicate mandatory roles (count occurrences instead of a Set) and let the loop generate one NPC per occurrence.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. At src/generators/npcGenerator.js:1470, STRESS_MANDATORY_ROLES.politically_fractured = ['Council Member', 'Council Member'] — deliberately listing the role twice (contrast succession_void at line 1475, which uses two DISTINCT roles ['Council Member','Chief Magistrate'], showing the repetition is intentional, not a leftover). At line 1481, mandatoryRoles = [...new Set([...tierRoles, ...stressRoles])] dedupes the duplicated pair down to a single 'Council Member'. The mandatory loop (lines 1492-1497) then spawns one NPC for that single entry. The fill stage afterward builds usedRoles = new Set(npcs.map(n => n.role)) and filters remainingCandidates to exclude used roles (lines 1506-1507), so no second 'Council Member' can be added organically. Net result: exactly one Council Member is generated for a politically_fractured settlement, defeating the apparent intent of two rival council members.

I confirmed 'Council Member' is a real candidate role: it lives in HISTORY_EVENTS.government (src/data/historyData.js:67) with minTier 'town' and no guild/port/keyword gate, and getUpgradeOpportunities (src/generators/economicGenerator.js:1505-1540) admits government-category roles when a government institution is present — so for town+ politically_fractured settlements the candidate exists and the mandatory lookup succeeds. The path is live: generateNPCs is called by both generateSettlementPipeline.js:79 and steps/generatePopulation.js:33. No test pins single-council-member as intended behavior (the only nearby reference, tests/domain/conditionPromotion.test.js, concerns condition promotion, not NPC counts).

One correction to the reviewer's evidence: the parenthetical claim that 'even without the Set only one would spawn' because the loop 'looks up one shared candidate per role name' is wrong. mandatoryRoles.forEach iterates array ELEMENTS, not unique names; without the Set, the two-element ['Council Member','Council Member'] array would call generateSingleNPC twice and push two NPCs. So the Set is in fact the sole cause of the collapse — which strengthens rather than weakens the core finding. The core claim, file:line, and mechanism are all accurate.

Severity is low and honest: this is a simulation-richness/intent gap (a fractured settlement gets one council member instead of two rivals), not data loss, money, security, a crash, or a determinism break.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct in direction. Concretely: stop deduping the stress roles. Keep tier roles deduped if desired, but allow stress-role duplicates, e.g. build mandatoryRoles as [...new Set(tierRoles), ...stressRoles] (or otherwise count occurrences) so the forEach at line 1492 runs once per occurrence and pushes two Council Member NPCs. Note the loop already calls generateSingleNPC fresh per iteration, so it will produce two distinct NPCs without further changes; the find() returning the same candidate object is fine because only role/title/category are read from it. Also confirm the downstream fill-stage usedRoles filter (line 1506-1507) is acceptable — it correctly prevents a THIRD council member, which is the desired cap.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed real via end-to-end trace. STRESS_MANDATORY_ROLES.politically_fractured = ['Council Member', 'Council Member'] (npcGenerator.js:1470) deliberately intends two rival council members for a fractured settlement. But mandatoryRoles = [...new Set([...tierRoles, ...stressRoles])] (line 1481) dedupes the pair to one. The mandatory loop (lines 1492-1497) then spawns only a single Council Member.

Reachability: generateSettlementPipeline.js:79 calls generateNPCs with config (carrying stressTypes/stressType), so primaryStress resolves to 'politically_fractured' on the live path. HISTORY_EVENTS defines exactly one role:'Council Member' object (historyData.js:67-73, category government, minTier town), so candidates holds at most one such candidate when a town+ settlement has a government-category institution (gate at economicGenerator.js:1530-1534). When that gate passes, the Set is the sole reason only one spawns. No downstream role-dedup exists in the pipeline or viewModel to make this moot.

CORRECTION to the verifier's reasoning: the claim 'even without the Set only one would spawn, because the loop looks up one shared candidate per role name' is FALSE. candidates.find() returns the same candidate object, but the loop calls generateSingleNPC(candidate.role, candidate.title, candidate.category, ...) on each iteration, and generateSingleNPC (lines 60-98) consumes the seeded PRNG to build a fresh, distinct NPC (unique name/gender/goal/secret) sharing only the role string. I verified with a node simulation that removing the Set yields two 'Council Member' spawns. The Set() is the only thing collapsing the pair.

Consequence: during a politically-fractured crisis (whose simulation premise is rival factions, with rival/enemy/political relationship weighting), the dossier and PDF show one council member instead of the intended two opposed figures. This is wrong simulation output versus authored intent — not a crash, money/security issue, data loss, or determinism break. Hence medium.
verdicts.fix_note: Do NOT simply delete the global Set — [...tierRoles, ...stressRoles] would then double-spawn roles legitimately shared across the tier/stress boundary (e.g. under_siege adds 'Guard Captain' which town/village tiers already provide; succession_void adds 'Council Member'/'Chief Magistrate'). Correct fix: dedup only across the tier↔stress boundary while preserving intentional duplicates WITHIN stressRoles. E.g. start from unique tierRoles, then append each stress role that is not already present in tierRoles, but allow repeats that originate within stressRoles itself (so the second 'Council Member' survives while a stress 'Guard Captain' that a tier already supplies is dropped). Also: the duplicate spawn only takes effect when 'Council Member' actually passes the candidate gate (government-category institution at town+); the fix should not assume the candidate always exists, and the mandatory loop's own `npcs.length < targetCount` guard still applies to both spawns.

confirmed.title: generateFactionLeader's institutions parameter is never supplied — institution-presence secret weighting is permanently in 'absent' mode
confirmed.evidence: generateFactionLeader(category, config, institutions = []) keys secret-type weights on hasCriminal/hasMagic/hasReligion derived from the institutions list (lines 385-411: criminal ×1.4 vs ×0.8, magical ×1.3 vs ×0.6, religious ×1.3 vs ×0.6). Its only call site is generateSingleNPC line 67: `generateFactionLeader(category, config)` — two args, so institutions defaults to [] for every NPC ever generated. A cathedral city and a godless mining camp weight religious secrets identically; only the priority sliders move the weights.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Thread the institutions list from generateNPCs → generateSingleNPC → generateFactionLeader (it is already in scope at the generateNPCs level).

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed by reading src/generators/npcGenerator.js. generateFactionLeader is defined at line 385 as (_category = 'other', config = {}, institutions = []). Its ONLY call site is line 67 inside generateSingleNPC: `const secret = generateFactionLeader(category, config);` — exactly two arguments, so the institutions parameter always defaults to []. grep across the codebase confirms no other caller. Inside the function, names = (institutions || []).map(...) is therefore always [], so hasCriminal/hasMagic/hasReligion (lines 394-396) are always false, and the secret-type weights at lines 401/404/405 permanently take the 'absent' branch (criminal 0.8, magical 0.6, religious 0.6). The institution-presence axis is dead. The claim that 'only the priority sliders move the weights' is precise: each weight is still multiplied by (1 + pri.X/100) and by stress boosts (lines 413-433), so secrets are not static or random — only the institution-presence signal is missing. Determinism is preserved (deterministically wrong, not nondeterministic), nothing crashes, no data/money/security impact. generateSingleNPC (line 60) does not even accept an institutions parameter, and none of its three callers (lines 211, 1495, 1523) pass it, though generateNPCs has institutions in scope (line 1235). This is a genuine unfinished seam degrading simulation coherence (a cathedral city and a godless mining camp weight religious secrets identically), affecting NPC secret flavor rather than a core invariant — hence low severity, matching the reviewer's rating.
verdicts.fix_note: Fix is correct but note generateSingleNPC currently has NO institutions parameter (signature is (role, namingTier, category, culture, tier, config)), so threading requires adding a new param to generateSingleNPC and updating all three call sites: line 1495 and 1523 (generateNPCs has `institutions` in scope from line 1235) and line 211 in filterByGuild (which also receives institutions as its first arg). Alternatively, since npcConfig is already built at line 1484 and passed to every generateSingleNPC call, the lowest-touch fix is to stash institutions onto npcConfig (e.g. _institutions) and read config._institutions inside generateFactionLeader — avoiding signature churn on generateSingleNPC entirely.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL. Traced end-to-end against the real modules.

Structural confirmation:
- generateFactionLeader(_category='other', config={}, institutions=[]) (npcGenerator.js:385) derives hasCriminal/hasMagic/hasReligion from the institutions arg (lines 393-396) and uses them in secret-type weights: criminal ×1.4/×0.8 (line 401), magical ×1.3/×0.6 (line 404), religious ×1.3/×0.6 (line 405).
- Its ONLY caller is generateSingleNPC line 67: `generateFactionLeader(category, config)` — two args, so institutions always defaults to [].
- generateSingleNPC's own signature (line 60: role, namingTier, category, culture, tier, config) has NO institutions parameter, so it structurally cannot forward institutions even though they exist upstream.
- generateNPCs destructures institutions (line 1235) and uses them for candidate selection/computeNPCWeights, but the npcConfig it builds (lines 1484-1489) omits institutions. All three generateSingleNPC call sites (211, 1495, 1523) therefore never carry institutions into the secret weighting.

Failure path: pipeline (generateSettlementPipeline.js:79 / steps/generatePopulation.js:33) supplies institutions to generateNPCs -> never threaded into generateSingleNPC -> generateFactionLeader receives [] -> hasCriminal/hasMagic/hasReligion are false for every NPC in every settlement -> the institution-presence multipliers permanently take the 'absent' branch (×0.8/×0.6/×0.6).

Demonstrated with a read-only node reproduction of the exact weighting math: at default-50 priorities, the INTENDED behavior gives a cathedral city ~17.3% religious-secret probability vs ~8.8% for a godless mining camp (the 1.3/0.6 ratio). With institutions===[] (what actually runs), both collapse to identical 8.8%. So the intended institution->secret-flavor coupling is silently inert; secret flavor is driven only by priority sliders + stress types.

Impact class: degraded/wrong simulation-output coherence (a deliberate realism coupling is dead), not a crash, data loss, money, security, or determinism break. Output stays deterministic. This is a cohesion/realism defect, hence medium. Notably, the project's own docs/SIMULATION_LOGIC_AUDIT.md:479-481 independently catalogs this exact issue as [medium], corroborating the trace.

Confidence very high (~0.95): the call-site arity, the missing parameter on generateSingleNPC, and the empirical distribution collapse all line up, and an independent audit doc agrees.
verdicts.fix_note: The proposed fix ("thread institutions into generateSingleNPC and pass them through to generateFactionLeader") is correct in direction but understates the work: generateSingleNPC currently has NO institutions parameter, and there are THREE call sites to update (npcGenerator.js:211 inside filterByGuild, 1495, and 1523), not one. Two viable approaches: (1) add an institutions param to generateSingleNPC and forward it at line 67 — requires touching all three call sites plus filterByGuild's signature; or (2) lower-footprint: include institutions in the npcConfig object built at lines 1484-1489 (e.g. _institutions: institutions) since npcConfig already flows to every call site, then in generateFactionLeader read institutions from config (config._institutions) when the explicit arg is empty. Either way, also pass institutions for the guild-master NPC path (filterByGuild line 211) for consistency. Verify NPC_CRIMINAL_SECRETS has populated arrays for criminal/magical/religious (it does) so the rebalanced weights actually change drawn output. Determinism is preserved either way since institutions are already deterministic for a given seed; but note the weighting change WILL shift snapshot/property tests that pin NPC secrets, so update fixtures.

confirmed.title: generatePressureSentence passes the wrong context shape to genSuccessionNarr — most succession/tension narrative branches can never fire
confirmed.evidence: genSuccessionNarr (powerGenerator.js:318-437) branches on r.topTension (17 tension-specific narratives), r.topNPCName/topNPCRole, r.viabilityIssues/isViable, r.hasNeighborConflict/neighbor, r.milForce, r.isCrossroads/isPort. generatePressureSentence calls it with `summary` from genSettSummary (narrativeGenerator.js:1036), which carries none of those fields (it has name/tier/commodity/prosperity/stability/govFaction/topFaction/...). The shape that matches every field is genPressureDetail's `detail` (lines 740-770), which the same function computes at line 1018 but does not pass. Result: all topTension branches, the viability branch, and the neighbour branch are dead; output degrades to the prosperity/stability generics with 'the most senior figure' placeholders.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Call genSuccessionNarr(detail) instead of genSuccessionNarr(summary) (or merge detail into summary before the call).

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading both shapes and the call site.

genSuccessionNarr (src/generators/powerGenerator.js:318-437) branches on fields that DO NOT exist on the object it is actually passed. generatePressureSentence calls genSuccessionNarr(summary) at src/generators/narrativeGenerator.js:1036, where `summary` is the return of genSettSummary (narrativeGenerator.js:392-413). That object exposes: name, tier, stressType, stressTypes, commodity, prosperity, stability, govFaction, topFaction, crimFaction, milFaction, relFaction, age, access, npcNames, pickNPC, factionCount. It has NO topTension, topNPCName, topNPCRole, isViable, viabilityIssues, hasNeighborConflict, neighbor, isCrossroads, isPort, or milForce.

genSuccessionNarr depends on exactly those missing fields:
- 17 topTension branches (lines 322, 326, 330, 334, 372, 376, 380, 384, 389, 394, 399, 404, 408, 412, 416, 420, 424, 428) — all dead because summary.topTension is undefined.
- viability branch (338-342) needs r.viabilityIssues?.length > 0 — undefined, dead.
- neighbour branch (343-347) needs r.hasNeighborConflict — undefined, dead.
- crossroads/port branches (356-365) need r.isCrossroads/r.isPort — undefined, dead.
- final fallback (432-434) uses r.topNPCName — undefined, so it renders the literal 'the most senior figure' placeholder.

By contrast, genPressureDetail's `detail` object (narrativeGenerator.js:740-769) supplies every one of those fields (topTension, topNPCName/Role, isViable, viabilityIssues, hasNeighborConflict, neighbor, isCrossroads, isPort, milForce), and it is already computed at line 1018 but not passed.

Reachability verified: the block at 1035-1038 is reached whenever detail.stressType is null (no stress) or not a key in PRESSURE_SENTENCES (so the early return at 1024-1032 does not fire), AND summary.stability includes 'Unstable' or 'Fractured'. That is a real, common config (unstable power, no acute stress). Note topTension comes from history.currentTensions (an axis independent of `stress`), so this succession block is precisely the dedicated handler for the "political tension, no acute stress" case — exactly the differentiation that is broken.

Only summary fields that survive — prosperity (348/352) and the stability generic (366) — can fire, and since the function returns succNarr[0] (the first push), the output is always a prosperity/stability generic, never a tension-specific line. genSuccessionNarr has only one production caller (grep across src confirms line 1036 is the sole invocation), always with summary, so the entire tension/viability/neighbour surface is dead in production.

pressureSentence is a live user-facing output: assembleSettlement.js:105 stores it, and it renders in the on-screen dossier (SummaryTab, OverviewTab, SummaryTabV2, TableView, QuickInspector) and the PDF (SummaryPage.jsx:82). So this degrades real output, not dead code.

Could not refute. No upstream guard merges the fields; no comment or test marks this as intentional; the cited API works exactly as the reviewer describes.

Severity medium is correct: no crash (no exceptions thrown), graceful degradation to readable generics, and the acute high-drama cases are caught earlier by the stressType early-return. The defect is reduced narrative coherence/variety on the tension axis, which fits the product's coherence goal but is not data loss, money, or security.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct. genSuccessionNarr(detail) supplies all required fields, and detail also carries name/govFaction/topFaction/prosperity/stability/commodity so the generic branches still work. Note one subtlety: detail.topFaction can be null (genPressureDetail line 710: factions[0]?.faction || null) whereas summary.topFaction defaults to 'the dominant faction'; genSuccessionNarr already guards every topFaction use with `|| 'the dominant faction'`/`|| 'the merchant class'`/etc., so passing detail is safe. Alternatively merge the missing fields into summary before the call.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via read + reproduction. At src/generators/narrativeGenerator.js:1036, generatePressureSentence calls genSuccessionNarr(summary) where summary = genSettSummary(settlement). genSettSummary's return object (lines 392-413) carries only: name, tier, stressType, stressTypes, commodity, prosperity, stability, govFaction, topFaction, crimFaction, milFaction, relFaction, age, access, npcNames, pickNPC, factionCount. genSuccessionNarr (powerGenerator.js:318-437) branches almost entirely on fields that summary does NOT have: r.topTension (17 distinct tension narratives), r.topNPCName/topNPCRole, r.isViable/r.viabilityIssues, r.hasNeighborConflict/r.neighbor, r.isCrossroads/r.isPort, r.milForce. Every grep hit for those fields in narrativeGenerator.js lands in genPressureDetail's return (lines 754-768), never in genSettSummary's. So with the summary shape, only the prosperity-generic and stability-generic branches plus the final 'most senior figure' fallback can ever fire.

The data the dead branches need is real and populated at runtime: history.currentTensions is set by historyGenerator.js:1076 and read by genPressureDetail at line 758 (topTension), so the tension-specific narratives would fire if the right shape were passed.

Reproduction (node, against the real powerGenerator.js module): with an Unstable settlement carrying topTension='succession_crisis', calling genSuccessionNarr with the summary shape pushes only 2 strings and returns[0] = the generic "looks stable from the outside; the relationship between X and the council is more contested than it appears." Calling it with the detail shape pushes 4 strings and returns[0] = the specific "X's ruler is ageing and the succession is contested; ..." This proves the wrong shape suppresses the intended specific output.

Failure path: pipeline -> assembleSettlement.js:105 sets settlement.pressureSentence = generatePressureSentence(settlement). For any settlement whose powerStructure.stability includes 'Unstable' or 'Fractured' (the gate at narrativeGenerator.js:1035) AND no higher-priority stress (PRESSURE_SENTENCES path at 1025) AND not falling through to relationship narrative, the succession block returns genSuccessionNarr(summary)[0] — always a prosperity/stability generic, never the tension-specific or viability/neighbour lines. pressureSentence is rendered in the on-screen dossier (SummaryTab.jsx, OverviewTab.jsx, SummaryTabV2.jsx, TableView.jsx, QuickInspector.jsx) as the Overview accent line, so the degradation is user-visible: a settlement with a succession_crisis / corruption_scandal / religious_tension / guild_conflict / etc. tension never surfaces that tension in its pressure line.

Severity medium: it is wrong/degraded simulation prose output on a visible surface, deterministic (no determinism break, no crash, no data loss). Not high because output is still a coherent fallback sentence, not an error, and the most-specific narratives are merely suppressed rather than producing garbage. Confidence ~0.9: the field mismatch is unambiguous and reproduced; the only minor uncertainty is whether a maintainer considers the generics 'acceptable enough' to call it cosmetic.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix (pass detail instead of summary to genSuccessionNarr) is correct and minimal. detail = genPressureDetail(settlement) is already computed at line 1018 and carries every field the branches read (topTension, topNPCName/Role, isViable, viabilityIssues, hasNeighborConflict, neighbor, isCrossroads, isPort, milForce, commodity, prosperity, stability, topFaction, govFaction, name). Both summary.stability and detail.stability derive from the same powerStructure.stability, so the gate at line 1035 (summary.stability) stays consistent with detail.stability that genSuccessionNarr reads — no need to also rewrite the gate, though using detail.stability there too would be tidier. One harmless behavioral nuance: detail.topFaction defaults to govFaction when no factions exist (line 750), whereas summary.topFaction defaulted to the literal 'the dominant faction'; both are non-null so the inner '|| the dominant faction' fallbacks were already dead and flavor differs only marginally. summary is still legitimately used for the gate, so keep it; just change the argument at line 1036 to genSuccessionNarr(detail).

confirmed.title: Cross-settlement conflicts mirror asymmetric relationships symmetrically and run unseeded at render time
confirmed.evidence: For directional relationships the mirrored description is built by swapping the NPC/faction args while keeping the same relType (lines 171, 214): the patron NPC template '`${npcB.name} of ${settB.name} is pressing ${npcA.name} ... invoking the patronage agreement to compel compliance`' (line 79) and the patron faction template '`${facB.name} of ${settB.name} exercises de facto oversight of ${facA.name}`' (line 125) therefore claim BOTH settlements are the patron in the paired forA/forB records — the two dossiers contradict each other about which side holds the patronage. Additionally RelationshipsTab.jsx:27 invokes generateCrossSettlementConflicts during render ('live') with no active seeded PRNG, so rngContext falls back to Math.random: the same settlement shows different conflict NPCs/text after every reload, and the PDF (which reads only persisted s.crossSettlementConflicts, pdf/lib/viewModel.js:1037) omits them entirely — a dossier/PDF parity gap.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Invert the relType (patron↔client) when building the mirrored description, and either persist the live-generated conflicts at generation/link time or seed them deterministically from the settlement seed + linkId.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified all three sub-claims against the actual code (note: the reviewer's path src/RelationshipsTab.jsx:27 is actually src/components/new/tabs/RelationshipsTab.jsx:27).

SUB-CLAIM 1 (symmetric mirroring of asymmetric patron relationship) — REAL, demonstrated by execution. generateCrossSettlementConflicts (src/generators/crossSettlementConflicts.js:142) receives a single relType and builds the mirrored record by swapping NPC/settlement args while reusing the SAME relType (lines 170-171 for NPCs, 213-214 for factions). The patron NPC template (line 79/80) and patron faction template (line 125) name the second-positional arg as the patron. Since forA passes (npcA,npcB,settA,settB) and forB passes (npcB,npcA,settB,settA), BOTH dossiers name the OTHER settlement as patron. I ran it: Aville's dossier says "Bcouncil of Bton exercises de facto oversight of Aldermen" while Bton's dossier says "Aldermen of Aville exercises de facto oversight of Bcouncil" — a direct contradiction. The asymmetry is load-bearing: src/domain/relationships/canonicalRelationship.js distinguishes patron/client with distinct sourceRole/targetRole (lines 58-63) and localPropagationType inverts patron<->client per side (lines 133-137). The generator is unaware of which side is patron. (Note: this only bites for relType==='patron'; the generator has no 'vassal'/'client'/'overlord' templates so those fall through to the neutral/symmetric pool, where mirroring is harmless. So the contradiction is specifically the patron case.)

SUB-CLAIM 2a (unseeded at render) — REAL. The pipeline sets the seeded PRNG only during step execution and clears it in a finally (src/generators/pipeline.js:106,114). By React render time _activeRng is null, so rngContext.random() falls back to Math.random() (src/generators/rngContext.js:36). RelationshipsTab.jsx:27 calls the generator with linkId 'live' inside a useMemo whose deps include the re-allocated settlement object; on reload the settlement is regenerated (persist.partialize stores only config+toggles), so the memo recomputes with no seeded PRNG -> different conflict NPCs/text each reload. extractNeighbourProfile (src/generators/neighbourGenerator.js:171-173) populates npcs/factions, so the live path produces real output for imported-neighbour generations.

SUB-CLAIM 2b (PDF parity gap) — REAL outcome, but the reviewer's stated mechanism is imprecise. crossSettlementConflicts is NEVER written anywhere (grep confirms only reads), so the PDF's dedicated crossConflicts block (viewModel.js:1037 -> Relationships.jsx:190) is dead. However the SAVED/linked conflicts are persisted into interSettlementRelationships (SettlementsPanel.jsx:874,877,935,938) and DO surface in the PDF via the interSettlement slice (viewModel.js:1036 -> Relationships.jsx:153 "SHARED FIGURES & STORIES"), contradicting "the PDF omits them entirely." The genuinely PDF-absent conflicts are the live-only ones from RelationshipsTab.jsx:27, which are computed in-component, never persisted, and thus never reach the PDF view model — a real screen/PDF parity gap for unsaved settlements.

Net: the defect is real on all the substantive technical points (patron contradiction + unseeded nondeterminism + a live-path parity gap), with one imprecision in the PDF-omission rationale. Severity low is honest: it triggers only for patron links with matching-category NPCs/factions, affects only on-screen/UI-derived text (no persisted-data corruption, no break to the deterministic pipeline output, no money/auth/crash), and the worst user-visible effect is contradictory flavor prose plus jittery live conflicts that aren't exported.
verdicts.fix_note: Fix for the patron contradiction: when building the mirrored (forB) record, invert the relType's directional sense rather than just swapping args — e.g. pass a per-side role-aware relType so the patron template renders the correct settlement as patron on each side (the generator currently has no 'client' templates, so a 'client' template set would need to be added, or the patron template made directional). For the parity/determinism part: persist the conflicts at generation/link time (the live 'live'-path output is never saved) OR seed the live generation deterministically from the settlement seed + linkId so it is stable across reloads. Separately, note the dead crossSettlementConflicts field — nothing writes it; either wire SettlementsPanel to populate it (and have the PDF/screen read it) or drop the dead read at viewModel.js:1037 and Relationships.jsx:190 to avoid future confusion.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL on all three sub-claims, with one mechanism correction. Verified end-to-end against the real module.

PATH/STATE: Two saved settlements are linked (SettlementsPanel.handleLink / save), or an unsaved settlement is previewed (RelationshipsTab.jsx:27). canonicalRelationship.relationshipDefinition for a patron/client selection collapses BOTH directions to a single relationshipType:'patron' (canonicalRelationship.js:59-62, 85-89). generateCrossSettlementConflicts is then called with that one relType, and it builds the partner perspective by simply swapping the NPC/faction args while keeping relType unchanged (crossSettlementConflicts.js:171, 214).

(1) DIRECTIONAL SYMMETRY CONTRADICTION — REAL (high). Demonstrated with the real module: for relType='patron' the two perspectives literally contradict each other about who is the patron.
  - Faction template (line 125): forA = "The Compact of Brackwater exercises de facto oversight of The Ledger['s] operations" (Brackwater=patron); forB = "The Ledger of Aldermoor exercises de facto oversight of The Compact['s] operations" (Aldermoor=patron).
  - NPC template (line 79): forA = "Esca of Brackwater is pressing Bren ... invoking the patronage agreement to compel compliance"; forB = "Bren of Aldermoor is pressing Esca ... invoking the patronage agreement to compel compliance".
  Each settlement's dossier claims the OTHER side holds the patronage. The `client` templates exist but are never used for the partner perspective because both sides carry relType='patron'. This is internally-incoherent simulation output (violates the product's coherence goal), persisted to both saves and shown in both dossiers.

(2) UNSEEDED LIVE RENDER — REAL (medium). setActiveRng is called ONLY inside pipeline.js (lines 106/114). Both SettlementsPanel handlers and RelationshipsTab.jsx:27 invoke the generator outside the pipeline, so rngContext._activeRng is null and pick() falls back to Math.random() (rngContext.js:36-47). Demonstrated: two consecutive live calls produced different conflict natures ('tribute enforcement standoff' vs 'garrison obligation dispute') -> SAME OUTPUT? === false. In RelationshipsTab the result is in a useMemo whose deps include the re-allocating r?.neighborRelationship object, so the live-preview conflicts visibly change across re-renders/reloads. (For the persisted SettlementsPanel path the value is frozen at link time, so determinism-on-seed is not strictly broken there, but it is non-reproducible.)

(3) PDF PARITY — REAL, mechanism partially corrected (medium). The field s.crossSettlementConflicts that viewModel.js:1037 reads into crossConflicts is DEAD: grep shows it is read in exactly two places (viewModel.js:1037, RelationshipsTab.jsx:41) and ASSIGNED nowhere in src/ or supabase/. The actual conflict records are written into interSettlementRelationships (SettlementsPanel.jsx:874,877,935,938). Therefore the PDF's dedicated 'CROSS-SETTLEMENT CONFLICTS' section (Relationships.jsx:189-225) never renders.
