HOWEVER the conflict objects DO surface in the PDF, because interSettlementRelationships feeds viewModel interSettlement (viewModel.js:1036) which renders under 'SHARED FIGURES & STORIES' (Relationships.jsx:152-187, keyed on rel.npcName/description). So the report's 'omits them entirely' is not literally true for persisted conflicts — they render under a mislabeled section, and faction_engagement rows (no npcName) humanize to 'Relationship N'. The genuinely omitted case is the live/unsaved RelationshipsTab conflicts (linkId='live'), which are never persisted and thus never reach the PDF, plus the dead crossConflicts section. Net: real parity drift and a dead read field, just not the clean omission the report describes.

Overall severity: high, driven by the directional-symmetry contradiction (wrong, self-contradicting simulation output baked into persisted dossiers). The unseeded-render and parity issues are medium.
verdicts.fix_note:
Three distinct defects; fix each rather than just swapping args:

1) Directional contradiction (root cause). For directional relTypes (patron/client, vassal/overlord) the partner perspective must use the INVERSE relType, not the same one. crossSettlementConflicts.js receives a single relType because canonicalRelationship collapses patron/client to relationshipType:'patron' (canonicalRelationship.js:59-62). Two viable fixes:
   (a) Pass per-side roles into generateCrossSettlementConflicts (the callers already compute sourceRole/targetRole / definition.sourceRole/targetRole at SettlementsPanel.jsx:861-869, 922-930) and select the template by role, so forA uses 'patron' templates and forB uses 'client' templates (which already exist in CONFLICT_NATURE and buildConflictDesc), or
   (b) Inside generateCrossSettlementConflicts, when relType is directional, compute an inverse for descB (patron<->client) before the second buildConflictDesc/buildFactionDesc call. Note buildFactionDesc currently has NO 'client' template — add one (or have the client side reference being-overseen) so the inverse renders correctly. Symmetric relTypes (rival/hostile/cold_war/allied/trade_partner/neutral) are fine to mirror as-is.

2) Unseeded/non-deterministic. Either (a) make the live RelationshipsTab path deterministic by seeding from a stable key (e.g. derive a PRNG from `${settA.name}|${settB.name}|${relType}` and pass it in / setActiveRng around the call), or (b) stop generating live and only show persisted conflicts. At minimum, the generator should not silently fall back to Math.random in a render path. The 'live' memo deps should also drop the whole r?.neighborRelationship object (it re-allocates) to avoid re-rolls.

3) Dead field / PDF parity. crossSettlementConflicts is never written. Either populate it where conflicts are created (so the PDF 'CROSS-SETTLEMENT CONFLICTS' section works and conflicts stop appearing under 'SHARED FIGURES & STORIES'), or change the PDF viewModel to derive crossConflicts by filtering interSettlementRelationships for type==='conflict'||'faction_engagement' (mirroring RelationshipsTab.jsx:39-42) and filter those same typed rows OUT of the interSettlement slice so they don't double-render mislabeled. Also note faction_engagement rows have no npcName and would humanize to 'Relationship N' under the current interSettlement renderer — another reason to route them through the dedicated section.

confirmed.title: Custom settlement ages 1-4 years produce events older than the settlement (negative timeline years)
confirmed.evidence: resolveSettlementAge custom mode accepts any 0-5000 (lines 38-41); generateHistory gates the timeline only on age > 0 (line 1048). Event placement uses `randInt(Math.max(minYearsAgo, 5), maxYearsAgo)` (line 868): for age < 5 the min bound (5) exceeds maxYearsAgo, and randInt(5, 4) collapses to 5 — yielding yearsAgo=5 in a settlement aged 1-4, so eventsTimeline's `year: age - e.yearsAgo` (line 1079) goes negative and the dossier shows an event predating the founding.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Clamp the lower bound to Math.min(5, age) and skip timeline generation when age < ~5, mirroring the age<=0 path.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED real. I read the cited code and empirically reproduced the bug.

Path trace:
1. resolveSettlementAge custom mode (src/generators/historyGenerator.js:38-41) clamps to [0,5000], accepting 1-4. The UI input (ConfigurationPanel.jsx:434) has min={0}, so ages 1-4 are user-reachable.
2. generateHistory gates the timeline only on age > 0 (line 1048), so ages 1-4 enter generateRelationshipEvent.
3. In generateRelationshipEvent, rawEventCount = Math.floor((age/60) * randInt(1,3)) = 0 for age<60. BUT the per-tier eventCount clamps (lines 788-799) apply Math.max(1,...) for hamlet/village/town, Math.max(2,...) for city, Math.max(3,...) for metropolis — forcing eventCount >= 1 even when rawEventCount is 0. (thorp uses Math.min(rawEventCount,3) with no floor, so eventCount=0 and the bug does NOT occur for thorp.)
4. The loop reaches line 868: yearsAgo = randInt(Math.max(minYearsAgo,5), maxYearsAgo). For age=4 the earliest slot has maxYearsAgo=floor(4*1)=4, so randInt(5,4). randInt (rngContext.js:51) = Math.floor(rand*(4-5+1))+5 = Math.floor(0)+5 = 5. Deterministically returns 5.
5. eventsTimeline maps year: age - e.yearsAgo = 4 - 5 = -1 (line 1079). Negative year predating founding.

Empirical reproduction (node, seeded PRNG, generateHistory directly):
- thorp: 0/300 negative-year runs (no floor) — matches the one edge the claim didn't emphasize but is consistent.
- hamlet/village/town: 300/300 produce {year:-1, yearsAgo:5} at custom age=4.
- city: 164/300, metropolis: 183/300 (they always run multiple events; the earliest collapses to yearsAgo=5).
- Across ages: age 0 -> 0/200 (gated), ages 1,2,3,4 -> 200/200, age 5+ -> 0/200. Boundary is exactly at 5, matching the claim.

User-visible symptom: HistoryTab.jsx renders the event as "5y ago" (lines 142,163) in a settlement only 1-4 years old; the timeline dot position is visually clamped to 3% by Math.max(3,...) at line 129, but the "Ny ago" label and the underlying negative year field are wrong — an event predating the founding, which breaks simulation coherence.

Every file:line citation in the claim is correct, the randInt(5,4)=5 arithmetic is correct, the age>0 gating is correct, and the affected range (1-4) is correct. Could not refute. Severity 'low' is appropriate: no crash/data-loss/security/money; requires an unusual deliberate custom config; produces an incoherent (not catastrophic) timeline.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound. Two refinements: (1) The minimum bound of 5 is an undocumented "events must be at least 5 years old" floor — the cleanest fix is to clamp the lower bound to Math.min(5, age) at line 868 (i.e. const lo = Math.min(5, Math.max(minYearsAgo, ... )) but capped at maxYearsAgo) so yearsAgo can never exceed age. Equivalently, clamp yearsAgo with Math.min(yearsAgo, age) after the randInt. (2) Skipping timeline generation for small ages (mirroring the age<=0 path) also works but changes behavior for ages 5-? — note the boundary is exactly age<5, so the skip threshold should be age < 5, not "~5", to avoid suppressing valid timelines at age 5. Also note thorp tier is already unaffected (no Math.max floor), so any guard need only target the tiers with a forced eventCount floor; a clamp at line 868/1079 covers all tiers uniformly and is the lower-risk fix.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed and reproduced end-to-end against the real modules. Failure path: A user sets settlementAgeMode='custom' with settlementAgeYears in 1-4 (the ConfigurationPanel input at src/components/ConfigurationPanel.jsx:434-437 accepts min=0 max=5000, and store/configSlice.js:15 defaults settlementAgeYears). resolveSettlementAge (src/generators/historyGenerator.js:38-41) returns that age verbatim. generateHistory (line 1039,1048) gates the timeline on age>0, so generateRelationshipEvent runs. For ages 1-4, rawEventCount = Math.floor((age/60)*randInt(1,3)) is always 0, but the per-tier clamps force >=1 event for every tier except thorp (hamlet/village/town: Math.max(1,...); city: Math.max(2,...); metropolis: Math.max(3,...)) (lines 787-799). Event placement at line 868 is randInt(Math.max(minYearsAgo,5), maxYearsAgo); with tiny ages, minYearsAgo/maxYearsAgo floor to 0..4, so the lower bound becomes 5 while the upper bound is < 5. I verified randInt's behavior with inverted bounds against src/generators/rngContext.js:51-53: randInt(5,4)->{5}, randInt(5,3)->{4}, randInt(5,2)->{3,4}, randInt(5,1)->{2,3,4}, randInt(5,0)->{1,2,3,4}. In every case the resulting yearsAgo exceeds age. Running generateHistory directly for ages 1-4 across all tiers produced negative timeline years for every non-thorp tier (e.g. hamlet age=2 -> event 'The Economic Divide' yearsAgo=3 year=-1; metropolis age=1 -> three events with years [-2,-1,0]). thorp correctly produces 0 events. eventsTimeline[].year = age - yearsAgo (line 1079) is therefore negative. Consequence (data + user-visible coherence, not a crash): the dossier (src/components/new/tabs/HistoryTab.jsx:80) prints '{age} years old' while the same panel lists an event '{yearsAgo}y ago' (lines 163,284) with yearsAgo > age — an event predating the settlement's founding. The timeline-position math at HistoryTab.jsx:129 computes a negative percentage that gets clamped to 3%, and the negative year flows into both the on-screen dossier and the PDF (src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:855 passes h.eventsTimeline through). This violates the product's internal-coherence goal. It is deterministic (same seed+config reproduces), so it is not merely an edge flake. Severity medium: it is a real coherence/data-integrity defect on a genuine user config path, but only affects the narrow custom-age 1-4 window, does not crash, and does not break determinism, lose data, or touch money/auth.
verdicts.fix_note: The claim's math is slightly narrower than reality: it states randInt(5,4) 'collapses to 5' for age 4, but the bug spans all ages 1-4 and yields yearsAgo in {2,3,4,5} depending on age (verified empirically), because randInt with min>max silently returns values in an unintended range rather than just the min. The real root cause is the hardcoded floor of 5 in Math.max(minYearsAgo, 5) at historyGenerator.js:868, which assumes auto-generated ages (decades+) and is incompatible with sub-5-year ages. A correct fix must clamp the placement window to [1, age], not just lower-bound it at 5. Suggested: const lo = Math.min(Math.max(minYearsAgo, 1), age); const hi = Math.max(lo, Math.min(maxYearsAgo, age)); const yearsAgo = randInt(lo, hi); — guaranteeing 1 <= yearsAgo <= age so year = age - yearsAgo is always >= 0. Alternatively/additionally, gate event generation so very young settlements (age below some threshold, or where rawEventCount===0) produce no timeline events instead of being force-clamped to >=1 by the tier minimums (lines 788-799) — that tier-minimum clamp is the secondary contributing cause and arguably wrong for any young settlement regardless of the floor. Whichever path, also confirm thorp's behavior (Math.min(rawEventCount,3) -> 0 events) is the intended baseline. Any fix changes RNG draw counts/values, so determinism snapshot/property tests will need regolden.

confirmed.title: Timeline event variety is collapsed: one fixed template per category and an effective ~8-event cap that makes the metropolis 20-event budget unreachable
confirmed.evidence: Inside the event loop, `HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA.find(e => (typeMap[cat]||[]).includes(e.type))` (lines 835-856) always returns the FIRST template in data order for each category even though typeMap lists 4-8 types (e.g. political lists 8 types but always selects 'succession_crisis', the first in HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA). Combined with usedCats/usedNames dedup (lines 824-861), at most ~8 distinct events can ever be produced before every iteration `continue`s — while the metropolis branch budgets up to 20 (line 799). The '(simplified — use a generic template)' comment acknowledges the template part but the count budget silently never binds, and political variety (corruption_scandal, leadership_vacuum, disputed_land, ...) only appears via the (index-buggy) anchor pass fallbacks.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Pick randomly among the typeMap-listed templates (seeded) instead of .find, and dedupe by template name while allowing multiple events per category for large tiers.

verdicts.reasoning:
I read src/generators/historyGenerator.js:782-1017 (the generateRelationshipEvent body), the typeMap/.find at 835-856, the dedup at 858-861, the anchor pass at 886-913, the resource-event gate at 998, and the HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA ordering in src/data/historyData.js:1088-1280. The claim's core mechanics check out:

1. Collapsed template variety (CONFIRMED). `HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA.find(e => (typeMap[cat]||[]).includes(e.type))` returns the FIRST array element matching any listed type. Because data order is fixed, each category resolves to exactly one deterministic template: economic→economic_disparity (data line 1101), political→succession_crisis (line 1090, first in data even though typeMap lists 8 types), disaster→external_threat, religious→religious_tension, magical→magical_controversy, occupation_infiltration→infiltration_fear, exile_return→occupation_legacy, demographic→population_friction. The in-code comment "(simplified — use a generic template)" (line 834) acknowledges this.

2. Effective ~8-event cap (CONFIRMED). generateSafetyNarrative2 (lines 247-291) defines exactly 8 categories (7 when config.magicExists===false deletes 'magical' at line 805). Each category yields one fixed template; templates have no name field so tmplName = tmpl.type (line 859), and the 8 resolved types are all distinct. usedNames (lines 858-861) makes every iteration after the 8th distinct template hit `continue`, producing nothing. The codebase corroborates this: line 998 only adds a resource event when `events.length < 7`.

3. Metropolis 20 budget unreachable (CONFIRMED). Line 799 sets eventCount up to 20 for metropolis, but the loop can emit at most ~8 distinct events, so the count budget silently never binds — fewer/less-varied events than the budget implies.

Determinism is intact (_rng from rngContext.js line 14, randInt from helpers line 13), so this is not a determinism break — it is a richness/quality shortfall. No test pins timeline event count or variety (tests/domain/historyBeats.test.js asserts only founding-cause beat rows, not timeline). 

One sub-claim is slightly imprecise but does not refute the finding: the claim says political variety (corruption_scandal, leadership_vacuum, disputed_land, ...) appears via the anchor-pass fallbacks. In fact the anchor pass typeMap (lines 894-900) also maps political→'succession_crisis' (a single string) and its `|| events[idx]` fallback merely keeps the existing event, so those extra political templates are effectively dead data in this path — making the defect marginally worse than described, not better. Severity low is correct: deterministic, no data loss / money / security / crash; the impact is less varied histories for large tiers than the data set and budget would allow.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is sound: seeded-pick among the typeMap-listed templates rather than .find, and dedupe by template type while permitting multiple events per category for large tiers so the metropolis budget can actually bind. Caveat to the reviewer's wording: the extra political templates do NOT currently surface via the anchor pass — that pass also collapses political to a single 'succession_crisis' string, so corruption_scandal/leadership_vacuum/disputed_land/etc. are unreachable dead data in generateRelationshipEvent until the .find selection is fixed. Also note the parallel typeMap inside the anchor pass (lines 894-900) needs the same treatment, otherwise re-anchored events stay collapsed even after fixing the main loop.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via end-to-end reproduction against the real modules. Concrete failure path: generateHistory (src/generators/historyGenerator.js:1048) -> generateRelationshipEvent. For a metropolis with a high age (e.g. settlementAgeMode:'custom', settlementAgeYears>=800), the eventCount budget at line 799 is Math.max(3, Math.min(rawEventCount, 20)) — i.e. up to 20 events. But the main event loop (lines 825-876) selects each event template with HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA.find(e => (typeMap[cat]||[]).includes(e.type)) (line 835). `.find()` always returns the FIRST template in data order. There are exactly 8 categories (economic, political, disaster, religious, magical, occupation_infiltration, exile_return, demographic; magical dropped only when magicExists===false), each collapsing to one fixed template. usedCats (line 823, 831-832) bars category repeats and usedNames (line 824, 858-861) bars name repeats, so the loop can emit at most one event per category.

Reproduction (300 seeds, metropolis, age 800 and age 2000, RNG seeded via rngContext.setActiveRng + prng.createPRNG):
- max events in ANY timeline = 7 (never 8, never 20); avg ~5.68. The 20-budget is unreachable.
- Only 7 distinct event NAMES are ever produced across all seeds: The Succession Crisis, The Religious Conflict, The Economic Divide, The Arcane Incident, The Siege, The Occupation, The Migration.
- Per-category variety collapse confirmed: with the most political-heavy stress config possible (politically_fractured, succession_void, indebted, occupied, infiltrated) over 500 seeds, the political category ONLY ever yields 'The Succession Crisis'. The 7 other political templates in the data (corruption_scandal, leadership_vacuum, infiltration_fear, disputed_land, population_friction, generational_divide, occupation_legacy — historyData.js lines 1189,1279,1231,1247,1263,1178,1215) NEVER surface, despite typeMap listing all 8 (lines 838-847).

The anchor pass (lines 886-913) cannot rescue variety: its typeMap (lines 894-901) maps political->'succession_crisis' (still first template) and omits demographic/exile_return/occupation_infiltration/disaster keys entirely, so for those cats typeMap[cat] is undefined, .find() returns undefined, and it falls back to events[idx] (line 902). That fallback is also index-buggy: anchored.forEach((event, idx) iterates the ascending-sorted copy `anchored` (sorted line 883) while events[idx] indexes the descending-sorted original `events` (sorted line 878), so it grabs a mismatched event and overwrites its .type — I observed an event named 'The Arcane Incident' relabeled type:'demographic' in real output, confirming the mislabel.

Consequences (data-level / simulation quality): (1) The advertised tier-scaled depth is a lie — a metropolis and a town are effectively indistinguishable in timeline length (both cap near 7-8), undermining the tier=>depth contract. (2) Historical chronicles for every settlement draw from the same 7-name pool, so the simulation's claimed coherence/variety in history is far thinner than the data supports (16 templates exist; only ~7 are ever reachable). (3) The mislabeled anchor events produce category/name mismatches (a 'demographic' event whose narrative is the arcane incident), a real data-integrity defect that flows into the dossier and historicalCharacter derivation (lines 1061-1067 count by .type). Not a crash, not money/security, so high (wrong simulation output + dead budget branch) rather than critical.
verdicts.fix_note: The claim's framing is accurate; two corrections to the implied fix. (1) Don't just pick a random template from the category's type list — although that fixes per-category variety (e.g. political's 8 types), it still caps total events at the number of categories (8), because usedCats/usedNames forbid category AND name repeats. To actually make the metropolis 20-budget bind, you must allow a category to be reused with a DIFFERENT (unused) template: replace the do/while category-dedup-with-attempts<5 plus the hard usedNames `continue` with a scheme that, on category collision, picks an as-yet-unused template within that category, and only `continue`s when the whole category's template pool is exhausted. With 16 templates total, the realistic ceiling is ~16 distinct events, still short of 20 for the oldest metropolis — so either accept the data-limited ceiling (and lower the metropolis Math.min cap to match the template pool) or expand HISTORICAL_EVENTS_DATA. (2) Independently fix the anchor pass at lines 886-913: add the missing typeMap keys (demographic, exile_return, occupation_infiltration, disaster already present), and fix the index bug — the `|| events[idx]` fallback indexes the original `events` array with an index from the re-sorted `anchored` array; either iterate over `events` directly or use `|| event` (the current loop item) instead of `events[idx]`. Both selection changes must continue to route through the seeded _rng()/pick so determinism (same seed => same settlement) is preserved; do NOT introduce Math.random or wall-clock. Add/adjust snapshot+property tests since timeline length and name distribution are load-bearing.

confirmed.title: findFaction searches the wrong faction list — faction-targeted events silently no-op on every generated settlement
confirmed.evidence: findFaction reads `const list = s.factions || s.powerStructure?.factions || []` (mutate.js:1383-1392). Generated settlements ALWAYS carry a top-level `factions` array — assembleSettlement.js sets `factions` to the NPC social-cluster output of powerGenerator.generateFactions ({name, members, dominantCategory} entries like 'The Tanners Circle'), while the real power factions ({faction:'Merchant Guild', power, ...}) live in `powerStructure.factions`. Since even an empty array is truthy, findFaction NEVER reaches powerStructure.factions on a generated settlement. Every other layer prefers powerStructure first: EventComposer offers targets from `settlement.powerStructure?.factions` (EventComposer.jsx:115), batch validation builds its namespace from `s?.powerStructure?.factions || s?.factions` (batch.js:307), propagate.js factionsList (line 278), factionResponses.js:32 and factionProfile.js:263-266 all do the same. So IMPAIR_FACTION / RESTORE_FACTION / EXPOSE_CORRUPTION (faction fallback) / swapNpcStanding's stable-id stamp pass batch validation, land their authored systemState deltas + narration in the canon timeline, fire the party-impact world ripple (partyEventLinkage maps IMPAIR_FACTION → undermine_faction), yet the local faction entity is never impaired — no impairment record, no propagation, no faction-response visibility. Empirically reproduced: mutateSettlement({factions:[social cluster], powerStructure:{factions:[{faction:'Merchant Guild'}]}}, IMPAIR_FACTION 'Merchant Guild') leaves powerStructure.factions[0].impairments undefined; same with `factions: []`. Tests pass only because the fixture (tests/domain/events.test.js:12-31) has no top-level factions array. Note addFaction (mutate.js:372) uses the OPPOSITE precedence (psFactions first), so an ADD_FACTION followed by IMPAIR_FACTION of that same faction also no-ops.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make findFaction search powerStructure.factions first (matching every reader and replaceFaction's write preference), or search both lists: `[...(s.powerStructure?.factions||[]), ...(s.factions||[])]`. Add a test fixture carrying both lists, as every generated settlement does.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code; the claim holds in every cited particular.

1) findFaction (src/domain/events/mutate.js:1384) reads `const list = s.factions || s.powerStructure?.factions || []` — top-level `factions` is checked first.

2) Generated settlements always carry a top-level `factions` array. assembleSettlement.js:77 assigns `factions` from the generatePopulation step (generatePopulation.js:35 = `generateFactions(npcs, relationships)`), whose entries are NPC social clusters shaped `{ name, members, dominantCategory }` (powerGenerator.js:2279). The real power factions, shaped `{ faction: 'Merchant Guild', ... }`, live separately in `powerStructure.factions` (generatePower.js / generatePowerStructure, e.g. powerGenerator.js:944 `faction: P`). Even when the social-cluster array is empty `[]`, it is truthy, so findFaction never falls through to powerStructure.factions on a generated settlement.

3) Every other layer prefers powerStructure first, creating a validation/mutation asymmetry: EventComposer faction target options come from `settlement.powerStructure?.factions` (EventComposer.jsx:115), so the targetId for IMPAIR/RESTORE/EXPOSE is a power-faction identity (id = item.id||item.faction||item.name, EventComposer.jsx:165). batch.js:307 builds the validation namespace from `s?.powerStructure?.factions || s?.factions` (powerStructure first), so those events PASS validation. factionResponses.js:32 and factionProfile.js:263 also read powerStructure first. Even within mutate.js, replaceFaction (1414) and addFaction (370) normalize on powerStructure.factions first — findFaction is the lone reader with inverted precedence.

4) The no-op is silent: impairFaction (mutate.js:336 `if (!faction) return s`), restoreFaction (353), and exposeCorruption faction-fallback (760 `if (!target) return s`) all return the settlement unchanged when findFaction returns undefined, so the registry-declared systemState deltas + narration still land in the canon timeline while the faction entity is never impaired — no impairment record, no propagateImpairment, no faction-response visibility. This is a coherence break: the dossier/timeline shows the event happened, but the targeted power faction is unaffected.

5) Tests pass because the fixture baseSettlement (tests/domain/events.test.js:24-31) puts factions ONLY under powerStructure.factions with no top-level `factions` array, so findFaction's fallthrough works in tests but never on real settlements.

The addFaction-then-impair note also checks out: addFaction (mutate.js:389) writes new factions into powerStructure.factions, which findFaction then cannot reach if a top-level factions array exists.

I attempted to refute via: (a) the path being unreachable — refuted, EventComposer surfaces power-faction targets and batch validation lets them through; (b) an upstream guard normalizing factions before mutate — not found; the engine deliberately tolerates both locations (replaceFaction's own comment at 1412-1413) but findFaction's precedence is the outlier; (c) intentional behavior — no comment or test supports top-level-first precedence; the surrounding code uniformly prefers powerStructure. The defect is real.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct. Preferred form matches every other reader and replaceFaction's write target: search powerStructure.factions first, then the top-level list, e.g. `const list = (s.powerStructure?.factions?.length ? s.powerStructure.factions : null) || s.factions || s.powerStructure?.factions || []`. Cleaner and more robust against ID collisions across the two lists is to search the union: `const list = [...(s.powerStructure?.factions||[]), ...(s.factions||[])]` — this also matters because replaceFaction normalizes on powerStructure, so findFaction must return an object that is === an element of the list replaceFaction will scan; with a union, a match found in powerStructure.factions will be correctly replaced there, while a top-level social-cluster match falls back to replaceFaction's second branch (s.factions). Add a regression fixture carrying BOTH a top-level `factions` array (social clusters with `name` only) and `powerStructure.factions` (with `faction:` keys), then assert IMPAIR_FACTION of a powerStructure-only faction writes an impairment to powerStructure.factions[i].impairments. Minor: the reviewer's one-line `[...ps, ...flat]` union is the safest choice given replaceFaction's identity-based (===) matching.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED and reproduced against the real module. findFaction (src/domain/events/mutate.js:1383-1384) reads `const list = s.factions || s.powerStructure?.factions || []` — top-level `factions` FIRST. On every generated settlement, top-level `factions` is the NPC social-cluster output of powerGenerator.generateFactions (entries `{name, members, dominantCategory}`, e.g. 'The Tanners Circle'), set unconditionally at assembleSettlement.js:77 from ctx.factions (generatePopulation.js:35/148). The real power factions (`{faction:'Merchant Guild', power,...}`) live in powerStructure.factions (assembleSettlement.js:73). Because generateFactions always returns an array (powerGenerator.js:2238 returns [] when empty) and even [] is truthy, `s.factions || ...` short-circuits before reaching powerStructure.factions — so findFaction searches the wrong list and a power-faction target name never matches (clusters carry no `faction`/`id`, and their `name` is a cluster name, not the guild name).

Reproduced with node against the real src/domain/events/mutate.js:
- {factions:[social cluster], powerStructure:{factions:[{faction:'Merchant Guild'}]}} + IMPAIR_FACTION 'Merchant Guild' => powerStructure.factions[0].impairments === undefined (no-op).
- {factions:[]} (empty but truthy) + same event => still undefined (no-op).
- Control {no top-level factions key} + same event => impairments populated correctly — isolating precedence as the cause.
- ADD_FACTION 'Smugglers Ring' (addFaction writes to powerStructure.factions, mutate.js:389-390) followed by IMPAIR_FACTION of that faction => impairments === [] (no-op) — confirming the opposite-precedence interaction.

End-to-end failure path: The UI EventComposer offers faction targets from settlement.powerStructure?.factions (EventComposer.jsx:115); batch validation builds its faction namespace from `s?.powerStructure?.factions || s?.factions` (batch.js:307-308), so IMPAIR_FACTION/RESTORE_FACTION/EXPOSE_CORRUPTION(faction fallback)/swapNpcStanding stable-id stamp/KILL_NPC faction impairments PASS validation and the event is admitted to the canon timeline with its authored systemState deltas + narration. partyEventLinkage.js:28 maps IMPAIR_FACTION→undermine_faction, so the party-impact world ripple fires. Yet the local faction entity in powerStructure.factions is never touched — no impairment record, no propagation (propagateImpairment looks via factionsList which is powerStructure-first, propagate.js:278), no faction-response visibility (factionResponses.js:32). Every other layer (EventComposer.jsx:115, batch.js:307, propagate.js:278, factionResponses.js:32, factionProfile.js:263-265, addFaction at mutate.js:370) prefers powerStructure FIRST; findFaction is the lone reversed reader.

Tests pass because tests/domain/events.test.js:12-31 baseSettlement has NO top-level factions key (only powerStructure.factions) — i.e. the control case that works — so the fixture shape masks the bug that every real generated settlement triggers.

Affected handlers (all 5 findFaction callers operate on power-faction concepts): impairFaction (335), restoreFaction (352), killNpcMutation faction impairments (661), exposeCorruption faction fallback (758), swapNpcStanding stable-id stamp (1356). Severity high (not critical): silent wrong simulation output / determinism-relevant state divergence on a main authoring path, not a crash or data-loss; the event still records, so the timeline isn't lost — but the faction substrate it claims to change is unaffected, breaking simulation coherence (validation says valid, mutation no-ops). No money/auth/security impact.
verdicts.fix_note: Correct fix: flip the precedence in findFaction (mutate.js:1384) to match every sibling layer — `const list = s.powerStructure?.factions || s.factions || []`. This is safe: all five callers want the real power-faction record (carrying id/impairments/power), never the NPC social-cluster, so preferring powerStructure is desirable; replaceFaction (mutate.js:1411-1433) already normalizes onto powerStructure.factions first, so a faction found there will be written back consistently. Consider extracting a single shared factionsList helper (it already exists at propagate.js:278 as `s?.powerStructure?.factions || s?.factions || []`) so mutate.js, batch.js, factionResponses.js, factionProfile.js, and EventComposer all share one precedence and this class of drift can't recur — and harden the test fixture (tests/domain/events.test.js:12) by adding a top-level `factions` array of NPC-cluster shape so the regression is actually exercised.

confirmed.title: Batch preview computes authored stateDeltas from the already-mutated settlement — RESOLVE_STRESSOR severity drifts from what apply delivers
confirmed.evidence: applyEventBatch mutates first (`working = mutateSettlement({settlement: working, event, now})`, line 238) and then calls `spec.stateDeltas(event, working)` (line 244) on the post-mutation settlement. The single-event pipeline calls `spec.stateDeltas(event, beforeSettlement)` (eventPipeline.js:184), and registry.js's RESOLVE_STRESSOR spec explicitly depends on that: 'the registry computes from the BEFORE settlement, so the entry is still present here' (registry.js:656-668) — it reads the removed stress entry's recorded severity. In the batch path the entry has already been removed by the event's own mutation, so the lookup misses and severity falls back to 0.5. Reproduced: a 0.9-severity famine yields summedStateDeltas resilience +6/volatility -6/resourcePressure -6 in the batch path vs +11/-11/-11 from the single-event authored deltas. The store previews batches via this function (settlementSlice.js:1434-1442 previewEventBatch → computeEventBatch) but APPLIES via the per-event applyEvent pipeline (settlementSlice.js:1463-1480), so the batch preview the DM confirms disagrees with what is committed — the exact drift class Phase 18 was built to eliminate.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Capture the pre-mutation settlement per event (`const before = working;`) before threading the mutation, and call `spec.stateDeltas(event, before)` — mirroring eventPipeline's contract.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified end to end; the claim holds.

DIVERGENT PATHS (confirmed): The store previews a batch via previewEventBatch → computeEventBatch (settlementSlice.js:1438), which is the aliased import `applyEventBatch as computeEventBatch` from domain/events/batch.js (settlementSlice.js:70). But it COMMITS the batch via the store's own applyEventBatch, which loops the per-event single-event pipeline `get().applyEvent(event)` (settlementSlice.js:1474-1476). So preview and apply genuinely use different delta-computation code.

THE BUG (confirmed): In batch.js, applyEventBatch mutates first — `working = mutateSettlement({settlement: working, event, now})` (line 238) — then calls `spec.stateDeltas(event, working)` on the POST-mutation settlement (line 244). The single-event pipeline instead passes the pre-mutation settlement: `spec.stateDeltas(event, beforeSettlement)` (eventPipeline.js:184).

WHY IT MATTERS FOR RESOLVE_STRESSOR (confirmed): The RESOLVE_STRESSOR mutation removes the stress entry — resolveStressor → crisisResolve filters it out of the canonical container with `list.filter((_,i)=>i!==idx)` (crisisLifecycle.js:296-309). The registry's RESOLVE_STRESSOR.stateDeltas scales by the REMOVED entry's recorded severity and explicitly documents its dependency on the BEFORE settlement: `// the registry computes from the BEFORE settlement, so the entry is still present here` (registry.js:656-668). It reads `Number(entry?.severity ?? event.payload?.severity)`; on a miss it falls back to sev=0.5.

THE FALLBACK ACTUALLY TRIGGERS (confirmed): Composer-built RESOLVE_STRESSOR events carry only `stressorType` and `label` in payload — no `severity` (EventComposer.jsx:376-380). And stress entries store a numeric severity (crisisLifecycle.js:156,187). So in the batch path the post-mutation lookup misses, `event.payload.severity` is undefined, rawSev is NaN, and sev collapses to 0.5 — exactly as claimed. The single-event path reads the live entry's real severity. For a famine (matches the scarcity regex → resourcePressure delta) at severity 0.85 (composer max), single-event yields +10/-10/-10 while batch yields +6/-6/-6; the claim's +11/-11/-11 vs +6/-6/-6 assumes severity 0.9 (achievable via generated or directly-authored stressors, which carry arbitrary 0..1 numeric severities). Magnitude differs slightly from the write-up but the drift class is exactly as described.

NO COMPENSATION / NOT INTENTIONAL: tests/domain/eventBatch.test.js exercises RESOLVE_STRESSOR reference resolution in batches but never asserts summedStateDeltas/severity parity with the single-event path, so nothing pins this as intended behavior. The within-batch case (apply then resolve in one batch, test line 207) hits the same post-mutation miss; the cleanest manifestation is resolving a pre-existing stressor — the composer's documented picker flow.

This is real preview-vs-apply SystemState parity drift: the DM confirms a batch preview whose RESOLVE_STRESSOR resilience/volatility/resourcePressure deltas do not match what the per-event commit actually writes. Wrong (but non-destructive, recoverable) simulation output on a real feature path → medium.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct and matches eventPipeline's contract: capture the pre-mutation settlement per event (`const before = working;`) before `working = mutateSettlement(...)`, then call `spec.stateDeltas(event, before)`. Note also that narrate is already correctly passed `beforeSettlement` (batch.js:251), so only the stateDeltas call needs the change. Minor accuracy caveat for the report: the exact reproduced deltas (+11/-11/-11) require severity 0.9; the EventComposer slider caps at 0.85 (severe → +10), though generated/authored stressors can carry higher numeric severities — the drift exists at any severity != 0.5, the magnitude just scales.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via end-to-end trace + live reproduction against the real modules.

Failure path:
1. DM stages a RESOLVE_STRESSOR for a non-0.5-severity stressor (e.g. a 0.9 famine) and clicks preview. Store previewEventBatch (settlementSlice.js:1434-1452) calls computeEventBatch = batch.js applyEventBatch.
2. batch.js:238 mutates first: `working = mutateSettlement({settlement: working, event, now})`. For RESOLVE_STRESSOR this routes mutate.js:148 -> resolveStressor -> crisisResolve -> windDownCrisis (crisisLifecycle.js:304-310), which removes the matching entry from the stress container (`list.filter((_,i)=>i!==idx)`).
3. batch.js:244 then calls `spec.stateDeltas(event, working)` on the POST-mutation settlement. The RESOLVE_STRESSOR spec (registry.js:661-668) does `settlement[containerKey].find(...)` to read the removed entry's recorded severity — but the entry is gone, so `entry` is null. `rawSev = Number(entry?.severity ?? event.payload?.severity)`; the real EventComposer (EventComposer.jsx:376-380) sets NO payload.severity for RESOLVE_STRESSOR, so rawSev = NaN -> fallback sev = 0.5.
4. The commit path is different: store applyEventBatch (settlementSlice.js:1463-1480) commits via per-event get().applyEvent, whose afterState comes from layerAuthoredDeltas(deriveSystemState(nextSettlement), event, state.settlement) (settlementSlice.js:1249) — passing the BEFORE settlement, where the entry is still present at full severity. This matches the single-event pipeline (eventPipeline.js:184 passes beforeSettlement).

Live reproduction (node, real modules) for a 0.9 famine with no payload.severity:
- Registry vs BEFORE (entry present): {resilience:11, volatility:-11, resourcePressure:-11}
- Single-event pipeline systemStateDeltas: resilience +11 / volatility -11 / resourcePressure -11
- Batch summedStateDeltas: {resilience:6, volatility:-6, resourcePressure:-6}
- Batch systemStateDeltas: resilience +6 / volatility -6 / resourcePressure -6

Consequence: The batch preview the DM confirms (settlementSlice.js:1446-1447 surfaces result.systemStateDeltas and afterSystemState, both derived from the wrong summedStateDeltas) disagrees with what is committed. Drift magnitude scales with |severity-0.5|*12 per dimension: ~5 points at sev 0.9, 0 at sev 0.5. This is precisely the preview==apply invariant Phase 18 was built to enforce (eventPipeline.js header: "preview and apply produce the same afterSystemState"). No determinism break and no data corruption — the committed values are the CORRECT ones; only the preview is wrong — hence medium, not high.

Scope note: the hazard is general to any spec whose stateDeltas reads an entity that its own mutation removes/alters. Today RESOLVE_STRESSOR is the only registry spec that reads a removed entry's pre-mutation state inside stateDeltas, so it is the only triggering event. Confidence 0.92: a skeptical reviewer with full access can run the same one-liner and see +6 vs +11.
verdicts.fix_note:
The right fix is to compute authored stateDeltas from the PRE-mutation settlement, mirroring the single-event pipeline (eventPipeline.js:184 uses beforeSettlement) which the commit path matches. Concretely, in batch.js capture the input to mutateSettlement before mutating and pass it to stateDeltas:

  const beforeThis = working;                         // pre-mutation for THIS event
  working = mutateSettlement({ settlement: working, event, now });
  const deltas = (typeof spec.stateDeltas === 'function'
    ? spec.stateDeltas(event, beforeThis)            // BEFORE, not post-mutation `working`
    : {}) || {};

Two caveats so the fix is actually correct, not just symmetric:

1. Use the per-event before-state (the settlement as it stood entering THIS event in the batch), NOT the batch's original beforeSettlement. Within a batch an earlier event could legitimately add the stressor a later event resolves (eventProduces registers APPLY_STRESSOR output for exactly this), so the resolving event's stateDeltas must read the threaded `working` as it was just before its own mutation — which beforeThis above captures. Passing the batch-wide beforeSettlement would miss an intra-batch-created entry. This is also why narrate at batch.js:251 currently uses beforeSettlement; for label resolution that's mostly fine, but for consistency narrate arguably wants beforeThis too if a label depends on intra-batch state — lower stakes, optional.

2. This only re-aligns the AUTHORED deltas (summedStateDeltas / the +11). The batch's structural afterSystemState is still derived once from the final `working` (batch.js:258), whereas the commit path re-derives structurally per event then layers authored deltas each time. For RESOLVE_STRESSOR the structural component is small, but a reviewer should confirm batch vs serial single-event afterSystemState agree on a multi-event batch after this fix — the authored-delta fix is necessary but the preview==apply invariant for batches ideally wants the batch preview to simply run the same per-event runEventPipeline sequence the commit does, rather than maintaining a parallel summation. Minimal fix (beforeThis) closes the reported drift; the structural-parity concern is a separate, smaller question worth a confirming test.

confirmed.title: Undo never reverts neighbour-relationship events; the _relationshipEventId stamp is written but read nowhere
confirmed.evidence: setNeighbourRelationship (SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE / BROKERED_ALLIANCE / OPENED_TRADE_ROUTE) overwrites link.relationshipType/displayRelationshipType and stamps `_relationshipEventId: event.id` (mutate.js:582-601). A repo-wide grep shows that stamp has exactly one occurrence — the write site; no scrub reads it. undoLastEvent (store/settlementSlice.js:1500-1524) strips causeEventId impairments and calls scrubUndoneEvent, which only handles conditions, stress entries, config annotation ledgers, destruction stamps, and snapshotted records (undoEvent.js:302-312); SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS (undoEvent.js:53-68) has no entry for these event types. So undoing a BROKERED_ALLIANCE restores systemState to beforeState but leaves the neighbour permanently 'allied' — and the prior relationshipType was overwritten, so it is unrecoverable without a pre-event snapshot. The orphaned provenance stamp strongly suggests undo support that was never wired. undoEvent.js's header claims 'everything mutate writes WITH provenance ... is scrubbed by that provenance', which this violates.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add these three event types to the snapshot family (snapshot the matched neighbourNetwork link pre-event onto logEntry.undo and restore it in restoreSnapshottedRecords), or scrub by the existing _relationshipEventId stamp with a stored previousRelationshipType.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code; the claim holds end to end.

WRITE: mutate.js:582-601 `setNeighbourRelationship` is dispatched for SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE / BROKERED_ALLIANCE / OPENED_TRADE_ROUTE (mutate.js:81-84). For a matched neighbourNetwork link it spreads `...link` and overwrites `relationshipType` + `displayRelationshipType` (line 597), discarding the prior values, and stamps `_relationshipEventId: event.id`.

ORPHANED STAMP: a repo-wide grep for `_relationshipEventId` returns exactly one hit — mutate.js:597 (the write). No code reads it. This matches the reviewer's claim and strongly signals undo wiring that was never finished.

NO SNAPSHOT: undo snapshots are produced by `captureEventUndoSnapshot` (applyEvent.js:40), which returns null for any type absent from SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS (undoEvent.js:53-68). None of the three relationship types are listed, so `logEntry.undo` is omitted (applyEvent.js:55). Thus `restoreSnapshottedRecords` (undoEvent.js:124-148) has nothing to restore for them.

NO SCRUB: undoLastEvent (settlementSlice.js:1500-1524) restores systemState to beforeState, strips causeEventId impairments, and calls `scrubUndoneEvent` (undoEvent.js:302-312). That entry point runs withoutEventConditions / withoutEventStressEntries / withoutEventAnnotations / withoutEventDestruction / restoreSnapshottedRecords / withEventConditionsSynced — none of which touch neighbourNetwork links or the `_relationshipEventId` stamp. So after undo the neighbour stays at the event-set relationshipType (e.g. permanently 'allied'), and the original value is unrecoverable.

USER-VISIBLE + PERSISTED: presentation reads `relationshipType` directly (RelationshipsTab.jsx:24, pdf/lib/viewModel.js:1042), so the wrong value shows in both dossier and exported PDF. The corrupted settlement is also written back to the save (settlementSlice.js:1529-1538), so it survives reload. This also violates the undoEvent.js header invariant that everything mutate writes with provenance is scrubbed by that provenance.

I could not find any upstream guard, dead-path, or intentional-comment that defuses this. The cited APIs behave exactly as the reviewer described.

Severity = medium (not high): it is real wrong simulation output / a broken undo seam, but it is edge-case — it requires applying one of three specific relationship events and then explicitly undoing. It is not a crash, money, security, or main-path break.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is sound. Cleanest path given the existing machinery: add the three event types to the snapshot family. SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS keys off config-level record keys, but neighbourNetwork is a top-level settlement array, so captureEventUndoSnapshot/restoreSnapshottedRecords would need a small extension to snapshot the matched neighbourNetwork link(s) pre-event onto logEntry.undo and restore them (delete-vs-restore semantics like snapshotKeys). Alternatively, scrub by the existing `_relationshipEventId` stamp, but that requires also storing previousRelationshipType/previousDisplayRelationshipType at write time (mutate.js:597) since the current overwrite destroys them. Note the same overwrite-class limitation called out elsewhere in undoEvent.js applies: two relationship events against the same neighbour, then one undo, can only restore the immediately-prior snapshot, not an arbitrarily older one — acceptable and consistent with the file's documented conventions.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end against the real modules. Trace:

INPUT/STATE: A settlement with a neighbourNetwork link (e.g. {name:'Stonehaven', relationshipType:'neutral'}) and a relationship event applied via the event system: BROKERED_ALLIANCE / SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE / OPENED_TRADE_ROUTE. mutate.js:81-84 routes all three to setNeighbourRelationship.

WHAT GOES WRONG (verified by reading + a read-only node run on the real modules):
- setNeighbourRelationship (mutate.js:582-601) OVERWRITES link.relationshipType and link.displayRelationshipType (the prior value is lost in place) and stamps _relationshipEventId: event.id.
- That stamp has exactly one occurrence in the whole repo — the write site (grep confirmed); nothing reads it.
- On undo, settlementSlice.undoLastEvent (1500-1524) restores systemState=beforeState (1507), strips causeEventId impairments off entity lists, and calls scrubUndoneEvent (1522). scrubUndoneEvent (undoEvent.js:302-312) handles only conditions, stress entries, config annotation ledgers, destruction stamps, and snapshotted records — it never touches neighbourNetwork.
- The snapshot escape hatch does not cover these events: captureEventUndoSnapshot (undoEvent.js:106-113) returns null for any type absent from SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS (53-68), and that table has no BROKERED_ALLIANCE / SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE / OPENED_TRADE_ROUTE entry. My node trace printed captureEventUndoSnapshot(BROKERED_ALLIANCE) = null and, after scrubUndoneEvent, relationshipType still 'allied' (expected 'neutral'). REVERTED CORRECTLY = false.

CONSEQUENCE: Undoing one of these events leaves the neighbour permanently at the event-set relationship (e.g. 'allied' / 'rival'). Because the prior label was overwritten in place with no snapshot, it is unrecoverable. This is durable, not transient: neighbourNetwork is NOT produced by the generator pipeline (no producer in src/generators; the pipeline only takes an importedNeighbour and emits the legacy singular neighborRelationship, which RelationshipsTab.jsx:53 notes is migrated into neighbourNetwork at save time). region/graph.js:326 (H10) explicitly treats the saves' neighbourNetwork as the CANONICAL relationship source. applyChange (settlementSlice.js:880) reconciles via settlementReconciliation.js, which does not regenerate neighbourNetwork. So a wrongly-left 'allied' link is rendered directly by the dossier (RelationshipsTab.jsx:24/56) and feeds threatProfile, regionalGraph, worldPulse, and region/graph edge labels until the user manually edits it.

PROVENANCE EVIDENCE OF INTENT: undoEvent.js header (lines 15-17) asserts 'everything mutate writes WITH provenance ... is scrubbed by that provenance'. setNeighbourRelationship writes provenance (_relationshipEventId) that is scrubbed by nothing — the orphaned stamp is a clear unfinished seam: undo support was stubbed but never wired.

NOT-A-CRASH, NOT money/security: it is wrong simulation output on the undo path for a real authored-event feature, with permanent data corruption of the prior relationship label (irrecoverable in-app). Severity medium: it is wrong/lossy output but confined to the undo path of three discretionary DM events and does not affect the main generation path, billing, or auth. Confidence high (~0.88) — a skeptical reviewer with full access would see the empty SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS, the single-occurrence stamp, the absent scrub handler, and the reproduced trace.
verdicts.fix_note:
The claim's diagnosis is correct; the suggested 'pre-event snapshot' approach is the right shape and matches the existing pattern. Concretely, the minimal honest fix mirrors the resource/trade-good family already in undoEvent.js:

Option A (snapshot, preferred, matches existing machinery): add neighbourNetwork capture to the snapshot path. Because neighbourNetwork is a top-level settlement key (not under config/economicState), captureEventUndoSnapshot/restoreSnapshottedRecords only audit config + economicState today, so a third bucket is needed. Add e.g. SNAPSHOT_ROOT_KEYS = { BROKERED_ALLIANCE:['neighbourNetwork'], SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE:['neighbourNetwork'], OPENED_TRADE_ROUTE:['neighbourNetwork'] }, have captureEventUndoSnapshot also snapshot settlement-root keys, and have restoreSnapshottedRecords restore them. Snapshotting the WHOLE link array (not just the one link's type) is correct and avoids the in-place-overwrite irrecoverability the claim flags. Note captureEventUndoSnapshot currently early-returns null when configKeys is falsy (line 108) — that guard must be widened or it will keep returning null for these types even after you add a root-keys table.

Option B (provenance scrub) is NOT sufficient by itself: _relationshipEventId only identifies WHICH link the event touched, not the PRIOR relationshipType/displayRelationshipType, which were overwritten in place. A pure provenance scrub could at most strip the stamp and reset to a default ('neutral'), which is wrong when the prior value was e.g. 'trade_partner'. So the snapshot (Option A) is required for correctness; the orphaned stamp can either be consumed as the match key during restore or removed.

Also worth covering in the same fix: the overwrite-of-overwrite limitation already documented for conditions/_activePlague (two relationship events on the same neighbour, then undoing the second) — the snapshot approach handles it naturally since each event snapshots the full prior array. And add an undo test to tests/domain/events/relationshipEvents.test.js, which currently tests only the forward mutation.

confirmed.title: Undo of CHANGE_RULING_POWER scrubs the condition but leaves the entire government transfer in place
confirmed.evidence: changeRulingPower delegates to transferRulingPower (rulingPower.js:376+), which renames the governing faction to the new government label, rewrites desc/modifiers ('seized_power', 'ascendant'), bumps the winner's power +6, reseeds powerStructure.publicLegitimacy by cause, and re-keys factionRelationships (rulingPower.js:399-440). None of these writes carry event provenance (no causeEventId / removedByEventId), and undoEvent.js/scrubUndoneEvent has no handler for them — only the government_overthrown condition (causes[0].eventId) is dropped and systemState reverts to beforeState. After undo, the settlement still shows the post-coup government, renamed factions, and reseeded legitimacy while the timeline and the 4-dim state claim the coup never happened — a persistent contradiction between the entity layer and SystemState. Contrast with DESTROY_SETTLEMENT, which stamps destroyedByEventId precisely so undo is exact (undoEvent.js:277-286).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Snapshot powerStructure (factions + publicLegitimacy + factionRelationships) pre-event onto logEntry.undo for CHANGE_RULING_POWER (extend SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS' mechanism to a settlement-path snapshot), or document/block undo for this event type.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. I traced the full apply/undo path against the actual code.

APPLY side:
- changeRulingPower (mutate.js:1048-1068) calls transferRulingPower, then stamps ONE provenance-bearing artifact: a 'government_overthrown' activeCondition whose causes[0].eventId === event.id (lines 1062-1066).
- transferRulingPower (rulingPower.js:376-504) makes many durable entity-layer writes with NO event provenance: governing faction renamed (faction/name/desc/modifiers 'seized_power'/isGoverning/legitimacyCrisis cleared, lines 399-410); winner power+6 and 'ascendant' modifier (412-417); publicLegitimacy reseeded by cause (422-426); factionRelationships rekeyed with symbiotic/competitive edges (435-466); governingName/government/stability/recentConflict set and previousGovernments appended (468-499). None carry causeEventId/removedByEventId/addedByEventId.

UNDO side:
- captureEventUndoSnapshot (undoEvent.js:106-113) returns null for CHANGE_RULING_POWER — it is absent from SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS (53-68). So applyEvent (applyEvent.js:40,55) stamps no `undo` snapshot.
- undoLastEvent (settlementSlice.js:1500-1524): restores systemState to beforeState, strips impairments via stripImpairmentsForEvent (212-225) which only filters `impairments` by causeEventId and reverses removedByEventId — it does NOT touch the ruling-power field rewrites — then calls scrubUndoneEvent.
- scrubUndoneEvent (undoEvent.js:302-312): withoutEventConditions drops the government_overthrown condition (causes[0].eventId match), but there is NO powerStructure handler and no snapshot to restore. The other scrubs are no-ops here.

Net: after undo, systemState/beforeState and the timeline assert the coup never happened, yet powerStructure still shows the renamed government, +6 winner, seized_power/ascendant modifiers, reseeded legitimacy, rekeyed relationships, and the previousGovernments entry — a persistent entity-vs-SystemState contradiction.

Refutation attempts all failed: (a) No regeneration runs during undo — RERUN_KEYS_FOR_EVENT is used only by batch.js staging (batch.js:255), not undo, so there is no re-derivation that would reset powerStructure. (b) No config record (no rulingPowerEdits-style key) reapplies the transfer, and none reverses it either — so it's not 'intended persistence' either way; it's an orphaned, irreversible write. (c) The mutate.js doc comment (1034-1047) only discusses the invalid-faction no-op-but-deltas-still-land case, not undo of a successful transfer. (d) No test pins the undo behavior (no undo coverage in coupEvents.test.js/rulingPower.test.js), so it isn't asserted as intentional. (e) The DESTROY_SETTLEMENT contrast cited (undoEvent.js:277-286) is accurate.

Severity: medium is fair. It's an editor/DM what-if undo flow, not a billing/auth/crash-on-main-path issue, and not a determinism break in generation. But it is a real, reproducible state-integrity defect (entity layer diverges from the 4-dim SystemState and the timeline) that survives in the persisted save (undoLastEvent persists the scrubbed-but-still-transferred settlement, settlementSlice.js:1528-1538).
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound. Preferred approach: extend captureEventUndoSnapshot to snapshot the pre-event powerStructure for CHANGE_RULING_POWER (factions, publicLegitimacy, factionRelationships, governingName, government, previousGovernments, stability, recentConflict — i.e. the whole powerStructure subtree is simplest and safest since transferRulingPower rewrites many of its fields), stamp it on logEntry.undo via applyEvent, and add a restore step in scrubUndoneEvent (a settlement-path snapshot restore, since these live on settlement.powerStructure, not settlement.config — the existing SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS/SNAPSHOT_ECONOMIC_KEYS mechanism only covers config/economicState, so it needs a new top-level snapshot bucket, e.g. snapshot.settlementPaths or a dedicated powerStructure key). Snapshotting the full powerStructure object is the most robust because the transfer touches stability/recentConflict/governingName/government too, which the reviewer's partial list (factions+publicLegitimacy+factionRelationships) would leave un-reverted. If a snapshot is judged too heavy, the lower-effort alternative is to block/disable undo for CHANGE_RULING_POWER and document it, consistent with the existing 'known limitation' posture in undoEvent.js — but that degrades UX. Note also the same defect class applies to APPLY_WORLD_PULSE coup path if it routes through transferRulingPower (worldPulse/coup.js references it) — worth checking whether world-pulse coups are undoable at all before fixing only the editor path.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via end-to-end trace and a live reproduction against the real modules.

Failure path:
1. DM commits a CHANGE_RULING_POWER event. mutate.js:1048 changeRulingPower delegates to transferRulingPower (rulingPower.js:376). That function rewrites powerStructure wholesale (rulingPower.js:399-503): renames the governing faction to the new government label, sets desc/modifiers ('seized_power'), bumps the winner's power +6 and tags it 'ascendant', reseeds publicLegitimacy by cause, re-keys factionRelationships (competitive→symbiotic) and appends new relationship/grudge edges, and sets governingName/government/previousGovernments/stability/recentConflict. It then promotes a government_overthrown activeCondition with causes[0].eventId = event.id (mutate.js:1058-1067).
2. applyEvent.js:40 calls captureEventUndoSnapshot(settlement, event). CHANGE_RULING_POWER is NOT in SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS (undoEvent.js:53-68), so it returns null → logEntry has no `undo` snapshot.
3. undoLastEvent (settlementSlice.js:1500) restores systemState = popped.beforeState, strips impairments by causeEventId / status by removedByEventId, then calls scrubUndoneEvent (settlementSlice.js:1522).
4. scrubUndoneEvent (undoEvent.js:302) only: drops conditions whose causes[0].eventId matches (drops government_overthrown), strips stress entries by addedByEventId, scrubs annotations by atEventId, reverts destruction by destroyedByEventId, and restores logEntry.undo (null here → no-op). NONE of the powerStructure writes carry any of those provenance keys and there is no handler for them.

Live reproduction (node, real applyEvent + scrubUndoneEvent): after apply the council is renamed 'Corrupt City Council', legitimacy reseeds 60→36, challenger power 30→36 with 'ascendant', relationship flips competitive→symbiotic, previousGovernments gets an entry. After undo: logEntry.undo === null; the government_overthrown condition is correctly dropped and activeConditions is []; but government/governingName/legitimacy/faction names/challenger power/relationship/previousGovernments are ALL still in their post-coup state — none restored.

Consequence: a persistent contradiction. The timeline shows no coup (event popped), the 4-dim SystemState reverts to beforeState (volatility delta etc. gone), yet the entity layer (powerStructure) still shows the renamed corrupt government ruling with reseeded legitimacy, boosted/ascendant challenger, rewritten faction relations, and a stray previousGovernments record. undoLastEvent then persists this corrupted settlement back to the save (settlementSlice.js:1529-1542), so it survives reload until a full regeneration re-derives powerStructure from config. This is wrong simulation output / a broken undo seam — exactly the determinism/coherence class the project guards. Contrast with DESTROY_SETTLEMENT, which stamps destroyedByEventId for an exact revival (undoEvent.js:277-286).

Severity high (not critical): it corrupts simulation/entity state and breaks a shipped feature (undo), but it is not money/auth/data-loss/main-path-crash; it requires the user to apply then undo a ruling-power transfer. No test covers CHANGE_RULING_POWER undo round-trip (tests/joins/undoLastEvent.test.js covers PLAGUE, APPLY/RESOLVE_STRESSOR, resource/trade-good, DESTROY_SETTLEMENT, and the campaign twin, but not ruling power), so the gate did not catch it. Confidence ~0.9.
verdicts.fix_note:
The fix is correct in spirit (give the event a reversible record) but the simplest framing — "just add CHANGE_RULING_POWER to SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS" — is INSUFFICIENT as written. The current snapshot machinery (captureEventUndoSnapshot/restoreSnapshottedRecords, undoEvent.js:106-148) only knows how to snapshot/restore settlement.config (+ its _config mirror) and settlement.economicState. transferRulingPower rewrites settlement.powerStructure (factions renamed IN PLACE, governingName, government, previousGovernments, publicLegitimacy, factionRelationships, stability, recentConflict) — there is no powerStructure path in the snapshot/restore code at all.

Two viable approaches:
(A) Snapshot approach (recommended, matches the resource/trade-good precedent): extend the snapshot machinery to also capture and restore the whole powerStructure subtree (or at minimum the exact keys transferRulingPower touches), add a SNAPSHOT key entry for CHANGE_RULING_POWER, and add restore handling in restoreSnapshottedRecords. Because the faction renames are in-place mutations of an array, snapshotting the entire pre-event powerStructure object is the safe, exact way back; restoreKeys' present-vs-absent semantics already handle the previousGovernments key that didn't exist before.
(B) Provenance approach (like DESTROY_SETTLEMENT): stamp causeEventId on each powerStructure write and add a scrub handler. This is far more invasive and error-prone here because transferRulingPower derives values (legitimacy reseed pulls on oldScore, +6 power) that cannot be inverted from a single id without also storing the prior values — i.e. you end up needing a snapshot anyway. Prefer (A).

Also note the same hole applies to the world-pulse coup path (domain/worldPulse/coup.js / applyWorldPulse.js:215) which calls the same transferRulingPower — but those are not undone via undoLastEvent (campaign world-pulse has its own bridge), so scope the fix to the CHANGE_RULING_POWER canon-event undo and add a tests/joins/undoLastEvent.test.js case asserting powerStructure round-trips byte-identically (government, governingName, faction names/power/modifiers, publicLegitimacy, factionRelationships, previousGovernments).

confirmed.title: EXPOSE_CORRUPTION (NPC path) stamps synthetic causeEventIds and irreversibly swaps in a successor — undo cannot scrub it
confirmed.evidence: exposeCorruptNpc (mutate.js:801-820) routes through applyCorruptionImpairments, which stamps impairments with `causeEventId: `corruption:${e.npcId}:${e.kind}`` (corruptionImpair.js:75, 80, 91) — NOT the triggering event's id — so undoLastEvent's stripImpairmentsForEvent(eventId) (settlementSlice.js:212-225) cannot remove them. It also replaces the exposed NPC wholesale via successorNpc (mutate.js:812), with no provenance and no scrub path. Undoing the event removes only the corruption_exposed condition; the institutions stay impaired under unmatchable cause ids and the original NPC is gone forever. This breaks the documented contract in undoEvent.js (lines 16-19): 'everything mutate writes WITH provenance (condition causes, ... ) is scrubbed by that provenance'. The faction/institution branch of EXPOSE_CORRUPTION (mutate.js:762-795) uses event.id correctly — only the NPC branch diverges because it reuses the organic world-pulse exposure path.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Thread the real event.id into applyCorruptionImpairments as the causeEventId for event-driven exposures (keep the synthetic id only for organic pulse exposures), and snapshot the pre-event NPC record onto logEntry.undo so the successor swap can be reverted.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim holds under direct code reading and empirical execution.

CAUSE-ID MISMATCH (confirmed): exposeCorruptNpc (src/domain/events/mutate.js:801-820) routes through applyCorruptionImpairments (src/domain/worldPulse/corruptionImpair.js:70-98), which stamps every impairment with synthetic cause ids: `corruption:${e.npcId}:${e.kind}` (line 75), `...:home` (line 80), and a `type:'corruption'` impairment `...:ousted:institutional` (line 91). Undo's stripImpairmentsForEvent (src/store/settlementSlice.js:212-225) filters by `i.causeEventId !== eventId`, and undoLastEvent (settlementSlice.js:1500-1522) calls it with the real event id. Event ids are `ev_${Date.now()}_${rand}` (EventComposer.jsx:393), so they can never equal the synthetic `corruption:...` ids. I ran mutateSettlement with event id `ev_1718000000000_abcd`: the home institution carried impairments `corruption:npc_guard:ousted:home` and `corruption:npc_guard:ousted:institutional`; stripImpairmentsForEvent removed 0 of 2.

IRREVERSIBLE NPC SWAP (confirmed): mutate.js:811-812 replaces the exposed NPC wholesale with successorNpc (src/domain/worldPulse/successorNpc.js:24-50), which returns a brand-new object retaining only `replacedNpc: ousted.name` (a name string) — no full snapshot of the original record, no event provenance. The undo snapshot machinery (captureEventUndoSnapshot in src/domain/events/undoEvent.js:106-113, called from applyEvent.js:40) returns null for any event type not in SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS (lines 53-68), which covers only the resource/trade-good/stressor families — EXPOSE_CORRUPTION is absent. So no pre-event NPC record is preserved; the original NPC is gone permanently. Empirically, after the event Captain Vex was absent and the successor 'Norit' carried only `replacedNpc:'Captain Vex'`.

PARTIAL UNDO (confirmed): the corruption_exposed condition's onset cause is `{source:'event', eventId: event.id}` (mutate.js:818), which withoutEventConditions (undoEvent.js:194-215) matches on causes[0], so undo DOES remove the condition — but leaves the impairments and the swapped NPC. This is exactly the partial scrub the reviewer describes, and it violates the undoEvent.js contract (lines 16-19): 'everything mutate writes WITH provenance ... is scrubbed by that provenance.'

DIVERGENCE (confirmed): the faction/institution branch (mutate.js:756-795) stamps `causeEventId: event.id` — I verified a faction target produced an impairment with causeEventId equal to the event id, which undo would scrub. Only the NPC branch diverges, because it reuses the organic world-pulse exposure path (applyCorruptionImpairments) that stamps NPC-keyed synthetic ids.

The dedicated test (tests/domain/events/exposeCorruptionNpc.test.js) covers only the forward direction and confirms the impairments-applied / NPC-removed behavior, but never tests undo, so no test contradicts the claim. Reachability is real: EXPOSE_CORRUPTION is a DM event in EventComposer, exposeCorruption (mutate.js:753-754) prefers the NPC path whenever the target is a corrupt NPC, and undo is available in canon phase. No upstream guard, dead-code condition, or reform path scrubs these (advanceInstitutionReform runs on world-pulse ticks, only clears type:'corruption' impairments, and only when no corrupt insider remains — it never runs on undo and leaves the legitimacy impairments untouched).

Severity medium is accurate: not money/security/crash and not the main path, but a fully reachable data-integrity defect that leaves an incoherent settlement (permanent phantom impairments + lost NPC) after a documented undo. I could not refute it.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct and sufficient: (1) thread the real event.id into applyCorruptionImpairments as the causeEventId for event-driven exposures (keep synthetic ids only for organic pulse exposures, so stripImpairmentsForEvent can scrub them) — note this must cover all three stamped impairments including the `type:'corruption'` ...:institutional one at corruptionImpair.js:91, since stripImpairmentsForEvent does not discriminate by impairment type; and (2) capture the pre-event NPC record so the successor swap reverts. The cleanest place for (2) is to add EXPOSE_CORRUPTION to undoEvent.js's snapshot map (snapshotting settlement.npcs) and restore it in scrubUndoneEvent, mirroring the existing resource/stressor snapshot pattern, rather than the ad-hoc logEntry.undo write the reviewer suggests — that keeps it consistent with the established snapshot mechanism. One subtlety: when threading event.id, the home institution gets two impairments that would then share causeEventId=event.id with different descriptions; that is fine for the strip (both removed), but verify no consumer relies on the distinct synthetic home/criminal cause ids elsewhere.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via end-to-end reproduction against the real modules (mutate.js, undoEvent.js, plus a faithful copy of settlementSlice.stripImpairmentsForEvent).

Concrete failure path: In canon mode a DM applies EXPOSE_CORRUPTION targeting a corrupt NPC (event.targetId resolves to an NPC with corrupt===true). exposeCorruption (mutate.js:753-754) routes to exposeCorruptNpc (mutate.js:801-820). That function:
  (a) calls applyCorruptionImpairments, which stamps impairments with causeEventId `corruption:${npcId}:${kind}` and `...:home` / `...:ousted:institutional` (corruptionImpair.js:75,80,91) — NEVER event.id; and
  (b) replaces the NPC wholesale via successorNpc (mutate.js:812), which mints a brand-new NPC object {id:`npc.successor_<n>`, clean identity, no causeEventId/originEventId, only `replacedNpc: <oldName>` for narrative} (successorNpc.js:24-50).

On undoLastEvent (settlementSlice.js:1500-1524): systemState is restored from popped.beforeState (which is SystemState only — applyEvent.js:30,45 — not a full settlement/NPC snapshot); stripImpairmentsForEvent(eventId) (settlementSlice.js:212-225) filters impairments by `causeEventId !== eventId`; and scrubUndoneEvent runs the conditions/stress/annotation/snapshot scrubs (undoEvent.js:302-312). captureEventUndoSnapshot returns null for EXPOSE_CORRUPTION (only resource/trade-good/stressor types are snapshotted — undoEvent.js:53-68, SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS), so there is no full-state fallback.

Reproduction output (standalone node against the actual src modules):
  - AFTER MUTATE: original NPC ('Captain Vex') gone; successor id npc.successor_903316, replacedNpc='Captain Vex'.
  - Institution impairment causeEventIds: 'corruption:npc.captain_1:ousted:home', 'corruption:npc.captain_1:ousted:institutional' (home/watch) and 'corruption:npc.captain_1:ousted' (criminal). None equal event.id ('evt-expose-123').
  - captureEventUndoSnapshot('EXPOSE_CORRUPTION') === null.
  - AFTER UNDO: Captain Vex restored? false. Successor still present? true. WATCH keeps legitimacy + corruption impairments and status='impaired'. SYNDICATE keeps its legitimacy impairment, status='impaired'. The corruption_exposed condition IS correctly removed (its causes[0].eventId === event.id), so undo scrubs ONLY the cosmetic condition while the load-bearing damage (impaired institutions, swapped NPC) persists.

Downstream consequence: the lingering impairments and status='impaired' are real engine inputs — read by corruption climate (corruption.js), institution reform/lifecycle (corruptionImpair.js advanceInstitutionReform keys off type:'corruption'), blockade/transport, and surfaced in the dossier (explanation.js + the 'impaired' badge in OutputContainer). The replaced NPC is also gone from the dossier and PDF view models. So an undone EXPOSE_CORRUPTION leaves a permanently-corrupted institution pair and a vanished pillar NPC that no further undo or regeneration can reverse (the records have no event-id provenance and no pre-event snapshot). This directly violates the undoEvent.js contract (lines 16-19): 'everything mutate writes WITH provenance ... is scrubbed by that provenance.'

The non-NPC branch (mutate.js:762-795) correctly stamps causeEventId: event.id and causes:[{eventId: event.id}], confirming only the NPC branch diverges because it reuses the organic world-pulse exposure path (applyCorruptionImpairments + successorNpc) which was designed for tick-driven pulses, not reversible DM events.

Severity medium (not higher): it is gated behind a specific path (canon mode + EXPOSE_CORRUPTION whose target resolves to a corrupt NPC + the DM hitting undo), so it is not a main-path crash, money, or security issue; but it produces wrong, irreversible simulation state and breaks the documented undo invariant, which is a real reversibility/coherence defect, not an edge cosmetic glitch.
verdicts.fix_note:
The reporter's diagnosis is correct, but the suggested 'use event.id' framing is incomplete — there are TWO distinct defects and the fix must address both:

1) Impairment provenance (recoverable via provenance scrub): make the DM path stamp impairments with the triggering event's id so stripImpairmentsForEvent can remove them. Cleanest option: have exposeCorruptNpc pass the event id down, e.g. add an optional `causeEventId` to applyCorruptionImpairments and use it instead of (or in addition to) the synthetic `corruption:${npcId}:${kind}` key when the call comes from EXPOSE_CORRUPTION. Note applyCorruptionImpairments stamps THREE distinct causeEventIds per ousting (…:home, …, …:institutional including the type:'corruption' impairment at line 91) — ALL three must be retagged to event.id, or stripImpairmentsForEvent (which matches by exact equality) will leave the others behind. Do NOT simply hardcode event.id inside corruptionImpair.js, since the organic/world-pulse caller (the tick path) has no event and DOES want the synthetic keys; thread it as a parameter so only the DM-event call switches behavior.

2) NPC replacement (NOT recoverable from provenance OR current snapshot): even after fixing #1, the wholesale successorNpc swap at mutate.js:812 has no undo path — beforeState is SystemState only and captureEventUndoSnapshot returns null for EXPOSE_CORRUPTION. Options: (a) extend captureEventUndoSnapshot/SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS to snapshot settlement.npcs (or just the targeted NPC) for EXPOSE_CORRUPTION and restore it in restoreSnapshottedRecords/scrubUndoneEvent; or (b) stamp the successor with originEventId: event.id and the ousted NPC's full prior record so undo can swap it back (a new scrub in undoEvent.js). Option (a) (npc snapshot) is more robust and mirrors the existing snapshot machinery. Whichever is chosen, add a regression test asserting that undo of an NPC-target EXPOSE_CORRUPTION restores the original NPC, removes the successor, and returns both institutions to status:'active' with zero impairments — the current test suite (tests/domain/corruptionImpair.test.js) does not cover the undo round-trip for this path.

confirmed.title: Creation/flag mutations (ADD_NPC, ADD_INSTITUTION/ADD_FACTION re-add, IMPOSE_CORRUPTION, PROMOTE/DEMOTE swap) persist after undo
confirmed.evidence: scrubUndoneEvent (undoEvent.js:302-312) covers conditions, stress entries, annotation ledgers, destruction stamps, and snapshotted resource/trade/stressor records — but several mutate.js writes carry no provenance and are never reverted: (1) ADD_NPC appends an npc created by createNpc with no creation stamp (mutate.js:634-645; npcs.js:86-103), so the NPC survives undo; (2) ADD_INSTITUTION / ADD_FACTION on an existing entity wipe impairments wholesale (`impairments: []`, mutate.js:283-285, 377) — undo cannot restore the destroyed impairment list, and a newly created institution/faction record persists; (3) IMPOSE_CORRUPTION sets npc.corrupt/corruptionVector/corruptTies with no eventId (mutate.js:845-866); (4) PROMOTE_NPC/DEMOTE_NPC swap standing fields and stamp factionId with no provenance (mutate.js:1325-1364); (5) severCorruptionTiesTo flips corrupt→false/ousted→true on REMOVE_INSTITUTION (mutate.js:825-837) and is not restored when the removal is undone. The store doc claims undo 'scrubs every settlement artifact the event left behind' (settlementSlice.js:1484-1498), which these contradict. The undoEvent.js header documents some overwrite-class limitations explicitly, but none of these are listed — they are gaps, not accepted trade-offs.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Stamp creation provenance (createdByEventId) on event-created npcs/institutions/factions and corruption flags so scrubUndoneEvent can drop/restore them, and snapshot pre-event entity records (the existing logEntry.undo mechanism) for the wipe/swap cases.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. undoLastEvent (settlementSlice.js:1500-1524) is purely incremental: it restores systemState from popped.beforeState, strips impairments by causeEventId (stripImpairmentsForEvent, settlementSlice.js:212-225), then calls scrubUndoneEvent.
