There is NO persisted full pre-event settlement snapshot — applyEvent.js:42-56 attaches only beforeState, deltas, and a conditional `undo` block from captureEventUndoSnapshot, which (undoEvent.js:106-113) returns null for every event type outside SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS (resource/trade-good/stressor only). scrubUndoneEvent (undoEvent.js:302-312) reverts only: promoted/eased conditions by causes[0].eventId, stress entries by addedByEventId, config annotation ledgers by atEventId, destruction stamps by destroyedByEventId, and the snapshotted records. None of these match the five flagged mutations.

All five leaks confirmed: (1) addNpc (mutate.js:634-645) appends createNpc (npcs.js:86-103) with no event-id stamp -> NPC survives undo. (2) addInstitution re-add (mutate.js:283-285) and addFaction re-add (mutate.js:376-378) set impairments:[] on an existing entity, destroying the prior list with no snapshot to restore it; a newly created institution/faction (mutate.js:287-295, 379-396) carries no removedByEventId, so it persists. (3) imposeCorruption (mutate.js:845-866) sets corrupt/corruptionVector/corruptTies via replaceNpc with no eventId. (4) swapNpcStanding (mutate.js:1325-1364) swaps importance/influence/structuralRank and stamps factionId with no provenance. (5) severCorruptionTiesTo on REMOVE_INSTITUTION (mutate.js:825-837) flips corrupt->false/ousted->true with no eventId and is never restored on undo of the removal.

Could not refute: the event types are all registered (registry.js:92,103,263,431) and offered by the DM-facing EventComposer.jsx, so reachable on the main path — not dead code. The undo test suite (tests/joins/undoLastEvent.test.js) covers conditions, causeEventId impairments, and the stressor snapshot, but none of these five cases — so they are not deliberately-accepted-and-tested behaviors. The store doc (settlementSlice.js:1484-1498) explicitly claims undo scrubs every settlement artifact, and undoEvent.js documents the condition/stressor/_activePlague overwrite limitations but omits these five — confirming they are unacknowledged gaps. The evidence holds exactly as the reviewer stated.

Severity medium is honest: this produces wrong post-undo simulation state (leaked NPCs, corruption flags, swapped standing, irreversibly wiped impairment lists) — a data-integrity defect on a secondary editor flow (undo), not money/security/crash and not a primary-path determinism break.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound and matches the codebase's two existing reversal idioms. Concretely: (a) For created entities (ADD_NPC, and the new-record branches of ADD_INSTITUTION/ADD_FACTION), stamp a createdByEventId on the record at creation and have scrubUndoneEvent drop any entity whose createdByEventId === eventId (mirrors the destroyedByEventId/removedByEventId pattern). (b) For the overwrite/wipe and flag-flip cases (ADD_INSTITUTION/ADD_FACTION re-add wiping impairments, IMPOSE_CORRUPTION, PROMOTE/DEMOTE swap, and severCorruptionTiesTo on REMOVE_INSTITUTION), extend SNAPSHOT_CONFIG_KEYS/captureEventUndoSnapshot to snapshot the affected pre-event entity records (the existing logEntry.undo mechanism) and restore them in restoreSnapshottedRecords — the IMPOSE_CORRUPTION / swap / corruption-sever cases are exactly the same overwrite class the header already snapshots stressorEdits for. Note that the new-NPC drop and the entity-record snapshot together also cover the re-add wipe (snapshot restores the impairment list) without needing a separate per-field provenance scheme.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL via end-to-end trace against the real modules. The undo architecture is provenance-scrubbing + a narrow snapshot, NOT a full-settlement snapshot restore. Store path (settlementSlice.js:1500-1524): undoLastEvent restores systemState from popped.beforeState, strips impairments by causeEventId (stripImpairmentsForEvent, also reversing removedByEventId removals), then runs scrubUndoneEvent. The only snapshot fallback is logEntry.undo, and applyEvent.js:40 only stamps it from captureEventUndoSnapshot, which returns non-null ONLY for the resource/trade-good/stressor families (undoEvent.js:53-68). For ADD_NPC / ADD_INSTITUTION / ADD_FACTION / IMPOSE_CORRUPTION / PROMOTE_NPC / DEMOTE_NPC / severCorruptionTiesTo, captureEventUndoSnapshot returns null (verified by direct call), so undo relies entirely on scrubUndoneEvent provenance. scrubUndoneEvent (undoEvent.js:302-312) only handles: conditions (causes[0].eventId), stress entries (addedByEventId), config annotations (atEventId), destruction stamps (destroyedByEventId), and snapshotted records. None of these mutations carry that provenance.

I reproduced all five sub-claims with node one-liners against mutate.js + undoEvent.js (simulating the store's apply→scrub path):

(1) ADD_NPC: createNpc appends an NPC with no eventId stamp (mutate.js:634-645; npcs.js:86-103). Trace: base 1 NPC → apply 2 → after undo STILL 2 (the added 'dora' survives). captureEventUndoSnapshot=null. CONFIRMED.

(2a) ADD_INSTITUTION re-add of an existing entity sets impairments:[] (mutate.js:284). Trace: institution had 1 pre-existing impairment (causeEventId 'old-evt', unrelated to this event) → apply wipes to 0 → undo leaves 0. The pre-existing impairment is destroyed and unrecoverable. CONFIRMED.
(2b) ADD_INSTITUTION new entity (mutate.js:287-295): appends institution with no eventId. Trace: 1→2 institutions, 'shrine' still present after undo. CONFIRMED.
(2c) ADD_FACTION re-add sets impairments:[] (mutate.js:377). Trace: faction's 1 impairment wiped, not restored after undo. CONFIRMED.

(3) IMPOSE_CORRUPTION (mutate.js:845-866) sets corrupt/corruptionVector/corruptTies with no eventId. Trace: cleo.corrupt true after apply AND after undo; corruptionVector 'greed' persists. CONFIRMED.

(4) PROMOTE_NPC/DEMOTE_NPC (swapNpcStanding, mutate.js:1325-1364) swaps importance/influence/structuralRank and stamps factionId, no provenance. Trace: bob.importance notable→key by apply, STILL key after undo; factionId stamp persists. CONFIRMED.

(5) severCorruptionTiesTo on REMOVE_INSTITUTION (mutate.js:825-837) flips corrupt→false / corruptionVector→null / ousted→true. Trace: after undoing the removal, vex stays corrupt:false, ousted:true, corruptionVector:null. (The institution's own status='removed' IS reverted, via stripImpairmentsForEvent's removedByEventId path — settlementSlice.js:218 — but the NPC corruption-field changes are not, since NPCs are only impairment-stripped.) CONFIRMED.

The store doc (settlementSlice.js:1484-1498) claims undo 'scrubs every settlement artifact the event left behind,' which these contradict. The undoEvent.js header (lines 1-29) and inline notes (182-193, 217-226, 238-246) document overwrite-class limitations (condition replace-by-id, stressor re-author, _activePlague last-writer) but list NONE of these — they are unacknowledged gaps. The undo test suite (tests/joins/undoLastEvent.test.js, 14 tests) covers conditions/impairments/plague/crisis but has zero coverage for any of these six handlers, consistent with them being overlooked, not accepted.

User-visible consequence: A DM applies one of these editor events as a canon timeline action, then clicks Undo. The timeline log entry is popped and systemState reverts, but the settlement entity state stays mutated — a ghost NPC remains in the roster, a re-added institution/faction's prior impairment history is permanently erased, an NPC stays corrupt/promoted/ousted. The undone change is then persisted to the save (settlementSlice.js:1529-1542), so the inconsistency is durable across reload. This is a real data-integrity/feature-correctness defect on the editor undo path, not a determinism or money/security issue.

Severity: medium. It's a real correctness bug on a real user path (canon editor undo), affects simulation/data integrity, and the destroyed-impairment case (2a/2c) is genuine data loss of prior history — but it's confined to a specific editor action sequence (apply-then-undo of these specific event types), not a main-path crash or billing/auth issue, and the systemState delta (the primary 4-dim surface) does revert correctly.
verdicts.fix_note: The cleanest fix reuses the existing snapshot mechanism rather than retrofitting provenance onto six handlers. Extend captureEventUndoSnapshot (undoEvent.js) to capture the affected entity arrays for these event types, and extend restoreSnapshottedRecords to restore them: ADD_NPC/IMPOSE_CORRUPTION/PROMOTE_NPC/DEMOTE_NPC snapshot settlement.npcs; ADD_INSTITUTION snapshots settlement.institutions; ADD_FACTION snapshots both settlement.factions and settlement.powerStructure.factions; REMOVE_INSTITUTION already partially reverts via removedByEventId but should also snapshot npcs to undo severCorruptionTiesTo's corrupt/corruptionVector/ousted flips. A full pre-event entity-array snapshot is exact (it restores the wiped impairment lists in 2a/2c, which provenance stamps alone cannot recover, since the old impairments belonged to OTHER events). Order note: in the store, scrubUndoneEvent runs AFTER stripImpairmentsForEvent; restoreSnapshottedRecords runs last inside scrubUndoneEvent, so a snapshot restore of npcs/institutions/factions will correctly overwrite the impairment-strip output for these events — verify that's intended (it is, for creation/flag events whose entire effect is being reverted). Caveat: snapshotting full entity arrays is heavier than the current config-key snapshots and these arrays can be large; if save-size is a concern, scope it to the touched entity only (e.g. snapshot just the target NPC/institution by id and merge on restore), but that reintroduces the create-vs-modify branching the array snapshot avoids. Either way, also (a) correct the store doc at settlementSlice.js:1484-1498 which currently overclaims, and (b) add undo-cleanliness tests for all six handlers to tests/joins/undoLastEvent.test.js. If a full fix is deferred, at minimum document these in the undoEvent.js header's limitation list so they're acknowledged trade-offs rather than silent gaps.

confirmed.title: createNpc uses Math.random() for NPC ids inside the pure event pipeline
confirmed.evidence: createNpc builds ids as `npc.${slug}_${shortRand()}` where shortRand() = `Math.random().toString(36).slice(2,7)` (npcs.js:87, 249-251). It runs inside mutateSettlement (ADD_NPC at mutate.js:634, ASSIGN_NPC_TO_ROLE at mutate.js:684 — assignNpcToRole calls createNpc again at npcs.js:185), which executes in runEventPipeline — documented 'Pure function. Pure; idempotent' (eventPipeline.js:34, 123) and run once for preview and once for apply. So the previewed nextSettlement and the applied one carry different NPC ids, two replays of the same event log produce different settlements (violating the project rule that unseeded global RNG in engine code is a bug), and batch.js explicitly works around it ('NPC ids carry a random suffix at apply time, so only the name is registered', batch.js:52-54). The codebase already has the correct pattern in this same file's neighborhood: exposeCorruptNpc seeds a PRNG from the event id (mutate.js:811 `createPRNG(`successor:${event.id}:...`)`).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Derive the suffix deterministically from available identity — e.g. shortHash(`${event.id}:${name}`) or a createPRNG(`npc:${event.id}`) draw threaded from the mutation handlers — and drop shortRand().

verdicts.reasoning:
The core fact is confirmed by direct reading: createNpc builds ids via shortRand() = Math.random().toString(36).slice(2,7) (src/domain/entities/npcs.js:87, 249-251), and it is reached inside mutateSettlement for ADD_NPC (mutate.js:634-644) and ASSIGN_NPC_TO_ROLE (mutate.js:683-702 -> assignNpcToRole -> createNpc at npcs.js:185-197). runEventPipeline is documented "Pure function" / "Pure; idempotent" (eventPipeline.js:34, 123), and batch.js:52-54 explicitly works around the random suffix. The correct seeded pattern (createPRNG(`successor:${event.id}:...`)) does exist a few lines away (mutate.js:811). These events are live (registered in registry.js:263, 302), not dead code. So an unseeded global RNG genuinely runs inside engine code that claims purity/idempotency — a real violation of the project's "unseeded RNG in engine code is a bug" rule, and two separate evaluations of the same ADD_NPC event produce different ids.

However, the reviewer's two HEADLINE impacts are overstated and do not actually manifest: (1) "the previewed nextSettlement and the applied one carry different NPC ids" — previewEvent.js:35-39 EXPLICITLY discards result.nextSettlement; the preview never exposes, compares, or persists the NPC id, and the deltas the user sees (afterSystemState, afterCausalState, faction responses, narrative) do not depend on the random suffix. The pinned preview==apply contract is about afterSystemState/afterCausalState/delta lists, none of which carry the id, so there is no observable parity break. The store's applyEvent (settlementSlice.js:1217-1311) runs domainApplyEvent fresh and persists that result, never reusing the preview's discarded settlement. (2) "two replays of the same event log produce different settlements" — there is NO event-log-replay reconstruction path in the codebase (no reduce/forEach over eventLog re-applying events to rebuild a settlement; loads restore the persisted settlement object directly, settlementSlice.js:1602). The settlement (mutated nextSettlement) is the persisted source of truth; the eventLog is a historical/undo/AI-grounding record, never replayed to regenerate state. So the determinism-on-replay scenario the reviewer describes does not occur.

Net: a real but narrow latent defect — engine code violates the stated no-unseeded-RNG / purity-idempotency contract and forced a downstream workaround in batch.js — but with no current user-facing wrong output, no actual preview/apply parity drift, and no actual replay divergence. That makes it low, not medium.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound and matches the existing in-file seeded pattern (createPRNG(`successor:${event.id}:...`) at mutate.js:811). Thread a deterministic suffix: derive it from event identity at the mutation handlers — e.g. addNpc/assignNpcMutation pass an explicit id like `npc.${slug}_${shortHash(`${event.id}:${name}`)}` or seed createPRNG(`npc:${event.id}:${name}`) — and drop shortRand(). Caveat: if a single event can create multiple NPCs in one call, the seed must include a per-NPC discriminator (index or name) to avoid id collisions. Also revisit the batch.js:52-54 workaround afterward: once NPC ids are deterministic, eventProduces (batch.js:75) could register the id instead of only the name, but that is a separate optional improvement, not required for correctness. Severity should be low rather than medium since neither the preview/apply parity break nor the replay divergence actually occurs in the current code paths.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED real. createNpc (src/domain/entities/npcs.js:86-87) builds ids as `npc.${slug}_${shortRand()}`, and shortRand() = `Math.random().toString(36).slice(2,7)` (npcs.js:249-251). grep confirms this is the only Math.random in the entity layer that runs inside the pure event pipeline.

End-to-end trace (preview→apply divergence):
1. ADD_NPC handler addNpc (mutate.js:634-644) calls createNpc with NO id → a random suffix is minted. ASSIGN_NPC_TO_ROLE for a not-yet-existing NPC also mints one: assignNpcMutation (mutate.js:684) does `findNpc(...) || createNpc({name})` then assignNpcToRole (npcs.js:185) spreads `{...npc}` through createNpc — id only preserved if it already existed; a fresh fallback NPC gets a random id.
2. Store preview: previewEvent (settlementSlice.js:1193-1203) → domainPreviewEvent → runEventPipeline → mutateSettlement → createNpc → id A, stored in pendingPreview.
3. Store commit: applyPendingPreview (settlementSlice.js:1419-1422) does NOT reuse the previewed nextSettlement — it calls state.applyEvent(pendingPreview.event), which re-runs the ENTIRE pipeline (applyEvent.js:33 → runEventPipeline → mutateSettlement → createNpc) → id B ≠ A. The persisted settlement.npcs[].id differs from what was previewed.

Demonstrated with a node one-liner against the real module: two identical createNpc({name:'Maro the Smith'}) calls returned npc.maro_the_smith_ceq4e vs npc.maro_the_smith_g8xme; identical assignNpcToRole calls on an id-less NPC returned npc.vela_09yt0 vs npc.vela_6wtic.

Why it matters: (a) DETERMINISM — the project's load-bearing rule "no Math.random in engine code so replays stay deterministic" (enforced across worldPulse/region/corruption with explicit comments) is directly violated; Math.random cannot be seeded, so same-log/same-seed replays are not byte-identical on npcs[].id. (b) PARITY — runEventPipeline documents the CONTRACT that preview and apply produce the same nextSettlement (eventPipeline.js:29-32; applyEvent.js:4-8), violated for ADD_NPC's id field. (c) The eventPipeline preview/apply invariant tests (tests/domain/eventPipeline.test.js:152-193) only deep-compare afterState/deltas/causalStateDeltas — never nextSettlement.npcs — so the bug ships green. batch.js:52-54 explicitly documents the workaround ("NPC ids carry a random suffix at apply time, so only the name is registered"), and the engine already threads options.now for deterministic timestamps (eventPipeline.js:129) — proving the determinism intent that this RNG site contradicts.

Why not higher than medium: findNpc (mutate.js:1394-1402) matches by id OR name, and batch refs register NPCs by name, so subsequent events still resolve the NPC despite a divergent id — no crash, no cross-reference corruption, no data loss on the main path, and no current test fails. It is a genuine determinism/parity defect, but its blast radius is contained by the name-fallback. So: real bug, engine-determinism class, medium.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix (seed a PRNG from the event id, mirroring exposeCorruptNpc at mutate.js:811 `createPRNG(`successor:${event.id}:...`)`) is the right pattern and createPRNG is already imported in mutate.js (line 25). Caveats: (1) Do NOT just reseed shortRand() inside npcs.js — createNpc has no access to event.id and is also called for manual user input (npcs.js:81). Thread a deterministic seed/id IN from the call sites: in addNpc (mutate.js:635) and the fallback in assignNpcMutation (mutate.js:684), pass an explicit `id` computed from `createPRNG(`addnpc:${event.id}:${slug}`)` (or pass the rng), so createNpc's `input.id || ...` short-circuit takes the deterministic value. (2) If any path ever mints more than one NPC per event, the seed must include an index (e.g. `${event.id}:${i}`) to keep ids unique within the event — event.id alone is not enough for multi-NPC events. (3) Manual-user-input callers (no event id) can keep a non-deterministic suffix safely since they are outside the replayable pipeline, but cleaner is to require callers to supply an id. (4) Note that even with a deterministic id, assignNpcToRole still re-runs createNpc on an already-id'd NPC and preserves it — fine — but verify no existing persisted save relies on the current random-id format before changing the scheme (ids are referenced by name elsewhere, so a format change is low-risk).

confirmed.title: Wall-clock fallbacks in domain event code embed nondeterministic timestamps into settlement state
confirmed.evidence: withImpairment defaults `appliedAt: impairment.appliedAt || new Date().toISOString()` (status.js:120); every impairment minted by mutate.js handlers (damageInstitution, impairFaction, plague, etc.) omits appliedAt, so each preview/apply/replay stamps a different wall-clock time into the settlement. mutate.js:49 and applyEvent.js:32 also fall back to `new Date().toISOString()` when an event carries no timestamp and the caller threads no `now`. propagate.js:127-130 documents that the world-pulse 'bans new Date()' and inherits the origin's appliedAt precisely to stay deterministic — but the origin impairments created in mutate.js have none to inherit. Consequences: preview-time and apply-time settlements differ byte-wise, and undoEvent.js's stated goal that 'an undone settlement stays byte-identical to one that never saw the event' (undoEvent.js:84-85) cannot hold across replays. Mitigated in the store path (applyEvent threads appliedAt as `now`), but the impairment-level default is never threaded.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Thread the event timestamp from mutateSettlement into every withImpairment call (the `timestamp` is already computed at mutate.js:49), and make withImpairment leave appliedAt undefined rather than defaulting to wall-clock.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified by reading the cited files and the full caller/callee chain. status.js:120 `withImpairment` defaults `appliedAt: impairment.appliedAt || new Date().toISOString()`. Every impairment minted directly by mutate.js handlers omits appliedAt: damageInstitution (235-240), impairInstitution (301-306), impairFaction (337-342), plague (953-958), killNpcMutation (672-677), raidOrMonsterAttack (1003-1008), exposeCorruption (762-767), assignNpcMutation restorations — none set appliedAt. The handlers DO receive `stampedEvent.timestamp` (computed at mutate.js:49-50) but only use it for config annotations (destroyedAt at 187, atTimestamp at 609/906), never for impairments. So withImpairment hits its wall-clock fallback on every direct impairment. Propagated impairments inherit `origin.impairment.appliedAt` (propagate.js:130), which is undefined for handler-minted origins, so applyImpairmentToEntity -> withImpairment also hits the wall clock there. Preview (runEventPipeline with no `now`, previewEvent.js:34) and apply (now=appliedAt, applyEvent.js:32-33) both fail to thread the timestamp into impairments, so impaired entities receive different appliedAt values between preview, apply, and each replay. This is a recognized determinism discipline elsewhere: corruptionImpair.js:75,92 deliberately stamps `appliedAt: now` ('the world-pulse bans new Date()'), and regionalNowThreading.test.js enforces 'stamps the threaded now, never the wall clock' with byte-identical replays. The mutate.js event-impairment path is the un-threaded gap. No property/snapshot test pins or excuses the wall-clock default (status.property.test.js and mutate.property.test.js never reference appliedAt). The 'mitigated in the store path' qualifier is actually generous to the code — applyEvent threads `now` into mutateSettlement but it still never reaches withImpairment. Severity is correctly low: appliedAt is pure metadata (effectiveStatus/severityFor ignore it; no simulation math depends on it), persist.partialize never persists generated settlements (only config+toggles), and the preview's user-visible surface omits nextSettlement (previewEvent.js:35), so the visible deltas are unaffected. The damage is confined to byte-level nondeterminism in in-memory/saved settlement state, replay/snapshot comparisons, and undo byte-identity (undoEvent.js:84-85 goal). Could not refute on any cited point.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is accurate and consistent with existing patterns. Concretely: thread the already-computed `timestamp` (mutate.js:49) into every handler-built impairment as `appliedAt: timestamp`, OR (cleaner) make withImpairment leave appliedAt undefined and stamp it once at the mutate.js/applyEvent layer — mirroring corruptionImpair.js's `appliedAt: now` and propagate.js's inherit-the-origin's-appliedAt pattern. Note the fix must also cover the indirect minters that don't construct the impairment in mutate.js itself: killNpc/assignNpcToRole in entities/npcs.js return impairment objects, and these likewise need the event timestamp threaded (verify npcs.js too) so the propagation origins carry appliedAt for inheritance at propagate.js:130. A regression test in the regionalNowThreading.test.js style (apply same event twice with a moving wall clock, assert impaired entities are JSON-identical) would pin it.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED at the mechanism level, with two corrections to the claim's framing and a downgrade on consequence.

Mechanism (real, reproduced): status.js:120 `withImpairment` stamps `appliedAt: impairment.appliedAt || new Date().toISOString()`. Every impairment minted in mutate.js handlers (damageInstitution L235-240, impairInstitution L301-306, impairFaction L337-342, plague L953-958, killNpcMutation L672-678, exposeCorruption L762-767, raidOrMonsterAttack L1003-1008) is built from event payload/description only and carries no appliedAt. propagate.js:130 then inherits `origin.impairment.appliedAt` (undefined), so propagated impairments also hit withImpairment's wall-clock fallback. I ran the real mutate.js twice on an identical settlement+event with an identical threaded `now`: impairment.appliedAt differed (2026-...:51.424Z vs :51.451Z), `JSON.stringify(a) !== JSON.stringify(b)`, and appliedAt != the threaded now. This is wall-clock time inside deterministic engine code — exactly the bug class the project's rules forbit, and it diverges from corruptionImpair.js (L75/L92) which correctly threads `appliedAt: now`.

Correction 1 — the claim says this is "mitigated in the store path (applyEvent threads appliedAt as now)." That is WRONG. applyEvent.js:33 threads `now` into runEventPipeline, and mutateSettlement uses it for the event timestamp + the annotation atTimestamp fields (via eventTime, mutate.js:1371), but NOTHING copies that timestamp into the impairment object, so withImpairment's wall-clock default fires on every apply regardless of `now`. My repro confirms appliedAt != threaded now even when now is supplied. So the bug is LESS mitigated than the report claims.

Correction 2 — the claim's undo consequence does NOT hold. It asserts undoEvent.js:84-85's byte-identity goal "cannot hold" because of this appliedAt. But on undo the cause-tagged impairments are removed wholesale (status.js withoutEventImpairments L135-143, and slice clearEntityImpairments L214), so the wall-clock appliedAt is deleted, not left behind. I reproduced apply→strip(ev1)→compare: the timestamped impairment is gone after undo. Whatever residual byte-difference exists after undo (empty-array vs absent key) is a separate pre-existing seam, not appliedAt. So appliedAt is not what breaks undo byte-identity.

Severity downgrade to LOW: the nondeterministic appliedAt IS persisted into saves (settlementSlice.applyEvent persists cloneJson(afterState.settlement)), but it is effectively write-only. Grepping every appliedAt read in src: the only consumers (causalViews.js:88, chronicleFeed.js:38, both Timeline views, canonStatus.js:46) read it off EVENT-LOG ENTRIES (the deterministic threaded value from applyEvent.js:44) — never off impairment objects. deriveSystemState / causal substrate / dossier / PDF / AI prompts do not consume impairment-level appliedAt (severityFor and effectiveStatus use only .severity / array length). preview discards nextSettlement entirely (previewEvent.js:35) and returns only numeric state deltas, so there's no preview-vs-apply user-visible divergence either. The undo invariant tests (tests/joins/undoLastEvent.test.js) deliberately assert per-field equality and impairment-absence rather than full settlement byte-equality, and generation.test.js snapshots never route through mutateSettlement — so the gate doesn't catch this and nothing downstream is affected. Net: a genuine latent determinism violation / unfinished seam in engine code, with no observed wrong simulation output, parity drift, data loss, money, security, or crash impact today. Real defect, low severity.
verdicts.fix_note: The report's suggested framing (fix the withImpairment default) is insufficient and partly misdiagnosed. The correct fix is to thread the already-available deterministic event timestamp into the impairment at mint time in mutate.js, mirroring corruptionImpair.js. Concretely: stampedEvent.timestamp is computed at mutate.js:49-50; add `appliedAt: stampedEvent.timestamp` to each impairment literal the handlers build (damageInstitution, impairInstitution, impairFaction, plague, killNpcMutation/killNpc's emitted impairments, exposeCorruption, raidOrMonsterAttack, assignNpcMutation restorations), OR pass the timestamp through withImpairment/propagateImpairment. Because propagate.js:130 inherits origin.impairment.appliedAt, fixing the origin impairments automatically fixes the propagated ones. Do NOT simply delete withImpairment's `new Date()` fallback — that would leave appliedAt undefined rather than deterministic; the fallback is fine as a last resort once handlers supply the value. Two corrections to the report itself: (1) it is NOT mitigated in the store/apply path — threading `now` never reaches the impairment, so the wall-clock default fires on every apply; (2) the undo byte-identity violation it cites is not caused by appliedAt — undo removes the cause-tagged impairment wholesale, deleting the timestamp. Given no downstream consumer reads impairment-level appliedAt, this is a correctness-of-principle / determinism-hygiene fix, not an urgent one.

confirmed.title: SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE missing from RERUN_KEYS_FOR_EVENT
confirmed.evidence: RERUN_KEYS_FOR_EVENT (registry.js:86-125) enumerates every event type in EVENT_REGISTRY except SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE (registry.js:222) — its §9 siblings BROKERED_ALLIANCE (line 109) and OPENED_TRADE_ROUTE (line 111) are present. batch.js:255 unions these keys into the batch preview's 'affected subsystems' list, so a staged settlement dispute reports no affected subsystems while a brokered alliance reports powerStructure. The registry's own header (lines 4-7) requires '(2) a rerun-affected map entry' for every event. Descriptive metadata only (the partial-rerun engine was retired), so impact is limited to a dishonest batch preview.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE: ['powerStructure', 'narrative']` (matching BROKERED_ALLIANCE) to RERUN_KEYS_FOR_EVENT.

verdicts.reasoning:
The factual core of the claim is verified: SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE (registry.js:222) is genuinely missing from RERUN_KEYS_FOR_EVENT (registry.js:86-125), while its §9 siblings BROKERED_ALLIANCE (line 109) and OPENED_TRADE_ROUTE (line 111) are present. batch.js:255 does union RERUN_KEYS_FOR_EVENT[event.type] || [] into the returned rerunKeys set, so a SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE-only batch yields empty rerunKeys. This also violates the registry's own documented contract (lines 4-7: every event needs "(2) a rerun-affected map entry"). So a real, if minor, internal-consistency defect exists.

HOWEVER, the reviewer's stated user-visible IMPACT — "a dishonest batch preview" where a staged dispute "reports no affected subsystems while a brokered alliance reports powerStructure" — is refuted. I traced rerunKeys end to end: it is produced by batch.js, stashed into pendingBatchPreview at settlementSlice.js:1449, and then NEVER read by any presentation code. A full-repo grep (src/, pdf/, supabase/, tests/) shows rerunKeys has zero consumers outside batch.js, the store stash, and one test (eventBatch.test.js:263, which actually asserts on a KILL_NPC+ADD_FACTION batch, not SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE). The only batch-preview UI, BatchCart (EventComposer.jsx:1083-1132), renders pendingBatchPreview?.systemStateDeltas (lines 1115-1117) exclusively — there is no "affected subsystems" list rendered anywhere. The registry's own comment (lines 78-84) confirms this is "Descriptive metadata only — there is no step-level partial-rerun engine (it was retired)."

Net: the omission is a genuine low-severity metadata/contract defect, so isReal=true. But the claimed observable symptom (a dishonest preview shown to the DM) does not occur because the rerunKeys field is dead/unrendered in the presentation layer. The defect's true nature is a latent, unsurfaced metadata gap, not a visibly wrong batch preview.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct and harmless: add SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE: ['powerStructure', 'narrative'] to RERUN_KEYS_FOR_EVENT for internal consistency with the registry's documented "(2) a rerun-affected map entry" contract and parity with its siblings. But note the description should be adjusted: this is purely restoring the module's own metadata invariant — it produces NO observable change, because rerunKeys is never rendered in any UI (BatchCart at EventComposer.jsx:1083 renders only systemStateDeltas). A more apt label for SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE's effects would arguably be ['powerStructure','economicState','narrative'] given its externalThreat/volatility deltas, but matching BROKERED_ALLIANCE is fine. Equally defensible alternative: delete the now-dead rerunKeys plumbing entirely (batch.js, the settlementSlice stash, and RERUN_KEYS_FOR_EVENT) since nothing consumes it.

verdicts.reasoning:
The factual core is verified. A node one-liner against the real modules confirms SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE (registry.js:222) is the ONLY one of 35 EVENT_REGISTRY entries absent from RERUN_KEYS_FOR_EVENT (34 entries), so RERUN_KEYS_FOR_EVENT['SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE'] === undefined. Its §9 siblings BROKERED_ALLIANCE (line 109) and OPENED_TRADE_ROUTE (line 111) are present. At batch.js:255 the `|| []` fallback means a staged settlement dispute contributes no keys to the batch's `rerunKeys` set. This violates the registry's own header contract (lines 4-7: every event needs a rerun-affected map entry), so as a latent data-integrity/unfinished-seam inconsistency it is real.

However, the claimed user-visible consequence — a "dishonest batch preview" reporting no affected subsystems — does NOT actually occur. I traced rerunKeys end-to-end: a full-repo grep (excluding node_modules) shows it has exactly three touchpoints: produced in batch.js (lines 225/255/270), stashed into pendingBatchPreview in settlementSlice.js:1449, and asserted in one unit test (tests/domain/eventBatch.test.js:263). NO component or PDF renderer ever reads pendingBatchPreview.rerunKeys. The only batch-preview renderer, BatchCart (EventComposer.jsx:1083-1132), displays the staged list, validation blocks, and systemStateDeltas (via DeltaRow) — never rerunKeys. There is no "affected subsystems" UI at all. The partial-rerun engine that consumed these keys was retired (registry.js:80-84; settlementSlice.js:829), so apply/regeneration paths don't read them either.

Crucially, what the DM actually sees for a staged dispute — the SystemState deltas — is computed independently at batch.js:243-248 via spec.stateDeltas, and SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE has a correct stateDeltas (registry.js:227-235). That output is fully unaffected by the missing rerun entry.

Net: the missing entry is a real but inert inconsistency / unfinished seam — it breaks the registry's documented invariant and would manifest as a wrong "affected subsystems" preview IF such UI were ever wired up, but today it has zero user-visible or data-level effect because the rerunKeys field is dead. Hence low severity, not the medium implied by a live "dishonest preview."
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct and trivial: add a SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE entry to RERUN_KEYS_FOR_EVENT to restore the registry invariant. Matching its stateDeltas (volatility, externalThreat, resilience) and its §9 sibling pattern, an honest entry would be: SETTLEMENT_DISPUTE: ['powerStructure', 'economicState', 'narrative'] (it sours relations with a neighbour — affects external standing/power and trade). The BROKERED_ALLIANCE-style minimal ['powerStructure','narrative'] would also satisfy the contract. Note for the reviewer: because rerunKeys currently has NO renderer, this fix is a contract-hygiene/future-proofing change, not a user-facing bug fix — consider either wiring an "affected subsystems" preview that consumes rerunKeys, or removing the now-dead rerunKeys field entirely, since shipping a computed-but-never-displayed field is the more durable defect here.

confirmed.title: Summary tab 'Tonight at the Table' renders '[object Object]' for NPC secrets
confirmed.evidence: tonightAtTheTable builds the NPC card body as `secret: ${truncate(npc.secret, 80)}` (line 45) and the TWIST fallback as `truncate(ranked[2].secret, 120)` (line 87), but the generator emits secret as an OBJECT: npcGenerator.js:93 `secret: { what: secret.secret, stakes: secret.stakes }`. truncate() does String(s) so the card body becomes 'secret: [object Object]'. Verified by execution: generateSettlementPipeline(seed 'probe-1') + tonightAtTheTable() returns entries like {kind:'NPC', title:'Aarav Acharya', body:'mayor · secret: [object Object]'}. This is the live path: OutputContainer.jsx:409 renders SummaryTabV2 (flag summaryMagazineV2 default true, flags.js:120) which calls tonightAtTheTable(settlement) at SummaryTabV2.jsx:90; TableView.jsx:58 (flag tableView default true) does the same. The unit test (tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js:34) masks this by fabricating string secrets.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Read the secret the way dossier/entityLinks.js:95 already does: `typeof npc.secret === 'string' ? npc.secret : npc.secret?.what`. Apply to both the NPC card trait (line 44-46) and the TWIST fallback (line 87), and add a test with the real object shape.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the code AND by direct execution. The generator emits object-shaped secrets: npcGenerator.js:93 `secret: { what: secret.secret, stakes: secret.stakes }`. tonightAtTheTable.js:45 interpolates `npc.secret` directly through truncate(), whose impl (line 109) is `String(s || '')`, producing `[object Object]`. The TWIST fallback at line 87 has the same flaw. Every other consumer in the repo correctly treats secret as an object — entityLinks.js:95 (`typeof npc.secret === 'string' ? npc.secret : npc.secret?.what`), pdf/lib/viewModel.js:882 (`typeof n.secret === 'object' && n.secret.what`), npcComponents.jsx:269, and npcGenerator.js:1101 — so the object shape is canonical and tonightAtTheTable is the lone offender. Live path verified: flags summaryMagazineV2 (flags.js:120) and tableView (flags.js:124) both default true; OutputContainer.jsx:408-409 mounts SummaryTabV2 which calls tonightAtTheTable at SummaryTabV2.jsx:90, and TableView.jsx:58 does the same. Executing generateSettlementPipeline({seed:'probe-1'}) + tonightAtTheTable() returned NPC entries with body `mayor · secret: [object Object]` and `elder · secret: [object Object]`, exactly as claimed. The narrativeGenerator.js:934-944 overwrite path does not save it — it only replaces secret when crimeLevel is truthy and otherwise lets the object pass through. The unit tests mask the bug by using only string secrets (tonightAtTheTable.test.js:23,34). Severity high is justified: it is wrong, user-visible output on the default-on Summary and Table session-running views, on essentially every settlement that has NPCs.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct and matches the established repo pattern (entityLinks.js:95). Read secret via `const secretText = typeof npc.secret === 'string' ? npc.secret : npc.secret?.what;` and use secretText in both the NPC-card trait (lines 44-46) and the TWIST fallback (line 87, on ranked[2].secret). Minor scope clarification: the line-45 NPC-card occurrence is the primary, near-universal hit; the line-87 TWIST occurrence is conditional — it only fires when settlement.history.legacyAnnotations is empty (in the probe run the annotation branch fired, so TWIST showed body ""). Both should be fixed. Add a test using the real { what, stakes } object shape, since the existing tests only exercise string secrets and would not catch a regression.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified end-to-end against the real modules. (1) The generator emits secret as an object: npcGenerator.js:93 `secret: { what: secret.secret, stakes: secret.stakes }`; confirmed by running generateSettlementPipeline({}, 'probe-1') — npcs[0].secret is `{what:"...", stakes:"..."}` (typeof object). (2) The consumer stringifies it: tonightAtTheTable.js:45 builds `secret: ${truncate(npc.secret, 80)}`, and truncate() (line 109) does `String(s || '')`, turning the object into "[object Object]". Same defect at the TWIST fallback line 87 `truncate(ranked[2].secret, 120)` — reproduced body "[object Object]". (3) Live path confirmed: tonightAtTheTable is imported and called in src/components/new/SummaryTabV2.jsx:30,90 and src/components/TableView.jsx:27,58, both rendering row.body directly (SummaryTabV2.jsx:282, TableView.jsx:219). Both gating flags default true: summaryMagazineV2 (flags.js:120) and tableView (flags.js:124). (4) Direct repro: feeding the real pipeline output into tonightAtTheTable yields NPC entries with body "mayor · secret: [object Object]" and "guild master · secret: [object Object]". (5) The unit test masks the bug by fabricating string secrets (tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js:23,34: secret:'corrupt', secret:'skimming the wall-fund') — a shape the generator never produces. Note: the claim's JSX file paths were imprecise (actual: src/components/new/SummaryTabV2.jsx and src/components/TableView.jsx, not src/components/output/...), and some JSX line numbers (409/90/58) don't all line up, but every load-bearing claim (object shape, tonightAtTheTable.js line numbers, flag defaults, live render path) is correct. Severity is medium not high: it is a visible presentation/data-integrity defect on a default-on main view (Summary tab + phone TableView show literal "[object Object]" instead of the NPC's secret), but no crash, no wrong simulation math, no money/auth/data-loss.
verdicts.fix_note: Read npc.secret.what instead of the whole object. Make it tolerant of both shapes since tests and possible legacy/AI data still pass plain strings. At line 44-46 use e.g. `const secretText = typeof npc.secret === 'string' ? npc.secret : npc.secret?.what; const trait = secretText ? `secret: ${truncate(secretText, 80)}` : (npc.want ? ... )`. Apply the same normalization at line 87 for the TWIST fallback: `truncate(typeof ranked[2].secret === 'string' ? ranked[2].secret : ranked[2].secret?.what, 120)`. Optionally also surface `.stakes`. Important: also update tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js to use the object shape `secret:{what:'...',stakes:'...'}` so the regression is actually guarded — the current string-only fixtures are what let this ship. Two NPCs also have no `npc.want` field in the real output, so consider that the fallback chain may resolve to role only; not a bug but worth noting for the fix author.

confirmed.title: 'Tonight at the Table' TWIST card reads nonexistent fields on legacyAnnotations — always generic title + empty body
confirmed.evidence: Lines 75-82 read `a.title || a.label` and `a.body || a.summary` from history.legacyAnnotations[0], but the producer emits `{ annotation, eventType, yearsAgo, severity, eventName }` (legacyGenerator.js:206-212, wired in generateNarratives.js:53). None of title/label/body/summary exist, so every real settlement renders a TWIST card titled 'The hidden thread' with an EMPTY body — verified by execution (seed 'probe-1' returns {kind:'TWIST', title:'The hidden thread', body:''}). Because annotations.length > 0 takes the if-branch, the legitimate NPC-secret fallback (line 83) is also permanently suppressed whenever annotations exist. The correct field name is proven by historyBeats.js:175-181, which reads `ann.annotation` / `ann.eventName` from the same array. The test (tonightAtTheTable.test.js:55) fabricates {title, body} annotations, masking the drift.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Read `a.eventName` for the title and `a.annotation` for the body (matching historyBeats.js), e.g. title: a.eventName || 'The hidden thread', body: truncate(a.annotation || '', 120); skip the annotation branch when a.annotation is empty so the NPC-secret fallback can run.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed real after attempting refutation from every angle. The producer deriveLegacyAnnotations (src/generators/legacyGenerator.js:208-214) emits annotations shaped {annotation, eventType, yearsAgo, severity, eventName} — there is no title/label/body/summary field. The only writer of history.legacyAnnotations (src/generators/steps/generateNarratives.js:53) assigns this producer output verbatim; no AI step or post-processor augments it with title/body. The consumer src/domain/summary/tonightAtTheTable.js:80-81 reads a.title||a.label and a.body||a.summary, all of which are undefined on real data, so it falls through to the literal defaults 'The hidden thread' and ''. I executed the consumer with a real-producer-shaped annotation and got exactly {kind:'TWIST', title:'The hidden thread', body:''}, matching the reviewer's seed-probe claim. Furthermore, because the if-branch fires whenever annotations.length>0 (the common case), the legitimate NPC-secret fallback at line 83 is permanently suppressed on real settlements. The correct field names are independently proven by two other consumers reading the same array: historyBeats.js:175-182 (ann.annotation / ann.eventName) and aiGroundingBundle.js:3406-3412. The test at tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js:55,95 masks the drift by fabricating {title, body} annotations the producer never emits. The consumer is reachable on live render paths: SummaryTabV2.jsx:90, TableView.jsx:58, and PDF SettlementPDF.jsx:120. The reviewer's cited test path (file:line) differs slightly from the actual location (tests/domain/...) but the content and substance are accurate.
verdicts.fix_note: Reviewer's suggested fix is correct and matches the proven convention in historyBeats.js. Concretely: title: a.eventName || 'The hidden thread', body: truncate(a.annotation || '', 120). To also restore the NPC-secret fallback, guard the branch on a non-empty annotation rather than just length: change `if (annotations.length > 0)` to `if (annotations[0]?.annotation)` (or `const a = annotations[0]; if (a?.annotation) {...} else if (ranked.length > 2 && ranked[2]?.secret) {...}`), so an annotation array of malformed/empty entries falls through to the NPC secret. Updating the test fixtures at tonightAtTheTable.test.js:55,95 to use the real shape {annotation, eventName, ...} is required, otherwise the corrected code would fail the now-wrong assertions.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via end-to-end trace and real-module execution.

Producer side: deriveLegacyAnnotations (src/generators/legacyGenerator.js:208-214) pushes objects shaped {annotation, eventType, yearsAgo, severity, eventName}. buildAnnotation (legacyGenerator.js:57-76) returns a plain string assigned to `annotation`. This array is wired onto history.legacyAnnotations in generateNarratives.js:53.

Consumer side: tonightAtTheTable.js:75-82 reads annotations[0] and builds the TWIST card from `a.title || a.label` (title) and `a.body || a.summary` (body). NONE of title/label/body/summary exist on the producer's objects, so title falls through to the literal 'The hidden thread' and body falls through to '' (truncate('') === '').

Corroboration that `annotation`/`eventName` are the real keys: historyBeats.js:174-184 and 235+ read exactly `ann.annotation` and `ann.eventName` from the same array. tonightAtTheTable.js is the ONLY reader anywhere in src/ that references title/label/body/summary on legacyAnnotations (grep-confirmed).

Concrete reproduction (read-only, real modules):
1. Direct call with the producer's exact shape returns {kind:'TWIST', title:'The hidden thread', body:''}.
2. Full generateSettlementPipeline over 10 seeds: 7 settlements had legacyAnnotations (keys exactly annotation,eventType,yearsAgo,severity,eventName) and ALL 7 produced the generic-title + empty-body TWIST card. Seed 'probe-1' reproduces precisely as the claim stated.

User-visible consequence: TableView.jsx:209/219 and SummaryTabV2.jsx:268/282 render row.title as the card heading and row.body as the card text. So on the on-screen Summary/Table-night view, every real settlement that carries legacy annotations (the common case — 7/10 here) shows a TWIST card titled 'The hidden thread' with an empty body. Additionally, because annotations.length>0 takes the if-branch, the legitimate NPC-secret fallback (line 83) is permanently suppressed whenever annotations exist — the feature's intended content never appears.

Scope correction to the original claim: the PDF is NOT affected. src/pdf/sections/TonightAtTheTable.jsx does not call the tonightAtTheTable() composer at all (it builds from vm.hooks/vm.npcs/vm.summary directly). The bug is confined to the two on-screen consumers. So this is a broken on-screen feature, not a PDF/dossier parity issue. Severity high (broken feature producing visibly empty/placeholder simulation content on the main Summary view), not critical (no data loss/money/security/crash).

The test at tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js:53-59 fabricates {title, body} annotations that don't match the producer, masking the drift — confirmed.
verdicts.fix_note:
The claim's diagnosis is correct but its suggested field mapping is slightly off, and the file/line cited for the test differs.

Correct fix in src/domain/summary/tonightAtTheTable.js:78-82 — map to the producer's real keys:
- body: truncate(a.annotation || '', 120)  (the actual narrative string)
- title: there is no natural short title on the object; the best source is a.eventName. Suggest: `title: a.eventName ? `Echo: ${a.eventName}` : 'The hidden thread'` (or just a.eventName). Keep 'The hidden thread' only as the final fallback.

Note truncate() already coerces null/undefined to '' and handles the long annotation strings (typically >120 chars), so truncate(a.annotation, 120) is safe.

Also fix the masking test (actual path: tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js:53-59, not src/domain/summary/tonightAtTheTable.test.js:55) to use the real shape {annotation:'...', eventName:'...', yearsAgo, severity, eventType} so it can't regress silently.

Out-of-scope observation found while reproducing (NOT this bug): when annotations are empty and the NPC-secret fallback at line 83-88 fires, several real seeds produced body '[object Object]' because npc.secret is an object, not a string (truncate stringifies it). That is a separate latent defect on the fallback branch and is currently hidden by this bug whenever annotations exist.

confirmed.title: 'Tonight at the Table' HOOK cards can never render: reads settlement.plotHooks/settlement.hooks which no generator writes
confirmed.evidence: Lines 56-69 source hooks from `settlement.plotHooks` falling back to `settlement.hooks`, ranked by `hook.tier` ('A'/'B'/'C') and rendered from `hook.title || hook.headline` / `hook.body || hook.summary`. Neither top-level field exists on any generated settlement: live probe (seed 'probe-1') shows both undefined, and aiLayer.js:202 documents 'settlement.plotHooks is never written — the live hooks are economicViability.plotHooks ({category, hook, severity}) and history.historicalEvents[].plotHooks (strings)'. No producer anywhere emits hooks with tier/title/headline/body fields (the real aggregator, dossier/plotHooks.js collectPlotHooks, emits {text, category, priority}). Result: the 'Top hook' cards — half of the composer's documented purpose (header lines 5-12) — never appear for any real settlement on the Summary tab or TableView. Tests fabricate {tier, title, body} hooks (tonightAtTheTable.test.js:43-47), so the suite passes while the feature is dead.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Source hooks via collectPlotHooks(settlement) from src/domain/dossier/plotHooks.js (or economicViability.plotHooks + history event hooks directly), rank by its numeric `priority`, and render `hook.text`; delete the tier/title/body vocabulary or map it from the real shape.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified by reading the code and live probing. tonightAtTheTable.js:56-58 sources hooks from settlement.plotHooks falling back to settlement.hooks, ranks by hook.tier (A/B/C, lines 59-62), and renders hook.title||hook.headline / hook.body||hook.summary (lines 66-67). No generator writes either top-level field: the final assembly step src/generators/steps/assembleSettlement.js (lines 52-102) defines no top-level plotHooks/hooks key, and neither do normalizeSettlement, generateCoherence, nor promoteStressorsToConditions. The in-code comment at src/generators/aiLayer.js:202-204 explicitly states 'settlement.plotHooks is never written.' Live probe confirms it: generating settlements (seed 'probe-1' plus 3 other tier/terrain/culture configs) yields settlement.plotHooks === undefined and settlement.hooks === undefined every time, and tonightAtTheTable returns 0 HOOK cards in all cases. The real aggregator collectPlotHooks (src/domain/dossier/plotHooks.js:59-151) emits {text, category, priority} — no tier/title/headline/body — so even the field names are wrong. The test tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js:41-51 fabricates {tier,title,body} plotHooks that no producer emits, so the suite passes while the feature is dead. The HOOK section runs unconditionally (no upstream guard), and the function is called live from TableView.jsx:58 and SummaryTabV2.jsx:90, both passing raw settlement. I could not refute any part of the claim. Minor scoping caveat: the composer is not entirely dead — NPC and TWIST cards do render — but the HOOK cards specifically never appear for any real settlement, matching the claim. (Separately observed: the RED 'don't mention' path is also dead because settlement.supplyChainState does not exist on generated settlements, but that is outside the scope of this specific claim.)
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct in direction: source via collectPlotHooks(settlement) from src/domain/dossier/plotHooks.js, rank by its numeric `priority` (descending — higher priority = more urgent), and render `hook.text` (collectPlotHooks has no title/body split). The tier/title/headline/body vocabulary should be removed. Note the existing test at tonightAtTheTable.test.js:41-51 must be rewritten to use the real {text,category,priority} shape, otherwise it will keep masking regressions. Worth also checking the RED-flag path: settlement.supplyChainState is never present on generated settlements, so that card is likely dead too (out of scope here but a candidate for the same fix pass).

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via live probe against the real pipeline. tonightAtTheTable.js:56-58 sources hooks from settlement.plotHooks falling back to settlement.hooks. Neither top-level field is ever written.

Trace, end-to-end:
- Input/state: A generated settlement from generateSettlementPipeline(config). I ran a read-only probe across 16 seed/tier combinations (probe-1/alpha/town-7/x x hamlet/village/town/city). In EVERY case settlement.plotHooks === undefined and settlement.hooks === undefined.
- What goes wrong: At line 56-58, `hooks` resolves to [] for all real settlements (both Array.isArray checks fail). The rankedHooks loop (63-68) iterates zero times, so no HOOK entries are ever pushed. Probe output: TATT entry kinds for probe-1 = "NPC, NPC, TWIST"; "# HOOK cards: 0" for all 16 combos.
- Why even the right source wouldn't match: The live hooks are nested, not top-level — economicViability.plotHooks ({category, hook, severity}), history.historicalEvents[].plotHooks (strings), and per-NPC/faction/institution plotHooks. The codebase's real aggregator collectPlotHooks (src/domain/dossier/plotHooks.js:59) emits {text, category, priority}. NONE of these have the tier/title/headline/body/summary fields this composer ranks and renders by. aiLayer.js:202 explicitly documents "settlement.plotHooks is never written."
- Consequence (user-visible): The "Top hook" cards — listed as a core deliverable in the file's own header (lines 5-12: "Top hook — Tier-A plot hook") — never render for any real settlement. Affects SummaryTabV2.jsx (Summary tab), TableView.jsx, and the PDF TonightAtTheTable section (SettlementPDF.jsx:120). Half the composer's documented purpose is dead output.
- Why the suite is green: tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js fabricates plotHooks as [{tier:'A', title, body}] (lines 43-44, 92-93, 103-104) — a shape no generator produces — so the HOOK assertions pass against synthetic data while the feature is dead in production.

Severity medium: it's a real, fully-dead feature path (broken seam) producing wrong/incomplete dossier output on a user-facing tab and the PDF, but it degrades gracefully (cards simply absent, no crash, no data loss). Not high because there's no determinism break, money, security, or simulation-correctness corruption — just missing presentation content. Confidence high (~0.9): live evidence + an in-repo comment that names the exact bug.
verdicts.fix_note:
Suggested fix should source hooks from the existing real aggregator, not invent new top-level fields. Import collectPlotHooks from src/domain/dossier/plotHooks.js, call it with the settlement, and map its output ({text, category, priority}) onto the HOOK cards: rank by descending `priority` (collectPlotHooks already returns them sorted by priority desc), use `category` for an optional label, and `truncate(hook.text, 120)` for the body. Drop the non-existent tier/title/headline/body/summary reads entirely.

Two scope corrections to the claim:
1. The defect is specific to the HOOK path. My probe showed the TWIST card DID render (via settlement.history.legacyAnnotations, which the pipeline does populate), so the "half the feature is dead" framing applies to the hook cards only — the NPC and TWIST paths are at least partly live. Verify the RED path (settlement.supplyChainState?.failures) similarly, as that field name should be confirmed against the real generator output before asserting it as a second dead path.
2. The fix must also update tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js, which currently fabricates {tier, title, body} hooks. After switching to collectPlotHooks, those fixtures must be rebuilt to the {text, category, priority} shape (or, better, the test should feed a real generated settlement) — otherwise the green suite continues to mask the same class of drift.

confirmed.title: 'Tonight at the Table' NPC ranking is a no-op: importance vocabulary matches no generator output
confirmed.evidence: Lines 38-41 rank NPCs by `npc.importance` against {major:0, primary:0, minor:1, supporting:1}. The pipeline's npcGenerator never sets `importance` (live probe: every npc.importance is undefined); the only writer is factionRoles.js:106 for synthesized structural NPCs, whose vocabulary is 'pillar'/'key'/'notable' (factionRoles.js:36-53) — none of which appear in the composer's order map, so every NPC gets rank 2 and the 'promote major NPCs' selection (doc header line 7, test at tonightAtTheTable.test.js:19) degenerates to array order. Similarly line 46 reads `npc.want`, which no generator emits (NPCs carry `goal: {short, long}`), so the wants-fallback is dead too.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Rank on the fields the generators actually emit: structuralRank ('dominant'/'subordinate', npcStructure.js:43) and/or power/influence, plus the factionRoles 'pillar'/'key'/'notable' importance values; read `npc.goal?.short` instead of `npc.want`.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. I read src/domain/summary/tonightAtTheTable.js, traced the NPC pipeline, and ran a live probe.

Composer order map (line 39): {major:0, primary:0, minor:1, supporting:1}; unknown → 2 (line 40).

Field-emission evidence:
- src/generators/npcGenerator.js emits goal:{short,long} (line 92), influence (95), power (96). Live probe of generateNPCs returned NPC keys = [id,name,gender,role,title,category,personality,physical,goal,secret,plotHooks,influence,power,presentation]. Every npc.importance = undefined/null; every npc.want absent; structuralRank null on the base generator output. So in production npc.importance is undefined → order[undefined] ?? 2 = 2 for every NPC → the sort is a stable no-op → 'promote major NPCs' degenerates to array order. Confirmed dead.
- Line 46 reads npc.want, which no generator emits (NPCs carry goal.short/goal.long), so the wants-fallback branch is dead; it falls through to role || 'major NPC'.

Vocabulary mismatch is total, not partial:
- The only writer of importance reaching settlement.npcs is factionRoles.js (ensureFactionStructuralNpcs, merged in assembleSettlement.js:143). Its values are 'pillar'/'key'/'notable' (factionRoles.js:36-53,106) — none appear in the order map, so even structural NPCs get rank 2.
- The broader domain importance vocabulary (roleCatalog.js, entities/npcs.js, npcAgency.js) is pillar/key/notable/minor. Only 'minor' overlaps the map; major/primary/supporting are emitted by nothing.

The test (tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js:19-29) passes only because it hand-feeds importance:'major'/'minor' — a vocabulary the real generators never produce, so it is self-confirming against fake data and does not protect the real path.

Consumer is SummaryTabV2.jsx (line 90) rendering the Summary-tab right column from pipeline settlement.npcs — the affected path is live, not dead.

This is a real, degraded-feature bug: the NPC ranking intent silently does nothing in production. Not a crash, not wrong simulation data, not a determinism break — it sits on a secondary presentation view, so medium severity is correct.
verdicts.fix_note: Fix direction is correct but adjust the field choices. Live probe shows base-generator NPCs do NOT carry structuralRank (it was null) — structuralRank is only present on the subset enriched via npcStructure.js, so it's not a reliable universal key. The fields actually present on every pipeline NPC are influence ('low'/'moderate'/'high') and power (numeric int). Recommend: primary sort on power (descending) or influence rank, then secondary recognition of the factionRoles importance vocabulary (pillar/key/notable) for structural NPCs, e.g. order = {pillar:0, key:1, notable:2} merged with a numeric power tiebreak. Read npc.goal?.short instead of npc.want (confirmed). Also update tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js to use real generator vocabulary (pillar/key/notable, influence/power) rather than the fictional 'major'/'minor' so the contract pins real behavior.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED with a live probe against the real pipeline. tonightAtTheTable.js:38-41 ranks NPCs by `npc.importance` against an order map keyed {major, primary, minor, supporting}, but the pipeline's NPCs do not use that vocabulary.

Reproduce (seed 12345, city config, real generateSettlementPipeline): settlement.npcs importance distribution = {undefined:4, key:1, notable:1}; npcs with .want = 0. Applying the composer's map (order[imp] ?? 2), EVERY npc maps to rank 2 — the post-sort order is byte-identical to the as-generated array order (verified: "array order first 4" === "after composer sort first 4"). So the doc-header promise (line 6-7, "governing faction's primary or most-corrupt NPC") and the test's "promotes major NPCs" intent silently degrade to "take the first two NPCs in generation order." The only map key that overlaps the real vocabulary is `minor` (rank 1), which never appears on pipeline NPCs; the live values are `undefined`, `key`, `notable` (and elsewhere in the codebase `pillar`), none of which the map recognizes.

Line 46's `npc.want` fallback is likewise dead: NPCs carry `goal:{short,long}` (npcGenerator.js:92), never `want` (probe: 0 NPCs with .want).

Two refinements to the claim: (1) factionRoles.js:106 is NOT the only importance writer — roleCatalog.js, entities/npcs.js (createNpc default 'notable'), and event mutations also write importance; and at runtime two structural NPCs did get key/notable. But this does not save the ranking, because key/notable/pillar are all absent from the order map too. (2) The original npcGenerator NPCs have importance === undefined, not merely an unrecognized string. Net effect is the same: ranking is a no-op.

inferImportance() exists in entities/npcs.js and would map roles→pillar/key/notable, but it is never applied to settlement.npcs before the composer (grep confirms only EventComposer/mutate/roleCatalog/store use it). TableView.jsx:58 and SummaryTabV2.jsx:90 pass the raw settlement straight in, as does the PDF TonightAtTheTable.

BONUS DEFECT (same lines, exposed by the probe): the body renders literally "secret: [object Object]" because line 45 stringifies npc.secret, which in real pipeline output is an object {what,stakes} (npcGenerator.js:93), not a string. This is user-visible garbage on both the on-screen dossier and the PDF — arguably worse than the ranking no-op. The test suite misses both because tonightAtTheTable.test.js feeds synthetic NPCs with importance:'major'/'minor' (matching the map) and string secrets — neither shape the pipeline emits, so the contract test pins behavior that never occurs in production.

Severity low: this is the Summary/Table-night convenience panel, not the simulation core. It does not corrupt persisted data, break determinism, or crash a main path — it degrades a feature's quality (wrong/garbled NPC picks on the cheat sheet). Real and user-visible, but cosmetic relative to economy/faction/billing correctness.
verdicts.fix_note:
The proposed framing (importance vocabulary matches no output) is correct but incomplete; a real fix must address three things, not one:

1. Ranking key mismatch: either (a) normalize via the existing inferImportance(npc) from domain/entities/npcs.js before sorting and use a map keyed to the real vocabulary {pillar:0, key:1, notable:2, minor:3} (plus treat undefined as lowest), or (b) better, rank by a signal that actually exists on pipeline NPCs — e.g. isGoverning/factionAffiliation === governing faction, presence of a secret, or the numeric `power` field (npcGenerator sets power 1-10). Note `influence` on pipeline NPCs is the STRING level 'high'/'moderate'/'low' (npcGenerator.js:95), not a number, so don't sort on it naively.

2. Dead `want` fallback (line 46): NPCs carry goal:{short,long}. Replace `npc.want` with something like `npc.goal?.short`.

3. The [object Object] bug (line 44-45, NOT in the original claim but in the same hot lines): npc.secret is an object {what,stakes} in pipeline output. Must read npc.secret?.what (or .stakes), not stringify the object. The test only passes because it uses string secrets; update tonightAtTheTable.test.js to use the real {what,stakes} object shape and the real importance vocabulary so the contract test reflects production data — otherwise the test will keep masking regressions.

confirmed.title: Phantom field settlement.supplyChainState.failures: read by two surfaces, written by nothing
confirmed.evidence: tonightAtTheTable.js:95 reads `settlement.supplyChainState?.failures` to build the RED 'Don't mention X' card, and src/components/map/RoutesToolbar.jsx:73 reads the same path for its route-risk hint. A repo-wide grep finds no writer: nothing ever assigns `supplyChainState` onto a settlement (live probe confirms the key is undefined), and domain/supplyChainState.js exports only derive functions (deriveSupplyChainState/deriveAllSupplyChainStates, lines 364/424) whose envelope has `status`, not `failures`. Both consumers are permanently dead — the canonical 'Don't mention salt — routes broken' beat documented at lines 92-94 can never appear. The test fabricates the field (tonightAtTheTable.test.js:75-85).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Derive failures live: filter deriveAllSupplyChainStates(settlement) to disrupted statuses ('blocked'/'collapsing'/'scarce', cf. hasDisruptedChains at supplyChainState.js:453) and map needLabel/failureConsequences into the {good, reason} shape both consumers expect — or change both consumers to call the derivation directly.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the claim holds. Both cited consumers read settlement.supplyChainState?.failures: tonightAtTheTable.js:95 (builds the RED "Don't mention X" card, lines 96-103) and RoutesToolbar.jsx:73 (drives topFailure, used at lines 179/195 for the route-risk hint). Neither path can ever fire because nothing writes supplyChainState onto a settlement.

Writer search (exhaustive, whole repo incl. edge/migrations): no `.supplyChainState =` assignment and no `supplyChainState:` object-literal key exist anywhere except the test fabrication at tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js:77 and :96. The economy generator (generateEconomy.js:255) calls deriveSupplyChainState(chain) locally for trace emission but never attaches the result under settlement.supplyChainState.

Envelope shape (live-probed): deriveSupplyChainState returns id,name,needKey,needLabel,status,legacyStatus,controller,dependencies,substitutes,beneficiaries,victims,failureConsequences,regionalPressures,... — `'failures' in env` === false. There is a `status` field and a `failureConsequences` field, but no `failures` and no top-level supplyChainState container (supplyChainState.js:371-420, deriveAllSupplyChainStates at 424-431).

Canonical field registry corroborates: fieldManifest.js:290-303 registers the supply-chain surface as the derivation deriveAllSupplyChainStates (producer = supplyChainState.js, called by region/aiGrounding/explanation), NOT as a settlement.supplyChainState property — there is no manifest entry for either a supplyChainState property or a failures field. Every other domain consumer (counterfactual, capacityModel, dailyLife, hookEscalation, aiGrounding, explanation, causalState, region, devAnomalies, qualitativeBands, devDebug) calls the derive function; only the two cited surfaces read the phantom path.

Consequence: tonightAtTheTable's failures is always [], so the documented canonical "Don't mention salt — routes broken" RED beat (comment at lines 92-94) never renders. RoutesToolbar's topFailure is always undefined, so the {topFailure && (...)} hint block at line 179 never renders. The test passes only because it fabricates the field, masking the dead seam. Both consumers are permanently dead. I could not refute the finding.
verdicts.fix_note:
Claim is accurate; one metadata nit: the test lives at tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js (the relevant test is at lines 75-85 there), not src/domain/summary/tonightAtTheTable.test.js as stated. Substance unchanged.

The suggested fix is sound. Note the envelope already exposes failureConsequences (per-chain) and needLabel, and hasDisruptedChains at supplyChainState.js (cited ~line 453) plus the disrupted statuses ('blocked'/'collapsing'/'scarce', and arguably 'captured'/'substituted') give a clean filter. Concrete shape both consumers expect is { good, reason }: map a disrupted chain's needLabel -> good and failureConsequences (or upstreamNote/magicNote) -> reason. Cleanest implementation is a small shared helper, e.g. deriveSupplyFailures(settlement) in supplyChainState.js that filters deriveAllSupplyChainStates to disrupted statuses and maps into {good, reason}, then have BOTH tonightAtTheTable.js:95 and RoutesToolbar.jsx:73 call it — avoids reintroducing the same phantom-path drift in two places and lets the existing tests assert against a real derivation rather than a fabricated field.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED. The claim reproduces exactly.

Concrete failure path, end to end:
1. A settlement is generated by the pipeline. Supply-chain status lives at settlement.economicState.activeChains[] with per-chain `status` (e.g. 'blocked','collapsing','scarce'). There is NO top-level `settlement.supplyChainState` property and NO `failures` array anywhere in the data model.
2. The canonical surface for chain state is derive-on-demand: domain/supplyChainState.js exports deriveSupplyChainState (line 364) and deriveAllSupplyChainStates (line 424). Their envelope keys are id,name,needKey,needLabel,status,legacyStatus,controller,dependencies,substitutes,beneficiaries,victims,failureConsequences,regionalPressures,outputs,... (verified via live node probe). The string "failures" does not appear anywhere in supplyChainState.js (grep -F exit 1). fieldManifest.js:291-303 documents this derive function — not a persisted property — as the producer.
3. Repo-wide grep finds zero writers of a `supplyChainState` property onto a settlement: no assignment, no object-literal `supplyChainState:` in any src file (only in the test fixture). The single emitChainTraces call site (generateEconomy.js:255) consumes the derived state locally to build trace receipts and discards it.
4. Consequence — two dead consumers:
   - tonightAtTheTable.js:95 reads `settlement.supplyChainState?.failures || []` to build the only `kind:'RED'` "Don't mention X" card (lines 96-103). Since the path is permanently undefined, the array is always empty and the RED card can NEVER render. This is consumed in production by TableView.jsx:58 and SummaryTabV2.jsx:90 (both render unconditionally), and the PDF section TonightAtTheTable.jsx. The RED styling machinery is fully wired (TableView KIND_ACCENT.RED/KIND_LABEL.RED, red accent #A23434) but dead.
   - RoutesToolbar.jsx:73 reads the identical dead path for its top route-risk hint (topFailure = failures[0]); that hint is therefore permanently absent.
5. Live probe proof: built a settlement with a 'blocked' salt chain; settlement.supplyChainState === undefined; deriveSupplyChainState env.failures === undefined, env.status === 'blocked'; no derived chain carries a `failures` property.
6. The masking test: tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js:75-85 (and :96) hand-fabricates `supplyChainState:{failures:[{good:'salt',reason:'routes broken'}]}` as a literal fixture and asserts the RED card — so the test is green while the production path is dead. The original evidence cited the test at the wrong path (src/.../tonightAtTheTable.test.js) but it exists at tests/domain/tonightAtTheTable.test.js; the substance (test fabricates the field) is exact.

This is a real broken feature / dead seam: the documented canonical 'Don't mention salt — routes broken' beat and the route-risk hint can never appear. Severity medium: no crash, data loss, or money/security impact (the `?.`/`|| []` guards make it fail safe to "no card"), but two user-facing surfaces are silently and permanently feature-dead, and a unit test gives false confidence. Confidence ~0.95.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix should not introduce a new persisted `supplyChainState` property (that would fight the derive-on-demand architecture documented in fieldManifest.js and settlement.schema.js:263). Instead, both consumers should derive failures from the canonical envelope. Concretely: import deriveAllSupplyChainStates(settlement), then map non-stable chains to failure shapes, e.g. const failures = deriveAllSupplyChainStates(settlement).filter(c => c.status !== 'stable').map(c => ({ good: c.name, reason: (c.failureConsequences && c.failureConsequences[0]) || c.upstreamNote || c.status })). Note the consumers currently expect `f.good` and `f.reason`; the envelope exposes `name` (not `good`), `status`, `failureConsequences` (array), `upstreamNote`, `magicNote` — so a real fix must map names, not just repoint the path. Caveat: tonightAtTheTable.js is documented as a pure composer; deriveAllSupplyChainStates is pure and store-agnostic, so importing it there is consistent with the three-layer rule. Also update the test to feed economicState.activeChains[] with a real disrupted chain rather than fabricating supplyChainState.failures, otherwise the test will continue to mask the path. RoutesToolbar.jsx (presentation) should call the same domain derive helper rather than reaching into raw state, to keep parity with the dossier surface.

confirmed.title: Public-safe projection strips 'landmarks' from every quarter: bare 'dm' substring in PRIVATE_KEY_RE over-matches
confirmed.evidence: PRIVATE_KEY_RE includes the bare alternation `dm` with no boundary, so any key CONTAINING 'dm' is dropped — including `lan**dm**arks`. Verified by execution: toPublicSafe(pipeline output) reduces spatialLayout.quarters[] from {name, location, desc, landmarks} to {name, location, desc}. landmarks is legitimately public content rendered by the public dossier path (PublicDossierView.jsx:118 → OutputContainer → OverviewTab.jsx:251, SummaryTab.jsx:326, pdf/sections/Overview.jsx:373), so every gallery/anonymous dossier silently loses its quarter landmarks. The server twin has the identical pattern (supabase/migrations/020_gallery_public_privacy.sql:38 `key ~* '(secret|private|dm|...)'`), so stored public reads are equally affected. The same regex's bare `note` also strips simulation-attribution fields the Economics surfaces use (magicNote, upstreamNote, storageNote — registered dossier consumers in fieldManifest.js:219-247). Fail-closed direction, so no leak — but real public-content loss.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Tighten the `dm` alternation to explicit keys or a boundary form (e.g. `dmNotes|dmCompass|\bdm\b|(^|[^a-z])gm([^a-z]|$)`) in BOTH the client regex and a follow-up SQL migration replacing _gallery_sanitize_public_json; per the file's own rule the denylist may only grow, so keep the explicit dm-prefixed keys while removing the substring over-match, and add a regression test that quarters keep landmarks.

verdicts.reasoning:
I could not refute this; every link in the chain is verified by reading code and by execution.

1. Regex over-match (src/domain/display/publicSafe.js:24): PRIVATE_KEY_RE = /(secret|private|dm|gm|...|note|...)/i. The bare `dm` and `note` alternations have no word boundary, so any key CONTAINING them is dropped. Verified by execution: RE.test('landmarks')=true (contains "dm"), RE.test('magicNote')/'upstreamNote'/'storageNote'=true (contains "note"). (Also incidentally over-matches things like 'admin'/'endmark'.)

2. The stripped content is real generator output: src/generators/spatialGenerator.js builds quarters[] with {name, location, desc, landmarks} (lines 25-171). Economics activeChains[] carry magicNote/upstreamNote/storageNote (registered dossier consumers in src/domain/fieldManifest.js:219-247).

3. End-to-end execution confirms the loss: importing toPublicSafe and running it on a sample pipeline shape reduces spatialLayout.quarters[0] from [name,location,desc,landmarks] to [name,location,desc], and activeChains[0] from [label,magicNote,upstreamNote,storageNote] to [label].

4. The path is live and public: src/lib/gallery.js:406 sets dossier.settlement = toPublicSafe(row.data, {full: gallery_share_dm}). GalleryDetail.jsx:207 renders <PublicDossierView dossier={dossier}/>; PublicDossierView.jsx:52 destructures settlement from dossier and PublicDossierView.jsx:118 passes it to <OutputContainer ... playerView={!shareDm}>. In playerView, OutputContainer.jsx:339 hides only summary/dm_notes/ai_notes — the Overview tab and Economics tab stay visible. Landmarks are rendered by OverviewTab.jsx:251 (and SummaryTab.jsx:326, which is hidden in player view, plus PDF Overview.jsx:373 and IdentityDailyLife.jsx:240). Economic notes render at EconomicsTab.jsx:128 (magicNote), SupplyChainsPanel.jsx:191/SupplyChainFlow.jsx:153 (upstreamNote), ViabilityTab.jsx:121. So default-mode (non-share_dm, i.e. the common gallery case) anonymous/gallery dossiers and their PDFs silently lose quarter landmarks and economic-chain attribution notes.

5. Server twin matches: supabase/migrations/020_gallery_public_privacy.sql:38 has the identical regex `key ~* '(secret|private|dm|gm|...|note|...)'` inside _gallery_sanitize_public_json, applied to stored public reads — so the loss is authoritative, not just client-side cosmetic.

6. Not intentional / no guard: the file header (lines 15-18) frames the list as fail-closed and "must only ever GROW," but nothing documents `dm`-substring or landmark stripping as deliberate. tests/domain/display/publicSafe.test.js and tests/security/gallery_privacy.contract.test.js assert only that genuinely-private keys are stripped (dmNotes, dmCompass, secret, etc.); no test asserts landmarks survive OR that they should be removed.
