The over-match is incidental.

Direction is fail-closed (drops content, no leak), so this is not a security defect — it is silent public-content loss / parity drift, which fits the product's stated "dossier/PDF parity" and "data integrity" concerns. Severity medium is appropriate: a real, user-visible loss of legitimately-public simulation output on every default-mode gallery/anonymous dossier and PDF, but no money/security/crash impact.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound. Replace the bare `dm` and `gm` substring alternations with boundary/explicit forms in BOTH the client regex (publicSafe.js:24) and a NEW follow-up SQL migration (do not edit 020 in place; add a migration that CREATE OR REPLACEs _gallery_sanitize_public_json with the corrected regex — Postgres regex uses \y / \m\M for word boundaries, e.g. `\m(dm|gm)\M|dmNotes|dmCompass`). Also tighten `note`: it over-matches the public economic-attribution fields magicNote/upstreamNote/storageNote, so it should be narrowed to the actually-private keys (e.g. dossierNotes|dmNotes|narrativeNotes|notes) or use a boundary, otherwise the fix leaves the economics notes still stripped. Be careful: toPublicSafe relies on the `note` match to strip top-level notes/dossierNotes in default mode (publicSafe.js comments at 76-80 note full-mode strips them explicitly, but default mode leans on the recursive `note` denylist) — so when narrowing `note`, keep the genuinely-private note keys covered. Add regression tests asserting quarters keep landmarks and activeChains keep magicNote/upstreamNote/storageNote, while dmNotes/dossierNotes/narrativeNotes are still removed, in both publicSafe.test.js and the SQL/contract path.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED by execution + full source trace.

THE OVER-MATCH (src/domain/display/publicSafe.js:24): PRIVATE_KEY_RE contains bare, unanchored alternations `dm` and `note`. The regex is /i and tested with .test(key), so any key CONTAINING those substrings is dropped. Executed against the real module: PRIVATE_KEY_RE.test('landmarks') === true, 'magicNote' === true, 'upstreamNote' === true, 'storageNote' === true (also collateral: 'admin', 'windmill', 'bedminster', 'foundmoney' all match).

REPRODUCED end-to-end with the real module:
  toPublicSafe({ spatialLayout:{ quarters:[{name,location,desc,landmarks:[...]}] }, economy:{magicNote,upstreamNote,storageNote} })
  => quarters[0] = {name, location, desc}  (landmarks GONE)
  => economy = {}  (all three notes GONE)

THE DATA IS REAL: spatialGenerator.js (lines 29,41,51,61,78,88,98,108,118,130,142,155,165,171) emits `landmarks` arrays on quarters; foodGenerator.js:280/313 and computeActiveChains.js / chainMagicSubstitution.js emit storageNote/magicNote/upstreamNote on chains. magicNote and upstreamNote are registered dossier fields in fieldManifest.js:219-247.

THE CONSUMERS ARE REAL & PUBLIC: landmarks rendered at OverviewTab.jsx:251, SummaryTab.jsx:326, pdf/sections/Overview.jsx:373, IdentityDailyLife.jsx:240. The economy notes rendered at EconomicsTab.jsx:128, SupplyChainsPanel.jsx:191, ViabilityTab.jsx:121, pdf/sections/SupplyChainFlow.jsx:153.

THE PUBLIC PATH IS REAL: fetchPublicDossier (gallery.js:165) -> sanitizeDossier (gallery.js:397) -> toPublicSafe(row.data, {full: gallery_share_dm}) at gallery.js:406 -> dossier.settlement -> PublicDossierView.jsx:118 OutputContainer (readOnly). In default (non-shareDm) mode the strip runs, so every gallery/anonymous dossier silently loses quarter landmarks AND supply-chain notes. The SERVER twin (020_gallery_public_privacy.sql:38) has the identical regex, so the server RPC already strips these too — defense-in-depth strips it twice; either way the public viewer never sees them.

DIRECTION: fail-closed (content dropped, never leaked) — so NOT a security bug, no DM-private leak. It is genuine public-content loss: the public dossier/PDF parity quietly degrades vs the owner's view.

SEVERITY medium: not money/security/crash; it is real, deterministic, user-visible loss of legitimate public simulation output (landmarks per quarter + supply-chain magic/import/storage notes) on the entire public/gallery surface, affecting both client and server twins. Confidence ~0.9: the only thing keeping it from 'high' is that it is cosmetic content loss on a secondary (public read) surface rather than the primary forge path, and a skeptical reviewer might argue some of these notes are intentionally DM-flavored — but landmarks are unambiguously public-rendered content.
verdicts.fix_note:
The EVIDENCE's framing is correct but the fix must address TWO independent over-matches, not just `dm`:

1. Bare `dm` over-matches `landmarks` (and `windmill`, `admin`, etc.). The only legitimate private dm* keys are `dmCompass`, `dmNote`, `dmNotes` (verified: grep finds no other dm* object keys). Replace the bare `dm` alternation with anchored/explicit forms, e.g. `\bdm[A-Z]` won't work for a whole-key test cleanly — simplest is to enumerate: `dmCompass|dmNotes?` (covers dmNote and dmNotes), or anchor with `^dm` if the regex is applied per-key with ^/$ anchoring. Same for `gm` (currently matches any substring — enumerate `gmNotes?`/`^gm`).

2. CRITICAL ADDITION the evidence understates: the bare `note` alternation ALSO over-matches the legitimately-PUBLIC economy fields magicNote / upstreamNote / storageNote (confirmed: all three .test() true). These are NOT DM-private — they are public supply-chain attribution rendered in the public dossier. The intended private note keys are only `notes`, `dmNotes`, `dossierNotes`, `narrativeNotes`. So `note` must be scoped to those exact keys (e.g. `(^|_)notes?$` style or explicit `dossierNotes|narrativeNotes|dmNotes|notes`), NOT a bare substring. Note `narrativeNotes` is already a separate alternation so you only need `dossierNotes|dmNotes|notes`.

3. Apply the SAME fix to the server twin (supabase/migrations/020_gallery_public_privacy.sql:38) — the Postgres `~*` regex has the identical bare `dm`/`note` problem and a new forward migration is required, since the server strips these before they ever reach the client.

4. After tightening, re-verify the still-intended drops survive: secret, private, guidance, plotHook(s), hook, compass, chronicle, pinnedNpc, aiData, aiSettlement, aiDailyLife, identityMarkers, frictionPoints, connectionsMap, dmCompass, dmNotes, dossierNotes, narrativeNotes — and that NPC goal/secret/plotHooks/relationships path-scoped drops (lines 39/41-42) are unaffected. Add a regression test asserting toPublicSafe preserves quarters[].landmarks and economicState.activeChains[].{magicNote,upstreamNote} while still dropping dmCompass/dmNotes/secret.

confirmed.title: normalizeSettlement stamps current SCHEMA_VERSION before running migrations — unversioned legacy saves will skip every future migration
confirmed.evidence: Step 1 (line 126) sets `out.schemaVersion = SCHEMA_VERSION` whenever the field is null/undefined, and only afterwards (line 160) calls migrateSettlementToLatest(out). The migration runner's documented contract is that missing schemaVersion is treated as v0 so the from:0 entry applies (settlementMigrations.js:37-39, 59-66: 'covers any pre-versioned save with no schemaVersion field at all'), but the pre-stamp makes currentVersion() == SCHEMA_VERSION, so the chain's while-loop never executes for unversioned saves. normalizeSettlement is the ONLY caller of migrateSettlementToLatest and is the load boundary for all saves (lib/saves.js:123 migrateSettlementShape). Today this is invisible because the sole 0→1 migration is a no-op stamp, but the moment SCHEMA_VERSION bumps to 2 with a real structural rewrite, every pre-versioning save loads stamped schemaVersion=2 while still carrying the pre-v1 shape — all migrations silently skipped, the exact silent-data-loss case the registry exists to prevent. The integration tests (tests/domain/settlementMigrations.test.js:127-131) can't catch it since the no-op makes both orders indistinguishable.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In normalizeSettlement, do not pre-stamp schemaVersion: leave it absent (or stamp 0) and let migrateSettlementToLatest assign the final version (its 0→1 entry already stamps 1); alternatively run migrateSettlementToLatest BEFORE the version-stamp block. Add a test that a versionless settlement actually passes through the from:0 migration (e.g. by asserting a marker the migration sets).

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the code and reproducing the mechanism at runtime. In src/domain/normalizeSettlement.js, step 1 (line 126) does `if (out.schemaVersion == null) out.schemaVersion = SCHEMA_VERSION;`, and only afterwards (line 160) returns `migrateSettlementToLatest(out)`. For an unversioned legacy save, the field is undefined, so it is pre-stamped to SCHEMA_VERSION (currently 1). The migration runner's loop is `while (currentVersion(out) < SCHEMA_VERSION)` (settlementMigrations.js:85) where currentVersion returns the stamped value; after the pre-stamp `1 < 1` is false, so the loop body never runs and the `from:0 → to:1` migrate() function is never actually invoked. I reproduced this with a node one-liner: an unversioned save passed through normalize's ordering yields `migration loop would run? false`. The runner's documented contract (settlementMigrations.js:37-39, 59-66) is that a missing schemaVersion is treated as v0 so the from:0 entry applies — the pre-stamp defeats that.

Caller chain verified: normalizeSettlement is the ONLY caller of migrateSettlementToLatest (grep), and saves.js:123/258 (migrateSettlementShape) calls normalizeSettlement on every loaded save, so this is the real load boundary.

The defect is currently dormant/invisible because the sole registered migration is a no-op that sets `schemaVersion: 1` — identical to what the pre-stamp produces. The no-op's own comment (settlementMigrations.js:47-49) explicitly notes it relies on normalize already stamping the version, which is the latent hazard. The integration test (tests/domain/settlementMigrations.test.js:127-131) only asserts result.schemaVersion === SCHEMA_VERSION, which passes under both orderings, so it cannot catch the bug. The direct-runner tests (lines 62-67) bypass normalize and test the runner correctly, masking the seam.

Activation: when SCHEMA_VERSION bumps to >=2 with a structurally-meaningful migration, a pre-versioned (true v0) save will be stamped directly to the latest version by line 126 while still carrying the old shape, and the entire migration chain (0→1, 1→2, ...) is silently skipped — the exact silent-data-corruption case the migration registry exists to prevent. I could not refute the claim; the evidence holds. Severity is medium rather than high because it is a presently-dormant broken seam (no real structural migration exists yet), but it is a confirmed defect in load-boundary migration logic that will fail silently the first time the migration system is used for its intended purpose.
verdicts.fix_note: Reviewer's fix is correct. Cleanest option: run `out = migrateSettlementToLatest(out)` BEFORE the version-stamp block, letting the chain assign the version from the save's true version; the existing 0→1 entry already stamps schemaVersion=1, and the runner's currentVersion() already treats missing/null as 0. Then keep the simulationVersion/generatorVersion stamps (those are unrelated to the migration runner and can stay as pre-stamps). Alternatively, simply do not pre-stamp schemaVersion at all and let migrateSettlementToLatest own it. Note the defensive nullish-input branch (lines 111-120) returns a fresh object stamped at SCHEMA_VERSION without going through the runner — that is fine since there is no legacy shape to migrate there. Add an integration test that distinguishes the orderings, e.g. give the from:0 migration a detectable side-effect/marker and assert normalizeSettlement on a versionless save carries that marker (the current no-op-equality is why the existing test can't catch it).

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED. The claim is accurate against the real code.

Order-of-operations evidence (src/domain/normalizeSettlement.js):
- Line 126: `if (out.schemaVersion == null) out.schemaVersion = SCHEMA_VERSION;` — stamps the CURRENT schema version onto any settlement that lacks the field.
- Line 160: `return migrateSettlementToLatest(out);` — the migration runner is invoked AFTER the stamp, on the same `out` object.

Runner contract evidence (src/domain/settlementMigrations.js):
- currentVersion() (lines 62-66) maps missing/null/0/NaN/negative schemaVersion to 0 so the from:0 entry applies. The 0->1 entry's description (line 45) and the file header (lines 37-39) explicitly state from:0 'covers any pre-versioned save with no schemaVersion field at all'.
- The runner loop (line 85) is `while (currentVersion(out) < SCHEMA_VERSION)`. Because normalizeSettlement pre-stamps schemaVersion = SCHEMA_VERSION, currentVersion(out) === SCHEMA_VERSION on entry, so the loop body NEVER executes for unversioned saves. The migration chain is entirely skipped.

Why it's invisible today: SCHEMA_VERSION = 1 (settlement.schema.js:47) and the sole migration (0->1) is an intentional no-op stamp (lines 46-51, header line 26). With a no-op the two orderings (stamp-then-migrate vs migrate-only) are indistinguishable, which is exactly why the integration test at tests/domain/settlementMigrations.test.js:127-131 passes either way and cannot catch this.

Load-boundary confirmation: normalizeSettlement is the only caller of migrateSettlementToLatest (grep verified), and lib/saves.js:123 (migrateSettlementShape) calls normalizeSettlement on every loaded save, including the local-load path at saves.js:258. So this is the real load boundary for all persisted settlements.

Concrete failure reproduction (node, against logic replicating the runner with a future SCHEMA_VERSION=2 plus a real 1->2 migration that renames legacy `pop` -> `population`):
  legacy input:    {name:'Oldtown', pop:1200, _seed:'abc'}  (no schemaVersion)
  BUGGY  result:   {name:'Oldtown', pop:1200, schemaVersion:2}      <- population MISSING, still carries pre-v1 `pop`, but stamped as v2
  CORRECT result:  {name:'Oldtown', schemaVersion:2, population:1200} <- migrated correctly
The buggy path produces a settlement labeled schemaVersion=2 that still holds the v0/v1 field shape with the structural rewrite silently skipped — the exact silent-data-corruption case the migration registry exists to prevent. A pre-v1 save would render/PDF/AI-process with the field still under its legacy name (undefined where consumers read the canonical name).

Fix-safety reproduction (node, against the REAL current modules, SCHEMA_VERSION=1): migrate-first with no pre-stamp yields schemaVersion=1 for unversioned, already-current, and garbage-version inputs alike — the runner stamps the latest version as the natural end state of the chain, so removing the pre-stamp does not regress any case.

Severity: medium, not high/critical. The bug is latent — it cannot cause incorrect output until SCHEMA_VERSION is bumped to 2+ with a genuine structural migration AND a genuinely pre-versioning save is loaded. It is a real, dormant data-integrity/migration-seam defect (broken seam) rather than a current-main-path failure. Confidence ~0.9 that a skeptical senior reviewer with full code access agrees: the ordering is unambiguous, the documented contract is unambiguous, and the failure is mechanically reproducible the instant a real migration is added.
verdicts.fix_note:
The reporter's framing is correct; here are precise fix options.

Preferred fix: delete the schemaVersion pre-stamp at normalizeSettlement.js:126 entirely (keep simulationVersion/generatorVersion stamps at 127-128, which are unrelated to the migration chain). migrateSettlementToLatest already stamps schemaVersion = SCHEMA_VERSION as the terminal state of the chain, so removal does not regress the stamping behavior — verified for unversioned, current, and garbage-version inputs (all still end at SCHEMA_VERSION). This makes currentVersion(out) read 0 for unversioned saves so the from:0 entry runs, and every future from:N entry runs in sequence.

Caveat to watch when implementing: the no-arg/nullish defensive branch (normalizeSettlement.js:108-121) returns an object with schemaVersion: SCHEMA_VERSION directly without calling the runner. That path is fine to leave as-is (a freshly-constructed minimal shape is by definition current). Only the main path (line 123 onward) needs the pre-stamp removed.

Also recommended: add a regression test that is NOT order-blind. The current integration test (settlementMigrations.test.js:126-150) is defeated by the no-op. A durable test would register a temporary real from:1->2 migration (or assert the relative order: that for a settlement with no schemaVersion, the value read by the FIRST migration's `from` matching is 0, e.g. by asserting a spy/probe on currentVersion, or by testing that an unversioned input with a legacy-only field gets the field transformed by a real future migration). Without such a test the seam silently re-breaks if someone re-adds a pre-stamp.

Minor: the StructuredOutput severity 'medium' reflects that this is dormant until the next real SCHEMA_VERSION bump. If the team treats migration-seam correctness as load-bearing (it is, per product goals on data integrity), they may choose to fix it now rather than discover it during a future migration when pre-versioned saves in the wild would corrupt on load.

confirmed.title: migrateLocalCustomContentToCloud reads the user-scoped localStorage bucket instead of the anon/grandfathered bucket, then permanently sets the migrated flag - grandfathered custom content never reaches the cloud
confirmed.evidence: The slice docstring (lines 13-15) says 'On sign-in as premium, any local items are migrated to the cloud once'. Items created before sign-in live under the unscoped key 'sf_custom_content' (scopedLocalKey returns LOCAL_KEY only for owner 'anon', line 27). But migrateLocalCustomContentToCloud computes ownerId = ownerIdFromState(get()) (line 245) - which is the signed-in user's id since canUseCustomContent() gates the call - and then flattenLocalContent(ownerId) (line 249) reads 'sf_custom_content:<uid>', a bucket that is empty on first premium sign-in (nothing writes it until the user is already signed in). With items.length === 0 the migrated flag is set to '1' (lines 250-252) and the function returns - so the real anon-bucket items are never migrated, and the user-scoped flag makes the miss permanent for that account/device. Corroborating: lib/customContent.js:188 provides readLocalForMigration() which reads the UNSCOPED key and is documented 'used for migration to cloud' - it has zero callers.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Migrate from the anon bucket: use flattenLocalContent('anon') (or the existing customContentService.readLocalForMigration()) as the migration source, and only set the migrated flag after a successful push (or after confirming the anon bucket is empty).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code; the claim holds on every step.

1. Grandfathered anon/free-created items live in the UNSCOPED key 'sf_custom_content'. scopedLocalKey() (src/store/customContentSlice.js:25-29) returns LOCAL_KEY only for owner 'anon'; addCustomItem writes via ownerIdFromState (line 140), which is 'anon' for unauthenticated users. The cache test (tests/store/customContentSlice.cache.test.js:37,41) explicitly confirms anon items land in the unscoped key while signed-in items go to 'sf_custom_content:<uid>'.

2. migrateLocalCustomContentToCloud (lines 242-273) computes ownerId = ownerIdFromState(get()) (line 245). The call is gated by canUseCustomContent() (line 244), which is true only for premium tier or elevated role (src/store/authSlice.js:364-368) — i.e. an authenticated user — so ownerId is the signed-in UUID, never 'anon'. It then reads flattenLocalContent(ownerId) (line 249) => the user-scoped bucket 'sf_custom_content:<uid>'.

3. That bucket is empty on first premium sign-in. The only writers of the user-scoped key are addCustomItem/updateCustomItem/deleteCustomItem (require the user to have already been signed in and editing) and loadCustomContentFromCloud (line 228) — and in App.jsx:219-220 migration runs FIRST, with loadCustomContentFromCloud only in the .then(). customContent is not in the Zustand persist partialize (src/store/index.js:63-74), so no hydration seeds it either. Confirmed via grep: no other writers of 'sf_custom_content' exist outside the slice/service.

4. With items.length === 0, the flag is set to '1' (lines 250-252) and the function returns; the early-return guard (line 247) makes the miss permanent for that account/device. So the real anon-bucket grandfathered items are never pushed to the cloud — directly contradicting the slice docstring (lines 13-15) "any local items are migrated to the cloud once".

5. The correct unscoped reader customContentService.readLocalForMigration() (src/lib/customContent.js:188-198), documented "used for migration to cloud", has ZERO callers (grep-confirmed) — corroborating that the migration was meant to read the unscoped bucket but doesn't.

Could not refute: the path is the main premium-upgrade flow (App.jsx:214-226), no upstream guard handles it, no test covers the migration path (the cache test only covers add/clear scoping, not migrate), and the cited APIs behave exactly as the reviewer states.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct in direction. Use the anon bucket as the migration source: flattenLocalContent('anon') (or customContentService.readLocalForMigration(), which already reads the unscoped key). Important additional point: only set the migrated flag AFTER a successful push (the early flag-set in lines 250-252 is itself fragile — if migration ever runs before the anon bucket has content, or push fails partway, the permanent flag locks out a retry). Also consider: after a successful migration the anon-bucket items should arguably be cleared (customContentService.localClear or equivalent) so a later sign-in on the same device with a different account doesn't re-migrate the same items into the second account — otherwise flattenLocalContent('anon') is non-empty for every subsequent first-sign-in on that device. At minimum, gate the flag on a confirmed-empty-source OR successful-push, not on the empty user-scoped read.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED real defect. Concrete end-to-end failure path:

SETUP (who has anon-bucket data): The slice docstring (src/store/customContentSlice.js:9-15) promises grandfathered local custom content is migrated to cloud once on first premium sign-in. Grandfathered items live under the UNSCOPED key 'sf_custom_content' because the original implementation (git 68463c9 / b76848c) wrote localWrite(content) unconditionally to LOCAL_KEY='sf_custom_content' for all tiers. In the current code scopedLocalKey('anon') returns that same unscoped LOCAL_KEY (line 27), while scopedLocalKey(uid) returns 'sf_custom_content:<uid>' (line 28). The UI gate (CompendiumPanel.jsx:746) prevents anon/free from ever reaching addCustomItem, so the uid-scoped bucket is provably empty until a user is already premium and signed in — nothing writes 'sf_custom_content:<uid>' before sign-in.

TRIGGER: User who created custom content under the old code (data sits in 'sf_custom_content'), then upgrades to premium and signs in. App.jsx:214-226 effect fires on the premium/elevated transition: migrateLocalCustomContentToCloud() then loadCustomContentFromCloud().

WHAT GOES WRONG (lines 245-253): ownerId = ownerIdFromState(get()) is the signed-in uid (canUseCustomContent() at line 244 gates the call, true only for premium/elevated, so auth.user.id is set). items = flattenLocalContent(ownerId) (line 249) reads scopedLocalKey(uid) = 'sf_custom_content:<uid>' — the EMPTY bucket. items.length===0, so it sets the per-uid flag (migrationFlag(uid)) to '1' (line 251) and returns. The real grandfathered items in 'sf_custom_content' are never read, never uploaded. The per-uid flag (line 247) makes the miss PERMANENT for that account on that device — a subsequent run early-returns.

DATA-LEVEL CONSEQUENCE: After the no-op migration, loadCustomContentFromCloud (App.jsx:220) overwrites state.customContent with the empty cloud result (slice line 223) and mirrors empty back to the uid-scoped bucket (line 228). The user's grandfathered custom institutions/resources/stressors/etc. silently disappear from their premium account and are never cloud-synced. (Not destructive on disk: the anon bucket is untouched and reappears via clearCloudCustomContent→loadAll('anon') on sign-out, but the premium-feature promise is broken and migration can never recover.)

PROOF OF REGRESSION: git -L on the function shows commit c65935c ('Campaign cloud persistence + offline-first sync') replaced the correct `customContentService.readLocalForMigration()` (which still reads the UNSCOPED key, src/lib/customContent.js:188-198) with `flattenLocalContent(ownerId)` and replaced the single global MIGRATED_FLAG with a per-uid migrationFlag. readLocalForMigration() now has ZERO callers (confirmed by grep), corroborating the orphaned seam. Reproduced the key divergence with a node one-liner: scopedLocalKey('anon')='sf_custom_content' (where data is) vs scopedLocalKey(uid)='sf_custom_content:<uid>' (what migration reads, empty).

Severity medium not high: it silently fails a premium feature and loses surfacing of user-authored data, but (a) the on-disk anon-bucket data is not destroyed, (b) it affects only the bounded population that held pre-gate local custom content and later upgraded, and (c) it does not corrupt the deterministic simulation, billing, or auth.
verdicts.fix_note: The straightforward fix is to migrate from the unscoped/anon bucket, restoring the pre-c65935c behavior: replace `flattenLocalContent(ownerId)` at line 249 with `flattenLocalContent('anon')` (equivalently call the existing `customContentService.readLocalForMigration()`, which reads the unscoped key and would also un-orphan that dead helper). Caveat: line 245 `ownerId` is still needed for the migration flag, so keep it — only the items source should change. A more robust variant migrates from BOTH buckets to also catch any uid-scoped local writes that occurred while offline-premium (e.g. items added via addCustomItem at line 140 before a cloud round-trip): items = [...flattenLocalContent('anon'), ...flattenLocalContent(ownerId)]. Either way, after a successful migration consider clearing/marking the anon bucket so a second premium account on the same device doesn't re-upload the same grandfathered items (the per-uid flag prevents re-upload for the SAME account, but a different uid on the same device would migrate them again — a minor duplication risk, not part of the reported bug). Also note the empty-bucket early-return still sets the flag (line 251); that is correct once the source bucket is fixed.

confirmed.title: Map undo/redo is dead: pushMapUndo is never called anywhere, so the AnnotateToolbar Undo/Redo buttons are permanent no-ops
confirmed.evidence: mapSlice defines pushMapUndo (line 361, 'snapshot-per-action'), mapUndo (373) and mapRedo (384). components/map/AnnotateToolbar.jsx:141-146 renders visible Undo/Redo buttons wired to mapUndo/mapRedo. But a repo-wide grep for pushMapUndo/mapUndoStack/mapRedoStack finds zero references outside store/mapSlice.js - no annotation, placement, or terrain action ever pushes a snapshot, so mapUndoStack is always empty, mapUndo() pops nothing and returns, and both toolbar buttons do nothing. Users who delete a label/marker/forest (AnnotateToolbar handleDelete) have no recovery despite the UI affordance promising one.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Call pushMapUndo(actionName) at the start of each mutating annotate/placement action (addLabel/updateLabel/deleteLabel, markers, forests, addPlacement/updatePlacement/removePlacementLocal), or wrap those actions to snapshot first; alternatively remove the Undo/Redo buttons until wired. Note the snapshot includes the multi-hundred-KB fmgSnapshot blob x30 entries - exclude fmgSnapshot from undo snapshots while fixing.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified the claim by reading mapSlice.js and AnnotateToolbar.jsx plus repo-wide greps. Findings: (1) pushMapUndo is defined exactly once at mapSlice.js:361; a repo-wide grep over all .js/.jsx/.ts/.tsx files (excluding node_modules) returns ZERO call sites — only the definition line. (2) mapUndoStack/mapRedoStack initialize empty (mapSlice.js:145-146) and nothing ever pushes onto mapUndoStack. (3) mapUndo (line 373) does `const entry = state.mapUndoStack.pop(); if (!entry) return;` so it always early-returns on an empty stack; mapRedo (384) is symmetric. (4) AnnotateToolbar.jsx:141-146 renders visible Undo/Redo buttons wired to mapUndo/mapRedo (lines 21-22), so both are permanent no-ops. (5) The mutating actions never snapshot first: HitLayer.jsx calls addLabel/addMarker/addForest (lines 58/62/73/88), AnnotateToolbar handleDelete calls deleteLabel/deleteMarker (27-28), PlacementDetailCard calls removePlacementLocal — all mutate mapState directly. (6) store/index.js has no middleware/subscription that auto-snapshots on mutation, ruling out an indirect feed. (7) No tests reference these actions. I tried to refute via: an alternate undo mechanism (TerrainToolbar has its own working undo via bridge.terrainUndo/terrainRedo — but that is FMG terrain history, entirely separate from the annotate mapUndo/mapRedo, so it does not cover the annotate buttons), an aliased/underscore import (none), store middleware (none), and tests asserting behavior (none). The claim holds. The reviewer's fmgSnapshot caveat is also correct: pushMapUndo deep-clones state.mapState (line 364) which includes the fmgSnapshot blob (line 90).
verdicts.fix_note: The reviewer's analysis and fix are accurate. One scoping clarification: TerrainToolbar.jsx has its OWN working Undo/Redo that delegates to FMG's history (bridge.terrainUndo/terrainRedo, lines 66-75), so the defect is strictly limited to the ANNOTATE-mode buttons backed by mapUndoStack — terrain editing undo is unaffected. When wiring pushMapUndo, also note mapUndo/mapRedo replace the WHOLE state.mapState with the snapshot (lines 381/392), which includes fmgSnapshot, placements, layers, and viewport — so an undo of a label delete would also revert any concurrent viewport/layer changes captured between snapshots; if that coarse granularity is undesirable, restrict the snapshot/restore to the annotation sub-trees (labels/markers/forests) rather than the entire mapState. And per the reviewer, exclude fmgSnapshot from the snapshot to avoid 30x deep-cloning a multi-hundred-KB blob.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end. The undo/redo machinery in src/store/mapSlice.js is wired correctly internally (pushMapUndo at :361 snapshots mapState onto mapUndoStack; mapUndo at :373 pops and restores; mapRedo at :384 is symmetric), but the producer is never invoked.

Evidence:
- Repo-wide grep for `pushMapUndo` across all of src/ and supabase/ returns exactly ONE hit: its own definition at mapSlice.js:361. Zero callers.
- `mapUndoStack`/`mapRedoStack` are referenced ONLY inside mapSlice.js (lines 145-146, 362-392). No external code, no middleware, no subscribeWithSelector listener pushes a snapshot. (The only useStore.subscribe in the map components is PlacementsLayer.jsx:22, which subscribes to viewport.scale for icon counter-scaling — unrelated.)
- The actual mutation sites never snapshot: HitLayer.jsx dispatches addLabel (:58), addMarker (:62), addForest (:73,:88); AnnotateToolbar.handleDelete (:24-29) dispatches deleteLabel + deleteMarker. The corresponding mapSlice actions (addLabel :253, addMarker :275, addForest :296, deleteLabel :270, deleteMarker :291, deleteForest :312) mutate mapState directly with no pushMapUndo call.

Concrete failure path: user enters ANNOTATE mode → adds/deletes a label/marker/forest (any of the above paths) → mapUndoStack remains [] because nothing ever pushed → user clicks the Undo button rendered at AnnotateToolbar.jsx:141 (wired to mapUndo at :21) → mapUndo executes state.mapUndoStack.pop() which returns undefined → guard `if (!entry) return;` (mapSlice:375) short-circuits → nothing happens. Redo (button :144 → mapRedo) is identically dead since mapRedoStack is only ever populated by mapUndo, which never runs meaningfully.

User-visible consequence: the Undo and Redo buttons (always rendered in the annotate toolbar) are permanent no-ops. The Delete button (AnnotateToolbar:124-138) destroys an annotation with no recovery path, despite the adjacent Undo affordance implying one exists. This is a broken feature / dead UI affordance — a real defect, not a style nit. No data-loss-beyond-session or determinism/billing impact, so not critical; it is a broken, user-facing feature, hence high-leaning-medium. I rate it medium: the affordance is misleading and delete is irreversible within a session, but it does not corrupt persisted data (undo stacks are intentionally non-persisted) or crash anything.
verdicts.fix_note:
The fix is to call pushMapUndo before each mutating annotation/placement action so a pre-mutation snapshot lands on the stack. Two viable approaches:

1) Call-site wrapping: in HitLayer.jsx call pushMapUndo('addLabel') immediately before addLabel (and likewise for addMarker/addForest), and in AnnotateToolbar.handleDelete call pushMapUndo('delete') before deleteLabel/deleteMarker. Also cover terrain/placement edits if undo is meant to span those.

2) Cleaner: fold the snapshot into the slice actions themselves (have addLabel/addMarker/addForest/deleteLabel/deleteMarker/deleteForest/updateLabel/etc. push the prior mapState before mutating, within the same set()). This guarantees coverage and avoids every caller having to remember.

Two correctness caveats for whoever implements it:
- mapUndo at mapSlice.js:373 snapshots the CURRENT mapState into the redo stack BEFORE restoring entry.snapshot. If you adopt approach (1) and snapshot AFTER the mutation, undo would restore the already-mutated state (no-op). The snapshot must be taken of the PRE-mutation state — i.e. pushMapUndo must run before the mutation. Since pushMapUndo itself reads state.mapState at call time, calling it as the first line of (or immediately before) the mutating action is required.
- The redo path in mapRedo (:384) and the cap/clear logic look correct, so no change needed there once a producer exists.

The claim's "AnnotateToolbar handleDelete" detail is accurate; note handleDelete only deletes labels/markers (not forests — there's no deleteForest in the toolbar), so undo coverage for forest deletion would need its own trigger wherever forests are deleted.

confirmed.title: Deleted campaigns resurrect from any other device's local cache: merge keeps locally-cached campaigns missing from remote and the backfill sync re-upserts them
confirmed.evidence: deleteCampaign on device A removes the row from cloud (lib/campaigns.js supabaseDelete). On device B, loadCampaigns (campaignSlice.js:393-421) loads the stale local cache containing the deleted campaign, primes signatures from remote only (primeCampaignSync(migratedRemote), line 404), merges with mergeCampaignLists (lib/campaignSync.js:45-93) whose stated policy keeps any local campaign not present remotely, then runs syncCampaignChanges(merged) as a 'cloud backfill' (line 411). getCampaignsNeedingSync (campaignSync.js:116-125) sees the deleted campaign's signature is absent from lastSyncedSignatures and upserts it - re-creating the cloud row under the same uuid (rowForCampaign sets row.id for uuid ids, campaigns.js:117). There is no tombstone/uploadedness tracking, so deletion can never propagate; every device that ever cached the campaign deterministically resurrects it.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Track deletions: keep a local tombstone list (id + deletedAt) per owner, filter tombstoned ids out of mergeCampaignLists, and only treat a local-only campaign as 'needs upload' if it carries a created-locally/never-synced marker rather than 'absent from remote'.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the full chain against the actual code; every link holds.

Deletion (campaignSlice.js:466-473 -> deletePersistedCampaignState -> deletePersistedCampaign at 122-130) removes the campaign from device A's local cache, calls forgetCampaignSync(id), and runs campaignService.delete(id) -> supabaseDelete (campaigns.js:149-152), which actually deletes the cloud row.

On device B, loadCampaigns (campaignSlice.js:393-421) does exactly what the claim states:
- cached = localLoad(ownerId) -> includes the stale, previously-cached campaign (campaigns persist in their own localStorage key sf_campaigns:<userId> via campaigns.js:37-39, independent of Zustand persist.partialize, so they survive reloads).
- migratedRemote = remote without the deleted campaign.
- primeCampaignSync(migratedRemote) (line 404) clears lastSyncedSignatures and seeds it from REMOTE ONLY (campaignSync.js:97-103), so the deleted campaign's id is absent from lastSyncedSignatures.
- merged = mergeCampaignLists(cached, migratedRemote) (line 405): mergeCampaignLists (campaignSync.js:45-93) adds every local campaign unconditionally via add(campaign,1) at line 77; a local-only campaign is kept unchanged (it only overlays authoritativeAccess when the id exists in remoteAccess). Confirmed by test "merge keeps local-only campaigns when remote campaigns load" (campaignSync.test.js:11-18).
- localWrite(merged) (line 410) re-persists the ghost to device B's cache.
- syncCampaignChanges(merged, ...) "cloud backfill" (line 411): getCampaignsNeedingSync (campaignSync.js:116-125) only skips non-active accessState; a normal campaign is 'active', changedId is null, and lastSyncedSignatures.get(id) is undefined !== its signature -> it "needs sync" -> service.upsert(campaign) -> supabaseUpsert (campaigns.js:130-141) with onConflict:'id' and rowForCampaign setting row.id for UUID ids (campaigns.js:107-118) -> re-creates the cloud row under the same UUID. Confirmed crypto.randomUUID() (campaignSlice.js:141-148) passes isUuid (campaigns.js:45-46). The exact local-only-upsert behavior is asserted by test "sync uploads only records that differ from the primed cloud snapshot" (campaignSync.test.js:54-80).

No tombstone, deletedAt, created-locally/never-synced marker, or remote-presence pruning exists anywhere in the campaign path (grep over src returned none in the sync code; the only "tombstone" hits are unrelated UI/data-manifest comments). The module header comment (campaignSync.js:4-7) intends to protect genuinely-unsynced local drafts, but the implementation cannot distinguish "never uploaded" from "uploaded then deleted remotely" — both present as "local-only, absent from remote" — so deletion can never durably propagate to any device that ever cached the campaign. Every refutation path (dead code, upstream guard, intentional-and-correct, API-differs) fails against the evidence.

Severity medium (not higher): it is a data-integrity/UX defect — deleted campaigns reappear — but not money/security/crash. It is scoped to multi-device authenticated users whose other device holds a stale cache; the deleting device and never-cached devices propagate correctly.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix (per-owner local tombstone list of {id, deletedAt}, filter tombstoned ids out of mergeCampaignLists, and only treat a local-only campaign as "needs upload" when it carries a created-locally/never-synced marker) is correct and addresses the root cause. Two practical refinements: (1) tombstones need a retention/expiry policy and should themselves be reconciled so they don't grow unbounded or block a legitimate same-id recreate; (2) a lighter-weight alternative that also closes the hole: have loadCampaigns drop local-only campaigns that carry a "previously synced" marker but are now absent from a successful full remote list (i.e., distinguish "synced before, gone now -> deleted remotely, drop it" from "never synced -> keep and upload"). The current code has no per-campaign synced-once flag, so either approach requires adding that bit of state.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via end-to-end trace and a read-only repro against the real modules.

Concrete failure path (same logged-in user, two devices):
1. Device A: deleteCampaign(id) (src/store/campaignSlice.js:466-473) hard-filters the campaign out of state.campaigns (line 470) and calls deletePersistedCampaignState -> deletePersistedCampaign (campaignSlice.js:122-130). That rewrites device A's owner-scoped local cache without the campaign, calls forgetCampaignSync(id) (only mutates device A's in-memory lastSyncedSignatures), and calls campaignService.delete(id) = supabaseDelete (campaigns.js:149-152), removing the cloud row. There is NO tombstone written anywhere (grep for tombstone/deletedAt/deleted_at/isDeleted/graveyard across src/lib and campaignSlice returned nothing).
2. Device B: on every app mount for a non-anon user, App.jsx:156-160 calls loadCampaigns(). loadCampaigns (campaignSlice.js:393-421) reads device B's stale owner-scoped local cache (still containing the deleted campaign), fetches remote (campaign now absent), and primes signatures ONLY from remote: primeCampaignSync(migratedRemote) (line 404) -> lastSyncedSignatures contains every remote id but NOT the deleted id (campaignSync.js:97-103).
3. mergeCampaignLists(cached, migratedRemote) (line 405; campaignSync.js:45-93) keeps the local-only campaign: a local id absent from remote is added with sourceRank 1 and never overwritten, so it survives the merge. mergeCampaignLists overlays remote access fields only for ids present in remoteAccess (lines 47-56, 83-86); the deleted id is not there, so it stays accessState:'active'.
4. syncCampaignChanges(merged) (line 411) -> getCampaignsNeedingSync (campaignSync.js:116-125): the deleted campaign is accessState 'active' (passes line 120) and lastSyncedSignatures.get(id) is undefined !== its signature (line 123), so it is flagged for upsert. service.upsert -> supabaseUpsert -> rowForCampaign sets row.id = campaign.id because the id is a uuid (campaigns.js:117), and upsert uses onConflict:'id' (campaigns.js:136). The cloud row is re-created under the same uuid.

My read-only node repro against src/lib/campaignSync.js confirmed: after primeCampaignSync(remote) + mergeCampaignLists(cached, remote), the deleted campaign is still in merged AND getCampaignsNeedingSync flags it for re-upsert.

Consequence: a deletion can never propagate to any device that ever cached the campaign. The resurrected row then re-populates the cloud, so the campaign re-appears on device A on its next load too. RLS does not block it (it is the owner's own row, supabaseUpsert sets user_id = user.id). This is a deterministic data-integrity defect (zombie/resurrected user data) on the auto-run main path, but it is not money/security/crash and a successful delete on a single-device user works, so high (not critical). Same-device delete-then-reload does NOT resurrect because device A's cache was rewritten without the campaign; the gap is strictly cross-cache/cross-device.
verdicts.fix_note:
The report's analysis is accurate. Notes on the fix:

- The right fix is durable, owner-scoped tombstones (e.g. a persisted set of deleted campaign ids with deletion timestamps) that: (a) deleteCampaign writes locally AND propagates to the cloud (a deletions table, or a soft-delete access_state='deleted' column on saved_maps so other devices observe the deletion via the existing access_state plumbing rather than via row absence), and (b) loadCampaigns consults during mergeCampaignLists to drop any local-only campaign whose id is tombstoned, and getCampaignsNeedingSync skips. Without cloud-visible deletion, device B has no way to distinguish 'never uploaded yet' (must keep — the module's stated policy, campaignSync.js:5-7) from 'deleted elsewhere' (must drop). The merge module is explicitly built to NOT erase local-only campaigns, so the resolution must come from a deletion signal, not from changing the merge default.

- Soft-delete via access_state is the lowest-risk option: the codebase already has access_state, inactive_reason, inactive_since, retention_expires_at columns and mergeCampaignLists already treats remote access fields as authoritative (campaignSync.js:47-56,83-86), and getCampaignsNeedingSync already skips non-active campaigns (line 120). A 'deleted' access_state would flow through existing plumbing: device B's remote fetch would carry the deleted state, the merge would overlay it, and the campaign would be filtered from the UI and skipped by sync — no resurrection, and deletion becomes observable cross-device. The current hard-delete (supabaseDelete) defeats this by removing the row entirely.

- Note the merge also needlessly re-upserts even legitimately-unchanged campaigns when local/remote bytes differ trivially (signature is full JSON.stringify); that is a separate efficiency concern, not the resurrection bug.

confirmed.title: loadCampaigns has no stale-owner guard after the awaited cloud list: a sign-out/sign-in completing mid-flight writes the previous user's campaigns into state and cache
confirmed.evidence: loadCampaigns captures ownerId = campaignCacheOwner(get()) at call time (line 394), then awaits campaignService.list() (line 401). When the promise resolves it unconditionally sets state.campaigns = merged (lines 406-409) and localWrite(merged, ownerId) (line 410) - never re-checking that the owner is still the same. If the user signs out (clearCampaigns resets state.campaigns) or another user signs in while list() is in flight, the late resolution overwrites the cleared/new state with the prior user's cloud campaigns and persists them under the captured owner key. The sibling slice proves this race is a known concern: loadCustomContentFromCloud re-checks `if (ownerIdFromState(get()) !== ownerId) return;` after its await (customContentSlice.js:221). campaignSlice lacks the equivalent guard on both the success and catch paths.
confirmed.suggested_fix: After the await, compare campaignCacheOwner(get()) to the captured ownerId and abort (no set, no localWrite, no backfill sync) when they differ, mirroring customContentSlice.js:221.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. loadCampaigns (src/store/campaignSlice.js:393-421) captures ownerId = campaignCacheOwner(get()) at line 394, awaits campaignService.list() at line 401, then in the .then unconditionally does set(state => { state.campaigns = merged; state.campaignsLoaded = true; }) (lines 406-409), localWrite(merged, ownerId) (line 410), and syncCampaignChanges(merged, ...) (line 411) — with NO re-check that the owner is still the same. The .catch (416-419) is likewise unguarded but only touches campaignsLoaded.

The race is reachable on real paths, not dead code: App.jsx:156-160 fires loadCampaigns() in a useEffect keyed on [authLoading, authTier, authUserId], so it re-runs on auth transitions. clearCampaigns (which resets state.campaigns=[]) is invoked on SIGNED_OUT via clearAuth (authSlice.js:96, 159) and on a user-switch (authSlice.js:162-165). campaignService.list() is a genuine async Supabase round-trip (supabaseList, src/lib/campaigns.js:121-128), so a prior user's list() can resolve after clearCampaigns or after a new user's auth lands — at which point the stale .then overwrites state.campaigns with the previous user's campaigns and runs a cloud backfill under whatever session is now active.

The sibling slice confirms this is a recognized concern handled elsewhere but missed here: loadCustomContentFromCloud has the exact guard `if (ownerIdFromState(get()) !== ownerId) return;` after its await (customContentSlice.js:221), and campaignCacheOwner (campaignSlice.js:78-80) is semantically identical to ownerIdFromState (customContentSlice.js:35-37). I found no in-flight token, sequence counter, abort, or owner re-check anywhere in loadCampaigns — the only early-return is the unrelated isConfigured check (line 400).

One nuance against over-rating severity: localWrite at line 410 uses the captured (correct prior) owner key, so the on-disk per-owner cache is keyed correctly; and RLS bounds the actual cloud write in syncCampaignChanges to the currently-authenticated user. The concrete observable defect is that state.campaigns (the live UI) gets repopulated with the previous user's campaigns after sign-out or during a user switch within the timing window — a cross-user data exposure in the session, not a guaranteed crash or a breach of the RLS security spine. Medium is the right severity: a real timing-window bug on an auth-related path, not main-path/critical.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct and directly portable. After `const remote = await/then`, re-check before mutating: in both the .then and .catch paths, add `if (campaignCacheOwner(get()) !== ownerId) return cached;` (or return merged without applying) before any set/localWrite/syncCampaignChanges, mirroring customContentSlice.js:221. Note the .catch currently only sets campaignsLoaded=true, which would also incorrectly flip the new/cleared owner's loaded flag on a stale rejection, so guarding the catch as well is worthwhile.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the code and tracing the path end to end.

FACTS VERIFIED:
- src/store/campaignSlice.js:394 captures `ownerId = campaignCacheOwner(get())` at call time; line 401 awaits `campaignService.list()`; the `.then` (402-415) unconditionally runs `primeCampaignSync(migratedRemote)` (404), `set(state => { state.campaigns = merged; state.campaignsLoaded = true; })` (406-409), and `localWrite(merged, ownerId)` (410) with the CAPTURED ownerId — never re-checking the current owner. The `.catch` (416-420) likewise sets `campaignsLoaded = true` without an owner check.
- The sibling proves the guard is a known requirement: src/store/customContentSlice.js:221 does `if (ownerIdFromState(get()) !== ownerId) return;` immediately after its await. campaignSlice has no equivalent.
- The race is reachable. clearCampaigns (campaignSlice.js:423-429) zeroes state.campaigns. It is invoked on sign-out via authSlice.clearAuth -> clearCampaigns (authSlice.js:96) and on a same-browser user switch via the onAuthChange SIGNED_IN handler when previousUserId !== user.id (authSlice.js:162-163). loadCampaigns is fired from an App.jsx effect keyed on authUserId/authTier (App.jsx:156-160), so an A->B switch can dispatch a fresh loadCampaigns while A's list() is still in flight, or A's in-flight list() can resolve after clearCampaigns has already run.
- src/lib/campaigns.js supabaseList() issues `from('saved_maps').select(...)` with NO user_id filter and NO getUser() re-check — it relies purely on RLS scoping at request-dispatch time (only supabaseUpsert re-derives the user via getUser, line 131). So a list() dispatched under A's JWT returns A's rows even though A is no longer the active session by the time it resolves.

CONCRETE FAILURE PATH:
1. User A is signed in; the App effect calls loadCampaigns(); ownerId='A' captured; list() dispatched with A's JWT.
2. A signs out (or B signs in same browser). clearAuth/clearCampaigns runs: state.campaigns=[], and clearCampaignSyncBookkeeping() -> primeCampaignSync([]) clears the signature map. Auth becomes anon or B.
3. A's list() resolves with A's rows. The unguarded .then re-primes the sync map with A's signatures (404), overwrites state.campaigns with A's merged campaigns (407), and writes them to A's scoped localStorage key (410, ownerId='A').

CONSEQUENCES (severity-bounding):
- IN-MEMORY LEAK (solid): state.campaigns now shows User A's campaigns while the active user is B (or while signed out). I demonstrated the merge that overwrites state via mergeCampaignLists against the real module — it yields A's campaign list. Any campaign UI reading from state would display A's private campaign names/contents to B.
- LOCAL CACHE: localWrite uses the captured ownerId='A', so it corrupts A's scoped cache key, not B's — the cross-user *cache* contamination is limited.
- CLOUD LEAK (partially mitigated, not fully): if B then mutates a campaign, persistCampaignState resolves owner via campaignCacheOwner(state)=B and would localWrite the contaminated state.campaigns to B's cache key and could sync. However syncCampaignChanges only pushes campaigns whose signature differs from lastSyncedSignatures, and the stale .then re-primed those signatures with A's campaigns — so A's contaminated entries match and are NOT re-upserted; only B's changed campaign syncs. So a direct push of A's rows into B's saved_maps under user_id=B is largely prevented by the priming, though A's data does land in B's local sf_campaigns cache on B's next mutation.

Net: a real determinism/data-correctness defect with a genuine cross-user information-disclosure surface (A's campaigns visible to B in-session, A's data written into B's local cache), but the worst case (A's rows persisted to B's cloud account) is mitigated by the sync-signature priming and the captured-ownerId localWrite. That puts it at medium, not high/critical. The sibling slice already guards against exactly this, so the omission is a clear, fixable seam, and there are zero tests touching loadCampaigns.
verdicts.fix_note: Mirror the sibling guard. In the .then, BEFORE primeCampaignSync/set/localWrite, add: `if (campaignCacheOwner(get()) !== ownerId) return cached;` (return cached or merged is fine since the result is largely ignored by the App.jsx caller). Placing it before primeCampaignSync matters: re-priming the sync-signature map with the previous owner's campaigns after clearCampaigns already cleared it is itself part of the corruption, so the early-return must precede line 404, not just the set on 406. In the .catch, only `set(state => { state.campaignsLoaded = true; })` if `campaignCacheOwner(get()) === ownerId` to avoid flipping loaded state for the wrong owner. Note campaignCacheOwner takes the whole state (get()), whereas the sibling uses ownerIdFromState(get()) — use campaignSlice's own campaignCacheOwner(get()) helper, not ownerIdFromState. A deeper hardening (out of scope for this guard) would be to also have supabaseList filter by/verify the current user_id rather than relying solely on RLS + request-time JWT, which would close the window where an in-flight query resolves under a swapped session.

confirmed.title: Optimistic local id vs cloud uuid race: update/delete issued before customContentService.add() resolves targets the temporary local id, failing the cloud op and resurrecting deleted items on next cloud load
confirmed.evidence: addCustomItem inserts an entry with a client id (makeId, line 130) and fires customContentService.add(); the cloud uuid only replaces the local id when the promise resolves (lines 144-151). In the window before that, deleteCustomItem (line 189) calls customContentService.delete(id) with the LOCAL id - supabaseDelete runs eq('id', '<inst_17...>') against a uuid PK column, which errors (invalid uuid input) and deletes nothing, while the insert completes server-side. The .then replacement then finds idx === -1 (item gone locally, line 147) and skips - leaving an orphan cloud row that resurrects the 'deleted' item on the next loadCustomContentFromCloud. updateCustomItem in the same window (line 174) likewise updates a row id that does not exist in the cloud, so the edit is silently lost server-side (surfaced only as customContentError).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Queue update/delete calls for items whose id is still local (e.g. track in-flight adds by localUid and chain the follow-up op on the add promise, using the returned cloud id), or perform delete/update server-side by the localUid stored in the jsonb body.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed real after reading the slice, the service, the migration, and the UI callers; could not refute it.

Mechanics verified:
- addCustomItem (src/store/customContentSlice.js:128-141) inserts an optimistic entry with a client id from makeId (line 130, e.g. `inst_1718...`). supabaseAdd (src/lib/customContent.js:74,76-81) STRIPS that id and inserts without it; the cloud PK is generated by Postgres (supabase/migrations/004_custom_content.sql:13: `id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()`). The cloud uuid replaces the local id only when the add promise resolves (customContentSlice.js:144-151). A real in-flight window exists.
- During that window, deleteCustomItem (line 189) calls customContentService.delete(id) -> supabaseDelete does .delete().eq('id', id) (customContent.js:105) with the LOCAL string id against a uuid PK column -> Postgres 22P02 invalid-uuid error -> thrown -> caught at customContentSlice.js:189-192, which only sets customContentError. Zero rows deleted; the already-fired insert survives as an orphan.
- updateCustomItem (line 174) likewise calls supabaseUpdate -> .update().eq('id', id) (customContent.js:96) -> same uuid error -> edit silently lost server-side.
- The add's .then replacement (lines 146-151) finds idx === -1 if the item was deleted locally and skips, so the orphan cloud row is never reconciled.
- loadCustomContentFromCloud (lines 213-236) does `state.customContent = merged` (line 222) with no reconciliation against local deletions, so the orphan row resurrects the 'deleted' item on next load.

Reachability confirmed: CompendiumPanel.jsx renders items from store state and passes item.id directly to deleteCustomItem (line 960) and handleEdit/updateCustomItem (lines 933, 761) with no in-flight disable/loading guard on the per-item buttons, so a user can act inside the sub-second window. The cloud branch is live for premium/elevated users (authSlice.js:364 canUseCustomContent; supabase isConfigured). 

Corroboration that the id-swap is a known hazard: the localUid system (customContentSlice.js:103-112; customRegistry.js:9-11) exists specifically because the row id is rewritten from local string to cloud uuid after add() resolves — but it only protects dependency references, not the CRUD targeting in this slice, which still keys on `id`. Could not find any debounce, optimistic-id guard, dedup-on-load, or test that mitigates the race.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound. Cleanest robust option: change supabaseUpdate/supabaseDelete (src/lib/customContent.js) to match on the stable jsonb body field rather than the PK — e.g. .eq('data->>localUid', localUid). localUid (customContentSlice.js:133) is set before the add fires, lives inside the jsonb data column, and is invariant across the cloud round-trip, so update/delete would target the correct row whether or not the add has resolved. This avoids having to track in-flight add promises. Alternatively (as proposed) chain the follow-up op on the add promise keyed by localUid. Either way the slice's update/delete should stop keying off the volatile `id`.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED real defect. Traced end-to-end against the actual modules.

THE RACE/ID MISMATCH IS REAL:
- src/store/customContentSlice.js:130 — addCustomItem stamps a LOCAL string id via makeId(category.slice(0,4)) -> e.g. "inst_1781354833558_88kp" (verified by running makeId; not a uuid).
- Line 143-152 — the cloud insert (customContentService.add) is async; the local id is only swapped for the cloud uuid inside the .then, after the round-trip resolves (lines 144-151). For the duration of one Supabase round-trip the store/UI carry the local id.
- The UI renders from store state keyed on item.id (CompendiumPanel.jsx:927-934) and wires delete to deleteCustomItem(activeCat, item.id) (line 960) and edit to updateCustomItem(activeCat, editingId=item.id, ...) (lines 761,771). So a delete/edit issued in the pre-resolve window passes the LOCAL id down.

THE DB-LEVEL FAILURE IS REAL:
- supabase/migrations/004_custom_content.sql:13 — id uuid PRIMARY KEY. 
- src/lib/customContent.js:104-107 supabaseDelete does .delete().eq('id', id); line 91-101 supabaseUpdate does .update(...).eq('id', id). PostgREST/Postgres filtering a uuid column by a non-uuid literal raises 22P02 "invalid input syntax for type uuid" (a 400, parsed BEFORE/independent of RLS), so both throw. Zero rows mutated. (if error) throw error => surfaces only as customContentError.

CONSEQUENCES (both verified):
1. delete-after-add: deleteCustomItem (slice:185) removes the item locally by local id; the cloud delete throws (no-op). The already-in-flight insert still completes server-side. When add's .then fires, findIndex(x => x.id === entry.id) === -1 (item gone, slice:147-148) so the cloud-id swap is skipped, leaving an ORPHAN cloud row with no local representation. loadCustomContentFromCloud (slice:218-228) replaces customContent wholesale from the cloud list with no dedup, so the "deleted" item RESURRECTS on next cloud load. Data-integrity / silent resurrection.
2. update-after-add: the cloud update throws (no-op) => edit lost server-side. Worse than the claim states: updateCustomItem keeps the same (local) id, so add's .then still finds the row and does customContent[category][idx] = saved (slice:149), where saved is built from the PRE-edit entry — clobbering the user's edit LOCALLY too. So the edit is lost both locally and in the cloud, surfaced only as customContentError.

LIKELIHOOD / SEVERITY CALIBRATION: The window is one network round-trip (~100-500ms). Hitting it requires a fast add->delete or add->edit on the same item, which is uncommon but plausible (fat-finger, "added the wrong thing, delete it"). Premium-only feature (canUseCustomContent gate, slice:143/170/188). No money/auth/crash; bounded blast radius (one custom catalog item per occurrence). Real determinism-adjacent data-integrity bug on a gated feature, edge-triggered => medium, not high.
verdicts.fix_note:
The claim's suggested framing is correct; concrete fixes:

(A) Best: stop using a throwaway local id as the cloud key. localUid already exists as a stable cross-cloud reference (slice:110-112, makeLocalUid; doc comment slice:102-109). Route cloud update/delete through localUid instead of the rewritten id: store localUid in a dedicated column (or query the data->>'localUid' jsonb path) so eq() never touches the uuid PK with a non-uuid. This also fixes the broader id-rewrite fragility the file's own comment warns about.

(B) Minimal/pragmatic: serialize per-item ops behind the add promise. Track an in-flight promise per local id (or per localUid); in updateCustomItem/deleteCustomItem, if the item's add is still pending, await it (or chain) so the cloud op runs with the resolved uuid. Equivalently, queue update/delete and replay after the .then reconciles the id.

(C) Guard rail (does NOT fully fix, but stops the orphan): in addCustomItem's .then, if findIndex === -1 (item was deleted locally during the window), the item was deleted — issue a compensating customContentService.delete(saved.id) with the now-known cloud uuid instead of silently skipping (slice:148). And in the .then, reconcile by localUid rather than id so a concurrent edit isn't clobbered (preserve local edits when replacing with saved, or re-apply the diff).

Note correction to the claim: the update-after-add case is slightly worse than described — the edit is also clobbered LOCALLY when add's .then overwrites [idx] with the pre-edit saved row (slice:149), not merely lost server-side. Any fix should preserve concurrent local edits during the id-swap.

confirmed.title: newCampaignId() fallback mints non-UUID ids that migrateCampaign immediately invalidates and rowForCampaign cannot upsert idempotently - campaign identity churns every load and each sync inserts a duplicate cloud row
confirmed.evidence: newCampaignId falls back to `camp_${Date.now()}_...` when crypto.randomUUID is unavailable (line 147 - e.g. non-secure-context deployments or older Safari). That id fails isUuid (line 150), so migrateCampaign re-mints it on EVERY localLoad (line 242) - and in the same environment the re-mint is again a non-UUID camp_ id, so the campaign's identity changes on every page load (activeCampaignId dangles, sync bookkeeping resets). Worse, lib/campaigns.js rowForCampaign only sets row.id when isUuid(campaign.id) (campaigns.js:117), so every syncCampaignChanges upsert for such a campaign omits the id and performs a fresh INSERT - one new saved_maps row per sync - and supabaseDelete(id) with the camp_ id errors on the uuid column so cleanup never works (campaigns.js:150).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the fallback generate an RFC-4122 v4 uuid from Math.random (so isUuid passes and the id is stable/upsertable), and have migrateCampaign persist re-minted ids immediately rather than only as a side effect of the next cloud-configured load.

verdicts.reasoning:
Every link in the reviewer's chain checks out against the actual code and schema:

1. newCampaignId() fallback (src/store/campaignSlice.js:147) returns `camp_${Date.now()}_...`, which fails the RFC-4122 isUuid regex (campaignSlice.js:150-152). Confirmed.

2. migrateCampaign re-mints any non-UUID id on every read: `if (!isUuid(next.id)) next.id = newCampaignId()` (campaignSlice.js:242), and localLoad runs `.map(migrateCampaign)` on every loadCampaigns (campaignSlice.js:83, 395). In a non-secure context crypto.randomUUID is unavailable, so the re-mint is itself another camp_ id — it never converges, so identity churns each load. Confirmed.

3. In the local-only (unconfigured) path, loadCampaigns returns the migrated list without writing it back (`return Promise.resolve(cached)` at line 400, no localWrite), so the re-minted id lives only in memory and the next plain load re-mints again. Confirmed.

4. lib/campaigns.js rowForCampaign sets row.id only when isUuid(campaign.id) (line 117). For a camp_ id, row.id is omitted, and supabaseUpsert does `.upsert(row, { onConflict: 'id' })` (line 136) — with no id this becomes an INSERT. saved_maps.id is `uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()` (migration 002_developer_role_maps_config.sql:12), so each sync mints a fresh row → duplicate cloud rows accumulate. Confirmed.

5. supabaseDelete does `.delete().eq('id', id)` against the uuid column (campaigns.js:150); a camp_ id is invalid uuid syntax → PostgREST errors → deletePersistedCampaign swallows it (campaignSlice.js:127-129), so cloud cleanup never works. Confirmed.

Strong corroboration that this is an unintended inconsistency rather than by-design: the sibling helper newSaveId() in src/components/SettlementsPanel.jsx:81-84 deliberately crafts a UUID-v4-shaped fallback (`00000000-0000-4000-8000-${...}`) so it passes the same isUuid gate and remains upsertable. newCampaignId() is the lone outlier that doesn't.

Severity is honestly low: crypto.randomUUID is available in every secure-context (HTTPS) deployment with a browser newer than ~early-2022, so the fallback never fires in normal production. The bug requires a non-secure-context (HTTP) deployment or an ancient browser; the cloud-duplication part additionally requires a signed-in premium user (campaigns are premium-gated at createCampaign, campaignSlice.js:433-434). It is a genuine data-integrity defect (orphaned, undeletable duplicate cloud rows + identity churn) when the environment hits it, but the trigger is an edge case. No upstream guard repairs non-UUID ids, and no test covers this path.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct and matches the existing precedent. Mirror src/components/SettlementsPanel.jsx:newSaveId() — emit an RFC-4122 v4-shaped id from Math.random in the fallback so it passes isUuid, stays stable across loads, and remains upsertable/deletable. Secondary improvement (also suggested): have migrateCampaign persist any re-minted ids immediately (localWrite) rather than relying on the next mutation or the configured-load backfill, so a re-mint converges in the local-only path too. With a UUID-shaped fallback the re-mint at line 242 would also stop firing, since the fallback id would itself pass isUuid.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end against the real code. Trigger: newCampaignId() (src/store/campaignSlice.js:141-148) falls back to `camp_${Date.now()}_${rand}` whenever globalThis.crypto?.randomUUID is unavailable — a real condition in non-secure contexts (plain-HTTP/LAN-IP/file:// — plausible for a self-hosted DM tool) and older Safari (<15.4). The codebase treats this as a live concern: 8 separate sites guard randomUUID with fallbacks. createCampaign mints the id directly (line 436), and campaigns are a premium/developer/admin feature (line 434) — exactly the signed-in users with cloud sync (isConfigured), so the affected population is paying users.

Cascade, each link verified: (1) The fallback id fails isUuid (src/store/campaignSlice.js:150-152; verified via the actual regex). (2) migrateCampaign re-mints on every load when !isUuid (line 242); in the same crypto-less environment the re-mint is again a different camp_ id (verified: re-mint != original), so campaign.id churns every page load — activeCampaignId dangles and lastSyncedSignatures (keyed by old id, campaignSync.js:101/123) never matches. (3) rowForCampaign only sets row.id when isUuid (src/lib/campaigns.js:117; verified id is omitted), so upsert(row,{onConflict:'id'}) against saved_maps.id (uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), supabase/migrations/002_developer_role_maps_config.sql:12) performs a fresh INSERT with a server UUID every single sync — orphaned duplicate saved_maps rows accumulate. (4) supabaseDelete does .eq('id', id) with the camp_ string against a uuid column (src/lib/campaigns.js:150) -> Postgres 22P02 invalid-input-syntax-for-uuid -> throws -> cleanup never works. (5) Compounding: after cloud reload campaignFromRow adopts row.id (a real uuid) for the server copy (campaigns.js:65) while local cache keeps the camp_-id copy; mergeCampaignLists keys by id (campaignSync.js:80) and keeps both -> visible duplicate campaigns.

Data-integrity defect: unbounded duplicate cloud rows + broken delete + identity churn for the paying-user feature. Not money-loss or a crash on the universal main path, and the trigger requires a non-secure-context/old-Safari deployment (not the default localhost/https dev path), so high rather than critical. Confidence ~0.85: a skeptical reviewer would agree the cascade is real; the only debate is how common a crypto-less deployment is in practice.
verdicts.fix_note: Fix newCampaignId's fallback to emit a real UUID, not a camp_ string, so isUuid passes everywhere downstream. crypto.getRandomValues is available in many contexts where randomUUID is not, so prefer a getRandomValues-based v4 generator, then a Math.random v4 as last resort, e.g.: function uuidV4(){ const b = (globalThis.crypto?.getRandomValues) ? globalThis.crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(16)) : Array.from({length:16},()=>Math.floor(Math.random()*256)); b[6]=(b[6]&0x0f)|0x40; b[8]=(b[8]&0x3f)|0x80; const h=[...b].map(x=>x.toString(16).padStart(2,'0')); return `${h.slice(0,4).join('')}-${h.slice(4,6).join('')}-${h.slice(6,8).join('')}-${h.slice(8,10).join('')}-${h.slice(10,16).join('')}`; }. This single change stops the re-mint churn (migrateCampaign keeps the id), restores idempotent upsert (rowForCampaign sets row.id), and fixes delete. Do NOT instead loosen isUuid to accept camp_ ids — the server column is uuid and would still 22P02 on insert/delete. Note migrateCampaign's re-mint at line 242 still legitimately fixes truly-legacy non-uuid ids; once the fallback yields real UUIDs the re-mint only fires for genuinely malformed historical data, which is the intended behavior. Also worth a targeted unit test asserting newCampaignId() output passes isUuid even with crypto.randomUUID stubbed out, to lock this in.

confirmed.title: resetMapState clears selectedBurgId and selectedAnnotationId but not selectedSettlementId/hoveredSettlementId - campaign deselect leaves a dangling settlement selection
confirmed.evidence: resetMapState (lines 354-358) resets mapState, selectedBurgId, and selectedAnnotationId. It does not touch selectedSettlementId - documented at line 129 as the 'primary key for detail panels' - or hoveredSettlementId. WorldMap's deselect flow (WorldMap.jsx:349-357) calls resetMapState() and clears placements, so after switching away from a campaign the detail panel key still points at a settlement from the previous campaign while the map shows nothing selected. replaceMapState (campaign load, line 337) similarly carries the previous campaign's selection across.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `state.selectedSettlementId = null; state.hoveredSettlementId = null;` to resetMapState (and consider doing the same in replaceMapState), unless a caller demonstrably relies on selection surviving a map reset.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim holds up under verification. Confirmed facts:

1. src/store/mapSlice.js:354-358 — resetMapState() resets mapState, selectedBurgId, and selectedAnnotationId, but does NOT touch selectedSettlementId (declared at :129 as "clicked settlement UUID — primary key for detail panels") or hoveredSettlementId (:135). replaceMapState (:337-351) likewise never touches either selection field.

2. Deselect flow confirmed dead-on: WorldMap.jsx handleSelectCampaign(null) (:349-358) calls setActiveCampaign(null) → resetMapState() → bridge.clearAllPlacements() → bumpGeometryVersion(). setActiveCampaign (campaignSlice.js:1244-1248) only mutates activeCampaignId. So nothing in the deselect chain clears the settlement/hover selection. selectedSettlementId is genuinely set during normal use: PlacementsLayer.jsx:167 calls setSelectedSettlement(it.settlementId) on placement click.

3. The dangling state is NOT dead/harmless. PlacementDetailCard.jsx resolves the selection against the GLOBAL savedSettlements list (line 31: saves.find(s => s.id === selectedSettlementId)), which is store-level and NOT campaign-scoped (settlementSlice.js:230 — persisted globally, never cleared on campaign switch). Its only gate is `if (!selectedSettlementId || !settlement) return null` (:43). So after deselecting a campaign, the stale id still resolves to a real save and the floating detail card renders over a map that shows nothing selected (placements were cleared, so PlacementsLayer won't highlight anything).

4. Additional downstream symptom the reviewer didn't mention but which strengthens the finding: SettlementsPanel.jsx:680-689 reads selectedSettlementId as a "pending focus" request and auto-opens the detail view for that settlement on the next Settlements-tab visit (then self-clears). A stale id therefore also causes an unexpected auto-open of a previous campaign's settlement.

No upstream guard clears the selection, and no test asserts the current behavior is intentional (grep for resetMapState/selectedSettlementId in test files returned nothing). All cited line numbers, API behaviors, and the deselect path match the actual code. I could not refute it.

Severity low is honest: no data loss, money, security, determinism, or crash impact; the worst case is a floating stale detail card or a one-time spurious auto-open of a settlement detail, both recoverable. The suggested fix (add state.selectedSettlementId = null; state.hoveredSettlementId = null; to resetMapState, and consider replaceMapState) is correct and minimal.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct. For replaceMapState (campaign load/switch, mapSlice.js:337), clearing both fields is also warranted, not just "consider" — loading a different campaign carries over a selection that may not be placed in the new campaign, producing the same floating-card glitch and the same SettlementsPanel auto-focus leak. Recommend adding `state.selectedSettlementId = null; state.hoveredSettlementId = null; state.selectedBurgId = null; state.selectedAnnotationId = null;` to BOTH resetMapState and replaceMapState so selection state is always tied to the currently-loaded map.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed via end-to-end trace. The selection fields selectedSettlementId and hoveredSettlementId are runtime-only (not in persist.partialize, src/store/index.js:63-74). They are set on a map click in PlacementsLayer.jsx:162-168 (onClick sets both selectedBurgId AND selectedSettlementId), and selectedSettlementId is a documented cross-panel intent channel (mapSlice.js:129).

resetMapState (mapSlice.js:354-358) clears mapState, selectedBurgId, selectedAnnotationId — but NOT selectedSettlementId or hoveredSettlementId. replaceMapState (mapSlice.js:337-351) clears none of the four selection fields. setActiveCampaign (campaignSlice.js:1244-1248) only touches activeCampaignId. There is no map-background-click handler that clears selection (MapOverlay.jsx has none), so the only clearers are PlacementDetailCard's close button and SettlementsPanel's consume-effect.

Concrete failure path:
1. Campaign A active on World Map; user clicks a placed settlement -> selectedBurgId + selectedSettlementId set; PlacementDetailCard renders.
2. User picks 'No campaign' in the dropdown (WorldMap.jsx:655 -> handleSelectCampaign(null)). Deselect branch (lines 349-358) runs setActiveCampaign(null) + resetMapState() + bridge.clearAllPlacements() + bumpGeometryVersion(). selectedBurgId cleared, but selectedSettlementId still points at Campaign A's settlement.
3. On-map consequence: PlacementDetailCard (PlacementDetailCard.jsx:29-43) resolves its settlement from account-wide savedSettlements, NOT from placements. The save still exists, so the detail card keeps rendering for a settlement that is no longer on the now-empty map — visible dangling card.
4. Sharper consequence: user navigates to the Settlements tab (onNavigate -> setView, App.jsx:513; navigation does not clear the field). SettlementsPanel reads pendingFocusId = selectedSettlementId (line 669) and its mount effect (lines 680-689) auto-opens that settlement's detail view even though the user never requested it this visit — a spurious replay of the map's 'Open' intent.

The Campaign A -> Campaign B direct-switch path goes through replaceMapState (not resetMapState), which clears none of the selection fields, so Campaign A's selectedSettlementId carries into Campaign B; because saves are account-wide, PlacementDetailCard will render Campaign A's settlement card over Campaign B's map even when that settlement isn't in Campaign B — a coherence/parity glitch on the map. Both claims in the evidence (resetMapState gap and replaceMapState carryover) are accurate.

No test covers resetMapState's effect on these fields. Severity is medium: it is a real broken-seam / stale-cross-panel-state bug producing a wrong-looking detail card and an unwanted auto-open on a normal navigation path, but it is recoverable (clicking the card's X, or selecting another settlement, fixes it) and causes no data loss, money, security, or determinism impact.
verdicts.fix_note: The reporter's implied fix (also clear selectedSettlementId and hoveredSettlementId in resetMapState) is correct but incomplete. Two adjustments: (1) Apply the same clearing in replaceMapState (mapSlice.js:337) — the campaign A->B direct switch path uses replaceMapState, and it currently carries ALL stale selection (selectedBurgId, selectedSettlementId, selectedAnnotationId, hoveredSettlementId) into the new campaign; burg/annotation ids from the old campaign also won't match the new placements, so replaceMapState should reset all four selection fields too, not just the settlement/hover pair. (2) Cleanest implementation: add `state.selectedSettlementId = null; state.hoveredSettlementId = null;` (and the existing `selectedBurgId`/`selectedAnnotationId = null`) to BOTH resetMapState and replaceMapState.
