With both behavior signals dead, the only surviving disjunct is `exportCount >= 3` (line 98, which correctly reads `campaignState.lastExportAt`). So multi-settlement campaign users who rely on neighbour networks or locks but haven't exported 3+ times are misclassified as 'intermediate' — precisely the audience the worldbuilder copy targets. The hook is live (used for audience-aware copy). The module's own doc comment (lines 12-15, 41-42) names neighbour usage and locks usage as intended signals, so this contradicts design intent rather than being deliberate.

Severity medium is honest: no crash on a main path, no data loss, no billing/auth/security exposure, no simulation-output or determinism break. The defect is degraded audience-targeted copy (a misclassification), matching the reviewer's scope. The corroborating same-pattern bug in LibraryToolbar.jsx:77 (`s.neighbourLinks` on a save) reinforces that this is a genuine path mistake.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct. Read neighbours via the same accessor everyone else uses: `(x.settlement?.neighbourNetwork || x.settlement?.neighbourLinks || []).length > 0` (mirror settlementFromSave + graph.js:263-266). Read locks as an object on campaignState: `Object.keys(x.campaignState?.locks || {}).length > 0`. Note there is a sibling instance of the same bug at src/components/library/LibraryToolbar.jsx:77 (`Array.isArray(s.neighbourLinks)` on a save envelope) — its hasNeighbours filter likely never matches either; worth folding the neighbour-presence check into one shared, tested helper used by both the hook and the toolbar.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via read + reproduction. Both worldbuilder behavior signals in src/hooks/useReaderAudience.js are permanently false.

NEIGHBOURS (line 101-104): the selector reads `x.neighbourLinks` at the SAVE ROOT. Grep confirms a top-level `save.neighbourLinks` is never written anywhere. Neighbour data is written exclusively to `save.settlement.neighbourNetwork` (SettlementsPanel.jsx:902,937,954; applyWorldPulse.js:349). Every robust consumer (graph.js:261-267 neighbourLinksFor, RelationshipEdges.jsx:69-73, deriveSystemState.js:226) reads `settlement.neighbourNetwork` first and treats `neighbourLinks` only as a legacy fallback alias on the nested settlement, never the save root. The canonical nesting helper across the codebase is `save?.settlement || save` (worldSnapshot.js:9), so the generated settlement and its neighbourNetwork live under `save.settlement`. The hook's `x.neighbourLinks` therefore always resolves undefined -> Array.isArray=false.

LOCKS (line 105-108): the selector does `Array.isArray(x.locks) && x.locks.length > 0`. locks is an OBJECT, not an array: initialized `locks: {}` (settlementSlice.js:477,1186; saves.js:100), written by key `state.locks[key]=value` (settlementSlice.js:1180-1182), persisted as `campaignState.locks` object (campaignSlice.js:185 cloneJson(state.locks||{})). The hook also reads the wrong path (save root, not save.campaignState.locks). The ONLY place in the codebase that treats locks as an array is this hook line 107; every other reference treats it as an object. Array.isArray(object) is always false, so even at the right path it would fail.

REPRODUCED with a node one-liner using realistic save shapes (5 saves, one with settlement.neighbourNetwork populated and campaignState.locks={economy:true,...}): hook hasUsedNeighbours=false, hasUsedLocks=false, while the actual-path checks both return true. With exportCount=1 (<3) the computeReaderAudience worldbuilder gate (line 61: savedCount>=5 && (nbr||locks||exp>=3)) is NOT taken; user falls through to 'intermediate'.

CONCRETE FAILURE PATH / IMPACT: A premium/free user who is a genuine worldbuilder by the file's own definition (multi-settlement arc: 5+ saves, linked neighbour network, locked sections) but who has exported <=3 PDFs is misclassified. Two real consequences: (1) FounderTile.jsx:58-62 gates `eligible` on `audience === 'worldbuilder'` -> the Founder lifetime checkout offer (startCheckout('founder_lifetime')) is never shown to exactly the target audience it was built for — a conversion/billing surface silently suppressed. (2) useCopy.js:39-44 never selects `${prefix}Worldbuilder` copy variants, so audience-aware UI text degrades to intermediate/new for these users.

NOT critical/high: the exportCount>=3 escape (line 61) still rescues frequent exporters, narrowing the blast radius to worldbuilders with 0-2 exports, and the failure is degraded UX/missed-offer, not data loss, crash, wrong simulation output, or determinism break. No test covers this hook (grep found zero), so the broken behavior is not asserted-as-intended. Medium with high confidence: predicates are provably always-false against the real save schema, and the downstream gate is real.

confirmed.title: LibraryToolbar 'has neighbours' filter chip checks the nonexistent save.neighbourLinks field — would always yield an empty list
confirmed.evidence: `if (filters.hasNeighbours) out = out.filter(s => Array.isArray(s.neighbourLinks) && s.neighbourLinks.length > 0);` — same dead field as useReaderAudience: saves carry neighbour data at `s.settlement.neighbourNetwork` (the rendering in SettlementsPanel.jsx:123-131 reads exactly that). Even once the parent's filtered-list bug (filteredSaves never rendered) is fixed, this chip will filter every save out. (Read one hop out from SettlementsPanel because applyLibraryFilters is exported from here and consumed there.)
confirmed.suggested_fix: Filter on `s.settlement?.neighbourNetwork?.length > 0` (optionally also `s.neighbour_links` from the Supabase row, which lib/saves.js:180 mirrors from neighbourNetwork).

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed real. LibraryToolbar.jsx:77 filters on `s.neighbourLinks` (bare top-level, no fallback), but the hydrated in-memory save envelope never carries that field. I traced the save shape: src/lib/saves.js supabaseList (lines 134-164) maps the DB row into an entry with `settlement: row.data` and DROPS the selected `neighbour_links` column entirely — no top-level `neighbourLinks` or `neighbour_links` is set on the entry. The localList path (251-258) and both migration helpers (migrateSaveToV2 91-109, migrateSettlementShape 121-124) likewise add no such field. So neighbour data exists only at `s.settlement.neighbourNetwork`, which is exactly what every other src/ consumer reads: the card rendering (SettlementsPanel.jsx:123-131, 215-216), relationshipGraph.js (104,117,127), roadNetwork.js (79,140), region/graph.js (263), deriveSystemState.js (226), etc. LibraryToolbar.jsx:77 is the sole outlier reading the dead field, so `filters.hasNeighbours` evaluates false for every save and yields an empty list. The same dead-field mistake exists in useReaderAudience.js:103, corroborating the bug class. The passing unit test (tests/components/libraryToolbar.test.js:17/23/29) gives false confidence because its fixtures hand-attach a top-level `neighbourLinks` that real hydration never produces. The reviewer's caveat is also accurate: the rendered list uses `unassignedSaves`/campaign folders, not `filteredSaves` (which feeds only `visibleCount={filteredSaves.length}`), so today's observable symptom is a wrong count badge — toggling the "Linked" chip shows "0 of N" even when settlements have neighbours — and the filter would break entirely once the list is wired to filteredSaves. Could not refute on any axis (path is reachable via the rendered toolbar, no upstream guard supplies the field, not intentional, API works as the reviewer describes).
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct: change line 77 to `out = out.filter(s => Array.isArray(s.settlement?.neighbourNetwork) && s.settlement.neighbourNetwork.length > 0);` To match other consumers' tolerance you could also fall back to `s.neighbourNetwork` (e.g. RelationshipEdges.jsx:69-72 reads both), but `s.settlement.neighbourNetwork` is the canonical, always-present location. Also note the unit test fixtures should be updated to put neighbour data under `settlement.neighbourNetwork`, otherwise the test will keep masking this regression. The identical dead-field read in useReaderAudience.js:103 should be fixed in the same pass.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED. The 'has neighbours' chip predicate at src/components/library/LibraryToolbar.jsx:77 is `s => Array.isArray(s.neighbourLinks) && s.neighbourLinks.length > 0`, reading a top-level `s.neighbourLinks` field that real save objects never carry.

Evidence of the real save shape:
- src/lib/saves.js supabaseList() (lines 134-163) hydrates each save with settlement at `s.settlement` and NO top-level `neighbourLinks`. The DB column `neighbour_links` (snake_case) is selected at line 131 but is never mapped into the returned object — only `data`, `config`, etc. are.
- localList() (line 258) likewise returns raw entries with no `neighbourLinks`.
- Neighbour data is written to the DB from `settlement.neighbourNetwork` (saves.js:59, 180, 201) and read everywhere else from `s.settlement.neighbourNetwork` — including the very same component tree: SettlementsPanel.jsx:123-131 renders neighbour badges from `s.settlement.neighbourNetwork`, and the delete-confirmation copy at 215-216 reads the same path. So a save that genuinely HAS neighbours exposes them at `s.settlement.neighbourNetwork`, and `s.neighbourLinks` is `undefined`.

Reproduced the failure with the exact predicate: over a real-shape save `{ settlement: { neighbourNetwork: [..1 link..] } }` the filter returns [] (should return that save); over the fabricated test-fixture shape `{ neighbourLinks: [..] }` it returns the save. The companion test tests/components/libraryToolbar.test.js:12-31 only passes because its fixtures invent a top-level `neighbourLinks` field that diverges from the production save envelope — false coverage that masks the bug.

This is the same dead-field class flagged in useReaderAudience.js:103 (`saves.some(x => Array.isArray(x.neighbourLinks) ...)`).

Severity nuance the claim gets right: SettlementsPanel.jsx computes `filteredSaves` via applyLibraryFilters (1026-1032) but renders the list from `saves`/`unassignedSaves` (1018, 1110-1158), using `filteredSaves` only for `visibleCount={filteredSaves.length}` (1072). So TODAY toggling the '🔗 Linked' chip does not blank the list — it only corrupts the count display to "0 of N · 1 filter" while every settlement (including linked ones) stays visible. The chip is simply broken/inert. Once the parent wires filteredSaves into the actual render (the evident intent of the whole toolbar), this predicate would filter out every save unconditionally. Rated medium: a real, demonstrable defect with wrong-output user impact, but currently bounded to a misleading count rather than data loss or a blank library, and gated behind a second unfinished seam (the toolbar's output isn't rendered yet).
verdicts.fix_note: Correct the predicate to read the canonical path used everywhere else in this file and in saves.js. Mirror the defensive fallbacks other readers use (e.g. RelationshipEdges.jsx:69-72, deriveSystemState.js:226): `out = out.filter(s => { const net = s.settlement?.neighbourNetwork || s.neighbourNetwork || []; return Array.isArray(net) && net.length > 0; });`. Two related items the reviewer should fix in the same pass so the bug doesn't reappear: (1) tests/components/libraryToolbar.test.js fixtures must be rebuilt to the real save envelope (neighbour data at `settlement.neighbourNetwork`), otherwise the test will keep passing against a shape that never occurs and re-mask the regression; (2) useReaderAudience.js:103 has the identical `x.neighbourLinks` dead-field check and should be corrected to `x.settlement?.neighbourNetwork` for the same reason. Note: the underlying toolbar seam (filteredSaves computed but never rendered in SettlementsPanel.jsx — the list maps over `saves`/`unassignedSaves`, not `filteredSaves`) is a separate, larger defect; fixing this predicate alone will not make the chip do anything visible until that wiring is completed.

confirmed.title: ConfigurationPanel display-only coercion of blocked tier/route combos lies about what will generate
confirmed.evidence: When `blockTownPlus` (noMagic && isolated) the Population select displays 'village' (`value={blockTownPlus && isTownPlus ? 'village' : config.settType}`, line 334) and the Trade Route select displays 'road' (line 357) — but neither writes the coerced value back to config; the onChange handlers only block re-selecting the hidden options. The blocked state is reachable: SettlementsPanel.onLoad → migrateConfig loads old saves verbatim, and migrateConfig (SettlementsPanel.jsx:32-38) infers `magicExists = priorityMagic > 0`, so a legacy save with priorityMagic 0 + settType 'town' + tradeRouteAccess 'isolated' lands in exactly this state. Generate then runs with the hidden town+isolated+no-magic config while the UI shows village/road — the 'impossible' combination the panel exists to prevent.
confirmed.suggested_fix: On detecting the blocked combo (e.g. in an effect or in updateConfig/migrateConfig), actually write the coercion into config (settType→'village' or tradeRouteAccess→'road') instead of only displaying it.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim's central consequence is refuted by the generation pipeline. The reviewer correctly observes that ConfigurationPanel.jsx:334 and :357 only DISPLAY 'village'/'road' for the blocked combo without writing the coercion back to config (updateConfig in store/configSlice.js:55-58 is a plain Object.assign with no coercion), and that the blocked state is reachable via SettlementsPanel onLoad -> migrateConfig (line 35 sets magicExists = priorityMagic>0, so a legacy save with priorityMagic:0 + settType:'town' + tradeRouteAccess:'isolated' lands in the blocked state). All of that UI-layer mechanics is accurate.

But the asserted impact -- "Generate then runs with the hidden town+isolated+no-magic config... the 'impossible' combination the panel exists to prevent" -- is FALSE. The deterministic engine independently enforces the same constraint at generation time. In src/generators/steps/resolveConfig.js (the first registered pipeline step, steps/index.js:8), line 86 computes noMagic = config.magicExists === false || basePriorityMagic === 0 and line 87 townPlus = TOWN_PLUS_TIERS.includes(tier). Line 119 then rewrites: const tradeRoute = (rawRoute === 'isolated' && townPlus && noMagic) ? 'road' : rawRoute. This rewrite is stored into effectiveConfig.tradeRouteAccess (line 153) and exposed as ctx.tradeRoute, which all downstream steps consume. Lines 222-239 even add a dedicated 'overridden' causal trace specifically for the EXPLICIT tradeRouteAccess:'isolated' case (no random pool) -- precisely the legacy-save scenario the reviewer describes.

Consequently isolationPass (steps/index.js:15, isolationPass.js:32 reads ctx.tradeRoute) calls applyTeleportationInfrastructure, which guards `if (!TOWN_PLUS_TIERS.includes(tier) || tradeRoute !== 'isolated') return` (isolationGenerator.js:20). Since the route was already rewritten to 'road', it returns early -- no forced teleportation circle, no incoherent no-magic+arcane-infrastructure output. The settlement generates as a coherent road-access town. The legacy generateSettlement.js no longer exists (strangler-fig migration completed for this path), so the pipeline is the sole active generation path.

Therefore the panel's display-only coercion is a cosmetic mirror of a hard backend guard, not the only thing preventing the impossible combo. There is a minor cosmetic dissonance (the dropdown shows 'village'/'road' while config still holds 'town'/'isolated' from the save until the user touches anything), but the actual generated settlement is correct and coherent. No simulation/data-integrity defect occurs. This is not a real bug at the severity claimed; at most it is a UI-state hygiene nit, and the reviewer's stated harm does not occur.
verdicts.fix_note: No fix needed for the claimed harm. If the cosmetic config/display dissonance is bothersome, the cleanest place to normalize is migrateConfig (SettlementsPanel.jsx:32) -- write settType/tradeRouteAccess to the coerced values on load -- but this is optional polish, not a correctness fix, since resolveConfig.js:119 already guarantees coherent generation.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED with a reproduction against the real modules. The claim is correct in substance but I refined exactly which axis diverges.

Reachability (verified): SettlementsPanel.onLoad (SettlementsPanel.jsx:519-520) loads a save's raw config via updateConfig(migrateConfig(rawConfig)). migrateConfig (SettlementsPanel.jsx:32-38) only sets magicExists and nearbyResourcesState — it infers magicExists = (priorityMagic ?? 50) > 0, so a legacy save with priorityMagic:0 and no magicExists field yields magicExists:false. updateConfig (configSlice.js:55-58) is a plain shallow merge with no coercion. A legacy save {settType:'town', tradeRouteAccess:'isolated', priorityMagic:0} therefore lands verbatim in the blocked state.

UI display coercion (ConfigurationPanel.jsx:306-312, 334, 357): blockTownPlus = noMagic && isIsolated is true. The Population select shows value='village' (line 334) and the Trade Route select shows value='road' (line 357). Neither onChange writes the coerced value back to config — they only block re-selecting hidden options (lines 338, 360). So config.settType stays 'town', config.tradeRouteAccess stays 'isolated'.

Generation (settlementSlice.js:503-558): generateSettlement reads config from the store verbatim (line 505, 531-542) and passes it to generateSettlementPipeline. The only pre-flight check is the user-tier gate (line 510-515: isTierAllowed), which is unrelated to the magic/isolation constraint. For any user allowed to generate towns (premium/elevated, or free if town is within maxAllowedTier — authSlice.js:382-386), the engine runs.

What the engine actually does (the key refinement): resolveConfig.js:119 coerces ONLY the route: `const tradeRoute = (rawRoute === 'isolated' && townPlus && noMagic) ? 'road' : rawRoute;`. It does NOT coerce the tier — tier stays 'town' (resolveConfig.js:76-78). I reproduced this with a node one-liner replicating migrateConfig + the resolveConfig branches:
  ENGINE resolves => tier: town | tradeRoute: road
  UI displays    => Population: village | TradeRoute: road
So the route axis is CONSISTENT between UI and engine (both -> road), but the TIER axis DIVERGES: UI shows 'village', engine generates 'town'.

Consequence: A user loads a legacy save, the panel shows a Village (401-900) config, clicks Generate, and receives a Town (901-5,000) settlement — town-tier population, institutions, economy, the whole dossier — contradicting the 'Village' shown in the panel. The dossier/PDF are internally coherent (town + road + no-magic is a valid combination the engine fully supports), so this is not data corruption or a crash; it is a display-vs-output lie in the one panel whose explicit job is to prevent impossible combinations, and it silently substitutes a different population tier than the user was shown. Note the route half of the original claim is technically a non-bug (UI 'road' matches engine 'road'); the real divergence is the population/tier half.

Not higher than medium: requires a specific legacy save shape (priorityMagic:0, town+, isolated, no magicExists field), output is coherent, no money/security/data-loss, no crash. But it is a genuine UI/engine parity defect with wrong user-visible tier, so not low and not not-a-bug.
verdicts.fix_note:
The original fix framing ("write the coerced value back to config") is directionally right but must respect the engine's actual asymmetry, or it will create a NEW lie. The engine coerces route -> 'road' (resolveConfig.js:119) but keeps the tier as 'town'. So:

1) Route axis: UI 'road' already matches engine output. Writing tradeRouteAccess:'road' back to config on entering the blocked state would make persistence/regeneration truthful and is harmless (matches the engine). Safe.

2) Tier axis: This is the actual bug, and the naive fix is WRONG. Coercing the displayed value back to settType:'village' (to match line 334's display) would make the engine ALSO generate a village — but that silently downgrades the user's saved town to a village, which is a different, arguably worse, surprise (and changes population, institutions, economy from what the saved settlement was). The truthful fix is the opposite: the population select should display the REAL tier (config.settType, i.e. 'town'), NOT 'village' — remove the `blockTownPlus && isTownPlus ? 'village'` coercion at line 334. Then surface that the no-magic+isolated constraint will be auto-resolved by dropping isolation to a road (which is exactly what the engine does), e.g. via the existing warning at line 351-353. That makes the panel match the engine: town + road, no magic.

Best fix: align the panel to the engine's resolveConfig.js:119 semantics — display the true settType, write tradeRouteAccess back to 'road' on entering the blocked state, and drop the spurious settType->'village' display coercion at line 334. Optionally, also harden migrateConfig (or a one-time load normalization) to coerce isolated->road for no-magic town+ saves so the stored config can never re-enter the divergent state. Do NOT simply write 'village' back, as that changes simulation output away from both the engine's behavior and the saved settlement.

confirmed.title: Mobile bottom nav silently evicts 'About' for signed-in users (and Gallery too under mobileSingleChrome) after Gallery was added to NAV
confirmed.evidence: The bottom-nav comment (lines 289-294) says it holds the 5 primary destinations including 'How To Use'. NAV now has 6 entries (gallery inserted before howto, lines 62-69). visibleNav hides 'map' for anon (5 items, all render), but for signed-in users visibleNav has 6 items and `visibleNav.slice(0, _readFlag('mobileSingleChrome') ? 4 : 5)` (line 578) drops 'howto' entirely — and with the flag on, drops 'gallery' too. Signed-in mobile users have no path to the About page (and no other mobile surface links it; HERO_LINKS is empty).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Pick the 5 slots intentionally (e.g. filter by an explicit mobile-priority list) instead of slicing the desktop NAV order, or move About into the account menu on mobile and document the eviction.

verdicts.reasoning: I verified the cited code and could not refute the core claim; the mechanism is exactly as described. NAV (App.jsx:62-69) has 6 entries with `howto` (About) last. `visibleNav` (App.jsx:266-269) removes only `map` and only for anon. The mobile bottom nav at App.jsx:578 renders `visibleNav.slice(0, _readFlag('mobileSingleChrome') ? 4 : 5)`. `mobileSingleChrome` defaults to false (flags.js:111-114), so the live default path is `slice(0, 5)`: - Anon: visibleNav = [generate, settlements, compendium, gallery, howto] (5 items, map removed) → slice(0,5) keeps all 5, including About. OK. - Signed-in (free/premium): visibleNav = [generate, settlements, map, compendium, gallery, howto] (6 items) → slice(0,5) = [generate, settlements, map, compendium, gallery], dropping `howto`/About. About is evicted from the bottom nav. Desktop nav (App.jsx:422) renders the full `visibleNav` with no slice, so desktop signed-in users keep About — confirming the eviction is mobile-only and a side effect of the Gallery insertion, not intentional. The design comment at App.jsx:290-291 still lists the intended 5 primary destinations as "Create / Settlements / Map / Compendium / How To Use" — but the code now produces "...Compendium / Gallery" for signed-in users, so the documented intent no longer matches behavior. With the flag ON (slice(0,4)), even anon loses About and signed-in loses both Gallery and About, exactly as claimed. One correction to the evidence: the claim that signed-in mobile users have "no path to the About page" is slightly overstated. AccountPage.jsx:569 contains a deep link `FAQ on the About page`, reachable via the account chip → Account page. So About is not completely unreachable on mobile — but it has no primary navigation affordance for signed-in users, so the defect (silent eviction of a primary destination) stands. This is a real navigation/discoverability defect with documented-intent drift introduced by the Gallery addition. Severity low: no data loss, money, security, or crash; a feature is harder to reach on one form factor. Confidence high.
verdicts.fix_note: The fix direction is sound. One factual nuance to capture: About is not entirely unreachable on mobile for signed-in users — AccountPage.jsx:569 has a deep link to /how-to?tab=faq reachable via the account chip → Account page FAQ section. So this is "no primary nav affordance" rather than "no path at all," which is why severity stays low. Preferred fix: drive the bottom nav from an explicit mobile-priority list rather than slicing desktop NAV order, so adding/reordering NAV entries can't silently evict a destination again (this is the recurring seam). Also update the stale comment at App.jsx:290-291, which still names "How To Use" as one of the 5 primary destinations that the slice now drops for signed-in users.

verdicts.reasoning: CONFIRMED. Traced end-to-end against the real modules. NAV (src/App.jsx:62-69) has 6 entries in order: generate, settlements, map, compendium, gallery, howto (label "About"). Gallery was inserted as the 5th entry, before howto. visibleNav (App.jsx:266-269) removes ONLY the 'map' item, and only for anon. The mobile bottom nav (App.jsx:578) renders `visibleNav.slice(0, _readFlag('mobileSingleChrome') ? 4 : 5)` — a cap written when NAV had 5 destinations (see the now-stale comment at App.jsx:289-294 still listing 5 items "Create / Settlements / Map / Compendium / How To Use"). I reproduced the slice math against the real filter logic for every tier x flag combination: - anon, flag=false (irrelevant since signed-in is the bug): map filtered → 5 items → all render. This is why the bug hides at first glance — the comment's "5 items" holds only for anon. - free/premium, flag=false (the PROD DEFAULT, flags.js:111-114 mobileSingleChrome default=false): visibleNav = [generate, settlements, map, compendium, gallery, howto] (6 items); slice(0,5) drops 'howto'/About. - free/premium, flag=true: slice(0,4) + the auth chip (App.jsx:606-628); DROPS both 'gallery' AND 'howto'. So on the shipping default, every signed-in mobile user's bottom nav silently omits the About page. The bottom nav is the primary mobile nav surface: the mobile top header (App.jsx:304-337) renders only the brand button + AccountMenu (no nav links), and the "mobile hero links" strip (App.jsx:343) is gated on HERO_LINKS.length > 0 while HERO_LINKS = [] (App.jsx:76) — its comment at 340-342 is stale and it renders nothing. The footer (App.jsx:540-566) has only copyright + a Support mailto. No test covers the slice cap, so the drift was unguarded. The 'howto' route is real and reachable (routes.js:37, /how-to → title "About"; App.jsx:516 renders ). User-visible consequence: signed-in mobile users lose the navigation affordance to the About page (which hosts the how-to guide, the Compare section per App.jsx:184-185, and the FAQ). It is a real, on-default UX regression caused by an off-by-one cap that didn't grow when NAV grew. Severity is medium (not high): it degrades an existing feature for a real population on the prod default, but it is not a crash, data-loss, billing/auth, simulation-correctness, or determinism issue, and a single indirect path to About survives. Correction to the claim's absoluteness: it is NOT strictly true that signed-in mobile users have "no path to the About page." AccountPage.jsx:569 contains an inline link `FAQ on the About page` inside the "Frequently asked" section, reachable by tapping the account chip → scrolling to that section. That is an obscure, non-nav escape hatch, so the practical conclusion (About is effectively unreachable as a nav destination on mobile for signed-in users) still stands.
verdicts.fix_note: The right fix is to grow the cap to match NAV's new length, not a fixed magic number that will drift again. Concretely, App.jsx:578 should show all of visibleNav: in the flag-off branch raise 5→6 (room for all 6 items, no auth chip in the bottom nav); in the flag-on branch the auth chip is a separate appended button (App.jsx:606-628), so to surface all nav items the nav slice should be 6 as well (4→6), yielding 6 nav buttons + 1 auth = 7 buttons. Caveat: 7 equal-flex buttons on a ~380px viewport (~54px each) is cramped and may need an icon-only/condensed treatment or an overflow affordance — verify the layout, don't just bump the number. Cleanest is to derive the cap from visibleNav.length (drop the slice entirely when flag is off; slice to visibleNav.length when on) so future NAV additions can't silently truncate again. Also: (1) update the stale comment at App.jsx:289-294 which still claims 5 destinations and omits Gallery; (2) remove or wire up the dead "mobile hero links" strip (App.jsx:340-343) whose comment promises Pricing/Gallery/Compare but renders nothing because HERO_LINKS = []; (3) add a regression test asserting the bottom nav exposes every visibleNav entry for a signed-in tier under both flag states.

confirmed.title: Systemic stripped-glyph copy artifacts across core components: ', ' placeholder fallbacks, missing separators, empty icon strings
confirmed.evidence: A bulk character-stripping pass left visible artifacts on user-facing strings: PowerTab section title `Power Distribution${pf.length} Factions` renders 'Power Distribution4 Factions' (PowerTab.jsx:153); EconomicsTab `Shadow Economy${bmc}% capture` renders 'Shadow Economy12% capture' (EconomicsTab.jsx:514); DefenseTab 'Criminal Structure{csd.label}' concatenates with no separator (DefenseTab.jsx:215); OverviewTab StatusTag renders the literal string ', ' as the missing-value placeholder (`value || ', '`, OverviewTab.jsx:38, also SummaryTab.jsx:204-205 and DailyLifeTab AnchorFact:25); ConfigurationPanel archetype dropdown shows '<option>, Choose an archetype' (ConfigurationPanel.jsx:81); GenerateWizard banners join headline and body with a bare `{', '}` (GenerateWizard.jsx:543, 706); HistoryTab TENSION_META and DefenseTab STRESS_STATUS icons are all empty strings producing stray gaps. Individually cosmetic, but they appear on the dossier (the flagship surface) and read as data corruption.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Sweep for the artifacts of the strip pass: replace `|| ', '` fallbacks with an em dash or 'Unknown', restore separators in template-literal titles ('Power Distribution · N Factions'), and remove empty icon slots or substitute lucide icons.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. I read every cited location and confirmed the artifacts, then corroborated the root cause via git history.

Confirmed artifacts (all on the on-screen dossier / config UI):
- src/components/new/tabs/PowerTab.jsx:153 `title={`Power Distribution${pf.length} Factions`}` renders "Power Distribution4 Factions" with no separator.
- src/components/new/tabs/EconomicsTab.jsx:514 `title={`Shadow Economy${bmc}% capture`}` renders "Shadow Economy12% capture".
- src/components/new/tabs/DefenseTab.jsx:215 `Criminal Structure{csd.label}` concatenates with no separator.
- src/components/new/tabs/OverviewTab.jsx:38 StatusTag `{value || ', '}` renders a literal ", " when value is falsy; line 122 even hard-codes `value={... : ', '}` for the unknown-viability case.
- src/components/new/SummaryTab.jsx:204-205 `eco.prosperity||', '` and `dp.readiness?.label||', '`.
- src/components/new/tabs/DailyLifeTab.jsx:25 AnchorFact `{value || ', '}`.
- src/components/ConfigurationPanel.jsx:81 `<option value="">, Choose an archetype</option>`.
- src/components/GenerateWizard.jsx:542 and :706 join headline and body with a bare `{', '}`.
- src/components/new/tabs/HistoryTab.jsx:42-51 TENSION_META `icon:''` are all empty strings, rendered at line 197 inside a `display:flex; gap:8` row — an empty `<span>` is still a flex item, so it produces a stray ~8px gap before each tension label.

Root-cause corroboration (decisive): git commit 62c5e8d "Restore em-dash/ellipsis typography stripped by the reformat" documents a prior bulk de-em-dash pass (its message: "em-dash x4206, ellipsis x147, en-dash x95"). The original commit e608542 shows the pre-strip text: `Power Distribution — ${pf.length} Factions`, `Shadow Economy — ${bmc}% capture`, `Criminal Structure — {csd.label}`, `<option>— Choose an archetype —</option>`, and `{' — '}{currentStepDef.hint}`. The restoration commit claimed to be lossless but HEAD still shows these with NO separator (em-dash plus surrounding spaces collapsed entirely, and in some cases replaced by a comma) — so the restoration was incomplete and these specific instances slipped through. This proves they are genuine stripped-glyph artifacts, not intentional styling.

One sub-claim is inaccurate: "DefenseTab STRESS_STATUS icons are all empty strings producing stray gaps." The STRESS_STATUS objects (DefenseTab.jsx:79-84) do contain empty/orphaned icon fields, but the render block at DefenseTab.jsx:135-142 never references `stressStatus.icon`, so it is dead data and produces no visible gap. This is a minor error in one secondary detail and does not undermine the overarching, well-evidenced finding.

Severity is honestly low: purely cosmetic. No data loss, no money/security impact, no determinism break, no wrong simulation output — but it is on the flagship dossier surface and reads as data corruption to users.
verdicts.fix_note: The cited file paths are slightly off: SummaryTab/ConfigurationPanel/GenerateWizard live in src/components/ (and src/components/new/SummaryTab.jsx), not src/components/new/tabs/. Line numbers match. The correct restoration target per git is the original em-dash form, e.g. `Power Distribution — ${pf.length} Factions`, `Shadow Economy — ${bmc}% capture`, `Criminal Structure — {csd.label}`, `<option>— Choose an archetype —</option>`, `{' — '}{currentStepDef.hint}`. For the `|| ', '` fallbacks (OverviewTab:38/122, SummaryTab:204-205, DailyLifeTab:25) use `|| '—'`. Drop the "STRESS_STATUS icons produce stray gaps" item — that icon field is never rendered. For HistoryTab TENSION_META the empty icon span does produce a real gap; either remove the `<span>` when icon is empty or restore icons. A repo-wide regex sweep for `'—'`/`' — '` losses (cross-referencing git e608542) would catch any remaining un-restored instances beyond the ten cited.

verdicts.reasoning: CONFIRMED via byte-level git archaeology. A bulk em-dash/typography strip pass re-damaged user-facing strings that had previously been restored, and the damage is live in HEAD. Timeline proven with `git show : | python3 repr`: - b76848c (pre-reformat): `Power Distribution - ${pf.length} Factions` (ASCII hyphen, with spaces) - 62c5e8d (Jun 4, commit literally titled "Restore em-dash/ellipsis typography stripped by the reformat"): restored to em-dash form, e.g. `Power Distribution — ${pf.length}`, `{value || '—'}`, `— Choose an archetype —`, `{' — '}` - 38e339f (Jun 5, "Checkpoint: relationship-memory sim ...") RE-STRIPPED: em-dash + surrounding spaces deleted entirely in some spots, replaced with `, ` (comma-space) in others - HEAD (df8babb): still broken. `git merge-base --is-ancestor` confirms both restore and re-strip are ancestors of HEAD and the re-strip is a descendant of the restore. Every cited line confirmed against HEAD working tree with fixed-string grep: - PowerTab.jsx:153 `Power Distribution${pf.length} Factions` -> renders "Power Distribution4 Factions" (Section/Primitives.jsx:79 renders {title} verbatim) - EconomicsTab.jsx:514 `Shadow Economy${bmc}% capture` - DefenseTab.jsx:215 `Criminal Structure{csd.label}` (no separator) - OverviewTab.jsx:38 `{value || ', '}` (restore had `'—'`); plus call-site OverviewTab.jsx:122 `via.viable===... : ', '` so the Viability tile literally renders ", " when viability is indeterminate - SummaryTab.jsx:204-205 `eco.prosperity||', '`, `dp.readiness?.label||', '` - DailyLifeTab.jsx:25 `{value || ', '}` - ConfigurationPanel.jsx:81 `` (restore had `— Choose an archetype —`) - GenerateWizard.jsx:542 & 706 `{', '}` joining headline+body (restore had `{' — '}`) - HistoryTab.jsx TENSION_META (lines 42-51) all `icon:''`, rendered at line 197 `{tm.icon}` => empty span / stray gap - DefenseTab.jsx STRESS_STATUS (lines 79-84) icons empty or orphaned U+FE0F variation selectors with no base glyph; rendered via stressStatus block at lines 135-140 Render-path proof: OutputContainer.jsx (the dossier renderer, the project's flagship surface) lazy-imports and renders all cited tabs (lines 47-58). So these artifacts appear on the live on-screen dossier. No test asserts any of these strings (grep over test/spec files = none), so `npm run check` is blind to the regression. The claim's framing of a "bulk character-stripping pass" is accurate; the only nuance is it is a RE-introduction after a documented restore, not the original strip. Not critical/high: no data loss, money, security, crash, or wrong simulation output. It is cosmetic, but pervasive and on the flagship surface, reading as data corruption. Medium is honest.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested mental model (restore the missing separators/placeholders/icons) is correct, but the fix is more precise than "add a separator" and should distinguish three damage modes: 1) Deleted separators (PowerTab:153, EconomicsTab:514, DefenseTab:215): restore `—` with surrounding spaces, e.g. `Power Distribution — ${pf.length} Factions`, `Shadow Economy — ${bmc}% capture`, `Criminal Structure — {csd.label}` — matching what commit 62c5e8d had. 2) `, ` placeholders (OverviewTab:38 and call-site :122, SummaryTab:204-205, DailyLifeTab:25): restore `'—'` (em-dash) as the empty-value placeholder, not a comma. 3) `{', '}` banner joiners (GenerateWizard:542, 706) and ConfigurationPanel:81 option: restore `{' — '}` and `— Choose an archetype —`. 4) Empty icons (HistoryTab TENSION_META lines 42-51, DefenseTab STRESS_STATUS lines 79-84): these were emoji that the strip removed (and left an orphaned U+FE0F variation selector in DefenseTab — strip those too). The fix is either to repopulate the intended emoji from history or, more robustly, to drop the empty `{tm.icon}` / icon rendering entirely so no stray gap/zero-width-char remains. Note: 62c5e8d's message claims emoji were "confirmed never reformatted," so the icon loss likely predates/differs from the dash strip — recovering the original emoji from git may be needed rather than assuming the restore commit holds them. Strongly recommend the canonical fix be: re-cherry-pick / re-apply commit 62c5e8d's hunks for these files (it was a verified lossless 3-way merge) on top of the unrelated edits 38e339f rode in with, OR add a guard so this regresses no further. Best long-term prevention given the project's eslint visual-budget philosophy: add a lint/CI check forbidding a bare `, ` placeholder/`{', '}` template artifact and asserting a few dossier section titles in a test (currently zero tests cover these strings, so `npm run check` will not catch a re-strip).

confirmed.title: renderTab passes narrativeNote: null to every tab — the NarrativeNote per-tab component is dead in the dossier
confirmed.evidence: All renderTab cases pass `narrativeNote: null` (OutputContainer.jsx:419-432), so the NarrativeNote card every tab mounts (e.g. OverviewTab.jsx:76, EconomicsTab.jsx:213) can never render — `if (!note) return null` (NarrativeNote.jsx:18). The per-tab narrative lens was reimplemented as the banner block inside OutputContainer (lines 783-820, reading `nsrc.narrativeNotes?.[selectedTab]`), but the old prop plumbing through 10+ tabs was left wired to a constant null. Harmless at runtime but a misleading seam: anyone editing a tab reasonably assumes the prop carries data.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Remove the narrativeNote prop and NarrativeNote mounts from the tabs (or actually thread the per-tab note if in-tab placement is preferred over the banner).

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against the live code. OutputContainer.jsx (src/components/OutputContainer.jsx) is the renderer that lazy-imports the new/tabs/* components (lines 47-55) and is the only place that mounts them. Every renderTab case passes a hardcoded narrativeNote: null (lines 419-432, confirmed verbatim). The tabs that destructure narrativeNote (OverviewTab.jsx:43/76, EconomicsTab.jsx:181/213, plus ServicesTab, PowerTab, DefenseTab, NPCsTab, HistoryTab, ResourcesTab, ViabilityTab) mount <NarrativeNote note={narrativeNote} />, and NarrativeNote.jsx:18 does `if (!note) return null;` — so with note always null, the per-tab card can never render. The per-tab narrative lens was indeed reimplemented as the banner block at OutputContainer.jsx:783-820, which reads nsrc.narrativeNotes?.[selectedTab] (line 794) independently of the prop. No other code path feeds these tabs a real narrativeNote value (the PowerTab references in HowToUse.jsx and CompendiumPanel.jsx are unrelated local components). RelationshipsTab gets narrativeNote: null too but doesn't even consume it (dead prop pass). Conclusion: the per-tab NarrativeNote mounts are dead code wired to a constant null — a genuine but harmless misleading seam. Correctly rated low: no crash, no wrong simulation output, no PDF/dossier parity break; narrative prose still surfaces via the banner. It is a maintenance hazard, not a functional defect.
verdicts.fix_note: Both proposed remedies are valid. Note that if the team prefers the in-tab placement over the banner, threading the note is straightforward: pass `narrativeNote: (showNarrative && aiSettlement ? aiSettlement : (publicDossier ? rawSettlement : null))?.narrativeNotes?.[selectedTab] ?? null` per case, but then the banner's NOTE_TABS branch (lines 792-816) must be removed to avoid double-rendering the same note. Simpler/safer fix given the banner is the chosen home: delete the narrativeNote prop plus the NarrativeNote import and mount from each of the ~8 tabs. RelationshipsTab needs no change (it ignores the prop).

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED at the mechanical level, but the severity framing in the claim ("the lens is dead in the dossier") overstates user impact.

Facts verified:
- renderTab in src/components/OutputContainer.jsx:419-432 hardcodes `narrativeNote: null` for all 9 tabs that accept it (overview, economics, services, power, defense, npcs, history, resources, viability).
- Those 9 tabs each mount `<NarrativeNote note={narrativeNote} />` (e.g. OverviewTab.jsx:76, EconomicsTab.jsx:213, plus ServicesTab:80, PowerTab:64, DefenseTab:114, NPCsTab:52, HistoryTab:74, ResourcesTab:36, ViabilityTab:74). Each tab's signature destructures `narrativeNote`.
- NarrativeNote (src/components/new/NarrativeNote.jsx:18) returns null when `!note`. With note===null, the card never renders. So the per-tab NarrativeNote component is dead via the prop path.
- A repo-wide grep confirms there is NO code path anywhere in src/ that ever passes a non-null narrativeNote (every `narrativeNote:` assignment is `: null`; the only JSX usage is `note={narrativeNote}`). The component is universally dead.

BUT the user-visible feature is NOT broken. The per-tab narrative lens was reimplemented as the banner block at OutputContainer.jsx:783-820, which reads the SAME real data: `nsrc.narrativeNotes?.[selectedTab]` (line 794) for NOTE_TABS = the same 8 functional tabs (economics, services, power, defense, npcs, history, resources, viability; overview gets the thesis instead via THESIS_TABS). That `narrativeNotes` data is genuinely produced (src/generators/aiLayer.js:385 buildLocalNotes; supabase/functions/generate-narrative/index.ts stores narrativeNotes[tab]) and the banner is a rendered sibling of renderTab() (both inside the same container; renderTab at line 867, banner at 783). So when narrative is on (showNarrative, line 233) or on a public dossier (publicDossier, line 315), the lens DOES appear to the user — just from the banner, not the in-tab card.

Conclusion: there is no data-level or user-visible failure path. This is dead/redundant prop plumbing left behind after the lens was relocated to the banner — exactly as the claim describes ("harmless at runtime but a misleading seam"). It is a real defect (dead code + a genuine footgun: a maintainer editing a tab reasonably assumes the prop carries data), but it does not affect simulation output, determinism, billing, auth, or dossier/PDF parity. Hence low severity, not high. isReal=true because it is a genuine, evidence-backed dead seam, not a style nit.
verdicts.fix_note: Do NOT "fix" this by wiring `narrativeNote: nsrc?.narrativeNotes?.[selectedTab]` into renderTab — that would render the lens TWICE (once in the banner block at OutputContainer.jsx:783-820, once as the in-tab NarrativeNote card). The two implementations overlap exactly on the 8 NOTE_TABS. Correct remediation is to pick one renderer and delete the other: either (a) remove the dead `narrativeNote` prop from renderTab (lines 419-432) and remove the `<NarrativeNote .../>` mounts + the `narrativeNote` destructure from all 9 tab components (overview, economics, services, power, defense, npcs, history, resources, viability) and the NarrativeNote import; or (b) delete the banner's per-tab note branch and instead feed the in-tab prop, accepting that overview would then need note vs thesis handling. Option (a) is lower-risk and matches the current design intent (the banner is the live path). Note also that wiring the prop without changing tab membership would still produce nothing on the `overview` tab (overview is in THESIS_TABS, not NOTE_TABS), so a naive prop-wiring fix is doubly wrong.

confirmed.title: FounderTile imports non-existent `getFounderSeatsRemaining` — live seat counter permanently dead, and checkout errors are swallowed silently
confirmed.evidence: FounderTile.jsx:48-49 does `const { getFounderSeatsRemaining } = await import('../../lib/founderSeats.js'); const n = await getFounderSeatsRemaining();` but lib/founderSeats.js exports only `fetchFounderSeatsTaken`, `fetchFounderSeatsRemaining`, and `FOUNDER_SEAT_CAP` (founderSeats.js:36,58). The destructured value is undefined, the call throws TypeError, and the enclosing `catch { /* RPC unavailable; stay null */ }` (line 51) hides it forever. Consequences: `seatsRemaining` is always null, so the "N of 500 seats remaining" line (line 120-127) and the personalized "Claim seat N" CTA (lines 77, 162) never render, and the scarcity eligibility check `seatsRemaining <= 0` (line 62) can never retire the tile when seats sell out — the tile keeps selling a $99 lifetime SKU after the 500-seat cap is exhausted (the edge function is the only backstop). PricingPage.jsx:246 uses the correct name `fetchFounderSeatsRemaining`, confirming this is a one-file drift, and tests/lib/founderSeats.test.js pins the `fetch*` names. Because the mismatch is inside a dynamic import destructure, Vite's unresolved-named-import onwarn promotion cannot catch it. Additionally, `handleClick` (lines 79-89) catches `startCheckout` failures with only `console.warn` — an anonymous worldbuilder (eligibility never checks tier!=='anon') clicks "Claim a Founder seat" and startCheckout throws 'You must be signed in to purchase', producing zero user-visible feedback: a silently stranded purchase entry point.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Import `fetchFounderSeatsRemaining` (the real export) and surface checkout failures in the tile (error line + optionally route anonymous users into the auth flow with the existing INTENTS.CLAIM_FOUNDER auth-intent before checkout).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified all material claims against the actual code.

1. IMPORT BUG (confirmed, 100% reproducible): src/lib/founderSeats.js exports only `fetchFounderSeatsTaken` (line 36), `fetchFounderSeatsRemaining` (line 58), and `FOUNDER_SEAT_CAP` (line 29). There is no `getFounderSeatsRemaining` export. FounderTile.jsx:48 destructures `getFounderSeatsRemaining` from the dynamic import → `undefined`; line 49 `await getFounderSeatsRemaining()` throws TypeError, swallowed by the bare `catch { /* RPC unavailable; stay null */ }` at line 51. `seatsRemaining` (useState(null), line 39) therefore stays null forever. PricingPage.jsx:246-247 uses the correct `fetchFounderSeatsRemaining`, confirming one-file drift. tests/lib/founderSeats.test.js pins the real `fetch*` names. Because the mismatch is inside a dynamic-import destructure, Vite's onwarn unresolved-named-import promotion cannot catch it (it only checks static imports) — accurate.

2. DOWNSTREAM CONSEQUENCES (confirmed): with seatsRemaining always null — the "{N} of 500 seats remaining" line (120-127, guarded by typeof seatsRemaining === 'number') never renders; `claimSeat = seatsRemaining ? ... : null` (line 77) is always null, so the personalized "Claim seat N" CTA (162) and the "seat N in the credits" line (138-141) never appear, only the generic "Claim a Founder seat" copy (163); and the scarcity-retirement clause `!(typeof seatsRemaining === 'number' && seatsRemaining <= 0)` (line 62) can never become false, so the tile cannot self-retire when the 500-seat cap sells out — the edge function is the only backstop.

3. ANON SILENT-CHECKOUT-FAILURE (confirmed): eligibility (lines 58-62) checks `tier !== 'premium'` but NOT `tier !== 'anon'`, so an anonymous worldbuilder is eligible. `founder_lifetime` ≠ `single_dossier`, so stripe.js sets isAnonymousProduct=false and throws 'You must be signed in to purchase' (stripe.js:93-94). handleClick catches with only console.warn (line 85), `finally` resets loading — the button just stops spinning with zero user-visible feedback. A stranded purchase entry point.

TEMPERING FACT (why not higher): the `founderRecognition` flag defaults to false (flags.js:92), and `eligible` requires `recognitionEnabled` (line 59), so in the default-shipping prod config the tile renders null and none of these consequences are user-visible today. However this is explicitly a dark-shipped surface ('flip when audience hook is stable', header comment lines 14 + flags.js:51-54), flags resolve from URL param / localStorage / env (flags.js:9-14), and the tile IS mounted unconditionally in AccountPage.jsx:558. The import bug becomes live the instant the flag flips. That gating is exactly why medium (not high/critical) is the honest severity: real, fully-verified defect, but no money/data loss in the current default config.

Could not refute on any cited point; all file:line evidence is accurate.
verdicts.fix_note: Reviewer's fix is correct. Primary: change line 48 to `const { fetchFounderSeatsRemaining } = await import('../../lib/founderSeats.js');` and call it at line 49 (or simply use a static top-level import — the module already only depends on supabase, no need for the dynamic import). Secondary: in handleClick, surface startCheckout failures (an error line in the tile) instead of console.warn-only. For the anon case, either add `tier !== 'anon'` to the eligibility check (line 58-62) so the tile doesn't show a purchase CTA the anon user cannot complete, or route anon clicks through the auth flow first using the existing CLAIM_FOUNDER auth-intent before startCheckout. Worth also adding a render-level test of FounderTile with the flag forced on (the bug survived because no test exercises the live tile with founderRecognition=true — tests/ui/mobile.smoke.test.jsx:60 mocks fetchFounderSeatsRemaining but PricingPage, not the tile).

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via read + reproduction. src/lib/founderSeats.js exports only FOUNDER_SEAT_CAP (line 29), fetchFounderSeatsTaken (line 36), fetchFounderSeatsRemaining (line 58). FounderTile.jsx:48-49 destructures getFounderSeatsRemaining — a name that exists nowhere in src/ or tests/ (grep confirms it appears only at FounderTile.jsx:48-49). Repro: destructuring a missing named export yields undefined; awaiting undefined() throws "TypeError: getFounderSeatsRemaining is not a function". The enclosing `catch { /* RPC unavailable; stay null */ }` (line 51) swallows it on every mount, so setSeatsRemaining is never called and seatsRemaining stays null forever.

Downstream consequences all verified against the source:
- Line 120: `typeof seatsRemaining === 'number'` is false → the "{n} of 500 seats remaining" scarcity line never renders.
- Line 77: `claimSeat = seatsRemaining ? 500 - seatsRemaining + 1 : null` → null → the personalized "Claim seat N" CTA (line 162) and the "your name in the credits (seat N)" line (138-141) never render; it always falls back to the generic "Claim a Founder seat" (line 163).
- Line 62: the sellout guard `!(typeof seatsRemaining === 'number' && seatsRemaining <= 0)` evaluates to !(false) = true when seatsRemaining is null, so the tile can NEVER self-retire when the 500-seat cap is exhausted. The edge function is the only backstop against overselling a $99 lifetime SKU — a real billing-integrity gap.

The contrast with PricingPage.jsx:246-247, which uses the correct fetchFounderSeatsRemaining via the same dynamic-import pattern, confirms this is a one-file name drift. Because the mismatch is inside a dynamic `await import()` destructure, Vite's onwarn unresolved-named-import promotion (which only fires on static imports) cannot catch it, and tests/lib/founderSeats.test.js pins only the fetch* names so no test exercises FounderTile's broken call. The _lazy/_Suspense aliases at AccountPage.jsx:24,27 are correct (lazy as _lazy), so the known underscore-import bug class is NOT present here.

Second sub-claim also confirmed: handleClick (FounderTile.jsx:79-89) catches startCheckout failures with only console.warn (line 85) and renders no error state. Eligibility (lines 58-62) gates tier !== 'premium' only, so anon and free both pass. startCheckout('founder_lifetime') is not the anonymous product (only 'single_dossier' is — stripe.js:86), so for an anon user supabase.auth.getSession() returns no session and stripe.js:94 throws 'You must be signed in to purchase'. That error is swallowed by console.warn → the loading spinner clears and nothing else happens → a silently stranded purchase entry point with zero user-visible feedback.

Live-blast-radius caveat (why high, not critical): flags.js:91 sets founderRecognition default:false, and `eligible` requires flag('founderRecognition') (line 59), so the tile is dark by default today and not on a live main path. But the component is shipped and mounted unconditionally (AccountPage.jsx:557-559); the broken seat-counter/scarcity/personalization path is the ONLY behavior it will ever exhibit the moment the flag flips, and the overselling-backstop loss is a genuine billing concern. Guaranteed-broken feature + billing-cap weakness → high.
verdicts.fix_note:
Primary fix is correct: rename the destructured import to the real export. Two acceptable forms — (a) `const { fetchFounderSeatsRemaining } = await import('../../lib/founderSeats.js'); const n = await fetchFounderSeatsRemaining();`, mirroring PricingPage.jsx:246-247 exactly; or (b) since the static `import {} from '../../lib/founderSeats.js'` at FounderTile.jsx top is absent, just add a static `import { fetchFounderSeatsRemaining } from '../../lib/founderSeats.js'` and drop the dynamic import entirely (the module is already pulled in by PricingPage and is tiny; the lazy-import indirection buys nothing here and is exactly what hid the typo from Vite's onwarn). Form (b) is preferable because it restores compile/lint-time name resolution.

Note fetchFounderSeatsRemaining already clamps to [0, cap] and returns null on any error/not-configured, so the existing `typeof n === 'number' ? n : null` guard (line 50) is still fine.

Suggested-fix addendum the reporter did not state but should be done: the checkout-error swallow (line 84-86) should surface user-visible feedback. Add an error state (e.g. const [error, setError] = useState(null); set it in catch; render it near the button) the same way PricingPage does (it has setCheckoutError at line ~258). Separately, eligibility (lines 58-62) should arguably also gate tier !== 'anon' (or pre-route anon clicks to sign-in) so an anon worldbuilder isn't shown a CTA that can only throw 'You must be signed in to purchase' — otherwise even with visible error feedback the entry point is a dead end for the anon audience the tile explicitly targets.

confirmed.title: WorldMap ⌘S keyboard save calls non-existent store action `saveMapToCampaign` — silently no-ops while suppressing the browser default
confirmed.evidence: The worldbuilder keymap (WorldMap.jsx:551-580) handles Cmd/Ctrl+S with `e.preventDefault(); const fn = useStore.getState().saveMapToCampaign; if (typeof fn === 'function') fn();`. No slice defines `saveMapToCampaign` — the campaign slice's action is `saveCampaignMap(campaignId, mapStateOverride)` (store/campaignSlice.js:520), and a repo-wide grep for `saveMapToCampaign` matches only this WorldMap line. So `fn` is always undefined: ⌘S does nothing, shows no toast, and `preventDefault()` has already eaten the keystroke. The advertised P112/M-8 behavior ('⌘S saves') is dead; a DM who relies on the muscle-memory shortcut believes the map saved when it did not (the AutoSaveChip mitigates only when the `mapAutosave` flag is on AND a campaign is active). The correct local handler `handleSaveMapToCampaign` (line 436) exists in the same component and is simply not referenced from the key handler. Even if the store action existed, it would be called with no campaignId argument.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Call the component's own `handleSaveMapToCampaign` from the keydown handler (add it to the effect deps), or implement/rename a `saveMapToCampaign` store action that resolves the active campaign id; show the same success/error toast as the toolbar Save button.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against the actual code. WorldMap.jsx:557-564 handles Cmd/Ctrl+S by calling e.preventDefault() then `const fn = useStore.getState().saveMapToCampaign; if (typeof fn === 'function') fn();`. A repo-wide grep for `saveMapToCampaign` matches ONLY this line (WorldMap.jsx:563). The campaign slice instead defines `saveCampaignMap(campaignId, mapStateOverride)` at store/campaignSlice.js:520, spread into the store via `...createCampaignSlice(set, get)` (store/index.js:56) under its real name. So `getState().saveMapToCampaign` is always undefined, the `typeof === 'function'` guard fails, and the keystroke no-ops — while preventDefault() has already suppressed any default. The correct local handler `handleSaveMapToCampaign` (WorldMap.jsx:436) does the full snapshot+save+toast and is wired to the toolbar Save button (line 673) but is NOT referenced by the keymap, nor is it in the effect deps (`[setMapMode, handleFit]`, line 580). Even if `saveMapToCampaign` existed on the store, it would be invoked with no campaignId, and saveCampaignMap requires one (it calls findActiveCampaign(state.campaigns, campaignId) and returns early if not found). WorldMap is a live view (App.jsx:514). The in-code comment at lines 559-563 mischaracterizes the store lookup as a 'fallback,' but it is the only path and it is dead. The mapAutosave-gated debounced save (lines 160-171) only mitigates when the flag is on AND a campaign is active. The advertised P112/M-8 '⌘S saves' (comment lines 546-549) is therefore dead. Could not refute any element of the claim; every cited fact checks out.
verdicts.fix_note: Reviewer's fix is correct. Cleanest: in the keydown handler call the component's own `handleSaveMapToCampaign()` instead of the non-existent store action, and add `handleSaveMapToCampaign` to the effect dependency array (currently `[setMapMode, handleFit]` at line 580) so the closure stays current. That handler already shows the same success/error toast as the toolbar Save button and resolves activeCampaignId internally. Also delete/correct the misleading 'fallback' comment at lines 559-563. Note `handleSaveMapToCampaign` early-returns silently when there is no active campaign (line 437), so ⌘S with no campaign selected will still be a no-op with no feedback — optionally add a toast for that case to fully honor the advertised shortcut.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end by reading the code and grepping the full store. WorldMap.jsx:563 looks up `useStore.getState().saveMapToCampaign`, but that action is defined NOWHERE: a repo-wide grep for `saveMapToCampaign` matches only this one line, and a programmatic scan of all 13 store slice files returns false. The real campaign action is `saveCampaignMap(campaignId, mapStateOverride)` at campaignSlice.js:520.

Concrete failure path: DM is on the worldbuilder map with focus on the canvas (not an input — the tag guard at line 553-554 passes), presses Cmd/Ctrl+S. onKey (line 552) enters the modifier branch (line 555), matches 's' (line 557), runs e.preventDefault() (line 558) which consumes the keystroke and kills the browser's native save, then reads fn = useStore.getState().saveMapToCampaign → undefined → `typeof fn === 'function'` is false → fn() never fires. Net: no snapshot captured, no saveCampaignMap call, no toast, nothing. The shortcut is a silent no-op while having already swallowed the keypress.

User/data consequence: the advertised P112/M-8 behavior ("⌘S saves", comment at line 548) is dead. A DM relying on the muscle-memory shortcut believes the map saved when it did not. The only mitigation is the autosave effect (line 160-170), which is hard-gated at line 161: `if (!flag('mapAutosave') || !activeCampaignId) return undefined`. So when the mapAutosave flag is off OR no campaign is active, there is no autosave at all and ⌘S is the only keyboard save affordance — and it does nothing. This is data-loss-adjacent (unsaved placements/labels can be lost on regenerate/navigation while the DM thinks they were saved), but it requires the flag-off/no-campaign state and only affects the keyboard path (the visible toolbar Save button presumably calls handleSaveMapToCampaign directly), so medium rather than high.

Secondary confirmation: even if the store action existed/were aliased, the keymap calls fn() with NO arguments. saveCampaignMap(campaignId) with campaignId=undefined → findActiveCampaign (campaignSlice.js:251-254) does campaigns.find(i => i.id === undefined) → null → early return → no-op. So merely wiring the store action is insufficient. No other global ⌘S handler exists in the codebase (only WorldMap intercepts it), so nothing else picks up the slack. The correct local handler handleSaveMapToCampaign (line 436) — which resolves activeCampaignId, captures the bridge snapshot, calls setMapSnapshot + saveCampaignMap(activeCampaignId, ...), and shows a toast — already exists in the same component and is simply not referenced from the key handler.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix direction is correct but should be sharpened. Do NOT just point the keymap at a store action: (1) the store has no saveMapToCampaign, and (2) the real action saveCampaignMap requires a campaignId that the keymap can't pass cleanly, and even more importantly the keyboard save needs the bridge snapshot-capture + setMapSnapshot + toast that only live in the local handleSaveMapToCampaign (line 436). Correct fix: call the local handler from the key handler — replace lines 563-564 with `handleSaveMapToCampaign();` and add handleSaveMapToCampaign to the effect's dependency array at line 580 (currently [setMapMode, handleFit]); omitting it would capture a stale closure / miss the activeCampaignId it closes over. handleSaveMapToCampaign already early-returns when !activeCampaignId (line 437), so the no-campaign case is handled gracefully (though ideally show a 'No campaign selected' toast there so ⌘S isn't silent in that state too). Keep e.preventDefault() — it's correct to suppress the browser dialog once a real save is wired up.

confirmed.title: Regenerate-world confirm reads non-existent `mapPlacements`/`mapLabels` store keys — loss counts always zero, P112/M-7 warning never shows
confirmed.evidence: handleRegenerate (WorldMap.jsx:508-522) under flag('mapAutosave') computes `const placedCount = (useStore.getState().mapPlacements || []).length; const labelCount = (useStore.getState().mapLabels || []).length;`. The map slice stores these as `mapState.placements` (an OBJECT keyed by burgId — mapSlice.js:94,222) and `mapState.labels` (array); `mapPlacements` and `mapLabels` exist nowhere in the store (repo-wide grep: only this call site). Both reads are always undefined → counts always 0 → the explicit "You'll lose N placements and M labels. This cannot be undone." confirm body never renders; users always get the generic line even with a fully-populated map. Note even the intended key would be wrong for placements: `.length` on the placements object is undefined — it needs `Object.keys(...).length` (as done correctly at line 453 and in mapDirtyKey at line 158).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Read `Object.keys(useStore.getState().mapState.placements || {}).length` and `(useStore.getState().mapState.labels || []).length`.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed real. At src/components/WorldMap.jsx:513-514, handleRegenerate reads `useStore.getState().mapPlacements` and `useStore.getState().mapLabels`. A repo-wide grep shows these two identifiers exist ONLY at these two call sites — they are not store keys. The map slice (src/store/mapSlice.js:94,96) stores the real data at `mapState.placements` (an OBJECT keyed by burgId, default `{}`) and `mapState.labels` (an array, default `[]`); nested under `mapState`, not at the top level. So `useStore.getState().mapPlacements` and `.mapLabels` both evaluate to `undefined`, and `(undefined || []).length === 0` for both. With placedCount and labelCount always 0, the guard `if (placedCount || labelCount)` (line 515) is always false, so the explicit "You'll lose N placements and M labels. This cannot be undone." message (lines 516-518) is never assigned — the user always sees the generic line at 511.

The path is LIVE, not dead: `flag('mapAutosave')` (line 512) is `default: true` and explicitly "PROMOTED default-on" per src/lib/flags.js:83-86, and `flag()` falls through to `decl.default` (flags.js:188). So this branch executes by default for every user.

The reviewer's secondary point also holds: even with the correct key, `.length` on the placements object is wrong because placements is an object, not an array — the codebase correctly uses `Object.keys(useStore.getState().mapState.placements || {}).length` at the analogous save site (WorldMap.jsx:453) and in the dirty-key logic (mapSlice.js per the report). The labels key is an array so `.length` is fine there.

Severity is honestly low: this is purely a cosmetic/UX degradation of a confirmation dialog. The user still gets a confirm dialog before any regenerate, just with the generic wording instead of the specific count. No data loss (the confirm still gates the destructive action), no crash (`(undefined || []).length` is safe), no money/security exposure, no simulation-output error, and no determinism break. It's a real but minor defect.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct. Replace lines 513-514 with: `const placedCount = Object.keys(useStore.getState().mapState.placements || {}).length;` and `const labelCount = (useStore.getState().mapState.labels || []).length;`. Note placements needs Object.keys(...).length (object), labels uses .length (array) — matching the correct usage at WorldMap.jsx:453.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed real. WorldMap.jsx:513-514 reads `useStore.getState().mapPlacements` and `.mapLabels` — top-level store keys that do not exist. Repo-wide grep (src/ + supabase/) finds these two names ONLY at this call site; no slice defines them. The map slice stores placements as `mapState.placements` (an OBJECT keyed by burgId, mapSlice.js:94,222) and labels as `mapState.labels` (an array, mapSlice.js:96). Both buggy reads resolve to undefined; the `|| []` fallback yields empty arrays, so `.length` is 0 for both.

Failure path (active by default): The branch is gated on `flag('mapAutosave')`, which is `default: true` and explicitly "PROMOTED default-on" (flags.js:83-86) — so this is the live path for all users, not an opt-in. A user populates the world map (placements/labels), clicks Regenerate, handleRegenerate runs: placedCount=0, labelCount=0, the `if (placedCount || labelCount)` guard (line 515) is always false, and the detailed "You'll lose N placements and M labels. This cannot be undone." message (line 516-518) never builds. The generic fallback string "Regenerate the world? All unsaved placements will be lost." is what reaches the user via setRegenerateConfirm(msg) -> ConfirmDialog body={regenerateConfirm} (line 521, 1002), even on a fully-populated map. The entire P112/M-7 explicit-count warning is dead code.

Reproduced read-only with a node one-liner against the real store shape: buggy logic gives placedCount=0/labelCount=0 (branch not taken, generic message), while the correct logic gives 3 placements / 2 labels.

The same component reads the correct keys correctly three other places — mapDirtyKey (line 158: `Object.keys(m.placements||{}).length` ... `(m.labels||[]).length`), the autosave effect (line 163), and the save toast (line 453) — which both establishes the intended shape and proves line 513-514 is a divergent typo, not an alternate-but-valid API.

Severity medium, not high/critical: the destructive Regenerate still works and still presents a confirm dialog with a generic warning, so there is no silent data loss, no crash, no money/auth/security impact — only a degraded (less-informative) safety prompt and a non-functional feature. Confidence ~0.97.

confirmed.title: useReturnVisit ranks saves with Number(ISO timestamp) — NaN comparator makes the "welcome back" settlement arbitrary
confirmed.evidence: The lastSettlement ranking (useReturnVisit.js:74-78) does `const aTs = Number(a.campaignState?.editedAt || a.savedAt || 0)`. Every save record gets `campaignState.editedAt = new Date().toISOString()` via pickleCampaignState (store/settlementSlice.js:197), so the `||` always selects the ISO STRING and `Number('2026-06-12T…')` is NaN for every entry. The comparator `bTs - aTs` returns NaN, which Array.sort treats as 'keep order', so `ranked[0]` is just the first element of savedSettlements (push-appended, i.e. typically the OLDEST save — settlementSlice.js:930-936), not the most-recently-touched one. The return-visit hero (X-9 'surface the prior settlement') therefore greets users with the wrong settlement whenever they have more than one save. (`savedAt` itself is numeric — saves.js:142,265 — so the fallback would have worked; the editedAt branch poisons it.)
confirmed.suggested_fix: Parse both shapes: `const aTs = Date.parse(a.campaignState?.editedAt) || Number(a.savedAt) || 0;` (Date.parse handles ISO, Number handles epoch millis).

verdicts.reasoning:
The reviewer's mechanical claims are individually correct: Number(ISO) is NaN (verified by execution), pickleCampaignState always stamps campaignState.editedAt as an ISO string (src/store/settlementSlice.js:197), the `||` selects that truthy ISO string over the numeric savedAt, the comparator operands are therefore NaN, and a NaN comparator leaves Array.prototype.sort order unchanged. So the sort in src/hooks/useReturnVisit.js:74-78 is genuinely a no-op — a real code smell.

However, the load-bearing claim — that ranked[0] is the OLDEST save and the user is greeted with the WRONG settlement — is refuted. That premise rests on savedSettlements being push-appended oldest-first, which the reviewer attributes to the store's saveSettlement method (src/store/settlementSlice.js:930, push). But that method is DEAD CODE: grep across src finds only its definition (settlementSlice.js:918), no call site anywhere. The actual save flow is savesService.save() (GenerateWizard.jsx:215, SettlementsPanel.jsx:580, store/index.js:105) followed by re-hydration via setSavedSettlements(savesService.list()) (SettlementsPanel.jsx:631/637, WorldMap.jsx:190, useGalleryPageState.js:90). savesService.list() returns NEWEST-FIRST in both backends: Supabase orders by updated_at DESC (src/lib/saves.js:132), and local mode unshifts new saves (src/lib/saves.js:265, 317). Because savedSettlements arrives newest-first and the broken comparator preserves array order, ranked[0] = savedSettlements[0] = the newest save = the correct 'welcome back' settlement.

Net: the code is fragile (it relies on incidental pre-sorted ordering rather than the sort it pretends to perform — a latent bug if the hydration ordering ever changes), but the user-facing symptom the reviewer describes ('greets users with the wrong settlement whenever they have more than one save') does not actually occur at runtime. The central evidential claim is wrong, so the defect as described is not real.
verdicts.fix_note: The sort comparator is genuinely a no-op (NaN comparison preserves order), which is worth tidying defensively, but it is not currently user-visible because savedSettlements is already hydrated newest-first by savesService.list() (Supabase ORDER BY updated_at DESC at src/lib/saves.js:132; local-mode unshift at saves.js:265/317). The reviewer's claim that ranked[0] yields the OLDEST save is incorrect — it relies on the store's saveSettlement push method (settlementSlice.js:930) which is dead code (no call sites). If the sort is fixed for robustness, note that Supabase-hydrated records carry campaignState=row.campaign_state (saves.js:156) whose editedAt may diverge from updated_at, and savedAt is the numeric updated_at mirror (saves.js:142); the suggested Date.parse(editedAt) || Number(savedAt) || 0 is a reasonable hardening but would change which timestamp drives ranking vs. the current incidental updated_at ordering.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed real defect at src/hooks/useReturnVisit.js:75-76. The comparator does `Number(a.campaignState?.editedAt || a.savedAt || 0)`. Verified facts: (1) pickleCampaignState (settlementSlice.js:197) always sets `editedAt: new Date().toISOString()`, a non-empty string, so the `||` always selects editedAt over savedAt. (2) Every save also carries a numeric savedAt (settlementSlice.js:932; saves.js:142). (3) Number('2026-06-13T...') === NaN (verified via node). (4) bTs - aTs === NaN for every pair, and Array.prototype.sort treats a NaN return as 'keep order', so the array is effectively NOT sorted — ranked[0] === savedSettlements[0]. Reproduced with a node one-liner using the exact comparator against a 3-element oldest-first array: buggy comparator returned OLDEST, the correct (savedAt-first) comparator returned NEWEST. Consequence is user-visible: lastSettlement = ranked[0] flows into WelcomeBackCard.jsx (line 27/48/92) which headlines the 'welcome back' hero with lastSettlement.name and wires its Open button to that settlement (line 39). So the return-visit hero can greet the user with the wrong settlement.

Severity is low, not high, for two reasons grounded in code. First, the consequence is cosmetic/UX (wrong name + wrong resume target on an optional welcome card) — no data loss, no money, no security, no determinism break, and it is gated behind flag('welcomeBack') which the component comment says is 'default off' (WelcomeBackCard.jsx:29 + comment line 9). Second, the claim's framing that ranked[0] is 'typically the OLDEST' overstates real-world impact: the savedSettlements slice is hydrated via saves.list(), and BOTH production loaders deliver it newest-first — Supabase `.order('updated_at',{ascending:false})` (saves.js:132) and local `unshift` (saves.js:265,317). So in practice savedSettlements[0] is usually already the newest, masking the symptom. The broken comparator nonetheless fails to GUARANTEE correctness: it surfaces whatever the loader left first, and bites whenever incoming order isn't already newest-first (e.g. an in-session saveSettlement push appends to the end of an already-loaded list at settlementSlice.js:930, so a freshly saved settlement would NOT win the ranking). It's a genuine logic bug whose blast radius is limited and partly masked by upstream ordering.
verdicts.fix_note: Swapping operand order to savedAt-first (matching the working LibraryToolbar.jsx:31-32 pattern) is the right direction but only half-fixes it: `Number(a.savedAt || a.campaignState?.editedAt || 0)` still yields NaN on the editedAt fallback branch because editedAt is an ISO string. savedAt is numeric on all real records so this rarely triggers, but for robustness parse the string branch: e.g. `const ts = (r) => Number(r.savedAt) || Date.parse(r.campaignState?.editedAt || '') || 0;` then `return ts(b) - ts(a);`. Also worth keeping the comparator NaN-safe (coerce any NaN to 0) so a future field change can't silently re-disable sorting. The doc comment at useReturnVisit.js:21 ('most recent savedAt or campaignState.editedAt') already implies savedAt-first ordering, so the code, not the doc, is wrong.

confirmed.title: Gallery list fetch state machine: query changes never set loading, and an in-flight loadMore can append stale-query pages onto a fresh result set
confirmed.evidence: Two related defects in the same state machine. (1) The query effect (useGalleryPageState.js:55-77) never calls `setListLoading(true)` when sort/search/filters change — listLoading is only true from its useState initializer. After first load, changing a filter or typing in search shows no loading state (GalleryTopbar.jsx:71 keeps showing the stale total, GalleryList's Load-more button stays enabled because `disabled={listLoading}` is false). (2) `loadMore` (lines 126-141) has no cancellation token and appends unconditionally: `setItems(prev => [...prev, ...res.items])`, then sets hasMore/page from its own (possibly stale) query. Sequence: click 'Load more' (slow request for page 1 of query A) → change sort/filter → effect resolves and replaces items with page 0 of query B → the stale loadMore resolves and appends query-A page-1 rows to the query-B list, and overwrites hasMore/total with query-A values. Result: a visibly mixed/duplicated list (duplicate `key={item.slug}` rows if the same dossier appears in both pages → React key collision warnings) and wrong pagination state. The page-0 effect protects itself with a `cancelled` flag; loadMore got no equivalent.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Set listLoading(true) at the start of the query effect; give loadMore a generation/abort token tied to galleryQuery (bail out of all setState calls if the query changed since the request started), and/or dedupe appended items by slug.

verdicts.reasoning:
Both halves of the claim are confirmed by direct reading of src/hooks/useGalleryPageState.js and its consumers.

Defect (1) — query effect never sets loading true: The effect at lines 55-77 runs on every galleryQuery change (sort/search/filters via the useMemo at line 53) but never calls setListLoading(true). The only writes to listLoading are the useState initializer (line 39, true) and the effect's .finally (line 74, false). So after the first load resolves, listLoading is false and remains false during any subsequent query change. Consequences verified: GalleryTopbar.jsx:71 renders the stale total instead of "Loading settlements..." (its `loading` prop is `listLoading`, wired at GalleryList.jsx:128), and the Load-more button stays enabled because `disabled={listLoading}` (GalleryList.jsx:164) is false.
