Defect (2) — loadMore has no cancellation/generation token: loadMore (lines 126-141) closes over galleryQuery/page/total and, on resolve, unconditionally does setItems(prev => [...prev, ...res.items]) (line 132) then overwrites hasMore/page/total (lines 133-135) with values from its own possibly-stale request. The page-0 effect guards every setState with a `cancelled` flag (lines 56, 63, 71, 74); loadMore has no equivalent. The race is reachable: loadMore sets listLoading(true) which only disables the Load-more button, but I verified neither GalleryTopbar nor GallerySidebar disables the sort/search/filter controls during loading (grep for `disabled` in both returned nothing). So the user can change the query while a loadMore is in flight; the query effect replaces items with page 0 of query B, then the stale loadMore appends query-A page-1 rows and clobbers hasMore/page/total. With key={item.slug} (GalleryList.jsx:147), slug overlap across the two pages yields duplicate-key warnings; mixed list and wrong pagination occur regardless of overlap.

Refutation attempts failed: no upstream guard, no nearby comment marking this intentional, and the only relevant test (tests/components/galleryPage.test.jsx) covers a basic render/open flow with no loading-on-query-change or loadMore-race assertions. The 'mine' path early-returns in loadMore (line 127) but is irrelevant to the public-feed race. The cited APIs behave as described.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is sound. Concretely: (a) call setListLoading(true) as the first statement inside the query effect (line 56-area) so query changes show the loading state; (b) capture a generation token tied to galleryQuery — e.g. reuse a ref/counter incremented per query, or capture `const q = galleryQuery` at loadMore start and bail from all setItems/setHasMore/setPage/setTotal calls if galleryQuery has changed by resolve time. Slug-dedup on append is a useful defense-in-depth but does not fix the stale hasMore/page/total overwrite, so the generation token is the primary fix. Severity medium is correct: secondary (community gallery) feature, UX/state-consistency rather than data loss/money/security/determinism. Note defect (1) (missing loading indicator) reproduces on every post-first query change and is the more reliably observable of the two; the mixed-list race needs a slow request plus the interleaved interaction.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the real module src/hooks/useGalleryPageState.js and its consumers.

DEFECT 1 (missing loading state on query change), useGalleryPageState.js:55-77. The effect that re-runs on galleryQuery change (sort/search/filters; galleryQuery is a useMemo over [sort,search,filters] at line 53, deps [galleryQuery] at line 77) issues the fetch and only calls setListLoading(false) in .finally (line 74). It never calls setListLoading(true) at the start. listLoading is set true ONLY by the useState(true) initializer (line 39) and by loadMore (line 129). So after the first load resolves, every subsequent filter/sort/search change runs with listLoading=false. Consequences confirmed in the UI: GalleryTopbar.jsx:71 renders `loading ? 'Loading settlements...' : `${total} public settlements`` — with loading=false during a query change it shows the STALE total (props wired straight through GalleryPage.jsx:69 -> GalleryList.jsx:128 -> GalleryTopbar loading={listLoading}). And the Load-more button GalleryList.jsx:164 `disabled={listLoading}` stays ENABLED during the in-flight query change. There is no search debounce (grep found none; GalleryTopbar.jsx:29 calls setSearch on every keystroke), so this is hit on normal typing.

DEFECT 2 (uncancelled stale loadMore append), useGalleryPageState.js:126-141. loadMore (useCallback, deps [galleryQuery,page,total]) captures page and galleryQuery at call time, awaits fetchPublicGallery, then UNCONDITIONALLY runs setItems(prev => [...prev, ...res.items]) (line 132), setTotal (133), setHasMore (134), setPage(nextPage) (135). There is no cancellation flag. The query effect protects itself with `cancelled` (lines 56,63,76) but that closure has no reference to and cannot abort an in-flight loadMore.

Concrete reachable race: (1) on query A page 0, click Load more -> loadMore captures page=0, query=A, issues slow fetch P_A1 for page 1. (2) user changes sort/filter -> galleryQuery becomes B -> effect fires, issues P_B0 (page 0 of B). (3) P_B0 resolves first: setItems([B0]), setPage(0), setHasMore(B), setTotal(B). (4) slow P_A1 resolves: with no guard, setItems(prev=[B0] => [B0, A1]) appends query-A page-1 rows onto the query-B list, and overwrites page=1, hasMore/total with query-A values. Net result: a visibly mixed list and corrupt pagination state (page=1 against a freshly-replaced page-0 result set, so a subsequent Load more would request page 2 and skip page 1 of B). If any dossier appears in both B0 and A1, the cards keyed `key={item.slug}` (GalleryList.jsx:146) collide -> React duplicate-key warning, as claimed; the mixed-list/wrong-pagination corruption itself does not require overlap and is certain given the race.

Severity medium: this is a gallery-list UX/state-correctness bug behind a slow-network + fast-interaction race. No data loss, no money, no auth/security, no determinism or simulation impact; it self-resolves on the next clean query change. The duplicate-key React warning is a real but cosmetic symptom. Real defect, edge-case/timing-dependent — medium.
verdicts.fix_note: Both halves are real and the suggested direction (give loadMore a cancellation equivalent + set loading on query change) is correct. Recommended concrete fix: (a) add setListLoading(true) as the first statement inside the query effect (line 56 area) so filter/sort/search changes show the loading state and disable Load more; (b) for the stale-append, the cleanest single mechanism is a generation counter shared by both code paths: keep a const genRef = useRef(0); in the query effect increment genRef.current and capture const myGen = genRef.current, bailing in both .then and .finally if myGen !== genRef.current; in loadMore capture const myGen = genRef.current at call time and guard every setState (setItems/setTotal/setHasMore/setPage and the finally) with `if (myGen !== genRef.current) return;`. A per-call `cancelled` boolean local to loadMore is NOT sufficient by itself because nothing flips it when the query changes — it must be tied to the effect's generation/cleanup. Note that fixing only (a) does not fix the data corruption in (b): adding setListLoading(true) to the effect leaves loadMore's loading flag self-healing (its own .finally still runs) but the unconditional setItems/setPage/setHasMore remain the actual corruption, so (b) is the load-bearing part. Minor: loadMore reads `total` from the closure (dep at line 141) for the setTotal(res.total ?? total) fallback — once a generation guard is in place this is harmless, but the guard makes the `total` dep mostly vestigial.

confirmed.title: Compendium stressor 'affects' pills render danger-on-danger — text is invisible
confirmed.evidence: The saved-item card's affects pills (CompendiumPanel.jsx:942-948) set `color: swatch.danger, background: swatch.danger` — identical token for foreground and background, so the channel labels ('economy', 'safety', …) render as a solid red lozenge with unreadable text. Every other pill in this file uses a translucent background derived from the accent (`background: `${accent}14`` in renderPills at line 798, `${color}18` in Tag at line 22), and the adjacent border `'1px solid #8b1a1a44'` confirms swatch.danger is the saturated #8b1a1a-family red used as a text color elsewhere (e.g. AccountPage pairs `color: swatch.danger` with `background: swatch.dangerBg`). This is the read-only display of the same data the renderPills edit control shows correctly, so the saved stressor card contradicts the edit form.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Use a translucent or paired token for the background, e.g. `background: swatch.dangerBg` or `background: `${swatch.danger}14``, keeping `color: swatch.danger`.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against the actual code. At src/components/CompendiumPanel.jsx:942-948 the saved-stressor card's `affects` pills set `color: swatch.danger` AND `background: swatch.danger` on the same span. swatch.danger resolves to '#8b1a1a' (src/design/tokens.js:126), a dark saturated red — so the channel labels are dark-red text on a dark-red background, rendering as a solid red lozenge with essentially unreadable text. The border `'1px solid #8b1a1a44'` (line 945) confirms swatch.danger is the foreground-red family. The block is reachable: 'affects' is a real stressor field (fields list includes 'affects' at line 266; item.affects guard at line 940), and the edit control for the same data renders correctly via renderPills using a translucent `${accent}14` background (line 798) with `color:accent`. So the read-only card contradicts the edit form for identical data. The established codebase convention pairs `color: swatch.danger` with a light/translucent background — Tag uses `background:`${color}18`` (line 22), renderPills uses `${accent}14`, and AccountPage/SettlementDetail/DeleteConfirmation pair swatch.danger with swatch.dangerBg ('#fdf4f4'). Note: this is an instance of a recurring contrast bug class — InstitutionalGrid.jsx:308 and :328 have the identical `color: swatch.danger, background: swatch.danger` defect — but the cited CompendiumPanel instance is genuinely broken on its own. Severity low is honest: no data loss, money, security, determinism, or simulation-output impact; the underlying data is intact and correctly editable. It is a readability/parity defect (read-only display vs edit form) confined to the custom-content authoring panel.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct and matches codebase convention: keep `color: swatch.danger` and change the background to a light/translucent token — `background: swatch.dangerBg` or `background: `${swatch.danger}14``. Note the same defect also exists at InstitutionalGrid.jsx:308 and :328 (color: swatch.danger on background: swatch.danger) if a broader cleanup is desired, though those are out of scope for this specific finding.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED. Traced end-to-end.

Facts verified by reading the code:
- src/design/tokens.js:126-127 — swatch.danger = '#8b1a1a' (saturated red), swatch.dangerBg = '#fdf4f4' (light pink).
- src/components/CompendiumPanel.jsx:944-945 — the saved-stressor card's affects pill sets `color: swatch.danger` AND `background: swatch.danger`, i.e. the identical token #8b1a1a for both text and fill, with only `border: '1px solid #8b1a1a44'` (27% alpha) to outline it.
- A read-only node contrast computation gives a ratio of exactly 1.000 for #8b1a1a-on-#8b1a1a (WCAG AA text needs >=4.5), so the label text is genuinely invisible.

Concrete failure path: catDef for category 'stressors' (line 265-266) declares field 'affects'. The edit form renders 'affects' via renderPills (line 854 -> 780-806), which draws readable translucent pills (`background: `${accent}14``, `color: accent`, lines 798-799). A user selects one or more affect categories from STRESSOR_AFFECT_CATEGORIES = ['economy','safety','supply chains','military','religion','magic','criminal','governance','population','morale'] (line 302-305), producing an `affects` array on the saved item. When that item is shown in the read-only saved-items list, the guard at line 940 (`Array.isArray(item.affects) && item.affects.length > 0`) passes and each entry renders as a span with foreground == background == #8b1a1a. Result: each affect renders as a solid red lozenge whose label ('economy', etc.) is unreadable.

The claim's supporting points all check out: every other pill in the file uses a translucent accent background (Tag at line 22 uses `${color}18`; renderPills at line 798 uses `${accent}14`), and AccountPage (e.g. lines 318, 609) correctly pairs `color: swatch.danger` with `background: swatch.dangerBg`. So the read-only stressor card contradicts both the edit form and the file's own conventions.

Impact is purely cosmetic: the underlying data is correct and persisted properly, determinism/simulation/billing are unaffected, and nothing crashes — the affect labels are simply unreadable in the saved-card display. Hence severity low (real but minor display defect). Confidence high; the offending line is read directly and the contrast is mathematically zero.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix (translucent background) is correct. Match the file's existing convention — use `background: `${swatch.danger}14`` and keep `color: swatch.danger` (mirrors renderPills line 798 and Tag line 22). Alternatively use the opaque-light pairing already used in AccountPage: `background: swatch.dangerBg` (#fdf4f4) with `color: swatch.danger` — the existing 27%-alpha border (#8b1a1a44) reads fine over either. Note for the maintainer: the identical-token bug exists verbatim elsewhere as the same bug class — InstitutionalGrid.jsx:308 and :328 ("{n} excluded" pills) both use `color: swatch.danger, background: swatch.danger`. Those are separate from this claim but worth fixing in the same pass.

confirmed.title: Corrupted copy family: em-dash separators degraded to ', ' (and one dropped entirely) across user-visible strings
confirmed.evidence: A consistent corruption where an em-dash separator became ', ' or vanished, producing visibly broken UI strings: (1) WorldMap.jsx:663 `<option value="">, No campaign</option>` — the campaign dropdown's default option reads ', No campaign'. (2) BuyThisDossier.jsx:87 `` `Buy this dossier${SINGLE_DOSSIER.priceLabel}` `` renders the purchase CTA as 'Buy this dossier$2.99' with no separator (the title attr at line 72 shows the intended phrasing). (3) CompendiumPanel.jsx:175 `{s.description||s.desc||', '}` shows a bare ', ' for stress types with no description. (4) map/PlacementDetailCard.jsx:47 and map/QuickInspector.jsx:56 both fall back to `|| ', '` for a missing tier, so the card shows ', ' as the tier line. (5) primitives/LockedDestination.jsx:61 default `ctaLabel = 'Upgrade, $6/mo'` while its own JSDoc at line 49 documents the default as `'Upgrade — $6/mo'` — direct proof the original glyph was an em-dash. Same family also at GenerateWizard.jsx:542,706 (`{', '}` prefixing copy).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Restore the em-dash (or a sensible separator/empty string) at each site: '— No campaign', 'Buy this dossier — $2.99', '' or '—' for missing description/tier fallbacks, 'Upgrade — $6/mo'.

verdicts.reasoning: All cited sites confirmed verbatim against the actual code. (1) WorldMap.jsx:663 `<option value="">, No campaign</option>` — the live campaign dropdown's default option (gated on canManageCampaigns, but reachable) literally reads ", No campaign". (2) BuyThisDossier.jsx:87 `` `Buy this dossier${SINGLE_DOSSIER.priceLabel}` ``; SINGLE_DOSSIER.priceLabel is '$2.99' (src/config/pricing.js:228), so the CTA renders "Buy this dossier$2.99" with no separator, while the title attr at line 72 ("Buy this dossier as a PDF for $2.99") shows the intended phrasing. (3) CompendiumPanel.jsx:175 `{s.description||s.desc||', '}` — bare ", " for descriptionless stress types. (4) map/PlacementDetailCard.jsx:47 and map/QuickInspector.jsx:56 both fall back to `|| ', '` for a missing tier. (5) primitives/LockedDestination.jsx:61 default `ctaLabel = 'Upgrade, $6/mo'` while its own JSDoc at line 49 documents the default as `'Upgrade — $6/mo'` — direct internal proof the original glyph was an em-dash. Same family at GenerateWizard.jsx:542 and :706 (`{', '}` prefixing "Set the foundations…" and the step hint, where an em-dash separator clearly belongs). The defect is genuine and user-visible. I could not refute it: the paths are live (not dead code), no upstream guard substitutes the separator, and nothing indicates intent — the JSDoc/default mismatch is conclusive evidence of a glyph corruption. Impact is purely cosmetic (broken copy in visible strings); no crash, data-loss, billing/auth, determinism, or PDF-parity consequence, so low severity is honest. The fallback-to-', '' cases (tier/description) are degraded-but-functional, and the WorldMap option's value is correctly empty — only its label text is wrong.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct. Recommended replacements: WorldMap.jsx:663 -> "— No campaign" (keep value=""); BuyThisDossier.jsx:87 -> `Buy this dossier — ${SINGLE_DOSSIER.priceLabel}`; CompendiumPanel.jsx:175 -> '' (empty string) rather than '—', since a bare em-dash for a missing description is also odd; PlacementDetailCard.jsx:47 and QuickInspector.jsx:56 -> '' or '—' for the missing-tier fallback (empty preferred so the line just collapses); LockedDestination.jsx:61 -> 'Upgrade — $6/mo' to match its JSDoc; GenerateWizard.jsx:542 and :706 -> {' — '} so the strong label and following copy read "Basic Generate — Set the foundations…" / "Step N: label — hint".

verdicts.reasoning: CONFIRMED. All cited locations verified verbatim and the corruption pattern is proven, not speculative. Smoking gun (LockedDestination.jsx): JSDoc line 49 documents the default as `[props.ctaLabel='Upgrade — $6/mo']` (em-dash intact in the comment) while the actual code default at line 61 is `ctaLabel = 'Upgrade, $6/mo'`. The comment and the string disagree by exactly an em-dash→', ' substitution — direct evidence a single editing/encoding pass degraded em-dashes in code string literals while leaving comments untouched. Em-dashes are pervasive and intentional in this codebase (present in 450 src files, including these same files' header comments at BuyThisDossier.jsx:2 and GenerateWizard.jsx:2), confirming `— ` is the house separator style. Concrete failure paths: 1) WorldMap.jsx:663 `` — the campaign-select default option (rendered whenever activeCampaignId is null/empty, i.e. the default state for every user without a selected campaign) displays the literal leading-comma string `, No campaign`. Visible on the World Map toolbar in the normal/default state. 2) BuyThisDossier.jsx:87 — template literal `` `Buy this dossier${SINGLE_DOSSIER.priceLabel}` `` with priceLabel='$2.99' (config/pricing.js:228) concatenates with NO separator. Demonstrated via node: renders exactly \"Buy this dossier$2.99\". The button title attr (line 72) shows the intended phrasing, so a separator was dropped entirely. This is on the anonymous-viewer purchase CTA — a monetization surface, though the defect is cosmetic (button still functions; handleBuy is unaffected). 3) CompendiumPanel.jsx:175 `{s.description||s.desc||', '}` — a stress type with no description/desc renders a bare `, ` as its body text. 4) map/PlacementDetailCard.jsx:47 (`s.tier||settlement.tier||', '`, rendered at line 109 `{tier}{pop...}`) and map/QuickInspector.jsx:56 — a placed/hovered settlement missing a tier shows `, ` as the tier line. 5) GenerateWizard.jsx:542 (`Basic Generate{', '}Set the foundations...`) and 706 (`Step N: label{', '}hint`) — a `{', '}` JSX child sits exactly where an em-dash separator belonged between a bold lead-in and following prose, rendering 'Basic Generate, Set the foundations…' and 'Step N: Label, hint' instead of '… — …'. These are all real, currently-shipping, user-visible string defects reachable on default/main paths. None is a crash, determinism break, data-integrity, parity, or billing/auth-logic failure — the billing CTA still works, it just reads wrong. Hence low severity (cosmetic copy corruption across a family of strings), high confidence it is a genuine defect.
verdicts.fix_note:
The suggested fix (restore em-dashes) is correct for the SEPARATOR cases but wrong/imprecise for the FALLBACK cases — they are two different bug shapes and must not be fixed identically.

SEPARATOR cases (restore em-dash WITH surrounding spaces):
- WorldMap.jsx:663 → `<option value=\"\">— No campaign</option>` (or simply `No campaign` with no glyph; a leading em-dash on a dropdown option is unusual — prefer plain `No campaign`).
- BuyThisDossier.jsx:87 → restore a separator: `` `Buy this dossier — ${SINGLE_DOSSIER.priceLabel}` `` (note the spaces; current code has neither dash nor space).
- LockedDestination.jsx:61 → `ctaLabel = 'Upgrade — $6/mo'` to match its own JSDoc.
- GenerateWizard.jsx:542 and 706 → `{' — '}` (em-dash with both spaces) in place of `{', '}`.

FALLBACK cases (these are MISSING-VALUE placeholders, NOT separators — do NOT insert ' — ' as a separator-with-spaces; use a bare em-dash placeholder or empty string):
- CompendiumPanel.jsx:175 `{s.description||s.desc||', '}` → use `'—'` (bare em-dash, conventional 'no value') or `''`.
- PlacementDetailCard.jsx:47 and QuickInspector.jsx:56 `|| ', '` → use `'—'` or `''`. Note PlacementDetailCard:109 renders `{tier}{pop ? ` · ${pop}` : ''}`; a `'—'` placeholder reads fine there.

Also recommend a repo-wide grep for the corruption signature to catch siblings beyond the six cited (e.g. `grep -rn \"|| ', '\" src/` and `grep -rn \"{', '}\" src/`), since this is a mechanical mass-substitution and other instances likely exist.

confirmed.title: PricingPage tier-card status bugs: Wanderer card shows 'Redirecting…' during Cartographer checkout and shows 'Current plan' to paying subscribers
confirmed.evidence: Two defects on the tier cards. (1) Line 371: `loading={loading === (tier.key === 'founder' ? 'founder_lifetime' : 'premium')}` — for the WANDERER card this evaluates `loading === 'premium'`, so the moment a user clicks the Cartographer buy button (setLoading('premium'), line 259), the free Wanderer card's CTA also flips to 'Redirecting…' (TierCard line 162) — two cards claim a checkout redirect is happening for one purchase. (2) Line 284: the Wanderer CTA label is `authTier === 'anon' ? t('pricing.tiers.wanderer.cta') : 'Current plan'` — every signed-in user including premium/Cartographer subscribers and founders sees 'Current plan' on the FREE tier card, while the Cartographer card simultaneously shows 'Manage subscription' (line 297). A paying user is told the free tier is their current plan.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Map each tier to its own product key for the loading comparison (wanderer → never loading), and only label Wanderer 'Current plan' when `authTier === 'free'` (non-elevated, non-founder).

verdicts.reasoning:
Both sub-claims verified against the actual code in src/components/PricingPage.jsx, with supporting facts from src/config/pricing.js and src/store/authSlice.js.

CLAIM 1 (loading state leak) — REAL. Line 371: `loading={loading === (tier.key === 'founder' ? 'founder_lifetime' : 'premium')}`. getVisibleTiers() returns [wanderer, cartographer, founder] (pricing.js:282-283), so all three TierCards render. For the Wanderer card, tier.key !== 'founder', so the prop reduces to `loading === 'premium'`. Clicking the Cartographer CTA calls buy('premium') (line 298) → setLoading('premium') (line 259). That makes BOTH the Cartographer card and the Wanderer card have loading=true, so both buttons render 'Redirecting…' (line 162). The Founder card uses 'founder_lifetime' as its key and is unaffected, which is why the bug is scoped to Wanderer-during-Cartographer-checkout exactly as described. Path is reachable on the live public pricing page; no upstream guard prevents it.

CLAIM 2 ('Current plan' on free tier for paying users) — REAL. auth.tier enum is 'anon' | 'free' | 'premium' (authSlice.js:64). Line 284: Wanderer label = `authTier === 'anon' ? t('pricing.tiers.wanderer.cta') : 'Current plan'`. Therefore every signed-in user (tier 'free' OR 'premium', and elevated/founder users who are not anon) gets 'Current plan' on the FREE Wanderer card. Meanwhile a premium/elevated user simultaneously gets 'Manage subscription' on the Cartographer card (currentPaid = authTier === 'premium' || isElevated, line 295-297). So a paying subscriber is told the free tier is their current plan — genuinely misleading and contradictory UI state.

Both are cosmetic CTA-label/state defects: no money handling, data loss, security, crash, or determinism impact, so severity 'low' is honest and correct. I could not refute either; the evidence holds with file:line precision.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct. Two refinements: (1) For the loading prop, map each tier.key to its own product key — wanderer has no purchase product so it should never be in a loading state (e.g. loading === (tier.key==='founder' ? 'founder_lifetime' : tier.key==='cartographer' ? 'premium' : null), which for wanderer compares loading === null and is false while a real product is in flight). (2) For the Wanderer label, gate 'Current plan' on the user actually being on the free tier and not elevated/founder: `authTier === 'free' && !isElevated && !isFounder ? 'Current plan' : t('pricing.tiers.wanderer.cta')` (or similar) — this also keeps premium/founder/elevated users from being told free is their plan. Note authTier values are 'anon'|'free'|'premium' (authSlice.js:64), so checking `=== 'free'` is the right positive condition rather than negating 'anon'.

verdicts.reasoning:
Both defects are confirmed against the source at src/components/PricingPage.jsx.

DEFECT 1 (loading flag bleeds onto the free card): Line 371 sets `loading={loading === (tier.key === 'founder' ? 'founder_lifetime' : 'premium')}`. For the WANDERER card, the founder check is false, so the prop becomes `loading === 'premium'`. Trace: a user clicks "Subscribe" on the Cartographer card → onCta is `buy('premium')` (line 298) → `setLoading('premium')` (line 259). On the next render, BOTH the Cartographer card (`loading==='premium'` true) AND the Wanderer card (`loading==='premium'` true) compute loading=true, so both render the button text 'Redirecting…' (line 162), both become disabled, both show `cursor:wait` and opacity 0.6. The founder card is unaffected because it checks 'founder_lifetime'. Wanderer has no Stripe checkout at all (its onCta just calls onNavigate('generate'), line 285), so it should never enter a 'Redirecting…' state — the ternary's else branch is wrong for any non-paid tier. getVisibleTiers() returns [wanderer, cartographer, founder] (pricing.js:283), so all three cards render together and the bleed is observable.

DEFECT 2 ('Current plan' shown to paying users on the FREE card): Line 284 sets the Wanderer label to `authTier === 'anon' ? t('pricing.tiers.wanderer.cta') : 'Current plan'`. auth.tier is one of 'anon' | 'free' | 'premium' (authSlice.js:64, resolveTier at line 55). Therefore ANY signed-in user — including a premium Cartographer subscriber or a Founder — sees 'Current plan' on the free Wanderer card. Simultaneously, a premium user sees 'Manage subscription' on the Cartographer card (currentPaid = authTier==='premium' || isElevated, lines 295-297) and a founder sees 'Founder active' on the founder card (line 290). So a paying subscriber is told the free tier is their current plan while another card shows their actual paid plan — contradictory and misleading.

Consequences are purely cosmetic/UX-misleading: clicking the Wanderer CTA still navigates to 'generate' regardless of the loading flag, the wrong loading badge does not change which checkout session is created, and no billing/auth/determinism/persistence behavior is affected. No data loss, money, security, or crash. Hence low severity. Both are genuine defects a reviewer would accept.
verdicts.fix_note: The report's implied fixes are correct in direction. Concretely: (1) Line 371 — make the loading prop reflect the tier's actual checkout product and never light up for tiers with no checkout. e.g. derive a per-tier product key: founder→'founder_lifetime', cartographer→'premium', wanderer→null, then `loading={loading != null && loading === productFor(tier.key)}`. The current else-branch incorrectly maps every non-founder tier (including wanderer) to 'premium'. (2) Line 284 — gate 'Current plan' on the user actually being on the free tier, not merely non-anon: `authTier === 'free' ? 'Current plan' : t('pricing.tiers.wanderer.cta')` (with anon also showing the 'Start free' CTA). Note the existing label strings 'Current plan', 'Manage subscription', 'Redirecting…', 'Founder active' are hard-coded English rather than going through t()/copy — out of scope for this bug but worth a follow-up given the project's i18n copy layer, and may trip the no-raw-button-copy lint rule.

confirmed.title: Shared Dialog primitive (and AuthModal) declare aria-modal but implement no focus management — background stays tabbable
confirmed.evidence: primitives/Dialog.jsx Shell (lines 22-119) renders `role="dialog" aria-modal="true"` and handles Escape, but: focus is never moved into the dialog on open (except TextInputDialog's input autoFocus), there is no focus trap (Tab walks the obscured page behind the overlay, contradicting aria-modal which tells AT the background is inert), and focus is not restored to the trigger on close. ConfirmDialog is used for destructive confirms (WorldMap regenerate, GenerateWizard unsaved-exit), where a keyboard user's focus remains on the now-obscured page — pressing Enter can activate a hidden control instead of Cancel/Confirm. The same gap exists in the hand-rolled AuthModal.jsx (lines 44-63: overlay + role=dialog + aria-modal, no Escape handler, no focus move/trap/restore) and ChroniclePanel's FullEntryModal (lines 116-125). The project's IconButton primitive documents WCAG-driven rigor ('audit found we strip focus outlines…'), so dialog focus management is an acknowledged requirement the primitive doesn't meet.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In Shell: on open, save document.activeElement, focus the dialog (or first focusable), trap Tab/Shift+Tab within the section, and restore focus on close. Reuse the same Shell for AuthModal or add Escape + the same focus management there.

verdicts.reasoning: Every load-bearing claim checks out against the actual code. In src/components/primitives/Dialog.jsx the Shell component (lines 9-120) renders role="dialog" aria-modal="true" (lines 40-41) and adds an Escape keydown listener (lines 10-17), but performs NO focus management: it never saves document.activeElement, never moves focus into the dialog on open, has no Tab/Shift+Tab focus trap, and never restores focus on close. The only focus move anywhere is TextInputDialog's input autoFocus (line 220); ConfirmDialog (122-140) and ChoiceDialog (142-186) move no focus at all. ConfirmDialog IS used for destructive confirms: WorldMap.jsx:998, GenerateWizard.jsx:938, SettlementDetail.jsx:855, OutputContainer.jsx:882, PhaseBadge.jsx:100/109. So a keyboard user's focus remains on the now-obscured page behind the overlay, and aria-modal="true" lies to assistive tech by declaring the background inert when Tab still walks it — a real WCAG/keyboard defect, and Enter could activate a hidden control rather than Cancel/Confirm. The same gap is confirmed in AuthModal.jsx (lines 44-63): overlay + role=dialog + aria-modal=true, no Escape handler in the file, no focus move/trap/restore. ChroniclePanel's FullEntryModal (lines 116-125) likewise has no Escape and no focus management (minor inaccuracy in the claim: it does NOT actually declare aria-modal, but the focus-management absence the claim rests on is real). IconButton.jsx lines 4-10 do document WCAG-driven focus rigor ('the audit found we strip focus outlines... a visible focus ring via the global :focus-visible rule'), so dialog focus management is an acknowledged project standard the primitive does not meet. No focus-trap library or shared focus utility exists anywhere (grep for focus-trap/FocusTrap/inert/useFocusTrap found nothing; no focus dependency in package.json). I found no upstream guard, dead-path, or intentional-comment that refutes the finding. Severity is honestly low: this is a genuine functional accessibility/keyboard defect (not a cosmetic style nit), but it is not data loss, money, security, crash on the main path, nor does it touch simulation correctness, determinism, or PDF/dossier parity — the product's core concerns. The worst concrete harm (Enter activating an obscured control during a destructive confirm) is a plausible but edge-case interaction, matching a low rating.
verdicts.fix_note: The reviewer's fix is sound. One correction to the evidence: ChroniclePanel's FullEntryModal (lines 116-125) does NOT actually declare aria-modal (it omits it), so unlike Shell and AuthModal it doesn't have the aria-modal-vs-tabbable contradiction; its only defect is the missing Escape handler and missing focus management. Best fix is to add focus management once in Shell (save activeElement on open, focus the dialog section or its first focusable, trap Tab/Shift+Tab within the section, restore focus on close) and route AuthModal and FullEntryModal through the same Shell (or replicate the same logic plus an Escape handler for AuthModal, which currently has none).

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED on every concrete point I could read.

1. src/components/primitives/Dialog.jsx:39-41 renders `role="dialog" aria-modal="true" aria-label={title}`. The only side effect on open is the useEffect at lines 10-17, which adds a window keydown listener for Escape -> onCancel. There is NO focus move into the dialog on open, NO focus trap, and NO focus restore on close. Only TextInputDialog gets partial mitigation via its input `autoFocus` (line 220); ConfirmDialog/ChoiceDialog get nothing.

2. No focus-trap/restore machinery exists anywhere in the app — `grep` for focus.trap/trapFocus/restoreFocus/useFocusTrap/inert(attr) returned zero hits in src. So the primitive is the canonical implementation and it does not meet the contract aria-modal advertises.

3. ConfirmDialog is genuinely used for destructive confirms, matching the claim verbatim: WorldMap.jsx:998 ("Regenerate world?", onConfirm=performRegenerate, an async world regen defined at WorldMap.jsx:524) and GenerateWizard.jsx:938 (unsaved-exit; body literally says "the exact result won't come back"; onConfirm=doExit at GenerateWizard.jsx:394). Also SettlementDetail.jsx:855, OutputContainer.jsx:882, PhaseBadge.jsx:100/109.

4. AuthModal.jsx:44-63 is hand-rolled: overlay div with onClick=onClose, inner div with role="dialog" aria-modal="true" aria-labelledby. There is NO Escape handler at all and no focus management; the email input is not even autoFocused. So AuthModal is worse than the primitive (Escape doesn't close it).

5. ChroniclePanel FullEntryModal (lines 116-125) is just a fixed overlay div with onClick=onClose; it has neither role="dialog" nor aria-modal nor focus handling.

6. The IconButton primitive (src/components/primitives/IconButton.jsx:4-5) does document the project's WCAG-driven rigor ("the audit found we strip focus outlines… WCAG 2.2's"), so dialog focus management is a self-acknowledged standard the primitives miss.

FAILURE PATH (traced): A keyboard/AT user triggers a destructive ConfirmDialog (e.g. tabs to the WorldMap "Regenerate" trigger or the wizard exit control and activates it). The dialog mounts with aria-modal="true", which tells assistive tech the background is inert — but focus stays on the now-obscured trigger. Tab continues to walk the obscured page (no trap), and an AT user is told there is nothing outside the dialog (the aria-modal lie). A keyboard user whose focus is still on a background button can press Enter/Space and activate that hidden control rather than Cancel/Confirm. This is a real WCAG 2.4.3 (focus order) / 4.1.2-aria-modal-contract violation and a usability hazard on destructive flows.

WHY LOW, NOT MEDIUM/HIGH: No data loss, money, security, or crash, and no simulation/determinism/parity impact (the engine and dossier are untouched). The worst-case "Enter activates a hidden control" is conditional — it needs keyboard-only interaction with focus parked on a specific background control — and is mitigated by working Escape-to-cancel and overlay-click-to-cancel on the shared primitive (though NOT on AuthModal, which lacks Escape). It is a genuine accessibility defect that contradicts an explicitly-stated project standard, so it is real, but it sits at the low end of severity for this product's bug bar.
verdicts.fix_note:
The suggested direction (add focus move-in, trap, and restore to the shared Shell, then route AuthModal and ChroniclePanel's FullEntryModal through it) is correct, but note these specifics:

- Implement once in Dialog.jsx `Shell`: on open, capture document.activeElement as the return target; move focus to the dialog section (give it tabIndex={-1} and ref.focus(), or focus the first focusable). On unmount/close, restore focus to the captured element. Add a Tab/Shift+Tab keydown handler scoped to the dialog that cycles within the focusable set (focus trap). The existing Escape useEffect can stay.

- The current Escape handler uses a window-level listener; if multiple dialogs ever stack, Escape will fire all of them. Low risk today (single-dialog usage) but worth scoping the listener to the dialog node when adding the trap.

- AuthModal.jsx is the larger gap: it is fully hand-rolled (AuthModal.jsx:44-63) with NO Escape handler and no focus management, and its email input is not autoFocused. Either migrate it onto the shared Shell or replicate the same focus-move/trap/restore + Escape there. Don't fix only the primitive and leave AuthModal/ChroniclePanel hand-rolled, or the inconsistency persists.

- ChroniclePanel FullEntryModal (lines 116-125) additionally lacks role="dialog" and aria-modal entirely — it needs those added regardless of focus work.

- `inert` on the background container is a cleaner alternative to a JS trap for true background-inerting, but it requires a single wrapping root for "everything except the dialog," which this app's portal-less inline overlays don't currently have — so the JS focus-trap approach is the pragmatic fix here.

confirmed.title: WorldPulsePanel self-defeats its own null-guard: reads `worldState.pulseHistory.length` on a possibly-undefined array
confirmed.evidence: Line 381: `const latestPulse = (worldState.pulseHistory || [])[worldState.pulseHistory.length - 1] || null;`. The `|| []` fallback proves the author expects `pulseHistory` may be missing (line 379 likewise defends `campaign.worldState || {}`), but the INDEX expression dereferences the original `worldState.pulseHistory.length` — if pulseHistory is undefined this throws TypeError and white-screens the World Pulse workspace. This executes before the `!worldState.canonizedAt` early-return (line 420), so it runs for draft worlds too. In practice campaignSlice.migrateCampaign → ensureWorldState (campaignSlice.js:246, worldState.js:63) backfills `pulseHistory: []` for campaigns that flow through the slice, so the crash needs a campaign object that bypassed migration (e.g. a worldState-less record handed to the panel directly) — hence latent rather than live; but as written the guard is dead code and the panel's defensive contract is broken.
confirmed.suggested_fix: `const history = worldState.pulseHistory || []; const latestPulse = history[history.length - 1] || null;` (or `.at(-1)`).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the claim against the actual code and it holds. At src/components/map/WorldPulsePanel.jsx:381 the expression is `const latestPulse = (worldState.pulseHistory || [])[worldState.pulseHistory.length - 1] || null;`. The `|| []` guards only the array being indexed; the index expression `worldState.pulseHistory.length` independently dereferences the raw value. I confirmed empirically via Node that with `worldState = {}` this throws `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'length')`. So the guard is genuinely dead code and the defensive contract is broken as described. Line 379 (`campaign.worldState || {}`) confirms the author expected a possibly-empty worldState, and line 381 runs unconditionally during render, before the `!worldState.canonizedAt` early-return at line 420, so it would also fire for draft worlds.

However, the crash is latent, not live, exactly as the reviewer concedes. Every ingestion path into `state.campaigns` normalizes worldState through `ensureWorldState` (src/domain/worldPulse/worldState.js:68, line 93 always produces `pulseHistory: cloneArray(...)`, i.e. an array): `createCampaign` uses `ensureWorldState(null, ...)` (campaignSlice.js:447); `loadCampaigns` runs `localLoad` (which maps `migrateCampaign` → `ensureWorldState`) for cached and `remote.map(migrateCampaign)` for remote (campaignSlice.js:403); the merge at line 405 takes two already-migrated lists. The sole call site (src/components/WorldMap.jsx:838) feeds `activeCampaign` sourced from `state.campaigns`. So no current path delivers a worldState-bearing campaign with undefined pulseHistory, and the crash does not fire in practice.

Net: a real, correctly-characterized low-severity latent defect — a self-defeating null guard / dead code that would TypeError-white-screen the World Pulse workspace only if a non-migrated worldState reached the panel. The evidence is accurate and the suggested fix (`const history = worldState.pulseHistory || []; history[history.length - 1]` or `.at(-1)`) is correct.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct. `const history = worldState.pulseHistory || []; const latestPulse = history[history.length - 1] || null;` (or `history.at(-1) || null`). Note `.at(-1)` on an empty array returns undefined, so keep the `|| null` for parity with current behavior.

verdicts.reasoning:
CODE DEFECT CONFIRMED, but LATENT (dead guard), not a live crash on any reachable path.

Line 381 of src/components/map/WorldPulsePanel.jsx is verbatim: `const latestPulse = (worldState.pulseHistory || [])[worldState.pulseHistory.length - 1] || null;`. The `|| []` fallback is genuinely dead — if pulseHistory is undefined, the throw occurs inside the index expression `worldState.pulseHistory.length` (unguarded original reference) before the fallback array is ever indexed. I reproduced the exact failure with a node one-liner against the real semantics: Case A (worldState with no pulseHistory) throws `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'length')`. It also executes before the `!worldState.canonizedAt` early-return at line 420, so it would run for draft worlds too — the claim's trace is accurate on the mechanics.

HOWEVER, no concrete in-repo path delivers a non-null worldState lacking pulseHistory to the panel:
- Sole render site is WorldMap.jsx:838, passing `activeCampaign`, derived only from `s.campaigns` (WorldMap.jsx:130-132).
- Every population path normalizes worldState: loadCampaigns runs cached (localLoad -> .map(migrateCampaign), campaignSlice.js:83) AND remote (.map(migrateCampaign), :403) through migration; createCampaign builds ensureWorldState(null,...) (:447). mergeCampaignLists only cloneJsons already-migrated inputs (campaignSync.js:45-78).
- ensureWorldState (worldState.js:93) unconditionally sets `pulseHistory: cloneArray(raw?.pulseHistory)`, and cloneArray (worldState.js:35-37) returns [] for any non-array. Reproduced: migrated worldState yields pulseHistory:[] (Case B), and even garbage pulseHistory is normalized to [] (Case C).
- Campaigns are NOT in Zustand persist partialize (index.js:63-74, only config+toggles), so no rehydrate path bypasses migration.
- All in-place worldState spread-mutations in campaignSlice (lines 641/645, 674/708, 770, 788, 907, 924, 1034) start from ensureWorldState(c.worldState, c) and override only specific keys (stressors/simulationRules/proposals), never dropping pulseHistory.

The existing test (tests/components/worldPulsePanel.test.jsx) always supplies pulseHistory (populated or []), so the suite neither catches nor disproves the latent path — consistent with the bug being unreachable in practice. A skeptical senior reviewer would agree the expression is wrong and the defensive contract is broken (high confidence the code is defective), but would also agree it does not crash today (so severity is low, not high). The claim's own framing ("latent rather than live ... the guard is dead code") is accurate.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested intent (collapse to a single guarded read) is correct, but the fix must ensure the INDEX also reads the guarded value, not the raw reference again. Use: `const history = Array.isArray(worldState.pulseHistory) ? worldState.pulseHistory : []; const latestPulse = history[history.length - 1] || null;` or simply `const latestPulse = (worldState.pulseHistory || []).at(-1) || null;`. Do NOT preserve the original shape of two separate `worldState.pulseHistory` references. Note this is a defensive-hardening fix only — no concrete reproduction path exists, so it does not fix an observed crash.

confirmed.title: useReaderAudience 'hasUsedLocks' signal is structurally dead — saves never have a root-level `locks` array
confirmed.evidence: Lines 105-108 compute `hasUsedLocks` as `saves.some(x => Array.isArray(x.locks) && x.locks.length > 0)`. Save records are `{ ...settlement, savedAt, id, campaignState }` (settlementSlice.js:930-936); lock state is persisted only as `campaignState.locks`, and it's an OBJECT, not an array (`locks: state.locks ? { ...state.locks } : {}` — settlementSlice.js:195). The settlement schema defines no top-level `locks` field (grep of settlement.schema.js). So `x.locks` is always undefined and the check is permanently false — and even pointed at the right path, Array.isArray would still fail on the object shape. Consequence: one of the three worldbuilder-detection signals documented in the hook header ('regenerate-with-locks usage') never fires, narrowing who sees worldbuilder-gated surfaces (FounderTile recognition, audience-led pricing copy).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Check the real shape: `saves.some(x => x.campaignState?.locks && Object.values(x.campaignState.locks).some(Boolean))`.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified all load-bearing claims by direct reading. (1) Save records are built as `{ ...settlement, savedAt, id, campaignState: pickleCampaignState(state) }` (src/store/settlementSlice.js:930-934). (2) Lock state is persisted only as `campaignState.locks`, and it is an OBJECT, not an array: `locks: state.locks ? { ...state.locks } : {}` (settlementSlice.js:195); `state.locks` is initialized to `{}` (line 477), mutated as a keyed map `state.locks[key] = value` (line 1182), and its `Locks` typedef (src/domain/types.js:45-57) declares a sparse object whose values are booleans or arrays — never a top-level array. (3) The settlement schema has no top-level `locks` field (grep of src/domain/settlement.schema.js matches only the substring "clocks"), so `x.locks` from the `...settlement` spread is always undefined. (4) No code anywhere lifts locks into a top-level array on save records (grep across src/services, src/lib, and whole src found nothing; only `navigator.locks` and "clocks" substrings). Therefore the check at src/hooks/useReaderAudience.js:105-108 — `saves.some(x => Array.isArray(x.locks) && x.locks.length > 0)` — is permanently false: x.locks is always undefined, and even pointed at the right object it would fail Array.isArray on the object shape. The hook header (lines 9-15) explicitly lists "regenerate-with-locks usage" as a worldbuilder signal, and the hook is consumed live by src/components/pricing/FounderTile.jsx:36 (worldbuilder gating) and src/hooks/useCopy.js:33 (audience-led copy), confirming the documented consequence. Could not refute. Severity is honestly low: no determinism/billing/security/data-integrity impact and no crash; it only weakens a soft UX-targeting heuristic, and the worldbuilder gate (savedCount>=5 && (hasUsedNeighbours || hasUsedLocks || exportCount>=3)) still has working fallback signals, so the surface is narrowed, not fully closed.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is shape-correct. `Object.values(x.campaignState.locks).some(Boolean)` handles both boolean and array lock values (non-empty array and `true` are both truthy). Slightly cleaner alternative, since setLock (settlementSlice.js:1178-1184) deletes falsy/empty entries so the object only ever holds active locks: `saves.some(x => Object.keys(x.campaignState?.locks || {}).length > 0)`. Note a parallel latent issue worth a separate look: `hasUsedNeighbours` (useReaderAudience.js:101-103) checks `x.neighbourLinks`, and grep found no `neighbourLinks` field in settlement.schema.js either — it may also be reading the wrong/absent path, though that is outside the cited finding.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED real defect. Traced end-to-end against the real modules.

Save-record shape (settlementSlice.js:930-934): `{ ...settlement, savedAt, id, campaignState: pickleCampaignState(state) }`. There is no root-level `locks`.

Lock state truth: the slice field is an OBJECT, not an array — declared `locks: {}` (settlementSlice.js:477), mutated via `state.locks[key] = value` / `delete state.locks[key]` (setLock/clearLocks, lines 1180-1186). It is persisted ONLY into `campaignState.locks` as an object spread: `locks: state.locks ? { ...state.locks } : {}` (pickleCampaignState, line 195). Confirmed via grep that settlement.schema.js has no top-level `locks` property and sampleDossier.json has no `locks` key (grep exit 1), and no generator writes `locks` onto a settlement.

Hook (useReaderAudience.js:105-108): `hasUsedLocks = saves.some(x => Array.isArray(x.locks) && x.locks.length > 0)`. Two compounded errors: (1) wrong path — `x.locks` is the settlement root, which has no such field, so it's always undefined; the data lives at `x.campaignState.locks`. (2) wrong type guard — even pointed at `x.campaignState.locks` it's an OBJECT, so Array.isArray is false and `.length` is undefined.

Reproduced with a node one-liner against the exact save shape: a user with `state.locks = {economy:true,'npc:3':true}` (2 locked sections) yields `x.locks === undefined`, `Array.isArray(campaignState.locks) === false`, `hasUsedLocks === false`. The correct check `Object.keys(campaignState.locks).length > 0` returns true.

Smoking gun for intent: the SIBLING signal in the same function (line 98) correctly reads `x.campaignState?.lastExportAt` for exportCount — the author knew lifecycle/provenance fields live under campaignState; the locks line just used the wrong path and the wrong type. And the parallel `hasUsedNeighbours` (line 103) works precisely because `neighbourLinks` IS a real top-level settlement field (used identically in LibraryToolbar.jsx:77, present in sampleDossier.json:132). So the asymmetry isolates locks as the genuine defect.

Consequence: `hasUsedLocks` is structurally dead — permanently false regardless of user behavior. It is one of three OR'd campaign-behavior triggers in the worldbuilder gate (computeReaderAudience line 61: `savedCount >= 5 && (hasUsedNeighbours || hasUsedLocks || exportCount >= 3)`). A user with 5+ saves who has locked sections but has fewer than 3 exports and no neighbour links is misclassified as 'intermediate' rather than 'worldbuilder', so worldbuilder-gated surfaces (FounderTile recognition at FounderTile.jsx:60, audience-led pricing copy via useCopy.js) don't surface. Locks is explicitly documented as worldbuilder-tier behavior (copy/en.js:459: 'The worldbuilder's tier: neighbour network, locks, drift, full export') and is listed in the hook header (line 14) as a worldbuilder signal, so this is a real intended-vs-actual gap, not a misread of design.

Severity rationale: not critical/high — no data loss, money, security, crash, or wrong simulation output, and the most prominent consumer (FounderTile) is behind flag `founderRecognition` which defaults to false (flags.js:92). It's a dead personalization/upsell signal that narrows an audience classification, with a partial OR-fallback (neighbours, exports) that masks it for many users. Real but bounded -> medium.
verdicts.fix_note: The claim is correct that the signal is dead, but the suggested mental-model fix needs the type correction made explicit. locks is an OBJECT keyed by section name (state.locks = {}, settlementSlice.js:477; setLock does state.locks[key] = value), persisted as campaignState.locks via object spread (settlementSlice.js:195). So BOTH the path and the type guard are wrong. Correct fix: `saves.some(x => x.campaignState?.locks && Object.keys(x.campaignState.locks).length > 0)` — read through campaignState (matching the sibling exportCount signal at line 98 which already uses x.campaignState?.lastExportAt), and use Object.keys(...).length, NOT Array.isArray/.length. A "fix" that only corrects the path to x.campaignState.locks but keeps Array.isArray would remain permanently false because the value is an object. Note: setLock deletes empty-array values, but normal lock values are truthy scalars stored on the object, so a non-empty Object.keys check is the right emptiness test. Optionally also guard that values are truthy, but presence of a key already implies a live lock given setLock's delete-on-false semantics.

confirmed.title: Paid single-dossier purchase permanently destroyed if user returns more than 1 hour after stashing (TTL shorter than Stripe session lifetime, destructive clear-on-read)
confirmed.evidence: The anonymous $2.99 dossier flow stashes the settlement to localStorage before redirecting to Stripe (BuyThisDossier.jsx:48-52) and is the ONLY copy of the purchased artifact. pendingDossier.js sets TTL_MS = 60*60*1000 (line 27) and readPendingDossier() (lines 87-90) destroys the stash via clearPendingDossier() as a side effect of any read past the TTL. create-checkout/index.ts sets no custom expires_at, so the Stripe Checkout session stays payable for Stripe's default 24 hours. A buyer who completes payment 1-24h after clicking Buy (left the Stripe tab open, mobile interruption, came back to the success URL later) lands on SingleDossierSuccessPage, whose useState initializer calls readPendingDossier() (SingleDossierSuccessPage.jsx:36) — the stash is wiped on that very read and the page shows 'We couldn't verify this dossier' with a support email. The settlement existed only in that stash, so even support cannot recover the product, only refund. The module's own docstring calls a paid receipt for an empty dossier 'the worst possible failure mode'. Compounding: App.jsx:132-137 strips session_id from the URL via checkCheckoutResult() BEFORE attachPendingDossierCheckout(result.sessionId), and ignores attach's boolean return — if the attach write fails the session id is irrecoverably gone from both URL and stash. tests/lib/pendingDossier.test.js:61 pins the 1-hour clear, so this is a deliberate but wrong constant, not a typo.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Raise TTL_MS to at least 24h (Stripe's default session lifetime) or set expires_at on the Checkout session to match the stash TTL. Make readPendingDossier non-destructive for the success-page path (only clear after a verified download or explicit user action), and have App.jsx check attachPendingDossierCheckout's return value before discarding session_id from the URL.

verdicts.reasoning:
I attempted to refute the claim by reading the cited file plus all callers/callees, and every load-bearing sub-claim checks out.

1. TTL is 1 hour: pendingDossier.js:27 sets TTL_MS = 60*60*1000.
2. Destructive clear-on-read past TTL: readPendingDossier() lines 87-90 call clearPendingDossier() and return null whenever Date.now()-stashedAt > TTL_MS. The success page reads via a useState initializer (SingleDossierSuccessPage.jsx:36, useState(() => readPendingDossier())), so the very first render after a >1h delay wipes the stash and falls into the "We couldn't verify this dossier" branch (lines 128-165).
3. The stash is genuinely the only copy. I confirmed the server never receives the settlement: verify-single-dossier/index.ts only retrieves the Stripe session and checks payment_status/metadata.checkout_token (lines 43-48); it never accepts or persists the settlement payload. create-checkout/index.ts likewise stores nothing recoverable. So once the localStorage stash is cleared, the purchased artifact is unrecoverable — support can only refund. The module docstring (lines 13-15) itself calls a paid receipt for an empty dossier "the worst possible failure mode."
4. Stripe session lifetime exceeds the stash TTL. create-checkout/index.ts:191-203 builds sessionParams with no expires_at, so the Checkout session uses Stripe's default ~24h expiry and remains payable for up to 24h — far beyond the 1h stash TTL. This creates a real 1h–24h window where payment succeeds but the artifact is already (or will be) destroyed on the success-page read.
5. The compounding URL-strip is real: checkCheckoutResult() in stripe.js deletes session_id from the URL via history.replaceState (lines 138-142) and App.jsx:135-140 calls attachPendingDossierCheckout(result.sessionId) without checking its boolean return, then navigates away. So if the attach write fails (e.g., stash already cleared, quota), the session id is gone from both the URL and the stash.
6. The 1-hour clear is deliberately pinned by tests/lib/pendingDossier.test.js:61-68, confirming a chosen-but-wrong constant rather than a typo.

I could not find any guard that makes this unreachable: the flow is the production anonymous $2.99 path (BuyThisDossier.jsx is the live anon CTA), the success page is the real Stripe-return landing (App.jsx:134-140 routes to it), and no server-side fallback exists. The only thing that limits impact is that the common-case checkout finishes in well under an hour, so the bug bites only delayed returns — which is why medium (not high/critical) is the honest severity.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound. Most robust single change: set expires_at on the Stripe Checkout session in create-checkout/index.ts to match the stash TTL so the two lifetimes can never diverge (or, conversely, raise TTL_MS to >=24h to cover Stripe's default). Independently, the success page should not let a routine read destroy the only copy of a paid artifact: in SingleDossierSuccessPage.jsx defer clearPendingDossier() until after a verified successful download or explicit user action, and consider not auto-clearing in readPendingDossier() at all on the success path (only validate freshness, clear elsewhere). Also have App.jsx check attachPendingDossierCheckout's boolean return before discarding session_id — though note the deeper issue is that checkCheckoutResult() already strips session_id from the URL inside stripe.js before attach is even attempted, so reordering (attach first, strip after) or persisting session_id more durably would be the real fix for the compounding loss.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED end-to-end against the real modules.

Core defect (TTL < Stripe session lifetime + destructive read):
- The anonymous $2.99 flow stashes the in-memory settlement to localStorage as the ONLY copy before redirecting to Stripe (BuyThisDossier.jsx:48-52). I confirmed the server never persists the settlement: verify-single-dossier/index.ts only checks payment_status + token match (lines 43-48), and stripe-webhook/index.ts:246-251 explicitly logs "Nothing to mutate ... the success-page redirect ... deliver the PDF". So the stash is irreplaceable.
- TTL_MS = 60*60*1000 (pendingDossier.js:27). readPendingDossier() at lines 87-90 calls clearPendingDossier() — a DESTRUCTIVE side effect — for any read past the TTL.
- create-checkout/index.ts:191-203 sets no expires_at, so the Stripe Checkout session stays payable for Stripe's default 24h. A buyer who pays 1-24h after clicking (left tab open, mobile interruption) returns to a stash that is both stale AND wiped on read.

I reproduced this against the real src/lib/pendingDossier.js in node: stashed a settlement, backdated stashedAt by 90 min, called readPendingDossier() -> returned null AND removed the localStorage entry ("stash present AFTER read: false"). The settlement is permanently destroyed; SingleDossierSuccessPage.jsx:128 then renders "We couldn't verify this dossier" with a support mailto. Money was taken (Stripe charged), product is gone, and support cannot recover it (server never had the payload) — only refund. The module's own docstring (lines 13-15) calls a paid receipt for an empty dossier "the worst possible failure mode", which is exactly what this produces.

Compounding defect also confirmed:
- App.jsx:132-136 calls checkCheckoutResult() FIRST, which strips session_id from the URL via history.replaceState (stripe.js:138-142), THEN calls attachPendingDossierCheckout(result.sessionId) and ignores its boolean return.
- I reproduced: when the attach localStorage write fails (quota/private-mode), attach returns false, but the settlement remains in the stash with sessionId=null. The session_id is now gone from both URL and stash. On the success page, SingleDossierSuccessPage.jsx:39-46 sets verification.status='failed' whenever sessionId is missing, dead-ending to the same "couldn't verify" screen even though the settlement itself survived.
- Independently, in the >1hr case attachPendingDossierCheckout itself calls readPendingDossier() internally (pendingDossier.js:97), so the stash is actually destroyed by App.jsx's attach call, BEFORE the success page ever reads it.

tests/lib/pendingDossier.test.js:61-68 pins "clears stale entries after one hour", so the 1-hour constant is deliberate, not a typo — it is simply wrong relative to the 24h Stripe session lifetime.

Severity: high (not critical). It is real money-for-no-product, but only on the off-happy-path where the buyer pays >1h after clicking, or storage write flakes. The common case (pay within an hour) works. Real revenue/trust loss on an edge that genuinely occurs (abandoned-then-resumed checkouts, especially mobile).
verdicts.fix_note:
The reporter's diagnosis is correct; the fix needs three coordinated changes, not just bumping the constant:

1) Make TTL >= the Stripe session payable window. Set expires_at in create-checkout (sessionParams) AND set TTL_MS to match with a safety margin. Stripe's default is 24h; either cap the session (e.g. expires_at = now + 30min and TTL = ~45min) or, if you keep the 24h default, raise TTL_MS to >24h. The two numbers MUST be coupled or this recurs. Capping the Stripe session shorter is the cleaner option because it also bounds how stale a stash can be.

2) Make readPendingDossier() NON-destructive for the stale case. It should return null on stale but NOT clearPendingDossier(), because the success-page flow already has explicit clears (SingleDossierSuccessPage.jsx:118 on "keep", :296 on "generate another"). Better: when stale, still return the payload (or a flag) so the success page can attempt verifySingleDossierPurchase against the real Stripe session — if Stripe confirms a paid 'complete' session matching the token, deliver the PDF regardless of local TTL. The TTL is a UX nicety ("don't surprise me with yesterday's dossier"), but it must never gate a PAID, server-verifiable purchase. Gating PDF delivery on a verified Stripe session (which the code already does via verification.status) is the real correctness boundary; the local TTL should not be able to override it.

3) Fix the App.jsx ordering/return-handling: call attachPendingDossierCheckout(result.sessionId) BEFORE checkCheckoutResult() strips the URL params, or capture sessionId and re-set it on the URL if attach returns false, and check the boolean. Currently App.jsx:136 throws away the return and the session_id is already gone from the URL — a failed attach is unrecoverable.

Also update tests/lib/pendingDossier.test.js:61-76 which currently lock in the 1-hour-clear-on-read behavior; those assertions encode the bug.

confirmed.title: Custom-content localStorage is written owner-scoped but only ever read from the anon key: grandfathered items are never migrated (flag set after reading the wrong key) and the offline mirror is never consulted
confirmed.evidence: Boot hydration is `customContent: loadAll('anon')` (line 116) — always the unscoped key `sf_custom_content`. But every write while signed in goes to the uid-scoped key via ownerIdFromState (localWrite at lines 140/150/167/185, scopedLocalKey lines 25-29). Consequences: (1) migrateLocalCustomContentToCloud (lines 242-253) reads flattenLocalContent(ownerId) — the uid-scoped key, which is empty on first premium sign-in because the 'grandfathered localStorage items' the header (lines 13-15) promises to migrate live under the unscoped/anon key. items.length === 0, so localStorage.setItem(flag,'1') runs (line 251) and permanently blocks any future migration; while signed in, loadCustomContentFromCloud then replaces state with cloud-only content (line 222-226), so the grandfathered items vanish from the UI for that account forever. (2) The 'mirror to local for offline read-only access' write (lines 227-228) writes to the uid key, but no code path ever reads the uid key on boot (loadAll('anon')) and an offline cloud load just fails — the offline mirror is dead. The service-layer comment in src/lib/customContent.js:9-10 ('Free users with grandfathered local items still load through localList()') only holds while signed out.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make migrateLocalCustomContentToCloud read BOTH the anon key and the uid key (grandfathered content lives under the anon key); only set the migrated flag after a successful insert. On boot/sign-in, hydrate from the owner-scoped key (falling back to anon) so the offline mirror is actually used.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual code and its callers. The uid-scoping refactor broke the grandfathered-items migration that the original code (commit 68463c9) handled correctly.

Boot hydration: src/store/customContentSlice.js:116 is `customContent: loadAll('anon')`, which is hardcoded to the unscoped key `sf_custom_content` (scopedLocalKey returns LOCAL_KEY for 'anon', lines 25-29). customContent is NOT in the zustand persist partialize list (src/store/index.js:63-74), so this slice-init read is the only boot hydration path. Grandfathered items (per the header at lines 9-15, items created before the gate) provably live under this unscoped key: in the pre-gate code (git show 68463c9:src/store/customContentSlice.js) every localWrite targeted the unscoped LOCAL_KEY = 'sf_custom_content'.

Migration regression: migrateLocalCustomContentToCloud (lines 242-253) now reads flattenLocalContent(ownerId) -> localLoad(ownerId) -> scopedLocalKey(uid) = the uid-scoped key `sf_custom_content:<uid>`, which is empty on first premium sign-in because grandfathered items are under the anon key. So items.length === 0, localStorage.setItem(flag,'1') runs (line 251) and permanently poisons the per-uid migrated flag. The original version read customContentService.readLocalForMigration() which reads the unscoped key (src/lib/customContent.js:188-198, localLoadAll -> 'sf_custom_content') — so the refactor is a genuine regression. App.jsx:219-220 then chains loadCustomContentFromCloud(), which does a full replace `state.customContent = merged` (line 223, no merge with the in-memory grandfathered items), so the grandfathered items disappear from the premium UI and are never pushed to cloud.

Offline mirror is dead: loadCustomContentFromCloud writes the mirror to the uid key (line 228, localWrite(..., ownerId)), but boot only ever reads the anon key (line 116), and the offline-failure catch path (lines 229-235) only sets customContentError — it never falls back to reading the uid mirror. So the mirror is write-only.

The existing test (tests/store/customContentSlice.cache.test.js:40-48) even sets up the exact dual-key state (an 'Anon Hall' under sf_custom_content plus an 'A Hall' under sf_custom_content:user_a), confirming the two keys hold distinct data; the anon->cloud migration source is not covered by any test. Reachable on the production path (Supabase configured + premium/elevated). Not permanent localStorage data loss (items survive in the anon key and reappear on sign-out via clearCloudCustomContent -> loadAll()), and no money/security/crash — but the central feature promise (migrate local items once on premium sign-in) is broken and the poison-flag makes it unrecoverable on retry. Medium is the honest severity.
verdicts.fix_note: The reviewer's fix is correct: migrateLocalCustomContentToCloud should read BOTH the anon key and the uid key and union them (grandfathered content is under the anon key). Note one nuance to the reviewer's 'only set the flag after a successful insert' wording: when BOTH keys are genuinely empty you still want to set the flag to avoid re-running every sign-in — so gate flag-setting on 'no items found in either key OR insert succeeded', not strictly 'insert succeeded'. Separately, to make the offline mirror real, boot/sign-in hydration should read the owner-scoped key (falling back to anon), and loadCustomContentFromCloud's catch path should hydrate from the uid mirror instead of leaving state empty.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED end-to-end with reproduction against the real source helpers.

Key-mismatch is real and load-bearing:
- Boot hydration: customContentSlice.js:116 `customContent: loadAll('anon')` -> scopedLocalKey('anon') = `sf_custom_content` (unscoped). (lines 25-29, 93-96)
- Signed-in writes go to the uid-scoped key `sf_custom_content:<uid>` via localWrite(..., ownerIdFromState(state)) at lines 140/150/167/185 and the cloud mirror at 228. The existing test tests/store/customContentSlice.cache.test.js:35-37 independently asserts signed-in writes land under `sf_custom_content:user_a` and that `sf_custom_content` stays null — i.e. the anon key is the grandfathered store and is never touched by signed-in writes.
- migrateLocalCustomContentToCloud reads flattenLocalContent(ownerId) at line 249 where ownerId = the uid (ownerIdFromState, line 245). On a first premium sign-in the uid key is empty (only premium/elevated users ever write it; a first sign-in hasn't yet), while the grandfathered items the header (lines 13-15) and service comment (customContent.js:9-10) promise to migrate live under `sf_custom_content`.

Failure trace (first premium sign-in with grandfathered items), reproduced:
1. Boot loadAll('anon') hydrates the grandfathered items into state (repro: count 1/2; UI shows them).
2. App.jsx:219-220 runs migrate() then loadCloud() on the premium transition. At that moment auth.user.id is populated (set by the same setAuth/initAuth that set tier), so ownerId is the real uid.
3. migrate(): flattenLocalContent(uid) reads `sf_custom_content:<uid>` = empty -> items.length===0 -> localStorage.setItem(flag,'1') (line 251) and returns; no bulkInsert. (repro: bulkInsert batches 0, flag set). Verified deterministically against the exact source helpers: migrate reads `sf_custom_content:user_a` (empty) while boot read `sf_custom_content` (1 item).
4. loadCustomContentFromCloud(): builds merged purely from cloud list() + EMPTY (lines 219-220) and unconditionally REPLACES state.customContent = merged (line 223). For a new premium account cloud is empty, so the boot-hydrated grandfathered items are wiped from the UI. (repro: post-sync count 0.)
5. The migrated flag (sf_custom_content_migrated:<uid>) is now permanently '1' -> migrate() early-returns forever on that device/account (line 247). The grandfathered data still sits on disk under `sf_custom_content` (repro confirms intact) but is permanently orphaned and invisible to the premium account.

Dead offline mirror sub-claim also confirmed: the uid key is written (lines 140/150/167/185/228) but grep shows NO reader ever loads `sf_custom_content:<uid>` into state. Both state-populating readers use the anon key: loadAll('anon') at boot (line 116) and loadAll() (default 'anon') in clearCloudCustomContent (line 278). flattenLocalContent(uid) at line 249 is the lone uid-key reader and it only gates migration, never populates state. So on an offline reboot, boot reads the anon key and loadCustomContentFromCloud throws (offline) leaving the anon content; the uid mirror is never consulted (repro test 2: offline boot count 0 despite a populated uid mirror).

customContent is NOT in store/index.js persist.partialize (lines 63-74), so localStorage via these keys is the sole persistence — confirming there is no other path that would resurrect the items.

Could not run vitest (local env broken: missing @rollup/rollup-darwin-arm64 native binary; the .bin/vitest shim also lacks exec permission), so the repro was run as a standalone node script that mirrors the exact slice helpers (scopedLocalKey/migrationFlag/localLoad/flattenLocalContent/loadAll) byte-for-byte; output matched the predicted failure on every line.

Severity high (not critical): no money/security/main-path crash, and the bytes survive on disk under the anon key, but it is a broken billing-adjacent feature with silent user-visible data disappearance for paying (premium) users plus a permanent flag that blocks any future fix on that device — a real, durable defect, not an edge case.
verdicts.fix_note:
The report does not propose a concrete fix; here is the corrected fix with two non-obvious hazards the naive fix misses:

1) Migration source must be the anon key, not the uid key. In migrateLocalCustomContentToCloud, read `flattenLocalContent('anon')` (the grandfathered `sf_custom_content`), not `flattenLocalContent(ownerId)`. Keep the per-uid flag so it stays once-per-account.

2) Do NOT blindly clear the anon key after migrating. `sf_custom_content` is a single shared key across ALL accounts on a device (scopedLocalKey('anon') === LOCAL_KEY). If account A migrates and deletes it, account B (also grandfathered, same browser) loses their items before they ever sign in. Safer: leave the anon key intact (idempotency is already protected by the per-uid flag), or move/namespace it only after confirming no other account still needs it. At minimum, gate any clear on "this is the only path that reads it."

3) The dead offline mirror needs a real reader. Either (a) on the premium transition, seed state from the uid mirror first (loadAll(uid)) before attempting the cloud fetch, and only replace on a SUCCESSFUL list(); or (b) make loadCustomContentFromCloud's catch fall back to localLoad(uid) instead of leaving the anon content. As written, line 223 hard-replaces state with cloud-only data even when that erases local content, and the catch branch (229-235) never restores the mirror. A merge/fallback that prefers cloud-on-success but falls back to the uid mirror on failure fixes both the offline-read and the don't-clobber-local concerns.

4) Independent of migration, line 223's unconditional `state.customContent = merged` is what makes the grandfathered items vanish even when migration is fixed but races: if loadCloud runs before/without a successful migrate, it still wipes boot-hydrated anon items. Ensure migrate completes (and its bulkInsert lands in cloud) before loadCloud replaces state — App.jsx already chains .then(), but migrate currently no-ops, so once migrate actually inserts, the chained loadCloud will correctly pull them back.

confirmed.title: Deleting a campaign on one device resurrects on every other device and is re-uploaded to the cloud (no deletion tombstone in the merge/sync protocol)
confirmed.evidence: campaigns delete is a hard row delete (src/lib/campaigns.js:149-152) plus local forgetCampaignSync on the deleting device only (campaignSlice.js deletePersistedCampaign, lines 122-130). mergeCampaignLists (campaignSync.js:45-93) merges the local cache and cloud rows purely by presence/updatedAt with no tombstone concept, so any OTHER device that still has the campaign in its sf_campaigns cache re-adds it on loadCampaigns. Worse, loadCampaigns (campaignSlice.js:401-414) primes lastSyncedSignatures from the REMOTE list only (primeCampaignSync(migratedRemote), line 404), then runs syncCampaignChanges(merged) as a 'backfill' (line 411) — the resurrected campaign has no signature entry, fails the needs-sync check (campaignSync.js:123), and is upserted straight back into saved_maps. The module header (lines 5-7) explicitly intends local-only campaigns to survive cloud loads, but that policy makes cross-device deletion impossible to make stick: delete on device A, open device B, campaign is back in the cloud for both.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add deletion reconciliation: either soft-delete (access_state='deleted' tombstone rows that mergeCampaignLists treats as authoritative wins) or track per-device 'known-synced' ids so a campaign that was previously synced but is now absent from the cloud is removed locally instead of re-uploaded.

verdicts.reasoning:
I attempted to refute this and could not. Every cited line behaves as described, and the project's own test suite encodes the exact broken behavior.

DELETE PATH (verified): deleteCampaign (campaignSlice.js:466-473) removes the campaign from state.campaigns (line 470), then calls deletePersistedCampaignState -> deletePersistedCampaign (lines 122-130), which (a) localWrite's the now-shrunk list to THIS device's cache, (b) forgetCampaignSync(id) clears the local signature, and (c) campaignService.delete(id). The cloud delete is a HARD row delete: supabaseDelete (campaigns.js:149-152) = saved_maps.delete().eq('id', id). No tombstone is written. I confirmed via grep that the only access_state values are 'active'/'inactive_plan'/'pending_delete' (migration 024:216), all server-set for billing retention — a user delete writes none of them; it just removes the row.

NO TOMBSTONE IN MERGE (verified): mergeCampaignLists (campaignSync.js:45-93) unions local (rank 1) and remote (rank 2) purely by id presence and campaignUpdatedAtMs, with remote access fields overlaid as authoritative. A campaign present in local but absent from remote is always kept (it lands in byId from the local pass and is never removed). There is no notion of a deleted/absent-from-cloud campaign being dropped.

DEVICE-B RESURRECTION + RE-UPLOAD (verified end-to-end): loadCampaigns (campaignSlice.js:393-421): cached = device B's stale local cache (still contains the campaign, since localStorage is per-device); remote = cloud list (no longer contains it). primeCampaignSync(migratedRemote) (line 404) clears lastSyncedSignatures and primes ONLY from remote, so the resurrected campaign id has NO signature entry. merged = mergeCampaignLists(cached, migratedRemote) (line 405) re-includes it. localWrite(merged) (line 410) re-persists it on B. Then syncCampaignChanges(merged) (line 411) -> getCampaignsNeedingSync: accessState 'active' passes (line 120), changedId null so no id filter, and lastSyncedSignatures.get(id) is undefined !== signature => needs sync (line 123) => service.upsert re-inserts it into saved_maps (campaigns.js:130-141). RLS does not block this: the INSERT policy with check (auth.uid()=user_id and access_state='active') (migration 024:262) is satisfied for the same authenticated user with default access_state='active'. So the deletion is undone for BOTH the local cache and the cloud.

CONFIRMING TESTS: tests/lib/campaignSync.test.js proves these primitives are designed to do exactly this. 'merge keeps local-only campaigns when remote campaigns load' (lines 11-18) keeps a campaign that is in local but absent from cloud. 'sync uploads only records that differ from the primed cloud snapshot' (lines 54-80) shows a 'local-only' campaign (primed from a cloud snapshot lacking it) is flagged needing-sync and uploaded. A device-A-deleted campaign is, from device B's view, indistinguishable from such a local-only campaign. The module header (campaignSync.js:4-7) states the intent — never erase a local-only campaign not yet uploaded — which is precisely the policy that makes cross-device deletion impossible to make stick.
