The defect is only reachable when an operator sets the OWNER_EMAIL env var to a different email whose profiles.role is 'user' — i.e. exactly the configurable-owner case the feature was built to support. No test pins the intended behavior. Given fail-closed semantics and config-gated reachability, severity is low.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested framing is accurate. For the fix, prefer aligning the two layers rather than just documenting: (1) the cleanest option that respects the architecture rule "RLS/DB is the security spine" is to remove the pure-email owner bypass from the edge gate for the two MUTATING actions (require profiles.role elevation for update_user_metadata/update_user_credits), and rely on migration 018 already promoting the canonical owner to role='admin'. This makes read and write actions consistent. (2) Alternatively, teach the DB to honor the owner override: have _assert_service_admin_actor also accept the actor when their profiles.email matches an owner allowlist — but the env-configured OWNER_EMAIL lives only in the edge runtime, not in Postgres, so this would require threading the owner email into the DB (e.g. a settings table or passing an explicit owner_override flag the edge function sets only after its own gate). Note also a latent inconsistency to flag while here: index.ts OWNER_EMAIL fallback is clausellstokes@aol.com while migration 018 hardcodes the same address, so the canonical-owner case is covered, but any operator who changes OWNER_EMAIL without also updating migration 018's role-promotion will hit exactly this bug. Whichever path is chosen, add an edge-function contract test (tests/edgeFunctions/contracts.test.js already exists) asserting that an OWNER_EMAIL caller with role='user' can either perform all four actions or none — pin the intended contract so this can't silently regress.

confirmed.title: admin-actions list_users interpolates raw search into a PostgREST .or() filter
confirmed.evidence: `query.or(`email.ilike.%${rawSearch}%,display_name.ilike.%${rawSearch}%`)` (line 245) splices caller-supplied metadata.search (rawSearch, line 234) directly into a PostgREST logical-filter string. Commas, parentheses, or operator tokens in rawSearch can break out of the intended ilike clauses and alter the filter (PostgREST or() filter injection). Impact is limited because the caller is already developer/admin and the query uses the service-role client (which bypasses RLS and can already read every profile), so there is no privilege escalation — worst case is a malformed query or reading columns the actor can already read. Still a real un-sanitized-input seam in an admin endpoint.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Sanitize/escape rawSearch (reject or strip PostgREST metacharacters , ( ) . *) or build the filter with parameterized .ilike() calls combined via the array form of .or() rather than string interpolation.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed by reading supabase/functions/admin-actions/index.ts. Line 245 interpolates caller-supplied input into a PostgREST logical-filter string: `query.or(`email.ilike.%${rawSearch}%,display_name.ilike.%${rawSearch}%`)`, where `rawSearch` is `metadata.search` only `.trim()`-ed (lines 234-236) with no escaping. supabase-js `.or(filterString)` passes the string verbatim as the `or=(...)` query param and does NOT escape commas/parentheses/operator tokens inside it, so values like `email.ilike.%x%,role.eq.admin` or `()`-grouped clauses break out of the two intended ilike clauses and alter the filter structure — a genuine PostgREST or() filter-injection seam. The path is reachable: the generic invoke path (src/components/AdminPanel.jsx:70 -> supabase.functions.invoke('admin-actions', { body })) lets any authenticated caller send `{ action: 'list_users', metadata: { search } }`, and the switch routes it to this branch with no sanitization upstream (only the role gate). The claim's impact bounding is also correct and is why severity stays low: the upstream guard (lines 162-178) requires role developer/admin or OWNER_EMAIL before reaching the switch, and the query uses adminClient (service-role, line 163) which bypasses RLS and can already read the entire profiles table by design. PostgREST or() filters operate on the queried resource's own columns and .select(\"*\") embeds no other tables, so there is no cross-table reach or privilege escalation — worst case is a malformed query returned as a 500, or reordering filters over profile rows/columns the actor is already authorized to read. So: real un-sanitized-input defect, no security/money/data-loss/crash-on-main-path consequence. Could not refute it; severity low is honest.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound. Note the identical pattern also exists client-side in src/components/AdminPanel.jsx:213 (query.or with interpolated searchQuery against profiles directly) — same low-impact injection seam, worth fixing in the same pass. Preferred remediation for the edge function: avoid string interpolation; either reject/strip PostgREST metacharacters (, ( ) . *) from rawSearch, or double-quote the interpolated value within the ilike pattern so commas/parens are treated literally, or use two separate .ilike() calls combined via the array form of .or().

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed the code at supabase/functions/admin-actions/index.ts:245: `query.or(`email.ilike.%${rawSearch}%,display_name.ilike.%${rawSearch}%`)` interpolates caller-supplied `metadata.search` (rawSearch, line 234-236) directly into a PostgREST `or()` logical-filter string. supabase-js renders this as the query param `or=(email.ilike.%PAYLOAD%,display_name.ilike.%PAYLOAD%)`, and commas/parentheses/operator tokens in PAYLOAD are structurally significant to PostgREST's or() grammar. So a payload like `x%,role.eq.admin,a.ilike.%y` injects an extra OR-term and `x))` triggers a parse error. This is a genuine un-sanitized-input / PostgREST or()-filter-injection seam, exactly as described.

However, the concrete failure path produces no real harm, which is why this is low and not higher:
1. Authz gating (lines 156, 173-178) means the caller is already JWT-authenticated AND role=developer/admin or the owner email — i.e. already privileged.
2. The query uses the service-role `adminClient` (line 163) which bypasses RLS, and already does `.select("*")`, so the actor can already read every column of every `profiles` row. Manipulating the or() filter only changes which rows match — no column/table/row becomes readable that wasn't already, no write, no privilege escalation.
3. PostgREST or() filters are scoped to the `profiles` resource columns; the grammar cannot reach other tables or mutate data. The worst structural outcome is a malformed query returning a 400 to the same privileged caller, or a logically-altered but still profiles-only result set.
4. Trigger reality: the current UI (src/components/AdminPanel.jsx:207-229) never invokes the `list_users` edge action at all — it queries `profiles` directly. So this is a dormant endpoint with no user-facing path; only a hand-crafted authenticated admin request reaches line 245.

Verdict: real defect, an unsanitized-input hardening seam in an admin endpoint, but self-limited exactly as the reporter notes — no data loss, money, privilege escalation, or main-path crash. Low severity.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested direction (sanitize input) is correct, but "escape" is the wrong frame: supabase-js provides no value-escaping for `.or()` filter strings, so manual handling is required. Robust fixes: (a) strip/reject PostgREST-significant characters from rawSearch before interpolation — at minimum `,` `(` `)` `:` and ideally the LIKE metacharacters `%` and `\` (and `*`) so the wildcard pattern can't be subverted; reject with a 400 if disallowed chars are present rather than silently stripping; or (b) avoid the logical-filter string entirely by routing the search through a parameterized RPC. Note the identical raw-interpolation pattern also exists client-side at src/components/AdminPanel.jsx:213 — that one runs under the user's own RLS-constrained client so it is likewise not an escalation, but if you fix one for cleanliness, fix both. Also consider validating that `metadata` is a record for the list_users case (currently only optional-chained), though that is cosmetic since `metadata?.search` already guards safely.

confirmed.title: stripe-webhook uses synchronous constructEvent in a Deno runtime (may fail signature verification at runtime)
confirmed.evidence: Signature verification uses the SYNCHRONOUS `stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(body, signature, webhookSecret)` (line 177) with the esm.sh stripe@14.14.0?target=deno build. In Supabase Edge (Deno) the documented/portable pattern is `await stripe.webhooks.constructEventAsync(body, signature, secret, undefined, Stripe.createSubtleCryptoProvider())`, because the SubtleCrypto provider cannot run in a synchronous context; the sync call can throw 'SubtleCryptoProvider cannot be used in a synchronous context.' If that applies to this runtime, EVERY webhook hits the catch at lines 178-181 and returns 400 — paying customers are charged by Stripe but never receive credits / premium / founder grants (crown-jewel breakage). CAVEAT: Deno's node:crypto compat may give the Stripe SDK a synchronous HMAC provider, in which case this works fine; I could not execute the function to confirm. Flagging for runtime verification given the severity if real.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Switch to `await stripe.webhooks.constructEventAsync(body, signature, webhookSecret, undefined, Stripe.createSubtleCryptoProvider())`, and add a smoke/integration test that posts a correctly-signed event and asserts a 200 + state mutation.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED by reading the actual Deno build source. supabase/functions/stripe-webhook/index.ts:177 calls the SYNCHRONOUS `stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(body, signature, webhookSecret)` against the `https://esm.sh/stripe@14.14.0?target=deno` build (index.ts:23). I fetched and de-minified that exact build (resolves to /stripe@14.14.0/deno/stripe.mjs) and traced the call chain decisively:

1. The Deno platform-functions class (Ge extends we) treats Deno as a non-Node environment: `createNodeCryptoProvider(){throw new Error("...not available in non-Node environments. Please use createSubtleCryptoProvider() instead.")}` and `createDefaultCryptoProvider(){return this.createSubtleCryptoProvider()}`. So there is NO synchronous Node HMAC provider in this build.
2. SubtleCryptoProvider (class Ce): `computeHMACSignature(){throw new j("SubtleCryptoProvider cannot be used in a synchronous context.")}` — the sync method unconditionally throws (j extends Error).
3. `constructEvent(a,c,d){try{this.signature.verifyHeader(a,c,d,...)}catch(v){throw v instanceof j&&(v.message+="\nUse `await constructEventAsync(...)` instead of `constructEvent(...)`"),v}...}` → `verifyHeader` with no provider arg → `m=m||h()` where `h(){return u||(u=r.createDefaultCryptoProvider())}` → SubtleCryptoProvider → `m.computeHMACSignature(...)` → throws.

This REFUTES the reviewer's own CAVEAT: the `?target=deno` build does not give the SDK a synchronous Node HMAC provider — it deliberately disables it. The thrown error is caught at index.ts:178-181 and returns HTTP 400 for EVERY webhook (checkout.session.completed, invoice.paid, invoice.payment_succeeded, customer.subscription.deleted). The trust-boundary comment at line 143 itself relies on constructEvent succeeding.

I verified there is no escape hatch: index.ts:25 constructs `new Stripe(key, { apiVersion })` with no httpClient/crypto-provider override; there is no import_map.json/deno.json; supabase/config.toml does not remap the stripe import. The contract tests (tests/edgeFunctions/contracts.test.js:93,1088) only regex the SOURCE TEXT (`/constructEvent(Async)?\s*\(/`) and never execute the function, so they cannot catch this runtime failure — and they even already accept the Async form, indicating the codebase expects it.

Net effect: customers are charged by Stripe but never receive credits/premium/founder grants, and subscription deletions never downgrade — a crown-jewel billing breakage on the main money path. Could not execute live (no deno binary in env), but the build source is unambiguous; the only way it wouldn't fire is if these endpoints were never invoked, which is false on a live billing path.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct. Use `event = await stripe.webhooks.constructEventAsync(body, signature, webhookSecret, undefined, Stripe.createSubtleCryptoProvider())` (the async path: verifyHeaderAsync → computeHMACSignatureAsync exists and works in this build). The same defect exists in any other edge function that verifies Stripe webhook signatures synchronously — only stripe-webhook/index.ts does signature verification here (create-checkout, verify-single-dossier, create-customer-portal use the SDK for API calls, not constructEvent, so they are unaffected). Critically, the existing contract tests are source-text-only and will NOT catch a regression; add a real integration smoke test that posts a correctly-signed event (using stripe.webhooks.generateTestHeaderString with createSubtleCryptoProvider) and asserts HTTP 200 plus the expected state mutation. Severity raised from the reviewer's 'high' to 'critical': this is silent loss of paid revenue / failure to deliver purchased goods on the primary billing path, which fits the 'money' critical bar.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via end-to-end trace + live execution against the exact pinned module.

Failure path: Stripe POSTs any webhook (e.g. checkout.session.completed after a real payment) to supabase/functions/stripe-webhook/index.ts. At line 177 the handler calls the SYNCHRONOUS stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(body, signature, webhookSecret) with no cryptoProvider argument. The import at index.ts:23 is `https://esm.sh/stripe@14.14.0?target=deno`.

I verified each link of the chain against the real stripe@14.14.0 source AND the live esm.sh deno bundle:
1. esm.sh ?target=deno resolves to /stripe@14.14.0/deno/stripe.mjs (confirmed by curling esm.sh live). package.json `exports.deno` maps to esm/stripe.esm.worker.js.
2. The worker/deno entry instantiates `new WebPlatformFunctions()` (stripe.esm.worker.js).
3. WebPlatformFunctions.createDefaultCryptoProvider() returns createSubtleCryptoProvider() i.e. a SubtleCryptoProvider. Confirmed in both the local package (esm/platform/WebPlatformFunctions.js:35-36) and the live served bundle (`createDefaultCryptoProvider(){return this.createSubtleCryptoProvider()}`).
4. Webhooks.constructEvent (sync) -> verifyHeader -> getCryptoProvider() (since no provider passed) -> the SubtleCryptoProvider -> cryptoProvider.computeHMACSignature(...).
5. SubtleCryptoProvider.computeHMACSignature UNCONDITIONALLY throws CryptoProviderOnlySupportsAsyncError('SubtleCryptoProvider cannot be used in a synchronous context.'). Confirmed in local source and live bundle (`computeHMACSignature(t,o){throw new j("SubtleCryptoProvider cannot be used in a synchronous context.")}`).
6. The throw is caught at index.ts:178-181 -> returns 400 'Invalid signature' for EVERY event, including valid ones.

I executed the real worker-build modules in Node with a GENUINELY VALID Stripe signature: the sync constructEvent threw CryptoProviderOnlySupportsAsyncError, while constructEventAsync(body, header, secret, undefined, provider) returned the event successfully. So a correctly-signed Stripe webhook is rejected with 400 purely due to the sync vs async crypto path.

Consequence (crown-jewel billing breakage on the main path): no case in the switch (index.ts:185-300) ever executes. Paying customers are charged by Stripe but never receive credits (credit pack 'purchase'), premium tier upgrade, founder_lifetime grant + badge + 30-credit bonus, or monthly_allowance (invoice.paid). customer.subscription.deleted never downgrades. Stripe marks the endpoint failing and retries, but every retry also 400s. Money in, nothing delivered.

The claim's own caveat (Deno node:crypto compat might supply a sync HMAC provider) is DISPROVEN: provider selection is fixed at module-load time by export-condition resolution to WebPlatformFunctions; the deno bundle never instantiates NodePlatformFunctions or consults node:crypto for the default provider, so the runtime's crypto capabilities cannot change the outcome.

Note: the repo's contract tests (tests/edgeFunctions/contracts.test.js) only assert the SOURCE TEXT matches /constructEvent(Async)?\s*\(/ — they do not execute the function in a Deno runtime, so they pass with the broken sync form and provide no protection here.

Not rated 1.0 confidence solely because I could not exercise the actual Supabase Edge (Deno Deploy) runtime, only Node + the byte-identical served module; but since provider selection is load-time and crypto-feature-independent, runtime variance cannot rescue it.
verdicts.fix_note:
Suggested fix is correct. Replace index.ts:177 with the async form, e.g.:

  event = await stripe.webhooks.constructEventAsync(body, signature, webhookSecret, undefined, Stripe.createSubtleCryptoProvider());

Verified details:
- `Stripe` is the default import (index.ts:23) and `createSubtleCryptoProvider` is a STATIC on the Stripe class (confirmed in stripe.core.js:140-141: `Stripe.createSubtleCryptoProvider = platformFunctions.createSubtleCryptoProvider`), so `Stripe.createSubtleCryptoProvider()` is valid.
- The 4th positional arg is `tolerance`; passing `undefined` preserves the SDK default (300s). The 5th is the cryptoProvider. I confirmed `constructEventAsync(body, header, secret, undefined, provider)` returns the event with a valid signature.
- The function body is already `async (req) => {...}`, so adding `await` requires no signature change.
- Strictly, because the deno build's default provider already IS the SubtleCryptoProvider, even `await constructEventAsync(body, signature, webhookSecret)` (no explicit provider) would work; but pass the explicit `Stripe.createSubtleCryptoProvider()` to match Stripe's official Supabase Edge guidance and be robust to default-provider changes.
- Update the trust-boundary comment at index.ts:143 (which says 'via stripe.webhooks.constructEvent') to reference constructEventAsync. The contract test regex already allows constructEventAsync, so it will still pass; but consider adding a real runtime test (or a regex that forbids the bare sync `constructEvent(` form) since the existing source-text assertions did not catch this.
- The other three functions (create-checkout, verify-single-dossier, create-customer-portal) use the same ?target=deno import but only call Stripe REST methods over fetch, not constructEvent, so they are unaffected and need no change.

confirmed.title: refund_credits lets any user reclaim credits for successful AI spends (free/unlimited generations)
confirmed.evidence: refund_credits(spend_ledger_row uuid, refund_reason text) is SECURITY DEFINER and `grant execute ... to authenticated` (line 363). Its only authorization is line 309 `if spend_row.user_id <> auth.uid() and not is_admin then raise 'not authorized'` — i.e. the OWNER of any spend row may refund it — plus an idempotency guard (line 315-321) that only blocks refunding the SAME spend twice. There is NO check that the spend corresponded to a failed generation. The function inserts a fresh `grant` ledger row of `spend_row.amount` and increments `profiles.credits`, fully reversing the spend. This is reachable directly by clients: AI spends are created via the user's JWT (supabase/functions/generate-narrative/index.ts:2006 `supabaseUser.rpc('spend_credits', ...)`), credit_ledger has a `Users read own ledger` SELECT policy (007), and refund_credits is a PostgREST RPC granted to `authenticated` (the edge function itself calls it as the user at generate-narrative/index.ts:2076). So a paying user can: run AI features normally, `select id from credit_ledger where kind='spend'`, then call rpc('refund_credits',{spend_ledger_row:id}) for each — keeping every generated artifact while getting all credits back. A single 30-credit purchase becomes infinite AI usage. refund_credits is defined only in 009 and never revoked/redefined, so this is the net state.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Refunds of successful spends must not be a user capability. Either (a) revoke refund_credits from `authenticated` and only grant to service_role, having the edge function call it after verifying the generation actually failed; or (b) add a server-tracked 'refundable/failed' flag on the spend row that spend_credits sets false and only a verified-failure path sets true, and gate refund_credits on it. At minimum require is_admin OR a server-set failure marker rather than mere ownership.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the full attack chain against the actual code; every cited premise holds.

REACHABILITY: supabase/migrations/009_profile_security.sql:363 grants execute on refund_credits(uuid,text) to `authenticated`. I grepped all 32 migrations: refund_credits is defined ONLY in 009 and is never revoked, dropped, or redefined (no `revoke ... refund_credits` / `drop function ... refund_credits` anywhere). It is SECURITY DEFINER, so PostgREST exposes it as an RPC any authenticated user can call with their own JWT. Confirmed the edge functions invoke it via `supabaseUser` (a client built from the anon key + the user's Authorization header — generate-narrative/index.ts:2076 and generate-chronicle/index.ts:102, client built at chronicle 76-80), i.e. it runs under the user's JWT, proving direct client reachability.

SPEND IDs ARE DISCOVERABLE: 007_credit_ledger.sql:97-100 `Users read own ledger` SELECT policy (using auth.uid()=user_id) lets a user enumerate `select id from credit_ledger where kind='spend'`. The current spend_credits (024_billing_retention_and_atomic_mutations.sql:166-167, the latest redefinition) inserts spend rows with kind='spend', so they pass the kind gate.

AUTHORIZATION IS OWNERSHIP-ONLY: refund_credits' only auth check is 009:309 `if spend_row.user_id <> auth.uid() and not is_admin then raise`. is_admin comes from current_user_is_privileged() (018:30-48), which is true only for role developer/admin or one hardcoded email — a normal free/premium user is NOT privileged, so the check reduces to "I own this spend," always true for the user's own spends.

NO SUCCESS/FAILURE GATE: I read the entire function body (009:284-361). The only guards are: authenticated (295-297), spend row exists and kind='spend' (303-305), owner-or-admin (309-311), and idempotency — not-already-refunded via metadata->>'refund_of' (315-321). There is NO column, flag, or check tying the refund to a failed generation. The `reversed_by` column (007:44) exists but is unused by the function. The function then inserts a fresh grant row of spend_row.amount (324-334) and increments profiles.credits (341-345), fully reversing a successful spend.

COMPOUNDING DEFECT: the latest spend_credits (024) tracks funding via credit_spend_allocations (FIFO), but refund_credits was never updated to reconcile those allocations, so the refunded credits are fully re-spendable. This strengthens, not weakens, the finding.

Net: a paying user runs AI features, reads own spend ids, calls rpc('refund_credits', {spend_ledger_row: id}) per spend, keeps every generated artifact, and recovers all credits — turning any finite purchase into unlimited AI usage. This is money/abuse-grade on the main billing path. Could not refute it.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct in direction; option (a) (revoke from authenticated, grant only to service_role, edge function refunds only on verified failure) is the clean fix. Important implementation caveat the reviewer did not flag: the edge functions currently call refund_credits as `supabaseUser` (the user's JWT), so merely flipping the grant to service_role will break the legitimate mid-stream refund path — the edge functions must switch to a service-role client for the refund call and pass the verified user/spend. Also note that since spend_credits (024) now allocates against grants via credit_spend_allocations, the refund path should additionally reverse/void those allocations, otherwise even a legitimate refund leaves the allocation ledger inconsistent.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end. refund_credits (supabase/migrations/009_profile_security.sql:284-363) is SECURITY DEFINER and granted to `authenticated` (009:363). Its authorization is exactly: auth.uid() not null (009:295), row exists and kind='spend' (009:303), `spend_row.user_id <> auth.uid() and not is_admin` (009:309 — so the OWNER passes), and a one-time idempotency guard keyed on metadata->>'refund_of' (009:315-321). There is NO check that the corresponding generation failed. On success it inserts a fresh `grant` of spend_row.amount and bumps profiles.credits (009:324-345), fully reversing the spend.

Every link in the exploit chain is present and reachable:
- spend_credits (009:193, granted to authenticated 009:273) inserts the user-owned `kind='spend'` row with user_id=auth.uid() (009:257-259). generate-narrative calls it as the user (supabase/functions/generate-narrative/index.ts:2006).
- credit_ledger has a `Users read own ledger` SELECT policy `using (auth.uid() = user_id)` (supabase/migrations/007_credit_ledger.sql:97-100), so the user can enumerate their own spend row ids.
- refund_credits is a PostgREST RPC the client can call directly; the edge function itself invokes it as the user via supabaseUser.rpc (generate-narrative/index.ts:2076; also generate-chronicle/index.ts:102).
- There is no user-facing INSERT policy on credit_ledger; the SECURITY DEFINER RPC bypasses RLS to write the refund grant, which is precisely the vector.

current_user_is_privileged is role-based (defined in 018_account_billing_models_credits.sql:30) and irrelevant here — ownership alone authorizes the refund. The function's own comment (009:467 "User- or admin-callable ... Idempotent") confirms the design only ever intended idempotency as the guard, not failure-conditioning. I verified refund_credits is defined ONLY in 009 and never revoked or redefined in any later migration (grep across all of supabase/migrations and src). Notably migration 017_fix_credit_auth_integrity.sql — the explicit trust-boundary audit-repair migration — touched welcome-credit triggers, admin_actions.actor_id, custom_content gating, and service-role admin RPCs, but left refund_credits untouched. So 009 is the net production state.

Concrete failure path: a paying user buys N credits, runs AI features (each spend completes and the artifact is kept/saved), then does `select id from credit_ledger where kind='spend'` and calls rpc('refund_credits',{spend_ledger_row:id}) once per spend. Each call passes all guards and returns the full amount. Net result: 100% of spent credits recovered while keeping every generated artifact — a single 30-credit purchase becomes unlimited AI usage. This is a direct billing/revenue-integrity bypass with no admin involvement, attacker-controlled via the public RPC surface. Severity critical (money).
verdicts.fix_note:
The proposed fix (require the spend to correspond to a failed generation) is the right direction but needs a concrete enforcement mechanism, because the DB currently has no record of generation outcome. Two viable approaches:

1) Make the refund a server-only operation. The legitimate caller is the edge function (generate-narrative / generate-chronicle), which knows the generation failed. Change `grant execute on function public.refund_credits(uuid, text)` from `authenticated` to `service_role` and have the edge functions call it via the service-role client (supabaseAdmin) rather than supabaseUser. Add an actor/service-role assertion inside the function (mirror `_assert_service_admin_actor` from 017) so a stolen anon/authenticated JWT can never reach it. This closes the hole completely and matches the existing service-role admin-RPC pattern (017:236-447). Note: generate-narrative/index.ts:2076 and generate-chronicle/index.ts:102 currently use supabaseUser for the refund and must be switched to the admin client.

2) If self-service refund must stay user-callable, add a real failure gate: record generation outcome on the spend row (e.g. a `status` / `refundable` column or a `metadata.refundable=true` flag set only when the edge function detects failure) and have refund_credits require it. The current idempotency check is necessary but not sufficient — it only blocks double-refund, not refund-of-success.

Either way, also revoke direct execute from `public`/`authenticated` so the RPC is not part of the anonymous/authenticated PostgREST surface. Approach (1) is simpler and consistent with the codebase's existing service-role trust-boundary pattern.

confirmed.title: Public gallery RPC leaks DM-facing NPC fields: server sanitizer denylists NPC keys while the intended projection is an allowlist
confirmed.evidence: _gallery_sanitize_public_json (020, net-state still used by get_gallery_dossier in 032:131/152) sanitizes NPCs with a DENYLIST: a key regex (line 38) plus, only when 'npcs' is in the path, dropping just `goal,secret,plotHooks,relationships` (lines 41-45). Every other NPC key survives. The product's authoritative projection, src/domain/display/publicSafe.js toPublicSafe() (lines 105-118), instead reduces each NPC to an explicit ALLOWLIST of ~11 fields (id,name,role,title,category,personality,physical,factionAffiliation,secondaryAffiliation,presentation,influence). publicSafe.js's own header (lines 9-13) states the SERVER (get_gallery_dossier) is the security boundary and the client is only defense-in-depth — yet the server is strictly MORE permissive than the client. Generated NPCs (src/generators/npcGenerator.js:72-98) always carry `gender` and `power`, and enriched NPCs gain `structuralPosition,activeConstraint,settlementCondition,structuralRank` (src/generators/npcStructure.js:430-437; documented on the NPC shape in src/domain/settlement.schema.js:354 and read by src/domain/npcProfile.js:371). None of these match the denylist regex or the npcs-path key list, so anonymous callers of get_gallery_dossier receive them even though the intended public view drops them. The crown-jewel fields (secret, goal, plotHooks) are stripped, so impact is moderate (DM strategic-analysis fields + power score), but the boundary leaks fields it is meant to exclude and any future NPC field leaks automatically.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the server NPC projection an allowlist mirroring toPublicSafe (keep only the same ~11 fields) instead of a denylist, or add the missing keys to the denylist; add a parity test asserting get_gallery_dossier output is a subset of toPublicSafe output for the same row.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified all load-bearing claims by reading the cited code and its callers/callees.

1. Path is live, not dead. get_gallery_dossier is redefined through migration 032 (latest). Its default (non-gallery_share_dm) branch at 032:130 calls public._gallery_sanitize_public_json(base.j) — the exact denylist function defined in 020:19-68. Granted to anon at 032:170. So the cited 020 sanitizer is the one anonymous callers hit.

2. The server NPC handling is a DENYLIST, confirmed at 020:37-45: a key regex (line 38) plus, only when 'npcs' is in path, dropping exactly goal/secret/plotHooks/relationships (lines 41-45). Recursion passes every other key.

3. The leaked fields exist and survive. Programmatically tested gender, power, structuralPosition, activeConstraint, settlementCondition, structuralRank against the 020 regex AND the npcs-path key list — all return false on both, so they pass through. Confirmed they are generated: gender (npcGenerator.js:75), power (npcGenerator.js:96), and structuralPosition/activeConstraint/settlementCondition/structuralRank added by enrichNPCsWithStructure (npcStructure.js:433-436). These are DM-facing: activeConstraint holds strategic-analysis prose (npcStructure.js:381), and structuralPosition/structuralRank are documented on the NPC shape (settlement.schema.js:354) and read by npcProfile.js:371.

4. The authoritative client projection toPublicSafe (publicSafe.js:105-118) IS an explicit allowlist of ~11 NPC fields that drops exactly these leaked fields. The header (lines 9-13) declares the SERVER the security boundary and the client mere defense-in-depth — yet the server is strictly MORE permissive than the client. The crown jewels (secret/goal/plotHooks) ARE stripped server-side, matching the claim's 'moderate impact' framing.

Refutation attempts that FAILED to kill it:
- Downstream guard: the client re-runs toPublicSafe(row.data,{full:false}) in sanitizeDossier (gallery.js:406), which DOES strip these fields before render. This masks the leak through the app UI. BUT the RPC is granted directly to anon (032:170), and the project's own stated boundary is the server, so an attacker calling the RPC directly bypasses the client and receives the leaked fields. This downstream guard mutes UI impact but does not refute the boundary defect.
- Intentional/tested: the contract test (tests/security/gallery_privacy.contract.test.js) gives the CHRONICLE path an exact-allowlist assertion (lines 97-107) but never does the same for NPCs, and no test asserts server⊆client parity. So the divergence is unguarded, not a tested design choice.

Conclusion: the defect is real. The server public projection leaks DM-facing NPC fields (structuralPosition, activeConstraint, settlementCondition, structuralRank) plus the power score (gender is benign) to anonymous direct RPC callers, contradicting publicSafe.js's stated server-is-the-boundary guarantee, and any future NPC field leaks automatically. Crown-jewel fields are stripped and the in-app UI re-strips client-side, so severity is medium, not high.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound. Prefer converting the server NPC projection to an allowlist mirroring toPublicSafe (publicSafe.js:105-118) rather than merely extending the denylist, because the denylist will silently leak any future NPC field again — the same failure mode that produced this bug. Concretely: in _gallery_sanitize_public_json, when 'npcs' is in the path on an NPC object, jsonb_build_object only the ~11 allowlisted keys (id,name,role,title,category,personality,physical,factionAffiliation,secondaryAffiliation,presentation,influence) instead of the current key-by-key denylist filter. Add a contract/parity test in the same spirit as the chronicle exact-allowlist test (gallery_privacy.contract.test.js:97-107): assert the SQL NPC projection's built key set equals toPublicSafe's NPC allowlist, so server and client cannot drift. Note the client gallery.js:406 already re-strips via toPublicSafe(full:false) for UI rendering, so the user-visible fix is primarily for direct anon RPC callers and the stated server-is-the-boundary guarantee — worth fixing but not a UI-breaking change.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED as a real boundary defect, with the impact correctly characterized as moderate-but-not-severe.

Structural facts (all verified by reading the files):
- supabase/migrations/020_gallery_public_privacy.sql:19-68 defines `_gallery_sanitize_public_json` as a DENYLIST: a key regex (line 38) plus, only when 'npcs' is in the path, dropping `goal,secret,plotHooks,relationships` (lines 41-45). This is the SOLE definition; `grep` over all migrations shows it is never redefined.
- The live `get_gallery_dossier` is the migration-032 version (032_gallery_public_chronicle.sql:88-167). On the default (non-shareDm) path it returns `data = public._gallery_sanitize_public_json(base.j)` (032:130) — i.e. the 020 denylist. RPC is `grant execute ... to authenticated, anon` (032:169-170).
- The authoritative client projection src/domain/display/publicSafe.js toPublicSafe() (lines 105-118) reduces each NPC to an explicit ALLOWLIST of ~11 fields; its header (lines 9-13) declares the SERVER (get_gallery_dossier) the security boundary and the client mere defense-in-depth.

Field-level confirmation (reproduced in node against the regex and the npcs-path list, and by reimplementing the SQL sanitizer's exact path mechanics on a realistic NPC object): `secret`, `plotHooks`, `goal` are correctly dropped; but `gender`, `power`, `structuralPosition`, `activeConstraint`, `settlementCondition`, `structuralRank` all SURVIVE. These are real, always/typically-present NPC fields: npcGenerator.js:75 (gender) and :96 (power) are set unconditionally; npcStructure.js:430-437 adds the four structural fields on enriched NPCs; settlement.schema.js:354 documents them on the NPC shape. So the server returns DM-strategic-analysis fields the intended public view excludes — and any future NPC field leaks automatically. The claim's structural and field-level analysis is accurate end-to-end.

Why LOW (not the claimant's implied moderate):
1. The crown-jewel fields (secret, goal, plotHooks, relationships) ARE stripped. The leaked set is a numeric power score (1-10), a gender string, and four structural-analysis strings — DM-flavor, not secrets/credentials/PII/money.
2. The in-app blast radius is masked: gallery.js:406 re-applies `toPublicSafe(row.data, {full: gallery_share_dm})` before render, so in the normal UI flow these fields never reach the DOM. The leak is real ONLY at the network/API layer (a direct anon RPC call via PostgREST/curl sees the extra JSON keys). That is still a genuine defect because the product explicitly designates the server as the security boundary and the client as defense-in-depth, and here the boundary is strictly MORE permissive than the layer that is supposed to be redundant — a real inverted-trust seam. But the practical exposure is low-sensitivity data over an already-public dossier.
3. Asymmetry corroboration: tests/domain/display/publicSafe.test.js exercises the client allowlist; there is no test over the SQL sanitizer (migrations are outside vitest), which is exactly why the server denylist drifted while the client stayed correct.

Concrete failure path: owner publishes a non-shareDm dossier -> anon caller hits get_gallery_dossier(slug) -> 032:130 runs the 020 denylist -> response data.npcs[i] carries power/gender/structuralPosition/activeConstraint/settlementCondition/structuralRank that toPublicSafe would have dropped -> visible to anyone reading the raw API response (not the in-app render). isReal=true, severity low.
verdicts.fix_note:
The claimant's suggested direction (make the server NPC projection an allowlist to mirror toPublicSafe) is correct and is the right fix. Concrete corrections/additions:

1. Fix location: the change must be inside `_gallery_sanitize_public_json` in migration 020 (or, better, a NEW migration 033 that `create or replace`s it — do not edit shipped migrations in place if they may already be applied in prod). Don't try to fix it in get_gallery_dossier; the denylist is the shared helper used by the default public path.

2. Replace the path-conditional NPC denylist (020:41-45) with an explicit allowlist matching publicSafe.js:106-118: when iterating an object whose path contains 'npcs', build the output from only {id,name,role,title,category,personality,physical,factionAffiliation,secondaryAffiliation,presentation,influence} (recursing into the kept object children so the regex denylist still applies within personality/physical). Then it is impossible for new NPC fields to leak by default.

3. Watch the path-detection subtlety: in the SQL recursion, when you are iterating the KEYS of an NPC object, `path` ends with 'npcs' (arrays recurse with the SAME path, then the object appends each key). So gate the allowlist on `'npcs' = any(path)` exactly as the current code already does for the denylist — that condition already fires at the correct level (verified by reproducing the path mechanics). Apply the allowlist BEFORE recursing.

4. Also mirror the client allowlist's `.filter(npc => npc.name || npc.role)` if you want strict parity, though that is cosmetic.

5. Add a SQL-level or pgTAP test (or at minimum an edge/validate check) asserting that get_gallery_dossier output for a known NPC contains ONLY the allowlisted keys — the absence of any server-side test is what let this drift; a client-only publicSafe.test.js is insufficient because the product declares the server the boundary.

6. Leave the shareDm (full) path and the chronicle column untouched — both already use allowlists/explicit reveals and are not part of this defect.

confirmed.title: 'Developers update any profile' UPDATE policy has USING but no WITH CHECK — unaudited privileged self-mutation
confirmed.evidence: Net-state policy (created 005:54-55, never dropped by 018 which only replaced the user-facing policies): `CREATE POLICY "Developers update any profile" ON public.profiles FOR UPDATE USING (public.current_user_is_privileged())` with no WITH CHECK. For UPDATE policies Postgres falls back to the USING expression as the check, and that expression does not reference the row, so a developer/admin can set ANY column on ANY profile to ANY value — including role='admin', credits=99999, is_founder=true, tier='premium' — directly via a table UPDATE, completely bypassing the audited SECURITY DEFINER RPCs (admin_set_role, admin_grant_credits, service_set_credits) that 009/017 built specifically so every privileged write lands in admin_actions. Non-privileged users are unaffected (they only satisfy the 018 'safe preferences only' policy whose WITH CHECK locks protected columns). Impact is confined to already-privileged accounts (audit-trail bypass + developer→admin self-promotion), hence low.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Drop this policy and route all privileged profile writes through the audited RPCs, or add an explicit WITH CHECK and keep the policy read-only-shaped; if direct dev writes are desired, still funnel through an audited path.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against the actual migrations. The policy at supabase/migrations/005_fix_profiles_rls_recursion.sql:54-55 is `CREATE POLICY "Developers update any profile" ON public.profiles FOR UPDATE USING (public.current_user_is_privileged())` with no WITH CHECK. A full grep across all 32 migrations confirms the policy name appears only in 003 (create) and 005 (drop+recreate); it is NEVER dropped or replaced afterward — so it remains live net-state. Migration 018:50-51 drops only the two user-facing policies ("Users update own profile (display_name only)" and "Users update own profile") and creates "Users update own profile (safe preferences only)" with a column-locking WITH CHECK (018:57-64); it does not touch the dev policy. The Postgres semantics claim is correct: for an UPDATE policy with USING but no WITH CHECK, Postgres uses the USING expression as the new-row check. `current_user_is_privileged()` (005:16-28) checks only the CURRENT user's role via auth.uid() and references no column of the target row, so the new-row check is satisfied regardless of what columns the UPDATE sets. A developer/admin can therefore set any column (role, credits, tier, is_founder) on any profile row directly, bypassing the audited SECURITY DEFINER RPCs. The audited path is confirmed as the deliberate design: 009's header (009:20-28) states RPCs are "the ONLY paths users have to affect protected columns" with admin_actions auditing, and admin_set_role (009:441-452) does the UPDATE plus _audit_action — exactly the audit trail the standing RLS policy circumvents. Impact is genuinely confined to already-privileged accounts (audit-trail bypass + developer self-promotion to admin), since non-privileged users are still blocked by 018's WITH CHECK. Two honest caveats that keep severity low: (a) exploitation requires direct table-level UPDATE access (e.g. authenticated PostgREST/SQL), not the normal RPC-based UI path; (b) the actor must already be privileged. But RLS is meant to be the security boundary independent of client behavior, and the policy directly contradicts the 009 "RPCs are the only path" design, so the audit-bypass and dev->admin self-escalation are real, not theoretical. Could not refute.
verdicts.fix_note: The reviewer's suggested fix is sound. Concretely: in a new migration, DROP POLICY IF EXISTS "Developers update any profile" ON public.profiles. If privileged users genuinely need to read all profiles, that is already covered by the separate "Developers read all profiles" SELECT policy (005:51-52) — the UPDATE policy is the only audit hole. All privileged writes already have audited RPC equivalents (admin_set_role 009:423, admin_grant_credits 009:369, service_set_credits 017:368), so dropping the UPDATE policy does not remove any needed capability; it just forces those writes through the audited path. If for some reason a direct-write escape hatch must remain, add a WITH CHECK that pins protected columns (mirroring the is-not-distinct-from pattern in 018:59-64) so it cannot be used for column tampering.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end against the real migrations. Net-state RLS on public.profiles after migration 032 has exactly two PERMISSIVE UPDATE policies: (1) "Users update own profile (safe preferences only)" created in 018:53-64 with USING auth.uid()=id and a strict WITH CHECK locking role/tier/credits/is_founder/stripe_customer_id/email; and (2) "Developers update any profile" created in 005:54-55 with USING public.current_user_is_privileged() and NO WITH CHECK. The policy name "Developers update any profile" appears only in 003 (recursive, dropped by 005:35) and 005 (recreated) — grep across all migrations shows no later DROP, and 018 only drops/recreates the two USER-facing self-update policy names (018:50-51,53). So policy (2) survives verbatim.

Postgres semantics make the bypass real: for UPDATE, permissive policies' USING clauses OR-combine for row visibility and WITH CHECK clauses OR-combine for the new row; a policy with no WITH CHECK uses its USING expression as the implicit check. For a developer/admin (current_user_is_privileged()=true): policy (2)'s USING grants visibility on ANY row (including other users'); policy (2)'s implicit WITH CHECK is current_user_is_privileged(), which does not reference the new row, so it passes for ANY column values; because WITH CHECK is OR-combined, this defeats policy (1)'s column-locking check. There are zero RESTRICTIVE policies anywhere (verified), so nothing ANDs-in a constraint. No table-level REVOKE on profiles exists, so the standard Supabase authenticated grant permits the raw PostgREST UPDATE, gated only by RLS.

Concrete failure path: a developer runs supabase.from('profiles').update({ role:'admin', credits:99999, is_founder:true, tier:'premium' }).eq('id', anyId) and it succeeds, completely bypassing the audited SECURITY DEFINER RPCs admin_set_role/admin_grant_credits/service_set_credits (009:369-458, 017:368-447) that every privileged write is supposed to go through, so nothing is written to admin_actions. App code itself correctly routes privileged writes through the RPCs (e.g., supabase/functions/admin-actions/index.ts:265 calls service_set_credits), which underscores that the raw-table path is an unintended hole, not the design. Impact is confined to already-privileged accounts but covers developer->admin self-escalation (a real privilege-tier jump), self/other free-credit grants (money), founder/premium grants, and full audit-trail evasion — hence medium rather than low.
verdicts.fix_note: The implied fix (add WITH CHECK (public.current_user_is_privileged())) is INSUFFICIENT: that check still does not constrain which columns/values the new row may have, so a developer could still set role/credits/is_founder on any row AND the write would still bypass admin_actions auditing — the core defect (unaudited privileged self-mutation + dev->admin escalation) remains. Correct remediation: DROP the "Developers update any profile" policy outright. Privileged column writes already have audited RPC paths (admin_set_role, admin_grant_credits, service_set_credits), so developers/admins do not need raw FOR UPDATE table access to profiles at all. If some narrow developer table-update need genuinely exists, replace the policy with one whose WITH CHECK pins the protected columns (role/tier/credits/is_founder/stripe_customer_id/email) to their prior values exactly as the user policy does, forcing all privileged changes to those columns through the audited RPCs. Also recommend an explicit REVOKE UPDATE ON public.profiles FROM authenticated as defense-in-depth so direct table writes can never reach RLS for protected columns.

confirmed.title: sync_profile_email SECURITY DEFINER trigger function not search_path-pinned
confirmed.evidence: sync_profile_email() (003:51-57) is `LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER` with no `SET search_path`, unlike every other SECURITY DEFINER function in the schema (005/007/009/017/etc all pin `set search_path = public`). It is wired as an AFTER UPDATE OF email trigger on auth.users (003:65-68) and is not superseded by any later migration, so it remains net state. Without a pinned search_path the function resolves unqualified operators/objects via the caller's session search_path, the exact pattern Supabase's function_search_path_mutable linter flags. The body uses a schema-qualified UPDATE on public.profiles which limits practical exploitability (an attacker would need to create shadowing objects in a schema ahead of public, which ordinary Supabase roles cannot), so severity is low — but it is a real hardening gap on a trigger attached to the auth schema.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `SET search_path = public` (and `pg_temp` last) to sync_profile_email, matching the other SECURITY DEFINER functions.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. supabase/migrations/003_admin_rls_policies.sql:51-57 defines public.sync_profile_email() as `LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER` with no `SET search_path`, and 003:65-67 wires it as an `AFTER UPDATE OF email ON auth.users` FOR EACH ROW trigger. grep confirms the function is defined exactly once and is never re-created, ALTERed, or dropped by any later migration, so it remains net schema state.

The comparative claim holds in net-state terms: every other SECURITY DEFINER function in the schema pins `set search_path = public` (007:73-74, 009:117-118/140-141/etc, 017:33-34/83-84/etc, 018, 020-030). The strongest corroboration is migration 017: it does `create or replace function public.handle_new_user()` (017:30) and explicitly adds `set search_path = public` (017:34) — i.e., the project ran a deliberate hardening pass that pinned search_path on the OTHER auth.users SECURITY DEFINER trigger function but missed sync_profile_email. (The reviewer's phrasing "every other ... all pin" is slightly loose because the ORIGINAL 001 handle_new_user was also unpinned, but 017 superseded it; sync_profile_email has no such successor.)

This matches Supabase's function_search_path_mutable linter exactly. Exploitability is genuinely low, as the reviewer concedes: the body schema-qualifies public.profiles and uses NEW.email, so an attacker would need to create shadowing objects in a schema ahead of public, which ordinary Supabase roles cannot. So it is a real, correctly-scoped hardening gap rather than a live security hole — net-state defect confirmed, severity low is honest.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct. Recreate with `LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER SET search_path = public` (optionally append pg_temp). Worth doing in the same migration that already pins the sibling handle_new_user (the 017-style pattern), since both are auth.users SECURITY DEFINER triggers and should be treated identically.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed against the source. public.sync_profile_email() at supabase/migrations/003_admin_rls_policies.sql:51-57 is declared `LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER` with NO `SET search_path`. It is the sole exception: every other SECURITY DEFINER function in the schema pins `set search_path = public` (005:21 current_user_is_privileged; plus 008, 009 x6, 010, 011, 012, 020, 029 — all verified via grep). The function is wired as an `AFTER UPDATE OF email ON auth.users` trigger (003:65-68) and is net state: it is defined only in 003, never re-created or ALTER FUNCTION'd to add a pin, and no later migration supersedes it (verified by grepping every .sql file). So the deviation from the project's own consistently-applied hardening convention is real, and it is exactly the pattern Supabase's function_search_path_mutable linter flags.

However, tracing the concrete failure path shows no plausible exploit today: (1) The trigger fires inside GoTrue's own email-change transaction — no application or edge-function code updates auth.users.email directly (confirmed via grep of src/ and supabase/functions/), so the attacker does NOT control the execution-context search_path; it is GoTrue's role's default. (2) The body's only object reference is the schema-qualified `UPDATE public.profiles ... WHERE id = NEW.id`, eliminating table-shadowing; only the `=` operator / implicit cast resolution is theoretically search_path-dependent. (3) Exploiting that would require planting a shadowing operator/object in a schema that resolves before public, which requires schema/object-creation privileges that ordinary Supabase roles (anon/authenticated) do not have — and no migration grants CREATE on schemas to those roles. Thus it is a genuine hardening gap and convention violation, but not a concretely exploitable or reachable defect. Low severity is honest, matching the claim's own assessment.
verdicts.fix_note: Proposed fix (pin SET search_path) is correct and matches the project's established convention. Add `SET search_path = public` to the function definition before the `AS`/body. Two refinements: (a) Because the body is already fully schema-qualified, the stricter `SET search_path = ''` (Supabase's recommended form) would also work and additionally neutralizes pg_temp injection; the project standard of `= public` is acceptable for consistency. (b) Since the function uses CREATE OR REPLACE and the trigger is created only if absent, re-running migration 003 with the added pin is idempotent and safe; alternatively ship a small new migration with `ALTER FUNCTION public.sync_profile_email() SET search_path = public;` to pin it in-place without re-defining. Severity does not warrant urgency; bundle with other linter-hardening cleanup.

confirmed.title: Per-tier save limit is enforced non-atomically (count-then-insert TOCTOU race)
confirmed.evidence: enforce_save_limit() (net state in 024:273-316, originally 014) does `select count(*) ... where user_id = new.user_id and access_state='active'` (lines 307-309) in a BEFORE INSERT trigger and rejects when count >= limit, but takes no lock that serializes concurrent inserts for the same user. Two simultaneous INSERTs (or two concurrent mutate_settlement_batch creates) both read count=2 against a free limit of 3, both pass the check, and both commit — yielding 4+ active settlements for a free user. The limit is still enforced against sequential abuse (this closes the original direct-upsert bypass the migration targeted), but the cap is exceedable by a few rows under deliberate concurrency.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Serialize per-user save accounting, e.g. `select count(*) ... for update` against a locking row, an advisory lock on user_id (pg_advisory_xact_lock), or a partial unique/exclusion constraint that bounds active rows per user.

verdicts.reasoning: The claim holds up against scrutiny. enforce_save_limit() (body redefined in 024:273-316, trigger created in 014:95-98 as BEFORE INSERT FOR EACH ROW on public.settlements) enforces the free-tier cap of 3 via a plain `select count(*) ... where user_id = new.user_id and access_state = 'active'` (024:307-309), then rejects if count >= limit (024:310). I verified there is NO serializing mechanism: no `for update` on the count, no pg_advisory_xact_lock, and no partial-unique or exclusion constraint anywhere in the migrations or the settlements table DDL (001:28-40 has only the PK and an FK; grep across all migrations found no such constraint). Under PostgreSQL's default READ COMMITTED isolation this is a genuine TOCTOU race: two concurrent transactions each count the pre-insert state (neither sees the other's uncommitted row, and count(*) takes no predicate lock that would block the sibling insert), both pass the check, both commit — yielding 4+ active rows for a free user. The path is reachable: authenticated users call mutate_settlement_batch (024:462) whose create loop (497-509) inserts with no per-user lock, and the RLS insert policy (024:261-262) also allows direct inserts. I tried to refute three ways and failed: (1) no hidden bounding constraint exists; (2) the `for update` at 024:491 only row-locks settlements being UPDATED, not a per-user accounting row, so it does not serialize concurrent creates; (3) the nearby comments (024:553-554) describe batch atomicity but never waive the count race as intentional. Severity is correctly low: exploitable only under deliberate concurrency, damage is bounded (a few extra active settlements, no money/data-loss/auth-bypass/crash), and the migration's primary goal — closing the direct-upsert and serial-abuse bypass — is still achieved.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fixes are sound. The cleanest robust fix is a transaction-scoped advisory lock keyed on the user at the top of the count branch, e.g. `perform pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtextextended(new.user_id::text, 0));` placed just before the count(*) at 024:307 — this serializes all concurrent inserts for the same user without a schema change and works regardless of which entry point (trigger via RLS insert or via mutate_settlement_batch) fired the insert. An exclusion constraint is harder here because the cap is N rows (not 1), so a partial unique constraint cannot express "at most 3 active per user" directly; the advisory lock is the most practical option. If chosen, ensure the same lock key is acquired by mutate_settlement_batch's create path so the trigger and any future direct-RPC accounting agree on the serialization point.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed real TOCTOU race. enforce_save_limit() (024:273-316) is a BEFORE INSERT trigger whose only gate is `select count(*) ... where user_id = new.user_id and access_state='active'` (024:307-309), evaluated with no serializing lock. The trigger from 014 (settlements_enforce_save_limit, created 014:95-98) remains attached because 024 only does `create or replace function`, never re-dropping/re-creating the trigger or adding any constraint.

Concrete failure path: a free user (tier='free', save_limit=3 per 024:301) with exactly 2 active settlements fires two near-simultaneous inserts — either two direct supabase.from('settlements').insert(row) calls (src/lib/saves.js:186-190) or two mutate_settlement_batch RPC creates (024:497-509). Under PostgreSQL's default READ COMMITTED isolation, each trigger's plain SELECT count(*) takes no lock and sees a snapshot in which the other transaction's uncommitted INSERT is invisible. Both read count=2, both evaluate 2>=3 as false (024:310), both pass, both commit. There is no unique constraint or partial index on settlements(user_id) — only the non-unique idx_settlements_user (001:42) — so the storage layer accepts the 4th row. Result: 4 active settlements for a free user capped at 3.

The authors clearly knew the serialization technique: the same migration uses `for update` to serialize concurrent ops at 024:153, 024:184, and 024:491; the save-count check is the one place a lock is omitted.

Impact is bounded and narrow: no data loss, crash, or auth bypass (the auth.uid() cross-check at 024:288 still holds; cross-user inserts still rejected). The migration's stated goal — blocking the sequential client-side upsert bypass — is fully met; only deliberate concurrency exceeds the cap, by a handful of rows. This is a minor quota/monetization leak requiring intentional parallel requests, not normal single-threaded UI usage. Hence medium, not high/critical. I did not run a live DB repro (no Postgres instance available in this read-only sandbox), but the mechanism is standard READ COMMITTED phantom behavior and the code path is unambiguous.
verdicts.fix_note: The fix direction (serialize same-user concurrent inserts) is correct, but do NOT implement it as `SELECT count(*) ... FOR UPDATE`: FOR UPDATE locks only existing matched rows and does not prevent a phantom new INSERT under READ COMMITTED, so the race would survive. Robust fixes: (1) take a transaction-scoped advisory lock keyed on the user at the top of enforce_save_limit() before counting, e.g. `perform pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtextextended(new.user_id::text, 0));` — this serializes all same-user inserts, makes the count accurate, auto-releases at commit, and lets different users proceed without contention; or (2) lock the profile row first, reusing the existing read: `select tier, role into user_tier, user_role from public.profiles where id = new.user_id for update;` (replacing the lock-free select at 024:285-286), which serializes per-user at the cost of contending with profile updates. A storage-level guarantee (per-user slot counter with CHECK, or a partial unique index on a 1..N ordinal column) would also close it but is a larger change. Note the same race exists for the batch path because mutate_settlement_batch's per-row INSERTs (024:499) fire the same trigger; an advisory lock inside the trigger covers both the direct-insert and batch-RPC entry points in one place.

confirmed.title: version_history has no server-side size bound (unbounded row growth)
confirmed.evidence: 016 adds settlements.version_history jsonb and documents it as 'Capped at 50 entries client-side to bound row size' (line 37-38) — the cap exists only in the client. The settlements UPDATE policy (024:264-266) and mutate_settlement_batch (024:533 writes `version_history = ... item->'version_history'` straight from client input) let an owner store an arbitrarily long array, each entry a full settlement clone (~5-30 kB per the migration comment). A user can therefore inflate a single row to many MB of TOASTed jsonb, and the same data also rides along on every read of that settlement. No cross-user impact, so low severity, but the documented invariant is unenforced server-side.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Enforce the 50-entry cap server-side: a CHECK on jsonb_array_length(version_history), or trim in mutate_settlement_batch / a BEFORE UPDATE trigger, so the bound does not depend on a cooperative client.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim holds up under direct reading of the cited code and all server-side write paths.

1. supabase/migrations/016_version_history.sql:34-38 adds `settlements.version_history jsonb` and the column comment explicitly documents the invariant "Capped at 50 entries client-side to bound row size." The migration itself adds no CHECK constraint, no trigger, no length bound.

2. The cap is genuinely client-only: src/store/settlementSlice.js:85 `const MAX_VERSION_HISTORY = 50` and :132 `cappedVersionHistory` does `history.slice(-MAX_VERSION_HISTORY)`. src/lib/saves.js (lines 66, 183, 207) only checks `Array.isArray(...)` before persisting — never array length. A modified/malicious client (or direct PostgREST call) bypasses this entirely.

3. Server-side write paths store the array verbatim with no bound:
   - RLS UPDATE policy (024:264-266) only enforces `auth.uid() = user_id and access_state = 'active'` in USING and WITH CHECK — no length predicate.
   - mutate_settlement_batch (security definer) writes `version_history = item->'version_history'` straight from client input on both the create path (024:506) and the update path (024:533), with no jsonb_array_length check or trim.

4. No refuting guard exists anywhere: the only `jsonb_array_length`-based trim in the migration tree is in 032 for gallery public-chronicle entries, unrelated to version_history. Triggers on settlements are set_updated_at (BEFORE UPDATE, timestamp only) and enforce_save_limit (BEFORE INSERT, row-count only) — neither bounds version_history size. No CHECK constraint on the column.

So an authenticated owner can store an arbitrarily long array, each entry a full settlement clone (~5-30 kB per the migration comment), inflating one TOASTed jsonb row to many MB, which also rides along on every read of that settlement. The documented invariant is unenforced server-side, exactly as claimed.

Severity is correctly assessed as low: the write is gated to the row's own owner (auth.uid() = user_id), so there is no cross-user impact, no privilege escalation, and no money/auth-security exposure — it is a self-inflicted storage-bloat / unenforced-invariant defect, not a critical one.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound. Two practical notes: (1) A pure CHECK on jsonb_array_length(version_history) <= 50 would reject the whole UPDATE (raising an error the client must handle) rather than silently trimming — fine, but it changes failure semantics. (2) Trimming in the RPC is cleaner UX: in mutate_settlement_batch, on both the create (024:506) and update (024:533) paths, wrap the value with a server-side cap, e.g. take the last 50 elements via `(select jsonb_agg(e) from (select e from jsonb_array_elements(item->'version_history') with ordinality t(e, ord) order by ord desc limit 50) ...)` reversed, or simpler: keep the tail. Also bound entry/byte size, since 50 entries x 30 kB is still ~1.5 MB; consider an additional pg_column_size guard if total-row-size is the real concern. Note the direct PostgREST UPDATE path (RLS policy) is separate from the RPC, so a CHECK constraint (which covers all write paths) is the more complete defense than RPC-only trimming.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end. The documented invariant ("Capped at 50 entries client-side to bound row size", supabase/migrations/016_version_history.sql:37-38) exists ONLY in client code and is enforced nowhere server-side.

Client cap (the only enforcement): src/store/settlementSlice.js:85 `MAX_VERSION_HISTORY = 50` and :131-133 `cappedVersionHistory()` = `history.slice(-MAX_VERSION_HISTORY)`, applied at :399, :407, :457 when snapshots are appended. Each snapshot is a full deep clone of the settlement: src/store/settlementSlice.js:391 `settlement: JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(sourceSettlement))`.

Server write paths — none validate length or byte size of the array:
- PostgREST direct: src/lib/saves.js:65-67 `mutationRow` copies `entry.versionHistory` through verbatim; written via `.insert(row)` (saves.js:188) and `.update(updates)` (saves.js:213, fed by :207). A caller can also bypass the JS client entirely and POST any array to the REST endpoint.
- RPC batch: src/lib/saves.js:240 calls `mutate_settlement_batch`; in 024_billing_retention_and_atomic_mutations.sql the INSERT branch (:506) and the UPDATE branch (:533 `version_history = case when item ? 'version_history' then item->'version_history' else version_history end`) store the client-supplied array straight to the column.

RLS only gates ownership + active state: the UPDATE policy at 024:264-266 and INSERT at 024:261-262 check `auth.uid() = user_id and access_state = 'active'`, nothing about content. The only triggers on settlements are `set_updated_at` (001:96) and `enforce_save_limit` (014:95, BEFORE INSERT, counts rows not bytes). No CHECK constraint or trigger references version_history (grep over all migrations confirms: only references are the migration that adds the column and the two write sites in 024). There is no jsonb_array_length guard anywhere on this column.

Concrete failure path: an authenticated owner (or anyone with the user's JWT hitting PostgREST/the RPC directly, ignoring the client) sends a `version_history` array of arbitrary length — e.g. thousands of entries, each a ~5-30 kB settlement clone per the migration comment — for one of their own active rows. The server accepts it. Result: a single settlement row inflates to many MB of TOASTed jsonb. The column is in the standard SELECT list (saves.js:131), so the bloated array is re-fetched on every read of that settlement (load list, open detail, etc.), inflating read payload/latency for that user. It is self-inflicted and scoped to the attacker's own rows — no cross-user data exposure, no money, no auth bypass, no crash on the main path — hence low severity. But the claim is accurate: a documented size invariant is unenforced server-side.

Severity low is correct: blast radius is the owner's own rows and their own read latency / their share of DB storage; it does not corrupt other users, leak data, or break determinism/billing. It is a real defect (unenforced documented invariant + abuse-of-own-account storage growth), not not-a-bug.
verdicts.fix_note:
Suggested fix direction is sound (enforce the bound server-side) with refinements:

1. Best enforcement point is a BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE trigger on public.settlements (a CHECK constraint cannot easily express "keep last 50" and a plain length CHECK would hard-reject rather than self-heal). The trigger should clamp, not error, to match client semantics and avoid breaking legitimate saves: e.g. if jsonb_typeof(NEW.version_history) = 'array' and jsonb_array_length(NEW.version_history) > 50 then keep only the last 50 elements (NEW.version_history := (select jsonb_agg(e) from (select e from jsonb_array_elements(NEW.version_history) with ordinality t(e, ord) order by ord desc limit 50) z ...) reversed to preserve order). Migration 032:70 already uses the same "keep last N via ordinality" pattern for the public_chronicle column — mirror it for consistency.

2. A pure length cap (50) does NOT bound bytes: each entry is a full settlement clone, so 50 large settlements can still be several MB. If the real goal is bounding row size (the migration's stated rationale), also consider an upper byte guard, e.g. reject or trim when pg_column_size(NEW.version_history) exceeds a threshold (say a few MB). At minimum, document that the 50-entry cap bounds count, not absolute bytes.

3. Apply the trigger on BOTH INSERT and UPDATE — the create path (024:506) is currently unguarded too, and saves.js:188 inserts version_history directly.

4. The trigger must run regardless of write path (PostgREST and the SECURITY DEFINER RPC), so a row-level trigger is the right layer; do not rely on patching mutationRow/mutate_settlement_batch alone since a raw PostgREST call bypasses the JS client entirely.

confirmed.title: Object/array-shaped settlementReason passed raw to <Text> in Overview chapter — crashes or garbles the whole PDF export
confirmed.evidence: overviewSlice passes `settlementReason: s?.settlementReason || null` with no coercion (viewModel.js:387) and Overview.jsx:283-287 renders `{o.settlementReason}` directly as a Text child. The field is documented and handled elsewhere as `string | string[] | { primary }`: the same file's historySlice coerces it (viewModel.js:822-824 `s?.settlementReason?.primary`), the web guards both shapes (OverviewTab.jsx:201-204 Array.isArray branch + `r.settlementReason?.primary||r.settlementReason`), and supabase/functions/generate-narrative/index.ts:529-560 explicitly preserves the array/object shape on the AI clone ('settlementReason can be string | string[] | { primary: string, ... }'). A plain-object child throws React's 'Objects are not valid as a React child' inside the react-pdf reconciler, failing the entire export; an array renders concatenated with no separators.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Coerce in overviewSlice exactly like historySlice: string passes through, arrays join with newlines/spaces, objects use `.primary`. Add a fixture with object-shaped settlementReason to tests/pdf/sections.smoke.test.js.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified by reading the cited code and by runtime probes against this repo's @react-pdf/renderer ^4.5.1.

CONFIRMED FACTS:
- viewModel.js:387 passes `settlementReason: s?.settlementReason || null` with no coercion; Overview.jsx:286 renders `{o.settlementReason}` directly as a <Text> child. No upstream guard exists in the PDF path (unlike the web OverviewTab.jsx:201-203 and the same file's historySlice viewModel.js:822-824, both of which coerce).
- The engine ALWAYS emits an array: generateSettlementReason (narrativeGenerator.js:964-1009) does `const lines = []; ... return lines`. Runtime probe across tiers: metropolis/city/hamlet/thorp -> 2-element array; town/village -> 1-element array. This array is assigned to the settlement (generateNarratives.js:42 -> assembleSettlement.js:46,74) and flows unchanged into overviewSlice.
- Empirical react-pdf render test (renderToString in this repo): STRING child renders OK; ARRAY child renders OK but with NO separator between elements (sentences glued together); OBJECT {primary} child THROWS "Objects are not valid as a React child" in the reconciler and crashes the whole render.

So the defect is real, but the title is partly mischaracterized:
1. The CRASH path (object {primary}) is currently NOT reachable — no code anywhere assigns an object-shaped settlementReason; it is only consumed defensively (historySlice, web, edge function). It is a documented-but-not-produced future shape. So "crashes the whole PDF export" is latent, not a present main-path crash.
2. The ARRAY path IS reached on every generation and produces garbled run-together output for 4 of 6 tiers (the multi-sentence case), while the web renders each element as a separate paragraph. That is genuine, currently-occurring PDF/dossier parity drift — a flagged bug class for this product.

The suggested fix (coerce in overviewSlice exactly like historySlice: string passthrough, array join, object .primary) is correct and addresses both the present array-garbling and the latent object-crash.
verdicts.fix_note:
The title's framing ("crashes or garbles the whole PDF") conflates two cases with very different reachability. Be precise: (a) ARRAY shape is the engine's ALWAYS-emitted shape and currently renders WITHOUT crashing but concatenates multi-sentence reasons with no separator (4 of 6 tiers: metropolis/city/hamlet/thorp are 2-element) — this is the real present-day defect (parity drift vs web, which renders each array element as its own

). (b) OBJECT {primary} shape WOULD crash the export with "Objects are not valid as a React child", but no current code path produces it — it is defensive-only/future. So this is medium (wrong-but-non-crashing output on a common path + a latent crash guard), not high (no determinism break, no money/security, no current crash). The proposed coercion in overviewSlice is the right fix; to match web semantics for arrays, join with a separator that reads as paragraph breaks rather than a single space (e.g. '\n' or ' '), and add a fixture covering BOTH the multi-element array shape (current reality) and the object shape (latent) to tests/pdf/sections.smoke.test.js — and note that the existing smoke test does not exercise the Overview chapter at all.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the full data path and by executing against the real @react-pdf/renderer v4.5.1. Data path: generateSettlementReason ALWAYS returns string[] (src/generators/narrativeGenerator.js:962 JSDoc @returns {string[]}, code builds `lines` and `return lines` at :1008). It flows verbatim into the settlement: generateNarratives.js:42 -> assembleSettlement.js:74 (`settlementReason,` passed through unchanged). The PDF builds its viewModel from this same settlement object: buildViewModel (viewModel.js:126) sets `active = useAi ? ai : raw` (:142) and calls overviewSlice(active) (:156), which sets `settlementReason: s?.settlementReason || null` with NO coercion (:387). Overview.jsx:286 renders `{o.settlementReason}` raw as a child. Empirical reproduction (read-only node script against node_modules/@react-pdf/renderer, file deleted after): - ARRAY child (string[]) -> renders OK but flattened into adjacent text runs with NO separator (matches claim "concatenated with no separators"). This is the engine default, so it fires on virtually every export. It is a real parity drift: web OverviewTab.jsx:202 maps each line to its own

; the AI seed joins with \n\n (edge fn index.ts:533); historySlice coerces (viewModel.js:822-824). - OBJECT child ({primary,...}) -> THREW "Objects are not valid as a React child (found: object with keys {primary, secondary})" inside the @react-pdf reconciler, aborting the entire render. The object shape is reachable: generate-narrative/index.ts:560 writes `clone.settlementReason = { ...orig, primary: r.settlementReason }`, and the PDF can build from aiSettlement (viewModel.js:142). - STRING child (control) -> OK. So the claim's two predicted behaviors are both confirmed against the real library. The correction to the claim: the ARRAY case (which is the engine DEFAULT) does NOT crash — react-pdf flattens it (garbled/no-separator), not throws. Only the OBJECT case throws and kills the whole export, and that case is gated behind the AI-prose object shape rather than the default path.
verdicts.fix_note: Fix direction is correct (coerce in the slice like historySlice already does) but must NOT collapse arrays to null. historySlice's coercion (viewModel.js:822-824) only handles string and `.primary`, so applied verbatim it would silently drop the array content that is the engine DEFAULT. Two acceptable fixes: (a) in overviewSlice normalize to `Array.isArray(x) ? x.filter(s=>typeof s==='string').join('\n') : (typeof x==='string' ? x : x?.primary || null)`, which both prevents the object crash AND fixes the run-together array; or (b) keep the slice shape but render an array branch in Overview.jsx that maps each line to its own <Text> (mirroring web OverviewTab.jsx:202) plus an object branch using `.primary`. Either is fine; a bare `.primary`-only coercion is NOT — it would blank out the default multi-line reason. Severity is medium rather than high/critical because the total-export crash requires the AI object shape (not the default), while the always-on case is garbling (concatenation) rather than a crash.

confirmed.title: Viability chapter reads three engine fields that don't exist (stressConsequences, byDesignContradictions, activeMagicChains) — web derives them, PDF renders nothing
confirmed.evidence: viabilitySlice exposes `stressConsequences: v?.stressConsequences || []`, `activeMagicChains: v?.activeMagicChains || []`, `byDesignContradictions: v?.byDesignContradictions || []` (viewModel.js:807-810). generateEconomicViability returns only `{ viable, issues, warnings, dependencies, suggestions, plotHooks, summary, metrics }` (src/generators/economicGenerator.js:2670-2686) — none of those three keys exist. The web derives them: ViabilityTab.jsx:28-30 filters issues/warnings on `type==='stress_consequence'`, :40 filters `severity==='by_design'`, :121 derives magic chains from `economicState.activeChains.filter(c=>c.magicNote)`. ViabilityAssessment.jsx:38-39 and :83-89 render these slice fields, so the PDF's By-Design Contradictions, stress Consequences, and magic-chain tags never appear while the screen shows them — and by_design/stress_consequence issues additionally land unfiltered in the PDF's ACTIVE ISSUES list, unlike the web.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Derive in viabilitySlice the same way the web does: byDesignContradictions = issues filtered by severity 'by_design'; stressConsequences = issues+warnings with type 'stress_consequence' (and exclude both from the main issues list); activeMagicChains = economicState.activeChains.filter(c => c.magicNote).map(c => c.label).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the claim by reading the cited code and tracing the data flow.
