The PDF viability slice (src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:807-810) reads three fields off economicViability: `stressConsequences`, `activeMagicChains`, `byDesignContradictions`. generateEconomicViability's return block (src/generators/economicGenerator.js:2670-2686) emits only {viable, issues, warnings, dependencies, suggestions, plotHooks, summary, metrics} — none of the three. A repo-wide grep shows those three identifiers appear ONLY in PDF code (viewModel.js writing the fallback `|| []`, ViabilityAssessment.jsx:38-39,65,83-89 reading them); no generator ever populates them. So in the PDF those three sections render empty.

Confirmed parity drift on two fields with airtight evidence:
- stressConsequences: `stress_consequence` type issues ARE pushed into economicViability.issues/warnings (economicGenerator.js:642, 2643). The web (ViabilityTab.jsx:28-30) derives them at render and shows them in an "Active Stress Effects" section (:205-215), AND excludes them from its issue/warning lists (:41 critical filter, :56-61 otherItems filter). The PDF never derives them (slice field undefined → empty), so the CONSEQUENCES section is empty, and worse, the PDF's ACTIVE ISSUES block (ViabilityAssessment.jsx:120-128) renders ALL v.issues via IssueRow with no type/severity filter — so stress_consequence rows leak into the PDF issue list, unlike the web. Real drift.
- activeMagicChains: source data economicState.activeChains with magicNote exists (economicGenerator.js:2349,2545) and reaches the settlement root (assembleSettlement.js:66). The web reads s.economicState.activeChains.filter(c=>c.magicNote) (ViabilityTab.jsx:121) and renders magic-chain notes; the PDF slice reads v.activeMagicChains off economicViability (the wrong object), which is never set → no tags. Real drift.

The third field (byDesignContradictions) is weaker: by_design severity is emitted only into structural.violations → structuralViolations (structuralValidator.js:701,720), never into economicViability.issues (severity audit of economicGenerator.js shows no by_design). The web's byDesignIssues = v.issues.filter(severity==='by_design') (ViabilityTab.jsx:40) therefore also reads an empty source, so the web likely shows nothing there too, and the suggested fix (derive from issues filtered by 'by_design') would not populate it. This sub-claim is partially wrong, but it does not change the verdict because the slice field is still dead and the other two fields establish genuine web/PDF parity drift.

Severity medium is honest: it's parity drift on a secondary dossier chapter (Viability) plus leakage of stress-consequence rows into the PDF issue list — not data loss, money, security, a main-path crash, or a determinism break.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct for stressConsequences (issues+warnings with type 'stress_consequence', and exclude them from the main issues list to match the web) and activeMagicChains (economicState.activeChains.filter(c=>c.magicNote).map(c=>c.label)). NOTE: the slice currently reads economicState as `s.economicState`, but viabilitySlice only has `s` and `v=s.economicViability` — derive activeMagicChains from `s.economicState?.activeChains`, not from `v`. For byDesignContradictions the suggested fix (filter issues by severity 'by_design') will NOT work: by_design items live in s.structuralViolations (from checkStructuralValidity), never in economicViability.issues. To actually populate the PDF's By-Design Contradictions, derive from s.structuralViolations.filter(x=>x.severity==='by_design') (and note the web's own by-design section reads v.issues and is therefore likely empty too — fixing parity may mean fixing the web read as well, or accepting both render empty). Also fix the issue-leak: the PDF should exclude type==='stress_consequence' (and by_design) from the ACTIVE ISSUES list the way the web does, otherwise those rows double-render.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end via empirical generation against the real modules. viabilitySlice (src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:807,809,810) reads `v?.stressConsequences`, `v?.activeMagicChains`, `v?.byDesignContradictions` from `economicViability`. generateEconomicViability (src/generators/economicGenerator.js:2670-2685) returns exactly `{viable, issues, warnings, dependencies, suggestions, plotHooks, summary, metrics}` — empirically confirmed `economicViability` keys are `dependencies, issues, metrics, plotHooks, suggestions, summary, viable, warnings`; none of the three claimed fields exist. So all three slice fields are permanently `[]`, and ViabilityAssessment.jsx (src/pdf/sections/ViabilityAssessment.jsx:38-39,83-89,93-117,193-205) renders nothing for them.

Two real parity drifts confirmed by reproduction:
1) STRESS CONSEQUENCES. `type:'stress_consequence'` issues DO land in `economicViability.issues` (economicGenerator.js:642; empirically 2-3 entries under siege+famine). The web (src/components/new/tabs/ViabilityTab.jsx:28-30,41,58) extracts them into a dedicated "Active Stress Effects" section AND excludes them from its structural issue lists. The PDF has no equivalent filter: the slice's `issues` (viewModel.js:795-802) carry them straight into the ACTIVE ISSUES block (ViabilityAssessment.jsx:120-130). Reproduced: 2 "Stress:"-titled entries appear in slice.issues while slice.stressConsequences is empty. So on-screen these are a distinct labeled section; in the PDF they are dumped into generic issues with no CONSEQUENCES section — genuine content/labeling drift.
2) MAGIC CHAINS. The web derives magic-chain notes from `economicState.activeChains.filter(c=>c.magicNote)` (ViabilityTab.jsx:121). Reproduced (seed m1): a chain carries `magicNote` and the web shows it, but the PDF's `activeMagicChains` slice is empty, so its magic Tags never render; in that case `magicDependency` was also false, so the PDF's entire magic-dependency block is suppressed while the web shows the note.

These are degraded/relocated rendering in the exportable PDF vs the on-screen dossier (a recognized bug class for this product), not wrong simulation, data loss, money, or a crash — hence medium.
verdicts.fix_note:
The core fix is correct: viewModel.js must DERIVE these three fields from real engine data instead of reading non-existent keys.
- stressConsequences: derive from `[...(v.issues||[]), ...(v.warnings||[])].filter(i => i.type==='stress_consequence')`, mirroring ViabilityTab.jsx:28-30. AND the slice's `issues` must EXCLUDE `type==='stress_consequence'` (and the web's other viability-excluded types/severities if full parity is desired), otherwise stress consequences keep double-appearing in the PDF's ACTIVE ISSUES list.
- activeMagicChains: derive from `active.economicState?.activeChains?.filter(c=>c.magicNote)` (map to label/note), mirroring ViabilityTab.jsx:121.
- byDesignContradictions: IMPORTANT CORRECTION to the claim. `severity:'by_design'` is produced ONLY by structuralValidator (src/generators/structuralValidator.js:701,720) into `structuralViolations` (assembled at steps/assembleSettlement.js:57), NEVER into `economicViability.issues` — empirically 0 by_design entries in ev.issues. Therefore: (a) the web's `byDesignIssues` filter on `v.issues` (ViabilityTab.jsx:40) is ALSO always empty; the web actually surfaces out-of-tier/exclusivity contradictions via its separate "Structural Crises" section sourced from `s.structuralViolations` (ViabilityTab.jsx:37,147). (b) The PDF already renders `v.structuralViolations` in its own STRUCTURAL VIOLATIONS block (ViabilityAssessment.jsx:36,148-160), so by-design contradictions are roughly at parity through the structuralViolations channel. (c) by_design does NOT leak into the PDF ACTIVE ISSUES list (it isn't in `issues`). So the claim's assertions that "the web shows by-design contradictions while the PDF shows nothing" and "by_design issues additionally land unfiltered in the PDF ACTIVE ISSUES list" are both inaccurate. If you want the PDF's dedicated By-Design block to actually populate (matching the web's Structural Crises naming), derive byDesignContradictions from `active.structuralViolations.filter(x=>x.severity==='by_design')` — not from economicViability.issues.

confirmed.title: Overview 'Warnings & Notes' reads coherenceNotes/structuralSuggestions from economicViability but they live at the settlement root — always empty in PDF
confirmed.evidence: overviewSlice: `coherenceNotes: v?.coherenceNotes || []` and `structuralSuggestions: v?.structuralSuggestions || []` where v = s.economicViability (viewModel.js:403-404). The engine's economicViability has no such keys (economicGenerator.js:2670-2686 — closest is `suggestions`). The web reads them from the ROOT: OverviewTab.jsx:259-279 (`r.coherenceNotes`, `r.structuralSuggestions`, `r.structuralViolations`) and SummaryTab.jsx:97 destructures `coherenceNotes` from r. So the PDF Overview's WARNINGS & NOTES block silently drops every coherence note and structural suggestion the screen displays.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Read `s?.coherenceNotes || []` and `s?.structuralSuggestions || []` from the settlement root (keep economicViability as a fallback), and make the item renderer handle the actual shapes ({note, severity} and {reason, suggested[]}).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code; the claim holds on every point.

WIRING: In overviewSlice, `v = s?.economicViability || {}` (viewModel.js:327). The slice then reads `coherenceNotes: v?.coherenceNotes || []` and `structuralSuggestions: v?.structuralSuggestions || []` (viewModel.js:403-404).

ENGINE: economicGenerator's economicViability return object (economicGenerator.js:2670-2686) has keys viable/issues/warnings/dependencies/suggestions/plotHooks/summary/metrics — NO coherenceNotes, and `suggestions` not `structuralSuggestions`. A grep confirms no code anywhere attaches coherenceNotes/structuralSuggestions onto economicViability. So `v?.coherenceNotes` and `v?.structuralSuggestions` are always undefined -> both default to []. Always empty in the PDF.

CANONICAL ROOT LOCATION: structuralViolations/structuralSuggestions are written at the settlement ROOT in assembleSettlement.js:57-58 (`structural?.violations`, `structural?.suggestions`). coherenceNotes is produced by generateCoherence (narrativeGenerator.js:1163-1209, returns `{...settlement, coherenceNotes}`) and merged onto the root via `Object.assign(settlement, coherenceUpdates)` at assembleSettlement.js:135-136. The web confirms root is canonical: OverviewTab.jsx:259-279 reads r.structuralViolations/r.coherenceNotes/r.structuralSuggestions; SummaryTab.jsx:97 destructures coherenceNotes from r.

CONSUMPTION: PDF Overview.jsx:384-399 gates and renders a WARNINGS & NOTES BulletList spreading o.coherenceNotes and o.structuralSuggestions, which the slice always supplies as []. So the screen's coherence notes and structural suggestions are silently dropped from the PDF — a real dossier/PDF parity defect, the project's documented bug class.

SECONDARY CONFIRMATION (strengthens, not refutes): even if the source were corrected, the item renderer is also wrong. Overview.jsx:398 uses `itemRender={(it) => label(it) || (typeof it === 'string' ? it : '')}`, and label() (format.js:48-53) only reads label/name/title/type/good. The real items are {severity, note} and {reason, suggested}, so label() returns '' and the string fallback also yields '' — every item would render blank. The suggested fix's note to handle the actual shapes is necessary.

Severity medium is correct: silent content loss in the PDF export (parity drift), but no crash, data loss, money, or security impact.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct and complete in spirit. Concretely: in viewModel.js overviewSlice read from the root — `coherenceNotes: s?.coherenceNotes || v?.coherenceNotes || []` and `structuralSuggestions: s?.structuralSuggestions || v?.structuralSuggestions || []`. AND fix the renderer in Overview.jsx:390-399 so item shapes render: coherenceNotes items are {note, severity}; structuralSuggestions items are {reason, suggested[]}. The current `label(it)` path returns '' for both shapes, so the data fix alone is insufficient. Additional parity gap worth addressing in the same change: the web Overview also shows structuralViolations (OverviewTab.jsx:260-262, {institution|group, reason}), which the PDF Overview WARNINGS & NOTES block omits entirely — consider including o.structuralViolations for full parity.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL — reproduced end-to-end against the live modules.

Trace:
1. Producers put both keys at the settlement ROOT: `structuralSuggestions` in steps/assembleSettlement.js:58 (`structural?.suggestions || []`), and `coherenceNotes` in narrativeGenerator.js:1194/1209 (spread into `...settlement`). Confirmed empirically: a real pipeline run exposes `s.coherenceNotes`, `s.structuralSuggestions`, `s.structuralViolations` as root arrays, and `economicViability` has ONLY keys `viable, issues, warnings, dependencies, suggestions, plotHooks, summary, metrics` (no coherenceNotes/structuralSuggestions — both `undefined`). economicGenerator.js:2670-2685 corroborates (the closest key is `suggestions`, not `structuralSuggestions`).

2. The PDF overviewSlice (viewModel.js:327 `const v = s.economicViability || {}`, lines 403-404) reads `v?.coherenceNotes` and `v?.structuralSuggestions` — i.e. from economicViability, which never has them. So both fields are ALWAYS `[]` in the PDF view model, regardless of settlement content.

3. The web reads the same data from ROOT and renders it: OverviewTab.jsx:259-279 uses `r.coherenceNotes` / `r.structuralSuggestions` / `r.structuralViolations` (contradictions even get a special red-bordered block at 264-269); SummaryTab.jsx:97 destructures `coherenceNotes` from `r`.

Concrete repro: config {tier:'hamlet', terrain:'plains', culture:'human', threatLevel:'low', tradeRouteAccess:'none', seed:'hamletplainshumanlownone'} yields root structuralSuggestions=[{type:'suggestion', reason:'Frontier region: even small settlements benefit from a palisade...', suggested:[...]}]. The web Overview shows this; the built PDF view model (buildViewModel(s).overview.structuralSuggestions) === [] — 1 suggestion DROPPED. A halfling/high/crossroads config produces a coherenceNote with severity:'contradiction' ("Settlement is under active siege...") — also dropped from the PDF Overview.

Consumer: only Overview.jsx (lines 384-399) consumes o.coherenceNotes/o.structuralSuggestions, in the "WARNINGS & NOTES" BulletList. structuralViolations is handled correctly elsewhere (viewModel.js:805 reads root `s.structuralViolations`, rendered by ViabilityAssessment.jsx:36) — so the claim is correctly scoped: it does NOT over-claim structuralViolations.

Consequence: the PDF "WARNINGS & NOTES" block silently omits every coherence note (including by-design contradiction warnings shown prominently on screen) and every structural suggestion. coherenceNotes and structuralSuggestions have NO other PDF home (grep confirms only Overview.jsx + this slice consume them), so the information is entirely absent from the PDF — a genuine dossier/PDF parity drift, the named bug class.

Severity medium: wrong/incomplete simulation output in the exported PDF, real parity drift, but not data loss/money/security/crash, and only the PDF surface (screen is correct).
verdicts.fix_note:
The root cause is correct: change viewModel.js:403-404 to read from the settlement root (`s`), not from `v` (economicViability). i.e. `coherenceNotes: s?.coherenceNotes || []` and `structuralSuggestions: s?.structuralSuggestions || []`.

BUT a source-only fix is insufficient and would render wrong text. The Overview.jsx BulletList (line 390-399) uses `itemRender={(it) => label(it) || (typeof it === 'string' ? it : '')}`, and `label()` (pdf/lib/format.js:48-54) extracts `item.label || item.name || item.title || item.type || item.good`. The actual item shapes are:
- structuralSuggestions: `{type:'suggestion', reason, suggested}` — label() returns the literal "suggestion", not `reason`. (Verified: label() yields "suggestion" for the frontier-palisade suggestion instead of its reason text.)
- coherenceNotes: `{type, severity, tab, note}` — label() returns the humanized `type` (e.g. "Stress Economic"), not the human-readable `note`.
So the fix must also surface the correct text per item: e.g. give itemRender a fallback that prefers `it.note || it.reason` (matching the web's `note.note || ...` and `v.reason`), or normalize the items in overviewSlice into strings before spreading them into the BulletList. Without this, the PDF would print "Suggestion"/"Stress Economic" placeholders.

Also note: the web treats coherenceNotes with severity:'contradiction' specially (red callout, OverviewTab.jsx:264-269); the flat PDF BulletList won't preserve that severity distinction, but that is a lesser parity concern than the total drop.

confirmed.title: Resources chapter: exportPotential, structuralGaps, and priorityNotes read wrong paths — Export Potential and Gaps & Opportunities sections never render
confirmed.evidence: resourcesSlice maps `exportPotential: ra?.exportPotential || s?.exportPotential` (:774), `priorityNotes: v?.priorityNotes` from economicViability (:775), `structuralGaps: ra?.structuralGaps || s?.structuralGaps` (:776). The web ResourcesTab reads the real engine fields: `res.exports` (ResourcesTab.jsx:157, items with .product/.good/.value/.reason — exactly the shape ResourcesProduction.jsx:214-247 expects), `res.gaps` (:173,183 — with .chain/.impact/.missing/.severity exactly matching ResourcesProduction.jsx:318-356), and `res.priorityNotes` on resourceAnalysis (:176), not economicViability. The PDF section code was clearly ported from the web tab but the slice feeds it from keys the engine never emits, so EXPORT POTENTIAL and GAPS & OPPORTUNITIES are permanently empty in print while populated on screen. The chapter headline call (ResourcesProduction.jsx:51) tries `r.exports` which isn't a slice key either.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In resourcesSlice read `ra?.exports || ra?.exportPotential`, `ra?.gaps || ra?.structuralGaps`, and `ra?.priorityNotes || v?.priorityNotes`.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against the engine source, the web tab, and the PDF section/slice. The engine's generateResourceAnalysis (src/generators/resourceGenerator.js:261-273) emits exactly `exports`, `gaps`, and `priorityNotes` on the resourceAnalysis object — never `exportPotential` or `structuralGaps`. generateEconomicViability (src/generators/economicGenerator.js:2583) emits no `priorityNotes` field. The web ResourcesTab reads the real keys (src/components/new/tabs/ResourcesTab.jsx:157 `res.exports`, :173/183 `res.gaps`, :176 `res.priorityNotes`, where res = r.resourceAnalysis), so they render on screen. The PDF slice (src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:774-776) instead reads `ra?.exportPotential || s?.exportPotential`, `v?.priorityNotes` (from economicViability), and `ra?.structuralGaps || s?.structuralGaps`. All of those resolve to undefined: `exportPotential`/`structuralGaps` are phantom keys that appear ONLY in the PDF section and slice (grep across all of src confirms no engine/data/test producer), the `s?.` top-level fallbacks don't exist on the assembled settlement (assembleSettlement.js:79 passes resourceAnalysis through unchanged with no renaming), and economicViability has no priorityNotes. So each defaults to []. The PDF section (src/pdf/sections/ResourcesProduction.jsx:208,285,318) gates EXPORT POTENTIAL on r.exportPotential?.length, the structural-gaps branch on r.structuralGaps?.length, and priority-note hooks on r.priorityNotes?.length — all permanently false in print while populated on screen. The chapter headline (:51) tries `r.exports || r.exportPotential`; `r.exports` isn't a slice key either, so it also gets []. This is a genuine PDF/web parity drift confirmed by reading both data sources and both renderers.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct in direction. Cleanest form: in resourcesSlice set `exportPotential: ra?.exports || [],` `priorityNotes: ra?.priorityNotes || [],` `structuralGaps: ra?.gaps || [],`. The `|| s?.exportPotential`, `|| s?.structuralGaps`, and `v?.priorityNotes` branches are dead (no producer) and can be dropped rather than retained. Note the headline at ResourcesProduction.jsx:51 reads `r.exports || r.exportPotential`; once the slice key `exportPotential` is fed from ra.exports it works, but `r.exports` there remains a non-existent slice key (harmless dead-OR). Reviewer's proposed `ra?.exports || ra?.exportPotential` etc. is also fine since ra.exportPotential is always undefined.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED — reproduced end-to-end against the real engine module.

Field-name mismatch in the PDF resources slice. The engine's resourceAnalysis (generated by generateResourceAnalysis, src/generators/resourceGenerator.js:261-273) emits these field names:
- `exports` (line 267)
- `gaps` (line 268)
- `priorityNotes` (line 272) — note: ON resourceAnalysis, not on economicViability.

But the PDF slice (src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js, resourcesSlice; bindings at :721-723 where s=settlement, ra=s.resourceAnalysis, v=s.economicViability) reads non-existent keys:
- :774 `exportPotential: ra?.exportPotential || s?.exportPotential || []`  → undefined||undefined → []
- :775 `priorityNotes: v?.priorityNotes || []`  → economicViability.priorityNotes is undefined → []
- :776 `structuralGaps: ra?.structuralGaps || s?.structuralGaps || []`  → undefined||undefined → []

Evidence the keys never exist: grep for `exportPotential`/`structuralGaps` across src/generators and src/data returns ZERO hits; across all of src/ excluding pdf/ and tests returns ZERO hits — so these names exist only as the (wrong) reads in the PDF code, with no producer or rename transform anywhere in the pipeline. A grep for `priorityNotes` in economicGenerator.js and generators/steps/ also returns zero, confirming economicViability never carries priorityNotes.

Reproduction (read-only node one-liner against the REAL module /Users/cstokes/Desktop/settlement-generator/settlement_work/src/generators/resourceGenerator.js): for terrains forest/coastal/plains/hills, the engine produced exports.length=4 and gaps.length=1-2, but simulating the exact slice reads from viewModel.js:774-776 yielded exportPotential.length=0, priorityNotes.length=0, structuralGaps.length=0 for every case. ('exportPotential' in ra)=false and ('structuralGaps' in ra)=false in every run.

User-visible consequence in src/pdf/sections/ResourcesProduction.jsx:
- EXPORT POTENTIAL section is gated on `r.exportPotential?.length > 0` (:208) → always false → section never renders in the PDF.
- GAPS & OPPORTUNITIES section is gated on `r.priorityNotes?.length > 0 || r.structuralGaps?.length > 0` (:285) → always false → section never renders.
- The gold ChapterHeadline (:50-52) passes `primaryExports: r.exports || r.exportPotential || []`; r.exports is not a slice key (the slice exposes availableCommodities + the broken exportPotential, never `exports`) and r.exportPotential is []. resourcesHeadline (headlines.js:144-152) therefore drops the "Exports ..." clause (:150), and if primaryImports is also empty it returns null entirely (:148).

The web ResourcesTab (src/components/new/tabs/ResourcesTab.jsx) reads the correct engine fields and renders all of this content on screen: res.exports (:157, items use .product/.good/.name/.value/.reason), res.gaps (:173,183, items use .chain/.impact/.missing/.severity), res.priorityNotes (:176). This is a textbook dossier/PDF parity drift: content present on screen, permanently absent in print. The PDF section's own field reads (e.product/e.good/e.name/.value/.reason at :233-240; g.chain/g.severity/g.impact/g.missing at :322-326) already match the real engine shapes — only the slice's source keys are wrong.

Severity high (not critical): no data loss/money/security/crash; it is broken/wrong simulation output in a shipped feature (whole sections of the exportable dossier silently missing, plus a degraded headline). Not a determinism break. Confidence ~0.96 — the only residual uncertainty is whether some intermediate step I did not exhaustively read renames these fields onto resourceAnalysis/economicViability/settlement, but the zero-hit greps across generators+steps+data strongly rule that out, and the live module run confirms the actual emitted shape.
verdicts.fix_note:
The claim's diagnosis and direction are correct. Fix is confined to resourcesSlice in src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:
- :774 read from ra.exports:  `exportPotential: ra?.exports || []`
- :775 read priorityNotes from resourceAnalysis, not economicViability:  `priorityNotes: ra?.priorityNotes || []`  (drop the `v?.` source)
- :776 read from ra.gaps:  `structuralGaps: ra?.gaps || []`

Additionally fix the headline call in src/pdf/sections/ResourcesProduction.jsx:51 — it references `r.exports`, which is not a slice key. After the slice fix it should pass the slice's actual key, i.e. `primaryExports: r.exportPotential || []` (the renamed exports list). Leaving `r.exports || r.exportPotential` works but the `r.exports` term is dead; cleaner to drop it.

No changes needed inside the PDF section's item-rendering: it already reads e.product/e.good/e.name/.value/.reason and g.chain/g.severity/g.impact/g.missing, which match the engine's exports[] and gaps[] item shapes (verified against both the engine emission and the web ResourcesTab). The exploitation-derived item shape (.value, .reason) flows through evaluateInstitutionDeps; confirm in a follow-up that exports[] items actually carry .value/.reason (the section defaults value to 'medium' if absent, so worst case is a default tag, not a crash). Optionally add the alias style import { ... } guard is irrelevant here.

confirmed.title: Defense chapter's 'ACTIVE MILITARY STATUS' siege/occupation banner can never render — stress matcher checks icon/key against wrong values
confirmed.evidence: defenseSlice: `stress.find(x => ['siege', 'occupied', 'civilWar'].includes(x?.icon || x?.key))` (viewModel.js:609). Stress entries are built by buildStressEntry (src/generators/stressGenerator.js:28-37) as `{ type, label, icon, colour, summary, crisisHook, ... }` — `icon` is an emoji, there is no `key` field, and the actual type keys are 'under_siege', 'occupied', 'wartime' (src/data/stressTypes.js:13,40,217; web tabHelpers.js:15 checks `s?.type === 'under_siege'`). 'siege' and 'civilWar' match no key under any field. So a settlement under siege or occupation exports a Defense chapter with no military-status callout (DefenseSecurity.jsx:64-78).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Match on type: `stress.find(x => ['under_siege', 'occupied', 'wartime', 'insurgency'].includes(x?.type))`.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed real. At src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:609 the Defense slice computes `const militaryStress = stress.find(x => ['siege', 'occupied', 'civilWar'].includes(x?.icon || x?.key))`. Two compounding errors make this always return undefined:

1. Wrong field. Stress entries are built by buildStressEntry (src/generators/stressGenerator.js:28-37) as `{ type, label, icon, colour, summary, crisisHook, viabilityNote, historyColour }`. There is no `key` field, and every stress type in src/data/stressTypes.js has a non-empty emoji `icon` (verified: all 15 types lines 13-239 carry truthy emoji icons). Because `icon` is always truthy, `x?.icon || x?.key` always resolves to the emoji string, never reaching `key` (which doesn't exist anyway). So the matcher compares an emoji against the literal strings 'siege'/'occupied'/'civilWar' — never a match.

2. Wrong vocabulary. The actual stress `type` keys are under_siege, occupied, wartime, insurgency, etc. (stressTypes.js Object.keys verified). 'siege' and 'civilWar' are not stress types at all — 'siege' belongs to the separate PULSE/world-map vocabulary (crisisLifecycle.js:389 comment explicitly contrasts the GENERATION type 'under_siege' vs the PULSE twin 'siege'; capacityModel.js/WorldPulsePanel.jsx use 'siege' as a threat type). Even 'occupied', a real type, is matched against `icon` not `type`, so it never fires.

Parity confirmed against the web reference: src/components/new/tabs/DefenseTab.jsx:78-88 renders the identical 'ACTIVE MILITARY STATUS'/'Military Status' callout by keying STRESS_STATUS on `s.type` (under_siege, famine, occupied, politically_fractured, recently_betrayed, plague_onset). The PDF callout (src/pdf/sections/DefenseSecurity.jsx:64-78) is gated solely on `d.militaryStress`, which is permanently undefined. Net effect: a settlement under siege/occupation/etc. exports a Defense chapter with no military-status callout while the on-screen dossier shows one — genuine PDF/web parity drift. No upstream guard, dead-code, or intentional-comment refutation found.
verdicts.fix_note: Direction of the suggested fix (match on `x?.type`) is correct, but the proposed set ['under_siege','occupied','wartime','insurgency'] does NOT match the web parity reference and would create a different drift: famine/politically_fractured/recently_betrayed/plague_onset render the web callout but would still be missing from PDF, while wartime/insurgency would appear in PDF but not web. To achieve true parity, mirror DefenseTab.jsx's STRESS_STATUS keys: stress.find(x => ['under_siege','famine','occupied','politically_fractured','recently_betrayed','plague_onset'].includes(x?.type)). Ideally factor the STRESS_STATUS set into a shared helper so the two surfaces cannot drift again.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed via read + executed node repro against the real modules. In src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:609 defenseSlice computes militaryStress with `stress.find(x => ['siege', 'occupied', 'civilWar'].includes(x?.icon || x?.key))`. Stress entries are built by buildStressEntry (src/generators/stressGenerator.js:28-37) with shape `{ type, label, icon (emoji), colour, summary, crisisHook, ... }` — there is NO `key` field, and `icon` is an emoji string, never 'siege'/'occupied'/'civilWar'. The real type keys are 'under_siege', 'occupied', 'wartime' (src/data/stressTypes.js:13,40,217). Running the matcher against entries built from STRESS_TYPE_MAP for all three war stress types returns false in every case (x?.icon || x?.key === the emoji or undefined, never a target literal); a scan of the whole map confirms no stress type's icon/key equals any of the three target strings, and the map has no `key` property at all. Because the field tested is wrong (icon/key vs type) and the literals are wrong ('siege'/'civilWar' don't exist as keys; only 'occupied' coincidentally collides but is tested against the wrong field), d.militaryStress is undefined for every settlement. DefenseSecurity.jsx:64-78 gates the entire 'ACTIVE MILITARY STATUS' callout on `d.militaryStress && (...)`, so the banner can never render. End-to-end path: user forces config.stressType='under_siege' (or it emerges; selection weights under_siege=10, occupied=7 at stressGenerator.js:179,182) -> entry {type:'under_siege', icon emoji} -> stressArray returns it unchanged (viewModel.js:44-48) -> matcher misses -> banner omitted from exported Defense chapter. This is both dead code and PDF/web parity drift: the web Defense surface reacts to siege via `s?.type === 'under_siege'` (src/components/new/tabHelpers.js:15), so a besieged/occupied settlement renders siege effects on-screen but the PDF silently drops the prominent military-status callout. Severity medium: real parity drift / always-unreachable feature branch, but no crash, data loss, money, security, or wrong-simulation-data impact — it is a missing presentation element on an export.
verdicts.fix_note: Test the `type` field against the real type keys instead of icon/key: `const militaryStress = stress.find(x => ['under_siege', 'occupied', 'wartime'].includes(x?.type));` Two caveats on the original intent/code: (1) There is no `civilWar`/`civil_war` stress type in STRESS_TYPE_MAP — the closest war-state key is `wartime`, so use `wartime` rather than the proposed `civilWar`. The chapter docstring (DefenseSecurity.jsx:5) saying 'civil war' is aspirational; map it to `wartime` or omit. (2) DefenseSecurity.jsx:67 falls back to `cap(d.militaryStress.icon || 'Active')` when label is missing — since icon is an emoji this would print a raw emoji; label is always present for these three types so it's harmless, but `d.militaryStress.type` (or `label(d.militaryStress.type)`) would be a cleaner fallback than the emoji icon.

confirmed.title: AI Appendix friction points print as blank numbered rows — canonical shape is {who, what} but the section reads parties/text/description
confirmed.evidence: The narrative edge function normalizes friction points to exactly `{ who, what }` (supabase/functions/generate-narrative/index.ts:978-982: filters `typeof x.who === 'string' && typeof x.what === 'string'`). The web renders p.who/p.what (DMCompassTab.jsx:107-111). AIAppendix.jsx:179 reads `fp.parties` (doesn't exist) and the body via textOf(fp) which checks text/description/summary/label/title/detail (:233-242) — `what` is not in the list. Result: the PDF's Friction Points section renders 3-5 rows containing only the index numbers '01'..'05' with no parties and no grievance text, while the screen shows full content. This is paid AI-narrative content silently lost in the export.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Render `fp.who` for the parties line and add `item.what` to textOf()'s field list (or read fp.what directly).

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed real after reading the full chain. (1) Canonical shape is {who, what}: the narrative edge function's apply normalizes friction points to exactly { who: x.who, what: x.what } and filters on typeof x.who/x.what === 'string' (supabase/functions/generate-narrative/index.ts:977-981; instruction at :983-989 requests the same). (2) The PDF view model passes them through unchanged: appendixSlice sets frictionPoints: ai.frictionPoints || [] with no remap (src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:1062), so the PDF receives {who, what} objects. (3) The PDF renderer reads the wrong keys: AIAppendix.jsx:179 gates the parties line on fp.parties (never present, never renders), and the body uses textOf(fp) whose field list is text||description||summary||label||title||detail (AIAppendix.jsx:240) — 'what' is NOT in that list, so it returns ''. Net result: friction rows render only the '01'..'05' index numbers with no parties and no grievance text. (4) Parity drift confirmed: the web renders p.who/p.what correctly (DMCompassTab.jsx:108-111; ChroniclePanel.jsx:178 also uses fp.who/fp.what). Refutation attempts failed: the path is reachable (gated only by narrativeMode + non-empty frictionPoints), no upstream guard remaps the keys, no test or comment indicates intentional behavior, and no PDF test covers this section. The aiLayer.js:400 frictionPoints is a distinct prompt-context array of plain strings (generator input, not the rendered appendix) and does not change the canonical output shape. The suggested fix is correct.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct and sufficient: render fp.who for the parties line (replacing the fp.parties check at AIAppendix.jsx:179-183) and surface fp.what for the body (either read fp.what directly at line 184 or add item.what to textOf's field list at line 240). Note: if textOf gains 'what', order it after the existing keys to avoid changing behavior for other item types that already use text/description; reading fp.who and fp.what directly in the friction block is the cleaner, lower-blast-radius option since this is the only consumer that needs the {who, what} shape.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end. The paid narrative edge function normalizes friction points to exactly `{who, what}` (supabase/functions/generate-narrative/index.ts:979-980: `.filter(x => typeof x.who === 'string' && typeof x.what === 'string').map(x => ({who: x.who, what: x.what}))`) and writes them to the top-level `clone.frictionPoints`, which becomes `aiSettlement`. Two web consumers confirm this is the canonical shape: DMCompassTab.jsx:108-111 renders `p.who`/`p.what`, and ChroniclePanel.jsx:178 renders `fp?.who - fp?.what`. The PDF viewModel passes the array through unchanged (viewModel.js:1062 `appendixSlice`: `frictionPoints: ai.frictionPoints || []`).

In the PDF, AIAppendix.jsx:179 gates the party line on `fp.parties` — a key that does not exist on `{who, what}`, so the party header never renders. The body comes from `textOf(fp)` (line 184), whose object branch (lines 239-241) only reads `item.text || item.description || item.summary || item.label || item.title || item.detail` — `who`/`what` are absent, so it returns `''`. I reproduced this with a node one-liner against a realistic `{who, what}` object: `fp.parties => undefined` (no party line) and `textOf(fp) => ''` (empty body). Net result: each Friction Points row in the exported PDF renders only the zero-padded index label ('01'..'05') with no party and no grievance text, while the on-screen dossier shows full content. This is a genuine dossier/PDF parity break that silently drops paid AI-narrative content from the export.

Severity medium (not high): it is confined to one AI-only sub-section of the PDF (gated behind narrativeMode + purchased narrative), does not crash, does not corrupt data, and does not affect the deterministic core simulation or the on-screen view — but it is real, reproducible, and loses content the user paid for. Confidence ~0.9.
verdicts.fix_note:
The suggested direction is correct but must handle TWO shapes, not just `{who, what}`. There is a second, non-AI production path: src/generators/aiLayer.js:400-404 builds `frictionPoints` as an array of plain STRINGS (from ctx.conflicts/tensions/criticalIssues, all string-mapped at lines 162-163,189). The current `textOf` already handles those via its `typeof item === 'string'` branch (line 238). A fix that blindly assumes objects would regress that path. Correct fix: (1) party line should read `fp.who` in addition to `fp.parties` (e.g. `const who = fp.who || fp.parties; if (who) render(Array.isArray(who) ? who.join(' ↔ ') : String(who))`); (2) add `what` to the body — either extend `textOf` to include `item.what` (and `item.who` as a last-resort fallback) in its OR-chain at line 240, or read `textOf(fp.what != null ? { text: fp.what } : fp)`. Extending the `textOf` OR-chain to include `what` is the minimal, safe change and preserves the string-fallback path.

ADJACENT DEFECT (same root cause, worth folding into the same fix): the Connections Map below it has identical parity drift. The edge function emits `{from, to, via, nature}` (index.ts ~1028-1040), but AIAppendix.jsx:250-252 `relationshipOf` only reads `relationship || type || kind` — never `nature` — so every PDF connection renders the literal placeholder pill 'LINK' instead of the real relationship, and `via` is dropped because line 219 reads `c.note` which doesn't exist (web reads `e.nature`/`e.via` at DMCompassTab.jsx:131-135). `from`/`to` survive only because they're strings. Add `c.nature` to `relationshipOf` and read `c.via || c.note` for the trailing note. Both bugs stem from the PDF section predating the edge function's canonical `{who/what}` and `{from/to/via/nature}` contracts.

confirmed.title: AI Appendix connections map labels every edge 'LINK' and drops `via` — canonical fields are nature/via, section reads relationship/type/kind/note
confirmed.evidence: The edge function normalizes connections to `{ from, to, via, nature }` (generate-narrative/index.ts:1027-1037, 'from/to/nature are canonical'). The web renders e.nature and e.via (DMCompassTab.jsx:131-136). AIAppendix.jsx relationshipOf() reads `c.relationship || c.type || c.kind || 'LINK'` (:250-253) — never `nature` — so every printed edge pill says 'LINK'; and the section reads `c.note` (:219) instead of `c.via`, so mediated edges lose their mediator. The relationship semantics (the entire point of the connections map) are absent from the PDF.
confirmed.suggested_fix: relationshipOf: `(c.nature || c.relationship || c.type || c.kind || 'LINK')`; render `c.via` (e.g. 'via {c.via}') instead of c.note.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against actual code. The edge function generate-narrative/index.ts:1015-1039 normalizes AI connections to the canonical shape `{ from, to, via, nature }` (comment line 1019: "from/to/nature are canonical"; it folds synonyms relationship/relation/kind/type INTO `nature` and through/mediator INTO `via`, so it never emits a top-level `relationship`/`type`/`kind`/`note`). The web renderer (src/components/new/tabs/DMCompassTab.jsx:131,132,135) correctly reads `e.nature` and `e.via`. The PDF data path is clean: viewModel.js appendixSlice() (line 1063) and the top-level builder (line 168, ai = aiSettlement) pass connectionsMap through verbatim with no field renaming. AIAppendix.jsx then mis-reads it: relationshipOf() at :250-253 reads `c.relationship || c.type || c.kind || 'LINK'` and never `c.nature`, so every printed edge pill falls through to the literal 'LINK'; and :219-221 renders `c.note` (which the edge function never writes) instead of `c.via`, so every mediated edge silently drops its mediator. This is a confirmed PDF/on-screen parity drift (a named bug class in this project) that erases the entire semantic content of the Connections Map in the PDF export whenever AI narrative is used. I could not refute it — the cited path is reachable (gated only by hasConnections > 0 and AI narrative mode), no upstream guard remaps the fields, and there is no comment indicating intent.
verdicts.fix_note: Fix is correct. Refinements: (1) relationshipOf should prefer nature: `(c.nature || c.relationship || c.type || c.kind || 'LINK').toUpperCase()` — keeping the legacy synonyms as fallback is harmless and tolerates any older persisted snapshots. (2) For the mediator, gate and prefix it to match the web ("via {c.via}") and keep c.note as a legacy fallback, e.g. render when `(c.via || c.note)` and show `via ${c.via || c.note}`. The 90pt-wide Pill column may truncate longer nature phrases like "competes with", so verify the pill width accommodates real `nature` values rather than the short 'LINK' it was sized for.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED — reproduced end-to-end against the real canonical data shape.

PRODUCER: supabase/functions/generate-narrative/index.ts:1024-1039 normalizes every connections-map edge to the canonical object `{ from, to, via, nature }` (comment at :1019 states "`from/to/nature` are canonical"). The refinement merge at :1567 preserves that shape via `prior.connectionsMap.map(x => ({ ...x }))`.

PASSTHROUGH: src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:1063 (appendixSlice) sets `connectionsMap: ai.connectionsMap || []` with no remapping; `ai` is the raw aiSettlement (:140). So the canonical `{from,to,via,nature}` objects arrive at AIAppendix unchanged as `c`.

CONSUMER BUG: src/pdf/sections/AIAppendix.jsx:250-253 `relationshipOf(c)` returns `(c.relationship || c.type || c.kind || 'LINK').toUpperCase()` — none of those keys exist on a canonical edge, so it ALWAYS falls through to 'LINK'. And :219 gates the mediator row on `c.note`, which never exists; the canonical mediator lives in `c.via`, so it is dropped.

REPRODUCED (node, against a canonical edge {from:'Mara Veil', to:'Silver Chain', via:'Moot Hall', nature:'reports to'}): relationship pill printed 'LINK'; via 'Moot Hall' dropped (c.note falsy). The from/to endpoints render fine.

PARITY: the web renderer src/components/new/tabs/DMCompassTab.jsx:131 reads `e.nature` and :132-136 reads `e.via` correctly. So the on-screen dossier shows real relationship semantics + mediator while the PDF shows uniform 'LINK' and no mediator — a genuine PDF/dossier parity drift, which is a named product bug class.

NO LEGACY SHAPE PROTECTS THE FALLBACKS: the only other in-app `connectionsMap` (src/generators/aiLayer.js:405) is an array of prompt-context STRINGS used to feed the AI, never rendered to the PDF. No generator ever emits object edges with `relationship`/`note`, so `c.relationship/type/kind/note` are pure dead reads.

NO TEST PINS THE BUG: aiGroundingContract.test.js:536 only asserts the `connectionsMap` refinement pass exists in the edge source; it does not assert PDF field names. Notably PDF_PARITY_AUDIT.md:458 itself MIS-records the consumer contract as `from,to,relationship/type/kind,optional note` — documenting the renderer's wrong expectation rather than the actual data, which is corroborating evidence of the drift.

Severity medium: not a crash, money, security, or determinism issue, and only affects narrative-mode PDFs (premium AI feature). But it is a real wrong-output defect — the entire semantic payload of the Connections Map (the relationship label and the mediator) is silently destroyed in the PDF, defeating the section's purpose, while the web view is correct. Confidence ~0.97.
verdicts.fix_note:
Fix is correct in spirit but must add `nature`/`via` as the PRIMARY reads, keeping legacy synonyms only as harmless tolerant fallbacks. Concretely:

1) relationshipOf (AIAppendix.jsx:250-253): read nature first ->
   return (c.nature || c.relationship || c.type || c.kind || 'LINK').toUpperCase();

2) The mediator row (AIAppendix.jsx:219-223): gate and render `c.via` (with c.note as a tolerant fallback) ->
   change `{c.note && (` to `{(c.via || c.note) && (` and render `{c.via || c.note}` inside the Text. For full parity with the web, render it as a "via <mediator>" between the rel-pill and the `to` endpoint rather than as a trailing note column; at minimum do not drop it.

3) Optional but recommended: mirror the web's endpoint reads. endpointOf already tolerates strings; canonical from/to are strings, so `c.from`/`c.to` (line 211/217 already cover `c.from`/`c.to` first) are fine — no change needed there.

Also worth correcting PDF_PARITY_AUDIT.md:458/463 which documents the wrong field contract (relationship/type/kind/note) — update to the canonical from/to/nature/via so the audit stops codifying the drift.

confirmed.title: Quarter descriptions never print — viewModel reads q.description but the engine field is q.desc
confirmed.evidence: identitySlice (:279-281) and overviewSlice (:383-385) map quarters with `description: q?.description || null`. The engine quarter shape is `{ name, location, desc, landmarks }` — documented and consumed in src/domain/districtProfile.js:5-6 ('light fields (name, location, desc, landmarks)'), :67 and :209 (`quarter.desc`), and the web renders `q.desc` (OverviewTab.jsx:250). So the PDF QUARTERS blocks (Overview.jsx:358-381) print quarter names and landmarks but silently drop every description the screen shows.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Map `description: q?.desc || q?.description || null` in both slices.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the full chain end-to-end; the claim holds.

1. Engine shape: src/generators/spatialGenerator.js produces every quarter with the field `desc` (e.g. :28, :40, :50, :60, :77...). There is NO `description` field on engine quarters anywhere in the generator.

2. Confirmed-correct consumers of the real field: src/domain/districtProfile.js:5-6 (doc), :67 (`quarter.desc`); web renderer src/components/new/tabs/OverviewTab.jsx:250 renders `{q.desc}` — and this component IS live (lazy-imported by OutputContainer.jsx:47, the dossier renderer). So the description is genuinely shown on screen.

3. The bug: src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js maps `description: q?.description || null` in BOTH the identity slice (:279) and the overview slice (:384), reading directly from `s.spatialLayout.quarters` (the raw engine shape) with no intervening normalization. Since engine quarters have no `description` key, this always resolves to null.

4. PDF consumer: src/pdf/sections/Overview.jsx:368 guards the description block with `{q.description && (...)}`. Because viewModel always sets that to null, the description Text at :369-371 never renders — names and landmarks still print, descriptions are silently dropped.

Refutation attempts that failed: searched the pipeline (generateSettlementPipeline.js) and legacy generator (generateSettlement.js) plus all of src/generators for any `desc`→`description` rename/normalization — none exists. The cited path is reachable (viewModel feeds the PDF Overview section). No nearby comment or test marks this as intentional. This is a genuine dossier/PDF parity drift: the screen shows quarter descriptions, the PDF omits them.

Minor evidence correction: the web renderer is at src/components/new/tabs/OverviewTab.jsx:250 (the reviewer wrote OverviewTab.jsx:250 without the `new/tabs/` segment), and the PDF block path is src/pdf/sections/Overview.jsx (reviewer said pdf/sections — correct) though the line numbers (358-381) match. These do not affect the conclusion.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix `description: q?.desc || q?.description || null` is correct and should be applied in BOTH slices (viewModel.js:279 and :384). The `|| q?.description` fallback is harmless/forward-compatible. Alternatively, given the engine canonically emits `desc`, `description: q?.desc || null` would also fully fix it. Confidence ~0.95.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED real PDF/dossier parity-drift bug.

Concrete end-to-end failure path:
1. Engine source of truth: src/generators/spatialGenerator.js builds every quarter object with the field key `desc` (e.g. line 28 `desc: 'Bustling center...'`, and identically at lines 40, 50, 60, 77, 87, 97, 107, 117, 129, 141, 154, 164, 170). There is NO `description` key. This is the only producer of `settlement.spatialLayout.quarters` (called from steps/economyReconcilePass.js:118). domain/districtProfile.js consumes `quarter.desc` (lines 67, 209) and its header comment (lines 5-6) documents the light shape as `(name, location, desc, landmarks)`.
2. Web renderer reads the engine field correctly: src/components/new/tabs/OverviewTab.jsx:250 renders `{q.desc}`, so the on-screen dossier shows the description.
3. PDF viewModel reads the WRONG field: src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:279 (identitySlice) and :384 (overviewSlice) both map `description: q?.description || null`. Since the engine never sets `description`, this always resolves to `null`.
4. PDF renderers gate the block on truthiness: src/pdf/sections/Overview.jsx:368 (`{q.description && ...}`) and src/pdf/sections/IdentityDailyLife.jsx:230 (`{q.description && <Field defaultValue={q.description}>`). With `description` always null, the QUARTERS description text never prints in the PDF and the editable Identity field is always empty.

Demonstrated with a read-only run of the real module: generateSpatialLayout('town', [...], 'road') yields quarter keys ['name','location','desc','landmarks']; q.desc is the populated string, q.description is undefined, and `q?.description || null` === null.

Consequence: every quarter's flavor description that the screen shows is silently dropped from BOTH the printed PDF Overview QUARTERS block and the editable Identity/Daily Life quarters section. Quarter names and landmarks still print, so the omission is easy to miss — a genuine dossier/PDF parity-drift bug, exactly the named bug class. Not critical (no data loss/money/security/crash; names+landmarks survive), but it is real wrong output across two PDF sections, so medium.

Corroborating evidence that this is an oversight rather than intent: line 440 of the same viewModel uses the correct defensive pattern for factions — `f?.description || f?.desc || null`. The two quarter mappings simply forgot the `|| q?.desc` fallback.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix direction is correct. Make the two quarter mappings read the engine field, mirroring the faction pattern already used at viewModel.js:440. Change both src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:279 and :384 from `description: q?.description || null` to `description: q?.desc || q?.description || null` (prefer `q.desc` since that is the real engine field; keep `|| q?.description` only as a harmless forward-compat fallback). No changes are needed in Overview.jsx or IdentityDailyLife.jsx — their `q.description &&` guards work once the viewModel populates the value. One caveat for the IdentityDailyLife editable field: its input name is `identity.quarter.<i>.description` (IdentityDailyLife.jsx:233), so confirm the PDF edit-replay/persistence layer keys off that same `description` name end-to-end; the rendering fix alone restores the printed/prefilled value regardless.

confirmed.title: Campaign PDF 'Cross-Settlement NPC Contacts' always shows the empty state — reads npc.interSettlementNpcs which nothing produces
confirmed.evidence: buildNPCConnections gathers `npc.interSettlementNpcs || []` per NPC (generateCampaignPDF.js:433-447). A repo-wide grep shows `interSettlementNpcs` appears ONLY in this file — no generator, store, or lib produces it. The real cross-settlement contact data lives at `settlement.interSettlementRelationships` (entries with npcName/partnerName/partnerRole/partnerSettlement/relType — exactly the columns this table wants, see RelationshipsTab.jsx:36,130-156) and `neighbourNetwork[].npcConnections` (RelationshipsTab.jsx:100-103). The campaign export therefore always prints 'No cross-settlement NPC contacts recorded' even for fully linked campaigns.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Iterate `save.settlement.interSettlementRelationships` (untyped entries) and/or `neighbourNetwork[].npcConnections`, mapping npcName/partnerName/partnerSettlement/relType into the table rows.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the cited file plus producers/consumers. buildNPCConnections (generateCampaignPDF.js:432-447) iterates per-NPC `npc.interSettlementNpcs || []`. Repo-wide grep shows `interSettlementNpcs` exists in exactly two lines, both in generateCampaignPDF.js (comment line 430, read line 435) — nothing in generators/, store/, lib/, or migrations ever writes `npc.interSettlementNpcs`. The actual cross-settlement NPC contact data is produced by buildInterSettlementNPCs (SettlementsPanel.jsx:74-75) as `settlement.interSettlementRelationships[]` entries carrying exactly npcName/npcRole/partnerName/partnerRole/partnerSettlement/relType/description, and stored on the settlement at link time (SettlementsPanel.jsx:874,935). It is also surfaced via neighbourNetwork[].npcConnections (consumed by RelationshipsTab.jsx:100-103,113-114 and neighbourComponents.jsx:42-44). The on-screen dossier (RelationshipsTab.jsx:130 filters interSettlementRelationships with !x.type) and the on-screen viewModel (viewModel.js:1036 reads s.interSettlementRelationships) read the correct fields — only the campaign PDF reads the phantom field. The path is live: generateCampaignPDF (exported, line 709) calls buildNPCConnections unconditionally whenever settlements.length>0 (line 742-743). Because npc.interSettlementNpcs is always undefined, `connections` is always empty and the function always renders the empty-state text 'No cross-settlement NPC contacts recorded.' (lines 450-457) even for fully linked campaigns. No upstream guard, fallback, or migration maps the real data onto the read field. I could find no refutation: the API does not work differently than claimed, the code is reachable, and there is no nearby comment/test indicating intentional behavior.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct in direction. Concretely: in buildNPCConnections, replace the per-NPC `npc.interSettlementNpcs` loop with iteration over `save.settlement?.interSettlementRelationships || []`, filtering out typed conflict/faction_engagement rows (`.filter(r => !r.type)`) to mirror RelationshipsTab.jsx:130 which treats only untyped entries as raw NPC contacts. Map fields directly from the entry: npc=r.npcName, role=r.npcRole, partnerName=r.partnerName, partnerRole=r.partnerRole, partnerSettlement=r.partnerSettlement, relType=r.relType — note these are top-level entry fields (npcName/partnerName), NOT npc.name/npc.role as the current code assumes; the entry already carries partnerSettlement. Optionally also fold in neighbourNetwork[].npcConnections (primaryNPCName/neighbourNPCName) for live/unsaved links, but interSettlementRelationships alone covers saved linked campaigns. The de-dup the legend/colors use REL_COLORS keyed by relType should be checked against the relType vocabulary actually emitted (trade_partner/allied/patron/client/rival/cold_war/hostile/neutral per RelationshipsTab.jsx:136) so colored pips resolve rather than always falling back to neutral.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end. The campaign PDF's buildNPCConnections (src/utils/generateCampaignPDF.js:432-435) reads `npc.interSettlementNpcs || []` per NPC. An exhaustive grep (--include js/jsx/ts/sql, excluding node_modules) shows `interSettlementNpcs` appears ONLY in this file (line 430 comment + line 435 read) — no generator, store, lib, or migration ever writes it onto an NPC object. The authoritative producer of cross-settlement NPC contacts is buildInterSettlementNPCs (src/components/SettlementsPanel.jsx:58-77), which on every neighbour-link writes records to settlement.interSettlementRelationships (consumed at SettlementsPanel.jsx:874,877,935,938), carrying exactly the fields the PDF table wants: npcName, npcRole, partnerName, partnerRole, partnerSettlement, relType, description, linkId. The on-screen dossier reads these correctly (RelationshipsTab.jsx:130 filters interSettlementRelationships entries with no `type`).

Concrete failure path: User clicks campaign export (SettlementsPanel.jsx:284) -> generateCampaignPDF -> buildNPCConnections is invoked unconditionally whenever the campaign has >=1 settlement (generateCampaignPDF.js:742-743). For every save it iterates npcs and reads the nonexistent npc.interSettlementNpcs, so `connections` is always [], and the page always prints "No cross-settlement NPC contacts recorded" (line 450-456) — even for fully linked campaigns whose on-screen Relationships tab shows those exact contacts. This is a real dossier/PDF parity drift on the normal export path. Non-crashing, no data loss, so medium severity. Confidence ~0.93: a skeptical reviewer with full code access would confirm the grep result and the field mismatch.
verdicts.fix_note: Fix direction is correct but needs precision. Source the data from save.settlement.interSettlementRelationships, NOT a per-NPC field. Filter to NPC-contact entries only: entries with NO `type` property (typed entries with type==='conflict' or 'faction_engagement' are cross-settlement *engagements*, a separate concept — see RelationshipsTab.jsx:38-43,130). Field mapping: home = save.name || save.settlement?.name; npc-name = isr.npcName (not npc.name); role = isr.npcRole; then isr.partnerName / isr.partnerRole / isr.partnerSettlement / isr.relType map directly to the existing FROM/NPC/CONTACT/AT columns. Caution on the proposed neighbourNetwork[].npcConnections fallback: it is a different shape (keys primaryNPCName/neighbourNPCName, RelationshipsTab.jsx:100-102,113-114) used by the live/legacy generator path and is NOT populated by the current save-time buildInterSettlementNPCs flow; treat interSettlementRelationships(no-type) as the primary/only source, and only add npcConnections as a secondary source if you also dedup against it.

confirmed.title: Campaign PDF digest 'OVERVIEW' block is always blank — reads characterSummary/description/overview which no settlement carries
confirmed.evidence: buildDigest: `const overview = s(st_.characterSummary || st_.description || st_.overview || '')` (generateCampaignPDF.js:581). None of these three fields is produced anywhere in src (grep finds them only in this file); the settlement's character prose lives at `history.historicalCharacter` (rendered by OverviewTab.jsx:90, HistoryTab.jsx:83, and the settlement PDF identity slice). Every digest card prints an 'OVERVIEW' header with three empty lines beneath it.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Use `st_.history?.historicalCharacter || st_.arrivalScene || st_.pressureSentence || ''` as the digest overview source.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the claim by reading the cited code and tracing the data shape. At generateCampaignPDF.js:581, `buildDigest` computes `const overview = s(st_.characterSummary || st_.description || st_.overview || '')`, where `st_ = save.settlement` (the full generated settlement object — confirmed at line 537). `buildDigest` is invoked unconditionally for any non-empty campaign (line 750), so the code path is live and reachable.

None of the three read fields is a top-level settlement field:
- `characterSummary`: grep across all of src finds it ONLY at this one line (no producer anywhere).
- `overview`: the only `overview:` producer is aiLayer.js:350, but it's nested inside `buildLocalNotes`/the `notes` sub-object (`notes.overview`), not top-level — so `st_.overview` is undefined.
- `description`: never assigned to a settlement's top level; only appears on nested sub-objects (history events, tensions, factions, neighbour links). The CanonicalSettlement typedef (settlement.schema.js:74-145) has no top-level characterSummary/overview/description.

The actual character prose lives at `history.historicalCharacter`, produced by historyGenerator.js:1077 and consumed by OverviewTab.jsx:90, HistoryTab.jsx:83, SummaryTab.jsx:319, viewModel.js:352/827, and headlines.js:174 — exactly as the reviewer states. Therefore the digest's OVERVIEW value is always '' and the section is always blank in the campaign PDF.

One small correction to the evidence wording: `wrap(d, '', ...)` will return zero lines, so `clampLines(ovLines, 3)` iterates nothing — the result is a blank OVERVIEW header + rule with no body, not literally "three empty lines." The substantive claim (block always blank) is correct.

This is a real, reachable parity/data-integrity defect on a user-facing exported artifact (campaign PDF digest), distinct from the main dossier/PDF code path. Not money/security/crash/wrong-simulation, so medium severity is appropriate. The suggested fix (`st_.history?.historicalCharacter || ...`) correctly targets the canonical field.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested primary source `st_.history?.historicalCharacter` is correct and matches the canonical field used everywhere else. Two notes on the fallbacks in the suggested fix: `st_.arrivalScene` and `st_.pressureSentence` were not verified as existing top-level fields here, so the fix should not assume they populate — `st_.history?.historicalCharacter || ''` alone resolves the reported defect. Also, the digest currently prints the 'OVERVIEW' header and rule line even when the body is empty; if desired, consider gating the header on a non-empty overview so blank cards don't render a dangling header. Minor evidence correction: the empty value yields zero wrapped lines (a blank section), not literally three empty lines.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end. In src/utils/generateCampaignPDF.js, buildDigest() iterates campaign settlements and for each one prints an unconditional 'OVERVIEW' header (line 578: d.text('OVERVIEW', L_X, bodyY)), then fills the body from `const overview = s(st_.characterSummary || st_.description || st_.overview || '')` (line 581), where st_ = save.settlement (line 537).

Trace of the real settlement object: src/generators/steps/assembleSettlement.js (the final assembly step, provides 'settlement') builds the object with top-level keys name/tier/population/institutions/neighborRelationship/economicState/npcs/factions/conflicts/history/etc. It never sets characterSummary, description, or overview. normalizeSettlement adds only version/id/container fields — grep of src/domain/normalizeSettlement.js for those three names returns nothing.

I reproduced against the real modules: generateSettlementPipeline({tier:'village',terrain:'temperate_forest',culture:'germanic',seed:12345}) yields characterSummary=undefined, description=undefined, overview=undefined, while history.historicalCharacter is a real non-empty string ("Religious history here is complicated..."). The product's character prose lives at settlement.history.historicalCharacter and is rendered exactly that way on-screen (OverviewTab.jsx:90, HistoryTab.jsx:83, SummaryTab.jsx:319) and in the settlement PDF (HistoryFounding.jsx:85). The campaign digest is the only consumer reaching for the three phantom fields (grep finds characterSummary/overview top-level reads only in this file).

Helper behavior confirms the failure surface: s(undefined) === '' (line 66-71); wrap(d,'',...) -> splitTextToSize('') -> [''], clampLines([''],3) -> [''], so the loop prints exactly one empty line under the header. The header itself is unconditional, so EVERY digest card renders an 'OVERVIEW' label with blank body — a dead label occupying the entire left column of every settlement card.

Reachability: generateCampaignPDF is exported (line 709), calls buildDigest unconditionally (line 750), and is wired to the campaign 'export PDF' button in SettlementsPanel.jsx:284. So any user exporting a campaign PDF gets this on every card.

Not critical/high: no data loss, no money/security, no crash, and the per-settlement PDF and on-screen views are unaffected. It is a real, always-on parity/content defect in a shipped export path — a blank labeled section plus loss of the available character prose — so medium.
verdicts.fix_note: The phantom fields should be replaced with the real source. Primary source is settlement.history.historicalCharacter (matches on-screen OverviewTab/HistoryTab and the settlement PDF identity slice for parity). Suggested: `const overview = s(st_.history?.historicalCharacter || st_.settlementReason || '')`. settlementReason is a genuine top-level field (assembleSettlement.js:74) and is a reasonable secondary fallback for the 'overview' intent if historicalCharacter is empty. Keeping characterSummary/description/overview in the chain is harmless but dead — they are never produced. Additionally, the header at line 578 should be made conditional on `overview` being non-empty (only print 'OVERVIEW' + hline when there is content), so cards without prose don't show a dangling label. Note also a sibling latent issue in the same function (out of scope for this claim): line 627 reads st_.neighbourNetwork, which freshly generated settlements also do not carry (the pipeline produces neighborRelationship, an object); neighbourNetwork is only populated later by SettlementsPanel link actions, so the 'N links' count silently reads 0 for unlinked settlements — confirm whether that is intended before fixing.

confirmed.title: Legacy (flag-off) food-balance path reads fb.production/fb.need but engine emits dailyProduction/dailyNeed — PDF shows 'Not calculated' and loses deficit %
confirmed.evidence: foodCore's non-canonical branch (viewModel.js:82-93) reads `fb?.production`, `fb?.need`, and computes deficitPct from `Number(fb?.need)`. The engine's metrics.foodBalance is `{ dailyNeed, dailyProduction, deficit, deficitPercent, surplus, importCoverage, rawDeficit, ... }` (economicGenerator.js:657-677) — no production/need keys, and the precomputed `deficitPercent` (which the web uses directly, OverviewTab.jsx:157-159) is ignored. With flag('canonicalViewModel') off, FoodBalanceBlock (EconomicsTrade.jsx:432-466) always shows 'Not calculated' for produced/needed and omits '% of need' even when the engine computed both; the screen shows the full deficit bar. (The canonical-path fix exists per the comment at viewModel.js:51-55, but the legacy path remains live behind the flag; flags.js was unreadable in this checkout.)
confirmed.suggested_fix: In the legacy branch read `fb?.dailyProduction ?? fb?.production`, `fb?.dailyNeed ?? fb?.need`, and `fb?.deficitPercent ?? computed` — or delete the legacy branch once canonicalViewModel is default-on.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim's technical mechanics are correct but its load-bearing premise — "the legacy path remains live behind the flag" — is false. The reviewer explicitly admitted "flags.js was unreadable in this checkout," and that file is the dispositive evidence.

Verified facts:
1. The field-name mismatch is real in isolation. The engine emits foodBalance as { dailyNeed, dailyProduction, deficit, deficitPercent, ... } (src/generators/economicGenerator.js:657-677) with NO production/need keys. The legacy branch (src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:82-93) reads fb?.production / fb?.need, so produced/needed resolve to null, legacyNeed = Number(undefined)||0 = 0, and deficitPct (line 92) is always null. Downstream, FoodBalanceBlock shows "Not calculated" (EconomicsTrade.jsx:464) and omits "% of need" (lines 471-473, gated on fb.deficitPct != null). So IF the legacy branch ran, the output would be broken as described.

2. But the legacy branch is unreachable on every real path. canonicalViewModel.default = true (src/lib/flags.js:68), documented as "PROMOTED default-on; flag retained as soak killswitch." Nothing in src/, env files, or the ~2400 tests sets it false — no setFlagOverride('canonicalViewModel', false), no VITE_FLAG_CANONICAL_VIEW_MODEL, no test override (grep confirmed empty). flag('canonicalViewModel') resolves to the default true in prod, CI, and default checkouts, so foodCore always takes the canonical branch (viewModel.js:67-81) via deriveFoodBalance, which reads the correct fb.dailyProduction/fb.dailyNeed (dossierViewModel.js:75-76) and produces the same data the web shows.

3. The legacy branch executes only if a developer/QA manually flips flag.canonicalViewModel=false via URL param or localStorage — i.e., deliberately exercising the rollback killswitch. Its retention (stale field names and all) is intentional per the flag doctrine (flags.js:16-18). The reviewer's own fix says "delete the legacy branch once canonicalViewModel is default-on" — and it IS default-on.

A skeptical senior reviewer would not call a default-off, never-enabled killswitch branch a real medium defect: the claimed user-facing impact ("PDF shows Not calculated, loses deficit %") does not occur on any real path.
verdicts.fix_note: Not actionable as a bug. If anything, since canonicalViewModel is default-on and no path disables it, the legacy branch is dead code in practice; the right cleanup (matching the reviewer's own alternative) is to delete the legacy branch and inline the canonical path per the flags.js doctrine ("If a flag would be on everywhere forever, delete the flag and inline the change"), not to patch the stale field reads. That is a cleanup, not a bug fix, and is out of scope for a defect finding.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED MECHANISM, DORMANT REACHABILITY. The technical claim is exactly correct and I reproduced it.

Field-name mismatch (confirmed): The engine's metrics.foodBalance is emitted at src/generators/economicGenerator.js:657-677 with keys dailyNeed, dailyProduction, deficit, deficitPercent, surplus, importCoverage, rawDeficit (plus agricultureModifier/importChannel/magicFoodOffset). There is NO production or need key anywhere. The legacy branch of foodCore (src/pdf/lib/viewModel.js:82-93) reads fb?.production and fb?.need, and computes deficitPct from Number(fb?.need). Against the real shape both production and need resolve to null, and legacyNeed=0 so deficitPct=null.

Downstream consequence (confirmed): FoodBalanceBlock (src/pdf/sections/EconomicsTrade.jsx:432-466) takes prod=finite(fb.production,0)=0 and need=finite(fb.need,0)=0 -> rawKnown=false -> renders the "Not calculated" branch instead of the produced/needed bars, and the omitted deficitPct drops the "% of need" while the engine's correct deficitPercent (e.g. 30) is discarded. I demonstrated this with a node one-liner replaying the legacy foodCore logic + FoodBalanceBlock gate against the engine's real foodBalance shape: output {production:null, need:null, deficit:300, deficitPct:null} -> "Not calculated", deficitPct null.

Parity drift (confirmed): The web reads the precomputed value directly — OverviewTab.jsx:157-159 uses foodBal.deficitPercent for the bar width and label — so on-screen the full deficit shows while the legacy-path PDF shows "Not calculated" with no percent. The canonical fix (src/domain/display/dossierViewModel.js:10-12, 75-76) explicitly documents this exact pre-existing PDF bug ("read foodBalance.production/.need — fields that don't exist") as the reason it reads dailyProduction/dailyNeed.

REACHABILITY is why this is only low, not high: flag canonicalViewModel defaults to TRUE (src/lib/flags.js:67-70, "PROMOTED default-on; flag retained as soak killswitch"). flag() (flags.js:184-189) returns the default unless overridden via URL param, localStorage, or VITE_FLAG_CANONICALVIEWMODEL. In normal production foodCore always takes the canonical branch and renders correctly; the legacy branch is dead code. It becomes live ONLY when the killswitch is explicitly flipped off (incident rollback / QA / staging env). No checked-in config flips it off. So this is a latent regression that resurfaces the original bug if the documented rollback path is ever exercised — real, but dormant. All three foodCore call sites (viewModel.js:365, 557) share the same flag gate, so the blast radius on flag-off is both the raw and active economics slices.
verdicts.fix_note: The diagnosis is accurate. Cleanest fix is to delete the legacy branch entirely and always route through deriveFoodBalance — the canonicalViewModel flag has soaked default-on and the legacy shape reads non-existent keys, so it has no correct behavior to preserve (it is strictly a regression-on-rollback). Per the flags.js doctrine note ("If a flag would be on everywhere forever, delete the flag and inline the change"), this flag's food-balance gating should be inlined. If the killswitch must be kept for the other behaviors it gates (viabilitySummaryFor at viewModel.js:103, deriveSystemState.js:109, ViabilityTab.jsx:20), then at minimum the legacy foodCore branch should read fb?.dailyProduction/fb?.dailyNeed and use fb?.deficitPercent directly rather than recomputing from the non-existent need key — i.e. mirror what deriveFoodBalance already does so flag-off no longer silently regresses PDF food balance.

confirmed.title: PDF Services chapter prints trade-impaired supply chains as 'Active' — ChainCard ignores chain.status/dependency that the web surfaces prominently
confirmed.evidence: ChainCard derives status only from resourceDepleted/substituteActive/entrepot (Services.jsx:196-210), falling through to 'Active' with a green tone. But the engine sets `chain.status = 'impaired' | 'vulnerable'` from critical trade dependencies (computeActiveChains.js:287-293) and attaches `chain.dependency`; the web ServicesTab's headline feature is exactly this impairment state (computeChainSets in tabHelpers.js + 'N impaired' badges throughout ServicesTab.jsx). A chain impaired by a severed critical import prints as a healthy 'Active' chain in the dossier.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Check `chain.status` first: impaired → tone 'bad'/'Impaired', vulnerable → 'warn'/'Vulnerable', then the existing depleted/substitute/entrepôt branches; optionally render chain.dependency.resource as the reason.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified the full data path and the claim holds. (1) Engine: computeActiveChains.js:269-293 cross-references tradeDependencies and sets chain.status = 'impaired' (critical severity) or 'vulnerable' (high severity), plus attaches chain.dependency {institution, resource, severity, impact, affectedServices}. These trade dependencies are critical IMPORT relationships, independent of the local-resource booleans. (2) Data reaches the PDF unmodified: viewModel.js:708 sets vm.services.activeChains = s.economicState.activeChains (the raw engine chain objects, including .status and .dependency). (3) The PDF ChainCard (Services.jsx:196-210) derives both tone and statusLabel ONLY from chain.resourceDepleted / chain.substituteActive / chain.entrepot, with the final fallthrough being tone 'good' (green) / label 'Active'. It never reads chain.status or chain.dependency. (4) An import-impaired chain (severed critical import) with a healthy local resource has resourceDepleted=false, substituteActive=false, entrepot=false, so it prints as green 'Active'. (5) The web ServicesTab makes impairment its headline: ServicesTab.jsx:22 computeChainSets, plus 'N impaired' badges at lines 89, 153, 211, 241. This is a genuine dossier/PDF parity drift — the named bug class. Not a crash/data-loss/determinism issue, and only manifests when tradeDependencies exist, hence medium severity. The suggested fix is correct in direction; minor refinement: status string values are 'impaired'/'vulnerable', and dependency.resource/institution can be surfaced via the existing note/meta slots.
verdicts.fix_note: Direction is correct. Concretely: in ChainCard at src/pdf/sections/Services.jsx:196, prepend status-based branches before the existing resourceDepleted/substituteActive/entrepot ladder — chain.status === 'impaired' -> tone 'bad', label 'Impaired'; chain.status === 'vulnerable' -> tone 'warn', label 'Vulnerable'. Surface the reason via the existing meta/note slots, e.g. push { label: 'DEPENDS', value: humanize(label(chain.dependency.resource)) } and/or chain.dependency.institution when chain.dependency is present (it carries {institution, resource, severity, impact, affectedServices}). Keep the depleted/substitute/entrepot branches as lower-priority fallthroughs since a chain can be both resource-depleted and import-impaired; impaired should win.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED — concrete failure path traced end-to-end with a working reproduction.

TRACE:
1. Engine: economicGenerator.js:2543-2545 puts `tradeDependencies: H` and `activeChains` onto `economicState`. computeActiveChains (computeActiveChains.js:270-296) enriches each chain: when a trade dependency matches a chain's processing institution, it sets `chain.dependency` and `chain.status = 'impaired'` (severity 'critical') or `'vulnerable'` (severity 'high'/'medium'). These are real engine outputs — computeChainSets in tabHelpers.js:12-37 consumes the same tradeDependencies, so impaired chains genuinely occur whenever a settlement has a critical import dependency.

2. PDF data path: viewModel.js:708 sets services.activeChains = `s?.economicState?.activeChains` directly (raw, un-mapped) — so the chain objects reaching the PDF DO carry `chain.status` and `chain.dependency`.

3. PDF render: Services.jsx renders each via ChainCard (Services.jsx:196-210). ChainCard derives tone/status ONLY from `resourceDepleted` -> 'bad', `substituteActive` -> 'warn', `entrepot` -> 'cool', else fall through to 'Active'/'good'. It NEVER reads `chain.status` or `chain.dependency` (grep of Services.jsx confirms no occurrence). palette.good = '#1a5a28' (green).

REPRODUCTION (ran the exact ChainCard ladder against the chain shape computeActiveChains emits):
  - chain {status:'impaired', dependency:{severity:'critical'}, resourceDepleted:false, substituteActive:false, entrepot:false} -> PDF renders {tone:'good', statusLabel:'Active'} (green).
  - chain {status:'vulnerable', ...} -> {tone:'good', statusLabel:'Active'} (green).

PARITY CONTRAST: web SupplyChainsPanel.jsx:92 reads chain.status via getStatus(chain.status) and renders 'impaired' as red (#8b1a1a, ✕ badge) and 'vulnerable' as amber (#8a5010, ◐), sorts impaired chains first (lines 203-204, 269-271), and ServicesTab.jsx shows prominent "N impaired" badges (lines 89, 211, 241). So the dossier's headline supply-chain health signal is silently dropped in the PDF: a chain crippled by a severed critical import prints as a healthy green 'Active' chain.

CONSEQUENCE: dossier/PDF parity drift on a load-bearing simulation signal. A DM reading the printed PDF sees a healthy economy where the on-screen settlement shows impaired/vulnerable supply chains.
