Not data loss, no crash, no money/security impact — hence medium, not high. It is a genuine wrong-output parity bug, not a style nit. Confidence ~0.9.
verdicts.fix_note:
Suggested fix is correct in direction (ChainCard must read chain.status/chain.dependency), with two refinements:

1. Keep resourceDepleted as the most-severe override. resourceDepleted (a depleted LOCAL resource — chain literally cannot run) is a separate dimension from status (trade-dependency severity). Layer status BELOW resourceDepleted but ABOVE the plain 'Active' fallback, e.g.:
   tone = resourceDepleted ? 'bad'
        : status === 'impaired' ? 'bad'
        : (substituteActive || status === 'vulnerable') ? 'warn'
        : entrepot ? 'cool'
        : 'good';
   with matching statusLabel ('Impaired' / 'Vulnerable'). This avoids regressing the existing resource-depleted/substitute/entrepot labels while surfacing dependency impairment. Note StatusCard already has STATUS_TONE entries impaired:'bad', vulnerable:'warn' (Visuals.jsx:237-243), so passing status straight through (status={chain.status}) without an explicit tone override would also map correctly — but you'd lose the resourceDepleted/substitute/entrepot labels, so an explicit ladder is safer.

2. Consider surfacing chain.dependency (institution/resource/impact) in the card note/body so the PDF explains WHY a chain is impaired, matching the web's depReasons tooltips — otherwise the PDF shows 'Impaired' with no rationale while the web shows the severed import.

confirmed.title: NPC power tints and high-power promotion are dead code — thresholds 40/60/80 vs engine NPC power scale 1-10
confirmed.evidence: powerTone (NPCQuickRef.jsx:25-31) returns 'bad'/≥80, 'warn'/≥60, 'gold'/≥40 — but engine NPC power is 1-10 (npcGenerator.js:54-56: high = 8+rng*3, mid = 4-7, low = 1-3; `influence` is the string level). Every pill is therefore 'muted', contradicting the chapter headline 'Power tints flag who's worth caring about' (:91). Same scale mismatch in NotableNPCs.jsx:59 (HIGH_POWER = 80): the documented 'a settlement with eight 90+ power players isn't artificially trimmed' promotion can never fire, so the major tier is always exactly the top 3.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Calibrate to the engine scale (e.g. ≥8 bad/notable, ≥6 warn, ≥4 gold; HIGH_POWER = 8) or use npc.influence ('high'/'moderate'/'low').

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified end-to-end and could not refute it. Engine NPC power is strictly 1-10: npcGenerator.js:54-56 sets power to 8+floor(rng*3) (high), 4+floor(rng*4) (mid, i.e. 4-7), 1+floor(rng*3) (low, i.e. 1-3), with `influence` being the string level ('high'/'moderate'/'low'). There is NO rescaling between engine and PDF: the pipeline (generateSettlementPipeline.js:14,79) calls generateNPCs, and viewModel.js:915 sets `power: n?.power || 0` verbatim; sorted lists (viewModel.js:930) just sort by that raw value. Faction power uses a separate, larger scale (powerGenerator.js: values 7-25+), but the NPC sections read npc.power, not faction power.

Given max NPC power = 10:
1) powerTone (NPCQuickRef.jsx:25-31): branches `>=80`/`>=60`/`>=40` are unreachable; npc.power is non-null in normal generation so the `p==null` branch is also not taken, so every pill falls through to the final `return 'muted'`. This contradicts the chapter headline "Power tints flag who's worth caring about" (NPCQuickRef.jsx:91). Dead code, as claimed.
2) NotableNPCs.jsx:59,65 HIGH_POWER=80: `p >= HIGH_POWER` is always false, so the only promotion path is `major.length < majorMin` (majorMin=3). The major tier is therefore always exactly the top 3, and the documented "eight 90+ power players isn't artificially trimmed" behavior (NotableNPCs.jsx:10-12) can never fire. Dead code, as claimed.

No test pins these thresholds (none found), legacy generateSettlement.js no longer exists (only the pipeline), and the on-screen dossier does not apply a comparable NPC power tint, so there is no parity expectation forcing the current values. Both cited defects are genuine.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct in direction. Two refinements: (a) For NPCQuickRef.powerTone, calibrating to the 1-10 scale is right; aligning to the engine's own buckets is cleaner than arbitrary cutoffs — e.g. >=8 'bad' (high tier), >=4 'warn'/'gold' (mid), else 'muted' (low). Using npc.influence ('high'/'moderate'/'low') is the most robust option since it is the engine's authoritative level and avoids re-deriving thresholds. Note the influence string is 'moderate' (not 'medium') per npcGenerator.js:55. (b) For NotableNPCs HIGH_POWER, set to 8 (top tier) so genuinely high-power figures past the top-3 cap get promoted to major. Either approach keeps determinism intact since power/influence are seeded engine outputs.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end. Engine NPC `power` is produced only by generateNPCGoal (npcGenerator.js:54-56): high = 8+floor(rng*3) → {8,9,10}, mid = 4+floor(rng*4) → {4..7}, low = 1+floor(rng*3) → {1,2,3}. So the domain is 1-10. The PDF viewModel passes it through unscaled (viewModel.js:915, `power: n?.power || 0`); no rescaling to 0-100 exists anywhere. Corroborated by every internal consumer treating power as 1-10 (npcGenerator.js:507/648/678: powerGap <= 1, >= 4) and by the WEB dossier, which buckets NPCs by the `influence` STRING ('high'/'moderate'/'low'), never by a numeric 0-100 scale (npcComponents.jsx:39 ord={high:0,moderate:1,low:2}). The 0-100 assumption lives only in the two PDF sites named.

Trace 1 (NPCQuickRef.jsx:25-31, 68): powerTone returns 'bad'/≥80, 'warn'/≥60, 'gold'/≥40, else 'muted'. Verified by node: for every reachable engine value 1..10 the function returns exactly 'muted' (the 40/60/80 branches are unreachable dead code). 'muted' maps to a flat off-white pill (theme.js:205 toneBg.muted='#f5f0e8', :19 palette.muted='#6b5340') — visually identical to a null-power pill. So the chapter's own headline (NPCQuickRef.jsx:91, "Power tints flag who's worth caring about") is never delivered: every PWR pill is the same flat gray. The numeric value still prints, so it is a degraded signal, not missing data.

Trace 2 (NotableNPCs.jsx:59,65): HIGH_POWER = 80, with promotion `p >= HIGH_POWER`. Max reachable power is 10, so the condition is never true. The documented "≥80 over-cap promotion so a settlement with eight 90+ power players isn't artificially trimmed" (NotableNPCs.jsx:8-13) is inert; the Major Figures tier is always exactly the top 3 by power (top 3 + next 4 + rest). Verified by node (10 >= 80 === false).

Not a determinism/data-loss/security issue and no crash; it is a broken/dead-code defect in PDF output where an advertised tint signal is absent and a documented tiering rule never fires. Medium.

Disambiguation checked: the unrelated powerTone in headlines.js:64 keys on a power-structure stability string and is used only for FACTION headlines (PowerStructure.jsx) — not affected. The Major/Compact PWR pills in NotableNPCs (lines 156, 350) are hardcoded tone='gold', so only NPCQuickRef uses the broken variable tint.
verdicts.fix_note: Fix direction is correct: rescale to the 1-10 engine scale. For NPCQuickRef.powerTone use the engine's level bands — p>=8 → top tone, p>=4 → mid tone, else 'muted'. For NotableNPCs set HIGH_POWER = 8 (matching the engine's 'high' floor). Two refinements: (1) Don't map highest power to 'bad' — 'bad' is the dark-red criminal/hostile tone (theme.js:31), semantically wrong for "most powerful"; prefer 'gold' for top and 'warn' (amber) for mid, which also keeps it consistent with the Major/Compact cards that already hardcode tone='gold'. (2) Cleaner still: tint off the existing `influence` string ('high'/'moderate'/'low') the same way the web side does (npcComponents.jsx), since that is the canonical, already-surfaced signal (npc.influenceLabel is in the viewModel) and avoids re-encoding magic numeric thresholds that drift from the generator. Either approach fixes both the dead-code tint and the never-firing promotion.

confirmed.title: Chapter headline helpers read fields that don't exist on the slices they receive — power/resources/relationships headlines degrade or never render
confirmed.evidence: All visible call sites pass the corresponding vm slice (economicsHeadline(vm.economics), defenseHeadline(vm.defense), npcsHeadline(vm.npcs), viabilityHeadline(vm.viability), hooksHeadline(vm.hooks)). But: resourcesHeadline reads `resources.primaryExports/primaryImports` (headlines.js:144-153) which the resources slice doesn't have — the ResourcesProduction.jsx:51 call site papers over it with `r.exports || r.exportPotential` where r.exports is also not a slice key and exportPotential is empty (see exports-field finding), so the headline is effectively always null; relationshipsHeadline reads `rel.all || rel.relationships` (:216-221) — relationshipsSlice has neither key (it has internal/interSettlement/neighbours), so it always returns null; powerHeadline reads `factions[].faction` (:48-58) but powerSlice normalizes factions to `{ name, ... }` (viewModel.js:434), so the 'X governs' / challenger phrases can never include faction names. defenseHeadline's `def.magicDependency` (:114) is also absent from the defense slice.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Align each headline to its slice shape: powerHeadline use f.name; relationshipsHeadline use rel.neighbours/internal; resourcesHeadline accept the slice's exports field once it's fixed; add magicDependency to defenseSlice.

verdicts.reasoning:
Mixed finding, but the core defect is real and verified. The reviewer's blanket premise — "All visible call sites pass the corresponding vm slice" — is FALSE for two of the four cited helpers, which refutes those two sub-claims:

(1) resourcesHeadline: ResourcesProduction.jsx:51 does NOT pass vm.resources. It adapts via `resourcesHeadline({ primaryExports: r.exports || r.exportPotential || [], primaryImports: importsCritical })`. So headlines.js:144-153 reading `resources.primaryExports/primaryImports` is correct for the adapter shape, and the headline is NOT "always null" — it renders an imports line whenever importsCritical is non-empty. (The exports half is dead only because exportPotential is never populated by any generator — grep of src/generators and src/data returns nothing — but that is the separate "exports-field" finding, not a headlines.js bug.)

(2) relationshipsHeadline: Relationships.jsx:60 passes `relationshipsHeadline({ all: r.neighbours || [] })`, so `rel.all` (headlines.js:218) is exactly the adapter key supplied. It renders whenever neighbours exist; it does NOT "always return null."

The other two sub-claims ARE genuine, verified field mismatches against slices passed directly:

(3) powerHeadline (headlines.js:53,55-56) reads `top?.faction` / `challenger?.faction`, but PowerStructure.jsx:41 passes vm.power directly and powerSlice (viewModel.js:433) normalizes each faction to `{ name: f?.faction || f?.name }` — there is no `.faction` field on the normalized object. So line 53's "X governs" branch never fires (falls to "<GovType> rule" at :54) and the challenger "pressing close behind" phrase (:56) never includes a faction name (falls to the generic "N other factions compete" at :57). The governing/challenger faction names genuinely never appear in this headline. REAL.

(4) defenseHeadline `def.magicDependency` (headlines.js:114): DefenseSecurity.jsx:60 passes vm.defense directly, but defenseSlice's return (viewModel.js:623-656) has no magicDependency key — the field actually lives on viabilitySlice (viewModel.js:808 `magicDependency: !!dp?.magicDependency`). So the "magic-dependent" tag is always omitted from the defense headline. REAL.

All four sections are reachable (SettlementPDF.jsx:126-135 render them) and there are no tests covering these helpers. Because two of the four cited mechanisms are concrete, evidence-backed defects (faction names never shown in power headline; magic-dependent tag never shown in defense headline), the finding's central thesis — headline helpers reading fields absent from the data they receive, degrading output — holds, even though the reviewer overstated scope and got the resources/relationships reasoning backwards.
verdicts.fix_note: Scope correction: the resources and relationships portions of this finding are FALSE positives — those call sites (ResourcesProduction.jsx:51, Relationships.jsx:60) build adapter objects with exactly the keys the helpers read (primaryExports/primaryImports, all), so no fix is needed there for the headline-vs-slice mismatch (the empty-exports issue belongs to the separate exportPotential finding). Only two fixes are warranted: (a) powerHeadline (headlines.js:53,55-56) should read f.name instead of f.faction to match powerSlice's normalized shape (viewModel.js:433); (b) defenseHeadline's magicDependency check (headlines.js:114) needs either adding `magicDependency: !!(active?.defenseProfile?.magicDependency)` to defenseSlice (viewModel.js:623-656) or wiring the helper to read it from the viability slice where it already exists (viewModel.js:808). Both are low-impact cosmetic improvements to a single TLDR line.

verdicts.reasoning:
I traced all four sub-claims against the real slice shapes (viewModel.js) and the actual call sites (the section JSX), and reproduced behavior with a node one-liner against the real headlines.js module.

CONFIRMED REAL:
1. powerHeadline (headlines.js:48-58) reads `top?.faction` / `challenger?.faction`, but powerSlice (viewModel.js:433-448) normalizes every faction to `{ name, power, ... }` with NO `faction` key. PowerStructure.jsx:41 passes the slice directly (`p = vm.power`). Reproduced: a realistic slice with governing faction "The Merchant Guild" and challenger "The Temple" yields just "Council rule." — faction names never appear and the "with X pressing close behind" challenger phrase can never fire (line 55 gates on `challenger?.faction`). This is the most material part: every settlement with named factions gets a degraded power-chapter headline in both on-screen dossier and PDF. Unguarded — no test imports pdf/lib/headlines.js.
2. defenseHeadline `def.magicDependency` (headlines.js:114): defenseSlice (viewModel.js:623-656) has no `magicDependency` key (it lives on viability/identity/overview slices). The "magic-dependent" tag never appends. Real but minor; rest of the headline works.
3. resourcesHeadline export half: real underlying data bug, but the claim is overstated. The call site (ResourcesProduction.jsx:51) does NOT pass the slice — it passes an adapter `{ primaryExports: r.exports || r.exportPotential || [], primaryImports: importsCritical }`. The slice has no `exports` key, and `exportPotential` (viewModel.js:774) reads `ra?.exportPotential` which the engine never sets — the engine emits exports under `resourceAnalysis.exports` (resourceGenerator.js:267, populated by evaluateInstitutionDeps). So the export half is always empty due to a wrong-key mapping. BUT the import half works: `importsCritical = r.imports?.critical` is a real, commonly-populated engine field (resourceGenerator buildViabilityReport). Reproduced: empty exports + ['iron','salt'] imports → "reliant on imported iron, salt." So resourcesHeadline is NOT "effectively always null" — it is exports-missing, imports-only.

CLAIM WRONG:
4. relationshipsHeadline: the call site (Relationships.jsx:60) passes an adapter `{ all: r.neighbours || [] }`, NOT vm.relationships. relationshipsHeadline reads `rel.all` and works fine — reproduced "2 external relationships on file." The assertion that it "always returns null because the slice has neither all nor relationships" is false; the slice is never passed.

Net: a real defect class exists (headline helpers vs slice/adapter shape mismatch), with the power/faction-name degradation being the strongest, clearly real, and on a main rendered path (dossier + PDF, every settlement). The magic-dependent and exports-key gaps are also real. Two of the four specifics (relationships, "resources always null") are inaccurate. No crash, no data loss, no determinism break, no parity drift between PDF and screen (both render from the same viewModel/headlines), so severity is medium.
verdicts.fix_note:
The finding is directionally right but two of its four specifics are inaccurate; fix only the real parts.

REAL, fix these:
1. powerHeadline (headlines.js:53,55,56): change `top?.faction` / `challenger?.faction` to `top?.name` / `challenger?.name`, since powerSlice (viewModel.js:433) emits `name`, not `faction`. This is the highest-value fix — without it the power-chapter headline degrades to bare government-type ("Council rule.") and never names the governing/challenger factions, on every settlement. Verified by reproduction.
2. defenseHeadline (headlines.js:114): `def.magicDependency` is never set on defenseSlice. Either add `magicDependency: !!dp?.magicDependency` to defenseSlice (viewModel.js defenseSlice return) — the same expression already used in overviewSlice:374/identitySlice:291 — or drop the line. Adding it to the slice is the parity-correct fix.
3. resources export half: the underlying bug is the wrong key. resourcesSlice (viewModel.js:774) reads `ra?.exportPotential`, but the engine emits exports under `resourceAnalysis.exports` (resourceGenerator.js:267). Map `exports: ra?.exports || []` (or alias it) and have the ResourcesProduction call site read it; otherwise the export half of the headline (and likely any export section) is permanently empty.

CLAIM INACCURATE, do NOT 'fix':
4. relationshipsHeadline is fine as-is: the call site (Relationships.jsx:60) passes an adapter `{ all: r.neighbours }`, so `rel.all` resolves. No change needed.
5. resourcesHeadline is NOT 'always null' — the import half works (`imports.critical` is a real populated field). Only the export half is broken.

Also: these chapter-headline helpers have zero test coverage (no test imports pdf/lib/headlines.js). A small unit test asserting power headline includes the governing faction name would have caught #1 and prevents regression.

confirmed.title: powerSlice defaults publicLegitimacy.breakdown to an array but the engine emits an object map
confirmed.evidence: powerSlice: `legitimacyBreakdown: s?.powerStructure?.publicLegitimacy?.breakdown || []` (viewModel.js:484). The engine's breakdown is an object: `breakdown: { prosperity: pContrib, safety: sContrib, defense: dContrib, food: fContrib }` (factionDynamics.js:130), and the web iterates it with Object.entries (PowerTab.jsx:91). The `|| []` default signals the PDF PowerStructure section expects an array — if it `.map()`s this field (PowerStructure.jsx was unreadable in this checkout), the export crashes with 'map is not a function' whenever publicLegitimacy exists; if it Object.entries it, the [] default is merely misleading. Either way the slice's declared shape disagrees with the engine.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Normalize in the slice: `Object.entries(breakdown || {}).map(([k, v]) => ({ key: k, delta: v }))` so the section gets a stable array.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed the data flow with file:line evidence. The engine emits publicLegitimacy.breakdown as a plain object {prosperity, safety, defense, food} (factionDynamics.js:131, JSDoc breakdown:Object at :74). assembleSettlement.js:114,119 mutates it as an object property (.breakdown.defense), never converting to an array, so it reaches the viewModel as an object. viewModel.js:484 reads it raw into legitimacyBreakdown with a `|| []` default that falsely declares an array shape, then passes it to ScoreWithBreakdown via PowerStructure.jsx:51 as the `breakdown` prop.

The reviewer's CRASH claim is REFUTED: ScoreWithBreakdown (Visuals.jsx:199) gates rendering on `breakdown?.length > 0` BEFORE calling `breakdown.map()` (line 201). A plain object's `.length` is undefined, so `undefined > 0` is false and the map() block is never entered — no 'map is not a function' crash. The reviewer never read this component (admitted it was "unreadable in this checkout") and guessed wrong on the crash path.

However the UNDERLYING defect is REAL: because the object has no `.length`, the guard fails and the PDF silently renders ZERO legitimacy breakdown chips, while the web (PowerTab.jsx:91) correctly renders them via `Object.entries(leg.breakdown).map(...)`. This is genuine PDF/dossier parity drift — a known bug class in this project — plus a misleading shape contract (`|| []` array default for object data). So isReal=true, but the mechanism is silent content omission, not a crash.

Severity: medium is right. It's not a crash on the main path (no data loss / money / security), and the PDF still renders fine — it just omits the legitimacy contribution chips that the on-screen dossier shows. That's real parity drift but cosmetic/informational, edge-section content. Confidence is high on the parity-drift defect existing; lower that a skeptical reviewer endorses the *crash* framing (which is false), but the finding's title and core claim ("declared shape disagrees with the engine") hold.
verdicts.fix_note:
The reviewer's crash mechanism is WRONG and should be corrected: ScoreWithBreakdown (src/pdf/primitives/Visuals.jsx:199) guards the render with `breakdown?.length > 0` BEFORE calling `breakdown.map()` (line 201). A plain object has `length === undefined`, so the guard short-circuits — there is NO 'map is not a function' crash. The actual bug is silent omission: the PDF renders zero legitimacy breakdown chips while the web (PowerTab.jsx:91) shows them via Object.entries — a real dossier/PDF parity drift, and the `|| []` default misdeclares the shape.

The suggested fix is on the right track but mislabels the keys. ScoreWithBreakdown reads each chip as `b?.delta ?? b?.value` and `b?.label || b?.name` (Visuals.jsx:202-203). The reviewer's `{ key, delta }` would render chips with EMPTY labels because `key` is never read. Correct normalization in viewModel.js:484: `Object.entries(s?.powerStructure?.publicLegitimacy?.breakdown || {}).map(([k, v]) => ({ label: k, delta: v }))`.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed shape mismatch, but the claimed crash does NOT occur — the real defect is silent parity drift.

Evidence chain (all read first-hand):
- Engine emits breakdown as an OBJECT: factionDynamics.js:131 `breakdown: { prosperity: pContrib, safety: sContrib, defense: dContrib, food: fContrib }`. Re-confirmed object shape by assembleSettlement.js:114/119 reading/writing `provLeg.breakdown.defense` as a keyed property. This is the canonical shape on both the legacy and pipeline paths (powerGenerator.js:2184 imports the same computePublicLegitimacy from factionDynamics.js).
- viewModel.js:484 passes it through raw: `legitimacyBreakdown: s?.powerStructure?.publicLegitimacy?.breakdown || []`. The `|| []` only fires when publicLegitimacy is absent; with legitimacy present (normal case) the raw OBJECT flows through unchanged. No array conversion anywhere (grep of src/pdf confirms only these two references).
- PDF consumer: PowerStructure.jsx:51 hands it to ScoreWithBreakdown as `breakdown`. ScoreWithBreakdown (Visuals.jsx:199-201) guards `breakdown?.length > 0` BEFORE `breakdown.map(...)`.

The original claim asserts an export crash (\"map is not a function\"). That is incorrect: a plain object's `.length` is undefined, so `undefined > 0` === false, the chips block is skipped entirely, and `.map()` is never reached. Verified with a node one-liner: `breakdown.length` = undefined, guard = false (no crash); `.map()` would only throw if it were reached, which it is not.

The REAL defect: the PDF silently renders ZERO legitimacy breakdown chips for every settlement that has a publicLegitimacy block (essentially all of them), whereas the on-screen web dossier renders them correctly — PowerTab.jsx:91 uses `Object.entries(leg.breakdown || {}).map(...)` producing chips like \"prosperity +10\", \"safety -5\". This is a deterministic dossier/PDF parity drift on the main export path. The slice's declared shape (array, per the `|| []` default) disagrees with the engine's object, and that disagreement causes data loss in the PDF, not a crash.
verdicts.fix_note:
The claim's crash mechanism is wrong (the `?.length > 0` guard preempts `.map()`); the correct mechanism is silent omission of the breakdown chips in the PDF. Fix should normalize the object map into the array of {label, delta} entries that ScoreWithBreakdown expects, at the viewModel layer to keep parity with the web. Concretely, in viewModel.js:484 replace the raw passthrough with something like:

  legitimacyBreakdown: Object.entries(s?.powerStructure?.publicLegitimacy?.breakdown || {}).map(([label, delta]) => ({ label, delta })),

ScoreWithBreakdown already handles object entries with `.label`/`.delta` (Visuals.jsx:202-205: `delta = b?.delta ?? b?.value`, `bLabel = b?.label || b?.name`), so this produces the same prosperity/safety/defense/food chips the web shows. Leaving `|| []` as the empty fallback is fine. Do NOT instead change ScoreWithBreakdown to call Object.entries, because the same primitive is reused elsewhere expecting array input — normalize at the slice. Severity is medium (parity drift / missing data in export), not a crash.

confirmed.title: Cover 'CAMPAIGN' field renders as a dangling label — EditableText with empty default and hideIfEmpty returns null
confirmed.evidence: Cover footer renders `CAMPAIGN` followed by `<EditableText name="cover.campaign" defaultValue="" />` (Cover.jsx:304-311). After the Editable v2 rewrite, EditableText with empty value and default hideIfEmpty returns null unless showField is set (Editable.jsx:73-79); only NotesField opts in. So the cover prints a 'CAMPAIGN' label followed by a 130pt empty gap — neither a value nor a writable field — despite the file header still declaring 'Editable: ... cover.campaign'.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Pass `showField` + `hideIfEmpty={false}` for cover.campaign (it's a deliberate write-in field like NotesField), or drop the CAMPAIGN label entirely.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. The cited component lives at src/pdf/primitives/Editable.jsx (not the path in the evidence, but the cited line behavior is exactly correct). EditableText (Editable.jsx:64-81) with the default hideIfEmpty=true and showField=false executes `if (hideIfEmpty && !value.trim()) return null;` (line 75) whenever the value is empty. In Cover.jsx the campaign field is `<EditableText name="cover.campaign" defaultValue="" />` (lines 306-310) — defaultValue is the hardcoded empty string with no upstream override; I grepped the entire src/pdf tree and viewModel.js and confirmed cover.campaign is never populated with data anywhere. So EditableText always returns null here.

The surrounding footer (Cover.jsx:296-315) unconditionally renders on every cover page: a "CAMPAIGN" Text label (line 304), then a fixed `width: 130` View (line 305) wrapping the null-returning EditableText, then a "DATE" label and the date. Result: a dangling "CAMPAIGN" label followed by a ~130pt empty gap that is neither a printed value nor a writable form field. The file header (line 9) still declares "Editable: ... cover.campaign", and the Editable v2 doc (lines 21-34, plus NotesField at 188-205) confirms only NotesField opts into showField — so this field silently lost its writable affordance in the v2 rewrite, exactly as claimed.

I could not refute it: the render path is reachable (always renders on the cover footer), there is no upstream guard, and the behavior is not intentional — the header comment and the dedicated 130pt slot show a write-in field was meant to exist. Severity low is honest: purely cosmetic on the cover footer, no data loss, no determinism break, no crash, no money/security impact.
verdicts.fix_note: Both suggested options are valid. Note the cited file path in the evidence is wrong — the component is at src/pdf/primitives/Editable.jsx, not src/pdf/components/Editable.jsx (line numbers shift: the early-return is Editable.jsx:75, not 73-79). Before applying showField + hideIfEmpty={false}, check the on-screen dossier cover for parity: if the screen cover has no campaign write-in field, adding a PDF-only field introduces a new dossier/PDF parity drift, so the cleaner fix is to drop the CAMPAIGN label (and its 130pt View) entirely and update the file-header "Editable:" list on line 9 to match.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the real modules. Cover.jsx:303-314 renders a baseline-aligned footer row: a Text label "CAMPAIGN" (line 304), then an unconditional fixed-width `<View style={{ width: 130 }}>` (line 305) wrapping `<EditableText name="cover.campaign" defaultValue="" style=... />` (lines 306-310), then "DATE" + the computed date.

Trace: EditableText is the v2 implementation at src/pdf/primitives/Editable.jsx:64-106. With defaultValue="" (hardcoded at the only call site; grep shows cover.campaign appears nowhere else as a data source), line 73 gives raw="", line 74 value=noLig("")="" (noLig's `if (!s) return s||''` guard returns ""), and line 75 `if (hideIfEmpty && !value.trim()) return null` fires because hideIfEmpty defaults to true (line 68) and the call site passes no override, no showField, no fallback. So EditableText returns null. The wrapping 130pt View has a fixed width and no conditional, so it stays as an empty spacer. Net rendered footer: "CAMPAIGN" + 130pt blank + "DATE" + June 13 2026. The "CAMPAIGN" label is orphaned with neither a value nor (post-v2) a writable field — exactly as claimed. grep confirms only NotesField opts into showField, so nothing rescues this site. The file header (Cover.jsx:9) still advertises cover.campaign as editable, which is now stale/false.

This is a real defect: a dangling label on the PDF cover footer, a regression introduced by the Editable v2 rewrite (which removed the click-to-edit TextInput default that previously filled that 130pt line). No test references "campaign" or imports Cover, so it is uncaught.

Severity is low, not higher: every dossier hits this code (cover is main path), but the consequence is purely cosmetic — an empty labeled space on the PDF cover footer. It is not a crash, not data loss, not a billing/auth issue, not a determinism break, and not wrong simulation output. It does not even affect text extraction (the v2 rewrite's stated goal) since there was no engine prose here to extract — the field was always blank scratch space. Confidence ~0.85 that a skeptical senior reviewer agrees it is a real (if minor) defect; the only reason it isn't higher is that a reviewer might deem an orphaned blank label on a cosmetic cover footer "intended cleanup, not a bug" given the v2 direction.
verdicts.fix_note: Evidence is accurate but one detail: the "130pt empty gap" is the fixed-width wrapper View at Cover.jsx:305 (width:130), not the EditableText — EditableText renders literally nothing (null); the gap is the unconditional spacer View. Two valid fixes: (1) Restore writability to match the documented intent and the cover.campaign header claim — pass `showField hideIfEmpty={false}` (optionally a `fallback` placeholder) to the EditableText at Cover.jsx:306, giving the DM a blank writable line for the campaign name. (2) If the campaign field is being deliberately retired, remove the "CAMPAIGN" Text label (line 304) AND the 130pt wrapper View (lines 305-311) together, and drop `cover.campaign` from the Editable: header comment at Cover.jsx:9. Either resolves the orphaned label; do not just leave the label without also addressing the wrapper, since the wrapper's fixed width keeps the blank gap even after the child is gone.

confirmed.title: Pipeline rail always reports 'No active stressors' — summary reads a ctx key no step provides
confirmed.evidence: STEP_METADATA.resolveStress.summary (stepMetadata.js:40-43) computes `const ss = ctx.stressors?.active?.length || 0; return ss ? `${ss} active stressor…` : 'No active stressors'`. But the resolveStress step returns `{ stress, stressTypes }` (src/generators/steps/resolveStress.js:153, provides: ['stress','stressTypes'] at line 16) — no step in the pipeline ever writes a top-level `ctx.stressors`, let alone a `{active:[]}` shape. Verified against the production bundle: dist/assets/engine-D8TwO_ZR.js contains the identical lambda (`e.stressors?.active?.length||0 … "No active stressors"`), and the only `stressors:` writes in the whole engine bundle are the settlement-object dual-write inside assembleSettlement (`stress:C, stressors:C` — the raw container, not {active}) and a frozen alias map. settlementSlice.generateSettlement (src/store/settlementSlice.js:567-573) calls meta.summary(ctx) per step and stores it in pipelineHistory; PipelineRail.jsx:98-102 renders entry.summary. Net effect: every generated dossier's 'How this was simulated' rail shows 'No active stressors' under 'Determine stressors' even when the run rolled active stressors — directly contradicted two rows later by stressConfirmPass's working summary ('N stressors confirmed', which correctly reads ctx.stressTypes). The rail is documented as the falsifiable receipt of the simulation (PipelineRail.jsx:10-14), so this is an affirmatively false claim on the main path, not just a cosmetic gap.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change the summary to read the keys the step actually provides, e.g. `const ss = Array.isArray(ctx.stressTypes) ? ctx.stressTypes.length : (ctx.stress ? 1 : 0)`, and add a sync test that runs the registered steps' provides lists against every STEP_METADATA summary's accessed keys (or runs the real pipeline once and asserts each non-null-summary step produces a non-null summary at least once across a few seeds).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the claim by reading the cited code and its callers/callees, and empirically running the summary function.

1. stepMetadata.js:40-43 — STEP_METADATA.resolveStress.summary computes `const ss = ctx.stressors?.active?.length || 0; return ss ? `${ss} active stressor…` : 'No active stressors'`. Confirmed verbatim.

2. resolveStress.js:16 declares `provides: ['stress','stressTypes']` and line 153 returns `{ stress, stressTypes }`. It never writes `ctx.stressors` (top-level). Confirmed.

3. No step in the entire engine writes a top-level `ctx.stressors`. `grep -rn "ctx.stressors\s*="` returns nothing (exit 1). The only `stressors:` write in generators is assembleSettlement.js:88 (`stressors: stress`), which is a key INSIDE the settlement object being built — and that step provides only `['settlement']`, so it merges `ctx.settlement`, never `ctx.stressors`. Even then it's the raw container, not `{active:[]}`.

4. pipeline.js:108-112 — after each step it does `Object.assign(ctx, patch)` then `onStep(name, ctx, patch)`. So ctx only ever gains the declared `provides` keys. `ctx.stressors` is always undefined.

5. settlementSlice.js:567-573 — onStep computes `meta.summary(ctx)` per step and pushes `{id, ts, summary}` into pipelineHistory.

6. PipelineRail.jsx (found at src/components/PipelineRail.jsx, not the path cited but the file exists) renders `entry.summary` at lines 98-102 under the step label; the rail is documented (lines 10-14) as the falsifiable receipt of the simulation. `'No active stressors'` is a truthy non-empty string, so it always renders.

7. The two-rows-later stressConfirmPass.summary (stepMetadata.js:79-82) correctly reads `Array.isArray(ctx.stressTypes)`, producing the direct contradiction the reviewer cites.

8. Empirical: running resolveStress.summary on the exact context the step returns ({stress, stressTypes:['famine']} and a 2-element variant) returns 'No active stressors' in both cases; the suggested fix reads stressTypes and returns 1 and 2 correctly.

Could not refute: the path is live on the main generation flow, no upstream guard populates ctx.stressors, the behavior is not intentional (the comment at stepMetadata.js:16-20 says the summary should show what actually happened on this run), and the cited API works exactly as described. This is a real defect: the rail affirmatively misreports every run that rolled active stressors as having none.

Severity medium is honest: it is a wrong/false claim on the main UI path (the rail's whole purpose is to be a truthful receipt), but it is not data loss, money, security, a crash, or a determinism break. The underlying simulation output (stress, stressTypes, downstream effects, the settlement object) is correct; only this one rail summary string is wrong.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct. `const ss = Array.isArray(ctx.stressTypes) ? ctx.stressTypes.length : (ctx.stress ? 1 : 0)` reads the keys the step actually provides. One nuance worth noting: stressTypes is filtered to CATALOG types only (STRESS_TYPE_MAP keys) at resolveStress.js:148, so a custom authored-only stressor (e.g. 'dragon_tax') rides `ctx.stress` (the container) but not `ctx.stressTypes`. If 'active stressor' is meant to count custom authored ones too, counting `ctx.stress` (normalize to array length) is more faithful than `ctx.stressTypes.length`; if it's meant to mirror stressConfirmPass's catalog-based count, stressTypes is consistent. Either is a strict improvement over the current always-zero read. The proposed sync test (run real steps' provides lists against each summary's accessed keys, or run the pipeline across a few seeds and assert each non-null summary produces a non-null result at least once) would have caught this and is the right structural prevention.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL. Traced end-to-end against the real modules.

Mechanism: runPipeline (pipeline.js:108-112) merges each step's returned patch via Object.assign(ctx, patch), then fires onStep(name, ctx). resolveStress returns {stress, stressTypes} (resolveStress.js:153; provides:['stress','stressTypes'] at line 16), so ctx only ever gains `stress` and `stressTypes` — never `stressors`. The resolveStress summary (stepMetadata.js:41) reads ctx.stressors?.active?.length||0. ctx.stressors is undefined, so the count is always 0 and the summary is always 'No active stressors'. No step writes a top-level ctx.stressors; the only stressors: write is assembleSettlement.js:88 (stressors: stress) onto the returned settlement object, and that value is the raw container shape (null | entry | entry[]), never {active:[...]}.

settlementSlice.generateSettlement (settlementSlice.js:558-574) calls meta.summary(ctx) per step in onStep and stores {id,ts,summary} into pipelineHistory -> state.pipelineHistory (line 615). PipelineRail.jsx:98-101 renders entry.summary literally.

Reproduction (read-only node against real runPipeline + real registered steps + real metaForStep): sampled 60 seeds with a config forcing intended stressors. Result: ctx.stressors was undefined in 100% of runs (hasCtxStressors:false); every summary === 'No active stressors'. Seed 1004 rolled stressTypes:["occupied"] (rolledCount 1) yet summary still reported 'No active stressors'. Demonstrates the summary is structurally incapable of ever reporting a nonzero count.

Production bundle dist/assets/engine-D8TwO_ZR.js contains the identical lambda verbatim: summary:e=>{const t=e.stressors?.active?.length||0;return t?`${t} active stressor${t===1?"":"s"}`:"No active stressors"} — so the defect ships.

Contrast: stressConfirmPass.summary (stepMetadata.js:79-82) reads ctx.stressTypes correctly ('N stressors confirmed'), so two rows down the rail can read 'No active stressors' / 'N stressors confirmed' for the same run — internally self-contradictory.

Severity: medium. It is a real, always-on, main-path defect (every generated dossier), determinism-clean but factually wrong, and it undermines the rail's documented purpose as a falsifiable simulation receipt. It is not data loss / money / crash, so not critical/high; it is a guaranteed wrong UI output, stronger than an edge case, so not low.

Confidence high: the failure path is fully read (write side, merge mechanism, read side, render side) and reproduced against the real modules and confirmed in the shipped bundle.
verdicts.fix_note:
The claimed fix (read the count the step actually provides) is correct. Use ctx.stressTypes, exactly as stressConfirmPass already does at stepMetadata.js:80: `const ss = Array.isArray(ctx.stressTypes) ? ctx.stressTypes.length : 0;` then `return ss ? `${ss} active stressor${ss === 1 ? '' : 's'}` : 'No active stressors';`.

Caveats verified against the engine, so the fix is honest about what 'active' means:
1. ctx.stressTypes is the CATALOG-typed list only — resolveStress.js:148 filters to STRESS_TYPE_MAP keys after editor overlays, so a custom authored stressor ('dragon_tax') rides ctx.stress but NOT ctx.stressTypes. If the rail intends to count ALL active stressors (including custom), derive from ctx.stress instead: `const arr = Array.isArray(ctx.stress) ? ctx.stress : ctx.stress ? [ctx.stress] : []; const ss = arr.length;`. Counting ctx.stress is the more faithful 'active stressors' count; counting ctx.stressTypes matches stressConfirmPass's convention but undercounts custom stressors by exactly the same amount the confirm row does (consistent, if narrower).
2. Do NOT 'fix' it by writing a new ctx.stressors key from the step — that would change the step's provides contract and could perturb downstream/determinism expectations; the metadata summary should read existing ctx keys, not introduce new ones.
Recommended: read ctx.stress (array-normalized) for an accurate all-stressors count; if matching the confirm row's catalog-only semantics is preferred, read ctx.stressTypes.

confirmed.title: Five more STEP_METADATA summaries read context keys the pipeline never writes — their factual receipts can never render
confirmed.evidence: Cross-checked every summary against the registered steps' actual patches (source for the 8 non-stripped step files; the dist engine bundle's provides lists for the rest — extracted from dist/assets/engine-D8TwO_ZR.js): (1) resolveConfig.summary reads `ctx.config?.tier` (line 27) but the raw input config uses `settType`; resolveConfig builds a NEW `effectiveConfig` (resolveConfig.js:144-160) and returns top-level `tier` — it never mutates ctx.config, and settlementSlice strips `tier` from any config fallback (DERIVED_CONFIG_KEYS, settlementSlice.js:104-110), so 'Target size: …' never renders. (2) resolveResources.summary reads `ctx.resources?.local` (lines 32-35) but the step returns `{ nearbyResources, nearbyResourcesDepleted, nearbyResourcesCustom }` (resolveResources.js:265). (3) generateEconomy.summary reads `ctx.economy?.prosperityBand` (lines 87-90) but the step provides only `economicState` (dist provides list). (4) generatePower.summary reads `ctx.power?.factions || ctx.factions` (lines 95-97) but the step provides `powerStructure`, and `factions` is only provided by the LATER generatePopulation step. (5) factionCorrelationPass.summary reads `ctx.tensions || ctx.history?.currentTensions` (lines 108-111) but the step provides [] and `history` is only provided by the later generateNarratives step; the string `tensions:` appears zero times in the entire engine bundle. All five therefore silently return null forever, so the rail (whose stated purpose is showing 'what *actually* happened on this run', stepMetadata.js:17-20) loses its factual line for config, resources, economy, power, and tensions. Same lambdas confirmed present in the production bundle.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Point each summary at the real context keys (effectiveConfig.tier / nearbyResources / economicState / powerStructure.factions / a tensions key actually threaded through ctx), and add the missing metadata-vs-pipeline sync test.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. The mechanism is unambiguous and I confirmed every link in the chain.

How summaries are invoked: settlementSlice.js:567-572 passes onStep to generateSettlementPipeline; onStep calls meta.summary(ctx) on the LIVE pipeline context for each step and freezes the resulting string into pipelineHistory. runPipeline (pipeline.js:98-112) builds ctx = {...initialContext} and merges each step's patch with a TOP-LEVEL Object.assign(ctx, patch), then fires onStep immediately. initialContext.config is the raw fullConfig (generateSettlementPipeline.js:45-47), which carries settType, sliders, and toggles but never a `tier` key (settlementSlice fullConfig, lines 531-542). Steps never mutate ctx.config — they return new top-level keys.

I checked each of the five against the actual step provides arrays:
(1) resolveConfig provides top-level `tier`/`effectiveConfig`/etc. (resolveConfig.js:37-42, return 299-304); it never writes ctx.config.tier. stepMetadata.js:27 reads ctx.config?.tier -> always undefined -> null. (No code anywhere assigns config.tier; the only grep hit is the read itself. DERIVED_CONFIG_KEYS at settlementSlice.js:104-110 even strips `tier` from any config fallback, reinforcing that ctx.config never carries it.)
(2) resolveResources provides nearbyResources/nearbyResourcesDepleted/nearbyResourcesCustom (line 32; return 265). No step ever provides `resources`. stepMetadata.js:33 reads ctx.resources?.local -> null forever.
(3) generateEconomy provides only `economicState` (line 338; return 341). No step provides `economy`. stepMetadata.js:88 reads ctx.economy?.prosperityBand -> null forever.
(4) generatePower provides only `powerStructure` (line 22; return 125). No step ever provides `power`; `factions` is provided by generatePopulation which runs AFTER generatePower, so at generatePower's onStep snapshot neither ctx.power nor ctx.factions exists. stepMetadata.js:96 -> null forever.
(5) factionCorrelationPass provides [] (return is _rosterChangedAfterEconomy only). No step ever provides a top-level `tensions` key; `history` is provided by generateNarratives, which runs AFTER factionCorrelationPass. So at this step's snapshot neither ctx.tensions nor ctx.history exists. stepMetadata.js:109 -> null forever.

Dumping every provides array in pipeline order confirmed none of resources/economy/power/tensions is ever a top-level ctx key, and that factions/history appear only at later steps than the summaries that read them. Summary exceptions are swallowed (try/catch at settlementSlice.js:570-571), so the rail simply loses its factual line and falls back to the label+description — degraded, not crashing.

Severity: low is correct. No data loss, no money/security exposure, no determinism break, no main-path crash — purely a UI 'receipt' rail silently dropping five of its factual one-liners. (Note: resolveStress's summary reads ctx.stressors?.active, another key the step doesn't provide — a sixth instance of the same pattern, not among the five claimed but corroborating the bug class. It doesn't affect the validity of any of the five.)
verdicts.fix_note: Reviewer's fix targets are correct. Concretely: (1) ctx.effectiveConfig?.tier (or ctx.tier); (2) ctx.nearbyResources?.length; (3) ctx.economicState?.prosperityBand (verify the actual field name inside economicState — confirm it exposes a prosperity band rather than a numeric score); (4) ctx.powerStructure?.factions?.length. (5) is the hardest: factionCorrelationPass produces no tensions value at all — it only records _rosterChangedAfterEconomy and pushes faction-pulled institutions. There is no live `tensions`/`history` key at that step, so the summary cannot simply be re-pointed; either thread a real correlation/tensions count through the patch (the step has powerStructure.factions and computes factionBoosts, so a faction-correlation count is available) or relabel the summary to what the step actually does (e.g. faction-pulled-institution count via _rosterChangedAfterEconomy / boostAdditions). Because each summary is snapshotted at its own onStep, fixes must reference keys that already exist AT THAT STEP — relying on a later step's provides won't help. The reviewer's call to add a metadata-vs-pipeline sync test is the durable guard: assert every STEP_METADATA.summary reads only keys present in the cumulative provides set up to and including that step.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via source read + live runtime trace. The five STEP_METADATA summaries read context keys the pipeline never holds at the moment their onStep fires.

Mechanism: runPipeline flat-merges each step's returned patch via Object.assign(ctx, patch) (pipeline.js:110). settlementSlice.generate passes an onStep callback that calls meta.summary(ctx) on the live accumulated ctx and stores the result as pipelineHistory[].summary (settlementSlice.js:567-573). PipelineRail renders that stored value only when truthy: `entry.summary && (...)` (PipelineRail.jsx:98-102). It does NOT re-invoke meta.summary, so a null computed at generation time is permanent for that run.

Each of the five reads a key that does not exist on ctx at that step:
1. resolveConfig.summary reads ctx.config?.tier (stepMetadata.js:27). The step builds a new effectiveConfig and returns top-level `tier` (resolveConfig.js:144-160,299-304); it never mutates ctx.config. Raw input config uses settType, not tier. So ctx.config.tier is undefined. Actual value lives at ctx.tier.
2. resolveResources.summary reads ctx.resources?.local (stepMetadata.js:33). Step returns {nearbyResources, nearbyResourcesDepleted, nearbyResourcesCustom} (resolveResources.js:265). No ctx.resources is ever written. Actual: ctx.nearbyResources (length 5 in test run).
3. generateEconomy.summary reads ctx.economy?.prosperityBand (stepMetadata.js:88). Step provides only economicState (generateEconomy.js:338,341). No ctx.economy. Also the field is `prosperity` (value "Prosperous"), not prosperityBand.
4. generatePower.summary reads ctx.power?.factions || ctx.factions (stepMetadata.js:96). Step provides powerStructure (generatePower.js:22,125); factions is provided only by the LATER generatePopulation step (generatePopulation.js:28), so it is undefined when generatePower's onStep fires. Actual: ctx.powerStructure.factions (length 6).
5. factionCorrelationPass.summary reads ctx.tensions || ctx.history?.currentTensions (stepMetadata.js:109). Step provides only {_rosterChangedAfterEconomy} (factionCorrelationPass.js:32,141). ctx.tensions is never written anywhere; history is provided only by the later generateNarratives step (generateNarratives.js:24). 

Runtime proof: ran generateSettlementPipeline against the real source modules with onStep mirroring settlementSlice exactly. All five summaries returned null on a live run, while sibling summaries (assembleInstitutions -> "53 institutions placed", subsumptionPass, stressConfirmPass, economyReconcilePass, structuralValidationPass -> "No structural findings", generatePopulation -> "7 NPCs named") returned their factual receipts. Snapshot of ctx at each broken step confirmed the correct data IS present under the right keys (ctx.tier="town", ctx.nearbyResources.length=5, ctx.economicState.prosperity="Prosperous", ctx.powerStructure.factions.length=6), so a key fix would make them render.

User-visible consequence: the Pipeline Rail ("How this was simulated") permanently omits the per-run factual line for config/target-size, resources, economy/prosperity, factions, and tensions rows — exactly the five most substantive engine decisions. Those rows show only the generic label+description, never "what actually happened on this run" (the rail's stated purpose, stepMetadata.js:16-20).

Severity low, not high: the settlement output, determinism, billing, auth, and PDF parity are all unaffected. This is a degraded informational UI surface (missing receipt lines), not wrong simulation output or a crash. Confidence ~0.95.
verdicts.fix_note:
The claim's diagnosis is correct, but two of the suggested fixes need field-name corrections (verified against live ctx):
- resolveConfig: read ctx.tier (not ctx.config.tier). e.g. ctx.tier ? `Target size: ${ctx.tier}` : null.
- resolveResources: read ctx.nearbyResources (length) instead of ctx.resources.local.
- generateEconomy: the correct path is ctx.economicState?.prosperity (a string like "Prosperous"), NOT ctx.economicState.prosperityBand — the field is named `prosperity`, no `Band` suffix exists. The claim's framing implies prosperityBand; that would still return null.
- generatePower: read ctx.powerStructure?.factions?.length. Keeping the `|| ctx.factions` fallback is harmless but never hits at this step.
- factionCorrelationPass is the weakest to fix: the step persists nothing tension-related to ctx (it only returns _rosterChangedAfterEconomy). The faction-relationship data actually lives on ctx.powerStructure.factionRelationships (an array of {pair,type,direction,...}, written by the EARLIER generatePower step), e.g. count entries where type==='competitive'/'hostile' or direction==='escalating'. Pointing the summary at ctx.powerStructure?.factionRelationships is the only way to give this row a real receipt; ctx.tensions/ctx.history will never be populated at this step.

Structural recommendation: the file header (stepMetadata.js:8-11) advertises a DEV test that flags label/registration drift but it does NOT validate that summary() reads keys the step's `provides` (plus prior steps' provides) actually expose. Adding such a check — invoke each summary against a representative real ctx and assert non-null where a value is expected, or statically diff the keys each summary touches against the cumulative provides-up-to-that-step — would have caught all five and prevents this bug class recurring.

confirmed.title: corruptionPass step registered but missing from STEP_METADATA; the 'loud in DEV' guard test it references does not exist
confirmed.evidence: steps/index.js:24 imports './corruptionPass.js' (19 registered steps; registration name 'corruptionPass' confirmed by extracting registerStep names from dist/assets/engine-D8TwO_ZR.js, and the built STEP_METADATA object in the same bundle has 18 keys — no corruptionPass). metaForStep (stepMetadata.js:151-153) falls back to `{ label: name, description: '' }`, so the user-facing 'How this was simulated' rail (PipelineRail.jsx:50) renders the raw machine name 'corruptionPass' with no description for every settlement. The file's own header (stepMetadata.js:8-11) says 'Keep entries here in sync with the registrations in steps/index.js … Both are loud in DEV through the test below' — but no such test exists anywhere in tests/ (the only files mentioning STEP_METADATA/metaForStep are the source files and tests/generators/corruptionPass.integration.test.js, which tests corruption logic, not metadata sync). Adjacent drift from the same step-count growth: copy/en.js pipelineSteps (lines 471-487) has only 15 of 19 steps (missing stressConfirmPass, economyReconcilePass, structuralValidationPass, corruptionPass — the reveal overlay skips unlabeled steps by design, PipelineReveal.jsx:53-61), and en.js:284 onboarding copy still tells users 'the rail on the right shows the fourteen steps the engine took' while the rail now lists 19.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a corruptionPass entry to STEP_METADATA (label/description/summary), write the promised sync test (import steps/index.js, compare getStepMeta() names against Object.keys(STEP_METADATA)), and update the en.js 'fourteen steps' copy.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified every load-bearing claim against the source:

1) corruptionPass is registered but absent from STEP_METADATA. src/generators/steps/index.js:24 imports './corruptionPass.js'; corruptionPass.js calls registerStep('corruptionPass', ...). STEP_METADATA (src/generators/steps/stepMetadata.js:23-144) has no corruptionPass key — it jumps from generatePopulation to generateNarratives, exactly mirroring index.js but minus corruptionPass.

2) Runtime impact on the "How this was simulated" rail is real and hits EVERY settlement. pipeline.js:100-116 executes all steps in stepOrder unconditionally and calls onStep(name, ...) for each (line 112). settlementSlice.js:567-572's onStep pushes { id: name } into pipelineHistory for every executed step. PipelineRail.jsx:289-296 renders every history entry; StepRow (line 50) calls metaForStep(entry.id), which for corruptionPass falls back at stepMetadata.js:152 to { label: 'corruptionPass', description: '' }. So the rail renders the raw machine name "corruptionPass" with no description. Note corruptionPass is a no-op when there's no criminal institution, but the step fn still runs and still emits onStep, so the raw name shows on all runs, not just corrupted ones.

3) The promised "loud in DEV" sync test does not exist. stepMetadata.js:8-11 claims "Both are loud in DEV through the test below," but grep over tests/ finds zero references to Object.keys(STEP_METADATA), a getStepMeta-vs-metadata comparison, or any metadata-sync assertion. The only related test is tests/generators/corruptionPass.integration.test.js, which exercises corruption logic, not metadata.

4) Adjacent drift confirmed: copy/en.js pipelineSteps (471-487) has 15 entries, missing stressConfirmPass, economyReconcilePass, structuralValidationPass, corruptionPass; en.js:284 step2Body still says "the fourteen steps the engine took." PipelineReveal.jsx:56-61 deliberately filters history to only entries present in pipelineSteps (".filter(s => s.label)"), so the reveal overlay silently skips the unlabeled steps — consistent with the claim that the overlay is unaffected while the rail is.

Could not refute. The path is reachable on the main settlement-generation flow, no upstream guard suppresses it, and the behavior contradicts the file's own header contract. Severity is correctly low: purely cosmetic — a user-facing rail shows a raw step name + empty description for one step, and an onboarding sentence has a stale count. No effect on simulation output, determinism, billing/auth, persistence, or PDF/dossier parity.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is sound. Concretely: (a) add a corruptionPass entry to STEP_METADATA with label/description/summary (e.g. summary counting ctx.npcs that carry a corruption vector, returning null when none — keeping the no-op case quiet); (b) write the promised sync test that imports steps/index.js to populate the registry, then asserts the set of names from pipeline.js getStepMeta() equals Object.keys(STEP_METADATA) (this catches both directions of drift); (c) update en.js:284 "fourteen steps" copy to be count-agnostic (e.g. "the steps the engine took") rather than hardcoding a new number that will rot again. Optionally also backfill the 4 missing en.js pipelineSteps entries if those steps are meant to appear in the reveal overlay — but note that omission is currently intentional-by-filter (PipelineReveal.jsx:53-61), so adding them changes the overlay; decide deliberately rather than as part of this fix.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end with reads + runtime checks.

STRUCTURAL CORE (verified): steps/index.js imports 19 step modules (resolveConfig…corruptionPass…assembleSettlement); STEP_METADATA in stepMetadata.js:23-144 has exactly 18 keys; the single missing one is corruptionPass (node diff confirms imported-but-missing = ['corruptionPass']). metaForStep('corruptionPass') (stepMetadata.js:151-153) returns the fallback {label:'corruptionPass', description:''} — runtime-confirmed: label === raw machine name.

FAILURE PATH (traced concretely):
1. generateSettlement (settlementSlice.js:558-574) runs the pipeline with an onStep callback that pushes {id:name, ts, summary} into pipelineHistory for every step.
2. runPipeline (pipeline.js:100-116) iterates ALL registered steps in topological order and calls onStep(name,ctx,patch) UNCONDITIONALLY after each step.fn — corruptionPass is index 16 of 19 in getStepOrder() (runtime-confirmed). The step's internal early-return (no criminal institution) does NOT suppress onStep, so the history entry is pushed on EVERY generation.
3. PipelineRail (PipelineRail.jsx:289-296) renders one StepRow per pipelineHistory entry; StepRow (line 50) calls metaForStep(entry.id). For corruptionPass it gets label 'corruptionPass', summary null, description ''.
4. Result: the rail row shows the bold raw machine name 'corruptionPass' (line 93-95), no summary line (null), and nothing on expand (empty description fails the `open && meta.description` guard at line 103).

USER-VISIBLE CONSEQUENCE: the "How this was simulated" rail — described in its own header as the most important UI trust surface answering "is this AI just inventing things?" — leaks the internal function name 'corruptionPass' as a step label on every settlement, with no human label/summary/description. Deterministic, every run. Does NOT affect the PipelineReveal overlay, which filters to labeled steps by design (PipelineReveal.jsx:56-61), nor any simulation math, billing, security, or determinism.

TEST CLAIM (verified): the only test referencing stepMetadata is tests/domain/trace.coverage.test.js; its VALID_STEPS whitelist (lines 167-174) is a separate hard-coded set used to validate emitted trace step names (and itself omits corruptionPass). It does NOT enforce STEP_METADATA ↔ registered-steps parity. No "loud in DEV" sync guard exists despite the header comment at stepMetadata.js:8-11 claiming one. Claim is accurate.

Severity medium: real, always-occurring presentation defect (machine name leaked where a human label belongs) on a prominent, explicitly-trust-load-bearing surface — but no crash, data loss, money, security, or simulation/determinism impact.
verdicts.fix_note:
Primary fix is correct and sufficient for the rail: add a corruptionPass entry to STEP_METADATA (stepMetadata.js) with a label (e.g. "Seed corruption"), a description, and an optional summary(ctx) — e.g. count ctx.npcs.filter(n => n.corrupt).length to read "N NPCs already compromised" / "No corruption seeded" so the rail shows what actually happened. Note: corruptionPass runs after generatePopulation but BEFORE generateNarratives/assembleSettlement, so place the new key between generatePopulation and generateNarratives to keep the file readable in step order (ordering in the frozen object is cosmetic; the rail renders in pipelineHistory order, not object-key order).

The header comment (stepMetadata.js:8-11) promises a DEV-loud sync test that does not exist — that is the durable fix for this recurring bug class (step count grew, metadata didn't). A real guard would import getStepOrder() from pipeline.js (after importing steps/index.js) and assert STEP_METADATA keys === registered step names (set equality, both directions). The existing trace.coverage VALID_STEPS set is NOT that guard and would also need corruptionPass added if the fix wants that whitelist current.

Secondary drift (correctly flagged, lower priority, NOT a crash): copy/en.js pipelineSteps (lines 471-487) is missing 4 of 19 steps (stressConfirmPass, economyReconcilePass, structuralValidationPass, corruptionPass) — but the reveal overlay intentionally skips unlabeled steps (PipelineReveal.jsx:56-61), so this is cosmetic-by-omission, not a defect on the same severity as the rail. en.js:284 onboarding copy still says "the fourteen steps" while the rail now lists 19 — stale but harmless.

confirmed.title: tsconfig.full.json uses brace-expansion glob unsupported by tsc — src/components is silently excluded from the gate's typecheck
confirmed.evidence: tsconfig.full.json:25 includes `"src/components/**/*.{js,jsx}"`. TypeScript's tsconfig include/exclude globs support only `*`, `?`, and `**/` — brace expansion `{js,jsx}` is NOT supported (longstanding TS limitation; patterns containing `{…}` are matched literally), so this pattern matches zero files and tsc raises no error because the other includes still match files. The config's own header (lines 2-13) and ARCHITECTURE.md (lines 147-150, 172-174) both claim the gate typechecks 'the whole src logic tree (domain/store/lib/hooks/generators/components/pdf) … at zero errors', and the brief's gate description repeats it. In reality none of the ~160 files under src/components/ are typechecked; line 26's `src/pdf/**/*.js` additionally omits every pdf/*.jsx section (SettlementPDF.jsx, sections/, primitives/) — only pdf *.js like viewModel.js is covered. So JSDoc type errors in components/pdf-JSX ship without tripping `npm run typecheck`, while the team believes the punch-list holds at zero. (Could not execute tsc to demonstrate — node_modules package.json files are stripped in this snapshot — but `tsc --listFilesOnly` would show zero src/components entries.)
confirmed.suggested_fix: Replace the brace glob with two patterns (`src/components/**/*.js`, `src/components/**/*.jsx`), decide explicitly whether pdf/*.jsx should be included, and re-run `npm run typecheck` expecting a new punch-list from the never-checked components tree.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via direct execution of the project's own tsc (run through `node ./node_modules/typescript/bin/tsc -p tsconfig.full.json --listFilesOnly --noEmit`).

Findings:
1. Gate wiring holds: package.json `typecheck => tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.full.json`, and `check` runs `npm run typecheck`. So tsconfig.full.json IS the gate's typecheck config (matches header lines 6-9).
2. tsc filelist contains 0 `src/components` entries despite 163 matchable .js/.jsx files on disk under src/components. The brace glob `src/components/**/*.{js,jsx}` (line 25) matches literally nothing — tsc tsconfig globs support only `*`, `?`, `**/`; `{js,jsx}` is treated literally and no path is literally named `{js,jsx}`. This reproduces exactly what the reviewer predicted but could not run.
3. Not an exclude artifact: tsconfig.full.json defines its own `exclude` (lines 28-35) that does NOT list src/components or src/pdf, and a child tsconfig's own `exclude` overrides the base's (tsconfig.json:45 lists src/components but is irrelevant here). So components are absent purely because the include pattern fails to match.
4. PDF JSX also uncovered: `src/pdf/**/*.js` (line 26) yields only 5 .js pdf files in the filelist; all 33 pdf .jsx files (SettlementPDF.jsx, sections/, primitives/) are excluded.
5. Both config headers (tsconfig.full.json:2-13, tsconfig.json:7-9) and the brief assert the gate typechecks the full tree "domain/store/lib/hooks/generators/components/pdf … at zero errors" — directly contradicted by the file list.

Could not refute. Every element of the claim is empirically true. Impact: JSDoc type errors in ~163 component files and ~33 pdf JSX files ship without tripping the gate while docs claim a zero-error full-tree punch-list — a real silent coverage gap / broken seam. It is not data loss, money, security, or a crash, so medium severity is correct. Confidence very high given direct empirical confirmation. The reviewer's suggested fix (split into `src/components/**/*.js` + `src/components/**/*.jsx`, decide on pdf/*.jsx explicitly, then re-run typecheck expecting a new punch-list) is correct.
verdicts.fix_note: Fix is correct. Replace line 25 with two patterns: "src/components/**/*.js" and "src/components/**/*.jsx". Also add "src/pdf/**/*.jsx" alongside line 26 to cover SettlementPDF.jsx, sections/, primitives/ (the dossier/PDF-parity bug class makes this especially worth covering). Then run `npm run typecheck` and expect a fresh punch-list of JSDoc errors from the ~163 components + 33 pdf JSX files that have never been checked — this gate change may surface many real errors that must be triaged before it goes green, so it is not a zero-risk one-line edit. Confidence ~0.97: directly reproduced with the project's own tsc (0 of 163 components and 0 of 33 pdf .jsx files appear in `tsc --listFilesOnly`).

verdicts.reasoning: CONFIRMED via direct reproduction against the real repo (TypeScript v6.0.3, the actual installed tsc). tsconfig.full.json:25 declares `"src/components/**/*.{js,jsx}"`. Running the exact gate command `tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.full.json --listFilesOnly` shows 0 of the 163 files under src/components/ are typechecked. Isolation test proves causation: a standalone config with `src/components/**/*.jsx` resolves 156 jsx files, whereas the identical config with `src/components/**/*.{js,jsx}` resolves 0 files and raises `error TS18003: No inputs were found` — tsc matches the brace pattern `{js,jsx}` literally (it supports only *, ?, **). In the full config that TS18003 is suppressed because the sibling includes (domain/store/lib/hooks/generators) DO match files, so tsc proceeds silently with src/components entirely absent. The secondary pdf claim is also confirmed: line 26's `src/pdf/**/*.js` includes only the 5 pdf .js files (theme.js, variants.js, lib/format.js, lib/viewModel.js, lib/headlines.js) and excludes all 33 pdf .jsx files (SettlementPDF.jsx, sections/*.jsx, primitives). Both tsconfig.full.json:2-13 and ARCHITECTURE.md:147-150,172 explicitly claim the gate typechecks the 'full src logic tree (domain/store/lib/hooks/generators/components/pdf)' at zero errors — directly contradicted by the resolved file list. Concrete failure path: any JSDoc type error introduced in the ~163 src/components/*.jsx files (highest-stakes UI) or the ~33 src/pdf/*.jsx PDF section/primitive files passes `npm run typecheck` and thus `npm run check`, so it ships while the team believes the type punch-list holds at zero — a silent gate-coverage gap. Severity medium not high: this is a broken gate seam / loss of a safety net, not itself a wrong-output, determinism, security, or crash defect; it widens the blast radius for other bugs (e.g. the documented underscore-named-import class in components/pdf-jsx would not be caught by typecheck, though the build's onwarn still catches unresolved named imports) but causes no incorrect behavior on its own. Confidence high: the behavior is mechanically reproduced, not inferred.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix direction is correct but must cover BOTH defects, not just the brace glob. (1) Replace line 25's single brace entry with two entries: "src/components/**/*.js" and "src/components/**/*.jsx". (2) Line 26 is a separate, independent bug the claim correctly flags: change/augment "src/pdf/**/*.js" to also include "src/pdf/**/*.jsx" so the 33 PDF jsx section/primitive files are covered. After fixing, run `tsc -p tsconfig.full.json --noEmit` (NOT just --listFilesOnly) to surface any latent type errors that were being silently skipped — the gate likely is NOT actually at zero errors once these ~196 previously-unchecked .jsx files are pulled in, so the fix may require accompanying type-annotation cleanup before the gate goes green again. Note jsx in these files requires the baseline tsconfig to set a jsx compiler option (jsx: 'preserve' or similar) and allowJs; verify tsconfig.json already provides these (it must, since the build/lint handle jsx) before assuming a one-line include change suffices.

confirmed.title: vendor-pdf lazy-load contract tests are dist-gated and never run in CI — CI runs tests before build and dist/ is gitignored
confirmed.evidence: tests/build/vendorPdfLazy.test.js:28 wraps the three build-output contracts (vendor-pdf is its own chunk; chunk is 500KB-3MB; index.html emits no modulepreload for it) in `describe.runIf(distExists)`. dist/ is in .gitignore, and .github/workflows/ci.yml runs 'Run tests' (line 57-58, `npm run test`) BEFORE 'Build' (line 66-67, `npm run build`) with no test re-run afterwards; the husky pre-push `npm run check` uses the same tests-then-build order. So on every CI run and clean pre-push the dist-gated suite silently skips, despite the file's own header claiming 'in CI we run it after the build step' (lines 16-18). Worse, when a developer runs tests locally with a STALE dist/ lying around, the suite validates last build's artifacts rather than current code. Only the source-level half (dynamic-import grep of SettlementDetail.jsx / SingleDossierSuccessPage.jsx, lines 74-91) actually executes. A regression that merges the ~619KB-gz PDF stack into a first-paint chunk (e.g. via a manualChunks edit or a new static import path not covered by the two greps) would pass the whole gate.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Either add a CI step after Build that re-runs vitest on tests/build/ (e.g. `npx vitest run tests/build`), or reorder so build precedes the test step, and make the suite fail loudly (not skip) when dist/ is absent in CI (e.g. assert on process.env.CI).

verdicts.reasoning: Verified all claims against the actual code. (1) tests/build/vendorPdfLazy.test.js:28 wraps the three build-output contract tests in describe.runIf(distExists); distExists requires dist/ and dist/assets/ to exist (lines 24-28). describe.runIf(false) skips the suite — it does not fail. (2) .gitignore:2 lists dist/, and `git check-ignore dist` confirms it is ignored, so a fresh CI checkout has no dist. (3) .github/workflows/ci.yml runs "Run tests" (npm run test = vitest run) at lines 57-58 BEFORE "Build" (npm run build) at lines 66-67, with no post-build test re-run. (4) .husky/pre-push runs `npm run check`, defined in package.json as `... && npm run test && npm run build` — same test-before-build order. (5) There is no globalSetup that builds first (grep returned nothing) and no npm script or CI step that targets tests/build after the build. (6) The second describe block (lines 74-89) is unconditional and is the only part that runs in CI — a source-level grep of two named .jsx files for dynamic import(). A regression via a manualChunks edit or a new static import path in an uncovered file would not be caught. (7) The file's own header (lines 15-18) claims "in CI we run it after the build step," which is contradicted by the actual ordering. (8) Stale-dist hazard confirmed: dist/ currently exists in the working tree (built Jun 12), so a local vitest run validates a stale artifact, not current code. Every factual assertion in the report holds with file:line evidence. Severity medium is appropriate: this is a broken test seam / coverage gap protecting a bundling/performance invariant (PDF stack must not load on first paint), not a data-loss/money/security/main-path-crash issue. The suggested fix (re-run vitest on tests/build after build, or reorder build-before-test, and fail loudly when dist absent in CI via process.env.CI) is sound.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is correct. Minor sharpening: the cleanest option is a dedicated CI step after "Build" running `npx vitest run tests/build`, since the existing test step legitimately needs to run before build for the rest of the suite (and rebuilding dist would slow it). Additionally guard against the stale-dist false-pass by failing the suite when process.env.CI is set but dist/ is absent (replace describe.runIf(distExists) with logic that asserts presence under CI), so a misconfigured pipeline surfaces loudly instead of green-skipping. Also note the source-level greps only cover two named files (SettlementDetail.jsx, SingleDossierSuccessPage.jsx); a new static-import path elsewhere would evade both the greps and the skipped dist checks — worth a manualChunks-config-level assertion too.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end with direct evidence. tests/build/vendorPdfLazy.test.js:28 wraps its three build-output contracts (vendor-pdf is its own chunk; size 500KB-3MB; index.html has no modulepreload for it) in describe.runIf(distExists), where distExists = existsSync(distDir) && existsSync(assetsDir) (line 26). I evaluated that gate directly: with no dist it is false, so the block SKIPS.

CI order (.github/workflows/ci.yml): npm ci (line 23) -> validate/typecheck/lint -> "Run tests" = npm run test = `vitest run` (lines 57-58) -> "Build" = npm run build (lines 66-67). No dist-producing step precedes the test step, dist/ is gitignored (.gitignore:2) and untracked (git ls-files dist => 0 files), so a fresh actions/checkout has no dist/ at test time => the three contracts silently skip on every CI run. The file's own header (lines 16-18) claims "in CI we run it after the build step," which is false. The husky .husky/pre-push hook runs `npm run check`, and check = "...&& npm run test && npm run build" — same tests-then-build order with no test re-run, so a clean pre-push also skips the block.

Stale-dist hazard is live: dist/ is currently present on disk in the repo, so a local `npm run test` would validate the PREVIOUS build's artifacts rather than current source — false confidence in both directions.

Coverage gap is concrete and real. The chunk source src/utils/generateSettlementPDF.js statically imports @react-pdf/renderer (line 18); src/utils/generateCampaignPDF.js statically imports jspdf (line 15). Both modules are members of vendor-pdf per vite.config.js manualChunks (matches node_modules/@react-pdf, node_modules/jspdf, pdfkit, fontkit => 'vendor-pdf'). The lazy contract therefore depends entirely on every consumer using dynamic import(). The surviving source-level half (lines 74-91) only greps SettlementDetail.jsx and SingleDossierSuccessPage.jsx. A genuine THIRD lazy consumer — SettlementsPanel.jsx (lines 9-10: `import('../utils/generateCampaignPDF.js')`), itself lazy-loaded in App.jsx:32 — is uncovered. Converting that (or any future consumer) to a top-level static import, OR editing the manualChunks/modulePreload filter in vite.config.js (which NO source-level test exercises — grep for manualChunks/modulePreload in tests/ returns only this file), would merge the ~619KB-gz PDF stack into a first-paint chunk and pass the entire `npm run check` gate undetected.

Severity is medium, not high: this is a broken CI/test safety seam, not a runtime defect. It cannot itself crash, lose data, or corrupt simulation output. Its consequence is that a real first-paint-bloat regression (a separate, genuinely high-impact perf defect) would ship without tripping the gate. Confidence ~0.9: a skeptical reviewer with full code access would agree the dist-gated suite never runs in CI/pre-push and that the source greps under-cover the consumer set. (Could not run vitest locally — node_modules is missing @rollup/rollup-darwin-arm64 — but execution is unnecessary: runIf semantics and the distExists evaluation are deterministic and were verified directly.)
verdicts.fix_note:
The claim states the problem accurately but offers no explicit fix; the obvious one ("move Build before Run tests in CI") is only half-right and partially harmful. Two independent fixes are needed:

1) Make the build contracts run against FRESH output, deterministically, in both CI and pre-push. Don't just reorder CI steps (that still leaves the stale-dist trap for local `npm run test`, and the husky check order). Better: have the build-output test build its own artifact in a beforeAll into a temp/throwaway dir (or assert a fresh build via vite's programmatic build({}) API and inspect the returned RollupOutput in-memory), so it never depends on a pre-existing dist/ and never reads a stale one. Then remove describe.runIf(distExists) so the contracts are unconditional. If that is too slow for the default gate, at minimum (a) move `npm run build` before `npm run test` in BOTH ci.yml and the `check` script, and (b) replace runIf(distExists) with a hard failure when dist is absent (e.g. `expect(distExists).toBe(true)` or throw in beforeAll) so a missing dist is loud, not a silent skip — never a soft skip that masquerades as passing.

2) Close the coverage gap independent of dist. The strongest source-level guard is to assert the lazy contract at the chunk-config level, not per-component: add a test that imports the manualChunks function and modulePreload.resolveDependencies from vite.config.js and asserts (a) @react-pdf/jspdf/pdfkit/fontkit map to 'vendor-pdf', and (b) the modulePreload filter strips vendor-pdf/engine deps.
