Separately, broaden the static-import guard from a hardcoded 2-file list to a glob over all src files that reference generateSettlementPDF/generateCampaignPDF/@react-pdf/jspdf, asserting none of them is a top-level static import outside the designated chunk-source modules (generateSettlementPDF.js, generateCampaignPDF.js, src/pdf/**). That would have caught the SettlementsPanel.jsx path the current greps miss. These source-level checks run with no dist and no build, so they actually execute in CI.

confirmed.title: create-customer-portal edge function is invoked by live client code but absent from BOTH deployment runbooks
confirmed.evidence: src/lib/stripe.js:117 invokes `supabase.functions.invoke('create-customer-portal')` from startCustomerPortal(), which is wired into AccountPage.jsx:199 and PricingPage.jsx:273 ('Manage subscription' — the only way users reach the Stripe billing portal to cancel/update). The function exists at supabase/functions/create-customer-portal/index.ts (3.5KB). But scripts/deploy.sh deploys only 5 functions (lines 138-151: create-checkout, verify-single-dossier, generate-narrative, generate-chronicle, stripe-webhook) while presenting itself as 'Full Deployment Setup … provisions the complete backend' and printing 'Edge functions: 5 deployed' (line 270); docs/DEPLOY.md's deploy list (lines 128-134) deploys 7 — admin-actions and send-email are there, but create-customer-portal is missing from it too. Any environment provisioned per either runbook 404s on the portal call, so paying users cannot manage or cancel subscriptions until someone notices the missing function.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `npx supabase functions deploy create-customer-portal` to both deploy.sh and docs/DEPLOY.md (and consider a test/script that diffs `ls supabase/functions/` against the deploy lists so a new function can't be forgotten).

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed by direct reads. (1) Eight deployable edge functions exist under supabase/functions/ (excluding _shared); create-customer-portal is one of them and is fully implemented (supabase/functions/create-customer-portal/index.ts: auth via getUser, stripe_customer_id lookup/create, stripe.billingPortal.sessions.create). (2) scripts/deploy.sh lines 138-151 deploy only 5 functions (create-checkout, verify-single-dossier, generate-narrative, generate-chronicle, stripe-webhook) and omits create-customer-portal (also admin-actions, send-email), yet the header (line 5) claims it 'provisions the complete backend' and line 270 prints 'Edge functions: 5 deployed'. (3) docs/DEPLOY.md lines 128-134 deploy 7 functions (adds admin-actions, send-email) but still omit create-customer-portal. grep confirms zero occurrences of create-customer-portal in either runbook. (4) The function is live-wired and reachable: src/lib/stripe.js:117 invokes('create-customer-portal') from startCustomerPortal(); that function is the handler for the rendered 'Manage subscription' button in AccountPage.jsx:474-486 (disabled only by portalBusy || !isConfigured) and the manageBilling() CTA in PricingPage.jsx:291,298 for founder/premium 'Manage subscription' — the only UI paths to the Stripe billing portal. (5) No automated guard catches the drift: scripts/validate-edge-functions.mjs only transpiles each function and checks the botGuard.reject contract; it does not diff deploy lists against the functions directory. Net effect: any environment provisioned per either runbook 404s on the portal invoke, so startCustomerPortal throws 'Billing portal failed' and paying users cannot manage/cancel subscriptions until an operator manually deploys the function. I attempted refutation (dead path, upstream guard, intentional behavior, validation coverage) and none holds.
verdicts.fix_note: Suggested fix is correct. Add `npx supabase functions deploy create-customer-portal` to scripts/deploy.sh (and fix the 'Edge functions: 5 deployed' tally on line 270, which is already wrong by 3) and to the docs/DEPLOY.md deploy list (lines 128-134). Stronger remediation: extend scripts/validate-edge-functions.mjs (already iterates supabase/functions/) to also assert every non-_shared function directory appears in both deploy.sh and docs/DEPLOY.md, wiring it into `npm run check` so a newly-added function cannot be silently forgotten from either runbook. Note: deploy.sh deploys generate-narrative/generate-chronicle WITHOUT --no-verify-jwt and the portal function requires an Authorization header, so deploy create-customer-portal with default JWT verification (no --no-verify-jwt), matching the doc's omission of the flag for it.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed end-to-end. The edge function supabase/functions/create-customer-portal/index.ts exists and is real (3546 bytes): it authenticates the caller via JWT (auth.getUser), resolves or creates the Stripe customer, and returns a Billing Portal session.url. The client invokes it at src/lib/stripe.js:117 inside startCustomerPortal(), which is the sole path to Stripe's billing portal. That function is wired into two user-reachable UI handlers: AccountPage.jsx:199 (handleManageBilling) and PricingPage.jsx:273 (manageBilling) — the latter is the literal "Manage subscription" CTA shown to premium/elevated users (PricingPage.jsx:295-298) and the "Founder active" CTA (line 291).

Neither deployment runbook deploys this function. scripts/deploy.sh deploys exactly 5 functions (lines 138-151: create-checkout, verify-single-dossier, generate-narrative, generate-chronicle, stripe-webhook), prints "Edge functions: 5 deployed" (line 270), yet advertises "provisions the complete backend" (line 5). docs/DEPLOY.md lines 128-134 deploys 7 (adds admin-actions, send-email) — create-customer-portal is still missing. I confirmed via grep across .sh/.md/.yml/.yaml/.json/.toml that NO deploy path mentions create-customer-portal anywhere, there is no no-arg catch-all `supabase functions deploy` (which would deploy all), no CI deploy step, no config.toml entry, and scripts/validate-edge-functions.mjs does not cross-check deploy coverage — so npm run check (the gate) cannot catch this drift.

Concrete failure trace: operator provisions a fresh environment per either runbook -> create-customer-portal is never uploaded -> a signed-in paying user clicks "Manage subscription" -> startCustomerPortal() passes its local guards (isConfigured true, session present) -> supabase.functions.invoke('create-customer-portal') hits /functions/v1/create-customer-portal which 404s -> invoke returns a non-2xx/FunctionsFetchError -> `if (error) throw` at stripe.js:120 -> caught by the UI handler -> user sees "Billing portal failed". Because Stripe's portal is the only self-serve route to cancel/update/downgrade a subscription (the customer.subscription.deleted webhook event the system handles is normally triggered FROM this portal), paying users cannot manage or cancel their subscription on any environment built from these runbooks until someone manually deploys the function.

Severity high rather than critical: it is a broken paid-user feature and a billing/retention problem on freshly-provisioned environments, but it is not data loss, money loss, a security hole, or a crash on the core generation path, and already-deployed environments where the function was manually pushed are unaffected.
verdicts.fix_note: Add the missing deploy line to BOTH runbooks (not just one). In scripts/deploy.sh, add after line 151: `npx supabase functions deploy create-customer-portal --project-ref "$PROJECT_REF"` then `ok "Deployed: create-customer-portal"`, and update line 270's hardcoded "5 deployed" to "6 deployed". Note this function reads the Authorization header and calls auth.getUser() to identify the user, so it should be deployed WITH JWT verification (default, i.e. do NOT pass --no-verify-jwt) — matching generate-narrative/generate-chronicle, not the --no-verify-jwt functions; the client already forwards the user's session token. In docs/DEPLOY.md, add `npx supabase functions deploy create-customer-portal` to the list at lines 128-134 (making it 8). Broader hardening worth flagging separately: the deploy runbooks have already drifted twice (5 in deploy.sh vs 7 in DEPLOY.md, both missing this 8th function on disk) — a small validator step that diffs the set of directories under supabase/functions/ against the functions named in deploy.sh + DEPLOY.md, wired into validate:edge, would prevent the entire recurring class.

confirmed.title: deploy.sh has drifted from the real backend: deploys 5 of 8 functions, omits required secrets, and sets a reserved SUPABASE_-prefixed secret
confirmed.evidence: Beyond create-customer-portal (separate finding), deploy.sh also never deploys admin-actions (admin panel mutations) or send-email (the entire email lifecycle; DevEmailBanner exists specifically to warn when its Resend secrets are missing), both of which exist in supabase/functions/ and are deployed by the newer docs/DEPLOY.md list. Its secrets step (lines 233-242) sets STRIPE_PRICE_CREDITS_10/50 — the 'early beta' legacy SKUs per supabase/functions/create-checkout/index.ts:32-33 — but none of the ACTIVE catalog prices the function requires (STRIPE_PRICE_CREDITS_25/60/150, STRIPE_PRICE_FOUNDER_LIFETIME, STRIPE_PRICE_SINGLE_DOSSIER, lines 45-50), so every current purchase product resolves to an empty price id and create-checkout throws 'Price ID not configured'. It also omits RESEND_API_KEY/RESEND_FROM_EMAIL (required per DEPLOY.md:142-143). Finally line 242 passes SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY to `npx supabase secrets set` — the SUPABASE_ prefix is reserved by the Supabase CLI (rejected or skipped with a warning depending on CLI version; the runtime auto-injects that key anyway), so under `set -euo pipefail` (line 21) the script may abort at step 8. The script is effectively a stale snapshot of an older product (3 Stripe products vs today's 6+, 5 functions vs 8).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Either delete scripts/deploy.sh in favor of docs/DEPLOY.md, or update it: deploy all 8 functions, create/set the active price catalog from src/config/pricing.js, add the Resend secrets, and drop the SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY secrets-set line.

verdicts.reasoning:
I verified every claim against the actual code; the finding holds.

1) Function drift (5 of 8): supabase/functions/ contains 8 deployable functions (admin-actions, create-checkout, create-customer-portal, generate-chronicle, generate-narrative, send-email, stripe-webhook, verify-single-dossier). scripts/deploy.sh:138-150 deploys only 5 (create-checkout, verify-single-dossier, generate-narrative, generate-chronicle, stripe-webhook). It omits admin-actions, send-email, and create-customer-portal. The maintained docs/DEPLOY.md:127-134 deploys admin-actions and send-email too. CONFIRMED.

2) Stripe price drift -> broken checkout: deploy.sh:237-238 sets STRIPE_PRICE_CREDITS_10/50, which create-checkout/index.ts:32-33 explicitly labels 'early beta' legacy SKUs. The active PRICE_MAP the function reads (index.ts:45-50) is credits_25/60/150, premium, founder_lifetime, single_dossier — corroborated by src/config/pricing.js:48-50,188,206,226. With only CREDITS_10/50/PREMIUM set, every active purchase product resolves to '' and create-checkout throws 'Price ID not configured' at index.ts:153. CONFIRMED — current catalog purchases are non-functional.

3) Omitted Resend secrets: deploy.sh:233-242 never sets RESEND_API_KEY/RESEND_FROM_EMAIL, which send-email/index.ts:264-265 reads (returns 'unconfigured' when missing) and DEPLOY.md:142-143 lists as required. DevEmailBanner.jsx + emailLifecycle.js exist specifically to surface this missing-secret state. CONFIRMED.

4) SUPABASE_-prefixed secret: deploy.sh:242 passes SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY into a single batched `npx supabase secrets set` call under `set -euo pipefail` (line 21). The SUPABASE_ prefix is reserved by the Supabase CLI and the runtime auto-injects that key (DEPLOY.md:152-154 annotates the SUPABASE_* group as 'already set by Supabase'). This is the one version-dependent claim, but the reviewer hedges it honestly ('rejected or skipped with a warning depending on CLI version'), and even discounting it entirely the finding stands on items 1-3.

I could not refute any claim. The script is a genuine stale snapshot of an older product; running it yields a backend with no working purchases, no email lifecycle, and no admin/customer-portal functions.

Severity medium (not higher): this is an interactive bootstrap/ops script, not runtime application code, and it is not auto-invoked by CI or the gate (`npm run check` does not run it). docs/DEPLOY.md is the maintained alternative. But anyone who follows the script's own documented usage gets a broken deployment, so it is a real, non-trivial defect — medium is correct.
verdicts.fix_note: The suggested fix is sound. Two refinements: (a) the function list to add is admin-actions, send-email, AND create-customer-portal (the reviewer notes the portal as a separate finding, but a corrected script must include all three to match DEPLOY.md's intent plus the 8th function); (b) for the SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY line, the cleanest fix is to simply drop it — the runtime auto-injects SUPABASE_URL/ANON_KEY/SERVICE_ROLE_KEY into edge functions, so it is both reserved-by-CLI and redundant. Preferred resolution given DEPLOY.md is the maintained source of truth: delete scripts/deploy.sh outright rather than carry two divergent deploy paths.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified end-to-end against the real code. (1) Function count: supabase/functions/ contains 8 functions; deploy.sh:138-150 deploys only 5 (create-checkout, verify-single-dossier, generate-narrative, generate-chronicle, stripe-webhook). admin-actions, send-email, and create-customer-portal exist and are in the canonical deploy list at docs/DEPLOY.md:135-141 but are never deployed by the script. (2) Stripe prices: deploy.sh:237-239 sets only STRIPE_PRICE_CREDITS_10/50/PREMIUM. The active catalog the client actually sells is credits_25/60/150 (pricing.js:48-50), premium (188), founder_lifetime (206), single_dossier (226). credits_10/credits_50 are not even in the client catalog. In create-checkout/index.ts, PRICE_MAP entries for the active non-premium SKUs (lines 45-50) read env vars the script never sets, so they resolve to '' and line 153 throws 'Price ID not configured for ${product}', returned as HTTP 400 to the client. Net effect: on a fresh deploy via this script, every credit pack, the founder lifetime, and the single-dossier microtransaction fail at checkout; only the $6/mo premium subscription works. That is a direct money/revenue-path break. (3) Resend secrets: send-email/index.ts:264-265 requires RESEND_API_KEY and RESEND_FROM_EMAIL; the script never sets them, and send-email isn't deployed at all. emailLifecycle.js + DevEmailBanner.jsx confirm the email lifecycle and its 'unconfigured' warning surface exist exactly as the finding states. (4) deploy.sh:242 passes SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY to `npx supabase secrets set`; the Supabase CLI reserves the SUPABASE_ prefix and the runtime auto-injects this key (DEPLOY.md:154 notes it is already set by Supabase; create-checkout reads it at index.ts:161 without the script setting it). Under `set -euo pipefail` (line 21) a CLI rejection would abort step 8 before step 8 finishes and before step 9 writes .env. The script's own summary (deploy.sh:270-271) advertises '5 deployed / 3 created', confirming it is a stale snapshot. All four sub-claims are evidence-backed. The CLI-prefix abort is the only conditional part (it hedges correctly on CLI version), but the function-omission and wrong-price defects are unconditional. Severity high (not critical): this is a one-shot operator setup script run manually by the owner, not a runtime code path that breaks for end users on main — but if used as-is it produces a broken backend whose primary purchase flows all fail, which an operator could ship to production. Not critical because the script is not on the user-facing main path and an operator must run it; the consequence is a misconfigured deploy rather than silent data loss in the running app.
verdicts.fix_note: The finding's suggested fix direction is correct; concrete corrections: (a) Add the missing deploy lines for admin-actions, send-email, and create-customer-portal (the separate finding) so all 8 functions deploy. admin-actions and create-customer-portal likely need --no-verify-jwt parity decisions consistent with DEPLOY.md; send-email is invoked via supabase.functions.invoke (authed), so keep JWT verification. (b) Replace STRIPE_PRICE_CREDITS_10/50 in the secrets step with the active SKUs the create-checkout PRICE_MAP requires: STRIPE_PRICE_CREDITS_25/60/150, STRIPE_PRICE_FOUNDER_LIFETIME, STRIPE_PRICE_SINGLE_DOSSIER (plus the existing STRIPE_PRICE_PREMIUM). This also means the Stripe product-creation block (deploy.sh:167-196) must be updated to create 25/60/150 packs at $4.99/$9.99/$19.99, a founder one-time price ($99), and a single-dossier one-time price ($2.99) and capture their price IDs — today it only creates 10/50/premium. Legacy credits_10/50/5/15/40 may be left unset (DEPLOY.md says they are optional for refund/replay continuity). (c) Add RESEND_API_KEY and RESEND_FROM_EMAIL prompts + secrets (DEPLOY.md:142-143). (d) Remove SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY from the `npx supabase secrets set` call entirely — the platform injects it automatically; passing a SUPABASE_-prefixed key is rejected by the CLI and is the most likely cause of an abort under set -euo pipefail. (e) Update the final summary text (deploy.sh:270-271) from '5 deployed / 3 created' to the real counts. One nuance to verify before merging a fix: confirm the Stripe webhook event set (deploy.sh:206-209) still matches what stripe-webhook/index.ts handles for the founder one-time and single-dossier flows (checkout.session.completed covers one-time payments, so it is likely fine, but worth a glance).

confirmed.title: DEPLOY.md deploys stripe-webhook without --no-verify-jwt — Stripe's webhook posts would be rejected before signature verification, so payments never credit
confirmed.evidence: docs/DEPLOY.md:128 says `npx supabase functions deploy stripe-webhook` with no `--no-verify-jwt`, while line 130 uses the flag for verify-single-dossier — so the author knows the flag; its absence on the webhook reads as a mistake, not style. Supabase functions deploy defaults verify_jwt=true (supabase/config.toml declares no per-function override), which makes the platform reject any request lacking a valid project JWT before function code runs. Stripe webhook deliveries carry only Stripe-Signature, no Authorization header, so every checkout.session.completed/invoice.paid event 401s and the function's own constructEvent signature check (the intended trust boundary per ARCHITECTURE.md:117-118, 'no user JWT on webhooks') never executes. On an environment provisioned by following DEPLOY.md verbatim, users are charged by Stripe but credits/subscriptions/founder seats are never granted — silent money loss until webhook failure alerts are noticed. scripts/deploy.sh:150 confirms the correct invocation (`stripe-webhook … --no-verify-jwt`), i.e. the two runbooks contradict each other on a money-critical flag.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `--no-verify-jwt` to the stripe-webhook deploy line in docs/DEPLOY.md (or add `[functions.stripe-webhook] verify_jwt = false` to supabase/config.toml so the flag can't be forgotten), and note in the doc why the function relies on Stripe signature verification instead of JWT.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against the actual files. DEPLOY.md:128 deploys `npx supabase functions deploy stripe-webhook` with NO `--no-verify-jwt`, while line 130 (verify-single-dossier) uses the flag — proving the author knows it and its omission on the webhook is anomalous. supabase/config.toml contains no `verify_jwt` setting and no `[functions.*]` blocks at all (grep returned empty), so Supabase's platform default `verify_jwt=true` applies: the platform rejects any request lacking a valid project JWT before the function body runs. The stripe-webhook function (supabase/functions/stripe-webhook/index.ts) authenticates ONLY via the Stripe signature — index.ts:171 reads the `stripe-signature` header and index.ts:177 calls `stripe.webhooks.constructEvent`; it reads no Authorization header. Stripe webhook deliveries carry no project JWT, so with verify_jwt on, every checkout.session.completed/subscription event 401s and the function's own signature verification (the documented trust boundary, index.ts:138-167: 'EVERY METADATA READ BELOW IS ONLY SAFE BECAUSE 1. We require a stripe-signature header AND verify it') never executes. The credit/subscription/founder grants flow through system_grant_credits inside the webhook, so they would never fire — users charged by Stripe but never credited. scripts/deploy.sh:150 deploys the same function WITH `--no-verify-jwt`, confirming that is the intended invocation and DEPLOY.md is the contradicting, incorrect runbook. Every cited fact in the claim holds.
verdicts.fix_note: Both proposed fixes are valid; the config.toml option is the more durable one. Recommend adding `[functions.stripe-webhook]\nverify_jwt = false` to supabase/config.toml so the setting is enforced regardless of which runbook (DEPLOY.md vs scripts/deploy.sh) is followed and can't be forgotten on a manual deploy, AND adding `--no-verify-jwt` to DEPLOY.md:128 with a one-line note that the webhook trusts Stripe's signature (constructEvent) instead of a project JWT. Note also that create-checkout is deployed with --no-verify-jwt in scripts/deploy.sh:138 but WITHOUT it in DEPLOY.md:129 — create-checkout requires a Supabase JWT internally per the webhook's own comment (index.ts:149), so leaving verify_jwt on there may be acceptable, but the DEPLOY.md/deploy.sh divergence on that line is worth a glance too while fixing the webhook. Severity high (not critical) because it is a doc-only defect in one of two runbooks and the canonical scripts/deploy.sh path is correct, so only an operator following DEPLOY.md verbatim is affected.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED via direct file reads. Every link in the chain holds:

1. docs/DEPLOY.md:128 deploys `stripe-webhook` with NO `--no-verify-jwt`, while docs/DEPLOY.md:130 uses the flag for verify-single-dossier — so the author knows the flag; its absence is a mistake, not style.

2. scripts/deploy.sh:150 deploys the identical function WITH `--no-verify-jwt`. The two runbooks directly contradict each other on a money-critical flag.

3. supabase/config.toml contains ZERO `verify_jwt` references (grep -ni confirmed none). With no per-function override, the Supabase gateway applies its default verify_jwt=true and rejects any request lacking a valid project JWT before function code runs.

4. supabase/functions/stripe-webhook/index.ts:169-181 — the function's sole trust boundary is its own Stripe signature check (stripe.webhooks.constructEvent at line 177). The trust-boundary comment block (lines 138-167) explicitly documents signature verification as THE boundary and assumes no user JWT on webhooks. Stripe deliveries carry only `stripe-signature` (read at line 171), never an Authorization: Bearer project-JWT.

Concrete failure path: operator follows DEPLOY.md's 'Edge function — manual' section verbatim → stripe-webhook deployed with gateway JWT enforcement ON → Stripe POSTs checkout.session.completed / invoice.paid with no project JWT → gateway returns 401 before line 177 → constructEvent never runs → the credit/subscription/founder-seat grants in the event handler (case at line 185+) never execute → user is charged by Stripe but receives nothing. Silent money loss until someone notices webhook delivery failures in the Stripe dashboard.

Severity is high rather than critical because this is a DOCUMENTATION defect, not a code defect: the production deploy path (scripts/deploy.sh) is correct. Harm requires an operator to hand-follow DEPLOY.md instead of running the script. That is plausible (DEPLOY.md is the named manual runbook and the section is literally titled 'manual'), making it a real money-loss bug, but it is one remove from broken code, capping it below critical.

Secondary observation (lower confidence, not the core finding): DEPLOY.md:129 also omits the flag for create-checkout, which scripts/deploy.sh:138 deploys WITH --no-verify-jwt. create-checkout does its own auth via supabase.auth.getUser() (index.ts:142), so impact there depends on client bearer behavior and CORS preflight handling — not asserted as a confirmed break.
verdicts.fix_note: Add `--no-verify-jwt` to docs/DEPLOY.md:128 so it reads `npx supabase functions deploy stripe-webhook --no-verify-jwt`, matching scripts/deploy.sh:150. For consistency and to match deploy.sh, also add `--no-verify-jwt` to create-checkout on DEPLOY.md:129 (deploy.sh:138 uses it). A more durable fix is to encode the policy declaratively instead of relying on CLI flags being remembered: add per-function blocks to supabase/config.toml, e.g. `[functions.stripe-webhook]` and `[functions.create-checkout]` and `[functions.verify-single-dossier]` each with `verify_jwt = false`. That makes the setting authoritative regardless of which runbook is used and removes the DEPLOY.md-vs-deploy.sh drift entirely. Note the verify-single-dossier line is already correct, and generate-narrative/generate-chronicle/admin-actions/send-email intentionally keep JWT verification (do not add the flag to those).

rejected.title: geographyData plains institutionModifiers targets institution name 'granar' — truncated, lowercase, unlike every sibling entry
rejected.why: The matcher consuming this entry (getResourceMultiplier, src/generators/steps/assembleInstitutions.js:82-90) lowercases both the institution name and mod.name and does a substring check — so 'granar' correctly and case-insensitively matches all three catalog granary institutions ('Town granary', 'City granaries', 'State granary complex'; institutionalCatalog.js:867, 1474, 2320). Verified by simulation. The 'granar' stem is the established idiom across the codebase (economicGenerator.js:611/1021, priorityHelpers.js:57, stressGenerator.js:86, resourceData.js:122/188/443, foodGenerator.js:61 uses 'city granari'), deliberately chosen to cover singular/plural and all tiers — an exact catalog name would only match one of the three granaries and would regress coverage. The reviewer's evidence misstates the matcher as case-sensitive and concedes the match works; what remains is a style observation about a data table entry, not a defect.

rejected.title: Exports-vs-necessityImports filter is a no-op: .name accessed on string items
rejected.why: The mechanical observation is correct: getGoodsModifiers returns plain strings (l.push(m), src/generators/economicGenerator.js:1260), so the filter at line 1216 (!necessityImports.includes(item.name)) tests includes(undefined) and removes nothing; the same expression sits in _computeIncomeStreams at line 968, which is dead code (defined, never called anywhere in src). However, no concrete failure path exists. necessityImports can only ever be a subset of {'Salt','Iron','Grain','Timber'} (the only push sites in getInstitutionServices, lines 1011-1029), and GOODS_MODIFIERS_BY_TIER (data/tradeGoodsData.js:452) is a static, closed key set (goodsToggles only flip existing keys; the custom-content hook only resolves requiredInstitution). A live node enumeration over every tier x every necessity-import combination proves the buggy filter and the exact-match-corrected filter produce IDENTICAL output: no export key at any tier exactly equals any of the four necessity-import strings ('Grain surplus' != 'Grain', 'Milled timber' != 'Timber', etc.). The corrected line is therefore extensionally identical to the buggy line over the entire reachable input space - no seed, config, or state produces a behavioral difference, so the claimed downstream impact via the v.exports keyword scans (lines 1968-2076) and stress export lists (2119/2122) carries zero delta. The one genuine coherence hazard in this area (hamlet+port exporting 'Grain surplus' while necessity-importing 'Grain') is (a) not preventable by the suggested fix since Array.includes is exact-match, and (b) already neutralized in final output by the trade-goods subsumption pass: reconcileTradeLists(subsumeTradeGoods(re), q) at economicGenerator.js:2398-2403 (re-applied in steps/economyReconcilePass.js:65-74) was demonstrated live to drop 'Grain surplus' from exports when 'Grain' is imported, using canonical-good matching; the salt-coupled variant is separately handled by the adjacent working filter (lines 1217-1220). Net: line 1216 is type-confused, permanently-inert dead-guard code - a cleanup candidate - but not a defect with any observable or even latent behavioral consequence under the current closed data set.

rejected.title: FIELD_ALIASES declares 'stressTypes' an alias of stressors, contradicting canonicalAccessors' deliberate exclusion — normalize can promote type-strings into the canonical object array
rejected.why:
The claim has a real documentation-level kernel but the asserted runtime defect is unreachable.

VERIFIED TRUE (the contract inconsistency): settlement.schema.js:57 lists `stressors: ['stress', 'stresses', 'stressTypes']`, and normalizeSettlement.js:91-98 + 139-143 will copy the first defined alias to the canonical `stressors` key when `out.stressors` is undefined. canonicalAccessors.js:24-27 explicitly documents `stressTypes` as intentionally excluded because it holds type-strings, not stressor objects. The two modules do state opposite things about the same alias. canonStressors (canonicalAccessors.js:34) does read s.stressors first, and downstream readers (conditionPromotion.js:84,120,123; deriveSystemState.js:236-244) do index .type/.name/.severity on the elements.

UNREACHABLE (the actual harm): The bug only triggers on a settlement carrying a TOP-LEVEL `stressTypes` string array with no stress/stresses/stressors. No such shape is ever produced:
- assembleSettlement.js:82-88 is the sole birthplace of a settlement object; it dual-writes top-level `stress` and `stressors` as the OBJECT array, and the type-strings live only under `config.stressTypes` (line 95). Because `out.stressors` is already defined, normalizeSettlement.js:141's `out[canonical] === undefined` guard blocks the stressTypes alias from ever firing.
- A repo-wide grep (src, supabase edge functions, tests, and `git log -S "stressTypes:"` history) finds ZERO writes of `stressTypes` at the top level of a settlement; it is exclusively a config-level key (config.stressTypes / effectiveConfig.stressTypes) in every codepath and every test fixture (tests/joins/*.test.js all assert config.stressTypes).
- settlementMigrations.js contains no stressor handling, so it neither creates nor relocates a top-level stressTypes.

The reviewer's own caveat ("trigger requires an old/imported save") is the load-bearing condition, and no version of this codebase ever produced a save with top-level stressTypes. The concrete artifact is a contradictory comment vs. alias list that yields no runtime defect on any reachable input — it neither breaks determinism, corrupts data, nor crashes a real path. The suggested fix (drop the alias, matching canonicalAccessors' rationale) is reasonable hygiene, but this is a latent contract smell, not a real behavioral bug. Per the engagement bar (uncertain/unreachable => isReal=false), I judge it not a real defect.

rejected.title: Auth-intent queue is write-only: no handler is ever registered and consume() is never called, so the flagship 'Save this town - free account' flow silently drops the save
rejected.why:
The claim is refuted by the actual code in this checkout. The reviewer explicitly caveated that store/index.js and authSlice.js were zero-byte in their checkout and that they could not rule out wiring there. In this checkout both files are fully populated and contain exactly the two call sites the reviewer said were absent:

1) Handler registration: src/store/index.js:101-138. registerAuthIntentHandlers() calls registerHandler(INTENTS.SAVE_SETTLEMENT, handler) where the handler invokes savesService.save({name, tier, settlement, config}) with the same payload shape GenerateWizard stashes (GenerateWizard.jsx:243-248). It also fires SAVE_SIGNUP_INTENT_FULFILLED analytics and surfaces a toast. Registration runs at module init via import('../lib/authIntents.js').then(registerAuthIntentHandlers) (index.js:134-138). savesService is imported at index.js:41.

2) Consumption: src/store/authSlice.js:213-219. On the SIGNED_IN auth-state-change event the listener does import('../lib/authIntents.js').then(({ consume }) => consume({ user, tier, role, ... })). This listener is wired up via authService.onAuthChange in initAuth (authSlice.js:157), with single-subscription guarding (lines 151-156).

So the round trip is complete: setPending on button click -> SIGNED_IN listener calls consume -> registry dispatches to the SAVE_SETTLEMENT handler -> savesService.save fires. The settlement is not silently dropped.

Notably, the implementation is more robust than the reviewer's own suggested fix: instead of hooking only AuthModal's onAuthed, it routes through the SIGNED_IN auth-state-change listener, which also covers magic-link / OAuth / email-confirmation returns that bypass the modal. The reviewer's read of AuthModal.jsx (onAuthed={onClose}) is irrelevant because consumption was never intended to live there in this implementation.

The reviewer's grep finding 'zero call sites' is a direct artifact of the broken/incomplete checkout they disclosed. With the populated files, the grep finds both registerHandler and consume call sites.

The one residual truth in the report — no test pins the stash->auth->save round trip (grep for authIntents in *.test.js/jsx returns nothing) — is a test-coverage gap, not a functional silent-drop bug, and does not support a 'high' severity defect.

rejected.title: loadCustomContentFromCloud leaves customContentLoading stuck at true when the owner changes mid-flight
rejected.why:
The early-return branch at src/store/customContentSlice.js:221 does indeed omit resetting customContentLoading, but the claimed harm — "After a sign-out during a cloud load, the flag stays true indefinitely" — does not actually occur, because every owner-change path that can trip the stale-owner guard also resets the flag via another channel.

Mechanism: ownerIdFromState (line 35-37) reads state.auth.user.id. For the guard at 221 to fire, the owner must change between line 216 (capture) and line 221 (compare). The owner changes only through auth mutations:

1. Sign-out (the reviewer's exact scenario): authSignOut (authSlice.js:284) and the Supabase SIGNED_OUT event (authSlice.js:158-159) both route through clearAuth(), which at authSlice.js:98 calls clearCloudCustomContent(). clearCloudCustomContent sets customContentLoading = false (customContentSlice.js:279). So a sign-out resets the flag — the headline scenario is refuted.

2. Switch to a different user: the SIGNED_IN/TOKEN_REFRESHED different-user branch (authSlice.js:162-166) calls clearCloudCustomContent() (resets flag) before installing the new user. Additionally, App.jsx's cloud-sync effect (App.jsx:214-226) depends on authUserId, so it re-runs and starts a fresh loadCustomContentFromCloud() for the new owner, which sets the flag true at line 217 and clears it on resolve/catch. The old in-flight load returning early at 221 correctly does NOT touch the flag the new load now owns.

In production, account switches and sign-outs always fire Supabase's onAuthStateChange (src/lib/auth.js:289), routing through the authSlice onAuthChange listener that performs these resets. There is no realistic path where the guard discards a stale payload and leaves customContentLoading stuck true with no subsequent reset.

The only true observation is a minor fragility: the early-return is not self-contained and relies on external callers/listeners to manage the flag. That is a robustness nit, not the observable stuck-loading bug claimed. CompendiumPanel's _customContentLoading (CompendiumPanel.jsx:725) would therefore not show a permanent loading state after sign-out.

rejected.title: Dead legacy hooks useConfig.js and useToggles.js (strangler-fig leftovers) are referenced by nothing
rejected.why:
Verified every factual sub-claim, but the defect does not meet the "real bug" bar.

CONFIRMED FACTS:
1. src/hooks/useConfig.js (39 lines) and src/hooks/useToggles.js (112 lines) both exist and each export only a `default` function (local-useState implementations, pre-Zustand).
2. Zero importers: grep for `hooks/useConfig`, `hooks/useToggles`, `from '...useConfig'`, default imports, and dynamic `import()` across the entire tree (excluding node_modules) returns NOTHING. There is no src/hooks/index.js barrel re-exporting them.
3. The only grep hit for "useConfig" outside the file itself is src/store/index.js:144 `export const useConfig = () => useStore(s => s.config)` — a SEPARATE Zustand selector hook defined inline, NOT an import of the legacy file. No module-level name collision exists because nothing imports the file-path version.
4. Drift claim is true: configSlice.js:14 defines `settlementAgeMode: 'auto'` and `useCustomContent` is read in settlementSlice.js:563/869, neither present in the legacy DEFAULT_CONFIG.

WHY NOT A REAL BUG (REPRODUCE/TRACE lens):
- These modules have NO execution path. Nothing imports them, so no input/state ever reaches this code at runtime. There is no concrete failure path that produces wrong simulation output, determinism break, data loss, billing/auth issue, crash, or PDF/dossier parity drift.
- The store's live `useConfig` is a distinct symbol; a developer importing useConfig from the store gets the correct Zustand version. The dead file does not shadow or interfere with it.
- The only way the stale-shape drift bites is a HYPOTHETICAL future developer importing the dead hook — that is latent maintenance risk / clutter, not a current reproducible defect. It also would surface immediately (consumers reading settlementAgeMode/useCustomContent would get undefined).
- The product's rules of engagement explicitly exclude clutter/style nits and speculative concerns; dead strangler-fig leftovers with zero importers are exactly that. Deleting them is legitimate cleanup, but their presence causes no defect.

So the underlying observations are accurate, but the claim as a BUG (with a failure path) is not real. It is dead code, severity not-a-bug.

rejected.title: GalleryComments initial fetch has no error handling — failures leave a silent, permanently-empty comment list and an unhandled rejection
rejected.why:
The claim's central, load-bearing mechanism — "a network/RLS failure produces an unhandled promise rejection" because the effect at GalleryComments.jsx:46-53 does `.then()` with no `.catch()` — is false.

The awaited function, `fetchGalleryComments` (src/lib/gallery.js:237-252), cannot reject on a network/RLS failure:
- Lines 240-243: `const { data, error } = await supabase.rpc('list_gallery_comments', ...); if (error) { console.error('[gallery] comments failed:', error); return []; }`. Any error path is caught and the function RESOLVES with `[]`. The only `await` inside is `supabase.rpc(...)`.
- postgrest-js 2.103.0 (node_modules/@supabase/postgrest-js/dist/index.cjs:294-335) converts every fetch failure — network errors, "Failed to fetch", header overflow, etc. — into a RESOLVED value `{ error: {...}, data: null, status: 0 }` whenever `shouldThrowOnError` is false. The codebase never calls `.throwOnError()` (grep for `throwOnError` in src returns nothing), so the default resolve-with-error behavior always applies.

Therefore the promise returned by `fetchGalleryComments(dossierId)` never rejects; the missing `.catch` on the `.then` at line 49 can produce no unhandled rejection. There IS a global `unhandledrejection` handler (src/lib/errorReporter.js:86) anyway, but it is moot here.

A residual, much weaker observation survives: on a read failure the component receives `[]`, renders "No comments yet." (line 153-156), never sets its `error` state (only submit/remove do, lines 64/77), and has no initial loading state, so a failed read is indistinguishable from a genuinely empty thread and may briefly flash. But (a) this is NOT the defect described (no rejection occurs), and (b) it is the deliberate, established degradation contract of the lib layer: fetchGalleryComments intentionally swallows the error, logs via console.error, and returns [] — exactly mirroring its siblings fetchMyGallery (105-108), fetchCuratedGallery (127-130), and fetchMoreByCreator (214-217). The component's .then-without-.catch is consistent with a function that by design never rejects. This residual is a cosmetic UX nicety at most, not the unhandled-rejection / silent-failure-from-an-unguarded-promise defect claimed.

rejected.title: RegenerationDeltaCard's empty-array semantics contradict its own comment: explicit empty newOpportunities/newRisks falls back to re-deriving from newEntities
rejected.why:
The reviewer correctly spots that the comment at src/components/primitives/RegenerationDeltaCard.jsx:169-170 says "use ??" while the code at lines 171-175 (and 181-185 for newRisks) uses a truthy-length guard `(Array.isArray(...) && ....length > 0) ? ... : delta.newEntities?.filter(...)`. So an explicit empty `newOpportunities: []` DOES take the fallback branch. That part of the claim is accurate.

However, the asserted consequence — that the card "resurrects" opportunities and "misreports what the rerun introduced" — is false, because of an invariant in the only producer of this delta. In src/domain/regenerationDelta.js:
- line 105: `const newOpportunities = added.filter(e => e.type === 'hook')`
- line 106-108: `const newRisks = added.filter(e => e.type==='threat' || 'condition' || 'clock')`
- line 138: `newEntities: added`

Thus newOpportunities is, by construction, the exact same filter the card's fallback applies to newEntities, and newEntities === added. When the derivation emits `newOpportunities: []`, that means `added` contained zero hooks, so the fallback `delta.newEntities?.filter(e => e.type === 'hook')` re-derives the SAME empty array — nothing is resurrected, and Section() returns null for the empty result (line 210). The newRisks filter at card line 184 (`['threat','condition','clock']`) is byte-for-byte identical to derivation lines 106-108, so the same holds.

The reviewer's hypothesized trigger ("deriveRegenerationDelta intentionally emits newOpportunities:[] e.g. it filtered hooks out as canon-preserved") does not exist anywhere in the code: there is no path where a hook lands in newEntities but is deliberately excluded from newOpportunities. I also confirmed the store (settlementSlice.js:699-702) sets lastRegenerationDelta directly from deriveRegenerationDelta with no reshaping, so there is no alternate producer that could violate the invariant. The empty-array fallback branch is therefore observationally indistinguishable from the documented `??` behavior for every reachable input — zero runtime impact, no misreporting.

What remains is a purely cosmetic comment/code divergence with no behavioral effect, which is a documentation nit explicitly out of scope (not wrong simulation output, not a parity/determinism/security/data issue).

rejected.title: Hardcoded owner-email grants client-side admin role regardless of profiles.role, contradicting the module's own trust model
rejected.why:
The claim's load-bearing assertions are refuted by the server-side code. The reviewer argues the email-based escalation in src/lib/auth.js:48 is a "hidden, client-only second authority" that grants admin "regardless of profiles.role," so "if the profiles row says 'user', the UI claims admin while every privileged RPC fails." That is factually wrong:

1) The owner email is NOT contradicted by profiles.role — supabase/migrations/018_account_billing_models_credits.sql:26-28 runs `update public.profiles set role = 'admin' where lower(email) = 'clausellstokes@aol.com'`. The DB row for this email IS 'admin', so client and DB agree; there is no drift and `profiles.role` is not bypassed.

2) Privileged RPCs do NOT fail for the owner — `current_user_is_privileged()` (migration 018:30-45) returns true when `role in ('developer','admin') OR lower(email) = 'clausellstokes@aol.com'`. This RPC backs the privileged RLS policies and mutations across migrations 009, 012, 017, 022, 024. The admin-actions edge function (supabase/functions/admin-actions/index.ts:54,173-178) applies the same owner-email override (env-configurable). So the privilege the client claims is server-backed everywhere it matters — the predicted "every privileged RPC fails" symptom does not occur.

3) Not "hidden" or drift-prone — the same owner identity is applied deliberately at the migration, RPC, edge-function, and client layers. The file header's "set via profiles.role only; user_metadata is not trusted" is specifically about not trusting user-WRITABLE metadata; the owner email is a deploy-time constant, not user-controllable input, and migration 018 reconciles the profiles row to match it.

4) The mock-mode point (auth.js:340) only executes when !isConfigured (no Supabase env vars = local dev with no backend), where there is no real DB or privileged RPC to bypass; it is an explicit dev convenience, not a security boundary.

The chain the reviewer traced (buildProfileResult -> auth.role -> isElevated() at authSlice.js:113 -> spendCredits short-circuit at creditsSlice.js:55 / AdminPanel render at App.jsx:521) is wired as described, but the conclusion — a UI/server privilege mismatch or a DB-drifting hidden authority — is incorrect because the server consistently honors the same owner identity and the migration sets the DB role to match. No wrong simulation output, no security hole, no broken feature, no crash. At most this is a minor architectural observation (privileged identity expressed as a constant), already reconciled to the DB, which does not rise to a defect.

rejected.title: saves.js localUpdate/localDelete use strict id equality while sibling functions String-normalize — silent persistence no-op when numeric local ids meet string ids
rejected.why:
The type asymmetry the reviewer cites is factually present: localUpdate (src/lib/saves.js:272) and localDelete (saves.js:280) use strict === / !==, while localMutateBatch (309-314) and localReactivateFreeSettlement (289) deliberately String()-normalize. That observation is accurate. However, the bug's load-bearing causal claim — that a stringified id "round-tripped through the /settlements/:id route param" reaches localUpdate/localDelete and silently no-ops — is refuted by the actual code.

(1) The route param string (routeId = params.id, App.jsx:513) is used ONLY to FIND a matching save: SettlementsPanel.jsx:705 does `const match = saves.find(s => String(s.id) === String(routeId))`. The matched object's OWN id (match.id) becomes detail.saveData.id — the string route param is never used as the id. (2) match.id is the original stored id from localList() -> localLoad() (saves.js:21-22), where JSON.parse preserves the numeric type minted by `const id = v2.id || Date.now()` (saves.js:264). So in local mode the id flowing downstream is numeric. (3) Every id that reaches localUpdate traces to a save record's own id: activeSaveId = save.id (settlementSlice.js:1599), saveId = detail?.saveData?.id (SettlementDetail.jsx:320/634), campaignSlice saveId: save.id (campaignSlice.js:311/1100/1164), aiSlice saveId props from the same source. None is the stringified route param. So localUpdate is always called with a number-vs-number match in local mode; strict equality succeeds.

(4) localDelete has NO caller at all. SettlementsPanel's delete path (deleteConfirmed, line 893-908) routes through persistBatch -> savesService.mutateBatch (the String-normalized localMutateBatch), not savesService.delete. A repo-wide grep finds no invocation of savesService.delete anywhere; the only `.delete` hits are campaignService.delete (a different service) and Set/Map.delete. So localDelete cannot produce the claimed reverted-on-reload symptom because it is never reached.

Because no reachable code path feeds a string id into localUpdate/localDelete, the claimed silent-persistence-no-op cannot occur. The asymmetry is at worst a latent style inconsistency with no observable defect — uncertain-to-refuted, and the mechanism in the evidence is demonstrably wrong.

rejected.title: Non-AI PDF exports silently drop arrivalScene and pressureSentence that the on-screen dossier shows
rejected.why:
The reviewer's mechanism is partly right but the load-bearing claim is wrong. Verified facts that hold: the raw engine writes top-level settlement.arrivalScene / settlement.pressureSentence (src/generators/steps/assembleSettlement.js:105-106); src/data/sampleDossier.json carries both; the web tabs render them straight off the (raw) settlement prop in non-narrative mode (src/components/new/tabs/OverviewTab.jsx:192-195 reads r.arrivalScene/r.pressureSentence, src/components/new/SummaryTab.jsx:190-198); OutputContainer.jsx passes activeSettlement = showNarrative ? aiSettlement : rawSettlement to those tabs (lines 234, 406, 419); and supabase generate-narrative/index.ts:434-435 reads s.arrivalScene/s.pressureSentence as refinement input. And yes, viewModel.js:223-224 (summarySlice) and :350-351 (overviewSlice) gate both fields behind useAi and never fall back to s?.arrivalScene.

But the conclusion — "every standard (non-AI) PDF export loses the signature opening prose that the screen displays" — is false, because the cited gate is on the active render path that no rendered chapter actually consumes:
1) summarySlice.arrivalScene/pressureSentence (line 223-224) are consumed ONLY by src/pdf/sections/SummaryPage.jsx (line 68-90, `const su = vm.summary`). SummaryPage is not imported anywhere in src/pdf/SettlementPDF.jsx; a full grep shows its only importer is tests/pdf/missingValuePlaceholders.test.js. There is no "summary" chapter key in variants.js and no SummaryPage in the rendered Document (SettlementPDF.jsx:117-136) or ToC (89-108). It is a dead chapter.
2) overviewSlice.arrivalScene/pressureSentence (line 350-351) are consumed by NO section. The only consumer of vm.overview is the included Overview chapter (src/pdf/sections/Overview.jsx, chapter 01), and a full-file grep shows it never references o.arrivalScene/o.pressureSentence — only its doc comment (line 9) claims "Situation prose (arrival scene + pressure sentence in AI mode)" while the code omits it. Cover.jsx reads vm.summary.identity/crisis/situation (lines 131-133) but not arrivalScene/pressureSentence either.

Therefore the PDF does not render arrivalScene/pressureSentence in EITHER mode today, so the useAi gate cited at :350 cannot be the cause of any "drop relative to what the screen shows" — removing the gate would change nothing visible because nothing on the active render path reads those slice fields. The defect as framed (a non-AI gate silently dropping prose the PDF would otherwise show) misdiagnoses the cause: the cited line 350 is effectively unreachable dead code for this symptom, and SummaryPage (the only place the symptom's wording would apply) is unused. Per the refutation criteria (cited path dead/unreachable; cited API behaves differently than claimed), the specific bug is refuted.

Note: there IS a real-but-different parity gap — the PDF Overview chapter omits the arrival/pressure prose the web Overview tab displays in both modes — but that is a missing-feature in Overview.jsx, not the useAi-gating bug the reviewer describes, and the reviewer's evidence pins the wrong line as the cause.

rejected.title: Every economic-flow card in the Economics chapter is titled the literal placeholder 'Chain' — slice reads name/chainName but the engine field is label
rejected.why:
The code-level field mismatch is real but the claimed user-visible consequence is contradicted by the default flag state.

VERIFIED FACTS:
- viewModel.js:507 maps `name: c?.name || c?.chainName || 'Chain'`. Engine chains from computeActiveChains.js:244-265 carry the display name in `label` (line 250) and have NO `name`/`chainName`/`isService`/`kind` fields. Enrichment (lines 279-303) adds only `dependency` and `status`. grep confirms no `name:`/`isService:`/`chainName:` assignment in computeActiveChains.js. So the slice does fall through to the literal 'Chain', and `serviceChains` (viewModel.js:565 = `chains.filter(c => c.isService)`) is always empty. Other consumers (Services.jsx, ViabilityTab.jsx, dailyLifeLogic.js:75) correctly use `c.label || c.chainId`, corroborating that `label` is the field.

WHY THE CONSEQUENCE IS WRONG (the disqualifier):
1. flags.js:71-73 — `pdfVisualChains` default is `true` ("PROMOTED default-on"). With the flag ON, EconomicsTrade.jsx:187-193 renders `<SupplyChainFlow chains={settlement.economicState.activeChains} ...>` directly from the RAW engine chains, bypassing the `e.chains` slice entirely. SupplyChainFlow.jsx/ChainRow read `chain.resource`, `chain.processingInstitutions`, `chain.outputs`, `chain.status`, `chain.needLabel`/`needKey` — never the slice's `name`. ChainRow renders a node-flow (resource > imports > institutions > outputs) with a need-category header (needLabel); it has no per-chain title element at all. So in production defaults NO user ever sees "Chain" titles.
2. The buggy slice's `name: c.name` is consumed ONLY at EconomicsTrade.jsx:194-198 (the StatusCard map), which is the `else` branch that runs only when an operator flips the killswitch flag OFF. grep found no other PDF consumer of `e.chains[].name`/`.incomeContribution`/`.isService`.
3. `serviceChains` (viewModel.js:565) has zero consumers anywhere in src/pdf/ — it is computed but never rendered, so "always empty" produces no observable effect.

NET: The claim's headline ("Every economic-flow card ... is titled the literal placeholder 'Chain'") is FALSE under shipping defaults. The defect only manifests if `pdfVisualChains` is disabled (an operator killswitch action), and even then it's a cosmetic title regression (cards titled "Chain" plus an unused empty serviceChains field), not wrong simulation output, money, security, or a crash. There IS a latent code smell — dead/misaligned slice fields reading nonexistent engine keys — but it is not a real user-facing bug on the main path. Marking isReal=false because the asserted failure path (PDF Economics chapter shows 'Chain' for every card) does not occur in the default configuration the claim assumes.

rejected.title: Defense chapter's criminal-faction card never renders — finds faction by category==='criminal' but power factions carry no category field
rejected.why:
REFUTED by direct code reading and empirical generation. The claim's central premise — "power factions carry no category field" — is factually wrong.

powerGenerator.js:2153-2156 tags EVERY faction with a category before returning: `p.forEach(f => { if (!f.category) f.category = inferFactionCategory(f.faction || ''); })`. The returned object is `{ factions: p, ... }` at line 2217, so the categorized array is exactly what becomes `powerStructure.factions`.

inferFactionCategory (powerGenerator.js:590-595) iterates FACTION_CATEGORY_KEYWORDS, whose `criminal` bucket (lines 522-535) includes 'Thiev','Criminal','Smuggl','Corrupt','Shadow','Underworld', etc., and returns the LOWERCASE string 'criminal'. So a "Thieves' Guild" (pushed at line 1156, name contains 'Thiev') gets category 'criminal'.

The viewModel matcher at viewModel.js:620, `(f?.category || '').toLowerCase() === 'criminal'`, therefore matches correctly. factionDynamics.applyLegitimacyMultipliers mutates faction objects in place (sets f.rawPower/f.power/f.powerLabel/f.crisisNote) and does NOT strip category; in fact line 475 reads `f.category === 'criminal'`, and lines 152/161/173/230/237 of factionDynamics.js all rely on factions carrying a 'criminal' category — confirming this field is a real, relied-upon contract, not absent.

Empirical proof: I imported generatePowerStructure and generated city/metropolis power structures with priorityCriminal:98 and criminal institutions. Every faction had a category; the Thieves' Guild and corrupt-government factions carried category:'criminal'; and the exact viewModel expression `find(f => (f?.category||'').toLowerCase()==='criminal')` returned a real faction object with a non-null .faction name. The DefenseSecurity.jsx:294-308 card renders `criminalFaction.faction || .name`, so it WILL appear.

The reviewer evidently inspected only factionDynamics.js:486-503 (which adds rawPower/powerLabel/crisisNote) and the powerGenerator faction-push sites, but missed the category-tagging loop at powerGenerator.js:2155 and the FACTION_CATEGORY_KEYWORDS.criminal mapping. The 'engine identifies criminals by name match' observation (line 1349/1487) is true but irrelevant — name keywords are precisely how inferFactionCategory derives the 'criminal' category, so the category-based viewModel lookup is equivalent.

Minor caveat (does not rescue the claim): the matcher can return a governing corrupt-government faction (e.g. 'Corrupt City Council', 'Shadow Senate') before a dedicated Thieves' Guild because find() takes the first by array order; one could argue the intended card subject is the non-governing criminal org. But that is a faction-selection nuance, not the alleged 'never renders' defect — the card definitively renders with a non-null name. The secondary subFactions-always-empty note is a separate cosmetic point and also does not make the headline claim true.

rejected.title: Campaign PDF relationship map draws nodes but (almost) never edges — expects neighbourNetwork entries to carry the target save's id
rejected.why:
The finding's central factual premise is false. It claims "neighbourNetwork entries don't store target save ids" and that the entry `id` is a link-record id like `live_${name}`. But the canonical link-creation paths in src/components/SettlementsPanel.jsx store the PARTNER SAVE'S id directly in `n.id`: entryForA `id: partnerSave.id` (line 859), entryForB `id: saveId` (866), entryForCurrent `id: linkedSave.id` (920), entryForPartner `id: detail.saveData.id` (926). relationshipLinkMetadata (canonicalRelationship.js:73-81) does not include `id`, so the spread does not clobber it. Therefore buildMap's check at generateCampaignPDF.js:313-314 (`n.id` equals another node's id) succeeds for exactly the user-created links that should produce edges between campaign members — the map DOES draw those edges.

The `live_${name}` id the finding cites (RelationshipsTab.jsx:59) is the `_liveEntry` synthesized ONLY for UNSAVED settlements' live generator output (`_liveNR?.name && !_net.some(...)`, lines 57-67). It never appears in saved-settlement data. generateCampaignPDF operates exclusively on saved settlements (generateCampaignPDF.js:713: `allSaves.filter(s => ids.has(s.id))`), so the cited `live_` id is unreachable here.

Corroborating that `n.id` is canonically the partner save id: deletion/writeback code throughout treats it that way — `n.id!==saveId` (877), `n.id !== id` (899), `n.id !== detail.saveData.id` (938), `const partnerId = removedEntry?.id` (957), and applyWorldPulse.js:325 uses `String(link?.id) === otherId` as the PRIMARY match key. buildGraph (relationshipGraph.js:97-156) uses linkId/name resolution as a robust generalization, not because entries lack `id`; for user links, `id` is present and authoritative.

Kernel of truth: the ONLY neighbourNetwork entries whose `id` is not a partner save id are migration/generation entries (`id: generated_${name}`, SettlementsPanel.jsx:833, no linkId, fromGeneration:true) and unsaved live entries. For a generated neighbour that coincidentally matches a separately-saved campaign member by name, the map would draw no edge (no matching id, no linkId) while the appendix's name-match fallback could connect them — a genuine but narrow parity gap. That is far from the "almost never draws edges" / "linked settlements print as disconnected dots" mass failure the finding asserts. The finding overstates severity on a mistaken premise about the data shape, so it is not a real defect as described.

rejected.title: Overview 'CHARACTER & ORIGIN' section can render as an orphaned header — gate includes foundedBy/origin but body renders neither
rejected.why:
The claim is a structural observation that the gate at src/pdf/sections/Overview.jsx:272 — `(o.character || o.history?.foundedBy || o.history?.origin)` — is wider than the body, which renders only `o.character` (:278) and `o.settlementReason` (:283). That mismatch is real on paper, but the predicted bad render (an orphaned 'CHARACTER & ORIGIN' header with empty body) is NOT reachable for any settlement the engine produces, so it is not a manifest defect.

Evidence traced through every production path:

1) Generation pipeline always co-populates the fields. `o.character` = `s.history.historicalCharacter` (viewModel.js:352); `o.history.foundedBy` = `s.history.founding.foundedBy` (viewModel.js:389). In generateHistory (historyGenerator.js:1031-1086), genArrivalDetail is called unconditionally (line 1044) and always sets `foundedBy` to a non-empty pick from FOUNDERS_BY_TIER (narrativeGenerator.js:630), and historicalCharacter is given a non-null default at line 1065 ('newly founded...' / 'stable and prosperous'). The assembly step then overwrites historicalCharacter via generateCoherence -> buildStressProfile (narrativeGenerator.js:1188, merged at assembleSettlement.js:135-136), and buildStressProfile ALWAYS returns a non-empty string (early return at line 108, and every branch returns a flavor string at 117/141/143). Net: whenever foundedBy/origin is truthy, historicalCharacter (=o.character) is also truthy, so the body renders o.character. No orphan header.

2) User edits cannot create the divergence. Although userEdits.js:81/86 list history.historicalCharacter and history.founding.foundedBy as editable, applyUserEditAction is wired into the UI in exactly one place — npcComponents.jsx:157, for ('npc', idx, 'secret.what'). No surface edits historicalCharacter or foundedBy. The PDF Editable* primitives (Editable.jsx) are read-only renderers (plain Text by default; showField emits a TextInput whose value lives only in the exported PDF form, never written back to settlement state) — they do not call applyUserEditAction.

3) AI mode only reads historicalCharacter (aiLayer.js:172/356); it never strips or nulls it.

Because historicalCharacter is invariantly a non-empty string co-present with foundedBy in every reachable settlement, the empty-header-strip the reviewer describes cannot occur. The over-broad gate is a harmless latent inconsistency, not a manifest bug; the reviewer's own severity (low) reflects that it is borderline. Uncertain reachability resolves to isReal=false.

rejected.title: Issue descriptions pass through without the object guard and message fallback the web deliberately added
rejected.why:
The reviewer's mechanical claims check out, but the premise that object-shaped or message-only issue descriptions actually occur in real data does not hold, so the bug is latent, not manifest.

Confirmed accurate parts:
- viewModel.js:550 (economicsSlice) and :798 (viabilitySlice) map `description: iss?.description` raw, with no `iss?.message` fallback and no object guard.
- EditableProse (src/pdf/primitives/Editable.jsx:117) does `String(defaultValue)`, so an object would print '[object Object]'; line 119 `hideIfEmpty` means a message-only issue prints no body. EconomicsTrade.jsx:376-383 feeds `iss.description` straight into EditableProse.
- The web ViabilityTab (src/components/new/tabs/ViabilityTab.jsx, not the cited path) guards at 166/191/212 with `typeof issue.description==='object' ? issue.description.short||issue.description.text||'' : issue.description||issue.message`.

Why it is not a real defect on real data:
- `grep "description: {"` across all of src/generators/ and src/domain/ returns nothing — no generator ever emits an object-shaped issue description. So the '[object Object]' path is unreachable with engine output.
- The only issue site in the entire engine that carries a `message` field is src/generators/economicGenerator.js:646 (`message: note`), and it ALSO sets `description: note` (a string) on the same object at line 647. There is no message-only issue anywhere, so the empty-body path is also unreachable.
- All five inline issues.push sites (economicGenerator.js:245, 548, 561, 641, 2641) use plain string `description`. The helper-spread issues (foodAnalysis/resource/water/stability/power) are all from this same file.
- aiLayer.js reads only `.title`/`.summary` from via.issues (lines 188-189); it does not reshape `description` into an object.
- The web guards have existed since the first commit (e608542), consistent with defensive boilerplate copied across all issue-rendering blocks (stressConsequences, warnings, etc.) rather than a response to a documented object-shaped data source.

So this is a genuine but harmless web/PDF robustness divergence with no producer of the guarded shapes. Skeptical reviewers would call it latent defensive drift, not a real bug. Uncertainty about whether some future AI-enriched path could inject an object description keeps me from claiming impossibility, but on the actual deterministic engine it cannot manifest.

rejected.title: Mobile bottom nav slice(0,5) silently dropped About when Gallery was inserted into NAV; mobileSingleChrome drops Gallery too — no alternate mobile path exists
rejected.why: The claim's verifiable premises check out, but its central, headline assertion is false, so the bug as stated does not hold. What is TRUE (read directly): - NAV has 6 items (src/App.jsx:62-69: generate, settlements, map, compendium, gallery, howto). - visibleNav filters out only `map` for anon (src/App.jsx:266-269). - The mobile bottom nav renders `visibleNav.slice(0, _readFlag('mobileSingleChrome') ? 4 : 5)` (src/App.jsx:578). - In default prod (mobileSingleChrome defaults to FALSE, src/lib/flags.js:111-113), a signed-in mobile user has a 6-item visibleNav and slice(0,5) drops `howto`/About from the bottom nav. The flag-on slice(0,4) branch (drops Gallery too) is a flagged-OFF experiment, not production. - The bottom-nav comment at src/App.jsx:290-293 is stale, HERO_LINKS is empty (src/App.jsx:76) so the mobile hero strip never renders, and AccountMenu has no About/Gallery entries. What REFUTES the bug as stated: the claim's load-bearing assertion — "no alternate mobile path exists" / "signed-in mobile users have no UI path to About" — is wrong. src/components/AccountPage.jsx:569 renders `FAQ on the About page`, a working link to the About page. AccountPage is reachable on mobile for signed-in users via the account chip (src/App.jsx:334 in flag-off mode, src/App.jsx:608 in flag-on mode). The router (src/hooks/useRoute.js) has no global anchor-click interceptor, so this `` performs a full-page navigation that resolves `/how-to` -> view `howto` (src/lib/routes.js:37) and renders HowToUse with ?tab=faq. So About IS reachable on mobile. The reviewer only searched for programmatic `navigate('howto')` calls and missed this anchor. Likewise, legacy /compare* links redirect to /how-to (src/App.jsx:185), a full-URL navigation that reaches About regardless of the bottom nav, so the redirect-target reachability is also fine. Residual real-but-minor issue (not the claimed bug): About is absent from the signed-in mobile bottom nav and the nav-holds-5 comment is stale, making About a less-discoverable second-class destination on mobile. That is a discoverability/comment-staleness nit, materially weaker than the asserted "unreachable destination" defect, and it rests on an incorrect premise. Severity: not the claimed functional dead-end.

rejected.title: ComparePage.jsx orphaned by the compare-to-About migration: routes and redirect still promise comparison content that has no importer
rejected.why:
Refuted. The claim's stated user-facing impact ("every legacy /compare link lands on a generic About page with no comparison tab content") is false, and its central premise depended on a caveat the reviewer flagged themselves.

Verified flow in the actual repo (not the reviewer's stripped snapshot):
1. App.jsx:184-185 redirects view.startsWith('compare') → navigate('howto', { replace: true, search: '?tab=compare' }).
2. HowToUse.jsx:530-535 initializes activeTab from URLSearchParams ?tab=; 'compare' is a valid TAB id (HowToUse.jsx:21 { id:'compare', label:'How We Compare' }), so activeTab becomes 'compare'.
3. HowToUse.jsx:567 and :599 render {activeTab==='compare' && <CompareTab />}.
4. HowToUse.jsx:471-509 defines CompareTab with REAL, fully-populated comparison content: three Insight blocks (vs AI prose generators, vs map-first tools, vs campaign wikis) plus a Tip. The comment at HowToUse.jsx:468-470 explicitly states the content was "folded in from the former standalone /compare pages (which now redirect here)."

Critically, the reviewer's confidence-lowering caveat was that "HowToUse.jsx is 0 bytes in this snapshot (stripped)... I cannot conclusively rule out that HowToUse statically imports ComparePage." In the real repository HowToUse.jsx is 40,413 bytes / 605 lines (ls + wc) and contains the comparison content inline — no import of ComparePage is even needed because the content was duplicated/migrated into CompareTab. So the redirect promise holds and legacy /compare/* links resolve to a genuine comparison surface.

The one true sub-observation: ComparePage.jsx has zero importers (grep across src/ and tests/ confirms only self-references; COMPARE_VIEWS is also unimported). That makes ComparePage.jsx an orphaned/dead file — a leftover from the migration. But that is unused leftover code, NOT the defect as titled/described. The routes do not "promise comparison content that has no importer": the redirected-to surface has the content. Dead-but-harmless marketing file cleanup is below the bug bar (no user-facing breakage, no simulation/determinism/data/billing/auth/parity impact) and outside scope (style/cleanup, not a real bug or unfinished functional seam). The functional behavior the route promises works correctly.
